alerts-config:active_rules.title: Active Rules
alerts-config:active_rules.formula: Active Rules = fixed count of enabled alert rules currently monitoring (7)
alerts-config:active_rules.formula_explanation: Counts the total number of alert rules that are currently enabled and monitoring your marketing metrics. Each rule defines thresholds for a specific metric (like ROAS, CPC, or Open Rate) and triggers alerts when values fall outside acceptable ranges.
how_to_read: The large number shows total active alert rules across all data sources
The sparkline shows rule count trend over the last 7 days
A higher count indicates more comprehensive monitoring coverage
Click to filter and view all configured rules
Tooltip shows breakdown by data source (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo)
decisions_answered: How many metrics are we actively monitoring?
Do we have sufficient alert coverage across all channels?
Have we recently enabled or disabled any monitoring rules?
Which data sources have the most alert rules configured?
alerts-config:active_rules.narration_text:
This KPI shows the total number of active alert rules monitoring your marketing performance.

Active rules are those currently enabled and evaluating thresholds in real-time. The formula counts all rules with status equals enabled.

A higher number indicates more comprehensive monitoring. However, too many rules can create alert fatigue. Aim for focused coverage of critical metrics.

The sparkline shows if you've been adding or removing rules recently. Click to see the full breakdown by data source.

This answers: How many metrics are monitored? Is coverage sufficient? Which sources have the most rules?

alerts-config:alert_history.title: Alert History
alerts-config:alert_history.formula: Log of alerts triggered over the past 90 days (daily revenue below threshold), each with severity, trigger time, resolution time and resolution duration; most recent first
alerts-config:alert_history.formula_explanation: Complete log of all alerts triggered in the last 30 days, including resolution details. Use this for auditing, identifying patterns, and measuring team response effectiveness. Duration shows how long each alert took to resolve.
how_to_read: Severity shows the alert level when triggered
Rule shows which configured rule generated the alert
Triggered shows when the alert first fired
Resolved shows when the alert was marked resolved (if applicable)
Duration shows total time from trigger to resolution
Acknowledged By shows who took ownership of the alert
Use search to find alerts for specific metrics or time periods
decisions_answered: What alerts have we had in the last 30 days?
How long does it typically take to resolve alerts?
Which team members are responding to alerts?
Are there recurring patterns in our alert history?
alerts-config:alert_history.narration_text:
This table shows the complete history of alerts over the last 30 days.

Each row is an alert that triggered, with details about when it happened, who acknowledged it, and how long it took to resolve.

Duration is particularly important. Alerts that take days to resolve indicate either false positives that should have been dismissed quickly, or real issues that weren't prioritized appropriately.

Look for patterns: the same rule triggering repeatedly, certain team members handling most alerts, or alerts that repeatedly go unacknowledged.

This answers: What's our alert history? How long do resolutions take? Who responds to alerts?

alerts-config:alert_rules.title: Alert Rules
alerts-config:alert_rules.formula: One row per configured alert rule with the metric it watches, its warning and critical thresholds, notification channels and recent trigger count; sorted by status then recent triggers
alerts-config:alert_rules.formula_explanation: Lists all configured alert rules with their thresholds, notification channels, and recent trigger activity. Rules define what conditions generate alerts. Sorted by enabled status then by recent trigger frequency to highlight active monitoring.
how_to_read: Status indicator shows enabled (green) or disabled (gray)
Rule Name describes what the rule monitors
Metric shows which metric is being watched
Warning and Critical columns show threshold values
7d Triggers shows how often this rule triggered recently
Channels shows which notification methods are configured
Actions allow edit, enable/disable, or delete
decisions_answered: What alert rules do we have configured?
Which rules are triggering most frequently?
Are the thresholds set appropriately?
Which notification channels receive which alerts?
alerts-config:alert_rules.narration_text:
This table shows all your configured alert rules.

Each rule defines a condition that triggers an alert. For example: trigger warning if ROAS drops below 5.91, trigger critical if below 4.89.

The 7-day trigger count helps identify noisy rules. If a rule triggers constantly, the threshold may be too sensitive. If it never triggers, it may be too loose.

Edit rules to adjust thresholds based on your business needs. Enable or disable rules seasonally. Delete rules that are no longer relevant.

This answers: What rules exist? Which trigger often? Are thresholds appropriate?

alerts-config:alert_trend.title: Alert Trend (30 Days)
alerts-config:alert_trend.formula: Daily count of triggered alerts (critical vs warning) over the last 30 days, derived from daily revenue crossing its thresholds (multi-line)
alerts-config:alert_trend.formula_explanation: Shows the trend of alert frequency over the last 30 days, broken down by severity level. Helps identify patterns like increasing alert frequency (potential systematic issues) or specific days with unusual alert spikes.
how_to_read: X-axis shows the last 30 days
Y-axis shows the count of alerts triggered
Red line represents critical alerts
Amber line represents warning alerts
Blue line represents informational alerts
Spikes indicate days with many threshold breaches
Upward trend over time suggests deteriorating performance
Flat low lines indicate stable, healthy performance
decisions_answered: Are alerts increasing or decreasing over time?
Were there specific days with unusual alert spikes?
Is performance generally stable or volatile?
Do alert patterns correlate with campaigns or seasonality?
alerts-config:alert_trend.narration_text:
This line chart shows alert frequency over the last 30 days by severity level.

Each line represents a severity: red for critical, amber for warning, blue for informational. The height shows how many alerts triggered each day.

Look for patterns: A consistent upward trend suggests performance is deteriorating. Spikes might correlate with campaign changes or external factors. Flat lines near zero indicate healthy, stable performance.

If you see increasing alerts over time, investigate systematic causes rather than treating individual alerts. The trend matters more than any single day.

This answers: Are alerts increasing? Were there spike days? Is performance stable?

alerts-config:critical_alerts.title: Critical Alerts
alerts-config:critical_alerts.formula: Critical Alerts = count of business metrics breaching their critical threshold (today's GA4 revenue < $21,301) + (trailing-7-day Google Ads ROAS < 4.89) + (trailing-7-day Meta Ads CTR < 2.0%)
alerts-config:critical_alerts.formula_explanation: Counts active critical alerts triggered in the last 24 hours. Critical alerts indicate metrics have breached severe thresholds requiring immediate attention, such as ROAS dropping below the P10 historical baseline or revenue falling significantly below expectations.
how_to_read: The number shows critical alerts currently active (last 24 hours)
Zero (0) is ideal - no critical threshold breaches
Any number above zero requires immediate investigation
Pulse animation appears when count > 0 to draw attention
Red coloring indicates severity requiring urgent action
Click to filter and view only critical alerts
decisions_answered: Are there any metrics in critical condition right now?
Which campaigns or channels need immediate attention?
Has performance degraded significantly overnight?
Do I need to take urgent action on any marketing activities?
alerts-config:critical_alerts.narration_text:
This KPI shows critical severity alerts currently active in the last 24 hours.

Critical alerts mean a metric has breached a severe threshold. For example, ROAS below the 10th percentile of historical performance, or daily revenue dropping more than 30% below expected.

Zero is your goal. Any positive number means something needs immediate attention. The pulse animation helps you notice when there are active critical alerts.

Click the card to see exactly which metrics are in critical status and investigate the root cause.

This answers: Are any metrics critical now? Which need immediate attention? Has performance degraded significantly?

formula_components.name: Revenue breach
formula_components.formula: 1 if today's GA4 total revenue < $21,301 else 0

formula_components.name: ROAS breach
formula_components.formula: 1 if trailing-7-day Google Ads ROAS (conversion value ÷ spend) < 4.89 else 0

formula_components.name: CTR breach
formula_components.formula: 1 if trailing-7-day Meta Ads CTR (link clicks ÷ impressions × 100) < 2.0% else 0

alerts-config:current_alerts.title: Current Alerts
alerts-config:current_alerts.formula: Alerts currently active or acknowledged — each monitored metric (daily revenue, Google Ads ROAS, Meta Ads CTR) whose current value is at or below its warning threshold, with severity and current-vs-threshold values
alerts-config:current_alerts.formula_explanation: Lists all alerts that are currently active or acknowledged but not yet resolved. Sorted by severity (critical first) then by trigger time (most recent first). This is your primary view for managing ongoing alert situations.
how_to_read: Severity column uses color badges: red for critical, amber for warning
Rule column shows which alert rule triggered this alert
Metric shows the specific metric being monitored (ROAS, CPC, etc.)
Current vs Threshold shows actual value against the trigger threshold
Status shows active (unacknowledged) or acknowledged (in progress)
Actions allow you to acknowledge, resolve, or mute each alert
Use filters to focus on specific severities or data sources
decisions_answered: Which alerts need my attention right now?
What are the actual metric values causing alerts?
Have any alerts already been acknowledged by team members?
Which alerts have been active the longest?
alerts-config:current_alerts.narration_text:
This table shows all current alerts requiring attention.

Alerts are sorted by severity first - critical on top - then by when they triggered. Color coding makes priority clear at a glance.

For each alert, you see the rule that triggered it, the specific metric, and the actual value compared to threshold. This tells you how far off the metric is.

Use the action buttons to acknowledge alerts you're investigating, resolve alerts when the issue is fixed, or mute alerts during known exceptional periods.

This answers: What needs attention now? What are the actual values? Who's working on what?

alerts-config:current_performance.title: Current Performance vs Thresholds
alerts-config:current_performance.formula: Each monitored metric's current value versus its warning and critical thresholds, with a normal/warning/critical status and percent-of-threshold
alerts-config:current_performance.formula_explanation: Shows each monitored metric's current value compared to its configured warning and critical thresholds. The percent of threshold column helps visualize how close each metric is to triggering an alert, even when in normal status.
how_to_read: Each row represents one monitored metric
Current Value shows today's actual metric value
Warning and Critical show the configured threshold levels
Status indicator shows normal (green), warning (amber), or critical (red)
Percent of Threshold shows how close to the warning threshold
Progress bar visually shows position relative to warning threshold
Metrics closer to 100% are approaching warning status
decisions_answered: How is each metric performing relative to its thresholds?
Which metrics are closest to triggering alerts?
Are any metrics significantly outperforming their thresholds?
Do any thresholds need adjustment based on current performance?
alerts-config:current_performance.narration_text:
This table shows every monitored metric with its current value and threshold comparison.

The status column tells you the key fact: is this metric normal, warning, or critical? But the percent of threshold column adds crucial context.

A metric showing 95% of threshold is technically normal but very close to warning. One showing 50% has plenty of buffer. This helps you anticipate problems before they trigger alerts.

Use this view to identify metrics that might need threshold adjustment. If a metric consistently sits at 90-95%, consider whether the warning threshold is set correctly for your business.

This answers: How are metrics performing? Which are close to alerting? Do thresholds need adjustment?

formula_components.name: Status
formula_components.formula: critical if value ≤ critical threshold; warning if value ≤ warning threshold; else normal

alerts-config:notification_channels.title: Notification Channels
alerts-config:notification_channels.formula: One card per configured notification channel (email, Slack, etc.) showing its type, enabled status and the alert severities it receives
alerts-config:notification_channels.formula_explanation: Notification channels determine how and where alerts are delivered. Each channel can be configured for specific severity levels. Multiple channels ensure alerts reach the right people through their preferred communication method.
how_to_read: Each card represents a configured notification channel
Channel type icons: envelope = email, chat = Slack, phone = SMS, code = webhook
Status badge shows enabled (green) or disabled (gray)
Severities show which alert levels trigger this channel
Toggle button enables/disables the channel
Edit button opens channel configuration
Add Channel button creates new notification destinations
decisions_answered: Where are alerts being sent?
Are the right people receiving the right alert types?
Do we need additional notification channels?
Are any channels misconfigured or disabled?
alerts-config:notification_channels.narration_text:
This section shows your configured notification channels for alert delivery.

Each channel represents a destination where alerts can be sent. Email sends to specified addresses. Slack posts to a channel. SMS texts to phone numbers. Webhooks call external APIs.

Configure each channel for appropriate severities. Critical alerts might go to SMS for immediate attention. Warnings might go to email for routine review.

Multiple channels provide redundancy. If someone misses an email, they might see the Slack message.

This answers: Where do alerts go? Are the right people notified? Do we need more channels?

alerts-config:percentile_baselines.title: Percentile Baselines
alerts-config:percentile_baselines.formula: For each monitored metric (daily revenue, Google Ads ROAS, Meta Ads CTR), the 10th/25th/50th/75th/90th percentiles of its last 12 months of daily values (grouped bars)
alerts-config:percentile_baselines.formula_explanation: Shows historical percentile distribution for each metric based on 12 months of data. P10 means 10% of historical days had values at or below this level. This helps set intelligent thresholds based on actual performance history rather than arbitrary values.
how_to_read: Each metric is shown as a horizontal grouped bar
P10 (red) represents the 10th percentile - typical critical threshold
P25 (orange) represents the 25th percentile - typical warning threshold
P50 (blue) is the median - typical/expected performance
P75 (green) is above-average performance
P90 (purple) represents excellent/exceptional performance
Compare your threshold settings to these historical baselines
decisions_answered: What are historically normal ranges for each metric?
Are my thresholds aligned with historical performance?
What constitutes truly exceptional performance for each metric?
Should I adjust thresholds based on historical distribution?
alerts-config:percentile_baselines.narration_text:
This chart shows historical percentile distributions for each metric over the past year.

Percentiles help you set intelligent thresholds. P10 means only 10% of historical days performed this poorly - a reasonable critical threshold. P25 is the bottom quarter - a reasonable warning threshold.

P50 is your median, typical performance. P75 and P90 represent above-average and excellent performance respectively.

If your critical threshold is set at P5, you'll rarely get alerts even during bad performance. If set at P30, you'll get alerts too frequently. Use this chart to calibrate appropriately.

This answers: What are normal ranges? Are thresholds aligned with history? What's exceptional performance?

alerts-config:response_time.title: Avg Response Time
alerts-config:response_time.formula: Avg Response Time = fixed average minutes to acknowledge alerts (15.5)
alerts-config:response_time.formula_explanation: Calculates the average time between alert trigger and acknowledgment over the last 7 days. Faster response times indicate efficient alert handling. Industry best practice suggests acknowledging critical alerts within 15 minutes and warnings within 1 hour.
how_to_read: The number shows average minutes to acknowledge alerts
Lower is better - faster response enables quicker action
Green (<15 min) indicates excellent response efficiency
Yellow (15-60 min) indicates acceptable but improvable
Red (>60 min) indicates response process needs improvement
Compare against your team's SLA for alert response
decisions_answered: How quickly is our team responding to alerts?
Are we meeting our response time SLAs?
Do we need to improve our alert notification channels?
Should we add SMS or additional channels for faster notification?
alerts-config:response_time.narration_text:
This KPI shows average time to acknowledge alerts over the last 7 days, measured in minutes.

The formula calculates time between trigger and acknowledgment for all acknowledged alerts. Faster response enables faster action to resolve issues.

Best practice: acknowledge critical alerts within 15 minutes, warnings within 1 hour. Green means excellent response. Yellow means room for improvement. Red means your notification process needs work.

If response times are slow, consider adding SMS notifications or Slack integration for faster team awareness.

This answers: How fast does our team respond? Are we meeting SLAs? Do we need better notification channels?

alerts-config:seasonal_factors.title: Seasonal Adjustment Factors
alerts-config:seasonal_factors.formula: Heatmap of seasonal index by month and channel = that month's average daily revenue for the channel ÷ the channel's overall average daily revenue (above 1.0 = peak, below 1.0 = slow)
alerts-config:seasonal_factors.formula_explanation: Shows seasonal performance patterns by month and channel. Index above 1.0 means above-average performance (peak season). Index below 1.0 means below-average (slow season). Use these factors to adjust thresholds seasonally and avoid false alerts during predictable low periods.
how_to_read: Each row represents a marketing channel
Each column represents a month (Jan-Dec)
Cell color indicates seasonal index: red = below average, white = average, green = above average
Numbers show the exact seasonality multiplier
Index > 1.5 indicates peak season (Feb shows 2.0+ for many channels)
Index < 0.5 indicates slow season (Jun-Sep shows low values)
Use these factors to adjust thresholds by month
decisions_answered: Which months are peak season for each channel?
How much should I adjust thresholds for seasonal variation?
When should I expect below-average performance?
Are there channels that don't follow typical seasonality?
alerts-config:seasonal_factors.narration_text:
This heatmap shows seasonal performance patterns for each marketing channel by month.

The seasonality index compares monthly average to overall average. An index of 2.0 means that month typically performs twice as well as average. An index of 0.5 means half as well.

For this business, January through March shows high indices - peak season. Summer months June through September show low indices - slow season.

Use these factors to adjust thresholds. During February with a 2.5 index, a revenue threshold of $27,000 should become $67,500. This prevents false alerts during predictably high periods and catches real issues during slow periods.

This answers: When is peak season? How much should thresholds adjust? When to expect low performance?

formula_components.name: Seasonality Index
formula_components.formula: Month's avg daily channel revenue ÷ channel's overall avg daily revenue

alerts-config:warning_alerts.title: Warning Alerts
alerts-config:warning_alerts.formula: Warning Alerts = count of business metrics in their warning band (today's GA4 revenue in [$21,301, $27,431]) + (trailing-7-day Google Ads ROAS in [4.89, 5.91]) + (trailing-7-day Meta Ads CTR in [2.0%, 2.85%])
alerts-config:warning_alerts.formula_explanation: Counts active warning alerts triggered in the last 24 hours. Warning alerts indicate metrics approaching critical thresholds - for example, ROAS between P10 and P25 historical baselines. These provide early warning before situations become critical.
how_to_read: The number shows warning alerts active in the last 24 hours
Warnings are less severe than critical but need monitoring
Yellow/amber coloring indicates moderate concern
A few warnings is normal; many warnings suggests review is needed
Warnings often precede critical alerts if not addressed
Click to filter and view only warning alerts
decisions_answered: Are any metrics trending toward critical thresholds?
Which campaigns might become problematic soon?
Should I proactively adjust bids or budgets?
Are there patterns in which metrics generate warnings?
alerts-config:warning_alerts.narration_text:
This KPI shows warning severity alerts currently active.

Warnings are your early warning system. They trigger when metrics approach critical levels but haven't crossed them yet. For example, ROAS between the 10th and 25th percentile.

Some warnings are normal in day-to-day operations. However, multiple warnings across different metrics may indicate a broader issue worth investigating.

Act on warnings before they become critical. Proactive adjustment is easier than reactive firefighting.

This answers: Are metrics trending toward problems? Which campaigns might become critical? Should I adjust budgets proactively?

formula_components.name: Revenue warning
formula_components.formula: 1 if today's GA4 total revenue between $21,301 and $27,431 else 0

formula_components.name: ROAS warning
formula_components.formula: 1 if trailing-7-day Google Ads ROAS between 4.89 and 5.91 else 0

formula_components.name: CTR warning
formula_components.formula: 1 if trailing-7-day Meta Ads CTR between 2.0% and 2.85% else 0

attribution:assisted_conversions.title: Assisted vs Last-Click Conversions
attribution:assisted_conversions.formula: Grouped bars per channel comparing last-click conversions versus assisted conversions, where assisted = max(0, first-touch conversions − last-click conversions) and assist ratio = first-touch conversions ÷ last-click conversions; top 10 channels by last-click conversions.
attribution:assisted_conversions.formula_explanation: Compares conversions where a channel was the final touchpoint (last-click) versus conversions where it assisted earlier in the journey. High assist ratios indicate channels that initiate journeys rather than close them.
how_to_read: Green bars show last-click conversions (channel closed the deal)
Light green bars show assisted conversions (channel was in the journey)
Channels with more assists than last-clicks are "introducers"
Channels with more last-clicks are "closers"
Paid Social often has high assist ratios
Brand Search typically has high last-click ratios
decisions_answered: Which channels start customer journeys vs close them?
Is Paid Social being undervalued by last-click attribution?
Should we give more credit to upper-funnel channels?
How do channels work together in the conversion path?
attribution:assisted_conversions.narration_text:
This chart compares two types of conversions for each channel.

Green bars are last-click conversions - where the channel got the final credit. Light green are assisted conversions - where the channel touched the customer earlier in their journey but someone else closed.

Channels with more assists than last-clicks are introducers - they start journeys. Paid Social often falls here. Channels with more last-clicks are closers - they finish journeys. Brand Search typically dominates here.

This matters for budgeting. Last-click attribution undervalues introducers. If a channel shows high assists, it may deserve more investment than last-click ROAS suggests.

attribution:attribution_gap_trend.title: Attribution Gap Trend
attribution:attribution_gap_trend.formula: Daily attribution gap percentage per channel plotted as area lines: Google gap % = (Google platform revenue − GA4 Google revenue) ÷ GA4 Google revenue × 100, Meta gap % likewise, plus a Klaviyo gap series (currently zero in the trend query).
attribution:attribution_gap_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks how the attribution gap percentage changes over time for Google and Meta. Increasing gaps may indicate tracking degradation or changing user behavior.
how_to_read: Blue line shows Google Ads gap percentage over time
Purple line shows Meta Ads gap percentage over time
Higher values indicate larger discrepancy from platforms
Spikes may indicate tracking issues or unusual campaign activity
Trend direction shows whether attribution is improving or degrading
Use crosshair to see exact values for any date
decisions_answered: Is the attribution gap getting better or worse over time?
When did significant changes in attribution occur?
Which platform has more volatile attribution?
Should we investigate recent tracking changes?
attribution:attribution_gap_trend.narration_text:
This chart tracks how attribution gaps change over 90 days.

The blue line is Google Ads gap percentage. The purple line is Meta Ads. Higher percentages mean platforms are claiming more than GA4 confirms.

Watch for trends. A rising line means the gap is worsening - maybe tracking degraded or user behavior changed. A falling line means improvement.

Spikes deserve investigation. They could indicate tracking issues, iOS changes, or unusual campaign activity.

This helps you monitor: Is attribution accuracy improving? When did problems start? And which platform needs closer attention?

formula_components.name: Google gap %
formula_components.formula: (Google platform revenue − GA4 Google revenue) ÷ GA4 Google revenue × 100

formula_components.name: Meta gap %
formula_components.formula: (Meta platform revenue − GA4 Meta revenue) ÷ GA4 Meta revenue × 100

attribution:attribution_model_comparison.title: Attribution Model Comparison
attribution:attribution_model_comparison.formula: One row per channel with revenue under six attribution models: platform self-reported; GA4 last-click; GA4 first-click; linear ≈ (first-click + last-click) ÷ 2; time decay ≈ last-click × 0.7 + first-click × 0.3; position-based ≈ first-click × 0.5 + last-click × 0.5. Ranked by last-click revenue.
attribution:attribution_model_comparison.formula_explanation: Each attribution model assigns conversion credit differently. Note: Linear, Time Decay, and Position-Based are approximations — GA4 only provides aggregated first-touch and last-touch data at the channel level, not user-level click journeys needed for true multi-touch attribution. These models blend first-click and last-click revenue using weighted averages as a practical substitute.
how_to_read: Compare Platform Self-Reported vs GA4 columns to see attribution gap
Green values indicate strong performance relative to spend
Red highlighting shows gaps greater than 30%
Channels with higher First-Click vs Last-Click are "introducers"
Channels with higher Last-Click vs First-Click are "closers"
Sort by any column to rank channels on that model
decisions_answered: How does each channel perform under different attribution models?
Which channels introduce customers vs close deals?
What is the attribution gap for each advertising platform?
Which attribution model should we use for budget decisions?
Should we reallocate budget based on first-touch contribution?
attribution:attribution_model_comparison.narration_text:
This table compares how revenue is attributed under six different models.

Platform Self-Reported shows what Google and Meta claim. Last-Click and First-Click come directly from GA4 data. Linear, Time Decay, and Position-Based are approximations — since GA4 only provides aggregated first-touch and last-touch data, not user-level journeys, these models blend first-click and last-click revenue using different weightings as a practical substitute for true multi-touch attribution.

Compare the columns to see which channels are "introducers" - high in first-click - versus "closers" - high in last-click.

The gap between Platform and GA4 reveals how much platforms over-claim. Use this to decide which attribution model best fits your business for budget allocation.

attribution:direct_blackhole.title: Direct Traffic Black Hole
attribution:direct_blackhole.formula: Donut breaking total GA4 direct-channel revenue into estimated sub-buckets (True Direct, Dark Social, Untracked Email, Untracked Ads, App Browsers) using fixed industry-research percentages applied to direct revenue.
attribution:direct_blackhole.formula_explanation: Estimates what is hidden inside the "Direct" traffic bucket. True Direct traffic is bookmarks and typed URLs. The rest is likely untracked email, social, or paid traffic that lost attribution.
how_to_read: Pie chart shows estimated breakdown of Direct traffic sources
Gray slice is "True Direct" - actual bookmarks and typed URLs
Purple slice estimates email clicks without UTM parameters
Red slice estimates paid traffic with broken UTM tags
Dark gray is "Dark Social" - private messages and shares
Green insight box shows potential recoverable revenue
decisions_answered: What is really hiding in our Direct traffic?
How much revenue could we attribute with better tracking?
Which untracked source is leaking the most attribution?
Should we prioritize UTM tagging improvements?
attribution:direct_blackhole.narration_text:
The Direct Traffic Black Hole breaks down what is hiding in unattributed traffic.

Not all Direct traffic is true direct - people typing your URL or using bookmarks. Much of it is tracking leakage from other channels.

The pie chart estimates: True Direct in gray, untracked email clicks in purple, broken paid UTMs in red, and dark social - private messages and shares - in dark gray.

The insight box shows how much revenue you could recover with proper UTM tagging.

This answers: What is really in your Direct bucket? How much attribution are you losing? And is UTM improvement worth the investment?

attribution:path_length_distribution.title: Path Length Distribution
attribution:path_length_distribution.formula: Bars showing the share of conversions by number of touchpoints (1, 2, 3, 4, 5+). Counts are estimated by applying fixed industry-pattern ratios to total GA4 conversions (GA4 lacks true multi-touch path data).
attribution:path_length_distribution.formula_explanation: Shows how many marketing interactions customers have before purchasing. Single-touchpoint conversions benefit last-click attribution. Multi-touch journeys require more sophisticated attribution models.
how_to_read: X-axis shows number of touchpoints (1, 2, 3, 4, 5+)
Bar height indicates percentage of conversions at that path length
High single-touchpoint rate favors simpler attribution
High multi-touch rate suggests need for multi-touch models
Average path length shown as annotation
Longer paths typically have higher order values
decisions_answered: How complex are our customers purchase journeys?
Do most customers convert in one touch or many?
Is last-click attribution appropriate for our business?
Should we invest in nurture sequences for longer journeys?
attribution:path_length_distribution.narration_text:
Path Length Distribution shows how many marketing touchpoints customers have before converting.

If most conversions happen in a single touch, last-click attribution works fine. If customers need multiple touches, you need multi-touch attribution models.

Look at the bar heights. High bars on the left mean simpler journeys. High bars on the right mean complex, multi-touch paths.

The average path length matters too. Longer paths often correlate with higher order values - customers who research more spend more.

This helps you understand: Is your purchase journey simple or complex? And does your attribution model match reality?

attribution:revenue_attribution_sankey.title: Revenue Attribution Flow
attribution:revenue_attribution_sankey.formula: Sankey flow from first-touch channels (left) to last-touch channels (right); node width = revenue, where each first-touch node = first-touch revenue per channel and each last-touch node = last-click revenue per channel, with each node's share of total revenue.
attribution:revenue_attribution_sankey.formula_explanation: Visualizes how customers journey from their first marketing touchpoint to their final converting touchpoint. Wider flows indicate more revenue following that path.
how_to_read: Left side shows first-touch channels (how customers discovered you)
Right side shows last-touch channels (where customers converted)
Flow width represents revenue volume following that path
Same-channel flows indicate single-touch or brand loyalty
Cross-channel flows show customer journey complexity
Hover for exact revenue values on each flow
decisions_answered: How do customers flow from discovery to conversion?
Which channels are primarily introducers vs closers?
What cross-channel paths generate the most revenue?
Should we invest more in channels that initiate journeys?
attribution:revenue_attribution_sankey.narration_text:
This Sankey diagram shows how revenue flows from first-touch to last-touch channels.

The left side is where customers first discovered you. The right side is where they finally converted. Flow width indicates revenue volume.

When revenue flows between different channels, it shows journey complexity. When it stays in the same channel, customers converted in a single touch or returned to familiar channels.

This answers: How do customers flow through your marketing? Which channels start journeys versus close them? And where should you invest to capture more revenue at different journey stages?

formula_components.name: First-touch node
formula_components.formula: first-touch revenue per channel ÷ total first-touch revenue × 100

formula_components.name: Last-touch node
formula_components.formula: last-click revenue per channel ÷ total last-click revenue × 100

attribution:time_to_conversion.title: Time to Conversion
attribution:time_to_conversion.formula: Bars showing the share of conversions by time-to-convert bucket (Same Day, 1-3, 4-7, 8-14, 15-30, 30+ days). Counts are estimated by applying fixed industry-pattern ratios to total GA4 conversions.
attribution:time_to_conversion.formula_explanation: Shows how long customers take from first marketing interaction to purchase. Same-day conversions are impulse buys. Longer windows require nurturing and may lose attribution to platform cookie windows.
how_to_read: X-axis shows time buckets (Same Day, 1-3 Days, etc.)
Bar height indicates percentage of conversions in that window
Same-day conversions are typically lower-funnel or retargeting
Longer windows often involve upper-funnel discovery channels
Compare to platform attribution windows (7-day, 28-day)
Conversions beyond window may lose platform attribution
decisions_answered: How quickly do customers convert after first touch?
Are we losing attribution to expired cookie windows?
Should we adjust platform attribution window settings?
What is the typical consideration period for our products?
attribution:time_to_conversion.narration_text:
Time to Conversion shows how long customers take from first marketing touch to purchase.

Same-day conversions are typically impulse buys or retargeting success. Longer windows indicate customers who need time to research or consider.

Compare your distribution to platform attribution windows. If many customers take more than 7 days, Meta's default 7-day click window may miss conversions. If more than 30 days, even Google's 30-day window might not capture the full journey.

This answers key questions: What is your typical sales cycle? Are platforms cutting off attribution too early? And should you extend attribution windows to capture delayed conversions?

attribution:vtc_impact.title: View-Through Conversion Impact
attribution:vtc_impact.formula: Horizontal bars of view-through conversions per campaign, with click conversions and VTC share = view-through conversions ÷ (click conversions + view-through conversions) × 100; campaigns with more than 10 view-through conversions, top 15 by view-through conversions.
attribution:vtc_impact.formula_explanation: View-through conversions are when someone sees an ad but does not click, then converts later. High VTC ratios can inflate platform-reported performance since these are harder to attribute definitively.
how_to_read: Blue portion shows click-through conversions (more reliable)
Yellow portion shows view-through conversions (less reliable)
Campaigns with high yellow are claiming view-through credit
PMAX campaigns typically show highest VTC ratios
Brand campaigns usually show minimal or zero VTC
High VTC may explain platform vs GA4 attribution gap
decisions_answered: Which campaigns claim the most view-through credit?
How much of platform-reported revenue is from VTC?
Should we adjust VTC attribution windows in platform settings?
Are certain campaign types over-relying on view-through claims?
attribution:vtc_impact.narration_text:
This chart shows how much each campaign relies on view-through conversions versus click conversions.

Click conversions - in blue - are more reliable. Someone clicked your ad, then converted. View-through conversions - in yellow - mean someone saw your ad, didn't click, but converted later. Platforms claim credit, but it's harder to prove causation.

Performance Max campaigns typically show the highest VTC ratios. Brand search campaigns usually have none.

High VTC explains why platforms report higher ROAS than GA4. If a campaign is mostly yellow, be skeptical of its reported performance. Consider adjusting your VTC attribution windows.

audience-performance:audience_type_compa.title: Audience Type Performance
audience-performance:audience_type_compa.formula: Grouped bar chart of impressions by audience category (Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, Prospecting) and platform (Google Ads, Meta Ads). Each bar = impressions summed for that category/platform; metadata carries spend, revenue, conversions, CTR, conversion rate, CPA and ROAS = revenue ÷ spend.
audience-performance:audience_type_compa.formula_explanation: Compares performance metrics across audience categories (Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, Prospecting) and platforms (Google Ads, Meta Ads). Each bar represents the aggregated performance of all audiences within that category.
how_to_read: Grouped bars show impressions by audience category and platform
Taller bars indicate higher impression volume for that category
Compare Google (blue) vs Meta (purple) performance within each category
Retargeting typically shows highest efficiency, Prospecting typically shows lowest
Use the legend to understand which platform each color represents
decisions_answered: Which audience type delivers the best performance across platforms?
How does Google Ads audience targeting compare to Meta Ads?
Should we invest more in retargeting vs prospecting?
Which platform should receive more budget for each audience type?
audience-performance:audience_type_compa.narration_text:
This chart compares audience performance across four key categories: Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, and Prospecting.

The grouped bars show impressions by category, with separate colors for Google Ads and Meta Ads.

Retargeting audiences typically perform best because they target users who already know your brand. Lookalike audiences extend your reach to similar users. CRM/Email audiences are your existing customers, and Prospecting reaches entirely new users.

Use this to decide: Which audience type should get more budget? Which platform performs better for each type? And is your current allocation aligned with performance?

audience-performance:custom_audience_per.title: Custom Audience Performance
audience-performance:custom_audience_per.formula: Table of Meta Ads audiences ranked by spend. Per audience: days active, impressions, clicks, conversions, revenue, spend, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPA = spend ÷ conversions, and ROAS = revenue ÷ spend.
audience-performance:custom_audience_per.formula_explanation: Shows performance for Google Ads custom audiences including AUTO (Google-generated), PURCHASE_INTENT, and SEARCH custom audiences. These are keyword-based and interest-based targeting options.
how_to_read: AUTO audiences are Google-generated based on your business signals
PURCHASE_INTENT audiences target users actively researching purchases
SEARCH audiences are based on custom search terms you define
Days active shows how long each audience has been running
Compare performance across audience types to optimize targeting strategy
decisions_answered: Which custom audience type performs best for my business?
Are Google auto-generated audiences effective?
Which search custom audiences should I expand?
Should I create more purchase intent audiences?
audience-performance:custom_audience_per.narration_text:
Custom audiences are keyword and interest-based targeting options in Google Ads.

There are three main types: AUTO audiences that Google generates automatically, PURCHASE_INTENT audiences targeting users researching purchases, and SEARCH audiences based on custom search terms you define.

This table helps you compare performance across these types. Look at the ROAS column to see which audiences drive profitable returns.

Purchase intent audiences often perform well because they target users with clear buying signals. Search custom audiences let you define exactly what topics to target.

Use this data to decide which custom audience types deserve more investment and which need refinement.

audience-performance:ga4_audiences.title: GA4 Predictive Audiences
audience-performance:ga4_audiences.formula: Table of GA4 audiences (including predictive audiences like Likely 7-day purchasers and Likely churners). Per audience: sessions, new users, active users, total revenue, average session duration, average page views, and revenue per user = total revenue ÷ active users.
audience-performance:ga4_audiences.formula_explanation: Shows performance of GA4 audiences including predictive audiences like "Likely 7-day purchasers" and "Likely churners". These audiences use machine learning to predict user behavior.
how_to_read: Purchasers audience shows your actual buying customers for comparison
Likely 7-day purchasers are predicted to buy soon - high priority targets
Likely churning purchasers are at risk of not returning
Revenue per user shows customer value by audience
Session duration and page views indicate engagement levels
decisions_answered: How accurate are GA4 predictive audiences?
Which predictive audience should I target for remarketing?
What is the revenue potential of likely purchasers?
How large is my at-risk customer segment?
audience-performance:ga4_audiences.narration_text:
GA4 predictive audiences use machine learning to identify users likely to take specific actions.

The Purchasers row shows actual buyers - your baseline for comparison. Likely 7-day purchasers are predicted to buy soon - these are high-value remarketing targets.

Likely churning purchasers are at risk of not returning - consider retention campaigns for this segment.

Revenue per user helps compare value across audiences. Non-purchasers typically show near-zero revenue, while purchasers and likely purchasers show much higher values.

Use these audiences to build more targeted campaigns. Export likely purchasers to Google Ads for high-value remarketing. Target likely churners with retention offers.

audience-performance:klaviyo_audiences.title: Klaviyo Email Lists
audience-performance:klaviyo_audiences.formula: Table of Klaviyo email lists and segments ranked by member count. Per row: list/segment name, type (List or Segment), and number of subscribers in it.
audience-performance:klaviyo_audiences.formula_explanation: Shows Klaviyo email list and segment membership counts. These are your owned audiences — subscribers who opted in to receive email marketing. List size directly impacts email channel reach and revenue potential.
how_to_read: Lists are sorted by member count (largest first)
Type column distinguishes static Lists from dynamic Segments
Member count shows active subscribers in each list
Compare list sizes to understand audience distribution
Use search to find specific lists by name
decisions_answered: How large are our email marketing audiences?
Which lists have the most subscribers?
How are subscribers distributed across lists?
Which lists should we prioritize for campaigns?
audience-performance:klaviyo_audiences.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email lists and segments with subscriber counts.

These are your owned audiences — people who opted in to receive your email marketing. Unlike paid ad audiences, these are free to reach.

Lists are sorted by member count so your largest audiences appear first. The type column shows whether it's a static List or a dynamic Segment.

Use this to understand your email reach potential and identify which lists to target for campaigns. Larger lists offer more reach, but engagement rates matter too — cross-reference with the Creative Performance email table.

audience-performance:network_device_heat.title: Network/Device Performance Matrix
audience-performance:network_device_heat.formula: Heatmap of Google Ads audience performance across ad networks (YouTube, Search, Display, etc.) rows and devices (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet) columns. Cell value defaults to ROAS = revenue ÷ cost (selectable to CPA, CTR or conversions); cell intensity reflects performance and size reflects relative spend.
audience-performance:network_device_heat.formula_explanation: Heatmap showing audience performance across different ad networks (YouTube, Search, Display, etc.) and devices (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet). Darker colors indicate better performance.
how_to_read: Rows represent different ad networks (YouTube, Search, Display, etc.)
Columns represent device types (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet)
Darker green cells indicate higher ROAS - better performance
Lighter or yellow cells indicate lower performance
Cell size corresponds to relative spend level
Click any cell to filter page by that network/device combination
decisions_answered: Which network/device combination has the best ROAS?
Should I allocate more budget to mobile or desktop?
Is YouTube performing better on certain devices?
Where should I reduce or increase targeting?
audience-performance:network_device_heat.narration_text:
This heatmap shows how audience targeting performs across different ad networks and devices.

Networks are on the Y-axis: YouTube, Search, Display, Discovery, and others. Devices are on the X-axis: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV.

Darker green cells indicate higher ROAS - these are your best performing combinations. Lighter colors or reds indicate opportunities for optimization.

For example, if YouTube on Mobile shows 4x ROAS but only gets 10% of budget, consider shifting more spend there. If Search on Desktop shows 1.5x ROAS, consider reducing allocation.

Use this to guide both network selection and device bid adjustments in your campaigns.

audience-performance:reach_frequency_ana.title: Reach vs Frequency Analysis
audience-performance:reach_frequency_ana.formula: Scatter plot of Meta ad sets: x = average frequency (impressions ÷ reach), y = CTR (clicks ÷ impressions × 100). Bubble size = spend, color = efficiency zone by frequency. Scope: Meta ad sets with reach > 0 and spend > 100.
audience-performance:reach_frequency_ana.formula_explanation: Scatter plot showing the relationship between average frequency (how often users see ads) and CTR (engagement rate) for Meta ad sets. Bubble size indicates spend level, color indicates efficiency zone.
how_to_read: X-axis shows average frequency (times each user sees your ad)
Y-axis shows CTR - higher means better engagement at that frequency
Green bubbles (1-2x frequency) are in the optimal efficiency zone
Yellow bubbles (2-3.5x frequency) are approaching saturation
Red bubbles (3.5x+ frequency) show audience saturation - consider refresh
Larger bubbles indicate higher spend - prioritize optimizing these
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing audience saturation (too high frequency)?
What is the optimal frequency before CTR starts declining?
Which high-spend ad sets need creative refresh?
Should I expand reach or accept higher frequency?
audience-performance:reach_frequency_ana.narration_text:
This scatter plot reveals the relationship between frequency and engagement for your Meta ad sets.

Frequency is how many times each user sees your ads. CTR measures how often they click. The goal is finding the sweet spot - enough frequency for message retention, but not so much that users tune out.

Green bubbles show ad sets in the optimal 1-2x frequency zone. Yellow indicates warning at 2 to 3.5x. Red shows saturation above 3.5x where performance typically drops.

Bubble size represents spend - bigger bubbles are your biggest investments, so prioritize those.

When you see red bubbles, consider refreshing creative, expanding the audience, or reducing bid caps to lower frequency.

audience-performance:saturation_curves.title: Audience Saturation Curves
audience-performance:saturation_curves.formula: Line chart of how CTR and CPC change as Meta ad frequency rises, bucketed into frequency bands (1.0–1.1x … 5.0x+). Per band: CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100 and CPC = spend ÷ clicks, showing diminishing returns at higher frequency.
audience-performance:saturation_curves.formula_explanation: Line chart showing how CTR and CPC change as frequency increases. Demonstrates diminishing returns - at higher frequencies, CTR drops and CPC rises, indicating audience fatigue.
how_to_read: X-axis shows frequency bands from 1.0 to 6.0+
Blue line shows CTR - typically highest at lower frequencies
Red dashed line shows CPC - typically rises with frequency
The optimal zone (usually 1.5-2.5x) balances reach and efficiency
Sharp CTR drops indicate the saturation point for your audiences
Use this to set frequency caps in your campaigns
decisions_answered: At what frequency do diminishing returns begin?
What frequency cap should I set for my campaigns?
How much does efficiency drop at high frequencies?
Is it worth the higher CPC for additional frequency?
audience-performance:saturation_curves.narration_text:
Saturation curves reveal how performance changes as users see your ads more frequently.

The blue CTR line typically starts high and drops as frequency increases - users get tired of seeing the same ads. The red CPC line usually rises because you're paying more for less engaged users.

The optimal zone is usually 1.5 to 2.5x frequency. Above this, you see diminishing returns.

This chart answers a critical question: at what point should I cap frequency? Find where CTR drops sharply - that's your saturation threshold.

Set your frequency caps just below this point to maintain efficiency while still reaching users enough times to drive action.

audience-performance:user_list_performan.title: User List Performance
audience-performance:user_list_performan.formula: Table of Google Ads remarketing user lists ranked by revenue. Per list: type, days active, impressions, clicks, conversions, revenue, cost, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPA = cost ÷ conversions, and ROAS = revenue ÷ cost.
audience-performance:user_list_performan.formula_explanation: Displays performance metrics for each Google Ads remarketing user list. Lists are categorized by type (REMARKETING, CRM_BASED, SIMILAR, etc.) and sorted by revenue to highlight top performers.
how_to_read: Rows are sorted by revenue by default - top performers appear first
ROAS column shows return on ad spend with color coding (green > 3.0x, yellow 2-3x, red < 2x)
Audience size indicates potential reach for each list
Type badges show the list category (remarketing, CRM-based, lookalike, etc.)
Use search to find specific lists, sort by any column to reorder
decisions_answered: Which remarketing lists generate the most revenue?
Are my CRM-based lists outperforming website remarketing?
Which lists have high CTR but low conversions (consideration stage)?
Should I increase bids on high-performing lists?
audience-performance:user_list_performan.narration_text:
This table shows performance for all your Google Ads remarketing lists.

The key metrics are impressions, clicks, conversions, revenue, cost, CTR, and ROAS. Lists are sorted by revenue so your top performers appear first.

The type column tells you what kind of list it is - REMARKETING for website visitors, CRM_BASED for customer uploads, SIMILAR for lookalikes, and RULE_BASED for custom definitions.

Color coding helps interpretation: Green ROAS means strong returns above 3x, yellow is acceptable at 2-3x, and red below 2x needs attention.

Use this to identify which lists deserve more budget and which need optimization or retirement.

budget-pacing:budget_exhaustion.title: Days Until Budget Exhaustion
budget-pacing:budget_exhaustion.formula: Days until budget exhaustion = budget remaining ÷ daily run rate. Daily run rate = average daily total spend over the selected date range. Budget remaining = monthly budget − month-to-date spend. Compared against days left in the month to flag exhaustion risk.
budget-pacing:budget_exhaustion.formula_explanation: Predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on your current spending velocity. Uses your 14-day average daily spend as the run rate. Compares exhaustion date against days remaining in the month to identify risk.
how_to_read: The large number shows days until budget runs out at current rate
Green means you'll have budget through month end
Yellow means exhaustion 10-20% earlier than month end
Red means you'll run out significantly before month end
Budget Remaining shows dollars left to spend
Daily Run Rate shows your current spending velocity
Exhaustion Date shows the projected day budget hits zero
decisions_answered: Will our budget last through the end of the month?
How many days of budget do we have at current rate?
Do we need to reduce daily spend to stretch the budget?
What's our spending runway if we don't make changes?
budget-pacing:budget_exhaustion.narration_text:
This KPI card predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on current spending velocity.

The formula: Budget remaining divided by your 14-day average daily spend gives days until exhaustion.

Green means you're on track - budget will last through month end. Yellow is a warning - you'll run out 10 to 20 percent earlier than expected. Red is critical - take action or budget runs out prematurely.

The exhaustion date shows the specific day budget hits zero. Compare this to days remaining in the month.

This answers: Will budget last the month? How many days of runway do we have? Do we need to slow spending to stretch the budget?

formula_components.name: Daily run rate
formula_components.formula: Average of daily total spend over the selected range

budget-pacing:budget_reallocation.title: Budget Reallocation Recommendations
budget-pacing:budget_reallocation.formula: Per-platform (Google Ads, Meta Ads) recommendation cards. Platform ROAS = period revenue ÷ period spend. Recommend INCREASE if platform ROAS ≥ average platform ROAS × 1.2 (suggest +20% budget); DECREASE if ROAS ≤ average × 0.8 (suggest −15%); otherwise MAINTAIN.
budget-pacing:budget_reallocation.formula_explanation: Analyzes 14-day platform performance and recommends budget adjustments. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, it recommends increasing budget by 20%. If ROAS is 20% below average, it recommends decreasing by 15%. Helps optimize budget allocation based on efficiency.
how_to_read: Each card shows a platform with its recommendation
Green INCREASE means allocate more budget to this platform
Red DECREASE means reduce budget from this platform
Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is appropriate
14-Day ROAS shows recent efficiency for the platform
Current vs Recommended shows suggested budget change
Rationale explains the reasoning behind recommendation
decisions_answered: Should we shift budget between Google and Meta?
Which platform deserves more investment?
Is our current budget allocation optimal?
What's the expected benefit of reallocation?
budget-pacing:budget_reallocation.narration_text:
These recommendation cards suggest budget reallocation based on 14-day platform performance.

The formula: Calculate each platform's ROAS over 14 days. Compare to average. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, recommend increasing its budget. If 20% below, recommend decreasing.

Green INCREASE means this platform is efficient - give it more budget. Red DECREASE means it's underperforming - shift budget away. Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is fine.

The rationale explains why: high ROAS suggests room to scale profitably. Low ROAS suggests diminishing returns.

This answers: Should we rebalance between platforms? Which deserves more investment? Is our allocation optimal?

formula_components.name: Recommendation
formula_components.formula: Increase if ROAS ≥ avg ROAS × 1.2; decrease if ROAS ≤ avg ROAS × 0.8; else maintain

budget-pacing:daily_spend_trend.title: Daily Spend Trend
budget-pacing:daily_spend_trend.formula: Stacked area chart of daily spend split into Google Ads and Meta Ads, with a 7-day moving average line. Daily spend = Google spend + Meta spend per day; moving average = average of daily spend over the trailing 7 days.
budget-pacing:daily_spend_trend.formula_explanation: Shows 30 days of daily spend as a stacked area chart. Google Ads spend is shown in blue, Meta Ads in lighter blue stacked on top. The moving average line smooths out daily fluctuations to reveal spending trends. The budget reference line shows daily target.
how_to_read: Blue area represents Google Ads daily spend
Light blue stacked area represents Meta Ads daily spend
Total height shows combined daily spend
The dashed horizontal line marks your daily budget target
Weekend periods may show lower spend (depending on campaigns)
The moving average line reveals spending trends
Hover for exact daily values by platform
decisions_answered: How consistent is our daily spending pattern?
Are there spending anomalies or sudden changes?
Which platform contributes more to daily spend?
Do weekends show different spending patterns?
Is spend trending up or down over the month?
budget-pacing:daily_spend_trend.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows daily spend over the last 30 days, broken down by platform.

The blue area is Google Ads spend. Stacked on top in lighter blue is Meta Ads spend. Total height shows combined daily spend.

The dashed line marks your daily budget target. When the area exceeds this line, you overspent that day. When it falls short, you underspent.

Look for patterns: consistent spending helps campaign algorithms learn. Sudden drops might indicate budget exhaustion or paused campaigns. Weekend dips are normal for some businesses.

This answers: How consistent is spending? Are there anomalies? Which platform dominates daily spend?

formula_components.name: Daily spend
formula_components.formula: Google Ads daily spend + Meta Ads daily spend

formula_components.name: 7-day moving average
formula_components.formula: Average of daily total spend over the trailing 7 days

budget-pacing:monthly_pacing.title: Monthly Pacing
budget-pacing:monthly_pacing.formula: Month-to-date pacing vs budget. Actual pacing % = month-to-date spend ÷ monthly budget × 100. Expected % = day of month ÷ days in month × 100. Projected spend = MTD spend + (average daily spend × days remaining). Status flags on-track / over / under.
budget-pacing:monthly_pacing.formula_explanation: Tracks month-to-date spend and projects end-of-month total based on current run rate. The projection uses your average daily spend to estimate final monthly spend. Status indicates whether you'll hit, exceed, or fall short of budget.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual MTD spend percentage
The marker shows expected percentage based on day of month
Projected Spend shows estimated month-end total at current rate
Days Remaining shows business days left in the month
Status badge shows ON_TRACK, OVER_PACING, or UNDER_PACING
Color indicates projected variance from monthly budget
decisions_answered: Will we hit our monthly budget target at current spending rate?
How much budget do we have remaining this month?
What daily spend rate do we need to hit the monthly target?
Are we trending to over-spend or under-spend for the month?
budget-pacing:monthly_pacing.narration_text:
This progress bar shows month-to-date budget pacing with end-of-month projection.

The formulas: Actual pacing is MTD spend divided by monthly budget. Expected pacing accounts for which day of the month we're on. Projection multiplies average daily spend by days remaining.

The status badge tells you the key insight: ON TRACK means you'll hit budget within 5%. OVER PACING means you'll exceed budget. UNDER PACING means you'll fall short.

This answers: Will we hit monthly budget? How much remains? What daily rate do we need to finish on target?

formula_components.name: Expected %
formula_components.formula: Day of month ÷ days in month × 100

formula_components.name: Projected spend
formula_components.formula: MTD spend + (average daily spend × days remaining)

budget-pacing:pacing_alerts.title: Pacing Alerts
budget-pacing:pacing_alerts.formula: Daily spend-vs-budget alerts. Spend ratio = daily spend ÷ daily budget. Critical when ratio above 1.15 or below 0.70; warning when above 1.05 or below 0.85; otherwise on track. Each alert shows the date and the over/under amount.
budget-pacing:pacing_alerts.formula_explanation: Monitors daily spend against budget and generates alerts when significant deviations occur. Critical alerts indicate >15% overspend or >30% underspend. Warning alerts catch moderate deviations of 5-15%. Helps proactive budget management.
how_to_read: Red CRITICAL alerts require immediate attention
Yellow WARNING alerts need monitoring
Blue INFO alerts are notable but not urgent
Each alert shows the date and specific issue
Dollar amounts show actual spend vs budget for that day
Empty state (green checkmark) means budget is on track
Alerts sorted by date, most recent first
decisions_answered: Were there any days with significant overspend?
Are there patterns in budget deviations?
Which days need investigation for spend issues?
Is overall budget management healthy or problematic?
budget-pacing:pacing_alerts.narration_text:
This alert stack shows budget deviations requiring attention over the last 7 days.

The formula: daily spend divided by daily budget gives the spend ratio. Critical alerts trigger when ratio exceeds 1.15 or falls below 0.70. Warning alerts trigger at 1.05 or 0.85.

Red critical alerts need immediate attention - you're significantly over or under budget. Yellow warnings need monitoring but aren't urgent. Blue info alerts are just notable observations.

If you see a green checkmark and no alerts, congratulations - your budget management is healthy.

This answers: Were there overspend days? Are there deviation patterns? Which days need investigation?

budget-pacing:platform_pacing.title: Platform Pacing Comparison
budget-pacing:platform_pacing.formula: Compares pacing of Google Ads vs Meta Ads. Platform pacing % = platform month-to-date spend ÷ platform monthly budget × 100. Platform ROAS = platform revenue ÷ platform spend. Expected pacing reflects the day of month; status flags over / under / on-track per platform.
budget-pacing:platform_pacing.formula_explanation: Compares pacing between Google Ads and Meta Ads. Shows whether platforms are spending at the same rate or if one is significantly ahead or behind. Includes ROAS comparison to understand efficiency differences.
how_to_read: Progress bars show pacing percentage for each platform
Blue bar represents Google Ads pacing
Light blue bar represents Meta Ads pacing
The marker shows expected pacing for the day of month
Grid shows MTD Spend, Budget, Daily Rate, and ROAS
ROAS badges are color-coded: green 8x+, yellow 6-8x, red <6x
Status badges show ON_TRACK, OVER, or UNDER for each platform
decisions_answered: Which platform is pacing faster - Google or Meta?
Should we rebalance budget between platforms?
Which platform has better ROAS efficiency?
Is one platform significantly over or under-spending?
budget-pacing:platform_pacing.narration_text:
This comparison shows budget pacing for Google Ads versus Meta Ads side by side.

The formula: each platform's MTD spend divided by its monthly budget gives the pacing percentage. ROAS is revenue divided by spend for each platform.

Progress bars show how each platform is tracking. The marker shows where they should be based on the day of month. Status badges indicate ON TRACK, OVER, or UNDER for each.

Compare the ROAS values carefully. If Meta has 13x ROAS but Google has 8x, Meta is more efficient per dollar spent.

This answers: Which platform paces faster? Should you rebalance? Which has better efficiency? Is one significantly off track?

formula_components.name: Platform pacing %
formula_components.formula: Platform MTD spend ÷ platform monthly budget × 100

budget-pacing:todays_pacing.title: Today's Pacing
budget-pacing:todays_pacing.formula: Gauge of how much of the daily budget is spent versus how much of the day has elapsed. Pacing % = today's spend ÷ daily budget × 100. Expected pacing % = current hour ÷ 24 × 100. Outside 85–115% of expected triggers warning bands.
budget-pacing:todays_pacing.formula_explanation: The gauge shows how much of your daily budget has been spent relative to how much time has passed today. If you've spent 50% of budget by noon (50% of the day), you're on track. Over 115% or under 85% of expected pacing triggers warning indicators.
how_to_read: The needle shows your current pacing percentage (0-150%)
The marker line indicates expected pacing based on time of day
Green zone (85-115%) means you're on track
Orange zones (70-85% or 115-130%) indicate warning
Red zones (<70% or >130%) indicate critical deviation
Bottom values show actual spend vs daily budget
decisions_answered: Are we spending too fast or too slow today?
Will we exhaust our daily budget before day end?
Should we adjust campaign bids mid-day?
Is today's spend aligned with time of day expectations?
budget-pacing:todays_pacing.narration_text:
This gauge shows today's budget pacing in real-time, updated every 15 minutes.

The formula divides today's spend by your total daily budget, times 100. A second formula calculates expected pacing based on time of day.

To read this: the needle shows current pacing percentage. The marker line shows where you should be based on the hour. Green means on track. Orange means moderate deviation. Red means critical - take action.

This answers: Are we spending too fast or slow? Will we exhaust budget early? Should we adjust bids mid-day to stay on track?

budget-pacing:weekly_pacing.title: Weekly Pacing
budget-pacing:weekly_pacing.formula: Week-to-date pacing vs the weekly budget. Weekly pacing % = week-to-date spend ÷ weekly budget × 100. Expected % = days elapsed ÷ 7 × 100. Week-over-week change = (projected weekly spend − last week's spend) ÷ last week's spend × 100.
budget-pacing:weekly_pacing.formula_explanation: Shows week-to-date spend progress against the weekly budget target. The expected pacing line accounts for which day of the week we're on. Week-over-week comparison shows spending trajectory changes.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual spend percentage (solid fill)
The vertical marker shows expected pacing for current day of week
Green bar if within 10% of expected, yellow if 10-20% off, red if >20%
WTD Spend shows total spent so far this week
Days Remaining shows workdays left to hit weekly target
WoW Change shows projected spend versus last week
decisions_answered: How is this week's spend tracking against last week?
Do we need to adjust daily spending to hit weekly targets?
Are there any days where we significantly over/under-spent?
What's our spending velocity trend week-over-week?
budget-pacing:weekly_pacing.narration_text:
This progress bar tracks week-to-date spending against your weekly budget.

The formula: week-to-date spend divided by weekly budget, times 100. The expected marker shows where you should be based on the day of week.

Color coding helps: green means within 10% of expected. Yellow means 10 to 20% variance. Red means more than 20% off track.

Week-over-week change shows if you're trending higher or lower than last week. This helps answer: Are we on pace this week? Do we need to adjust daily spend? How does this compare to last week?

formula_components.name: Week-over-week change
formula_components.formula: (Projected weekly spend − last week spend) ÷ last week spend × 100

campaign-matrix:campaign_performance_tab.title: Campaign Performance Details
campaign-matrix:campaign_performance_tab.formula: One row per campaign (Google Ads + Meta Ads) with spend, revenue, conversions, clicks, impressions, and ROAS = revenue ÷ spend, CPC = spend ÷ clicks, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, conversion rate = conversions ÷ clicks × 100; each tagged with its performance quadrant.
campaign-matrix:campaign_performance_tab.formula_explanation: The performance table consolidates all campaign metrics for detailed analysis. Each row represents one campaign with its key performance indicators. Green highlighting indicates strong performance, red indicates areas needing attention.
how_to_read: Click column headers to sort by any metric
Use search to find specific campaigns by name
Quadrant badges show Scale (green), Opportunity (gold), Optimize (red), or Test (gray)
ROAS values are color-coded: green >=3x, neutral 1-3x, red <1x
Toggle column visibility to focus on specific metrics
Export data to CSV or Excel for further analysis
decisions_answered: Which specific campaigns are our top performers?
What are the exact metrics for any given campaign?
How do campaigns compare when sorted by specific KPIs?
Which campaigns have high CTR but low conversion rate?
What is the full breakdown of spend, revenue, and efficiency?
campaign-matrix:campaign_performance_tab.narration_text:
The Campaign Performance Table gives you the complete picture for every campaign.

Each row shows one campaign with its channel, type, spend, revenue, ROAS, CPC, CTR, and conversion rate. Quadrant badges indicate whether it's in Scale, Opportunity, Optimize, or Test zone.

Click any column header to sort. Search filters by campaign name. Toggle columns to focus on what matters. Export to CSV or Excel for deeper analysis.

Color coding helps interpretation: green ROAS values are 3x or better, red means under 1x.

This answers: What exactly are the numbers for each campaign? How do they compare when ranked by any metric? And which campaigns have the best or worst specific KPIs?

campaign-matrix:klaviyo_campaigns.title: Klaviyo Email Campaigns
campaign-matrix:klaviyo_campaigns.formula: One row per Klaviyo email campaign with sends, opens, clicks, and rates: open rate = opens ÷ sends × 100, click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100, click-to-open rate (CTOR) = clicks ÷ opens × 100; ranked by sends.
campaign-matrix:klaviyo_campaigns.formula_explanation: This table shows email campaign performance from Klaviyo, separated from the paid scatter plot since email campaigns have no ad spend. Metrics are sourced from Klaviyo event data (sends, opens, clicks) aggregated at the campaign level.
how_to_read: Sends = total emails delivered for the campaign
Opens = total email opens (including multiple opens per recipient)
Clicks = total link clicks within the email
Open % = percentage of sends that were opened
Click % = percentage of sends that received a click
CTOR = click-to-open rate, clicks as a percentage of opens (engagement quality)
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns drive the most engagement?
What open and click rates are our campaigns achieving?
Which campaigns have high open rates but low click-through (content issue)?
Which campaigns have the best click-to-open ratio (best content)?
campaign-matrix:klaviyo_campaigns.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email campaign performance, separated from the paid campaign scatter plot since email has no ad spend.

Each row is one email campaign with its engagement metrics. Sends is total emails delivered. Opens and clicks track engagement.

Open Rate tells you subject line effectiveness. Click Rate shows overall campaign performance. CTOR - click to open rate - is the purest content quality metric, showing what percentage of people who opened actually clicked.

A good benchmark: 40 to 50 percent open rates, 2 to 3 percent click rates, and 5 percent or higher CTOR.

campaign-matrix:performance_matrix.title: Campaign Performance Matrix
campaign-matrix:performance_matrix.formula: Scatter of paid campaigns by spend (X, log scale) versus ROAS = revenue ÷ spend (Y); bubble size = revenue. Quadrant boundaries set at the median spend and median ROAS of campaigns with positive spend and ROAS (Scale, Scale Opportunity, Optimize/Cut, Test/Kill).
campaign-matrix:performance_matrix.formula_explanation: The 2x2 matrix plots campaigns by their spend (x-axis, log scale) and ROAS (y-axis). Quadrant boundaries are set at the median spend and median ROAS of all visible campaigns. Bubble size represents revenue - larger bubbles indicate higher-revenue campaigns.
how_to_read: Each bubble represents one campaign - size indicates revenue
Colors indicate channel: Blue = Google Ads, Purple = Meta Ads, Green = Klaviyo
Top-right (Scale) = High spend, high ROAS - increase budget here
Top-left (Scale Opportunity) = Low spend, high ROAS - hidden gems to grow
Bottom-right (Optimize/Cut) = High spend, low ROAS - needs attention or reduction
Bottom-left (Test/Kill) = Low spend, low ROAS - evaluate or pause
Dashed lines show median spend and ROAS thresholds
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are high-efficiency opportunities ready to scale?
Which campaigns are burning budget with poor returns and should be cut?
Where are our hidden gems with high ROAS but low spend?
How does campaign performance vary by channel?
What is the total addressable scaling opportunity in our portfolio?
campaign-matrix:performance_matrix.narration_text:
The Campaign Performance Matrix is a 2x2 quadrant analysis that instantly reveals which campaigns to scale, optimize, or cut.

Each bubble is one campaign. The horizontal axis shows spend on a log scale - further right means more spending. The vertical axis shows ROAS - higher means better returns. Bubble size represents revenue.

The matrix divides into four quadrants based on median spend and ROAS. Top-right is your Scale zone - high spend and high ROAS. These are your winners - increase budget here.

Top-left is Scale Opportunity - low spend but high ROAS. These are hidden gems waiting to grow. Bottom-right is Optimize or Cut - high spend but poor ROAS. These need immediate attention.

Bottom-left is Test or Kill - low spend and low ROAS. Evaluate whether to keep testing or pause entirely.

This chart answers: Which campaigns should get more budget? Which are wasting money? And where are the untapped opportunities?

formula_components.name: Quadrant
formula_components.formula: Scale if spend ≥ median spend and ROAS ≥ median ROAS; Scale Opportunity if spend < median and ROAS ≥ median; Optimize/Cut if spend ≥ median and ROAS < median; else Test/Kill

campaign-matrix:roas_distribution.title: ROAS Distribution
campaign-matrix:roas_distribution.formula: Histogram of campaign counts grouped into ROAS buckets (0-0.5x, 0.5-1x, 1-2x, 2-3x, 3-4x, 4-5x, 5-7x, 7-10x, 10x+), where campaign ROAS = revenue ÷ spend; each bar stacked by channel (Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo).
campaign-matrix:roas_distribution.formula_explanation: This histogram shows how campaigns are distributed across ROAS performance buckets. Each bar is stacked by channel, showing the contribution of Google Ads, Meta Ads, and Klaviyo to each bucket.
how_to_read: X-axis shows ROAS buckets from poor (0-0.5x) to excellent (10x+)
Y-axis shows the count of campaigns in each bucket
Stacked colors show channel breakdown within each bucket
Target ROAS marker (typically at 2-3x) indicates minimum acceptable performance
Campaigns left of the target line may need optimization or pausing
Right skew (more campaigns in higher buckets) indicates healthy portfolio
decisions_answered: What is the distribution of ROAS across our entire campaign portfolio?
How many campaigns are below our target ROAS threshold?
Which channels have more campaigns in high-ROAS buckets?
Is our portfolio skewed toward efficient or inefficient campaigns?
What percentage of campaigns exceed our break-even point?
campaign-matrix:roas_distribution.narration_text:
The ROAS Distribution histogram shows how your campaigns spread across performance buckets.

On the horizontal axis, you see ROAS ranges from 0 to 10x plus. Each bar shows how many campaigns fall into that range, stacked by channel.

The vertical dashed line marks your target ROAS - typically around 2 to 3x. Campaigns to the left need attention. Campaigns to the right are performing well.

A healthy portfolio has a right skew - more campaigns in higher buckets. If most campaigns cluster on the left, you have a systemic efficiency problem.

Use this to answer: How is performance distributed? How many campaigns are below target? And which channels dominate the high-performance buckets?

ceo-pulse:conversion_rate.title: Site Conversion Rate
ceo-pulse:conversion_rate.formula: Site Conversion Rate = orders ÷ website sessions × 100; Change % vs comparison period
ceo-pulse:conversion_rate.formula_explanation: Percentage of website sessions that result in a purchase. This measures overall site effectiveness at converting visitors to customers. Ecommerce benchmarks typically range from 2-4%, but seed/gardening businesses often achieve higher rates.
how_to_read: The main value shows today's conversion rate as a percentage
Compare to the 7-day average to identify unusual days
The sparkline shows daily conversion rate trend
