Sudden drops may indicate site issues or traffic quality problems
Click to analyze conversion by device, source, or landing page
decisions_answered: What percentage of visitors are buying today?
Is our conversion rate healthy compared to recent performance?
Are there site issues causing conversion problems?
Should we investigate traffic quality from certain channels?
ceo-pulse:conversion_rate.narration_text:
This KPI shows your site conversion rate - the percentage of sessions that result in a purchase.

The formula is straightforward: purchases divided by sessions, times 100. The 7-day average provides a baseline for comparison.

Reading this card: Today's rate is shown as a percentage. Compare it to your 7-day average. For gardening e-commerce, healthy rates are often 7 to 10 percent, higher than general retail.

Watch for sudden drops. A conversion crash could indicate site bugs, payment issues, or a sudden influx of low-quality traffic. The sparkline helps spot these patterns.

Key questions: What's today's conversion rate? Is it normal? Are there potential site issues? Click to drill down by device, traffic source, or landing page for root cause analysis.

formula_components.name: Conversion Rate
formula_components.formula: total orders ÷ sessions × 100 over the period

ceo-pulse:marketing_spend.title: Marketing Spend
ceo-pulse:marketing_spend.formula: Total ad spend = Google Ads spend + Meta Ads spend, summed over the selected range; the gauge shows that spend as a percentage of the daily budget (default 10,000), with green up to 80%, amber 80-100%, and red over 100%.
ceo-pulse:marketing_spend.formula_explanation: Combined advertising spend across Google Ads and Meta Ads for today. The gauge shows progress toward the daily budget, with color coding to indicate whether spending is on pace, approaching limit, or over budget.
how_to_read: The gauge arc shows spend progress toward daily budget
Green zone (0-80%) indicates healthy spend pace
Amber zone (80-100%) indicates approaching budget limit
Red zone (100%+) indicates over-budget spending
Platform breakdown shows Google vs Meta allocation
decisions_answered: How much are we spending on marketing today?
Are we on pace to hit our daily budget?
Is spend split appropriately between Google and Meta?
Do we need to adjust campaigns to stay within budget?
ceo-pulse:marketing_spend.narration_text:
This gauge shows today's marketing spend across all paid channels - essential for budget management and efficiency tracking.

The formula combines Google Ads spend, converted from micros, with Meta Ads spend. The gauge shows progress as a percentage of your daily budget.

Reading this gauge: The colored arc fills as spend accumulates. Green means healthy pace. Amber as you approach budget. Red means over-budget - campaigns may need pausing.

The breakdown below shows Google versus Meta split. Imbalanced spending may indicate one platform is performing better or has different pacing rules.

Key questions: How much spent today? Are we on budget? What's the platform split? Use this to make intraday budget adjustments if needed.

formula_components.name: Total Ad Spend
formula_components.formula: Google Ads spend + Meta Ads spend over the selected range

ceo-pulse:revenue_trend.title: 7-Day Revenue Trend
ceo-pulse:revenue_trend.formula: Daily net revenue summed per day over the selected range, drawn as an area chart (Magento is the revenue source of truth).
ceo-pulse:revenue_trend.formula_explanation: Area chart showing daily revenue over the past 7 days. This visualization reveals patterns, trends, and any anomalies in recent performance. The gradient fill emphasizes magnitude while the line shows trajectory.
how_to_read: The line shows revenue trajectory over 7 days
Higher points indicate higher daily revenue
Look for weekly patterns (weekday vs weekend differences)
Sudden drops or spikes may indicate promotions, issues, or events
The most recent point (today) may be partial if viewing mid-day
decisions_answered: How has revenue trended over the past week?
Are there concerning drops or encouraging spikes?
Is there a weekly pattern we should plan around?
How does today compare to the recent trend?
ceo-pulse:revenue_trend.narration_text:
This area chart shows your 7-day revenue trend - providing context for today's performance within recent history.

The formula aggregates daily revenue from GA4 ecommerce data. The visualization emphasizes both magnitude through the filled area and trajectory through the line.

Reading this chart: Follow the line left to right from 7 days ago to today. Consistent upward slope is positive. Downward slope requires attention. Today's point may be partial if you're viewing mid-day.

Look for patterns: Many businesses see weekday versus weekend differences. Spikes might indicate promotions or email campaigns. Sudden drops could signal site issues or competitive actions.

Key questions: How's the recent trend? Any concerning patterns? How does today fit? Click any point to drill into that day's detailed performance breakdown.

ceo-pulse:top_products.title: Top 5 Products Today
ceo-pulse:top_products.formula: The five products with the highest revenue over the selected range, each bar showing that product's revenue with units sold alongside.
ceo-pulse:top_products.formula_explanation: Ranks the top 5 products by revenue for today, showing both dollar value and quantity sold. This reveals which products are driving today's performance and helps identify trends or anomalies in product mix.
how_to_read: Bars show revenue contribution for each product, ranked highest to lowest
Longer bars indicate higher revenue contribution
The seed catalog typically leads during peak ordering season
Watch for unusual products appearing in top 5 (may indicate promotions or trends)
Click any product bar to drill into detailed product performance
decisions_answered: Which products are selling best today?
Is our product mix aligned with expectations?
Are promotions driving expected products to the top?
Should we feature certain products more prominently?
ceo-pulse:top_products.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows your top 5 products by revenue today - revealing what's driving sales performance.

The formula sums item revenue for today, grouped by product name, then ranks the top 5. Quantity sold is shown alongside for context.

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean higher revenue. Products are ranked from top to bottom by revenue contribution. For seed companies, the annual catalog often leads during Q1.

Watch for surprises: A product you didn't expect in the top 5 might indicate a trending item or successful promotion. A usually-popular product missing might signal inventory issues.

Key questions: What's selling best? Is the mix expected? Are promotions working? Click any product to analyze its hourly sales pattern, customer segment, and historical performance.

ceo-pulse:traffic_channels.title: Traffic by Channel Today
ceo-pulse:traffic_channels.formula: Sessions by traffic channel over the selected range, top eight channels by sessions, with users shown alongside each channel.
ceo-pulse:traffic_channels.formula_explanation: Shows session distribution across traffic channels for today. Understanding traffic sources helps identify which marketing channels are driving visitors and whether the mix aligns with marketing investments.
how_to_read: Bars show session count per channel, color-coded by channel type
Green indicates organic channels (search, social)
Orange indicates paid channels (search, social)
Purple indicates direct traffic
Blue indicates email marketing
Click any channel to analyze its performance in detail
decisions_answered: Where is our traffic coming from today?
Are paid channels delivering expected volume?
Is organic traffic growing or declining?
Does our traffic mix align with marketing spend allocation?
ceo-pulse:traffic_channels.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows where today's website traffic is coming from - essential for understanding marketing channel performance.

The formula counts sessions by channel grouping, ordered by volume. Color coding distinguishes channel types: green for organic, orange for paid, purple for direct.

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more sessions from that channel. Unassigned traffic often leads, which typically represents direct or untagged sources.

Compare to your marketing spend: If paid channels receive significant budget but show low sessions, investigate campaign settings. If organic is strong, your SEO and content are working.

Key questions: Where's traffic coming from? Are paid channels delivering? Is organic growing? Click any channel to analyze its conversion rate, revenue contribution, and user behavior.

churn-analysis:churn_by_orders.title: Churn Risk by Order Count
churn-analysis:churn_by_orders.formula: Bar chart of average churn probability by historical order-count band (1, 2, 3-5, 6-10, 10+ orders), with customer count and average lifetime value per band. Churn probability is the rule-based Magento churn risk score.
churn-analysis:churn_by_orders.formula_explanation: Groups customers by their historical order count and calculates average churn probability for each group. This reveals how purchase frequency correlates with churn risk - typically, customers with more orders have lower churn probability.
how_to_read: Each bar shows average churn probability for customers with that order count
Longer bars indicate higher churn risk for that customer segment
One-time buyers typically show the highest churn risk
As order count increases, churn probability usually decreases
Labels show exact churn percentage for precision
Click any bar to filter and explore customers in that order bucket
decisions_answered: How does purchase frequency correlate with churn risk?
What is the churn risk for one-time buyers vs. repeat customers?
At what order count does churn risk significantly decrease?
Should we focus retention efforts on converting one-time buyers?
churn-analysis:churn_by_orders.narration_text:
This chart reveals how order count correlates with churn risk.

One-time buyers almost always show the highest churn probability - often 80% or higher. They've tried your product once but haven't committed. This is your biggest opportunity.

Watch how churn drops with each additional order. The second purchase is the critical hurdle. Once customers buy a third, fourth, or fifth time, they become significantly more loyal.

Customers with 10+ orders are your advocates. Their churn risk is low because they've proven their loyalty through behavior.

The insight here is clear: focus your retention budget on converting one-time buyers to second-time buyers. That's where you get the biggest reduction in churn risk per dollar spent.

churn-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.title: Churn Risk Distribution
churn-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.formula: Donut chart of customer count per churn-risk tier (Critical/High/Medium/Low/Minimal), also returning total lifetime value at risk, average orders and average order value per tier. Tier is a rule-based level from the Magento churn risk score.
churn-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.formula_explanation: Segments customers into five risk tiers based on Klaviyo predictive churn probability scores. Each tier represents a 20% probability range, with High Risk (80-100%) being customers most likely to churn.
how_to_read: The donut chart shows the proportion of customers in each risk segment
Red/orange slices indicate high churn probability - these customers need immediate attention
Yellow indicates medium risk - worth monitoring and proactive engagement
Green slices show low risk - your loyal, engaged customer base
Center number shows total customers with churn predictions
Click any segment to filter and view customers in that risk tier
decisions_answered: How is our customer base distributed across churn risk levels?
What percentage of customers are at high risk of churning?
Is our overall churn risk profile improving or worsening?
Which risk segment should we prioritize for retention campaigns?
churn-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by predicted churn probability from Klaviyo analytics.

Red and orange represent your danger zone - customers with 60% or higher probability of churning. These need immediate intervention with win-back campaigns or personal outreach.

Yellow is your watch list at 40-60% - they could go either way. Proactive engagement can tip them toward staying.

Green shows your loyal base under 40% churn probability. These customers are engaged and likely to continue purchasing.

The key insight is the proportions. If more than half your customers are in red or orange, you have a serious retention problem that will impact future revenue.

churn-analysis:lifecycle_stages.title: Customer Lifecycle Stages
churn-analysis:lifecycle_stages.formula: Bar chart of customer count by lifecycle stage based on days since last order: Active (0-30d), At Risk (31-60d), Lapsing (61-90d), Lapsed (91-180d), Churned (180+d), with average churn probability and total lifetime value per stage.
churn-analysis:lifecycle_stages.formula_explanation: Classifies customers based on recency of their last engagement with your brand. Active customers engaged within 30 days are healthy. Beyond 90 days, customers are considered lapsing or churned, requiring re-engagement efforts.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a lifecycle stage based on days since last activity
Green (Active) shows customers engaged in the last 30 days - your healthy core
Yellow/Orange (At Risk, Lapsing) shows customers drifting away - intervene now
Red (Lapsed, Churned) shows customers who have disengaged - need win-back campaigns
Bar length indicates customer count in each stage
Click any bar to filter and see customers in that lifecycle stage
decisions_answered: What is the lifecycle distribution of our customer base?
How many customers are actively engaged vs. at risk of churning?
Which lifecycle stage has the most CLV at risk?
When should we trigger win-back campaigns?
churn-analysis:lifecycle_stages.narration_text:
This funnel chart shows your customers distributed across lifecycle stages based on recency of engagement.

Active customers - those engaged in the last 30 days - are your healthy core. Keep them engaged with regular communication.

At Risk and Lapsing stages - 31 to 90 days inactive - are your intervention window. These customers haven't churned yet, but they're drifting. A well-timed email or offer can bring them back.

Lapsed and Churned - beyond 90 days - are harder to recover. They need aggressive win-back campaigns, and expect lower response rates.

The shape of this funnel tells a story. A healthy business has most customers in Active, with progressively fewer in each subsequent stage. If you see bulging in Lapsed or Churned, you have a retention problem.

churn-analysis:next_order_window.title: Next Expected Order Window
churn-analysis:next_order_window.formula: Bar chart bucketing repeat customers by next expected order window — Overdue, Due in 30 days, Due in 31-60 days, Due in 61-90 days, Due in 90+ days — derived from days since last order versus average days between orders, with customer count and lifetime value per bucket. Rule-based on Magento order history, not a Klaviyo prediction.
churn-analysis:next_order_window.formula_explanation: Uses Klaviyo predictive analytics to forecast when customers are expected to make their next purchase, then segments them by urgency. "Overdue" customers have passed their expected order date and need immediate re-engagement.
how_to_read: Red (Overdue) shows customers who have passed their predicted next order date
Green (Due in 30 days) shows customers expected to order soon - good opportunity
Yellow/Blue segments show customers with longer expected purchase windows
Large Overdue segment indicates retention intervention is needed
Hover to see customer count and associated CLV for each window
Click any slice to filter and view customers in that window
decisions_answered: How many customers are overdue for their next purchase?
What is the predicted CLV at risk from overdue customers?
When should we send reminder or re-engagement emails?
How are customers distributed across expected purchase windows?
churn-analysis:next_order_window.narration_text:
This donut chart shows when customers are expected to make their next purchase based on Klaviyo's predictive analytics.

The red Overdue segment is critical - these customers should have ordered by now based on their historical patterns but haven't. They're at elevated churn risk and need immediate attention.

Green shows customers due in the next 30 days. These are prime targets for reminder emails or offers to accelerate their purchase.

The longer windows - 60 days, 90 days, beyond - represent customers with naturally longer purchase cycles. They may need less frequent but well-timed engagement.

A large Overdue segment signals trouble. These are potential lost customers. Calculate the CLV at stake and prioritize win-back campaigns accordingly.

churn-analysis:priority_customers_table.title: Priority At-Risk Customers
churn-analysis:priority_customers_table.formula: One row per priority at-risk customer — churn risk level High or Critical and lifetime value over $100 — showing churn probability, total lifetime value, orders, last order date and days inactive; ranked by lifetime value (top 100).
churn-analysis:priority_customers_table.formula_explanation: Identifies high-value customers at high churn risk for prioritized retention intervention. These customers have both significant lifetime value (>$100 CLV) and elevated churn probability (70%+), making them the highest priority for personalized outreach.
how_to_read: Table shows customers with both high CLV and high churn probability
Sorted by CLV descending - highest value at-risk customers first
Churn probability is color-coded: red >90%, orange 80-90%, yellow 70-80%
Days inactive shows recency of engagement - red if 90+ days
Search by email or name to find specific customers
Click any row to view full customer profile and history
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers are most at risk of churning?
How much CLV is at risk from our top at-risk customers?
Which customers deserve personalized retention outreach?
What is the engagement history of our most valuable at-risk customers?
churn-analysis:priority_customers_table.narration_text:
This table identifies your highest priority retention targets - customers with both high lifetime value and high churn risk.

The list is sorted by CLV, putting your most valuable at-risk customers at the top. These deserve personalized attention, not just automated emails.

Pay attention to the churn probability and days inactive columns. A customer with $5,000 CLV who is 90% likely to churn and hasn't engaged in 100 days needs immediate, personal outreach.

This is where ROI on retention is highest. Saving one $5,000 CLV customer is worth more than winning back 50 low-value customers. Assign your best customer success resources here.

Export this list for your win-back campaigns, or use it to prioritize phone calls and personalized emails.

churn-analysis:weekly_churn_trend.title: Churn Probability Trend
churn-analysis:weekly_churn_trend.formula: Line chart of the week-over-week churn rate over time (active customers in the prior period who did not return ÷ prior-period active customers × 100), aggregated to the selected granularity, with active customer count per period.
churn-analysis:weekly_churn_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks the average churn probability across your customer base week by week. Rising trends indicate increasing churn risk across the customer base, while declining trends suggest improving retention.
how_to_read: The line shows average churn probability percentage over 90 days
Upward trends indicate increasing churn risk - customers are disengaging
Downward trends suggest improving retention - your efforts are working
Sudden spikes may indicate external events or campaign issues
Hover over any point to see the exact week and churn percentage
Click a point to analyze that specific week in detail
decisions_answered: Is our overall churn risk increasing or decreasing?
Are there seasonal patterns in churn risk?
Did recent campaigns or changes impact churn positively or negatively?
When did significant shifts in churn risk occur?
churn-analysis:weekly_churn_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your average churn probability week by week over the last 90 days.

An upward trend is a warning sign - more of your customers are at risk of leaving. Investigate what changed. Was there a product issue? Price increase? Campaign that backfired?

A downward trend is positive - your retention efforts are working. Keep doing what's effective.

Look for sudden spikes or drops. These often correlate with specific events - a major sale, a new competitor, or changes in your product or service.

Use this chart to measure the impact of retention initiatives. If you launched a win-back campaign, did the average churn probability decrease in following weeks?

cohort-analysis:acquisition_source_table.title: Cohort Performance by Acquisition Source
cohort-analysis:acquisition_source_table.formula: One row per acquisition channel showing customers acquired, average lifetime value (first-order revenue ÷ new customers), total lifetime value, and average first-order value; plus portfolio-wide average orders, repeat rate and average churn risk repeated on every row. Channel comes from Magento order attribution; all value metrics are Magento-derived.
cohort-analysis:acquisition_source_table.formula_explanation: Compares customer quality metrics across different acquisition sources. The email marketing method field indicates how customers first subscribed (API, Magento, List Import, etc.), serving as a proxy for acquisition channel.
how_to_read: Each row represents an acquisition source/method
Customers column shows total acquired through that channel
Avg CLV reveals which sources bring highest-value customers
Total LTV shows absolute value contribution
Repeat Rate indicates loyalty potential from each source
Churn Risk highlights which sources produce at-risk customers
Table is sortable by any column - click headers to sort
decisions_answered: Which acquisition sources produce the highest-value customers?
Where should we invest more marketing budget?
Which sources have the best repeat purchase rates?
Are certain sources associated with higher churn risk?
What is the ROI potential of each acquisition channel?
cohort-analysis:acquisition_source_table.narration_text:
This table ranks your customer acquisition sources by quality and value metrics.

Each row represents a different method by which customers entered your database - API integrations, e-commerce platform imports, list uploads, and more. The metrics reveal which sources bring your best customers.

Sort by Average CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition sources. These deserve more investment. But don't ignore volume - a source with slightly lower average CLV but 10x the customers might generate more total value.

Repeat Rate is a key quality indicator. Sources with high repeat rates produce engaged customers who come back. Low repeat rates might indicate promotional-driven acquisitions who bought once for a discount and never returned.

The Churn Risk column is a warning signal. High churn risk sources might need different lifecycle marketing approaches, or they might not be worth the acquisition investment at all.

formula_components.name: Avg AOV
formula_components.formula: Weighted average first-order value across the channel

cohort-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.title: Churn Risk Distribution by Cohort
cohort-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.formula: 100% stacked area chart showing, for each acquisition cohort period, the percentage of customers in each churn-risk tier (Minimal/Low/Medium/High/Critical). Risk tier is a rule-based level from the Magento churn risk score.
cohort-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.formula_explanation: Customers are classified into churn risk tiers using Klaviyo predictive analytics. This stacked area chart shows how churn risk distributes across cohorts, helping identify at-risk customer groups and prioritize retention efforts.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort quarter
Y-axis shows percentage of cohort (100% stacked)
Green area (Low Risk) represents stable, engaged customers
Yellow (Medium Risk) shows customers worth monitoring
Orange (High Risk) indicates proactive retention needed
Red (Critical Risk) requires immediate intervention
Healthy cohorts should have more green area
decisions_answered: How is churn risk distributed across different cohorts?
Which cohorts have the highest proportion of at-risk customers?
Is churn risk improving or worsening over time?
What is the total value at risk in each cohort?
Where should we focus retention marketing efforts?
cohort-analysis:churn_risk_distribution.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows the churn risk composition of each quarterly cohort.

The colors represent risk tiers from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Green at the bottom represents low-risk customers - those with less than 25% churn probability. As you move up through yellow, orange, and red, churn probability increases.

Older cohorts naturally show different risk profiles than recent ones. Watch for patterns: Do Q1 spring cohorts consistently show lower churn risk? This might indicate that gardening season acquisitions produce more committed customers.

The critical insight is intervention timing. If you see a cohort's red area growing, these customers need immediate win-back campaigns before they're lost entirely. The cost of retention is almost always lower than the cost of acquiring replacement customers.

Target the high-value customers within critical risk tiers first - not all churning customers are equally worth saving.

cohort-analysis:cohort_clv_trend.title: Cohort CLV Trend
cohort-analysis:cohort_clv_trend.formula: Line chart of average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort period. The Total CLV and Historic CLV lines are both the average net lifetime value per cohort; the Predicted CLV line is always zero (no predicted-CLV source exists).
cohort-analysis:cohort_clv_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks how average customer lifetime value varies by acquisition month. Total CLV combines historic realized value with predicted future value. Comparing these metrics across cohorts reveals acquisition quality trends and how CLV matures over customer tenure.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort acquisition month over the past 3 years
Y-axis shows average CLV in dollars
Blue solid line shows Total CLV (historic + predicted)
Green solid line shows Historic CLV (realized purchases only)
Purple dashed line shows Predicted CLV (expected future value)
Older cohorts naturally show higher historic CLV as they have had more time to purchase
The gap between historic and predicted narrows as cohorts mature
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?
How does CLV mature as cohorts age?
Which acquisition months/seasons produce our highest-value customers?
How much future value is predicted for recent cohorts?
Is there seasonality in customer acquisition quality?
cohort-analysis:cohort_clv_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value across monthly acquisition cohorts.

The blue line shows total CLV - the sum of what customers have already spent plus what they're predicted to spend. The green line shows just the historic, realized CLV. The gap between them represents predicted future value.

Older cohorts on the left will naturally show higher CLV - they've had more time to make purchases. But watch for trends: Are recent cohorts tracking above or below where older cohorts were at the same age? This reveals whether acquisition quality is improving or declining.

The spring months often show CLV peaks for gardening businesses - customers acquired during planting season tend to be more engaged and valuable. If you see dips, investigate what marketing channels or campaigns were active during those periods.

The predicted CLV line is particularly valuable for recent cohorts - it forecasts their expected value trajectory based on similar customer behavior patterns.

formula_components.name: Avg Total / Historic CLV
formula_components.formula: Average net lifetime value per cohort period

cohort-analysis:cohort_detail_table.title: Cohort Detail Table
cohort-analysis:cohort_detail_table.formula: One row per monthly cohort showing cohort size, average lifetime value (revenue per customer), total lifetime value (size × revenue per customer), average orders, average order value, average churn risk, converted (active) customers and repeat buyers (active × retention rate). VIP count is always zero.
cohort-analysis:cohort_detail_table.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table showing detailed metrics for each monthly cohort. Enables comparison of acquisition quality, customer development, and lifetime value across time periods.
how_to_read: Each row is a monthly cohort, newest at top
Cohort Size shows customers acquired that month
Avg CLV and Total LTV show value metrics
Churn Risk is color-coded (red = high risk)
Converted/Repeat/VIP show customer development counts
Q1 months (Jan-Mar) often highlighted as spring season
Sort by any column to rank cohorts
decisions_answered: Which monthly cohorts perform best across all metrics?
How do spring cohorts compare to other seasons?
Which cohorts have the highest/lowest churn risk?
What is the VIP conversion rate for each cohort?
Are recent cohorts on track compared to historical benchmarks?
cohort-analysis:cohort_detail_table.narration_text:
This comprehensive table provides detailed metrics for every monthly cohort in your database.

Each row represents customers acquired in that specific month. You can see cohort size, average and total CLV, order metrics, and customer development progression from converted to repeat to VIP.

The Churn Risk column is color-coded for quick identification. Red indicates cohorts with elevated churn probability that may need immediate retention attention. Green indicates healthy, stable cohorts.

Q1 rows (January through March) are often highlighted because spring is critical for gardening businesses. Compare Q1 cohorts across years to see if acquisition quality is improving.

Use the sorting capability to rank cohorts by any metric. Sort by Avg CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition months. Sort by VIP count to find cohorts that produced the most high-value customers.

This table is your cohort analysis command center - it aggregates everything you need to evaluate customer acquisition and development performance.

cohort-analysis:cohort_retention_heatmap.title: Cohort Retention Heatmap
cohort-analysis:cohort_retention_heatmap.formula: Heatmap of retention rate by cohort month (rows) and months since signup 0-24 (columns); retention rate = customers from the cohort who ordered in month N ÷ original cohort size × 100. Cell color intensity tracks retention.
cohort-analysis:cohort_retention_heatmap.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows the percentage of customers from each cohort who made a purchase in subsequent months. Cohorts are grouped by acquisition month, and retention is calculated as the percentage of the original cohort still actively purchasing at each time interval.
how_to_read: Y-axis shows cohort months (most recent at top), with cohort size in parentheses
X-axis shows months since signup (M0 = first month, M24 = two years later)
Cell color intensity indicates retention rate - darker green = higher retention
Month 0 (M0) shows initial purchase rate, typically highest
Look for diagonal patterns to understand typical retention decay curves
The M12 column often shows a bump due to annual seasonal purchase cycles
decisions_answered: How does customer retention change over time for each cohort?
Which cohorts have the strongest/weakest retention curves?
At what month do we see the biggest retention drop-off?
Is there an annual purchase cycle (M12 bump) in our customer behavior?
Are recent cohorts retaining better or worse than historical cohorts?
cohort-analysis:cohort_retention_heatmap.narration_text:
This heatmap visualizes how customer cohorts retain over time after their initial acquisition.

Each row represents a monthly cohort - a group of customers who first appeared in that month. The cohort size shows how many customers were acquired. As you move right across the row, you see what percentage of those original customers made a purchase in subsequent months.

Month 0 shows the initial purchase rate. The natural decay as you move right is expected - not all customers continue purchasing. However, pay attention to patterns: Do you see a bump at Month 12? This often indicates seasonal or annual purchase behavior - customers who bought seeds last spring return again this spring.

Compare cohorts vertically to identify quality variations. If Q1 cohorts consistently show better retention than Q3 cohorts, this suggests seasonal acquisition quality differences. Spring gardening season customers may be more engaged.

Use this to identify optimal intervention timing - where does retention drop most sharply? That's when lifecycle emails and retention campaigns should trigger.

cohort-analysis:cohort_size_trend.title: Cohort Size & Conversion Trend
cohort-analysis:cohort_size_trend.formula: Combo chart per cohort period: bars for new customers acquired and for converted customers (2+ orders), plus a conversion-rate line = converted ÷ new customers × 100.
cohort-analysis:cohort_size_trend.formula_explanation: Shows the volume of customers acquired each month and what percentage converted to making at least one purchase. This reveals acquisition volume trends and the effectiveness of converting acquired customers into buyers.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort acquisition month
Light blue bars show total new customers acquired that month
Dark blue bars show customers who converted to purchasers
Orange line (secondary axis) shows conversion rate percentage
Tall bars indicate high acquisition volume periods
Bar gap indicates customers who never made a first purchase
Seasonal patterns often visible with Q1 peaks
decisions_answered: How many customers are we acquiring each month?
What percentage of acquired customers make their first purchase?
Are we scaling acquisition while maintaining conversion quality?
Which months show the highest acquisition and conversion?
Is there seasonality in our customer acquisition patterns?
cohort-analysis:cohort_size_trend.narration_text:
This combo chart shows customer acquisition volume alongside conversion effectiveness.

The light blue bars represent total customers who entered the database each month. The dark blue bars show how many of those actually made a purchase - converting from prospects to customers. The gap between them represents unconverted database entries.

The orange line tracking conversion rate is crucial. You want both high volume AND high conversion. If volume spikes but conversion drops, you may be acquiring lower-quality leads. Q1 typically shows both higher volume and higher conversion for gardening businesses due to seasonal demand.

Compare year-over-year patterns: Are you acquiring more customers than last year's same month? Is your conversion rate improving? These metrics directly tie to marketing efficiency and customer acquisition cost effectiveness.

cohort-analysis:order_frequency_distribu.title: Order Frequency Distribution
cohort-analysis:order_frequency_distribu.formula: Grouped bar chart (by cohort period) of customer count across average days-between-orders bands — under 30, 30-90, 90-180, 180-365, 365+ days — restricted to customers with 2+ orders, with average lifetime value per band.
cohort-analysis:order_frequency_distribu.formula_explanation: Customers with at least 2 orders are grouped by their average days between orders. This reveals purchase frequency patterns and helps identify the natural buying cycles of your customer base.
how_to_read: X-axis shows order frequency bands (time between orders)
Y-axis shows customer count
Bars grouped by cohort year for comparison
Shorter intervals (< 30 days) indicate highly engaged customers
Annual (180-365 days) is common for seasonal products like seeds
Click any bar to see average CLV for that frequency band
Distribution shape reveals your natural purchase cycle
decisions_answered: What is the typical purchase frequency for repeat customers?
Is there a dominant buying cycle (monthly, seasonal, annual)?
How does purchase frequency differ across cohort years?
Which frequency bands have the highest customer value?
When should we trigger re-engagement campaigns?
cohort-analysis:order_frequency_distribu.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart reveals the natural purchase cycles of your repeat customers.

Only customers with 2 or more orders are included - we need repeat behavior to measure frequency. The bars show how many customers fall into each time-between-orders band.

For gardening and seed businesses, the 180-365 day band often dominates. This makes sense - customers buy seeds for spring planting and return the following year. However, you might see more customers in shorter bands if you successfully encourage season-extension or indoor gardening.

Compare cohort years: Are more recent cohorts showing shorter purchase intervals? That would indicate improving customer engagement. If older cohorts are concentrated in longer intervals, consider whether your retention programs are successfully increasing purchase frequency.

The average CLV for each frequency band is illuminating - frequent buyers almost always have significantly higher lifetime value.

cohort-analysis:retention_curve_comparis.title: Retention Curve Comparison
cohort-analysis:retention_curve_comparis.formula: Line chart comparing retention curves across cohort periods; each line plots average retention rate (active customers ÷ cohort size × 100) by months since signup (0-24).
cohort-analysis:retention_curve_comparis.formula_explanation: Compares retention curves across selected quarterly cohorts. Each line tracks the percentage of customers still active (making purchases) at each month since acquisition, enabling direct comparison of cohort quality over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months since customer acquisition (M0-M24)
Y-axis shows retention rate as percentage of original cohort
Each colored line represents a different quarterly cohort
Select up to 4 cohorts for clear comparison
All curves start at M0 initial purchase rate and decay over time
Look for curves that stay higher longer - those are better cohorts
M12 bumps indicate annual purchase cycles
decisions_answered: How do different cohorts compare in retention over time?
Which cohorts retain customers best/worst?
At what point do retention curves stabilize?
Is there a consistent M12 annual purchase bump?
Are recent cohorts retaining better than historical cohorts?
cohort-analysis:retention_curve_comparis.narration_text:
This multi-line chart lets you directly compare retention curves across different cohorts.

Each line represents a quarterly cohort tracking from Month 0 through Month 24. All curves start with the initial purchase rate and decay as customers stop buying. The shape and rate of this decay tells you a lot about cohort quality.

A curve that stays higher longer indicates a better cohort - those customers continued purchasing at higher rates. Compare Q1 cohorts (spring acquisition) to other quarters - they often show better retention due to gardening seasonality alignment.

Look for the M12 bump. If customers show increased activity around their anniversary, you have an annual purchase cycle to leverage. Time your retention campaigns to capture this natural behavior.

Select different cohort combinations to answer specific questions: Are 2024 cohorts better than 2023? Do spring cohorts consistently outperform fall cohorts? This comparison view makes cohort quality differences visually obvious.

cohort-analysis:segment_distribution.title: Customer Segment Distribution by Cohort
cohort-analysis:segment_distribution.formula: Stacked bar chart (by cohort period) of customer count across order-frequency segments: No Purchase (0), One-Time (1), New (2), Growing (3-5), Loyal (6-10), VIP (10+).
cohort-analysis:segment_distribution.formula_explanation: Customers are segmented by their purchase frequency into behavioral tiers. This chart shows how each quarterly cohort distributes across these segments, revealing the quality and maturation of customer relationships over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort quarter (when customers were acquired)
Y-axis shows customer count (toggle to percentage mode available)
Each color represents a customer segment based on order frequency
Gray (No Purchase) at bottom shows unconverted customers
Green segments (Loyal, VIP) at top represent highest-value customers
Older cohorts should show larger proportions in higher segments
Recent cohorts will be bottom-heavy (mostly One-Time) until they mature
decisions_answered: How does customer quality distribute across different cohorts?
What percentage of each cohort becomes repeat, loyal, or VIP?
Are we successfully moving customers up the loyalty ladder?
Which cohorts produced the most VIP customers?
How long does it take for cohorts to mature into higher segments?
cohort-analysis:segment_distribution.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows how customers within each quarterly cohort distribute across behavioral segments.

The segments are based on purchase frequency: customers who never purchased (gray), one-time buyers (red), repeat buyers with 2-3 orders (yellow), loyal customers with 4-6 orders (light green), and VIP customers with 7+ orders (dark green).

Older cohorts on the left should show more green - they've had time to mature into higher segments. Recent cohorts will naturally be mostly gray and red since customers haven't had time to reorder yet.

The key insight is trajectory: Compare Q1 2022 to Q1 2023 at the same maturity point. Are you converting more customers to repeat buyers? Growing your VIP percentage? This reveals whether your retention and loyalty programs are improving.

Switch to percentage mode to normalize for cohort size differences and focus purely on segment mix quality.

cohort-analysis:top_states_by_ltv.title: Top States by Total LTV
cohort-analysis:top_states_by_ltv.formula: Horizontal bar chart ranking states by total lifetime value (sum of net lifetime value per state), with customer count per state; top 15 states. (Uses Magento net lifetime value, not a predicted-CLV field.)
cohort-analysis:top_states_by_ltv.formula_explanation: Ranks states by total lifetime value contribution rather than average. This highlights which regions generate the most absolute value, combining both customer quality (CLV) and quantity (volume).
how_to_read: States ranked from highest to lowest total LTV
Bar length represents total customer lifetime value
Dollar amounts shown on bar ends
Customer count shown in parentheses for context
High-volume states often lead even with moderate per-customer CLV
Click bars to drill into state-level cohort analysis
decisions_answered: Which states contribute the most total customer value?
Is our revenue concentrated in a few states or diversified?
How does total LTV correlate with state volume?
Which states represent our biggest market opportunities?
Where are we underperforming relative to market potential?
cohort-analysis:top_states_by_ltv.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart ranks your top states by total customer lifetime value.

Unlike average CLV, total LTV accounts for volume. California, Texas, and Florida often lead this chart due to their large populations, even if their average per-customer CLV is below states like Washington or Utah.

The customer count in parentheses provides context. A state with $2M total LTV from 10,000 customers is very different from $2M from 50,000 customers - the first has higher quality customers.

Use this to prioritize marketing investments. Your top 5 states likely represent the majority of your business value. But also look for efficiency opportunities - states with high average CLV but low total LTV might have untapped growth potential.

Revenue concentration is a risk factor. If 50% of your value comes from 3 states, consider diversification strategies.

formula_components.name: Avg CLV
formula_components.formula: Total state lifetime value ÷ state customer count

competitive:impression_share_trend.title: Impression Share Trend (60 Days)
competitive:impression_share_trend.formula: Line chart of daily impression-weighted top-position rate over the period. Per day: top-position rate = sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions; metadata carries absolute-top rate and below-top share = 100 − top-position rate.
competitive:impression_share_trend.formula_explanation: Daily impression share shows what percentage of eligible impressions your ads captured. Budget lost share indicates impressions missed due to insufficient budget. Rank lost share shows impressions missed due to ad rank (quality score + bid) being lower than competitors.
how_to_read: Blue line shows your actual impression share - higher is better
Orange dashed line shows share lost due to budget constraints
Red dashed line shows share lost due to competitive rank issues
Sum of all three should theoretically equal 100% of eligible impressions
Spikes in budget lost indicate days where budget ran out
Rising rank lost trends suggest increasing competitive pressure
decisions_answered: How has our competitive position changed over the past 60 days?
Are we losing more impressions to budget or rank issues?
When did significant competitive changes occur?
Is our impression share trending up or down over time?
competitive:impression_share_trend.narration_text:
This multi-line chart tracks your competitive position over the past 60 days across three key metrics.

The blue line shows your impression share - the percentage of eligible impressions where your ads appeared. Higher is better, and above 50% typically indicates strong market presence.

The orange dashed line shows budget lost share - impressions you missed because your budget ran out. If this is consistently high, you need more budget to capture available demand.

The red dashed line shows rank lost share - impressions you missed because competitors outranked you. This is driven by quality score and bid amounts.

Watch for trends: rising budget lost means increasing demand you're not capturing. Rising rank lost means competitors are becoming more aggressive. A healthy account typically shows impression share trending up while both loss metrics trend down.

formula_components.name: Top-position rate
formula_components.formula: Sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions per day

creative-performance:ad_strength_distrib.title: Ad Strength Distribution (Google)
creative-performance:ad_strength_distrib.formula: Donut of Google Ads spend split by ad-strength rating (Excellent / Good / Average / Poor / Unspecified). Each segment = spend summed for ads of that strength; segment metadata also carries ad count and CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100.
creative-performance:ad_strength_distrib.formula_explanation: Visualizes Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating. Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on headlines, descriptions, and asset variety. This reveals how many ads fall into each quality tier and their associated spend.
how_to_read: The donut chart segments represent spend allocation by ad strength category
Green segments (Excellent/Good) indicate high-quality ads following best practices
Orange (Average) and red (Poor) segments highlight improvement opportunities
The center displays total spend across all ad strength categories
CTR metrics below each category show engagement correlation with quality
decisions_answered: What percentage of our Google ads have strong quality ratings?
How much budget is going to low-quality ads that could be improved?
Is there a correlation between ad strength and click-through rate?
Which ads should be prioritized for headline and description optimization?
creative-performance:ad_strength_distrib.narration_text:
This donut chart displays your Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating.

Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on asset variety, headline quality, and description relevance. The formula counts distinct ads per strength category and sums their associated spend.

Green segments show Excellent and Good rated ads. Orange represents Average. Red indicates Poor ratings. The center shows your total spend.

This helps answer: What percentage of ads have strong ratings? How much budget goes to low-quality ads? And which ads need optimization attention?

formula_components.name: Spend per strength
formula_components.formula: Spend summed for ads in each ad-strength rating

formula_components.name: CTR per strength
formula_components.formula: Clicks ÷ impressions × 100 for ads in that rating

creative-performance:creative_fatigue_cu.title: Creative Fatigue Curve
creative-performance:creative_fatigue_cu.formula: Line chart of average CTR by days-since-launch, one line per platform (Google + Meta). For each day N, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100 across all ads alive on day N (impression-weighted). Scope: ads with a 14+ day lifespan, days 1–60.
creative-performance:creative_fatigue_cu.formula_explanation: Tracks CTR degradation over time to detect creative fatigue. Ads typically experience declining engagement as audiences become saturated. This curve helps identify the optimal refresh timing before performance deteriorates significantly.
how_to_read: Lines show average CTR for ads at each day since their launch
Blue line represents Google Ads, purple line shows Meta Ads
Downward slopes indicate fatigue setting in over time
Steeper drops suggest faster audience saturation
First 30 days are shown as this is the critical fatigue window
decisions_answered: How quickly do our creatives experience fatigue?
What is the optimal creative refresh cadence for our campaigns?
Do Google and Meta ads fatigue at different rates?
At what point should we rotate in fresh creative assets?
creative-performance:creative_fatigue_cu.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative fatigue by showing CTR over time since ad launch.

The formula calculates average CTR for all ads at each day since launch using row numbering to track ad age. A 20% CTR decline signals the fatigue threshold.

Blue shows Google Ads performance, purple shows Meta. Downward slopes indicate fatigue. Steeper drops mean faster saturation.

This answers critical questions: How quickly do creatives fatigue? What's the optimal refresh timing? And do platforms fatigue differently?

creative-performance:creative_type_perfo.title: Spend by Creative Type
creative-performance:creative_type_perfo.formula: Bar chart of total spend by creative type, grouped by platform — Google ad types (RSA, Shopping, Display, Video) and Meta object types (Share, Video, Photo). Each bar = spend summed per type; metadata carries CTR (clicks ÷ impressions × 100), ad count and ROAS (conversion value ÷ spend).
creative-performance:creative_type_perfo.formula_explanation: This chart aggregates spend and performance by creative type across both Google Ads (RSA, Shopping, Display, Video) and Meta Ads (Share, Video, Photo). It reveals which creative formats receive the most investment and generate the best engagement.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent total spend for each creative type
Google types (blue) and Meta types (purple) are grouped separately
Longer bars indicate higher investment in that creative format
Compare bar lengths to identify budget allocation patterns across formats
Hover over bars to see exact spend values and related metrics
decisions_answered: Which creative types receive the most budget allocation?
How does spend distribution differ between Google and Meta platforms?
Should we shift budget from underperforming formats to winning ones?
Are we diversifying our creative mix adequately across formats?
creative-performance:creative_type_perfo.narration_text:
This chart shows your spend distribution across creative types for both Google and Meta platforms.

The formula aggregates spend by creative type: Google spend from ad stats divided by one million to convert from micros, and Meta spend summed directly.

Horizontal bars display total investment per type. Blue represents Google formats like RSA and Shopping. Purple shows Meta formats like Share and Video.

Use this to answer: Which types get most budget? How does allocation differ between platforms? And should you shift investment to better-performing formats?

creative-performance:cta_performance.title: CTA Button Performance (Meta)
creative-performance:cta_performance.formula: Bar chart of Meta Ads spend by call-to-action button type (Shop Now, Learn More, Order Now, etc.). Each bar = spend summed per CTA type; metadata carries CTR (clicks ÷ impressions × 100) and cost-per-click (spend ÷ clicks).
creative-performance:cta_performance.formula_explanation: Compares performance of different Meta Ads call-to-action buttons. CTAs signal user intent and affect both click quality and conversion likelihood. This analysis reveals which CTAs drive the most engagement and efficiency.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show total spend for each CTA button type
SHOP_NOW typically dominates for e-commerce brands
CTR percentages below indicate engagement rate per CTA type
Compare spend allocation with CTR to identify efficiency opportunities
High spend with low CTR may indicate CTA mismatch with audience intent
decisions_answered: Which call-to-action buttons drive the highest engagement?
Is our CTA selection aligned with campaign objectives?
Should we test different CTAs on high-spend, low-CTR ads?
What CTA types work best for our specific audience segments?
creative-performance:cta_performance.narration_text:
This chart analyzes Meta Ads call-to-action button performance.

The formula sums spend and averages CTR by CTA type, covering buttons like Shop Now, Learn More, and Order Now. Different CTAs signal different user intents.

Horizontal bars display spend per CTA. The CTR values below show engagement rates. Compare these to spot efficiency gaps.

Use this to answer: Which CTAs drive highest engagement? Are we using the right CTAs for our goals? And should we A/B test different buttons on underperforming ads?

creative-performance:email_creative_perf.title: Email Creative Performance (Klaviyo)
creative-performance:email_creative_perf.formula: Table of top 20 Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by emails sent. Columns: sends, opens, clicks, conversions, revenue, plus open rate = opens ÷ sends × 100, click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100, click-to-open rate = clicks ÷ opens × 100, and revenue per send = revenue ÷ sends.
creative-performance:email_creative_perf.formula_explanation: Shows top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics. CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures how compelling the email content is for those who actually opened it.
how_to_read: Campaigns are sorted by total sends (highest first)
Open % shows what fraction of recipients opened the email
Click % shows clicks as a percentage of total sends
CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures clicks relative to opens — a better indicator of content quality
Use search to find specific campaigns by subject line
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns reached the most recipients?
Which subject lines drove the highest open rates?
Which email content generated the most clicks?
How does engagement vary across different campaign types?
creative-performance:email_creative_perf.narration_text:
This table shows your top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics.

Open percentage is opens divided by sends. Click percentage is clicks divided by sends. CTOR, or click-to-open rate, measures clicks relative to opens — a better indicator of content quality since it only counts engaged recipients.

Use this to identify which subject lines drive opens, which content drives clicks, and how engagement varies across campaigns.

creative-performance:media_type_comparis.title: Image vs Video Performance (Meta)
creative-performance:media_type_comparis.formula: Compares Meta Ads spend between media formats (Image, Video, Other). Each format shows spend summed for that media type, its spend share, CTR (clicks ÷ impressions × 100) and cost-per-click (spend ÷ clicks).
creative-performance:media_type_comparis.formula_explanation: Compares performance between image and video creative formats on Meta. This analysis helps determine whether your audience responds better to static or motion creative, informing content strategy and production priorities.
how_to_read: Cards display spend share percentage for each media type
CTR values show engagement rate per media type
Bar chart compares absolute spend between Image, Video, and Other
Higher CTR with proportional spend indicates efficient format
Large spend gaps may suggest format testing opportunities
decisions_answered: Does our audience respond better to image or video ads?
Should we invest more in video production or static images?
What is the efficiency difference between media types?
Are we allocating budget appropriately between formats?
creative-performance:media_type_comparis.narration_text:
This chart compares image versus video creative performance on Meta.

The formula categorizes creatives by checking for video ID or image hash, then aggregates spend and CTR per type. This reveals which format resonates with your audience.

Cards show spend share percentages. CTR indicates engagement per type. The bar chart displays absolute spend comparison.

Use this to answer: Does your audience prefer images or video? Should you invest more in video production? And is your budget allocation optimal between formats?

creative-performance:monthly_trend.title: Creative Type Spend Trend (12 Months)
creative-performance:monthly_trend.formula: Line chart of monthly Google Ads spend per creative (ad) type over the trailing 12 months. Each line = an ad type; each point = spend summed for that type in that month.
creative-performance:monthly_trend.formula_explanation: Shows how spend allocation across creative types has evolved over the past year. This long-term view reveals seasonal patterns, strategic shifts, and trends in creative format investment.
how_to_read: Each line represents a different creative type over 12 months
Upward trends indicate increasing investment in that format
Crossing lines show changing format priorities over time
Seasonal patterns may appear around key shopping periods
Sudden shifts might indicate strategic pivots or market changes
decisions_answered: How has our creative mix evolved over the past year?
Are there seasonal patterns in creative type performance?
Which formats are gaining or losing share over time?
Do our spend trends align with strategic priorities?
creative-performance:monthly_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative type spending trends over 12 months.

The formula aggregates spend by creative type and month, using date truncation to group by calendar month. This reveals long-term patterns.

Each line represents a creative type. Upward slopes mean increasing investment. Crossing lines show shifting priorities. Watch for seasonal patterns around holidays.

Use this to understand: How has your creative mix evolved? Are there seasonal patterns? Which formats are gaining share? And do trends match your strategy?

creative-performance:top_ads_table.title: Top Performing Ads by Spend
creative-performance:top_ads_table.formula: Table of individual ads ranked by spend, in tabbed Google and Meta views. Per ad: spend, impressions, clicks, conversions, days active (distinct active days), CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, and ROAS = conversion value ÷ spend.
creative-performance:top_ads_table.formula_explanation: Ranks individual ads by spend to reveal your highest-investment creatives and their performance. Combines Google Ads and Meta Ads in tabbed views with comprehensive metrics for quick comparison and optimization decisions.
how_to_read: Tabs switch between Google Ads and Meta Ads performance tables
Rows are sorted by spend to show highest-investment ads first
ROAS color coding: green (3x+), yellow (2-3x), red (below 2x)
Use search and column sorting to find specific ads or metrics
Click rows to drill into individual ad-level analytics
decisions_answered: Which individual ads receive the most budget?
Are our highest-spend ads also our highest-ROAS ads?
Which ads should be scaled up or paused based on performance?
How do performance metrics vary between Google and Meta ads?
creative-performance:top_ads_table.narration_text:
This table ranks your top ads by spend across Google and Meta platforms.

Key formulas: CTR is clicks over impressions times 100. ROAS is conversion value divided by spend. Days Active counts unique active dates.

Switch tabs to compare platforms. Rows are sorted by spend. ROAS is color-coded: green for 3x plus, yellow for 2 to 3x, red for below 2x.

This answers: Which ads get most budget? Are high-spend ads also high-ROAS? And which should you scale or pause?

formula_components.name: Days active
formula_components.formula: Count of distinct days the ad ran

cro:browser_conversion.title: Browser Conversion
cro:browser_conversion.formula: One row per browser with its users, conversions, conversion rate (conversions ÷ users × 100) and revenue, ranked by users
cro:browser_conversion.formula_explanation: This table compares conversion rates across different web browsers. Significant variations may indicate technical compatibility issues or bugs specific to certain browsers that harm conversions.
how_to_read: Browsers are listed with their user counts for context
Compare conversion rates across browsers for parity
Large variations suggest browser-specific technical issues
Focus on high-volume browsers with below-average conversion
Safari on iOS may show different behavior than Safari on Mac
decisions_answered: Do all browsers convert equally well?
Are there browser-specific technical issues hurting conversion?
Which browsers should QA prioritize for testing?
Is Chrome conversion representative of overall performance?
cro:browser_conversion.narration_text:
This table compares conversion rates across web browsers like Chrome, Safari, Firefox, and Edge.

Browser conversion rate equals conversions divided by users for that browser. Ideally, all browsers should convert similarly.

Large variations indicate technical problems. If Safari converts much worse than Chrome, there might be checkout bugs on Safari. High-volume browsers with low conversion deserve immediate QA attention.

This answers: Do browsers convert equally? Are there browser-specific bugs? Which browsers need QA focus? And is Chrome performance representative?

cro:cart_abandonment_trend.title: Cart Abandonment Trend
cro:cart_abandonment_trend.formula: Daily cart abandonment rate = (add-to-cart users − purchase users) ÷ add-to-cart users × 100, over the selected range (line)
cro:cart_abandonment_trend.formula_explanation: This daily trend shows the percentage of users who added items to their cart but did not complete a purchase. Lower abandonment rates indicate better performance. The 90-day view helps identify patterns and the impact of optimization efforts.
how_to_read: The X-axis shows daily data over 90 days
The Y-axis shows the cart abandonment rate as a percentage
Lower values are better - they indicate fewer lost carts
Weekend vs. weekday patterns may reveal behavioral differences
Spikes may correlate with site issues, pricing changes, or competitive events
decisions_answered: Is cart abandonment improving or worsening over time?
Are there day-of-week patterns in abandonment behavior?
Did recent optimizations reduce cart abandonment?
What external events correlate with abandonment spikes?
cro:cart_abandonment_trend.narration_text:
This line chart shows daily cart abandonment rates over 90 days. Cart abandonment measures users who add items but don't purchase.

The formula: users who added to cart minus those who purchased, divided by cart adders, times 100. Lower is better.

Look for trends - a declining line means your cart experience is improving. Spikes may indicate technical issues or external factors like competitor promotions. Weekend patterns might reveal different user intentions.

This answers: Is abandonment improving? What day patterns exist? Did recent changes help? And what events trigger spikes?

cro:channel_conversion.title: Conversion by Channel
cro:channel_conversion.formula: Conversion rate (conversions ÷ users × 100) by marketing channel, sorted to highlight relative performance (horizontal bars)
cro:channel_conversion.formula_explanation: This chart compares conversion rates across traffic acquisition channels. Some channels drive high traffic volume with lower conversion, while others bring fewer but more purchase-ready visitors. This informs budget allocation and channel optimization priorities.
how_to_read: Channels are sorted to highlight relative performance
Longer bars indicate higher conversion rates for that channel
Compare paid channels (Paid Search, Paid Social) to organic ones
High-volume channels with low conversion may need targeting refinement
High-converting channels with low volume may warrant increased investment
decisions_answered: Which channel has the highest conversion rate?
Are paid channels converting better than organic?
Which channels should receive increased budget?
Which channels need conversion optimization?
cro:channel_conversion.narration_text:
This bar chart compares conversion rates across your traffic channels - from Direct to Paid Search to Social.

Each bar shows that channel's conversion rate: conversions divided by users, times 100. Longer bars mean better conversion.

Compare paid versus organic channels. High conversion with low volume suggests opportunity for increased spend. Low conversion despite high volume means targeting or landing page issues need attention.

This answers: Which channel converts best? How do paid and organic compare? Where should budget increase? And which channels need optimization?

cro:checkout_dropoff.title: Checkout Drop-off Analysis
cro:checkout_dropoff.formula: User counts across the four checkout stages (Begin Checkout → Add Shipping → Add Payment → Purchase) with the step-to-step drop-off rate between each pair
cro:checkout_dropoff.formula_explanation: This focused funnel tracks user progression through the four checkout stages: Begin Checkout, Add Shipping Info, Add Payment Info, and Purchase. It reveals which specific checkout step causes the most friction and abandonment.
how_to_read: Begin Checkout is the starting point - users who initiated the checkout process
Add Shipping and Add Payment show users who completed those form steps
Purchase shows users who successfully completed the transaction
The largest drop-off between stages indicates the most problematic checkout step
Compare this to the main funnel to isolate checkout-specific issues
decisions_answered: Where exactly in checkout are users abandoning?
Is shipping information or payment information causing more friction?
What is our checkout completion rate?
Should we invest in shipping info simplification or payment optimization?
cro:checkout_dropoff.narration_text:
This focused funnel zooms into the checkout process, breaking it into four stages: Begin Checkout, Add Shipping, Add Payment, and Purchase.

Drop-off at each stage equals users lost divided by the starting users for that step. Checkout completion is purchases divided by those who began checkout.

Look for the biggest gap between bars - that's where users struggle most. Shipping drop-off suggests address form issues. Payment drop-off might indicate trust concerns or payment method problems.

This answers: Where exactly do checkout abandonments happen? Is shipping or payment the bigger issue? And where should checkout optimization focus?

cro:device_conversion.title: Conversion by Device
cro:device_conversion.formula: By device type, conversion rate (conversions ÷ users × 100) alongside revenue per user (revenue ÷ users) — dual bars
cro:device_conversion.formula_explanation: This comparison shows how mobile, desktop, and tablet users convert and spend differently. Desktop typically shows higher conversion rates while mobile drives more traffic volume. The dual metrics help balance conversion rate with revenue potential.
how_to_read: Blue bars show conversion rate by device type
Green bars show revenue per user by device type
Compare mobile vs. desktop gaps to assess mobile UX quality
A large mobile conversion gap suggests mobile optimization opportunity
Higher desktop revenue per user is typical but the gap size matters
decisions_answered: How much worse is mobile conversion than desktop?
Is our mobile experience adequately optimized?
Which device type generates the most revenue per user?
Should we prioritize mobile UX improvements?
cro:device_conversion.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart breaks down performance by device: desktop, mobile, and tablet. Two metrics are shown: conversion rate and revenue per user.

Conversion rate is conversions divided by users. Revenue per user is total revenue divided by users.

Desktop typically converts higher, but mobile often drives more traffic. If the mobile conversion gap is large, there's significant optimization opportunity. Revenue per user shows which device attracts higher-value customers.

This answers: How big is the mobile gap? Is mobile UX good enough? Which device drives most value? And should mobile optimization be prioritized?

cro:funnel.title: Conversion Funnel
cro:funnel.formula: User counts across the e-commerce funnel (Sessions → Product Views → Add to Cart → Begin Checkout → Purchase) with the step-to-step conversion rate, plus overall purchase ÷ session rate
cro:funnel.formula_explanation: The funnel visualizes the customer journey from initial session through to completed purchase. Each stage shows the absolute user count and the percentage who progressed from the previous stage. The overall conversion rate represents the end-to-end efficiency of turning visitors into buyers.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a stage in the e-commerce funnel: Sessions, View Item, Add to Cart, Begin Checkout, Purchase
Bar width indicates the relative volume compared to the initial session count
Percentages between stages show the step-to-step conversion rate (drop-off)
Identify the largest drop-offs to prioritize optimization efforts
Green highlighting on the final Purchase stage indicates successful conversions
decisions_answered: What is our overall site conversion rate?
Where in the funnel are we losing the most potential customers?
Which funnel stage requires the most urgent optimization?
How does our funnel performance compare to e-commerce benchmarks?
cro:funnel.narration_text:
This funnel chart shows the complete customer journey from initial site visit to purchase completion.

The formula calculates each stage's conversion rate by dividing users at that stage by the previous stage, times 100. Overall conversion is purchases divided by sessions.

To read this: each bar is a funnel stage. Larger bars mean more users at that point. The percentages between bars reveal where customers drop off. The biggest drops indicate your highest-priority optimization targets.

This answers: What's our conversion rate? Where do we lose customers? Which stage needs attention? And how do we compare to industry standards?

cro:landing_page_performance.title: Landing Page Performance
cro:landing_page_performance.formula: One row per Google Ads landing page URL with its clicks, impressions, click-through rate (clicks ÷ impressions × 100), cost and page-speed score, ranked by clicks (top 10)
cro:landing_page_performance.formula_explanation: This table shows Google Ads landing page metrics including click-through rate, cost efficiency, and page speed scores. It helps optimize paid traffic landing experiences for better conversion and lower acquisition costs.
how_to_read: Landing Page shows the destination URL for ad clicks
Clicks shows how many times users clicked ads to that page
Impressions shows how many times ads for that page were shown
CTR measures ad effectiveness in driving clicks
Cost shows total spend driving traffic to each landing page
decisions_answered: Which landing pages receive the most ad clicks?
Which landing pages have the best click-through rates?
Are we overspending on underperforming landing pages?
Which landing pages need speed or UX optimization?
cro:landing_page_performance.narration_text:
This table shows Google Ads landing page metrics - the pages where paid clicks land.

CTR equals clicks divided by impressions, times 100. Cost per click equals total cost divided by clicks.

High CTR means your ads resonate with the audience. High cost with low conversions suggests the landing page needs work. Speed scores below 50 indicate page load issues that hurt conversions.

This answers: Which landing pages get most clicks? Which have best CTR? Are we overspending on poor performers? And which pages need speed optimization?

cro:page_exit_analysis.title: Page Analysis
cro:page_exit_analysis.formula: One row per page type with its page views, unique page views, orders, conversion rate, average time on page and revenue, ranked by page views
cro:page_exit_analysis.formula_explanation: This table analyzes individual page performance, showing which pages receive traffic and generate revenue. Pages with high traffic but low revenue may have UX issues or be missing conversion opportunities.
how_to_read: Page paths show the URL structure of each page
Page Views indicates traffic volume to each page
Users shows unique visitors to the page
Revenue shows direct attribution to that page
High traffic pages with zero revenue may be informational or problematic
decisions_answered: Which pages receive the most traffic?
Which pages generate the most revenue?
Are there high-traffic pages failing to convert?
Which pages should be prioritized for optimization?
cro:page_exit_analysis.narration_text:
This table analyzes your site pages by traffic and revenue contribution.

Revenue per view equals page revenue divided by page views. This measures how well each page contributes to conversions.

High-traffic pages with low revenue need attention - they might be missing CTAs or have poor user experience. Product pages should show revenue; if they don't, there may be add-to-cart friction.

This answers: Which pages get most traffic? Which generate revenue? Are popular pages failing to convert? And where should page optimization focus?

cro:regional_conversion.title: Regional Conversion
cro:regional_conversion.formula: One row per region with its users, conversions, conversion rate (conversions ÷ users × 100), revenue and average order value (revenue ÷ conversions), ranked by users
cro:regional_conversion.formula_explanation: This geographic breakdown shows conversion rates and order values by region. It helps identify strong markets for expansion, weak markets needing investigation, and regional patterns in customer behavior.
how_to_read: Regions are ranked by user volume
Compare conversion rates across regions for consistency
AOV differences may reflect demographic or product preferences
Low conversion in high-population regions represents opportunity
Consider shipping costs and delivery times as conversion factors
decisions_answered: Which regions have the highest conversion rates?
Are there underperforming regions we should investigate?
Which regions generate the highest order values?
Should we adjust regional marketing or shipping strategies?
cro:regional_conversion.narration_text:
This table shows conversion performance broken down by geographic region.

Regional conversion rate is conversions divided by users in that region. Average order value is revenue divided by conversions.

Compare regions for conversion consistency. Large variations may indicate shipping cost concerns, delivery time issues, or regional marketing gaps. High-population regions with low conversion represent major opportunity.

This answers: Which regions convert best? Are any regions underperforming? Where are order values highest? And should we adjust regional strategies?

cro:source_medium.title: Source/Medium Conversion
cro:source_medium.formula: One row per source/medium pair with its sessions, conversions, conversion rate, revenue and revenue ÷ sessions, ranked by sessions (top 20)
cro:source_medium.formula_explanation: This table provides granular conversion data at the source/medium level, going deeper than channel groupings. It reveals which specific traffic sources (google, facebook, direct) combined with their medium (organic, cpc, referral) perform best for conversions and revenue.
how_to_read: Source/Medium combines the traffic origin with how users arrived
Sessions show traffic volume from each source/medium pair
Conversions and Revenue show the business outcome
Conv Rate helps compare efficiency regardless of volume
Sort by different columns to find top performers or problem areas
decisions_answered: Which source/medium combinations convert best?
Are specific paid campaigns underperforming?
Which referral sources drive valuable traffic?
Where should we focus acquisition budget?
cro:source_medium.narration_text:
This table breaks down conversion performance by source and medium - for example, google organic versus google paid, or facebook referral versus facebook paid.

Conversion rate equals conversions divided by sessions. Revenue per session shows the dollar value each visit generates.

Use this to compare similar sources with different mediums. A source with high traffic but low conversion needs landing page work. High conversion with low volume might deserve more budget.

This answers: Which source/medium pairs convert best? Are campaigns underperforming? Which referrals are valuable? And where should acquisition spend focus?

cro:top_products.title: Top Converting Products
cro:top_products.formula: Products ranked by revenue, each with units sold, orders containing the product and average selling price (top 10)
cro:top_products.formula_explanation: This table shows products ranked by revenue, with their view-to-purchase conversion data. It helps identify products that convert well and drive revenue, versus products that get views but fail to convert.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by revenue contribution
Views shows how many times the product was viewed
Purchases shows actual units sold
Purchase Rate reveals conversion efficiency
High views with low purchase rate indicates pricing or description issues
decisions_answered: Which products are our top revenue drivers?
Which products have the best view-to-purchase conversion?
Are there popular products that fail to convert?
Which products should receive more marketing attention?
cro:top_products.narration_text:
