This table ranks your products by revenue while showing their conversion metrics.

Purchase rate equals items purchased divided by items viewed, times 100. This tells you how efficiently each product converts viewers to buyers.

High revenue products validate your strategy. High views but low purchase rate suggests pricing or description problems. High conversion rate with low views means marketing opportunity.

This answers: What are our top revenue products? Which convert best? Are popular products failing to convert? And which deserve more marketing?

cro:weekly_trend.title: Weekly Conversion Rate Trend
cro:weekly_trend.formula: Weekly conversion rate (purchases ÷ sessions × 100, GA4) over the selected range, with weekly sessions, purchases and Magento revenue in the tooltip (line)
cro:weekly_trend.formula_explanation: This trend line shows how the overall conversion rate changes week over week across a 26-week period. Upward trends indicate improving site performance, while downward trends or volatility may signal issues requiring investigation.
how_to_read: The X-axis shows weeks, spanning 26 weeks (roughly 6 months)
The Y-axis shows the conversion rate as a percentage
An upward trend indicates improving conversion performance
Sudden drops may correlate with site issues, seasonality, or campaign changes
Consistent performance within a tight range suggests stable operations
decisions_answered: Is our conversion rate trending up, down, or stable?
When did significant conversion rate changes occur?
Are there seasonal patterns in our conversion rate?
How quickly do we recover from conversion rate drops?
cro:weekly_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your weekly conversion rate over 26 weeks, revealing long-term performance trends.

The formula is simple: weekly conversions divided by weekly users, times 100. Each data point represents one week's aggregate performance.

An upward slope means your site is converting better over time. Sudden dips warrant investigation - check for site issues, failed experiments, or external factors. Seasonal patterns can inform campaign timing.

This answers: Is conversion trending positively? When did major changes occur? What seasonal patterns exist? And how quickly do we bounce back from problems?

customer-acquisition:cac_trend.title: CAC by Platform
customer-acquisition:cac_trend.formula: Customer acquisition cost per platform per period = platform ad spend ÷ total new customers in the period (spend from the marketing-daily mart by channel, new customers from the customer-acquisition mart). One line per platform.
customer-acquisition:cac_trend.formula_explanation: Customer Acquisition Cost is calculated by dividing total ad spend on each platform by the number of new users attributed to CPC (cost-per-click) traffic from that platform in GA4.
how_to_read: Each line tracks a platform's CAC over 90 days
Blue line is Google Ads, purple/navy is Meta Ads
Lower position means cheaper customer acquisition
Rising trend indicates declining acquisition efficiency
Compare lines to see relative platform efficiency
decisions_answered: What is our cost to acquire a customer by platform?
Is CAC trending up or down over time?
Which platform acquires customers more cost-effectively?
When did significant CAC changes occur?
customer-acquisition:cac_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks Customer Acquisition Cost by platform over 90 days.

CAC is calculated by dividing platform ad spend by the number of new users from CPC traffic. Lower is better - it means cheaper customer acquisition.

The blue line tracks Google Ads, the navy/purple line tracks Meta Ads. Compare their positions: the lower line is more efficient.

Watch for trends. Rising CAC means declining efficiency. Spikes warrant investigation. This helps answer: What does a customer cost per platform? Is CAC improving? And which platform is more cost-effective?

formula_components.name: Platform CAC
formula_components.formula: Platform spend ÷ new customers in period

customer-acquisition:channel_breakdown.title: Channel Breakdown
customer-acquisition:channel_breakdown.formula: New customers acquired per acquisition channel (top 10), with first-order revenue and revenue per new customer per channel. Bars ranked by new customers.
customer-acquisition:channel_breakdown.formula_explanation: Groups all traffic acquisition sessions by their default channel grouping from GA4. This shows which channels drive the most traffic to your site.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate channels driving more session volume
Bar colors differentiate channels for easy identification
Organic Search (green) and Direct (indigo) are typically largest
Paid channels (Paid Search, Paid Social) show advertising impact
Click any bar to filter the page by that channel
decisions_answered: Which channels drive the most traffic to our site?
How does paid compare to organic traffic volume?
Is email marketing driving significant session volume?
Which channels should receive more marketing investment?
customer-acquisition:channel_breakdown.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows sessions by marketing channel over the last 30 days.

Each bar represents a channel's session volume, calculated by summing all sessions grouped by GA4's default channel grouping.

Longer bars mean more traffic. Organic Search and Direct are typically your largest sources. Paid Search and Paid Social show the impact of your advertising spend.

Click any bar to filter the entire page by that channel. This helps answer: Which channels drive the most traffic? How does paid compare to organic? And where should you invest more?

customer-acquisition:customer_quality.title: Customer Quality by Segment
customer-acquisition:customer_quality.formula: One row per order-frequency segment (First-time, Repeat, Growing, Loyal, VIP) with the customer count in that segment plus average order value, average lifetime value and average churn risk. Segments ranked by customer count.
customer-acquisition:customer_quality.formula_explanation: Visualizes customer distribution across order frequency segments from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Segments range from first-time buyers to VIP customers.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count per segment
First-time Buyer (light blue) is typically largest
VIP customers (dark blue) represent highest-value segment
Bar length indicates segment size in absolute numbers
Color progression shows increasing loyalty/value
decisions_answered: How many first-time vs repeat customers do we have?
Is our customer base weighted toward new or loyal buyers?
What is the size of our VIP customer segment?
Are we successfully converting first-time buyers to repeat?
customer-acquisition:customer_quality.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows your customer base segmented by purchase frequency.

The segments are: First-time Buyer with 1 order, Repeat with 2-3 orders, Loyal with 4-6 orders, and VIP with 7 or more. Data comes from Klaviyo's predictive analytics.

Bar length shows absolute customer count per segment. The color progression from light to dark blue reflects increasing customer value.

This answers important questions: How many first-time versus repeat customers do you have? What's the VIP segment size? And are you converting first-timers into loyal buyers?

customer-acquisition:new_customer_trend.title: New Customer Trend
customer-acquisition:new_customer_trend.formula: New customers acquired per period (sum of new customers from the acquisition mart), trended as an area; also shows first-order revenue and first-purchase average order value per period.
customer-acquisition:new_customer_trend.formula_explanation: Aggregates all new users by date from GA4 User Acquisition First User Source/Medium Report. This represents unique first-time visitors attributed to their initial acquisition source.
how_to_read: Each point on the chart represents daily new user acquisition count
The filled area shows the volume trend - larger area means more acquisitions
Rising trend indicates growing customer acquisition momentum
Dips may indicate weekends, seasonality, or campaign performance changes
Hover over any point to see exact new user count for that day
decisions_answered: How many new customers are we acquiring daily?
Is our acquisition volume trending up or down?
Are there any concerning dips that need investigation?
Which days or periods show peak acquisition?
customer-acquisition:new_customer_trend.narration_text:
This area chart shows your daily new customer acquisition over the last 30 days.

The formula is straightforward: we sum all NEW_USERS from GA4's User Acquisition report for each day.

To read this chart: the filled area represents acquisition volume. A rising trend means you're acquiring more customers. Dips might indicate weekends, seasonal effects, or changes in campaign performance.

Hover over any point to see the exact count. This chart answers key questions: How many new customers are we acquiring daily? Is the trend going the right direction? And when are our peak acquisition days?

customer-acquisition:platform_breakdown.title: New Users by Platform
customer-acquisition:platform_breakdown.formula: New customers per acquisition source/platform with each platform's share of total new users; also first-order revenue and revenue per new customer per platform. Pie sliced by new users.
customer-acquisition:platform_breakdown.formula_explanation: Calculates each platform's share of first-touch new user acquisition. The platform attribution is based on the user's first interaction source from GA4.
how_to_read: Each slice represents a platform's share of new user acquisition
Center value shows total new users across all platforms
Google Ads (blue) and Manual/Organic (green) are typically largest
Hover over any slice for exact count and percentage
Click a slice to navigate to that platform's detailed dashboard
decisions_answered: Which platform drives the most new customers?
What percentage of acquisitions come from paid vs organic?
Is our platform investment aligned with acquisition results?
Should we shift budget between Google Ads and Meta Ads?
customer-acquisition:platform_breakdown.narration_text:
This donut chart shows new user acquisition by platform over the last 30 days.

Each slice's size represents that platform's share of total new users. The center displays the total across all platforms.

Google Ads typically appears in blue, organic sources in green. Hover over any slice for exact numbers. Click a slice to drill into that platform's detailed performance page.

This answers critical questions: Which platform acquires the most customers? What's the paid versus organic split? And should you reallocate budget between platforms?

formula_components.name: Platform share
formula_components.formula: Platform new customers ÷ total new customers × 100

customer-acquisition:source_medium.title: Source/Medium Performance
customer-acquisition:source_medium.formula: One row per GA4 traffic channel grouping (top 20) showing new users, sessions, pageviews and average engagement rate. (Backend reads the GA4 traffic-channel mart; despite the title it is keyed on channel grouping.)
customer-acquisition:source_medium.formula_explanation: Shows acquisition performance at the granular source/medium level, including engagement rate which measures the quality of acquired traffic.
how_to_read: Source column shows the traffic origin (google, facebook, direct)
Medium column shows traffic type (cpc, organic, referral)
Color-coded badges help identify paid (amber) vs organic (green)
High engagement rate (blue) indicates quality traffic
Sort by any column to find best or worst performers
decisions_answered: Which source/medium combinations perform best?
Are paid sources delivering engaged users?
Which referral sources drive quality traffic?
Where should we focus acquisition optimization?
customer-acquisition:source_medium.narration_text:
This table breaks down acquisition by source and medium - the most granular view of where customers come from.

Source is the origin: google, facebook, direct. Medium is the type: CPC for paid, organic for free search, referral for links from other sites.

The engagement rate column shows traffic quality - higher means users are more engaged. AOV is color-coded: green for strong, yellow for acceptable, red for weak.

Use this to identify: Which combinations work best? Is paid traffic engaged? And where should you optimize your acquisition efforts?

customer-acquisition:top_campaigns.title: Top Campaigns by New Users
customer-acquisition:top_campaigns.formula: Campaigns ranked by clicks (top 10), combining Google Ads and Meta Ads campaign marts; each row shows clicks (labelled new users), conversion value (revenue), value-per-click and impressions.
customer-acquisition:top_campaigns.formula_explanation: Lists campaigns ranked by new user volume, with revenue and calculated Average Order Value per campaign to show both quantity and quality of acquisition.
how_to_read: Table is sorted by New Users column by default (highest first)
Green AOV values indicate strong first-purchase value (>$40)
Yellow AOV values are acceptable ($35-$40)
Red AOV values need attention (<$35)
Click any row to view detailed campaign performance
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most new customer volume?
Which campaigns acquire high-value first-time buyers?
Are high-volume campaigns also generating good AOV?
Which campaigns need optimization or should be scaled?
customer-acquisition:top_campaigns.narration_text:
This table ranks campaigns by new user acquisition volume over the last 30 days.

For each campaign, you see new users, revenue, and AOV - which is revenue divided by new users. AOV is color-coded: green means strong value over 40 dollars, yellow is acceptable between 35 and 40, red needs attention below 35.

Sort by any column to re-rank. Click a row for campaign details.

Use this to answer: Which campaigns drive volume? Which attract high-value customers? And where should you scale up or optimize?

customer-feedback:nps_by_segment.title: NPS by Customer Segment
customer-feedback:nps_by_segment.formula: Horizontal bar chart of review/feedback volume grouped by customer lifetime-value segment. (Magento reviews carry no NPS field, so the labelled NPS value is returned as zero and only the response count per CLV segment is real.)
customer-feedback:nps_by_segment.formula_explanation: Breaks down NPS by customer value segment to understand if your most valuable customers are also your most satisfied. High-value customer satisfaction is critical for revenue retention.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a customer value segment
Bar length shows the NPS score for that segment
Green bars indicate positive NPS, red bars indicate negative
Compare segments to identify satisfaction disparities
The number of responses per segment is shown alongside
decisions_answered: Are our high-value customers more or less satisfied than others?
Which customer segment needs the most attention?
Is there a correlation between customer value and satisfaction?
Should we offer different service levels by segment?
customer-feedback:nps_by_segment.narration_text:
This bar chart breaks down NPS by customer value segment, helping you understand if satisfaction varies by how much customers spend.

High Value customers - those spending over $500 lifetime - are your VIPs. Their NPS matters most for revenue retention.

Medium Value customers are your growth opportunity. Good satisfaction here can help them become high value.

Low Value customers might be new or infrequent buyers. Their feedback reveals acquisition experience.

The ideal pattern is consistent high NPS across all segments. If high-value customers have lower NPS, that's a red flag - they may churn, taking significant revenue with them. Prioritize understanding and addressing their concerns.

customer-feedback:nps_distribution.title: NPS Distribution
customer-feedback:nps_distribution.formula: Donut chart splitting reviews into Promoters (4-5 stars), Passives (3 stars) and Detractors (1-2 stars) by their share of total reviews. Star ratings from Magento reviews are used as an NPS proxy; there is no true NPS survey data.
customer-feedback:nps_distribution.formula_explanation: Net Promoter Score (NPS) segments survey respondents into three categories based on their likelihood to recommend. Promoters (9-10) are loyal enthusiasts, Passives (7-8) are satisfied but unenthusiastic, and Detractors (0-6) are unhappy customers who can damage your brand through negative word-of-mouth.
how_to_read: The green slice shows Promoters (scores 9-10) - your most loyal customers
The yellow/amber slice shows Passives (scores 7-8) - satisfied but not enthusiastic
The red slice shows Detractors (scores 0-6) - unhappy customers at risk of churning
The center number shows total survey responses in the period
Percentages in the legend show the proportion of each segment
decisions_answered: What is the overall distribution of customer sentiment?
Are we building a base of loyal brand advocates (promoters)?
How many customers are at risk of negative word-of-mouth (detractors)?
Should we focus on converting passives to promoters or reducing detractors?
customer-feedback:nps_distribution.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your NPS survey respondents break down into three key segments.

The green slice represents your Promoters - customers who scored 9 or 10. These are your advocates who actively recommend your brand.

The amber slice shows Passives - scores of 7 or 8. They're satisfied but not enthusiastic, and could easily switch to competitors.

The red slice represents Detractors - scores from 0 to 6. These unhappy customers can damage your brand through negative reviews and word-of-mouth.

A healthy distribution has a large green slice and small red slice. If detractors are growing, investigate why. If promoters dominate, leverage them for referrals and testimonials.

customer-feedback:nps_trend.title: NPS Score Trend
customer-feedback:nps_trend.formula: Line chart of the review-based NPS proxy over time at the selected granularity, where each period's score = share of 4-5 star reviews − share of 1-2 star reviews (range −100 to +100).
customer-feedback:nps_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks your Net Promoter Score over time by calculating the weekly NPS based on all survey responses in each week. A positive score means more promoters than detractors, with +50 considered excellent and +70 world-class.
how_to_read: The blue line shows your NPS score over time (weekly)
Score above 0 means more promoters than detractors
Score above 50 is excellent, above 70 is world-class
Negative scores indicate serious customer satisfaction issues
Look for trends - improving, declining, or stable over time
The green reference line at 50 shows the target threshold
decisions_answered: Is our customer satisfaction improving or declining over time?
Did recent changes to products/service impact customer sentiment?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer satisfaction?
When did significant shifts in customer sentiment occur?
customer-feedback:nps_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your NPS score over time, typically by week.

The y-axis ranges from negative 100 to positive 100. Above zero means you have more promoters than detractors. The green reference line at 50 marks excellent territory - that's your target.

Watch for patterns. A rising trend means your efforts are working. A declining trend is a warning sign that needs investigation. Sudden drops often correlate with specific events - product issues, service problems, or pricing changes.

Seasonality matters too. Some businesses see natural dips during certain periods. Compare year-over-year to separate seasonal effects from real changes in customer satisfaction.

customer-feedback:product_reviews.title: Top Reviewed Products
customer-feedback:product_reviews.formula: One row per product showing total review count, unique reviewing customers, and average star rating; ranked by review count (top 20).
customer-feedback:product_reviews.formula_explanation: Lists products with the most reviews and their average ratings. High review counts with high ratings indicate successful products, while high review counts with low ratings signal products needing attention.
how_to_read: Products are sorted by total review count (most reviewed first)
The Reviews column shows total review volume
Avg Rating shows the mean star rating (out of 5)
Positive % shows the proportion of 4-5 star reviews
Green ratings are excellent (4+), yellow is average (3-4), red is poor (<3)
decisions_answered: Which products are getting the most customer feedback?
Are our best-selling products also our best-rated?
Which products need quality improvement based on ratings?
Should we feature highly-rated products more prominently?
customer-feedback:product_reviews.narration_text:
This table shows your most-reviewed products and their ratings.

High review count combined with high ratings - that's your star performer. Feature these products prominently and study what makes them successful.

High review count with low ratings is a red flag. These products sell but disappoint. Investigate quality issues, unclear product descriptions, or mismatched expectations.

Low review count with high ratings might be hidden gems. Consider promoting these products to increase visibility.

The Positive Percentage column quickly shows sentiment - above 80% is excellent, below 60% needs attention. Focus improvement efforts on products with high volume but low positive percentages for maximum impact.

customer-feedback:rating_distribution.title: Rating Distribution
customer-feedback:rating_distribution.formula: Bar chart of review counts at each star rating (1-5), with each rating's share of total reviews.
customer-feedback:rating_distribution.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of product review ratings across the 1-5 star scale. A healthy distribution skews toward 4-5 stars, with limited 1-2 star reviews indicating product quality issues.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a star rating (1-5)
Bar height shows the count of reviews with that rating
Green colors indicate positive ratings (4-5 stars)
Red colors indicate negative ratings (1-2 stars)
A J-curve shape (high 5s, low middle, spike at 1) suggests polarized opinions
decisions_answered: What is the overall sentiment from product reviews?
Are most reviews positive (4-5 stars) or negative (1-2 stars)?
Is there a significant number of 3-star reviews indicating mediocrity?
Which products or issues are driving low ratings?
customer-feedback:rating_distribution.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how product reviews distribute across the 1 to 5 star scale.

A healthy distribution has the tallest bar at 5 stars, followed by 4 stars, with small bars at 1-2 stars. This bell curve skewed right indicates satisfied customers.

Watch out for the J-curve pattern - lots of 5-star reviews but also lots of 1-star reviews. This polarization suggests some customers love you while others have significant issues.

Heavy concentration at 3 stars is concerning - it means customers are neither impressed nor complaining. Mediocrity doesn't build loyalty.

If 1-2 star reviews are growing, dig into the comments. Common themes reveal systemic issues that need addressing.

customer-feedback:recent_feedback.title: Recent Feedback
customer-feedback:recent_feedback.formula: A live list of the most recent product reviews (newest first, latest 10), each showing masked customer email, date, review title and comment, and product name.
customer-feedback:recent_feedback.formula_explanation: Displays a live feed of recent customer feedback including NPS responses, product reviews, and support requests. Allows quick monitoring of emerging issues or praise.
how_to_read: Feedback items are sorted by recency (newest first)
Badge color indicates sentiment: green (positive), gray (neutral), red (negative)
Rating number shows the NPS score or star rating
Feedback type shows the source (NPS Survey, Product Review, etc.)
Click items to view full feedback details and customer profile
decisions_answered: What are customers saying about us right now?
Are there emerging issues we should address immediately?
Which customers recently gave exceptional praise?
Are feedback patterns changing in real-time?
customer-feedback:recent_feedback.narration_text:
This live feed shows your most recent customer feedback, giving you a pulse on what customers are saying right now.

Watch for patterns in real-time. Multiple negative feedbacks about the same issue within hours signals something urgent that needs investigation.

The sentiment badges give quick visual cues. A stream of green badges means happy customers. Red badges appearing frequently indicate growing problems.

Use this for daily monitoring. Check it each morning to catch any overnight issues. After launching new features or promotions, watch the feed closely for immediate customer reactions.

This is your early warning system. Acting quickly on negative feedback can turn detractors into promoters.

customer-health:avg_health_score.title: Average Health Score
customer-health:avg_health_score.formula: Average Health Score = average of the per-customer health score (0-100) across scored customers
customer-health:avg_health_score.formula_explanation: Health score is the inverse of churn probability. A 20% health score means 80% churn probability. Lower scores indicate higher churn risk across your customer base.
how_to_read: The percentage shows average health across all scored customers
Green indicates healthy scores (50%+), red indicates concern (<30%)
Compare to industry benchmarks: 50%+ is healthy, 30-50% needs attention
Trend shows whether overall customer health is improving or declining
Low scores indicate urgent need for retention campaigns
decisions_answered: What is the overall health of our customer base?
Should we prioritize retention over acquisition right now?
Is our customer health improving or declining over time?
Do we have a systemic customer satisfaction problem?
customer-health:avg_health_score.narration_text:
This KPI tracks your average customer health score - a critical indicator of retention risk across your entire customer base.

The formula inverts Klaviyo's churn probability. A health score of 20 percent means customers have an 80 percent average churn probability. Lower health scores signal higher risk.

Reading this card: The color coding provides quick assessment. Green above 50 percent is healthy. Yellow between 30 and 50 needs attention. Red below 30 percent requires urgent action.

For seed businesses, health scores may appear lower due to seasonal purchase patterns and long buying cycles. Industry context matters when interpreting this metric.

Key questions answered: How healthy is your customer base? Should retention be prioritized? Is health trending better or worse? This guides your overall retention strategy.

customer-health:clv_at_risk.title: CLV at Risk
customer-health:clv_at_risk.formula: CLV at Risk = sum of net lifetime value of customers at Critical churn-risk level (churn-risk score ≥ 80)
customer-health:clv_at_risk.formula_explanation: Total customer lifetime value from customers at critical churn risk. This represents the revenue exposure if these customers churn without intervention.
how_to_read: The main value shows total CLV from critical risk customers
Compare to total portfolio CLV in the subtitle for context
Higher amounts justify larger retention investment
This is the maximum revenue at stake without intervention
Click to see CLV breakdown by risk tier
decisions_answered: How much revenue is at risk from churning customers?
What budget can we justify for retention campaigns?
Is the CLV at risk growing or shrinking over time?
Should we escalate retention to executive priority?
customer-health:clv_at_risk.narration_text:
This KPI quantifies the financial exposure from critical risk customers - the total lifetime value at stake if they churn.

The formula sums the predicted customer lifetime value for all customers with 80 percent or higher churn probability. This represents your maximum revenue exposure.

Reading this card: The dollar amount shows potential revenue loss. Compare to total portfolio CLV to understand relative exposure. If over 50 percent is at risk, retention requires urgent prioritization.

This metric justifies retention investment. If you have $50 million at risk, spending $500,000 on retention campaigns that save even 10 percent delivers positive ROI.

Key questions: How much revenue is at stake? What retention budget is justified? Should this escalate to executive attention? Use this to frame the business case for retention.

customer-health:clv_by_risk_tier.title: CLV by Risk Tier
customer-health:clv_by_risk_tier.formula: Treemap where each cell is a churn-risk-tier × lifetime-value-segment combination; cell size = total lifetime value in that combination and cell color = risk tier (green to red). Risk tier is a rule-based level (Minimal/Low/Medium/High/Critical) derived from the Magento churn risk score.
customer-health:clv_by_risk_tier.formula_explanation: Shows total CLV distributed across risk tiers and CLV buckets. Larger cells represent more revenue. Color indicates risk level. Helps identify high-value at-risk segments.
how_to_read: Cell size represents total CLV in that segment
Cell color indicates risk level (green = low, red = critical)
Large red cells are your highest priority - high CLV at high risk
The treemap helps visualize where revenue concentration meets risk
Click any cell to filter and view those customers
decisions_answered: Where is high-value CLV concentrated by risk level?
Which high-CLV segments need urgent retention attention?
Is our at-risk CLV concentrated or distributed?
What is the revenue exposure by risk tier?
customer-health:clv_by_risk_tier.narration_text:
This treemap visualizes customer lifetime value distributed across risk tiers and value buckets - essential for prioritizing retention by revenue impact.

The formula creates cells sized by total CLV for each risk tier and CLV bucket combination. Colors range from green for low risk to red for critical risk.

Reading this treemap: Focus on large cells with warm colors. A large red cell means significant CLV at critical risk - your highest retention priority.

Small cells are less urgent regardless of color. Even critical risk customers with low CLV may not justify intensive intervention. Prioritize by cell size and color together.

Key questions: Where is high-value CLV at risk? Which segments need urgent attention? How is exposure distributed? Use this to focus retention resources where revenue impact is greatest.

customer-health:critical_risk_count.title: Critical Risk Customers
customer-health:critical_risk_count.formula: Critical Risk Customers = count of customers at Critical churn-risk level (churn-risk score ≥ 80)
customer-health:critical_risk_count.formula_explanation: Counts customers with 80% or higher predicted churn probability. These customers are most likely to stop purchasing without intervention.
how_to_read: The main value shows customers at critical churn risk (80%+ probability)
The percentage indicates what share of scored customers are critical
Higher counts require more aggressive retention campaigns
Compare to your retention campaign capacity to prioritize
Click to view the critical risk customer list for immediate action
decisions_answered: How many customers need immediate retention intervention?
Is our critical risk count manageable or overwhelming?
Do we have sufficient retention resources for this volume?
Should we trigger emergency win-back campaigns?
customer-health:critical_risk_count.narration_text:
This KPI highlights customers at critical churn risk - those with 80 percent or higher probability of churning without intervention.

The formula counts all customers whose Klaviyo predictive score exceeds the 80 percent threshold. This represents your most urgent retention priority.

Reading this card: The count shows immediate action needed. The percentage reveals what share of your database is at critical risk. High percentages indicate systemic issues.

These customers should receive priority outreach: personalized offers, check-in emails, or special attention from customer service. Every day without action increases churn likelihood.

Key questions: How many need immediate intervention? Is the volume manageable? Should you trigger emergency campaigns? Use this to size your retention response.

customer-health:engagement_recency.title: Engagement Recency
customer-health:engagement_recency.formula: Bar chart of customer count by recency band of last order: Active (0-30 days), At Risk (31-60), Lapsing (61-90), Lapsed (91-180), Churned (180+), with average churn probability and lifetime value per band.
customer-health:engagement_recency.formula_explanation: Segments customers by days since their last tracked activity (purchase, email open, site visit). More recent engagement correlates with lower churn risk.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each engagement recency level
Active and Engaged segments are your healthy, engaged customers
At Risk and Dormant need re-engagement campaigns
Churned customers (180+ days) may be permanently lost
The churn percentage on each bar shows that segment average
decisions_answered: How engaged is our customer base currently?
How many customers are slipping toward churn?
Which engagement segments need immediate campaigns?
Is our engagement distribution improving over time?
customer-health:engagement_recency.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by engagement recency - a leading indicator of churn before it happens.

The formula groups customers by days since last activity: Active within 7 days, Engaged 8 to 30, At Risk 31 to 90, Dormant 91 to 180, and Churned over 180 days.

Reading this chart: Longer bars in Active and Engaged indicate a healthy, engaged database. Large At Risk or Dormant segments signal re-engagement opportunity.

The average churn probability shown for each segment demonstrates the correlation. Active customers typically show lower churn, while Churned customers approach 90 percent plus churn probability.

Key questions: How engaged is your base? How many are slipping away? Which segments need campaigns? Use this to size your re-engagement and win-back initiatives.

customer-health:expected_order_timing.title: Expected Order Timing
customer-health:expected_order_timing.formula: Bar chart bucketing repeat customers by expected next-order timing — Overdue, Due This Week, Due This Month, Due in 1-3 Months, Due in 3+ Months — derived from each customer's days since last order versus their average days between orders. Timing is rule-based on Magento order history, not a Klaviyo prediction.
customer-health:expected_order_timing.formula_explanation: Segments customers by when Klaviyo predicts their next order should occur based on historical purchase patterns. Overdue customers have missed their expected purchase window.
how_to_read: Red (Overdue) shows customers who have missed their predicted order window
Orange and yellow show upcoming purchase opportunities
The predicted revenue shows potential value at stake
Overdue customers should receive immediate outreach
Click any segment to view and action those customers
decisions_answered: How many customers have missed their expected purchase date?
What revenue is at risk from overdue orders?
Which customers should receive proactive outreach?
Is our overdue rate improving or worsening?
customer-health:expected_order_timing.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by expected next order timing - a powerful predictor of retention opportunity.

Klaviyo calculates when each customer should next purchase based on their historical patterns. The formula groups customers by how their expected date compares to today.

Reading this chart: The red Overdue segment represents immediate action needed - these customers have passed their predicted purchase window. The predicted revenue shows what is at stake.

Green and blue segments are healthy - customers expected to purchase in the future. Yellow and orange are approaching their windows and may benefit from proactive outreach.

Key questions: How many are overdue? What revenue is at risk? Who needs outreach now? Use this to trigger timely, personalized purchase reminders.

customer-health:health_trend.title: Health Trend (Last 12 Months)
customer-health:health_trend.formula: Line chart of average health score over time (health score = 100 − average churn probability) at the selected granularity, with active customer count and critical-risk percentage per period as detail.
customer-health:health_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks how average churn probability and critical risk percentage have changed over the past 12 months. Reveals seasonal patterns and retention campaign effectiveness.
how_to_read: The red line shows average churn probability trend over 12 months
The orange line shows critical risk percentage trend
Downward trends indicate improving customer health
Look for seasonal patterns - Q1 often shows spring buying activity
Hover over any point to see exact monthly values
decisions_answered: Is our overall customer health improving or declining?
Are retention campaigns having measurable impact?
What seasonal patterns affect customer health?
Should we expect improvement or decline going forward?
customer-health:health_trend.narration_text:
This multi-line chart tracks customer health metrics over 12 months - essential for understanding whether retention efforts are working.

The formula calculates monthly average churn probability and critical risk percentage for customers active in each month.

Reading this chart: Follow the red churn line. Declining trends indicate improving health, rising trends signal problems. The orange critical risk line shows your most urgent segment.

Look for seasonality. Seed businesses often see better health in Q1 spring planting season when customers are actively purchasing. Off-season months may show elevated churn.

Key questions: Is health improving? Are campaigns working? What seasonal patterns exist? Use this to evaluate retention strategy effectiveness and plan seasonal campaigns.

formula_components.name: Critical Risk %
formula_components.formula: Customers at Critical churn level ÷ all customers in period × 100

customer-health:high_value_at_risk.title: High-Value At-Risk Customers
customer-health:high_value_at_risk.formula: One row per high-value at-risk customer — lifetime value ≥ $500 and churn risk level High or Critical — showing health score, churn probability, total lifetime value, total orders, average order value, days inactive and risk tier; ranked by lifetime value (top 100).
customer-health:high_value_at_risk.formula_explanation: Shows individual high-value customers who are at elevated churn risk. These customers deserve personalized retention attention due to their revenue importance.
how_to_read: Health score badge shows customer risk level (color coded)
Sort by Total CLV to prioritize highest value customers first
Days inactive indicates how long since last engagement
Overdue flag shows customers past their expected purchase date
Use search to find specific customers by name or email
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers need immediate attention?
Who should receive personalized outreach from our team?
What is the profile of our at-risk VIP customers?
Are high-value customers showing warning signs?
customer-health:high_value_at_risk.narration_text:
This table displays your highest-priority retention targets - customers with significant lifetime value who show elevated churn risk.

The filter selects customers with CLV of $500 or more and churn probability of 60 percent or higher. These are your VIP customers at risk.

Reading this table: The health score badge provides quick risk assessment. Sort by CLV to prioritize the highest value customers. Days inactive and overdue flags indicate urgency.

These customers deserve personalized attention - not just automated emails. Consider direct outreach, special offers, or customer success calls for the highest value at-risk customers.

Key questions: Who needs immediate attention? Who deserves personalized outreach? What patterns do at-risk VIPs show? Use this as your retention priority list.

customer-health:order_frequency_health.title: Order Frequency Health
customer-health:order_frequency_health.formula: Bar chart of customer count by historical order-count band (1, 2, 3-5, 6-10, 10+ orders), with average churn probability, average lifetime value and average order value per band.
customer-health:order_frequency_health.formula_explanation: Shows customer count and average CLV by historical order frequency. Customers with more orders typically show higher CLV and lower churn.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each order frequency level
One-order customers are your conversion opportunity
Higher order frequencies correlate with higher CLV shown below
The goal is to move customers rightward to higher frequency
Click any bar to filter and view that customer segment
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are one-time buyers?
How does CLV correlate with order frequency?
Where should second-purchase campaigns focus?
Are we successfully converting to repeat customers?
customer-health:order_frequency_health.narration_text:
This bar chart shows customers distributed by historical order count - the key to understanding repeat purchase patterns.

The formula groups customers into order frequency buckets and calculates average CLV for each. More orders typically means higher lifetime value.

Reading this chart: One-order customers typically form the largest bar - this is your biggest conversion opportunity. The CLV metrics below each segment show the value of moving customers up.

The jump from 1 to 2 orders is the critical hurdle. Customers who make a second purchase are far more likely to continue purchasing. Focus retention campaigns here.

Key questions: What's the one-time buyer percentage? How does CLV correlate with frequency? Where should campaigns focus? Use this to design your repeat purchase journey.

customer-health:regional_health.title: Regional Health (Top 10)
customer-health:regional_health.formula: Intended as a table of average churn probability and lifetime value by US state for the top states by customer count, but the backend returns no rows — geographic data is not available in the customer marts.
customer-health:regional_health.formula_explanation: Shows average churn probability and CLV by US state for top 10 states by customer count. Reveals geographic patterns in customer health.
how_to_read: States are sorted by customer count (highest first)
Red churn percentages indicate states with concerning health
Compare avg CLV across states to identify quality variations
Geographic patterns may indicate regional marketing or product fit issues
Click any state to filter and analyze that region
decisions_answered: Which states have the healthiest or least healthy customers?
Are there geographic patterns in churn risk?
Do certain regions show higher customer value?
Should we adjust regional marketing strategies?
customer-health:regional_health.narration_text:
This table shows customer health metrics by US state - revealing geographic patterns that may inform regional strategy.

The formula calculates average churn probability and CLV for customers in each state, ranked by customer count.

Reading this table: States with high churn percentages shown in red indicate regional health concerns. Compare CLV across states - some regions may attract higher-value customers.

Look for outliers. If one state shows dramatically different metrics, investigate whether it reflects data quality issues, regional competition, or product-market fit variations.

Key questions: Which states are healthiest? Are there geographic patterns? Do some regions show higher value? Use this to consider regional marketing adjustments or targeted campaigns.

customer-health:risk_distribution.title: Risk Distribution
customer-health:risk_distribution.formula: Donut chart of customer count per churn-risk tier — Minimal (<20), Low (20-39), Medium (40-59), High (60-79), Critical (80+) — where the tier is a rule-based level from the Magento churn risk score (0-100), shown with average and total lifetime value per tier.
customer-health:risk_distribution.formula_explanation: Segments customers into four risk tiers based on Klaviyo predicted churn probability. Distribution shows the health composition of your customer base.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of customers in each risk tier
Green (Low Risk) and yellow (Medium) are your healthy customer core
Orange (High Risk) needs proactive nurturing campaigns
Red (Critical Risk) requires immediate retention intervention
Click any segment to filter and view those customers
decisions_answered: How is churn risk distributed across our customer base?
What percentage of customers are at high or critical risk?
Do we have more healthy or at-risk customers?
Where should we focus our retention resources?
customer-health:risk_distribution.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how churn risk is distributed across your customer base - essential for prioritizing retention efforts.

The formula creates four risk tiers: Low risk below 30 percent churn, Medium between 30 and 60, High between 60 and 80, and Critical at 80 percent or above.

Reading this chart: A healthy distribution has more green and yellow than orange and red. If critical risk dominates, your entire customer base needs attention.

The center shows total scored customers for reference. Hover over segments to see exact counts and CLV at stake for each tier.

Key questions: How is risk distributed? What percentage is high or critical risk? Where should retention focus? Click any segment to drill into that customer list for targeted action.

customer-journey:attribution_model_compa.title: Attribution Model Comparison
customer-journey:attribution_model_compa.formula: Per channel (top 10), first-touch revenue versus last-touch revenue: first-touch credits the channel that first acquired the customer, last-touch credits the channel of the converting order. Grouped bars compare the two models.
customer-journey:attribution_model_compa.formula_explanation: Compares two attribution models: First-Touch (credits the channel that first acquired the user) vs Last-Touch (credits the channel of the converting session). Reveals how channels play different roles in awareness vs conversion.
how_to_read: Blue bars show First-Touch attribution (user acquisition)
Green bars show Last-Touch attribution (conversion session)
Channels with higher First-Touch drive awareness and discovery
Channels with higher Last-Touch close sales and drive conversions
Similar bars indicate the channel works across the full funnel
Compare bars to understand each channel role in the journey
decisions_answered: Which channels drive initial awareness vs final conversion?
Are we undervaluing awareness channels with last-touch attribution?
How do channels work together in the customer journey?
Should we adjust budget based on full-funnel contribution?
customer-journey:attribution_model_compa.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares First-Touch and Last-Touch attribution models - revealing how channels play different roles across the customer journey.

First-Touch credits revenue to the channel that first brought the user to your site. Last-Touch credits the channel of the actual converting session. These often differ significantly.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show First-Touch, green shows Last-Touch. Channels with taller blue bars drive awareness - they bring new users who may convert later through other channels. Taller green bars indicate closing power.

Paid Social often shows higher First-Touch than Last-Touch - it generates awareness but users convert through Direct or Search later. Email often shows stronger Last-Touch as it closes existing customers.

Key questions: Which channels drive awareness vs conversion? Are we undervaluing upper-funnel channels? How should we allocate budget considering full-funnel impact?

formula_components.name: First-touch revenue
formula_components.formula: Order revenue credited to the first-touch channel

formula_components.name: Last-touch revenue
formula_components.formula: Order revenue credited to the last-touch channel

customer-journey:channel_attribution.title: Channel Attribution
customer-journey:channel_attribution.formula: Per GA4 channel grouping (top 12): order revenue attributed to the channel, sessions, conversions, revenue per session (revenue ÷ sessions) and conversion rate (conversions ÷ sessions × 100). Horizontal bars by sessions.
customer-journey:channel_attribution.formula_explanation: Aggregates session-level revenue attribution from GA4 by default channel grouping. Shows which marketing channels drive the most revenue, enabling budget allocation decisions across paid, organic, and owned channels.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show revenue contribution from each channel
Longer bars indicate higher revenue attribution to that channel
Direct and Organic Search typically lead for established brands
Compare paid channels (Paid Search, Paid Social) to organic performance
Hover to see sessions, conversions, and revenue per session metrics
Click any channel to filter the entire dashboard by that channel
decisions_answered: Which marketing channels drive the most revenue?
How does paid channel performance compare to organic channels?
Is our Email marketing driving proportional revenue?
Which channels offer the best ROI for additional investment?
customer-journey:channel_attribution.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows revenue attribution by marketing channel - essential for understanding where your customers come from and what drives purchases.

The formula sums total revenue for each GA4 default channel grouping over the selected date range, then calculates supporting metrics like sessions and revenue per session.

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more revenue from that channel. Direct and Organic Search often lead for established brands with strong brand awareness. Paid channels show your advertising impact.

Compare your paid channels to organic. High organic revenue means strong brand equity. High paid means your advertising is working but you may be more acquisition-dependent.

Key questions: Which channels drive most revenue? How do paid vs organic compare? Where should you invest more? Click any channel to focus the entire page on that traffic source.

customer-journey:channel_revenue_trend.title: Channel Revenue Trend
customer-journey:channel_revenue_trend.formula: Sessions per GA4 channel grouping per period, drawn as a stacked area; the total height is combined daily sessions across channels. (Despite the title the plotted quantity is sessions, not revenue.)
customer-journey:channel_revenue_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks daily revenue contribution from each major channel as a stacked area chart. Shows how channel mix evolves over time and reveals seasonal patterns, campaign impacts, and channel interdependencies.
how_to_read: Each colored area represents one marketing channel stacked on others
The total height shows combined daily revenue across all channels
Watch for channel proportions changing over time
Spikes may indicate successful campaigns or seasonal events
Consistent patterns suggest stable channel performance
Click to isolate individual channel series for closer analysis
decisions_answered: How is our channel mix evolving over time?
Are any channels growing or declining in contribution?
What impact do campaigns have on channel performance?
Are there seasonal patterns in channel effectiveness?
customer-journey:channel_revenue_trend.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows how channel revenue contributions evolve over time - revealing trends, patterns, and campaign impacts.

The formula sums daily revenue for each channel, then stacks them to show both individual channel performance and total revenue trajectory.

Reading this chart: Each colored layer represents a channel. The total height shows combined revenue. Watch how layer proportions shift over time - growing layers indicate channels gaining importance.

Look for spikes that coincide with campaigns. A spike in Paid Search might show a Google Ads campaign working. A jump in Email indicates a successful send. Dips might signal issues needing investigation.

Key questions: How is channel mix evolving? Are channels growing or shrinking? What patterns exist? Click any channel in the legend to isolate its trend line.

customer-journey:cohort_clv.title: Cohort CLV Trend
customer-journey:cohort_clv.formula: Average net lifetime value per acquisition cohort (customers grouped by first-order period), with the number of new profiles per cohort. Line trends average CLV across cohorts.
customer-journey:cohort_clv.formula_explanation: Tracks average predicted customer lifetime value by the month customers were acquired. Shows whether customer quality is improving or declining over time, helping evaluate acquisition strategy effectiveness.
how_to_read: Each point represents customers acquired in that month
The Y-axis shows average predicted lifetime value
Rising line indicates improving customer quality over time
Declining line suggests acquisition strategy may be degrading
Seasonal dips may reflect promotional acquisition periods
Hover to see the cohort size and exact CLV value
decisions_answered: Is our customer quality improving or declining?
Which acquisition months brought the highest-value customers?
Are recent cohorts more or less valuable than historical ones?
Should we adjust acquisition strategy to improve CLV?
customer-journey:cohort_clv.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort - showing whether your customer quality is improving over time.

The formula groups customers by the month they were created in Klaviyo, then calculates the average predicted CLV for each cohort from Klaviyo predictive analytics.

Reading this chart: Each point represents a monthly cohort. The Y-axis shows their average lifetime value. An upward trend means you're acquiring higher-value customers over time - your strategy is improving.

Watch for seasonal patterns. Cohorts acquired during heavy promotional periods may show lower CLV due to discount-seeking customers. Organic growth periods often produce higher-value cohorts.

Key questions: Is customer quality improving? Which months produced the best customers? Are recent cohorts valuable? Click any point to analyze that specific acquisition cohort.

formula_components.name: Average CLV
formula_components.formula: Net lifetime value averaged across customers in the cohort

customer-journey:conversion_funnel.title: Conversion Funnel
customer-journey:conversion_funnel.formula: Funnel of customer-journey touchpoint stages, each stage being the count of touchpoints of that type (ordered largest to smallest). Stage % = stage count ÷ top-stage count × 100; drop-off = (previous stage − current) ÷ previous × 100; overall conversion = purchase stage ÷ top stage × 100.
customer-journey:conversion_funnel.formula_explanation: Aggregates GA4 e-commerce funnel events to show customer progression from initial session through final purchase. Each stage shows the absolute count and the conversion rate to the next stage, helping identify where customers drop off.
how_to_read: The funnel flows left to right showing customer journey stages
Bar width represents volume at each stage relative to sessions
Percentages show conversion rate from the previous stage
Drop-off indicators highlight where customers leave the funnel
Red drop-off arrows show percentage lost between stages
Click any stage to drill into detailed analysis for that step
decisions_answered: What is the overall conversion rate from session to purchase?
At which funnel stage do we lose the most customers?
Is cart abandonment or checkout abandonment our bigger problem?
How does our funnel conversion compare to industry benchmarks?
customer-journey:conversion_funnel.narration_text:
This funnel visualization shows your customer journey from initial session through final purchase - the core of conversion optimization.

The formula counts each funnel event type from GA4: session starts, product views, cart additions, checkout initiations, and purchases. Conversion rates are calculated between adjacent stages.

Reading this chart: Follow the horizontal funnel from left to right. Each bar represents volume at that stage. The percentages between stages show your stage-to-stage conversion rates.

Watch for the biggest drops. Is it view-to-cart where product appeal matters? Cart-to-checkout where friction enters? Or checkout-to-purchase where trust and payment issues arise?

Key questions answered: What's our overall conversion rate? Where do we lose the most customers? What's the priority for optimization? Click any stage for deeper analysis into that conversion point.

customer-journey:device_journey.title: Device Journey Distribution
customer-journey:device_journey.formula: Per device category (mobile, desktop, tablet): users, revenue, average engagement rate and revenue per user (revenue ÷ users). Donut sized by users.
customer-journey:device_journey.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution and performance across mobile, desktop, and tablet devices. Helps identify device-specific experience issues and opportunities for optimization by revealing where users engage but may not convert.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportional user distribution by device
Blue represents Mobile - typically the largest segment
Green represents Desktop - often highest conversion
Orange represents Tablet - usually smallest segment
Center shows total users across all devices
Hover to see revenue and engagement rate by device
decisions_answered: What is our device mix for website traffic?
Which devices show highest engagement and conversion?
Is our mobile experience converting as well as desktop?
Do we need device-specific UX optimizations?
customer-journey:device_journey.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how users distribute across mobile, desktop, and tablet devices - critical for understanding where to invest in UX optimization.

The formula sums users by device category from GA4, along with revenue and engagement metrics for each device type.

Reading this chart: Mobile typically dominates user volume - most browsing happens on phones. Desktop often shows higher conversion rates due to easier checkout. Tablet is usually the smallest segment.

The key insight is comparing device proportions for users vs revenue. If mobile is 70 percent of users but only 40 percent of revenue, there is a mobile conversion opportunity worth investigating.

Key questions: What's our device mix? Do conversion rates match user volume? Is mobile experience holding back revenue? Click any segment to analyze that device in detail.

customer-journey:klaviyo_touchpoints.title: Klaviyo Touchpoint Metrics
customer-journey:klaviyo_touchpoints.formula: Per journey touchpoint type (and channel): count of touchpoint events and count of unique customers. Horizontal bars by event volume.
customer-journey:klaviyo_touchpoints.formula_explanation: Tracks email and SMS marketing touchpoints from Klaviyo throughout the customer journey. Shows engagement levels at each stage and how marketing communications influence customer behavior.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show event volume for each Klaviyo metric
Email metrics include Received, Opened, and Clicked
SMS metrics include Received and Clicked
Commerce events show Viewed Product through Placed Order
Compare Opened to Received for open rate insights
Click any metric to analyze that touchpoint in detail
decisions_answered: How many emails and SMS messages are we sending?
What are our email open and click rates?
How do Klaviyo touchpoints influence purchases?
Is our email/SMS engagement improving over time?
customer-journey:klaviyo_touchpoints.narration_text:
This chart shows Klaviyo touchpoint metrics - measuring how your email and SMS marketing engages customers throughout their journey.

The formula counts events by metric type from Klaviyo: emails sent, opened, clicked, SMS messages, and commerce events like product views and purchases.

Reading this chart: The bars show event volume for each metric type. Compare Received Email to Opened Email to gauge open rates. Compare Opened to Clicked for click-through performance.

Commerce events like Placed Order show how many Klaviyo-tracked customers are completing purchases. These help attribute revenue back to your email and SMS marketing efforts.

Key questions: What's our email volume? How are open and click rates performing? What commerce activity ties to Klaviyo? Click any metric to dive into that specific touchpoint.

customer-journey:landing_pages.title: Top Landing Pages
customer-journey:landing_pages.formula: Top landing pages (top 15 by page views): per page, page views, new users, revenue (currently 0 in mart) and average engagement seconds.
customer-journey:landing_pages.formula_explanation: Shows which landing pages receive the most traffic and drive the most value. Helps identify high-performing pages to replicate and underperforming pages that need optimization or elimination.
how_to_read: Each row shows a landing page with key performance metrics
Sort by Page Views to see highest traffic pages
Sort by Revenue to see pages driving the most value
High New Users indicates effective acquisition pages
Avg Engagement shows page quality and relevance
Use search to find specific pages or page categories
decisions_answered: Which landing pages receive the most traffic?
What pages drive the most revenue attribution?
Which pages have the best engagement metrics?
Where should we focus landing page optimization efforts?
customer-journey:landing_pages.narration_text:
This table shows your top landing pages by traffic and performance - essential for understanding where customers enter your site and how well those entry points convert.

The formula aggregates page views, new users, revenue, and engagement time for each page path from GA4. This shows which pages serve as effective entry points.

Reading this table: Sort by Page Views for traffic volume, by Revenue for value generation. Home page often leads traffic, but product and category pages may drive more revenue per view.

Look for mismatches. A page with high traffic but low revenue may need conversion optimization. A page with great revenue per view but low traffic deserves more promotion.

Key questions: Which pages get most traffic? What drives most revenue? Where should optimization focus? Use the search to analyze specific page categories like product pages or blog posts.

customer-journey:order_frequency.title: Customer Order Frequency
customer-journey:order_frequency.formula: Customer count per order-frequency bucket (1, 2, 3-5, 6-10, 10+ orders), with average lifetime value per bucket. Bars by customer count.
customer-journey:order_frequency.formula_explanation: Segments customers by their historical purchase frequency from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Shows the distribution of one-time vs repeat buyers and highlights the value of moving customers up the frequency ladder.
how_to_read: Each bar shows customer count at that purchase frequency
One-time buyers (1 order) typically form the largest group
Smaller bars to the right represent loyal repeat customers
The goal is to grow higher-frequency buckets over time
Hover to see average CLV for each frequency segment
Click any bucket to filter and analyze that customer segment
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are one-time buyers?
How many customers have become loyal repeat purchasers?
What is the LTV difference between frequency segments?
Where should retention campaigns focus?
customer-journey:order_frequency.narration_text:
This bar chart shows customer distribution by purchase frequency - revealing your repeat purchase opportunity and customer loyalty levels.

The formula buckets customers by their historical order count from Klaviyo, grouping them into meaningful frequency segments from one-time buyers to loyal ten-plus order customers.

Reading this chart: The leftmost bar shows one-time buyers - typically the largest segment. Each bar to the right shows higher-frequency customers. The goal is to shift customers rightward over time.

Notice how customer count shrinks but value grows at higher frequencies. A customer with ten-plus orders might have ten times the LTV of a one-time buyer. Moving just a fraction of one-time buyers to two orders significantly impacts revenue.

Key questions: What's our one-time buyer percentage? How many loyal customers exist? What's the LTV at each frequency? Click any bucket to see those customers and plan targeted campaigns.

customer-ltv:aov_tiers.title: CLV by AOV Tier
customer-ltv:aov_tiers.formula: Customers bucketed by average order value (<$25, $25-49, $50-99, $100-199, $200+); each tier shows average realized lifetime value, total tier LTV and average orders. Bars = average CLV per AOV tier.
customer-ltv:aov_tiers.formula_explanation: Analyzes how average order value correlates with customer lifetime value. Customers are segmented by their average order value, then CLV is calculated for each tier. This reveals whether high-AOV customers also have high lifetime value.
how_to_read: X-axis shows average order value tiers from lowest to highest
Bar height represents average CLV for customers in that AOV tier
Generally, higher AOV correlates with higher CLV
However, frequency matters too - high AOV with few orders may show lower CLV than moderate AOV with many orders
Click any bar to see the detailed customer breakdown
decisions_answered: Do high-AOV customers have higher lifetime value?
Is it better to attract high-AOV customers or increase order frequency?
Which AOV tier represents the sweet spot of value and volume?
Should we focus on upselling to increase AOV?
customer-ltv:aov_tiers.narration_text:
This chart shows how average order value relates to customer lifetime value.

Intuitively, you might expect high-AOV customers to have the highest CLV. But the relationship is nuanced. Yes, $200+ AOV customers have higher individual order values, but they may place fewer orders over time.

Sometimes the $25-49 tier shows strong CLV despite lower individual orders, because these customers purchase more frequently. Multiple smaller purchases can add up to more value than few large purchases.

The strategic question this answers: Should you focus on increasing order size or order frequency? Often, the answer is both - but if you must prioritize, frequency typically has higher impact because it indicates engagement and habit formation.

Consider your product mix: Are high-AOV customers buying premium items that need less frequent replacement?

customer-ltv:channel_revenue.title: Channel Revenue Attribution
customer-ltv:channel_revenue.formula: Per GA4 channel grouping: new users, sessions, all users and average engagement rate. (Backed by the GA4 traffic-channel mart; the bar value is new users per channel despite the revenue-attribution title.)
customer-ltv:channel_revenue.formula_explanation: Shows revenue attribution by acquisition channel from Google Analytics. Each rectangle is sized by total revenue and can be colored by efficiency (revenue per user). This reveals which channels drive the most value and which are most efficient.
how_to_read: Rectangle size represents total revenue from that channel
Larger rectangles indicate higher revenue contribution
Labels show channel name and total revenue
Direct and Organic Search often dominate in mature businesses
Paid channels show efficiency of marketing spend
Click any cell to drill into that channel detail
decisions_answered: Which marketing channels drive the most revenue?
What is the revenue contribution mix across channels?
Are paid channels delivering sufficient ROI?
Should we shift budget between channels?
customer-ltv:channel_revenue.narration_text:
This treemap shows revenue attribution across your marketing channels.

Rectangle size represents total revenue - larger boxes mean more revenue from that channel. Direct traffic and Organic Search typically dominate for established brands, representing customers who know your brand and come directly or search for you.

Paid channels like Paid Search and Paid Social show the impact of your marketing investments. Compare their box sizes to understand relative contribution.

The key insight is efficiency: A smaller box with high revenue per user may be more valuable to scale than a large box with lower efficiency. Email often shows high efficiency despite moderate total volume.

This answers: Where is our marketing budget best spent? Which channels should we scale or reduce?

customer-ltv:churn_risk.title: Churn Risk Matrix
customer-ltv:churn_risk.formula: Per churn-risk tier (Minimal, Low, Medium, High, Critical) from the precomputed churn risk level: customer count, average lifetime value, total LTV at risk and average orders. Bars show CLV at risk per tier.
customer-ltv:churn_risk.formula_explanation: Churn probability comes from Klaviyo predictive analytics model. Customers are grouped into risk tiers, and the total CLV at risk is calculated for each tier. Critical risk customers have >75% probability of churning and require immediate intervention.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show CLV at risk for each churn tier
Green (Low Risk) customers are stable - focus on maintaining their engagement
Yellow (Medium Risk) shows customers worth monitoring
Orange (High Risk) indicates customers needing proactive retention
Red (Critical Risk) customers require immediate attention - highest value at stake
Dollar amounts on bars show total CLV that could be lost if customers churn
decisions_answered: How much customer value is at risk of churning?
Which risk tier requires the most urgent attention?
What is the potential revenue impact if we dont act on churn?
Should we invest more in retention or acquisition?
customer-ltv:churn_risk.narration_text:
This chart shows customer lifetime value at risk across different churn probability tiers.

The red Critical Risk bar is the most concerning - it represents customers with greater than 75% probability of churning. In many businesses, this can represent $60M or more in potential lost value from nearly 500,000 customers.

However, not all at-risk customers are worth the same retention investment. Notice the Low Risk segment has fewer customers but higher average CLV - these stable, valuable customers are worth protecting proactively.

The key insight is prioritization: Focus retention campaigns on high-CLV customers within Critical and High Risk tiers first. A customer with $500 CLV at critical risk is more valuable to save than ten $20 CLV customers.

This answers the critical question: How much is at stake if we do nothing about churn?

customer-ltv:clv_tier_distribution.title: CLV Tier Distribution
customer-ltv:clv_tier_distribution.formula: Customer count per CLV tier (the precomputed LTV segment: No Revenue, Low, Medium, High, Very High, VIP) with each tier's share of customers, average CLV, total tier CLV and average orders. Donut sized by customer count.
customer-ltv:clv_tier_distribution.formula_explanation: Customers are segmented into value tiers based on their total Customer Lifetime Value from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Each tier represents a range of total CLV combining both historic (realized) purchases and predicted future purchases.
how_to_read: Each donut segment represents a CLV tier, sized by customer count
Center value shows total customers with CLV data (704K)
Legend table shows customers count, percentage, and total CLV per tier
Green segment ($1000+) represents your most valuable customers - typically 1-2% but drive significant value
Gray segment ($1-49) is the largest by count but lowest individual value
Click any segment to filter the page to that CLV tier
decisions_answered: How is customer value distributed across our customer base?
What percentage of customers are high-value ($1000+) vs low-value?
Is our CLV distribution healthy or are we over-reliant on a few high-value customers?
Which tier should we target for retention campaigns?
customer-ltv:clv_tier_distribution.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base.

Each segment represents a CLV tier, from $1-49 at the bottom to $1000+ for your highest value customers. The center displays your total customers with CLV data - currently 704,000.

The legend table on the right shows detailed breakdowns. Notice that while the $1000+ tier is only about 1.3% of customers, these 9,300 Champions generate nearly $16M in lifetime value.

The $1-49 tier has the most customers at 38%, but each customer contributes much less value individually. This concentration pattern is typical - a small percentage of high-value customers often drive a disproportionate share of total value.

Use this to decide: Should you focus on retaining top-tier customers or moving more customers up tiers?

customer-ltv:cohort_clv.title: CLV by Acquisition Cohort
customer-ltv:cohort_clv.formula: Average realized lifetime value per acquisition cohort (customers grouped by first-order period), with cohort customer count, total cohort CLV, average orders and average order value. Line trends average CLV across cohorts.
customer-ltv:cohort_clv.formula_explanation: Tracks how customer lifetime value varies by when customers were acquired. Each cohort represents customers who first appeared in the system during that quarter. Comparing average CLV across cohorts reveals acquisition quality trends and how CLV matures over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows acquisition quarter from Q3 2021 forward
Blue line tracks average total CLV per customer for each cohort
Green dashed line shows average historic (realized) CLV only
Older cohorts typically show higher CLV as they have had more time to mature
Declining CLV in recent cohorts may indicate lower acquisition quality
Large gaps between total and historic CLV indicate strong predicted future value
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?
How does CLV mature as cohorts age?
Which acquisition period brought our most valuable customers?
Should we investigate recent cohorts showing lower CLV?
customer-ltv:cohort_clv.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort over time.

Each point represents customers who joined in that quarter. The blue line shows total CLV including predicted future value, while the green dashed line shows only historic realized value.

Older cohorts naturally show higher CLV because customers have had more time to make purchases. Q3 2021 cohorts might show the highest average CLV after having 4+ years to mature.

Watch for declining CLV trends in recent cohorts - this could indicate lower quality acquisitions from changed marketing strategies or channels. However, some decline is expected since newer customers haven't had time to mature.

Use this to answer: Is our customer acquisition quality improving or declining? When did we acquire our best customers, and can we replicate those conditions?

formula_components.name: Average cohort CLV
formula_components.formula: Realized lifetime value averaged across customers in the cohort

customer-ltv:maturation_curve.title: CLV Maturation Curve
customer-ltv:maturation_curve.formula: Average realized lifetime value plotted against months since acquisition (months between each customer's first order and today), showing how CLV accumulates as customers age. Area chart with customer count per month band.
customer-ltv:maturation_curve.formula_explanation: Shows how customer lifetime value accumulates over time since acquisition. By plotting average realized CLV against months since acquisition, we can see the typical value trajectory and identify when customers reach maturity.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months since customer acquisition (0-36 months)
Y-axis shows average realized (historic) CLV
The curve shows how CLV grows as customers age with your brand
Steep initial slope indicates strong early purchases
Flattening curve indicates customers reaching maturity
Compare actual curve to expected payback period
decisions_answered: How does CLV mature over time?
When do customers reach CLV maturity/payback?
What is the typical value trajectory for a customer?
How quickly do we recoup customer acquisition costs?
customer-ltv:maturation_curve.narration_text:
This area chart shows the CLV maturation curve - how customer value accumulates over time since acquisition.

The X-axis represents months since a customer joined, from 0 to 36 months. The Y-axis shows average realized CLV at each point. This creates a typical value trajectory.

Early months show steep growth as customers make initial and early repeat purchases. Over time, the curve typically flattens as purchase frequency stabilizes.

Key insights to look for: When does the curve flatten? This indicates CLV maturity. Where does the curve cross your customer acquisition cost? This is your payback period. How much value is generated in the first 12 months vs months 12-24?

This chart answers critical questions for financial planning: How long until we recover acquisition costs? What is the typical lifetime of customer value growth?

customer-ltv:order_frequency.title: CLV by Order Frequency
customer-ltv:order_frequency.formula: Customer count per order-frequency bucket (1, 2, 3-5, 6-10, 10+ orders); each bucket shows average CLV, total CLV, average order value and average churn probability. Bars = average CLV per frequency bucket.
customer-ltv:order_frequency.formula_explanation: Shows how customer lifetime value correlates with purchase frequency. Customers are bucketed by their total number of historic orders, then average CLV is calculated for each bucket. This reveals the multiplicative effect of repeat purchases on customer value.
how_to_read: X-axis shows order frequency buckets from single-purchase to 10+ orders
Bar height represents average CLV for customers in that frequency bucket
Strong upward progression indicates healthy repeat purchase value growth
The jump from 1 to 2 orders often shows the largest relative CLV increase
Compare bar heights to understand the value multiplier of repeat purchases
Click any bar to filter to customers in that frequency bucket
decisions_answered: How much does CLV increase with each additional order?
What is the value difference between one-time and repeat customers?
Should we prioritize converting first-time buyers to second purchases?
At what order frequency do customers become truly valuable?
customer-ltv:order_frequency.narration_text:
This bar chart reveals one of the most important insights in customer value: the relationship between order frequency and lifetime value.

Each bar shows average CLV for customers with that many orders. The pattern is consistent across businesses - CLV grows significantly with each additional purchase.
