The most critical finding is often the jump from 1 to 2 orders. A customer who makes a second purchase might have nearly double the CLV of a single-purchase customer. This is because second purchases indicate engagement and predict future purchases.

Customers with 10+ orders typically show CLV 8-10 times higher than single-purchase customers. These are your Champions.

This answers a key strategic question: Where should you invest marketing dollars? Converting one-time buyers to repeat customers often has the highest ROI in e-commerce.

customer-ltv:rfm_segments.title: RFM Segment Analysis
customer-ltv:rfm_segments.formula: Per RFM segment (Champion, Loyal, At Risk, etc., read from the RFM mart): customer count, share of customers, average CLV, total segment CLV, share of total CLV and average churn probability. Horizontal bars ranked by total segment CLV.
customer-ltv:rfm_segments.formula_explanation: RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) segmentation classifies customers based on their purchase behavior and churn risk. This implementation uses Klaviyo predictive analytics data combining order frequency and churn probability to create actionable segments.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show total CLV value for each customer segment
Bar length represents the total value contributed by customers in that segment
Segments are sorted by total CLV value descending - highest value segments at top
Champions (green) are your best customers: high frequency, low churn risk
At Risk (orange/red) segments need immediate retention attention
Click any bar to filter the page to that segment
decisions_answered: Which customer segments contribute the most to our lifetime value?
How many customers are Champions vs At Risk?
Where should we focus retention marketing spend?
Are we successfully converting Potential Loyalists into Loyal Customers?
customer-ltv:rfm_segments.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows the total lifetime value contributed by each customer segment using RFM analysis.

Potential Loyalists typically dominate this chart, representing customers who have made 3 or more orders. They may contribute over 60% of total CLV. Champions - your best customers with 10+ orders and low churn risk - often show high individual value despite smaller numbers.

At Risk segments are critical to monitor. These customers have high churn probability, meaning their future value is uncertain. Even though At Risk New customers have lower individual CLV, the volume of customers at risk can represent significant value.

Focus retention efforts on converting Potential Loyalists to Loyal Customers, and preventing At Risk customers from churning. Your Champions need VIP treatment to maintain their loyalty.

customer-ltv:state_clv.title: Top States by Average CLV
customer-ltv:state_clv.formula: Average realized lifetime value per US shipping state (top 20 by average CLV), with customer count, total CLV and average orders per state. Bars ranked by average CLV.
customer-ltv:state_clv.formula_explanation: Ranks US states by average customer lifetime value. Only states with sufficient customer volume (1,000+) are included to ensure statistical significance. This reveals geographic patterns in customer value that can inform regional marketing strategies.
how_to_read: States are ranked by average CLV, highest at top
Bar length shows average CLV per customer in that state
Dollar values on bars show exact average CLV
States like Alaska and Hawaii often rank high due to customer demographics
Compare your key markets to the average to identify underperformers
Click any state to filter the page to customers in that region
decisions_answered: Which geographic regions have the highest-value customers?
Should we adjust marketing spend by region based on CLV?
Are there underperforming regions we should investigate?
Where are our best acquisition opportunities?
customer-ltv:state_clv.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart ranks US states by average customer lifetime value.

States like Alaska and Hawaii often appear at the top. This can be due to several factors: shipping constraints may mean only committed customers order, or demographics may skew toward higher income. Montana, Utah, and Washington frequently rank high for gardening businesses due to strong gardening culture.

More important than the absolute rankings is the comparison to your national average. States significantly below average may represent optimization opportunities - are you acquiring the right customers there, or is there a product-market fit issue?

High-CLV states might deserve more marketing investment. But also consider volume - a state with lower average CLV but 10x the customers might still generate more total value.

This answers: Where should we focus geographic marketing efforts?

customer-overview:active_customers.title: Active Customers
customer-overview:active_customers.formula: Active Customers = count of distinct customers (with ≥1 order) whose most recent order is within 90 days of the period end. Active Rate = active customers ÷ total customers × 100
customer-overview:active_customers.formula_explanation: Counts customers who have engaged with your brand in the last 90 days through any activity: purchases, email opens, site visits, or other tracked events. The active rate shows database health.
how_to_read: The main value shows customers with recent engagement (90 days)
The active rate badge shows what percentage of your database is engaged
Healthy databases maintain 70%+ active rates
Declining active rates may indicate list hygiene issues
Click to segment customers by engagement recency
decisions_answered: What percentage of our customer base is actively engaged?
Is our database healthy or full of dormant contacts?
Do we need win-back campaigns for inactive customers?
Should we clean our list of permanently inactive contacts?
customer-overview:active_customers.narration_text:
This KPI tracks active customers - those who have engaged with your brand within 90 days. It measures the health of your customer database.

The formula counts customers whose last event date falls within the past 90 days. Events include purchases, email opens, website visits, and other tracked interactions.

Reading this card: The main number shows your active customer count. The badge displays the active rate - what percentage of your total database remains engaged.

Database health matters for deliverability and efficiency. A 70 percent or higher active rate is healthy. Below 50 percent suggests you may need aggressive win-back campaigns or list cleanup.

Key questions: How engaged is your database? Should you run win-back campaigns? Is list hygiene needed? Click to segment customers by their engagement recency.

formula_components.name: Active Customers
formula_components.formula: count of distinct customers whose last order date is within the trailing 90 days of the period end

customer-overview:cac_by_channel.title: CAC by Channel
customer-overview:cac_by_channel.formula: Per marketing channel: CAC = total channel spend over the period ÷ total new customers acquired in the period (new customers come from the customer-acquisition mart, channel spend from the marketing-daily mart). Bars rank channels by spend.
customer-overview:cac_by_channel.formula_explanation: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) measures how much you spend to acquire each new customer through a specific marketing channel. Lower CAC indicates more efficient acquisition, but must be balanced against customer quality and LTV.
how_to_read: Bars show CAC for each marketing channel - longer bars mean higher cost
Compare CAC across channels to identify most efficient acquisition sources
Email typically shows lowest CAC due to minimal direct costs
Paid channels (Google, Meta) show higher but scalable CAC
Click any bar to filter the page by that channel
decisions_answered: Which channel acquires customers most cost-effectively?
Is our paid advertising CAC sustainable relative to LTV?
Should we reallocate budget toward lower-CAC channels?
Which channels offer the best scaling opportunities?
customer-overview:cac_by_channel.narration_text:
This chart shows Customer Acquisition Cost broken down by marketing channel - essential for understanding where your marketing dollars work hardest.

The formula divides each channel's total advertising spend by the number of new customers attributed to that channel. Lower CAC means more efficient acquisition.

Reading this chart: Horizontal bars represent CAC per channel. Longer bars indicate higher cost per customer. Email typically shows lowest CAC. Paid channels like Google and Meta show higher but more scalable CAC.

Important context: Low CAC isn't always best. Paid channels may have higher CAC but also higher customer quality and LTV. Always compare CAC to the lifetime value those customers generate.

Key questions: Which channel is most efficient? Is paid CAC sustainable? Should you shift budget? Click any channel to dive deeper into that channel's performance.

formula_components.name: Channel CAC
formula_components.formula: Channel spend ÷ new customers acquired in period

formula_components.name: Channel spend
formula_components.formula: Ad spend summed per channel over the period

customer-overview:customer_growth.title: Customer Growth Trend
customer-overview:customer_growth.formula: New customers acquired per period (count of customers whose first order falls in the period), shown as an area with a running cumulative total line; conversion rate per period = customers with more than one order ÷ new customers × 100.
customer-overview:customer_growth.formula_explanation: Tracks new customer acquisition over 24 months with a running cumulative total. The area shows monthly additions while the cumulative line shows total database growth over time.
how_to_read: The shaded area shows new customers added each month
Peaks indicate successful acquisition periods or campaigns
Look for seasonal patterns - Q1 peaks for gardening businesses
Consistent baseline growth indicates healthy organic acquisition
Click any month to analyze that cohort in detail
decisions_answered: How is our customer base growing over time?
Are there seasonal acquisition patterns we should plan around?
Which months produced the most new customers?
Is year-over-year growth accelerating or slowing?
customer-overview:customer_growth.narration_text:
This area chart shows your customer growth trend over 24 months - revealing both patterns and overall trajectory.

The formula counts new customers by month, then calculates a running cumulative total. Year-over-year comparisons show growth acceleration or deceleration.

Reading this chart: The shaded area represents monthly new customer acquisitions. Peaks indicate strong acquisition periods, often tied to campaigns or seasonality. For gardening, expect Q1 spring peaks.

The overall trend matters most. Consistent upward slope means healthy growth. Flattening suggests market saturation or competitive pressure. Declining trends require immediate attention.

Key questions: How is the customer base growing? What seasonal patterns exist? Is year-over-year growth accelerating? Click any month to analyze that acquisition cohort in detail.

formula_components.name: New customers per period
formula_components.formula: Count of customers whose first order date is in the period

formula_components.name: Cumulative customers
formula_components.formula: Running total of new customers across periods

formula_components.name: Conversion rate
formula_components.formula: Customers with more than one order ÷ new customers × 100

customer-overview:customer_health.title: Customer Health Overview
customer-overview:customer_health.formula: Customers grouped into health bands from their health score (Excellent ≥80, Good ≥60, Fair ≥40, Poor below 40); each band shows customer count plus average lifetime value, average churn risk and total lifetime value at stake.
customer-overview:customer_health.formula_explanation: Composite health score combining lifetime value and churn probability. Excellent/Good customers are your stable core. Fair needs nurturing. Poor requires immediate retention intervention to protect at-risk LTV.
how_to_read: Four gauges show percentage of customers in each health status
Green (Excellent) and blue (Good) are your healthy customer core
Yellow (Fair) customers need nurturing to prevent decline
Red (Poor) represents customers likely to churn - act immediately
The table below shows detailed metrics and LTV at stake
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are in healthy status?
How much LTV is at risk from poor-health customers?
Which health segment needs immediate intervention?
Are customer health trends improving or declining?
customer-overview:customer_health.narration_text:
This panel provides a comprehensive view of customer health - combining LTV and churn probability into actionable segments.

The formula creates four health tiers. Excellent customers have high LTV and low churn probability. Good is solid but with moderate metrics. Fair shows warning signs. Poor indicates imminent churn risk.

Reading this panel: The four gauges show percentage in each health status. Green and blue represent your stable core. Yellow needs nurturing before declining further. Red requires immediate intervention.

The table below quantifies the stakes. Look at the LTV at Stake column for Poor status - this is the revenue you'll lose if these customers churn without intervention.

Key questions: What percentage is healthy? How much LTV is at risk? Which segment needs urgent action? Use this to prioritize your retention resources effectively.

formula_components.name: Health band
formula_components.formula: Excellent if health score ≥ 80, Good ≥ 60, Fair ≥ 40, else Poor

formula_components.name: Total LTV at stake
formula_components.formula: Lifetime value summed across customers in the band

customer-overview:customer_segments.title: Customer Segments by LTV
customer-overview:customer_segments.formula: Customer count per lifetime-value segment (the precomputed LTV segment tier), with each segment's total LTV, average LTV, share of customers and share of total LTV. Donut sized by customer count.
customer-overview:customer_segments.formula_explanation: Segments customers into value tiers based on lifetime value and churn probability. VIP customers require premium treatment, while at-risk segments need retention intervention.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of customers in each value segment
Gold (VIP) segment is small but contributes outsized revenue
Purple (High Value) represents your loyal customer core
Gray (New/Inactive) includes recent signups and dormant contacts
Click any segment to view customer list for targeted actions
decisions_answered: How are customers distributed across value tiers?
What percentage of customers are VIPs worth protecting?
How large is our at-risk or inactive segment?
Where should we focus retention and growth efforts?
customer-overview:customer_segments.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by lifetime value - essential for prioritizing marketing and retention efforts.

The formula creates five tiers: VIP customers have $500-plus LTV with low churn. High Value sits at $200 to $500. Medium and Low Value follow. New or Inactive captures recent signups and dormant contacts.

Reading this chart: The gold VIP slice is typically small but critical - these customers drive disproportionate revenue and deserve premium treatment. Purple High Value customers form your loyal core.

Gray New/Inactive may be your largest segment. This includes both recent signups who haven't proven value yet and dormant customers needing win-back campaigns.

Key questions: How are customers distributed? What percentage are VIPs? How large is the at-risk segment? Click any segment to take targeted action on that customer group.

customer-overview:geographic_distributio.title: Geographic Distribution
customer-overview:geographic_distributio.formula: Customer count per US state (top 15 by count), with average and total lifetime value per state. Bars ranked by customer count.
customer-overview:geographic_distributio.formula_explanation: Shows customer concentration by US state, ranked by customer count. Geographic analysis reveals regional strengths and opportunities for targeted marketing or expansion.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count per state, ranked highest to lowest
Virginia leads due to headquarters location and regional strength
California and Texas show population-driven large customer bases
Look for states with high LTV relative to customer count
Click any state to filter and analyze regional performance
decisions_answered: Where are our customers concentrated geographically?
Which states offer expansion opportunities?
Should we invest in regional marketing campaigns?
Do certain regions show higher customer quality?
customer-overview:geographic_distributio.narration_text:
This chart shows your customer distribution across US states - revealing where your brand has the strongest presence.

The formula counts customers per state and calculates average LTV by region. States are ranked by total customer count to highlight concentration.

Reading this chart: Longer bars indicate more customers in that state. Virginia leads, likely due to headquarters proximity and regional marketing. California and Texas show high counts driven by large populations.

The opportunity: Look beyond raw counts. Some states may have fewer customers but higher average LTV. These represent quality expansion opportunities with proven customer value.

Key questions: Where are customers concentrated? Which states offer growth potential? Should you run regional campaigns? Click any state to explore that region's detailed performance.

customer-overview:ltv_cac_trend.title: LTV:CAC Ratio Trend
customer-overview:ltv_cac_trend.formula: Average customer lifetime value per period (averaged across customers whose first order falls in that period), trended over time. CAC is not available in this mart so the displayed series is the LTV trend only (the ratio is shown as N/A).
customer-overview:ltv_cac_trend.formula_explanation: The LTV:CAC ratio measures customer acquisition profitability. A ratio of 3:1 or higher indicates sustainable growth - you earn $3 in lifetime value for every $1 spent acquiring. Below 2:1 suggests unprofitable acquisition.
how_to_read: The line tracks monthly LTV:CAC ratio over 12 months
The green shaded zone indicates healthy ratios (3:1 or higher)
Yellow zone (2:1 to 3:1) suggests caution
Red zone (below 2:1) indicates unprofitable acquisition
Look for seasonal patterns - Q1 often shows best ratios for gardening
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring customers profitably?
Is our LTV:CAC ratio improving or declining?
Which months show the best acquisition economics?
Should we scale or reduce customer acquisition spending?
customer-overview:ltv_cac_trend.narration_text:
This chart tracks your LTV to CAC ratio over time - the single most important metric for sustainable growth.

The formula divides average customer lifetime value by customer acquisition cost. A 3 to 1 ratio means you earn $3 for every $1 spent acquiring customers.

Reading this chart: Follow the line across months. The green zone above 3 to 1 indicates healthy, profitable acquisition. Yellow between 2 and 3 to 1 suggests caution. Red below 2 to 1 means you're losing money on acquisition.

Watch for seasonality. For gardening businesses, Q1 spring planting season often shows the best ratios as high-intent customers purchase more.

Key questions: Are we profitable on acquisition? Is the ratio improving? When should we scale spending? This guides your overall marketing investment strategy.

customer-overview:ltv_distribution.title: LTV Distribution
customer-overview:ltv_distribution.formula: Customer count per $50 lifetime-value bucket (from $0 up to $1000), forming a histogram; each bucket also shows total LTV and its share of all customers.
customer-overview:ltv_distribution.formula_explanation: Shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base. Most businesses have a long tail of low-LTV customers with a smaller segment of high-value VIPs driving disproportionate revenue.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a $50 LTV bucket showing customer count
The leftmost bars typically contain the most customers (low LTV)
Look for the distribution shape - is it heavily skewed left?
The median and average LTV are shown for reference
Click any bucket to filter and view customers in that LTV range
decisions_answered: How is customer value distributed across our base?
What percentage of customers are high-value VIPs?
Is our LTV distribution healthy or too skewed toward low value?
Should we focus on moving customers up the value ladder?
customer-overview:ltv_distribution.narration_text:
This histogram shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base - a critical view of where your revenue really comes from.

The formula groups customers into $50 LTV buckets and counts how many fall into each. The median and average LTV lines provide reference points.

Reading this chart: Most customers cluster in the lower LTV buckets on the left. This is normal - the 80/20 rule often applies where 20 percent of customers drive 80 percent of revenue.

The key insight is the shape. A heavy left skew means most customers have low value. The goal is to move customers rightward into higher buckets through retention and upselling.

Key questions: How is value distributed? What percentage are VIPs? Should you focus on moving customers up the value ladder? Click any bucket to explore that customer segment.

formula_components.name: LTV bucket
formula_components.formula: Lifetime value rounded down to the nearest $50

customer-overview:new_customers.title: New Customers
customer-overview:new_customers.formula: New Customers = count of distinct customers whose first order falls within the selected period
customer-overview:new_customers.formula_explanation: Counts customers whose profile was created within the last 30 days. This represents fresh acquisition activity and indicates the effectiveness of marketing campaigns at driving new customer growth.
how_to_read: The main value shows customers acquired in the last 30 days
Compare to previous periods to assess acquisition momentum
A declining trend may indicate marketing effectiveness issues
This metric directly reflects your acquisition campaign performance
Click to view acquisition source breakdown and channel analysis
decisions_answered: How many new customers did we acquire this month?
Is our acquisition rate improving or declining?
Are our marketing campaigns effectively driving new customers?
Do we need to adjust acquisition spending or strategy?
customer-overview:new_customers.narration_text:
This KPI tracks your new customer acquisition over the last 30 days - a critical leading indicator of business growth.

The formula counts all customers whose profile was created in the past 30 days. This directly measures the output of your marketing and acquisition efforts.

Reading this card: The main number shows your 30-day acquisition count. The percentage change compares to the previous 30-day period. Green indicates improving acquisition, red suggests declining performance.

New customer acquisition is a leading indicator. If this number drops, revenue impacts will follow. Conversely, strong acquisition today means future repeat purchase revenue.

Key questions answered: How many new customers this month? Is acquisition improving? Are campaigns effective? Use this to calibrate your marketing spend and strategy.

customer-overview:purchase_frequency.title: Purchase Frequency Distribution
customer-overview:purchase_frequency.formula: Customer count per order-frequency bucket (1, 2, 3-5, 6-10, 10+ orders), with average lifetime value, average churn risk and total LTV per bucket.
customer-overview:purchase_frequency.formula_explanation: Shows how many customers fall into each purchase frequency bucket. One-time buyers are conversion opportunities, while high-frequency customers are your most valuable advocates.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each purchase frequency level
One-time buyers typically form the largest segment
Bar color gradient reflects average LTV at each frequency
High frequency (5+ orders) shows significantly lower churn
Click any bucket to analyze customers at that frequency
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are one-time buyers?
How many customers have become repeat purchasers?
Is our repeat purchase rate improving over time?
Where should we focus second-purchase campaigns?
customer-overview:purchase_frequency.narration_text:
This bar chart shows your customers distributed by purchase frequency - the key to understanding your repeat purchase opportunity.

The formula counts customers at each order frequency: 1, 2, 3 to 4, 5 to 9, and 10-plus orders. Bar colors reflect average LTV at each level.

Reading this chart: One-time buyers typically form the largest bar - this is normal but represents your biggest conversion opportunity. Getting that second purchase is the critical hurdle.

Notice the color gradient. Higher frequency customers show significantly better LTV and lower churn. Once customers reach 3-plus orders, retention becomes much easier.

Key questions: What's the one-time buyer percentage? How many repeat purchasers exist? Where should second-purchase campaigns focus? Click any bucket to target that customer group directly.

customer-overview:returning_customers.title: Returning Customers
customer-overview:returning_customers.formula: Returning Customers = count of distinct customers acquired before the period who placed an order within the period. Retention Rate = returning customers ÷ total customers × 100
customer-overview:returning_customers.formula_explanation: Counts customers who have made two or more purchases historically. The retention rate shows what percentage of customers with at least one order have returned to purchase again.
how_to_read: The main value shows customers with 2+ historical purchases
The retention rate badge indicates repeat purchase percentage
High retention (60%+) suggests strong product-market fit
Low retention may indicate product quality or experience issues
Click to analyze retention patterns and customer lifecycle
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers make repeat purchases?
Is our retention rate healthy compared to benchmarks?
Are we successfully converting one-time buyers to repeat customers?
Do we need to invest more in retention marketing?
customer-overview:returning_customers.narration_text:
This KPI measures returning customers - those who have purchased from you more than once. Retention is often more valuable than acquisition.

The formula counts customers with two or more historical orders. The retention rate divides this by total customers who have made at least one purchase.

Reading this card: The main number shows your repeat customer count. The badge displays your retention rate percentage. For e-commerce, 60 percent or higher is considered healthy.

Why this matters: Acquiring a new customer costs 5 to 25 times more than retaining one. A high retention rate means strong customer satisfaction and product-market fit.

Key questions: What's our retention rate? Are we converting one-time buyers? Should we invest more in retention marketing? Click to explore customer lifecycle patterns.

formula_components.name: Returning Customers
formula_components.formula: count of distinct customers whose first order is before the period start and whose last order is within the period

customer-overview:revenue_by_customer_ty.title: New vs Returning Revenue
customer-overview:revenue_by_customer_ty.formula: Net revenue per period split into new-customer revenue and returning-customer revenue, with each type's share of the period total. Stacked bars by period.
customer-overview:revenue_by_customer_ty.formula_explanation: Splits monthly revenue between first-time purchasers and repeat customers. Healthy businesses derive 60%+ of revenue from returning customers, indicating strong retention and customer satisfaction.
how_to_read: Light green bars show revenue from first-time purchasers
Dark green bars show revenue from repeat customers
Healthy businesses have taller dark green (returning) segments
The 40/60 benchmark line shows ideal new/returning split
Click any month to analyze revenue composition in detail
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our returning customer revenue share improving?
Are we too dependent on new customer acquisition?
Do we need to invest more in retention to balance revenue?
customer-overview:revenue_by_customer_ty.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows your monthly revenue split between new and returning customers - a key indicator of business health.

The formula sums order values, separating first-time purchasers from those with previous orders. The returning share percentage shows retention's contribution to revenue.

Reading this chart: Light green shows first-time purchase revenue. Dark green shows repeat customer revenue. Ideally, dark green dominates - 60 percent or more from returning customers indicates a healthy business.

Why this matters: New customer revenue is expensive to generate. Returning customer revenue has near-zero acquisition cost and higher margins. Businesses too dependent on new customers face growth challenges.

Key questions: What's the revenue split? Is returning share growing? Do you need more retention investment? Click any month for deeper revenue analysis.

customer-overview:total_customers.title: Total Customers
customer-overview:total_customers.formula: Total Customers = count of distinct customers with ≥1 order who had any order activity overlapping the selected period
customer-overview:total_customers.formula_explanation: Counts all unique customer profiles in the Klaviyo database, excluding any soft-deleted records. This includes both purchasers and email subscribers who have not yet made a purchase.
how_to_read: The main value shows the total unique customer count across your entire database
The percentage change indicates month-over-month growth or decline
A green upward trend suggests healthy customer acquisition
Click this card to drill down into the full customer list
The sparkline shows the 30-day trend of total customer growth
decisions_answered: What is the total size of our customer database?
Is our customer base growing or shrinking month-over-month?
How does current customer count compare to previous periods?
customer-overview:total_customers.narration_text:
This KPI card displays your total customer count - the foundation of your customer health metrics.

The formula is straightforward: we count all distinct customer IDs in your Klaviyo database, excluding any deleted records. This captures both paying customers and email subscribers.

To read this card, focus on the main value showing your total customer base. The percentage below indicates month-over-month change - green means growth, red means decline.

The sparkline gives you a quick visual of the 30-day trend. Consistent upward movement indicates healthy acquisition, while flat or declining trends may signal issues needing attention.

This metric answers fundamental questions: How large is your customer base? Is it growing? At what rate? Click the card to drill into the complete customer list for deeper analysis.

customer-retention:churn_risk.title: Churn Risk Breakdown
customer-retention:churn_risk.formula: Customer count per churn-risk tier (Minimal, Low, Medium, High, Critical) from the precomputed churn risk level, with average lifetime value, average orders and average order value per tier. Donut sized by customer count.
customer-retention:churn_risk.formula_explanation: Uses Klaviyo predictive analytics to segment customers by their probability of churning. The donut chart shows the distribution across risk tiers, helping prioritize retention efforts on high-risk valuable customers.
how_to_read: Green slice shows low-risk customers (<25% churn probability) - your loyal base
Orange slice shows medium-risk customers (25-50%) - worth monitoring
Red/dark slices show high-risk customers (>50%) - require immediate attention
Center number shows total customers with churn predictions
Click any slice to filter and view customers in that risk segment
decisions_answered: What percentage of our customers are at high churn risk?
How many customers need immediate retention intervention?
Is our churn risk profile healthy or concerning?
Which risk segment should we prioritize for campaigns?
customer-retention:churn_risk.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by predicted churn risk.

Green is your safe zone - low risk customers who are likely to keep buying. Orange is your watch list - they could go either way. Red and dark red are your danger zone - high probability of never purchasing again.

The key insight is not just the proportions, but the absolute numbers. If you have 10,000 customers in high risk segments, that's revenue walking out the door.

Use this to prioritize. High-value customers in the red zone deserve personal outreach. Medium risk customers might respond to a discount. Low risk customers can receive standard nurturing.

customer-retention:cohort_retention.title: Cohort Retention by Month
customer-retention:cohort_retention.formula: Per monthly cohort (customers grouped by first-order month, last 12 cohorts): retention rate = retained customers ÷ cohort size × 100, where retained = those who made a repeat purchase one month after acquisition.
customer-retention:cohort_retention.formula_explanation: Groups customers by the month of their first purchase (cohort) and measures what percentage made a repeat purchase. Higher retention rates indicate more effective customer acquisition and product-market fit.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a monthly cohort of first-time customers
The percentage shows what portion of that cohort made repeat purchases
Higher bars indicate better retention for that acquisition month
Compare recent cohorts to historical ones to spot trend changes
Labels display exact retention percentages for precision
decisions_answered: Which acquisition months produced the most loyal customers?
Is our customer quality improving or declining over time?
Did specific marketing campaigns drive better retention?
What is our baseline retention rate across cohorts?
customer-retention:cohort_retention.narration_text:
This bar chart shows retention rates by customer acquisition cohort.

Each bar represents customers who made their first purchase in a specific month. The height shows what percentage came back for a second purchase.

Strong cohorts have high retention - 40% or more is excellent for most e-commerce. Weak cohorts under 25% signal problems with that month's acquisition quality or product experience.

Compare cohorts over time. If recent cohorts have lower retention, investigate. Was there a marketing change? Product issue? This helps you understand not just if customers are staying, but when they were acquired matters.

customer-retention:first_vs_repeat_reven.title: First vs Repeat Purchase Revenue
customer-retention:first_vs_repeat_reven.formula: Revenue split into first-purchase versus repeat-purchase: for each type, gross revenue, order count, average order value and unique customers (orders grouped by new vs returning customer type).
customer-retention:first_vs_repeat_reven.formula_explanation: Compares revenue generated from first-time purchases versus repeat purchases over the last 30 days. A healthy business typically sees 40-60% of revenue from repeat customers, indicating strong retention.
how_to_read: Blue bar shows revenue from customers making their first purchase
Green bar shows revenue from returning customers
Longer repeat bar indicates strong retention and customer loyalty
Below each bar, see order counts and average order values
Compare AOV between segments - repeat customers often spend more
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our revenue mix healthy (40-60% from repeat)?
Do repeat customers have higher average order values?
Should we invest more in acquisition or retention?
customer-retention:first_vs_repeat_reven.narration_text:
This comparison chart shows where your revenue comes from - first purchases versus repeat purchases.

The blue bar is new customer revenue. The green bar is revenue from customers who've bought before. For a healthy e-commerce business, repeat revenue should be 40-60% of total.

If first purchase dominates, you might have a retention problem - customers aren't coming back. If repeat dominates heavily, you might have an acquisition problem - not enough new customers entering the funnel.

The AOV comparison below matters too. If repeat customers spend more per order, that's proof that retention investments pay off. Your best customers get better over time.

customer-retention:retention_trend.title: Retention Trend
customer-retention:retention_trend.formula: Count of purchasing (returning) customers per period over the last 12 periods, with month-over-month growth rate = (current − previous) ÷ previous × 100.
customer-retention:retention_trend.formula_explanation: This chart tracks the number of unique customers who made purchases each month over the last 12 months. It provides a direct view of customer engagement and helps identify trends in repeat purchase behavior.
how_to_read: The blue line shows the count of unique customers making purchases each month
Upward trends indicate improving customer retention and engagement
Downward trends may signal issues with customer satisfaction or marketing effectiveness
Hover over any point to see the exact customer count for that month
Click a point to filter the page to that specific month
decisions_answered: Is our customer base growing or shrinking over time?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer purchasing behavior?
When did significant changes in customer engagement occur?
Should we launch re-engagement campaigns based on declining trends?
customer-retention:retention_trend.narration_text:
This chart tracks your purchasing customer count over 12 months.

The blue line shows unique customers making purchases each month. An upward trend means you're keeping customers engaged and coming back. A downward trend signals potential problems.

Look for patterns. Q4 often spikes for retail due to holidays. Post-holiday dips in January are normal. But sustained decline needs attention.

This answers: Is our customer base healthy? When did changes happen? And should we launch win-back campaigns?

customer-retention:time_between_purchase.title: Time Between Purchases
customer-retention:time_between_purchase.formula: Customer count per gap bucket of average days between orders (0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 91-180, 180+ days), restricted to repeat customers (2+ orders); also shows total orders per bucket.
customer-retention:time_between_purchase.formula_explanation: Calculates the gap between consecutive purchases for repeat customers and groups them into time buckets. This reveals purchase velocity patterns and helps identify optimal re-engagement timing windows.
how_to_read: Each bar shows how many customers fall into that time gap bucket
Shorter time gaps (0-30 days) indicate highly engaged repeat customers
Longer gaps (180+ days) may represent at-risk or infrequent buyers
The distribution shape reveals your typical purchase cycle
Click any bar to explore customers in that time bucket
decisions_answered: What is the typical time between customer purchases?
When is the optimal time to send re-engagement emails?
How many customers have long purchase gaps requiring intervention?
Is our purchase velocity improving with retention efforts?
customer-retention:time_between_purchase.narration_text:
This distribution chart reveals how long customers wait between purchases.

The 0-30 day bucket is your power users - highly engaged customers who buy frequently. The 31-60 and 61-90 day buckets are your regular customers. Beyond 90 days, customers are becoming infrequent, and past 180 days, they're at risk of churning.

Use this to time your marketing. If most customers naturally repurchase at 45 days, send your reminder at 40 days. If you see a large 180+ day bucket, those customers need win-back campaigns now.

This tells you when to intervene and how to structure your email flows.

customer-rfm:at_risk_customers.title: At-Risk Customer Alerts
customer-rfm:at_risk_customers.formula: One row per at-risk customer (RFM segment is At Risk, Cannot Lose, Need Attention, Hibernating or Lost), showing days since last order, total orders, lifetime value, churn risk score, and RFM score; ranked by lifetime value (top 500). Churn risk is a rule-based score derived from days since last Magento order, not an ML/Klaviyo prediction.
customer-rfm:at_risk_customers.formula_explanation: Identifies high-value customers at risk of churning based on RFM scores and predictive churn probability. These are customers who have purchased frequently and spent significantly but have not been active recently. They require immediate attention.
how_to_read: Table shows customers flagged as high churn risk, sorted by lifetime value
Days since last activity shows how long since their last purchase or engagement
Churn Risk percentage is Klaviyos predictive model output
RFM Score shows their R, F, M values concatenated (e.g., 155 = R1, F5, M5)
Red badges indicate critical churn risk (>80%)
Click a customer row to view their full profile and history
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers are most likely to churn?
How much revenue is at risk from churning customers?
Which customers should receive immediate personal outreach?
What is the common pattern among at-risk customers?
customer-rfm:at_risk_customers.narration_text:
This alert table highlights your highest-value customers who are at risk of churning.

The selection criteria: Customers who used to buy frequently with high order values - indicated by F and M scores of 4 or 5 - but have not purchased recently, shown by R scores of 1 or 2. We also include anyone with churn probability above 80%.

Reading the table: Customers are sorted by lifetime value by default - your most valuable at-risk customers appear first. The churn risk percentage comes from Klaviyos predictive analytics. Days since activity shows how long since they last engaged.

This is your action list: These customers represent significant revenue at risk. A $2,000 CLV customer with 90% churn risk needs immediate attention - perhaps a personal email, exclusive offer, or phone call. Export this list for your retention campaign.

customer-rfm:cohort_retention.title: Cohort Retention
customer-rfm:cohort_retention.formula: One row per first-purchase month, showing cohort customer count, repeat customers, repeat rate %, average orders, average lifetime value, and total lifetime value. Repeat rate = active customers ÷ cohort size × 100; average lifetime value = revenue per customer for that cohort.
customer-rfm:cohort_retention.formula_explanation: Groups customers by their first purchase month (cohort) and tracks their repeat purchase behavior. Shows how different acquisition cohorts perform - some months produce better long-term customers than others.
how_to_read: Each row represents customers who made their first purchase in that month
Repeat Rate shows what percentage became repeat buyers (2+ orders)
Higher repeat rates indicate better quality customer acquisition that month
Avg CLV shows the long-term value of customers in each cohort
Recent cohorts have lower CLV because they have not had time to mature
Color intensity on Repeat Rate indicates performance vs benchmarks
decisions_answered: Which acquisition months produced the highest-quality customers?
Is our repeat purchase rate improving over time?
How long does it take for a cohort to reach full CLV potential?
Should we replicate campaigns from high-performing cohort months?
customer-rfm:cohort_retention.narration_text:
This cohort grid shows how customer acquisition quality varies by month.

The formulas: Repeat Rate is the percentage of cohort customers who made at least 2 purchases. Average CLV is total lifetime value divided by cohort size.

Reading the grid: Each row is a monthly cohort - all customers who made their first purchase that month. A 50% repeat rate means half of those new customers came back to buy again. Green highlighting means the cohort outperformed benchmarks.

Important context: Recent cohorts will always show lower CLV because they have not had time to accumulate purchases. Compare repeat rates across cohorts to identify which acquisition channels or campaigns produced the best long-term customers.

customer-rfm:rfm_heatmap.title: RFM Heatmap (Recency x Frequency)
customer-rfm:rfm_heatmap.formula: 5×5 matrix of Recency score (1-5) by Frequency score (1-5); each cell value is the count of customers with that Recency-Frequency combination, with average and total lifetime value as hover detail.
customer-rfm:rfm_heatmap.formula_explanation: This 5x5 matrix shows customer density across all combinations of Recency scores (1-5) and Frequency scores (1-5). Each cell represents customers with that specific R-F combination. Darker cells indicate higher customer concentration.
how_to_read: Y-axis shows Recency score (R5 = most recent, R1 = longest ago)
X-axis shows Frequency score (F5 = most frequent, F1 = one-time buyer)
Top-right corner (R5, F5) contains your best customers
Bottom-right (R1, F5) shows high-frequency customers at risk - they used to buy often
Top-left (R5, F1) shows new customers - recent but only one purchase
Darker cells mean more customers concentrated in that R-F combination
decisions_answered: Where are most of our customers concentrated in the R-F matrix?
Do we have many high-frequency customers at risk (R1-R2, F4-F5)?
What portion of customers are one-time buyers vs repeat?
Which R-F combinations should we target for campaigns?
customer-rfm:rfm_heatmap.narration_text:
This heatmap shows your customer distribution across Recency and Frequency scores.

The formula counts customers for each R-F combination, creating a 5x5 matrix. Scores range from 1 (worst) to 5 (best) on each dimension.

Reading the matrix: The Y-axis shows Recency - R5 means they purchased very recently, R1 means a long time ago. The X-axis shows Frequency - F5 means they buy very often, F1 means one-time buyer. Darker cells have more customers.

The top-right corner is your sweet spot - recent and frequent buyers. The bottom-right is concerning - these are customers who used to buy frequently but have gone quiet. The top-left represents your opportunity - new customers who could become frequent buyers with the right nurturing.

customer-rfm:segment_distribution.title: RFM Segment Distribution
customer-rfm:segment_distribution.formula: Horizontal bar chart of customer count per RFM segment (Champion, Loyal Customer, At Risk, etc.), with each segment's share of customers, total revenue, share of revenue, and average lifetime value; ordered by total revenue.
customer-rfm:segment_distribution.formula_explanation: Customers are assigned to segments based on their RFM scores (Recency, Frequency, Monetary). Each segment represents a distinct behavioral pattern. The chart shows the relative size of each segment as a percentage of your total customer base.
how_to_read: Each horizontal bar represents one customer segment (Champions, Loyal, At Risk, etc.)
Bar length shows customer count - longer bars mean larger segments
Champions (best customers) have high R, F, and M scores - recent, frequent, high value
At Risk segments have high historical value but low recent activity
Colors indicate segment health: green for healthy, red/orange for attention-needed
decisions_answered: What is the distribution of customers across behavioral segments?
Which segments contain the most customers?
Are we growing our Champions segment over time?
How many customers need re-engagement campaigns?
customer-rfm:segment_distribution.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows how your customers are distributed across RFM segments.

The formula calculates each segment's share of your total customer base. Customers are assigned to segments based on three scores: Recency (how recently they purchased), Frequency (how often they buy), and Monetary value (how much they spend).

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more customers. Your Champions segment represents your best customers - they buy recently, frequently, and spend a lot. At Risk and Need Attention segments are customers who were once valuable but have gone quiet.

Key questions answered: What does your customer portfolio look like? Are you building Champions or losing them? Which segments need immediate marketing attention? Use this to prioritize campaigns and resource allocation.

customer-rfm:segment_performance.title: Segment Performance Table
customer-rfm:segment_performance.formula: One row per RFM segment showing customer count, share of customers, total revenue, share of revenue, average recency days, average orders, average lifetime value, and average churn probability (churn risk score ÷ 100); ordered by total revenue.
customer-rfm:segment_performance.formula_explanation: This table provides detailed metrics for each segment including customer count, revenue contribution, average CLV, average order frequency, and churn risk. It helps compare segment performance across multiple dimensions.
how_to_read: Segments are sorted by total revenue contribution by default
Compare % Customers vs % Revenue to find high-value segments
Champions may be 14% of customers but 33% of revenue - disproportionate value
Green churn risk values are healthy, red indicates urgent attention needed
Click column headers to sort by different metrics
Click any row to drill into that segments customer list
decisions_answered: Which segments drive the most revenue?
What is the average CLV difference between segments?
Which segments have the highest churn risk?
How does revenue contribution compare to customer count?
customer-rfm:segment_performance.narration_text:
This performance table provides detailed metrics for each customer segment.

The key formulas: Average CLV is total segment revenue divided by customer count. Churn risk is the average predicted churn probability for customers in each segment.

Reading this table: Look for disproportionate value - Champions might be 14% of customers but contribute 33% of revenue. That means each Champion is worth more than twice the average customer. Sort by churn risk to identify segments needing immediate retention campaigns.

Questions answered: Which segments drive your business? Where should marketing focus? A segment with high CLV but high churn risk represents both opportunity and threat - these customers are valuable but at risk of leaving.

formula_components.name: Avg CLV
formula_components.formula: Total segment lifetime value ÷ segment customer count

customer-rfm:segment_trend.title: Segment Size Trend
customer-rfm:segment_trend.formula: Line chart tracking the number of customers in each RFM segment over time, grouped by customer first-order date at the selected granularity; one line per segment.
customer-rfm:segment_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks how segment sizes change over time using monthly snapshots. This reveals whether your customer health is improving (growing Champions, shrinking At Risk) or deteriorating. Helps identify seasonal patterns and campaign impacts.
how_to_read: Each line represents one customer segment tracked over 12 months
Rising Champions line indicates improving customer quality
Rising At Risk line is a warning sign - more customers disengaging
Look for seasonal patterns (spring/fall planting seasons for garden business)
Sharp changes may indicate campaign impacts or market events
Toggle segments on/off in the legend to focus on specific comparisons
decisions_answered: Are our best segments growing or shrinking over time?
Is our At Risk segment getting larger (customer health declining)?
What seasonal patterns affect customer engagement?
Did specific campaigns or events impact segment migration?
customer-rfm:segment_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks segment sizes over the past 12 months.

The formula counts customers in each segment at monthly intervals, creating a time series for each segment.

Reading the trends: You want to see Champions and Loyal Customers trending upward - that means your customer base is getting healthier. At Risk and Hibernating trending upward is concerning - more customers are disengaging. Look for seasonal patterns too - a garden business might see Champions spike in spring when customers are actively buying.

Key questions: Is customer health improving overall? Are retention efforts working? Did a specific campaign cause segment migrations? Use this to measure the long-term effectiveness of your marketing programs.

data-quality:anomaly_log.title: Recent Anomalies
data-quality:anomaly_log.formula: Recent days (past 30) where GA4 sessions or revenue deviated more than 1.5 standard deviations from the period mean, each with its z-scores, anomaly type and spike/drop direction
data-quality:anomaly_log.formula_explanation: Lists all detected statistical anomalies from the past 30 days sorted by date. Each entry shows the date, metric values, z-scores, anomaly type, and direction. This log helps prioritize which anomalies to investigate first based on severity.
how_to_read: Date column shows when the anomaly occurred
Sessions and Revenue show actual values that day
Z-Score columns show how many standard deviations from mean
Higher absolute z-scores indicate more severe anomalies
Type indicates which metric triggered the anomaly
Direction shows SPIKE (positive) or DROP (negative)
Red background indicates critical severity
Click a row to investigate that date further
decisions_answered: What anomalies have occurred recently?
Which anomalies are most severe and need attention first?
Are anomalies mostly spikes or drops?
Is there a pattern to when anomalies occur?
data-quality:anomaly_log.narration_text:
This table logs all detected statistical anomalies from the past 30 days.

Each row shows: date, sessions value, revenue value, z-scores for each, anomaly type, and direction. Z-scores over 2 are critical. Direction tells you if it was a spike or drop.

Color coding helps prioritize: red rows are most severe. Orange are moderate. Focus on red rows first.

This answers: What anomalies occurred recently? Which are most severe? Are they mostly spikes or drops?

formula_components.name: Sessions Z-Score
formula_components.formula: (day sessions − mean sessions) ÷ standard deviation of sessions

formula_components.name: Revenue Z-Score
formula_components.formula: (day revenue − mean revenue) ÷ standard deviation of revenue

formula_components.name: Direction
formula_components.formula: Spike if z-score positive, Drop if negative

data-quality:avg_sync_lag.title: Average Sync Lag
data-quality:avg_sync_lag.formula: Average Sync Lag = average across schemas of (current time − most recent Fivetran sync time), in minutes
data-quality:avg_sync_lag.formula_explanation: Calculates the average time elapsed since the last successful sync across all monitored schemas. Lower values indicate fresher data. This metric helps identify if data is becoming stale across the board.
how_to_read: Displays average minutes behind across all schemas
Formatted as minutes (m), hours (h), or days (d)
Green: < 60 minutes (data is fresh)
Yellow: 60-360 minutes (acceptable but monitor)
Red: > 360 minutes (data is stale, investigate)
Sparkline shows lag trend over time
decisions_answered: How fresh is my data on average?
Is there a general slowdown in data syncing?
Do I need to wait for fresher data before reporting?
Is the overall sync performance degrading over time?
data-quality:avg_sync_lag.narration_text:
This KPI shows the average time since last successful sync across all schemas.

The formula: sum of minutes since sync for each schema, divided by schema count. Each schema's lag is calculated from the most recent Fivetran sync timestamp.

Under 60 minutes is fresh and green. 60 to 360 minutes is acceptable but worth monitoring. Over 360 minutes indicates stale data requiring investigation.

This answers: How fresh is my data? Is syncing slowing down? Should I wait for fresher data before reporting?

data-quality:completeness_chart.title: Data Completeness
data-quality:completeness_chart.formula: Percentage of NULL values in critical columns of key source tables (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads) against a 99% completeness target
data-quality:completeness_chart.formula_explanation: Shows the percentage of NULL values in critical columns across key tables. High NULL percentages indicate data quality issues that could affect analysis accuracy. The 99% completeness threshold represents best practice for marketing data.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a table and its NULL percentage
Shorter bars (lower %) indicate better data quality
Orange color highlights fields with NULL values
The 99% threshold line shows completeness target
Bars extending beyond 1% warrant investigation
Hover for exact NULL counts and percentages
Click a bar to see field-level breakdown
decisions_answered: Are there data quality issues with any tables?
Which fields have the most missing values?
Is my data complete enough for accurate reporting?
Do I need to address data quality with upstream systems?
data-quality:completeness_chart.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows NULL value percentages for critical columns in key tables.

The formula: count of NULL values divided by total rows, times 100. Lower percentages mean better data quality. The target is 99% completeness or under 1% NULLs.

Orange bars show the NULL percentage. Bars extending beyond 1% need investigation. Common causes include tracking issues, schema changes, or upstream data problems.

This answers: Are there data quality issues? Which fields have missing values? Is data complete enough for reporting?

formula_components.name: NULL %
formula_components.formula: Rows with a NULL value ÷ total rows × 100

data-quality:critical_anomalies.title: Critical Anomalies
data-quality:critical_anomalies.formula: Critical Anomalies = count of days in the trailing 7 days where sessions or revenue z-score exceeds 2 standard deviations from the mean
data-quality:critical_anomalies.formula_explanation: Uses statistical z-scores to detect unusual patterns in key metrics like sessions and revenue. A z-score measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean. Values beyond 2 standard deviations occur less than 5% of the time and warrant investigation.
how_to_read: The number shows count of days with critical anomalies in the past 7 days
Zero (green) means no unusual patterns detected
One or more (yellow/red) indicates days with significant deviations
Accompanying warning count shows less severe anomalies
Click to see anomaly details and investigate causes
Red pulsing indicates active critical anomalies
decisions_answered: Are there any data quality issues I should investigate?
Did any unusual events affect my metrics recently?
Is my data behaving normally compared to historical patterns?
Should I be concerned about any sudden changes?
data-quality:critical_anomalies.narration_text:
This KPI counts days with critical anomalies detected in the past 7 days using statistical z-scores.

The formula: calculate z-score as value minus mean, divided by standard deviation. Critical anomalies have z-scores beyond 2, meaning they're outside 95% of normal values.

Zero is ideal - no unusual patterns. Higher counts mean investigate further. Anomalies could indicate real events like promotions, or data quality issues.

This answers: Are there data quality issues? Did unusual events affect metrics? Should I investigate any sudden changes?

formula_components.name: Critical
formula_components.formula: count of days where |z-score| of daily sessions or revenue > 2

formula_components.name: Warning
formula_components.formula: count of days where |z-score| of daily sessions or revenue is between 1.5 and 2

data-quality:daily_metrics_trend.title: Daily Metrics Trend
data-quality:daily_metrics_trend.formula: Daily GA4 sessions and revenue over 60 days, with anomaly markers where a day's value exceeds ~2 standard deviations from the 60-day mean (dual-line)
data-quality:daily_metrics_trend.formula_explanation: Displays daily sessions and revenue trends over 60 days with anomaly detection. The chart overlays anomaly markers when values deviate significantly from historical averages. This helps identify both data quality issues and genuine business events.
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily sessions (left Y-axis)
Green line shows daily revenue (right Y-axis)
Red circles mark anomaly points (z-score > 2)
Yellow triangles mark warning points (z-score 1.5-2)
Hover over any point to see exact values and z-scores
Sudden spikes or drops warrant investigation
Consistent patterns indicate stable data quality
Click a date to drill into detailed breakdown
decisions_answered: Are there unusual spikes or drops in my key metrics?
When did the last anomaly occur?
Is the variance in my data normal or concerning?
Should I investigate any specific dates?
data-quality:daily_metrics_trend.narration_text:
This dual-line chart shows daily sessions and revenue over 60 days with anomaly detection.

Sessions are in blue on the left axis. Revenue is in green on the right axis. Red markers indicate critical anomalies where z-score exceeds 2. Yellow markers show warnings between 1.5 and 2.

Look for: sudden drops could indicate tracking issues. Spikes could indicate real events or data errors. Consistent patterns indicate stable data quality.

This answers: Are there unusual spikes or drops? When did anomalies occur? Should I investigate specific dates?

data-quality:date_coverage_matrix.title: Date Coverage Matrix
data-quality:date_coverage_matrix.formula: Heatmap marking, for each source schema (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo) and each recent date, whether any data exists for that date
data-quality:date_coverage_matrix.formula_explanation: A heatmap visualization showing which dates have data for each schema. Green cells indicate data is present; red cells indicate missing data for that date/schema combination. This helps identify gaps in historical data that could affect reporting accuracy.
how_to_read: Each row represents a data schema (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo)
Each column represents a date (last 14 days shown)
Green cells mean data exists for that date
Red cells mean data is missing for that date
Gray cells indicate future dates or today
Click a cell to see details for that date/schema
Patterns of red may indicate sync failures or data issues
decisions_answered: Are there any missing dates in my data?
Which schemas have date coverage gaps?
Was data collection working properly last week?
Do I have complete data for reporting periods?
data-quality:date_coverage_matrix.narration_text:
This heatmap shows data availability by date and schema over the last 14 days.

Each row is a schema. Each column is a date. Green means data exists. Red means data is missing. The formula checks if any records exist for each date-schema combination.

Look for patterns: a column of red indicates a day when all syncs failed. A row with red spots indicates schema-specific issues.

This answers: Are there missing dates? Which schemas have gaps? Was data collection working properly?

data-quality:klaviyo_volume.title: Klaviyo Event Volume
data-quality:klaviyo_volume.formula: Klaviyo email/SMS event types over the last 7 days ranked by event count, each with its sync freshness status (top 15)
data-quality:klaviyo_volume.formula_explanation: Displays Klaviyo email and SMS event volumes by type over the last 7 days. Each event type (Received Email, Opened Email, Clicked Email, etc.) has its own count and sync status. This helps validate that Klaviyo events are flowing correctly.
how_to_read: Each bar represents an event type
Bar length indicates event volume (count)
Labels show formatted counts (e.g., 1.2M)
Event types sorted by volume (highest first)
Top 10 event types displayed
Freshness badge next to each shows sync status
Click an event type to drill into Klaviyo details
decisions_answered: Are Klaviyo events syncing properly?
What's the volume distribution across event types?
Are email opens tracking correctly relative to sends?
Has event volume changed unexpectedly?
data-quality:klaviyo_volume.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows Klaviyo event volumes by type over the last 7 days.

The formula counts events by type and tracks the last sync timestamp for each. Types include Received Email, Opened Email, Clicked Email, Placed Order, and more.

Volume distribution helps validate tracking: opens should be a percentage of receives. Clicks should be a percentage of opens. Unexpected ratios indicate tracking issues.

This answers: Are Klaviyo events syncing? What's the volume distribution? Are email metrics tracking correctly?

data-quality:schema_coverage.title: Schema Coverage
data-quality:schema_coverage.formula: One row per source schema with its earliest and latest data date, total days of coverage, and days behind today
data-quality:schema_coverage.formula_explanation: Summarizes the historical date range available for each data schema. Shows how far back data goes and how current it is. Longer coverage enables better historical analysis; being behind today indicates potential sync issues.
how_to_read: Each row represents one data schema
Earliest Date shows how far back data is available
Latest Date shows the most recent data
Days Coverage shows total span of historical data
Days Behind shows how far behind current date
Green (0-1 days) means data is current
Yellow (2-3 days) means slight delay
Red (>3 days) means significant delay requiring action
Click a schema to see detailed table breakdown
decisions_answered: How far back does my historical data go?
Is my data current or lagging behind?
Which schemas have the longest historical coverage?
Do I have enough history for trend analysis?
data-quality:schema_coverage.narration_text:
This table summarizes date range coverage for each data schema.

The formulas calculate days between earliest and latest dates, and days between latest date and today. Longer coverage enables better historical analysis.

Days Behind is key: zero or one means data is current. Two or three is a slight delay. More than three days indicates sync issues requiring investigation.

This answers: How far back does data go? Is data current or lagging? Do I have enough history for trends?

formula_components.name: Days Coverage
formula_components.formula: Latest data date − earliest data date

data-quality:schema_health.title: Schema Health
data-quality:schema_health.formula: Schema Health % = (fresh + acceptable schemas) ÷ total schemas × 100, where fresh = synced < 60 min ago and acceptable = synced 60–360 min ago
data-quality:schema_health.formula_explanation: Measures the overall health of your data pipeline by tracking how recently each schema was synced. Fivetran syncs data at regular intervals, and this metric shows what percentage of your data sources are current. A 100% score means all schemas synced within 6 hours.
how_to_read: The percentage shows the proportion of schemas that are fresh or acceptable
Green (100%) means all 4 data sources are syncing properly
Yellow (75%) means 1 schema is stale or critical
Red (<50%) means multiple schemas have sync issues requiring immediate attention
Sparkline shows schema health trend over recent days
Click to navigate to Master Config for detailed schema management
decisions_answered: Are all my data sources syncing properly?
Is the data pipeline healthy enough for reliable reporting?
Do I need to investigate any sync failures?
Which schemas are experiencing delays?
data-quality:schema_health.narration_text:
This KPI shows overall schema health as a percentage of data sources that are fresh or acceptable.

The formula: add fresh and acceptable schema counts, divide by total schemas, times 100. Fresh means synced within 60 minutes. Acceptable means within 6 hours.

A 100% score means all data sources are current. Below 75% indicates sync issues. Below 50% is critical and requires immediate investigation.

This answers: Are all data sources syncing? Is the pipeline healthy for reporting? Which schemas need attention?

data-quality:spend_comparison.title: Ad Spend Comparison
data-quality:spend_comparison.formula: Daily Google Ads spend versus Meta Ads spend over the last 30 days (grouped bars), flagging days where one platform's spend is missing or zero
data-quality:spend_comparison.formula_explanation: Compares daily ad spend between Google Ads and Meta Ads over the last 14 days. This cross-platform validation helps identify missing spend data that could affect budget tracking and ROAS calculations. Missing spend is a common data quality issue.
how_to_read: Blue bars represent Google Ads daily spend
Purple bars represent Meta Ads daily spend
Bars are grouped by date for easy comparison
Missing or zero spend shows as empty space
Striped pattern indicates missing data (not just $0)
Hover for exact dollar amounts
Look for days where one platform is missing
Click a date to drill into platform details
decisions_answered: Is any ad spend data missing?
Are both platforms reporting spend consistently?
How does Google Ads spend compare to Meta Ads?
Were there any days with zero spend that need investigation?
data-quality:spend_comparison.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares Google Ads and Meta Ads spend over the last 14 days.

Blue bars are Google. Purple bars are Meta. They're grouped by date for easy comparison. The formula converts Google's micros to dollars and sums Meta's spend directly.

Look for missing bars - a day with Google but no Meta indicates missing data, not necessarily zero spend. Cross-platform comparison validates data completeness.

This answers: Is spend data missing? Are both platforms reporting? How do platform spends compare?

formula_components.name: Google Spend
formula_components.formula: Sum of cost micros ÷ 1,000,000 per day

formula_components.name: Meta Spend
formula_components.formula: Sum of Meta spend per day

formula_components.name: Status
formula_components.formula: Flag a day when Google or Meta daily spend is zero or missing

data-quality:sync_status_table.title: Sync Status by Table
data-quality:sync_status_table.formula: One row per monitored source table with its last Fivetran sync time, minutes since that sync, row count and a fresh/acceptable/stale/critical freshness status
data-quality:sync_status_table.formula_explanation: Provides a detailed breakdown of sync status for every monitored table. Each row shows when the table was last synced, how long ago that was, the current row count, and the freshness status. This is your primary tool for identifying specific tables with sync issues.
how_to_read: Schema column groups tables by data source
Table Name identifies the specific Snowflake table
Last Sync shows the timestamp of most recent data
Minutes Since Sync shows time elapsed (sortable)
Row Count indicates table size
Status badge shows FRESH/ACCEPTABLE/STALE/CRITICAL
Click any row to drill into table details
Use search to find specific tables
Sort by Minutes Since Sync to find stale tables first
decisions_answered: Which specific tables are experiencing sync delays?
When was each table last updated?
Are there any tables that haven't synced in days?
Which tables have the most rows?
Is the sync delay affecting critical tables?
data-quality:sync_status_table.narration_text:
This table shows detailed sync status for every monitored table in your data warehouse.

Each row displays: schema name, table name, last sync timestamp, minutes since sync, row count, and freshness status.

Sort by minutes since sync descending to see stale tables first. Use search to find specific tables. Status badges quickly identify problems: red for critical, orange for stale, green for fresh.

This answers: Which tables have sync delays? When was each table updated? Are critical tables affected?

data-quality:tables_monitored.title: Tables Monitored
data-quality:tables_monitored.formula: Tables Monitored = count of distinct tables across the GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, and Klaviyo source schemas
data-quality:tables_monitored.formula_explanation: Shows the total number of database tables being monitored across all four data schemas. This represents the breadth of your data infrastructure. Each table contains specific marketing data that feeds into dashboard analytics.
how_to_read: The primary number shows total tables across all schemas
Breakdown by schema: GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo
Stable count indicates consistent data structure
Sudden changes may indicate new tables added or tables dropped
Higher counts generally mean more granular data available
Click to explore table inventory in Master Config
decisions_answered: How comprehensive is our data coverage?
Are all expected tables present in each schema?
Has our data infrastructure changed recently?
Which schema has the most tables to monitor?
data-quality:tables_monitored.narration_text:
This KPI shows the total count of tables being monitored across all data schemas.

The count is a sum of tables from GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, and Klaviyo. Each schema contributes different amounts based on data granularity.

A stable count indicates consistent infrastructure. Changes might indicate schema updates from Fivetran or data model changes.

This answers: How comprehensive is our data coverage? Are all expected tables present? Has our infrastructure changed?

email-campaigns:engagement_timeline.title: Engagement Timeline
email-campaigns:engagement_timeline.formula: Unique opens and unique clicks per hour over the first 72 hours after the campaign send (line chart)
email-campaigns:engagement_timeline.formula_explanation: This chart tracks how engagement unfolds over the 72 hours after send. Most engagement happens in the first 24 hours, with a long tail afterward. The shape reveals audience behavior patterns.
how_to_read: Steep initial peak indicates strong immediate engagement
Green line shows opens over time since send
Purple line shows clicks over time since send
Peak engagement hour reveals optimal send time insights
Long tail indicates ongoing engagement from delayed openers
decisions_answered: When does most engagement happen after sending?
What is the optimal send time for this audience?
How long does engagement typically continue?
Is there a secondary engagement peak at certain hours?
email-campaigns:engagement_timeline.narration_text:
The engagement timeline shows how your audience interacted with this campaign over 72 hours post-send.

The green line tracks opens, the purple line tracks clicks. Most activity happens in the first 6 to 12 hours, then tapers off.

Look for peak engagement hour - this tells you when your audience is most active. If peak is at 2 PM, consider sending future campaigns around that time.

A steep initial curve means strong immediate engagement. A gradual curve might mean people are opening later, perhaps on mobile in the evening.

The gap between opens and clicks shows how well content converts openers to clickers.

email-campaigns:geographic_distribution.title: Geographic Distribution
email-campaigns:geographic_distribution.formula: Recipient count per country, each slice = country recipients ÷ total recipients × 100 (pie chart)
email-campaigns:geographic_distribution.formula_explanation: This visualization shows where your subscribers are located. Understanding geographic distribution helps optimize send times for different time zones and reveals opportunities for location-specific content.
how_to_read: Larger slices indicate more recipients in that country
Hover for exact recipient counts and percentages
Consider time zones when planning send times
International audiences may need localized content
Use table view for detailed country-by-country metrics
decisions_answered: Where are my subscribers concentrated geographically?
Should I adjust send times for different time zones?
Are there opportunities for location-specific campaigns?
Which regions have the highest engagement rates?
email-campaigns:geographic_distribution.narration_text:
Geographic distribution shows where your subscribers live around the world.

Larger slices mean more recipients in that country. For most businesses, domestic subscribers dominate, but international audiences can be valuable.

Consider time zones when you see significant international presence. A 10 AM send in New York is evening in Europe and middle of the night in Asia.

Compare engagement rates across regions. Some countries might have higher open rates due to cultural factors or send time alignment.

If you have substantial audiences in different regions, consider sending at times optimized for each group, or creating region-specific content that resonates with local interests.

email-campaigns:historical_comparison.title: Historical Comparison
email-campaigns:historical_comparison.formula: One row per recent campaign with open rate, click rate, click-to-open rate and bounce rate (all computed per campaign from sends, opens, clicks and bounces) for benchmarking
email-campaigns:historical_comparison.formula_explanation: Comparing the current campaign to recent similar campaigns reveals performance trends and helps identify what makes certain campaigns succeed or fail.
how_to_read: Current campaign is highlighted for easy identification
Sort by any column to rank campaigns on that metric
Green values indicate strong performance on that metric
Red values indicate underperformance requiring investigation
Click any row to navigate to that campaign for details
decisions_answered: How does this campaign compare to recent sends?
What patterns emerge across successful campaigns?
Which campaigns should I analyze for best practices?
Is overall email performance trending up or down?
email-campaigns:historical_comparison.narration_text:
Historical comparison lets you benchmark this campaign against recent sends.

The current campaign is highlighted so you can easily spot it. Sort by any column to find top and bottom performers.

Look for patterns. Do your best campaigns share common traits like certain subject line styles, send days, or content types?

Green values indicate strong metrics. Red values flag underperformance. When you see red, dig into why - was it the timing, the content, or the audience?

Click any row to jump to that campaign and analyze what worked or what went wrong. Build a mental library of what resonates with your audience.

email-campaigns:revenue_attribution.title: Revenue Attribution
email-campaigns:revenue_attribution.formula: Daily attributed revenue and sessions (distinct engaged persons) in the 7-day window after send, with revenue per email = revenue ÷ emails sent, revenue per click = revenue ÷ engaged sessions and engagement rate = engaged sessions ÷ sessions × 100 (area chart)
