email-campaigns:revenue_attribution.formula_explanation: GA4 attribution tracks how much revenue was generated within 7 days of the campaign send. This connects email engagement to actual business outcomes.
how_to_read: Sessions show how many website visits the campaign drove
Revenue shows total attributed sales from the campaign
Revenue per Email (RPE) indicates overall campaign efficiency
Engagement Rate shows quality of traffic from the campaign
Daily bars show revenue distribution over the 7-day window
decisions_answered: How much revenue did this campaign generate?
What is the financial return on this email send?
How engaged was the traffic this campaign drove?
When does most purchase activity occur after the send?
email-campaigns:revenue_attribution.narration_text:
Revenue attribution connects email engagement to actual sales using Google Analytics 4 data.

Sessions tell you how many website visits came from this campaign. Revenue shows total sales attributed within a 7-day window after sending.

Revenue per Email is your efficiency metric - divide total revenue by emails sent. This helps compare campaigns of different sizes on equal footing.

The daily bars show when purchases happened. Most conversions typically occur in the first 24 to 48 hours, but some products have longer consideration cycles.

Use this to identify your highest-revenue campaigns and understand what content and timing drives purchases, not just opens and clicks.

email-campaigns:segment_performance.title: Segment Performance
email-campaigns:segment_performance.formula: Per audience segment, open rate = segment opens ÷ segment recipients × 100 and click rate = segment clicks ÷ segment recipients × 100 (bar chart)
email-campaigns:segment_performance.formula_explanation: Breaking down performance by audience segment reveals which subscriber groups engage most. This enables targeted content strategies and helps identify underperforming segments that may need different messaging.
how_to_read: Blue bars show Open Rate by segment
Green bars show Click Rate by segment
Longer bars indicate higher engagement from that segment
Compare segment performance to campaign average
Top segments may warrant exclusive content or offers
decisions_answered: Which audience segments engage most with this content?
Are there segments that need different messaging?
Should we create segment-specific versions of future campaigns?
Which segments justify more personalized content investment?
email-campaigns:segment_performance.narration_text:
Segment performance breaks down how different audience groups responded to this campaign.

Blue bars show open rates by segment. Green bars show click rates. Longer bars mean higher engagement.

Look for segments that dramatically outperform or underperform the average. High performers might justify exclusive content or early access offers. Low performers might need different messaging angles.

Climate zones show geographic segments. You might find warm climate subscribers engage more with spring planting content while cold climate subscribers prefer indoor gardening tips.

Use these insights to create more targeted, relevant campaigns that resonate with each segment's unique interests.

email-flows:email_sequence_dropoff.title: Email Sequence Drop-off
email-flows:email_sequence_dropoff.formula: Recipients remaining at each email step of a flow, with drop-off rate = (previous step recipients − current step recipients) ÷ previous step recipients × 100; first step is the 100% baseline (funnel)
email-flows:email_sequence_dropoff.formula_explanation: This funnel shows how many recipients progress through each email in a flow sequence. Drop-off rate measures the percentage lost between consecutive emails due to unsubscribes, bounces, or flow exits.
how_to_read: Each bar represents one email in the flow sequence, ordered top to bottom
Width of each bar corresponds to the number of recipients at that step
Percentage shown is the retention rate from the previous email
Larger drops between steps indicate potential optimization opportunities
The first email always shows 100% as the baseline entry point
decisions_answered: Where are the biggest drop-offs in our email flows?
Which emails are causing subscribers to leave the flow?
Is our email sequence too long or aggressive?
Which specific emails need content or timing optimization?
email-flows:email_sequence_dropoff.narration_text:
This funnel visualization reveals where subscribers drop off in your email flows.

The formula: Drop-off Rate equals previous step recipients minus current step recipients, divided by previous step, times 100.

Each bar is one email in the sequence, ordered from top to bottom. Bar width shows recipient count - narrower bars mean fewer people reached that step. The percentage shows retention from the previous email.

Look for large drops between steps - these are optimization opportunities. A healthy flow maintains at least 60% retention between emails. Steep drops suggest content issues, poor timing, or subscriber fatigue.

This answers: Where are the biggest drop-offs? Which emails drive unsubscribes? And is your flow too long or aggressive?

email-flows:performance_by_position.title: Performance by Email Position
email-flows:performance_by_position.formula: Average open rate and average click rate across all flows by email position number (1st, 2nd, …) in the sequence (bar chart)
email-flows:performance_by_position.formula_explanation: Aggregates performance metrics across all flows by email position number. This reveals how engagement naturally declines as subscribers progress through email sequences, helping identify the optimal flow length.
how_to_read: Blue bars show average open rate for emails at each position across all flows
Green bars show average click rate for emails at each position
Position 1 (first email) typically has the highest engagement
A steep decline suggests subscribers lose interest quickly
Flattening curves indicate a sustainable engagement level has been reached
decisions_answered: How does engagement change throughout our email sequences?
What is the optimal length for our email flows?
At which position do we see the steepest engagement drop?
Should we consolidate or remove emails at certain positions?
email-flows:performance_by_position.narration_text:
This bar chart reveals how engagement changes throughout your email flow sequences.

The formula: We average the open rates and click rates across all flows for emails at each position. Email 1 rates, Email 2 rates, and so on.

Blue bars are open rates, green bars are click rates. Position 1 - the first email - typically has highest engagement. Each subsequent position usually shows lower engagement.

Watch the slope of decline. A steep drop means subscribers lose interest quickly - consider shortening flows. If the curve flattens, you've found a sustainable engagement level.

This answers: How does engagement decay over sequences? What's the optimal flow length? And which positions underperform enough to warrant removal?

email-flows:performance_trend.title: Performance Trend
email-flows:performance_trend.formula: Weekly open rate = unique opens ÷ emails sent × 100 and click rate = unique clicks ÷ emails sent × 100 across all flow emails over the last 90 days (line chart)
email-flows:performance_trend.formula_explanation: Open Rate measures the percentage of recipients who opened an email at least once. Click Rate measures the percentage who clicked any link. Both are calculated weekly across all flow emails to show engagement trends over time.
how_to_read: Blue line shows weekly average open rate across all flow emails
Green line shows weekly average click rate across all flow emails
Upward trends indicate improving email engagement over time
Sudden drops may indicate deliverability issues or content fatigue
Compare the gap between open and click rates to assess content relevance
decisions_answered: Is our overall email engagement improving or declining?
Are there seasonal patterns in our flow email performance?
When did significant engagement changes occur?
Should we investigate recent performance dips?
email-flows:performance_trend.narration_text:
This trend chart shows how your email flow performance has evolved over the past 90 days.

The formula: Open Rate is unique opens divided by emails sent, times 100. Click Rate is unique clicks divided by emails sent, times 100.

The blue line tracks weekly open rates, the green line tracks click rates. Watch for upward trends - they indicate improving engagement. Sudden drops could signal deliverability problems or audience fatigue.

The gap between open and click rates tells you about content relevance. A wide gap means people open but don't find content compelling enough to click.

Use this to answer: Is engagement trending up or down? Are there seasonal patterns? And when should you investigate sudden changes?

email-overview:campaign_vs_flow_performa.title: Campaign vs Flow Performance
email-overview:campaign_vs_flow_performa.formula: Campaigns versus flows compared on open rate = unique opens ÷ unique recipients × 100 and click rate = unique clicks ÷ unique recipients × 100 (bar chart)
email-overview:campaign_vs_flow_performa.formula_explanation: Compares engagement metrics between manually-sent campaigns and automated flow emails. Flows typically show higher engagement because they are triggered by specific user actions or behaviors, making them more relevant and timely.
how_to_read: Blue bars show open rate for each email source type
Green bars show click rate for each source type
Compare bar heights to see which source drives better engagement
Flows often outperform campaigns due to behavioral targeting
Large gaps between campaign and flow performance suggest optimization opportunities
decisions_answered: Which email type drives more engagement - campaigns or flows?
Should we invest more in building automated flows?
Are our manual campaigns performing as well as automated emails?
Where should we focus optimization efforts for maximum impact?
email-overview:campaign_vs_flow_performa.narration_text:
This bar chart compares engagement between your manual campaigns and automated flows.

Blue bars show open rates, green bars show click rates. Compare the heights to see which performs better.

Flows typically outperform campaigns because they're triggered by user behavior - abandoned carts, browse activity, post-purchase. This makes them more relevant and timely.

If you see large gaps between campaign and flow performance, it suggests you should invest more in building automated flows. If campaigns are lagging significantly, focus optimization there first.

Use this to decide: Where should you invest development time? Are campaigns worth the manual effort?

email-overview:content_type_performance.title: Content Type Performance
email-overview:content_type_performance.formula: Open rate = unique opens ÷ recipients × 100 and click rate = unique clicks ÷ recipients × 100 by content category derived from campaign names (bar chart)
email-overview:content_type_performance.formula_explanation: Compares engagement rates across different campaign content categories. Content types are derived from campaign names - promotional, product launch, newsletter, and other. This reveals which content resonates most with your audience.
how_to_read: Blue bars show open rate for each content category
Purple bars show click rate for each category
Higher bars indicate stronger engagement for that content type
Compare open vs click rates to understand engagement depth
Content types with high opens but low clicks may need better CTAs
decisions_answered: Which content type drives the most engagement?
Should we send more promotional or newsletter content?
Are product launches generating sufficient interest?
Which content types need optimization to improve clicks?
email-overview:content_type_performance.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart compares engagement across different content categories.

Blue bars represent open rates, purple bars represent click rates. Content types are derived from campaign names - promotional, product launch, newsletter, and other.

Look at both metrics together. High opens with low clicks means people are opening but not engaging - you might need better calls to action. Low opens suggest subject line or timing issues.

Typically, promotional content has higher clicks because it has clear actions. Newsletters build relationships but may have lower immediate engagement.

Use this to decide: What content should you create more of? What needs optimization?

email-overview:email_list_health.title: Email List Health
email-overview:email_list_health.formula: Composition of the email list — subscribed, unsubscribed and never-subscribed profiles — with subscription rate = subscribed profiles ÷ total profiles × 100; center shows total profiles (donut)
email-overview:email_list_health.formula_explanation: Measures the health of your email list by showing the breakdown of subscribed, unsubscribed, and never-subscribed profiles. A healthy list has high subscription rates and low unsubscribe counts, indicating good list hygiene and audience quality.
how_to_read: Green segment shows actively subscribed profiles who can receive marketing emails
Red segment shows profiles who have unsubscribed from marketing
Gray segment shows profiles who were never subscribed (e.g., transactional only)
Center value displays total profiles in your database
Health indicator badge shows overall list health status
decisions_answered: What percentage of our list is actively subscribed?
Do we have a concerning unsubscribe rate?
How many profiles could we potentially convert to subscribers?
Is our list health improving or declining over time?
email-overview:email_list_health.narration_text:
This gauge chart shows the health of your email subscriber list.

Green represents actively subscribed profiles - these can receive your marketing emails. Red shows unsubscribed profiles who opted out. Gray shows profiles who never subscribed, often from transactional interactions.

The center displays your total profile count. The health badge at top right indicates overall status - green for healthy, yellow for caution, red for concern.

Above 90% subscribed is excellent. Between 70 and 90% is acceptable but worth monitoring. Below 70% indicates list health issues.

Use this to answer: Is our list healthy? Do we have unsubscribe problems? How many profiles could we convert to subscribers?

email-overview:email_program_trend.title: Email Program Trend
email-overview:email_program_trend.formula: Weekly emails sent (volume), open rate = unique opens ÷ recipients × 100 and click rate = unique clicks ÷ recipients × 100 over ~90 days (combined area + line chart)
email-overview:email_program_trend.formula_explanation: This chart tracks three key metrics over 90 days: total emails sent (volume), open rate (engagement), and click rate (action). Open and click rates are calculated using unique recipients to avoid inflation from multiple opens or clicks by the same person.
how_to_read: Blue area shows total email volume sent per week (scaled to thousands)
Green line tracks open rate percentage - aim for 25%+ for healthy engagement
Purple line tracks click rate percentage - aim for 3%+ for strong performance
Rising trends indicate improving engagement; declining trends warrant investigation
Look for correlation between volume changes and engagement rate changes
decisions_answered: Is our overall email engagement improving or declining over time?
Does sending more emails hurt our engagement rates?
When did significant performance shifts occur that need investigation?
Are we maintaining healthy open and click rates consistently?
email-overview:email_program_trend.narration_text:
This chart shows your email program performance over the past 90 days with three key metrics.

The blue area represents total emails sent each week, scaled to thousands. The green line tracks your open rate - aim for 25% or higher for healthy engagement. The purple line shows click rate - 3% or higher indicates strong performance.

Watch for trends: Rising lines mean improving engagement. If you see declining trends, investigate what changed. Also look for correlation - sometimes sending more emails can hurt engagement rates.

This chart helps you answer: Is engagement improving? Does volume affect engagement? And when did major shifts occur that need attention?

email-overview:monthly_order_revenue.title: Monthly Order Revenue
email-overview:monthly_order_revenue.formula: Total order value attributed to email (Klaviyo) summed per month, with order count per month, over the trailing 6 months (area chart)
email-overview:monthly_order_revenue.formula_explanation: Tracks total order value attributed to email marketing through Klaviyo over the past 6 months. This includes revenue from both campaign and flow-attributed conversions, showing the direct financial impact of your email program.
how_to_read: Area height shows total order revenue for each month (scaled to thousands)
Rising trend indicates growing email-attributed revenue
Summary values at top right show total revenue and order count
Seasonal patterns may appear - compare year-over-year when possible
Sudden drops warrant investigation into email performance or attribution
decisions_answered: How much revenue is email marketing generating?
Is email revenue growing or declining over time?
What are the seasonal patterns in email-attributed orders?
How does email contribute to overall business revenue?
email-overview:monthly_order_revenue.narration_text:
This area chart tracks revenue attributed to your email marketing over the past 6 months.

The height of the gold area shows total order value each month, scaled to thousands. The summary at top right shows cumulative totals - total revenue and order count.

A rising trend indicates your email program is generating more revenue over time. If you see decline, investigate what changed - email performance, attribution windows, or business factors.

Watch for seasonal patterns. Holiday months typically spike. Compare year-over-year when possible to separate growth from seasonality.

This answers the critical question: How much is email actually worth to your business?

funnel:checkout_abandonment.title: Checkout Abandonment Analysis
funnel:checkout_abandonment.formula: One row per day of GA4 checkout flow: started checkout, added shipping, added payment, completed (purchases), and rates: abandonment = (begin checkout − purchases) ÷ begin checkout × 100, drop before shipping = (begin checkout − add shipping) ÷ begin checkout × 100, drop at shipping = (add shipping − add payment) ÷ add shipping × 100, drop at payment = (add payment − purchases) ÷ add payment × 100.
funnel:checkout_abandonment.formula_explanation: This table tracks daily checkout flow performance, showing where users drop off during the multi-step checkout process. High abandonment at specific stages (shipping or payment) indicates friction that can be addressed with targeted optimizations.
how_to_read: Each row shows one day of checkout funnel data
Started Checkout is users who began the process
Completed is users who finished purchasing
Abandonment Rate shows the overall drop-off percentage
Red highlighting indicates days with above-average abandonment
decisions_answered: What is our daily checkout abandonment rate?
Which checkout step causes the most drop-offs?
Are there specific days with unusually high abandonment?
Is abandonment trending up or down over time?
funnel:checkout_abandonment.narration_text:
This table provides a daily breakdown of your checkout funnel, tracking every step from start to purchase.

The abandonment rate formula: begin checkout minus purchases, divided by begin checkout, times 100. The same logic applies to each sub-step like shipping and payment.

Each row is one day. Compare Started Checkout to Completed to see your success rate. The abandonment rate column highlights problem days in red.

This answers critical questions: What's our daily abandonment rate? Which step loses most users - shipping or payment? Are there spike days to investigate? And is the trend improving over time?

funnel:conversion_funnel.title: Conversion Funnel
funnel:conversion_funnel.formula: Funnel from sessions → product views → add to cart → begin checkout → payment info → purchase (unique GA4 users per stage). Each step shows its count and step conversion rate = stage count ÷ prior stage count × 100; overall conversion rate = purchases ÷ sessions × 100.
funnel:conversion_funnel.formula_explanation: The funnel visualizes customer journey progression from initial session through to purchase. Each stage shows the absolute count and the percentage that moved forward from the previous stage. Drop-off between stages represents lost potential customers.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a stage in the customer journey
Bar width shows relative volume compared to the starting point
Percentages between stages show the conversion rate from one step to the next
The largest drop-offs indicate where optimization efforts should focus
Green color for the final purchase stage highlights successful conversions
decisions_answered: What is our overall conversion rate from session to purchase?
Where are we losing the most potential customers?
Which funnel stage needs the most optimization attention?
How does our funnel compare to e-commerce benchmarks?
funnel:conversion_funnel.narration_text:
This funnel chart visualizes your customer journey from initial session through to purchase completion.

The formula is straightforward: each stage's conversion rate equals the stage count divided by the previous stage count, times 100. Overall conversion is purchases divided by sessions.

To read this chart: each bar represents a journey stage. Bar width shows relative volume. The percentages between stages reveal where customers drop off.

Focus on the largest drop-offs - those are your biggest optimization opportunities. This chart answers: What's our conversion rate? Where do we lose customers? And which stage needs the most attention?

funnel:funnel_by_channel.title: Funnel by Channel
funnel:funnel_by_channel.formula: Bars per marketing channel showing GA4 sessions and conversions, with channel conversion rate = conversions ÷ sessions × 100, engagement rate, revenue and AOV; enriched with platform ad spend, cost per session and cost per conversion. Top 10 channels by sessions.
funnel:funnel_by_channel.formula_explanation: This comparison shows how different traffic sources perform through the funnel. Some channels drive high volume but low conversion, while others may bring fewer visitors who convert at higher rates.
how_to_read: Blue bars show session volume by channel (quantity of traffic)
Green bars show conversions by channel (quality of traffic)
Compare the ratio of green to blue to assess channel efficiency
High sessions with low conversions suggest poor traffic quality
Small channels with high conversion rates may warrant increased investment
decisions_answered: Which channel drives the most traffic?
Which channel has the highest conversion rate?
Where should we allocate more marketing budget?
Are there underperforming channels we should optimize or reduce?
funnel:funnel_by_channel.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares funnel performance across your traffic channels.

Blue bars show sessions - the quantity of traffic each channel drives. Green bars show conversions - the quality of that traffic. The ratio between them reveals channel efficiency.

Look for mismatches: high sessions but low conversions suggest poor traffic quality. Small channels with high conversion rates may deserve more investment.

Use this to answer: Which channel drives volume? Which converts best? Where should budget shift? And which channels need optimization or reduction?

funnel:funnel_by_device.title: Funnel by Device
funnel:funnel_by_device.formula: Bars per device category (mobile, desktop, tablet) showing GA4 device conversion rate = conversions ÷ users × 100 and device AOV = revenue ÷ conversions, plus users, engaged sessions, revenue and engagement rate.
funnel:funnel_by_device.formula_explanation: Device breakdown reveals how mobile, desktop, and tablet users behave differently through the funnel. Mobile typically has lower conversion but higher traffic, while desktop often shows higher conversion rates and order values.
how_to_read: Purple bars show conversion rate by device type
Orange bars show Average Order Value by device
Compare mobile vs desktop performance gaps
Higher mobile conversion rates indicate good mobile UX
Lower mobile AOV may indicate different product preferences
decisions_answered: How does mobile conversion compare to desktop?
Is our mobile experience optimized for purchases?
Which device type generates the highest order values?
Should we invest more in mobile optimization?
funnel:funnel_by_device.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart breaks down funnel performance by device type - mobile, desktop, and tablet.

Purple bars show conversion rate for each device. Orange bars show Average Order Value. These metrics often diverge by device.

Typically, desktop converts higher but mobile drives more traffic. If your mobile conversion is close to desktop, your mobile UX is strong. If there's a big gap, there's optimization opportunity.

This answers: How does mobile compare? Is mobile experience optimized? Which device drives highest order values? And should mobile UX be a priority investment?

funnel:funnel_by_geography.title: Funnel by Geography
funnel:funnel_by_geography.formula: Bars per US state showing GA4 users and revenue, with revenue per user = state revenue ÷ state users and state conversion rate = conversions ÷ users × 100; states with more than 500 users, top 20 by users.
funnel:funnel_by_geography.formula_explanation: Geographic breakdown shows which states or regions have the highest user counts and revenue. This helps identify strong markets for expansion and weak markets that may need targeted campaigns or operational improvements.
how_to_read: Bar length shows user volume by state
States are sorted by user count (top markets first)
Compare user distribution to revenue distribution
High users with low revenue indicate conversion or AOV problems
Consider population size when evaluating state performance
decisions_answered: Which states have the most users?
Where is our revenue concentrated geographically?
Are there states with high traffic but low revenue per user?
Which regions should we target for growth campaigns?
funnel:funnel_by_geography.narration_text:
This bar chart shows your top states by user volume, helping you understand geographic distribution.

Bar length indicates user count, sorted from highest to lowest. The top states are your strongest markets for traffic.

But traffic isn't everything - compare this to revenue data. States with many users but low revenue per user have conversion or AOV issues worth investigating. Consider population size too - small penetration in California may beat saturation in Wyoming.

Use this to answer: Where are our users? Where's revenue concentrated? Which states underperform on conversion? And where should growth campaigns focus?

funnel:funnel_trend.title: Funnel Trend
funnel:funnel_trend.formula: Daily line chart of GA4 sessions and purchases over time; daily conversion rate = daily purchases ÷ daily sessions × 100.
funnel:funnel_trend.formula_explanation: This time-series chart tracks the volume of sessions and purchases over time, revealing patterns, seasonality, and the impact of marketing activities or site changes on funnel performance.
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily session volume (traffic)
Green line shows daily purchase volume (conversions)
The gap between lines represents unconverted traffic
Look for correlation between traffic spikes and purchase increases
Identify weekday vs weekend patterns and seasonal trends
decisions_answered: How are sessions and purchases trending over time?
Are traffic increases translating to proportional purchase increases?
When did significant performance changes occur?
What day-of-week or seasonal patterns exist in our funnel?
funnel:funnel_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks sessions and purchases over time, revealing your funnel's health and trends.

The blue line shows daily sessions - your traffic. The green line shows daily purchases - your conversions. The gap between them represents lost opportunities.

Look for patterns: Do purchase spikes follow traffic spikes proportionally? What happens on weekends versus weekdays? Are there seasonal trends?

This chart answers: How is performance trending? Are traffic gains converting to sales? When did major changes occur? And what patterns can inform your marketing calendar?

funnel:stage_conversion_rates.title: Stage Conversion Rates
funnel:stage_conversion_rates.formula: Horizontal bars of step-to-step GA4 conversion rates: session→view = product views ÷ sessions × 100, view→cart = add to cart ÷ product views × 100, cart→checkout = begin checkout ÷ add to cart × 100, cart→purchase = purchases ÷ add to cart × 100.
funnel:stage_conversion_rates.formula_explanation: Each bar shows the percentage of users who successfully moved from one stage to the next. Higher percentages indicate smoother transitions, while lower percentages reveal friction points in the customer journey.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher conversion rates between stages
Compare bar lengths to identify weakest conversion points
Industry benchmarks help contextualize your performance
Session to View rates above 100% are common as users view multiple products
Focus optimization on stages with the lowest conversion rates
decisions_answered: Which stage transition has the lowest conversion rate?
Where should we prioritize conversion rate optimization efforts?
How do our stage conversion rates compare to industry standards?
Are there specific transitions that need immediate attention?
funnel:stage_conversion_rates.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down conversion rates between consecutive funnel stages.

Each bar shows the percentage of users moving from one stage to the next. Session to View is views divided by sessions. View to Cart is adds divided by views. And so on through checkout to purchase.

To read it: longer bars mean better conversion. Shorter bars reveal friction points. Note that Session to View can exceed 100% since users often view multiple products.

Use this chart to identify: Which transition is weakest? Where should you optimize? And how do you compare to industry benchmarks? Focus your CRO efforts on the shortest bars.

google-overview:device_performance.title: Device Performance
google-overview:device_performance.formula: Spend, ROAS and CPA broken down by device type (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, Connected TV) over the selected period
google-overview:device_performance.formula_explanation: Shows how ad spend is distributed across device types: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, Connected TV. Understanding device performance helps optimize bid adjustments and creative strategy per device.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher spend on that device type
Blue represents Mobile, Green represents Desktop, Orange represents Tablet
Compare bar lengths to understand device budget allocation
ROAS badges on bars show efficiency per device
Click any bar to filter the entire page by that device
decisions_answered: How much are we spending on mobile versus desktop?
Which device type delivers the best ROAS?
Should we adjust bid modifiers for certain devices?
Is our device mix aligned with where conversions happen?
google-overview:device_performance.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows spend distribution across device types: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV.

The formula aggregates spend by device from account stats. Longer bars mean more budget allocated to that device.

Mobile typically dominates e-commerce traffic. But watch the ROAS badges - if desktop has higher ROAS with lower spend, you might be missing an opportunity to scale desktop.

This answers: How much goes to mobile versus desktop? Which device is most efficient? And should you adjust bid modifiers to shift budget toward better-performing devices?

google-overview:keyword_portfolio.title: Keyword Portfolio
google-overview:keyword_portfolio.formula: Count of distinct keywords by match type (Exact, Phrase, Broad), stacked by status (Enabled, Paused, Removed)
google-overview:keyword_portfolio.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of your 191K+ keywords across match types: Exact, Broad, and Phrase. Stacked colors indicate status: Enabled, Paused, or Removed. A balanced portfolio typically uses all match types strategically.
how_to_read: Bars are stacked by status: green for enabled, yellow for paused, gray for removed
Total bar length shows all keywords of that match type
Exact match gives most control but limited reach
Broad match has widest reach but less control over query matching
Phrase match balances reach and control
decisions_answered: How are our keywords distributed across match types?
Do we have too many broad match keywords with limited control?
What percentage of our keyword portfolio is actually enabled?
Is our match type strategy balanced for performance and reach?
google-overview:keyword_portfolio.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows your keyword portfolio across match types: Exact, Broad, and Phrase. With over 191,000 keywords, understanding this distribution matters.

The bars are stacked by status. Green means enabled and actively bidding. Yellow is paused. Gray is removed.

Match type strategy matters: Exact match gives you the most control but limits reach. Broad match casts a wide net but can match irrelevant queries. Phrase is the middle ground.

This answers: How are keywords distributed? Are too many paused or removed? And is your match type mix balanced between control and reach?

google-overview:network_performance.title: Network Performance
google-overview:network_performance.formula: Spend and ROAS broken down by Google ad network (Search, Shopping, YouTube, Display, Gmail, Search Partners) over the selected period
google-overview:network_performance.formula_explanation: Shows spend distribution across Google's advertising networks: Search, Shopping, YouTube, Display, Gmail, and Search Partners. Different networks serve different purposes and typically have different ROAS profiles.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher spend on that network
Search and Shopping typically have highest intent and ROAS
YouTube and Display are awareness-focused with different efficiency expectations
Compare ROAS badges across networks to understand relative efficiency
Click any bar to filter campaigns by network type
decisions_answered: How is spend distributed across Google's ad networks?
Which network delivers the best return on investment?
Are we over-investing in low-ROAS networks?
Should we shift budget between Search and Display?
google-overview:network_performance.narration_text:
This chart shows how your Google Ads budget is distributed across ad networks like Search, Shopping, YouTube, and Display.

Each network serves a different purpose. Search captures high-intent users actively looking for products. Display and YouTube build awareness. Shopping shows product listings directly.

Compare the ROAS badges carefully. Search typically has highest ROAS because users have purchase intent. If YouTube or Display have surprisingly high ROAS, that's a signal to scale. If they're low but spending heavily, reconsider allocation.

This helps answer: Which network is most efficient? And should you shift budget toward higher-performing networks?

google-pmax:campaign_spend_trend.title: Campaign Spend Trend
google-pmax:campaign_spend_trend.formula: Daily spend for the top 5 Performance Max campaigns by spend, one line per campaign, over the selected period
google-pmax:campaign_spend_trend.formula_explanation: Shows daily spend for the top 5 Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range. Each campaign is a separate colored line, allowing you to see how budget distributes across campaigns day by day.
how_to_read: Each colored line represents one campaign — the legend shows which color maps to which campaign
Click legend items to hide/show individual campaigns for focused comparison
Higher lines indicate campaigns consuming more daily budget
Overlapping lines mean campaigns spend similar amounts on those days
Hover over any date to see exact spend, revenue, clicks, and conversions for each visible campaign
decisions_answered: Which campaign is consuming the most budget day to day?
Are any campaigns spiking or dropping unexpectedly?
How does spend distribution across campaigns change over time?
Should budget be reallocated between campaigns based on trends?
google-pmax:campaign_spend_trend.narration_text:
This chart shows daily spend for your top 5 Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range.

Each colored line represents a different campaign. The legend above the chart shows which color maps to which campaign name. You can click legend items to hide or show individual campaigns.

When reading this chart, look for which campaigns dominate spend. If one campaign line is consistently much higher than others, that campaign is consuming most of your budget.

Watch for sudden spikes or drops — a campaign suddenly increasing spend might indicate a bidding change or new asset group. A sudden drop could mean the campaign was paused or budget was capped.

This complements the Campaign Performance table, which shows aggregate totals. This chart answers the time dimension: How is each campaign trending day by day?

formula_components.name: Daily Spend per Campaign
formula_components.formula: Spend summed per campaign per day

formula_components.name: Top 5
formula_components.formula: The 5 campaigns with the highest total spend in the period

google-pmax:campaign_table.title: Campaign Performance
google-pmax:campaign_table.formula: One row per Performance Max campaign (top 50 by spend) with status, bidding strategy, spend, clicks, impressions, conversions, conversion value, ROAS and CPA over the selected period
google-pmax:campaign_table.formula_explanation: Displays all Performance Max campaigns with key metrics over the last 30 days. Includes campaign status, bidding strategy, budget, spend, clicks, conversions, conversion value, ROAS, and CPA. Color-coded for quick identification of high and low performers.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank campaigns on that metric
Green ROAS values (>=10x) indicate excellent performance
Yellow ROAS (6-10x) is good, red (<6x) needs attention
Low CPA (green) is better - means cheaper customer acquisition
Check Optimization Score column for Google's health assessment
decisions_answered: Which PMAX campaigns are top performers?
Which campaigns need optimization or budget changes?
How do different bidding strategies perform?
Are any campaigns wasting budget with poor ROAS?
google-pmax:campaign_table.narration_text:
This table shows all your Performance Max campaigns with comprehensive metrics.

Key formulas: ROAS is conversion value divided by spend. CPA is spend divided by conversions.

To read this table effectively: click any column header to sort. Green values indicate strong performance - high ROAS above 10x, low CPA below 5 dollars. Red flags struggling campaigns.

Look at the bidding strategy column - compare how Maximize Conversion Value performs versus Maximize Conversions. Check the Optimization Score for Google's own health assessment.

This answers critical questions: Which campaigns are winners? Which need attention? Should you reallocate budget from poor to strong performers? Are certain bidding strategies outperforming others?

google-pmax:device_breakdown.title: Device Breakdown
google-pmax:device_breakdown.formula: Performance Max spend, ROAS and CPC broken down by device type (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, Connected TV) over the selected period
google-pmax:device_breakdown.formula_explanation: Aggregates Performance Max spend and conversions by device type (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, Connected TV). Each device contributes differently to overall performance based on user behavior patterns.
how_to_read: Bar length represents spend allocation - longer bars mean more budget goes to that device
Mobile typically dominates spend due to higher user volume
Hover to see ROAS and CPA for each device - efficiency can vary significantly
Desktop often has higher ROAS despite lower spend due to higher conversion intent
Connected TV spend is typically smaller but growing for brand awareness
decisions_answered: How is PMAX budget distributed across devices?
Which device delivers the best ROAS efficiency?
Should we adjust device targeting based on performance?
Is mobile spend proportional to mobile conversions?
google-pmax:device_breakdown.narration_text:
This chart breaks down your Performance Max spend and performance by device type.

The formula groups all campaign stats by device - Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV - then calculates spend and ROAS for each.

Reading this chart: longer bars mean more spend on that device. Mobile usually dominates because that's where most users are. But don't confuse volume with efficiency.

Hover over each bar to see the full picture - ROAS, CPA, and conversion rate. Desktop often shows higher ROAS despite lower spend because desktop users tend to convert with higher intent.

Use this to answer: Which device is most efficient? Is mobile spend justified by its returns? Should you adjust any device targeting?

google-pmax:performance_metrics.title: Performance Metrics
google-pmax:performance_metrics.formula: Daily spend, revenue, clicks and conversions for Performance Max campaigns over the selected period (spend and revenue on the currency axis, clicks and conversions on the count axis)
google-pmax:performance_metrics.formula_explanation: Tracks four key volume metrics for Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range. Spend and Revenue share the left (currency) axis, while Clicks and Conversions share the right (count) axis. This complements the Performance Trend chart which focuses on efficiency (Spend + ROAS).
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily spend — the amount invested each day
Purple line shows daily revenue (conversion value) — higher is better
Amber line shows daily clicks — traffic volume indicator
Green line shows daily conversions — purchase/goal completions
Left axis ($) scales Spend and Revenue; right axis scales Clicks and Conversions
Hover over any date to see exact values for all four metrics
decisions_answered: How do spend and revenue track together over time?
Are clicks converting into actual purchases?
Which days or periods show the highest volume?
Is there a gap between traffic (clicks) and outcomes (conversions)?
google-pmax:performance_metrics.narration_text:
This chart tracks four key volume metrics for your Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range.

The blue line shows your daily spend - how much you're investing each day. The purple line shows daily revenue from conversions. These share the left dollar axis.

The amber line tracks daily clicks - your traffic volume. The green line shows conversions - actual purchases or goal completions. These share the right count axis.

When reading this chart, look for how spend and revenue move together. If revenue consistently exceeds spend, your campaigns are profitable. Watch for days where clicks spike but conversions don't follow - that might indicate landing page issues.

This complements the Performance Trend chart above, which shows efficiency (ROAS). This chart answers volume questions: How much am I spending? How much am I earning? How much traffic and how many conversions?

google-search:campaign_performance.title: Campaign Performance
google-search:campaign_performance.formula: One row per Search campaign (top 50 by spend) with spend, clicks, impressions, conversions, revenue, ROAS, CTR and conversion rate over the selected period
google-search:campaign_performance.formula_explanation: Comprehensive performance table for all Search campaigns. Shows spend, engagement metrics (clicks, CTR), efficiency metrics (CPC, conversion rate), and return metrics (conversions, ROAS). Color-coded thresholds highlight performance status.
how_to_read: Rows are sortable by any column - default sort is by spend descending
Status column shows campaign state: green=enabled, gray=paused, red=removed
ROAS column is color-coded: green > 12x, yellow 5-12x, red < 5x
CTR column is color-coded: green > 5%, yellow 2-5%, red < 2%
Click column headers to sort; use search to filter by campaign name
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are driving the most value?
Which campaigns have concerning ROAS that needs investigation?
How do Brand vs Non-Brand campaigns compare in efficiency?
Which campaigns should receive more or less budget?
google-search:campaign_performance.narration_text:
This table shows comprehensive performance metrics for all Search campaigns.

Key formulas: CTR is clicks divided by impressions times 100. CPC is spend divided by clicks. Conversion rate is conversions divided by clicks times 100. ROAS is revenue divided by spend.

The table is sortable by any column - default is spend descending. Color coding helps quickly identify status: ROAS above 12x is green, 5-12x is yellow, below 5x is red and needs attention.

Use this to answer: Which campaigns drive the most value? Which have concerning ROAS? How do Brand vs Non-Brand compare? This informs budget allocation decisions.

google-search:device_performance.title: Device Performance
google-search:device_performance.formula: Spend, ROAS and CPC broken down by device type for Search campaigns over the selected period
google-search:device_performance.formula_explanation: Breaks down Search campaign performance by device type (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet). Shows spend allocation and efficiency across devices to identify where ads perform best.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show spend amount per device type
Bar length indicates relative spend - longer bars mean more investment
Blue = Mobile, Green = Desktop, Purple = Tablet
Hover to see ROAS for each device type
Compare bar lengths to understand device budget allocation
decisions_answered: Which devices receive the most Search budget?
How does ROAS compare across mobile vs desktop?
Should we apply device bid adjustments?
Are there opportunities to shift budget to higher-performing devices?
google-search:device_performance.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows how Search spend is distributed across device types.

The formula calculates spend per device and ROAS for each. Mobile, Desktop, and Tablet are tracked separately.

Bar length indicates spend amount - longer bars mean more budget allocated. Colors distinguish devices: blue for mobile, green for desktop, purple for tablet. Hover for ROAS values.

This helps you understand: Which devices get the most budget? How does mobile ROAS compare to desktop? Should you adjust device bids? Use this to optimize budget allocation across devices for better returns.

google-search:quality_score_distribution.title: Quality Score Distribution
google-search:quality_score_distribution.formula: Count of distinct Search keywords at each Quality Score from 1 to 10 (keywords with no Quality Score excluded)
google-search:quality_score_distribution.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of Quality Scores across all active keywords. Quality Score (1-10) is Google's rating of keyword relevance, expected CTR, and landing page experience. Higher scores lead to lower CPCs and better ad positions.
how_to_read: X-axis shows Quality Score values from 1 to 10
Y-axis shows the count of keywords at each score level
Red bars (1-4) indicate poor-performing keywords needing attention
Yellow bars (5-6) show average keywords with improvement potential
Green bars (7-10) represent well-optimized keywords
decisions_answered: What is the overall health of our keyword portfolio?
How many keywords need Quality Score improvement?
Are we meeting the target benchmark of 7+ average QS?
Which score ranges have the most keywords to optimize?
google-search:quality_score_distribution.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how your keywords are distributed across Quality Score levels from 1 to 10.

Quality Score is Google's rating of keyword health based on expected CTR, ad relevance, and landing page experience. Higher scores mean lower CPCs and better positions.

Color coding helps interpretation: Red bars for scores 1-4 need immediate attention. Yellow for 5-6 have improvement potential. Green for 7-10 are well optimized. The target benchmark is 7 or higher.

Use this to answer: What's the health of your keyword portfolio? How many keywords need improvement? This guides where to focus optimization efforts.

google-search:top_keywords.title: Top Keywords
google-search:top_keywords.formula: Top 100 Search keywords by spend, each with match type, Quality Score, spend, impressions, clicks, conversions, CPC and CTR
google-search:top_keywords.formula_explanation: Lists top-performing keywords by spend with performance metrics. Quality Score column shows Google's relevance rating. Match type badges indicate targeting precision. Use this to identify high-value keywords and optimization opportunities.
how_to_read: Keywords are sorted by spend descending by default
Quality Score column shows 1-10 rating with color coding
Match Type badges: BROAD (blue), PHRASE (purple), EXACT (green)
Use filters to focus on specific QS ranges or match types
Click any row to see search terms that triggered this keyword
decisions_answered: Which keywords consume the most budget?
Are high-spend keywords delivering proportional returns?
Which keywords have low Quality Scores that need improvement?
What is the conversion rate by match type for top keywords?
google-search:top_keywords.narration_text:
This table displays your top-performing keywords ranked by spend.

The formulas: CTR is clicks divided by impressions times 100. CPC is spend divided by clicks. Quality Score is Google's 1-10 rating shown with color coding.

To read this table: keywords are sorted by spend. Quality Score colors range from red for poor to green for excellent. Match type badges show targeting precision.

This helps answer: Which keywords consume the most budget? Are high-spend keywords delivering returns? Which need Quality Score improvement? Use this to prioritize keyword optimization efforts.

google-shopping:campaign_spend_trend.title: Campaign Spend Trend
google-shopping:campaign_spend_trend.formula: Daily spend for the top 5 Shopping campaigns by spend, one line per campaign, over the selected period
google-shopping:campaign_spend_trend.formula_explanation: This multi-line chart tracks daily spend for the top 5 Shopping campaigns over the selected date range. Each campaign is a separate line, making it easy to compare spend patterns and identify which campaigns dominate budget allocation day by day.
how_to_read: Each colored line represents a different campaign — top spender is listed first
Click legend items to hide/show individual campaigns
Hover over any date to see spend, revenue, conversions, and ROAS for all campaigns
Steep upward trends may indicate scaling or bid increases
Flat or declining lines suggest budget caps or reduced competition
decisions_answered: Which campaigns consume the most budget over time?
Are any campaigns showing sudden spend changes that need investigation?
How does spend distribution shift across campaigns during the period?
Should budget be reallocated from lower to higher performing campaigns?
google-shopping:campaign_spend_trend.narration_text:
This chart tracks daily spend for your top 5 Shopping campaigns over the selected date range.

Each colored line represents a different campaign, ordered by total spend. The top spender gets the first color.

Hover over any date to see a detailed breakdown: spend, revenue, conversions, and ROAS for each campaign on that day.

You can click legend items to hide or show specific campaigns. This helps isolate trends for individual campaigns.

Look for patterns: Consistent lines mean stable spend. Sudden drops might mean budget caps were hit. Rising lines indicate scaling.

This answers: Where is your budget going day by day? Are campaigns spending consistently? Should you reallocate budget from low performers to high performers?

formula_components.name: Daily Campaign Spend
formula_components.formula: Spend summed per campaign per day

formula_components.name: Top 5
formula_components.formula: The 5 campaigns with the highest total spend in the period

google-shopping:device_performance.title: Spend by Device
google-shopping:device_performance.formula: Shopping spend split by device type (Desktop, Mobile, Tablet, Connected TV) over the selected period, with ROAS per device
google-shopping:device_performance.formula_explanation: This donut chart shows how Shopping campaign spend is distributed across device types (Desktop, Mobile, Tablet, Connected TV). The total spend is shown in the subtitle.
how_to_read: Slice size shows relative spend on each device type
Larger slices indicate where most of your budget is going
Green (Mobile) often has highest volume in Shopping campaigns
Blue (Desktop) typically shows higher purchase intent
Hover over slices to see exact spend amounts
decisions_answered: Where is most of the Shopping budget being spent?
Should we adjust device bid modifiers?
Are we allocating budget appropriately across devices?
Which devices should we prioritize for Shopping campaigns?
google-shopping:device_performance.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your Shopping campaign spend breaks down by device.

The formula calculates each device's share of total spend as a percentage.

Look at slice sizes to see where your budget goes. Mobile often dominates volume in Shopping. Desktop users typically have higher purchase intent.

The subtitle shows your total spend. Hover over any slice for exact amounts.

Use this to decide: Should you adjust device bids? Where is budget concentrated? Are you missing opportunities on certain devices?

google-shopping:shopping_performance_met.title: Performance Metrics
google-shopping:shopping_performance_met.formula: Daily spend, revenue, clicks and conversions for Shopping campaigns over the selected period (spend and revenue on the currency axis, clicks and conversions on the count axis)
google-shopping:shopping_performance_met.formula_explanation: Tracks four key volume metrics for Shopping campaigns over the selected date range. Spend and Revenue share the left (currency) axis, while Clicks and Conversions share the right (count) axis. This complements the Performance Trend chart which focuses on efficiency (Spend + ROAS).
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily spend — the amount invested each day
Purple line shows daily revenue (conversion value) — higher is better
Amber line shows daily clicks — traffic volume indicator
Green line shows daily conversions — purchase/goal completions
Left axis ($) scales Spend and Revenue; right axis scales Clicks and Conversions
Hover over any date to see exact values for all four metrics
decisions_answered: How do spend and revenue track together over time?
Are clicks converting into actual purchases?
Which days or periods show the highest volume?
Is there a gap between traffic (clicks) and outcomes (conversions)?
google-shopping:shopping_performance_met.narration_text:
This chart tracks four key volume metrics for your Shopping campaigns over the selected date range.

The blue line shows your daily spend - how much you're investing each day. The purple line shows daily revenue from conversions. These share the left dollar axis.

The amber line tracks daily clicks - your traffic volume. The green line shows conversions - actual purchases or goal completions. These share the right count axis.

When reading this chart, look for how spend and revenue move together. If revenue consistently exceeds spend, your campaigns are profitable. Watch for days where clicks spike but conversions don't follow - that might indicate landing page issues.

This complements the Performance Trend chart above, which shows efficiency (ROAS). This chart answers volume questions: How much am I spending? How much am I earning? How much traffic and how many conversions?

google-shopping:shopping_performance_tre.title: Shopping Performance Trend
google-shopping:shopping_performance_tre.formula: Daily spend and ROAS for Shopping campaigns over the selected period (dual-axis line chart)
google-shopping:shopping_performance_tre.formula_explanation: This dual-axis line chart shows daily spend (left axis) and ROAS (right axis) for Shopping campaigns over time. It helps visualize performance trends and identify patterns in campaign efficiency.
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily spend - look for spending patterns and anomalies
Green line shows ROAS - ideally stable or trending upward
Left Y-axis shows spend in dollars, right Y-axis shows ROAS multiplier
Hover over any point to see exact values for that day
Look for correlation: does higher spend maintain good ROAS?
decisions_answered: Is Shopping campaign performance improving or declining over time?
Are there specific days or periods with exceptional performance?
Does scaling spend negatively impact ROAS?
What is the optimal daily spend level for maintaining target ROAS?
google-shopping:shopping_performance_tre.narration_text:
This trend chart shows daily performance for your Shopping campaigns with a dual-axis view.

The blue line tracks daily spend on the left axis. The green line shows ROAS on the right axis.

Look for patterns: Are spend and ROAS moving together or inversely? Stable ROAS while spend increases means you're scaling efficiently.

Watch for anomalies - sudden drops in ROAS or spikes in spend that didn't deliver results.

This answers: Is performance trending up or down? What days perform best? Can you scale spend while maintaining ROAS?

google-youtube:campaign_performance.title: Campaign Performance
google-youtube:campaign_performance.formula: One row per video campaign (top 50 by spend) with video format, views, view rate, spend, cost per view, conversions and ROAS over the selected period
google-youtube:campaign_performance.formula_explanation: This table provides a comprehensive breakdown of all video campaigns with key metrics. Each row represents a campaign with its video format type, views, view rate, spend, cost per view, conversions, and return on ad spend.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank campaigns by that metric (click column header)
Green ROAS values indicate campaigns meeting targets (above 3.0x)
Red CPV values indicate campaigns with higher than average view costs
Campaign subtype column shows video format (Action, Reach, Bumper, etc.)
Click any row to navigate to detailed campaign analysis
decisions_answered: Which video campaigns are our top performers?
Which campaigns should receive increased budget based on ROAS?
Are there campaigns with high views but low conversions that need optimization?
How do different video formats compare in efficiency?
google-youtube:campaign_performance.narration_text:
The campaign performance table gives you a complete view of all your video campaigns with key metrics in one place.

Standard formulas apply here: View rate is views divided by impressions, CPV is spend divided by views, and ROAS is conversion value divided by spend. Each row represents one campaign.

To read this table effectively, use the sort functionality. Click any column header to rank campaigns by that metric. Color coding helps quickly identify performance: green indicates strong ROAS, while red flags high CPV.

This table answers the critical allocation questions: Which campaigns deserve more budget? Which are underperforming? And how do different video formats compare? Use this as your go-to reference for campaign-level optimization decisions.

google-youtube:device_performance.title: Performance by Device
google-youtube:device_performance.formula: Video views and view rate broken down by device type (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, Connected TV) over the selected period
google-youtube:device_performance.formula_explanation: This horizontal bar chart breaks down video performance by device type: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV. Each bar represents total video views on that device, while the color intensity can indicate view rate efficiency.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate more video views from that device type
Compare bar lengths to understand where your audience watches videos
Mobile typically dominates YouTube viewing, but Connected TV is growing rapidly
Consider device-specific creative optimizations based on viewing patterns
Click any bar to filter all page metrics by that device type
decisions_answered: Which devices generate the most video views for our campaigns?
Should we create device-specific video creatives?
Is our Connected TV presence growing compared to mobile?
How should we adjust bidding strategies by device?
google-youtube:device_performance.narration_text:
This device performance chart reveals where your audience is actually watching your video ads, which is crucial for creative and bidding optimization.

The formula calculates view rate and share for each device category: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV. Each bar length represents total video views from that device.

When reading this chart, pay attention to which device dominates. Mobile typically leads for YouTube, but Connected TV is the fastest-growing segment. Longer bars mean more views from that device.

Understanding device distribution helps answer key questions: Where should you focus creative efforts? Should you bid differently by device? And is your Connected TV strategy keeping pace with market trends? Use these insights to optimize your video creative and targeting strategy.

google-youtube:hour_day_heatmap.title: View Rate by Hour and Day
google-youtube:hour_day_heatmap.formula: Average video view rate for each hour-of-day and day-of-week combination, from the rolling 30-day pre-aggregated YouTube data
google-youtube:hour_day_heatmap.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows video view rate across all combinations of hour of day (0-23) and day of week. Darker cells indicate higher view rates, revealing optimal scheduling patterns for video campaigns.
how_to_read: X-axis shows hour of day from midnight (00) to 11 PM (23)
Y-axis shows days of the week from Monday to Sunday
Darker/warmer colors indicate higher view rates - better performance
Lighter/cooler colors indicate lower view rates - less engagement
Look for clusters of high-performing time slots to inform ad scheduling
decisions_answered: What are the best hours and days to run video ads?
Should we use dayparting to focus budget on high-performing time slots?
Are weekends or weekdays better for video engagement?
How does evening performance compare to daytime?
google-youtube:hour_day_heatmap.narration_text:
The hour-day heatmap reveals hidden patterns in when your video ads perform best, which is essential for ad scheduling optimization.

The calculation is simple: each cell shows the average view rate for that specific hour and day combination. Colors range from cool to warm based on performance intensity.

Reading this heatmap, look for clusters of dark cells - these are your golden time slots. Common patterns include evening peaks, weekend variations, and lunch hour bumps. Light areas show times when viewers are less engaged.

This answers scheduling questions: When should you concentrate your video budget? Should you use dayparting? Are weekends worth the higher CPMs? Use this data to create custom ad schedules that maximize your view rate and efficiency.

formula_components.name: Cell Value
formula_components.formula: Average view rate (video views ÷ impressions × 100) for that hour and weekday

google-youtube:network_performance.title: Views by Network
google-youtube:network_performance.formula: Video views distributed across Google's video networks (YouTube Watch, YouTube Search, Video Partners, Google TV) over the selected period
google-youtube:network_performance.formula_explanation: This donut chart shows how your video views are distributed across Google's video networks: YouTube Watch, YouTube Search, Video Partners, and Google TV. The center displays total views across all networks.
how_to_read: Larger slices indicate networks generating more video views
Center value shows total video views across all networks
YouTube Watch (in-stream) typically dominates video inventory
Video Partners extends reach beyond YouTube to partner sites
Hover over any slice to see exact view count and percentage share
decisions_answered: Which video networks drive the most views for our campaigns?
Should we expand to Video Partners network for more reach?
How does YouTube Search discovery perform compared to in-stream?
Is our network distribution aligned with our brand safety requirements?
google-youtube:network_performance.narration_text:
This network distribution chart shows where your video ads are actually being viewed across Google's video ecosystem.

The formula is straightforward: each network's views divided by total views gives you the percentage share. The donut visualization makes it easy to see relative proportions at a glance.

When reading this chart, note that YouTube Watch typically dominates - that's in-stream ads on YouTube videos. YouTube Search shows discovery ads. Video Partners extends your reach to Google's network of partner sites. The center value gives you total views.

Understanding network distribution helps answer strategic questions: Should you expand to Video Partners for more reach? Is YouTube Search worth the investment? And is your distribution aligned with brand safety requirements? Use this to inform network targeting decisions.

formula_components.name: Network Share
formula_components.formula: Network video views ÷ total video views × 100

google-youtube:performance_trend.title: Video Performance Trend
google-youtube:performance_trend.formula: Daily video views, view rate, cost per view and ROAS for video campaigns over the selected period (area chart of views)
google-youtube:performance_trend.formula_explanation: This chart tracks three key video metrics over a 12-week period. View Rate measures the percentage of impressions that resulted in video views. Cost Per View shows your average spend for each video view. ROAS indicates the revenue generated per dollar spent on video advertising.
how_to_read: The blue area shows total video views over time - higher peaks indicate weeks with more video engagement
Track the shape of the trend to identify growth periods, declines, or seasonal patterns
Steep drops may indicate budget changes, audience fatigue, or competitive pressures
Compare peaks to campaign launches or promotional periods to understand what drives video views
Click any data point to drill down into that specific week's daily performance
decisions_answered: Are our video campaigns gaining or losing momentum over time?
Which weeks showed the strongest video performance and why?
Is there a seasonal pattern to our video engagement?
When should we increase video ad spend based on historical performance?
google-youtube:performance_trend.narration_text:
This trend chart tracks your video campaign performance over a 12-week period, giving you a clear view of how your YouTube and video ads are performing over time.

The area chart displays total video views week by week. The formula behind this is straightforward: impressions multiplied by view rate equals views, while cost per view is simply your spend divided by views.

To read this chart effectively, look at the overall shape of the trend. Rising areas indicate growing engagement, while dips might signal budget changes or audience fatigue. Pay attention to peaks and correlate them with your campaign launches.

This visualization helps answer critical questions: Are your video campaigns gaining momentum? Which weeks performed best? And is there a seasonal pattern you can leverage for future planning?

google-youtube:quartile_completion.title: Video Completion Funnel
google-youtube:quartile_completion.formula: Video completion funnel showing the share of viewers reaching 25%, 50%, 75% and 100% of the video, with the drop-off between stages, weighted by video views
google-youtube:quartile_completion.formula_explanation: The funnel visualizes viewer retention through four quartiles of video playback. Starting from initial views, it shows what percentage of viewers reach 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% completion. The drop-off between stages reveals where viewers lose interest.
how_to_read: Top of funnel represents all video views (100% starting point)
Each subsequent level shows the percentage of viewers who reached that quartile
Width of each stage is proportional to the viewer percentage retained
The gap between stages indicates drop-off - larger gaps mean more viewers leaving
The bottom level shows your completion rate - viewers who watched the entire video
decisions_answered: At what point in our videos do most viewers drop off?
Is our video content engaging enough to keep viewers watching?
Should we shorten our video ads based on completion patterns?
Which video formats (skippable, non-skippable, bumper) retain viewers best?
google-youtube:quartile_completion.narration_text:
The video completion funnel is one of the most insightful charts for understanding viewer engagement with your video content.

The formula here measures quartile retention: what percentage of viewers reach each 25% milestone in your video. Drop-off rate is calculated as the difference between early and late quartiles, divided by the starting point.

Reading this funnel is intuitive. The top represents all initial views. As you move down, each level shows how many viewers reached that point. A steep narrowing indicates high drop-off early in videos, while a gradual taper suggests strong engagement.

This answers essential questions: Where do viewers lose interest? Are videos too long? Should you front-load key messages? Understanding this funnel helps optimize video creative to maximize completed views.

formula_components.name: Quartile Rate
formula_components.formula: Viewers reaching that quartile ÷ total video views × 100

inventory:category_health_matrix.title: Category Health Matrix
inventory:category_health_matrix.formula: Per level-4 category (cart adds > 100): conversion % = purchased ÷ cart adds × 100, count of likely out-of-stock items, and est. lost revenue = (benchmark conversion − actual conversion) × cart adds × avg price; status from conversion (CRITICAL <10%, WARNING <15%, MONITOR <25%, else HEALTHY)
inventory:category_health_matrix.formula_explanation: Aggregates inventory health metrics by product category. Combines conversion rates, out-of-stock item counts, and estimated lost revenue to provide a comprehensive category-level view of availability status.
how_to_read: Status badges show category health at a glance - red needs immediate attention
OOS Items column shows count of stockout items within each category
Conversion % relative to benchmark indicates overall category health
Est. Lost Revenue quantifies the business impact
Click on a category row to see specific items within it
Sort by any metric to prioritize your category review
decisions_answered: Which product categories have the worst inventory health?
How many items are out of stock in each category?
Which categories are underperforming on conversion?
What is the total revenue impact by category?
inventory:category_health_matrix.narration_text:
This table provides a category-level health check - your strategic inventory dashboard.

The formula aggregates multiple signals per category: conversion rate, out of stock count, and lost revenue. The Status badge summarizes the situation - Critical means under 10% conversion.

Reading this table: Start with red Critical badges. These categories have systemic availability issues. The OOS Items count shows how many products need attention. Lost Revenue shows the business impact.

Categories in Monitor or Warning are at risk of becoming Critical. Keep these on your radar for proactive management.

Key questions answered: Which categories need immediate inventory attention? How is availability affecting each product line? Where should purchasing prioritize? Use this for strategic inventory planning across your catalog.

formula_components.name: Estimated lost revenue
formula_components.formula: (benchmark conversion − actual conversion) × cart adds × avg price

inventory:daily_funnel_trend.title: Daily Funnel Trend
inventory:daily_funnel_trend.formula: Daily funnel volumes from GA4 ecommerce: views, add-to-cart (cart adds capped below 10000), and purchases, plus revenue; view-to-cart % = cart adds ÷ views × 100 and cart-to-purchase % = purchases ÷ cart adds × 100 (remove-from-cart and begin-checkout hardcoded to 0)
inventory:daily_funnel_trend.formula_explanation: Aggregates event counts from GA4 Events Report, grouped by date. Each line shows the daily volume at each funnel stage: item views, cart additions, and purchases. The gap between lines represents drop-off at each stage.
how_to_read: Three stacked areas show View, Add to Cart, and Purchase volumes
The purple area (Add to Cart) relative to blue (Views) shows cart intent
The green area (Purchases) relative to purple shows fulfillment success
A widening gap between cart and purchase suggests inventory issues
Look for sudden drops in purchase volume while cart stays high - stockout signal
Hover over any date to see exact values for all three metrics
decisions_answered: Are customers adding items to cart but not completing purchases?
Which days show unusual gaps between cart activity and purchases?
Is the overall funnel health improving or degrading over time?
Are there specific days where potential stockouts are evident?
inventory:daily_funnel_trend.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your daily shopping funnel - from item views through cart additions to purchases.

The formula tracks three event counts from GA4. Views in blue show customer interest. Add to cart in purple shows purchase intent. Purchases in green show fulfillment.

Reading this chart: The key insight is the gap between purple and green. When cart adds stay high but purchases drop, customers want to buy but cannot. This is your stockout signal.

Look for days where the green line suddenly drops while purple stays flat or rises. These are likely stockout events costing you revenue.

Key questions answered: Is inventory limiting sales? Which days show the biggest fulfillment gaps? Is the problem getting better or worse? Use this to prioritize restocking and monitor recovery.

inventory:items_stopped_selling.title: Items That Stopped Selling
inventory:items_stopped_selling.formula: Items previously selling well (>20 units 4 weeks ago) compared across this week, last week and 4 weeks ago over a 60-day window; trend status = stopped selling (this week = 0), major decline (this week < 25% of 4-weeks-ago) or declining (this week < 50% of 4-weeks-ago), with 60-day revenue
inventory:items_stopped_selling.formula_explanation: Compares weekly purchase volumes across a 60-day window. Items that were previously selling well (>20 units 4 weeks ago) but have now stopped or dramatically declined are flagged as potential inventory issues.
how_to_read: Compare the three week columns left to right for trend direction
Zero in "This Week" with high "4 Weeks Ago" indicates stopped selling
Red status badges highlight the most severe declines
Total 60-day revenue shows the item's importance to your business
Sort by any column to find patterns - try sorting by revenue
Items here were successful - the decline is likely availability, not demand
decisions_answered: Which previously successful products have suddenly stopped selling?
Are we losing revenue from items that were performing well?
Which products may have run out of inventory recently?
What is the revenue impact of products that stopped selling?
inventory:items_stopped_selling.narration_text:
This table catches gradual stockouts - items that were selling well but have stopped.

The formula compares weekly sales over 60 days. Items selling 20 plus units four weeks ago but zero now are flagged Stopped Selling. Those below 25 percent of prior volume are Major Decline.

Reading this table: The 60-day revenue column shows what you are losing. A 500 dollar item that stopped selling is costing you roughly 250 per month.

The pattern matters. Zero this week after steady sales is inventory. A gradual decline might be seasonality or trend. But for proven sellers, sudden stops are almost always stock issues.

Key questions answered: What popular items have we run out of? How much historical revenue is at risk? What items need urgent inventory review? This is your early warning system.

inventory:lost_revenue_by_category.title: Lost Revenue by Category
inventory:lost_revenue_by_category.formula: Per level-4 category: lost revenue = (benchmark cart-to-purchase conversion − actual conversion) × cart adds × avg price, where benchmark = average conversion of categories with >100 cart adds and purchases; sorted descending, top 10
inventory:lost_revenue_by_category.formula_explanation: Calculates lost revenue by comparing each category's cart-to-purchase conversion against the benchmark. The gap multiplied by cart volume gives lost units, then multiplied by average price gives lost revenue opportunity.
how_to_read: Each horizontal bar represents a product category
Bar length shows estimated lost revenue from below-benchmark conversion
Categories are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities at top
Longer bars indicate categories with more recovery potential
Click on a category bar to drill down to specific items
The color gradient intensifies with higher lost revenue amounts
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most revenue recovery potential?
Where should inventory restocking efforts be prioritized?
Which categories are underperforming against the benchmark?
How much revenue could be recovered by fixing availability issues?
inventory:lost_revenue_by_category.narration_text:
This bar chart shows estimated lost revenue by product category - your restock priority list.

The formula calculates a benchmark conversion from healthy items, then measures how much each category falls short. The gap times cart volume times average price equals lost revenue.

Reading this chart: Start from the top. These categories have the biggest recovery potential. A 50 thousand dollar bar means if you fixed availability, you could capture that revenue.

The calculation is conservative - actual lost revenue may be higher if stockouts are driving customers away entirely.

Key questions answered: Which categories need restocking first? How much is each category costing you? Where will inventory investment have the biggest return? Use this to prioritize purchasing decisions.

formula_components.name: Lost revenue
formula_components.formula: (benchmark conversion − actual conversion) × cart adds × avg price

inventory:low_conversion_items.title: Low Conversion Items
