inventory:low_conversion_items.formula: Items with cart adds > 100, purchases > 0, and conversion below half the benchmark: conversion % = purchased ÷ cart adds × 100, benchmark % = average conversion of items with >50 cart adds and purchases, gap % = benchmark − conversion; ranked by revenue, top 25
inventory:low_conversion_items.formula_explanation: Compares each item's cart-to-purchase conversion rate against the healthy benchmark. Items with significant cart activity but below-benchmark conversion indicate potential availability issues - customers add to cart but cannot complete purchase at normal rates.
how_to_read: Actual % vs Benchmark % shows how far below normal the item converts
Large gaps suggest inventory constraints limiting purchases
Estimated Lost Revenue quantifies the opportunity cost
Items are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities first
Red highlighting on Est. Lost Revenue emphasizes the impact
Click any row to investigate the specific item further
decisions_answered: Which items have abnormally low cart-to-purchase conversion?
How much revenue are we losing from conversion underperformance?
Which items might have inventory issues causing low conversion?
Where should we investigate potential stockouts or limited inventory?
inventory:low_conversion_items.narration_text:
This table identifies items converting below benchmark - potential low stock situations.

The formula compares each item's cart to purchase rate against the average of healthy items. When customers add to cart at normal rates but purchase at lower rates, something is blocking completion.

Reading this table: The gap between Actual and Benchmark tells the story. A 5% actual versus 27% benchmark means customers want to buy but 80% are blocked. That is often limited inventory.

Est. Lost Revenue shows the stakes. This is calculated conservatively - the real impact may be higher if frustrated customers leave entirely.

Key questions answered: Which items have suspicious conversion gaps? How much revenue is at risk? Where should we check inventory levels? Use this to find items with limited stock before they fully stock out.

inventory:out_of_stock_alerts.title: Out of Stock Alerts
inventory:out_of_stock_alerts.formula: Items with cart adds > 50 but zero purchases over the period (likely stockouts): views, cart adds, days with activity, and demand score = views × 0.3 + cart adds × 0.7; ranked by cart adds, top 50
inventory:out_of_stock_alerts.formula_explanation: Identifies items with significant cart activity but zero purchases over the past 30 days. These are high-confidence stockouts - customers clearly want these items but cannot complete purchases. The demand score weights cart adds higher than views since cart adds show stronger purchase intent.
how_to_read: Items are sorted by cart adds - highest demand items at top
Zero purchases with high cart adds is a strong stockout indicator
Days Active shows how long the availability issue has persisted
Demand Score combines views and cart adds to prioritize restocking
Use the search to find specific items by name
Click any row to see the item's detailed history
decisions_answered: Which items are definitely out of stock based on customer behavior?
How many customers have tried to buy items we cannot fulfill?
Which out-of-stock items have the highest customer demand?
How long have these availability issues been ongoing?
inventory:out_of_stock_alerts.narration_text:
This table shows your confirmed stockouts - items customers want but cannot buy.

The formula is simple: significant cart activity plus zero purchases equals out of stock. The threshold of 50 cart adds ensures we are seeing real demand, not one-off adds.

Reading this table: Every row is lost revenue. Cart adds of 6 thousand means 6 thousand times customers tried to buy and could not. Multiply by average order value to estimate impact.

Days Active shows persistence. Thirty days of activity means you have been losing sales for a month. The Demand Score helps prioritize - higher scores mean more customer demand.

Key questions answered: What items are definitely out of stock? How much demand are we missing? What should we restock first? This is your urgent action list.

klaviyo-campaigns:engagement_timeline.title: Engagement Timeline
klaviyo-campaigns:engagement_timeline.formula: Unique opens and unique clicks over the first 72 hours after the campaign send, by hour since send (dual-axis line)
klaviyo-campaigns:engagement_timeline.formula_explanation: Tracks unique opens and clicks over the first 72 hours after a campaign is sent. The dual-axis design scales clicks to the opens range so both trends are clearly visible.
how_to_read: The green line shows unique email opens over time, read against the left axis
The purple line shows unique email clicks over time, read against the right axis
The first few hours typically show the steepest engagement curve
Hover over any point to see exact opens and clicks at that hour
A long tail suggests your audience engages over multiple days
decisions_answered: How quickly do recipients engage after send?
What is the peak engagement window for this campaign?
How does the click curve compare to the open curve?
Is engagement front-loaded or spread across 72 hours?
klaviyo-campaigns:engagement_timeline.narration_text:
This chart shows opens and clicks over the first 72 hours after your campaign was sent.

The green line tracks unique opens on the left axis, while the purple line tracks unique clicks on the right axis. Both are scaled for easy comparison.

Look for the steepest part of the curve to identify your peak engagement window. A long tail means your audience keeps engaging over multiple days.

This helps you understand: How quickly do recipients engage? When is peak activity? And how does the click curve compare to opens?

klaviyo-campaigns:historical_comparison.title: Historical Comparison
klaviyo-campaigns:historical_comparison.formula: One row per past campaign with open rate = unique opens ÷ sends × 100, click rate = unique clicks ÷ sends × 100, click-to-open rate = unique clicks ÷ unique opens × 100 and bounce rate = bounces ÷ sends × 100
klaviyo-campaigns:historical_comparison.formula_explanation: Displays recent similar campaigns side by side for benchmarking. Shows key email metrics including open rate, click rate, click-to-open rate, and bounce rate to track performance trends.
how_to_read: Each row represents a past campaign with its key performance metrics
Compare open rates and click rates across campaigns to spot trends
CTOR (click-to-open rate) measures content effectiveness among openers
Bounce rate helps monitor list health over time
Sort by any column to rank campaigns by specific metrics
decisions_answered: How does this campaign compare to recent similar sends?
Are our email metrics trending up or down over time?
Which past campaigns had the best engagement?
Is our list health improving or declining based on bounce rates?
klaviyo-campaigns:historical_comparison.narration_text:
This table shows recent similar campaigns for benchmarking your current campaign's performance.

Each row displays a past campaign with its open rate, click rate, click-to-open rate, and bounce rate. Sort by any column to find top performers.

CTOR, or click-to-open rate, specifically measures how compelling your content is among people who opened.

Use this to answer: How does this campaign compare? Are metrics trending up or down? Which past campaigns performed best? And is list health improving based on bounce rates?

klaviyo-campaigns:segment_breakdown.title: Segment Performance
klaviyo-campaigns:segment_breakdown.formula: Per audience segment, open rate = unique opens ÷ recipients × 100 and click rate = unique clicks ÷ recipients × 100, top 10 segments (horizontal bar)
klaviyo-campaigns:segment_breakdown.formula_explanation: Compares open rates and click rates across different audience segments (such as climate zones) to identify which segments are most engaged with your campaign content.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show open rate (green) and click rate (purple) per segment
Longer bars indicate higher engagement from that segment
Compare the gap between open rate and click rate to assess content relevance
Segments with high open but low click rates may need better content alignment
Top 10 segments are displayed, sorted by performance
decisions_answered: Which audience segments are most engaged?
Are certain segments opening but not clicking?
Should we create segment-specific content for future campaigns?
Which segments should we prioritize for targeting?
klaviyo-campaigns:segment_breakdown.narration_text:
This chart compares performance across your audience segments.

Green bars show open rates and purple bars show click rates for each segment. The top 10 segments are displayed.

Compare the gap between open and click rates. A large gap means people open but don't click, suggesting the content may not resonate with that segment.

Use this to answer: Which segments engage most? Are some opening but not clicking? Should you create segment-specific content? And which segments deserve more targeting?

klaviyo-email:campaign_performance.title: Campaign Performance
klaviyo-email:campaign_performance.formula: One row per campaign with sends, opens, clicks, bounces and unsubscribes, plus open rate = opens ÷ sends × 100, click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100 and click-to-open rate = clicks ÷ opens × 100; most recent send dates first
klaviyo-email:campaign_performance.formula_explanation: This comprehensive table shows all sent campaigns with key performance metrics. Open rate shows inbox impact, click rate shows overall engagement, and CTOR reveals content effectiveness. Together with bounce and unsubscribe data, you can identify both top performers and problem campaigns.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to find top or bottom performers
Green open rates above 50% indicate strong subject lines and deliverability
Green CTOR above 10% indicates compelling content and CTAs
High bounces on a campaign suggest list quality issues with that segment
Click any row to drill into detailed campaign analytics
decisions_answered: Which campaigns performed best and why?
Are there problem campaigns dragging down overall metrics?
Which subject lines and content types drive highest engagement?
What campaigns should we replicate or avoid repeating?
klaviyo-email:campaign_performance.narration_text:
The campaign performance table is your complete view of all sent campaigns.

The key formulas: Open Rate equals opens divided by sends. Click Rate equals clicks divided by sends. CTOR equals clicks divided by opens. Each reveals different aspects of performance.

How to use this table: Sort by any column to surface insights. Green highlighting means strong performance - above 50% open rate, above 10% CTOR. Watch for high bounce or unsubscribe numbers that signal problems.

Questions answered: Which campaigns succeeded? Which failed? What subject lines work? What content drives clicks? Use the export function to analyze patterns in your top performers and avoid repeating the approach of your worst campaigns.

klaviyo-email:content_performance.title: Content Type Performance
klaviyo-email:content_performance.formula: Open rate = opens ÷ sends × 100 and click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100, grouped by content category derived from the campaign name (Promotional, Product Launch, Newsletter, Other), ranked by recipients (bar chart)
klaviyo-email:content_performance.formula_explanation: Groups campaigns by content type or category and calculates average engagement rates for each. This reveals which types of content resonate most with your audience and which topics underperform.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a different content category or campaign type
Higher bars indicate better engagement for that content type
Compare open rate and click rate to understand where content resonates
Low-performing categories may need content strategy changes
Use top performers as templates for future campaigns
decisions_answered: Which content types get the best engagement?
What topics should we create more content about?
Are there content categories we should stop sending?
How should we adjust our content calendar based on performance?
klaviyo-email:content_performance.narration_text:
This chart breaks down engagement rates by campaign content type or category.

The formulas: Open Rate and Click Rate are calculated per content category by averaging across all campaigns of that type.

Reading this: Each bar shows how a particular content type performs. Look for categories that consistently outperform others. These are the topics your audience cares about most.

Key questions answered: What content resonates with your subscribers? Which categories should you double down on? Which should you rethink? Use these insights to shape your content calendar and prioritize the topics that drive the strongest engagement.

klaviyo-email:deliverability_health.title: Deliverability Health
klaviyo-email:deliverability_health.formula: Deliverability score = (1 − (bounces + spam complaints) ÷ sends) × 100, shown on a gauge alongside bounce rate = bounces ÷ sends × 100, spam rate = spam complaints ÷ sends × 100 and unsubscribe rate = unsubscribes ÷ sends × 100
klaviyo-email:deliverability_health.formula_explanation: The deliverability score shows what percentage of your emails successfully reached inboxes. Bounce rate tracks hard and soft bounces. Spam rate measures recipients marking your email as spam. These three metrics together indicate your sender reputation health.
how_to_read: Main gauge shows overall deliverability score - aim for 98%+ (green)
Bounce Rate should stay under 2% to maintain good sender reputation
Spam Rate is critical - keep under 0.1% to avoid blocklisting
Unsubscribe Rate under 0.5% indicates healthy list engagement
Red indicators mean immediate attention required
decisions_answered: Is our sender reputation at risk?
Do we need to clean our email list (high bounces)?
Are recipients marking us as spam (reputation damage)?
Are we sending to engaged subscribers or burning our list?
klaviyo-email:deliverability_health.narration_text:
The deliverability health panel is your sender reputation dashboard.

The main formula: Deliverability Score equals sends minus bounces minus spam complaints, divided by sends, times 100. It tells you what percentage of emails actually reach inboxes.

Reading this panel: The central gauge should be green, above 98%. Below that, you're risking deliverability issues. The sub-metrics matter too: Bounce rate under 2%, spam rate under 0.1%, unsubscribe rate under 0.5%.

Critical questions answered: Is your sender reputation healthy? Do you need list cleaning? Are people marking you as spam? Red indicators mean act now - your ability to reach inboxes is at stake.

klaviyo-email:email_volume_trend.title: Email Volume Trend
klaviyo-email:email_volume_trend.formula: Total emails sent per day over the selected range (area chart), with opens, clicks and revenue available per point
klaviyo-email:email_volume_trend.formula_explanation: Counts all email send events grouped by month to show how email volume fluctuates over time. Each data point represents the total emails sent in that month across all campaigns and flows.
how_to_read: The area chart shows email send volume over the last 24 months
Higher peaks indicate months with more email sends (campaigns, promotions)
Look for seasonal patterns - are there consistent spikes during certain periods?
Declining volume may indicate list shrinkage or reduced campaign frequency
Compare volume to engagement rates to find optimal send frequency
decisions_answered: Are we sending more or fewer emails compared to previous periods?
What months have the highest email volume (seasonal campaigns)?
Is our email frequency sustainable relative to list engagement?
When should we plan major campaigns based on historical patterns?
klaviyo-email:email_volume_trend.narration_text:
This area chart shows your email send volume over the past 24 months.

The formula simply counts all email send events per month. Each point represents total emails sent across all campaigns and automated flows.

To read this chart: Look for patterns in the peaks and valleys. Higher areas mean more emails were sent. Seasonal businesses often see spikes during promotional periods.

Key questions this answers: Are you sending more or fewer emails over time? What months see the highest volume? Is your send frequency sustainable? Use these patterns to plan future campaign calendars and avoid subscriber fatigue.

klaviyo-email:engagement_heatmap.title: Engagement by Day and Hour
klaviyo-email:engagement_heatmap.formula: Engagement score per day-of-week and hour (UTC), where engagement combines opens and clicks; darker cells mean higher engagement (heatmap)
klaviyo-email:engagement_heatmap.formula_explanation: This heatmap aggregates email engagement (opens + clicks) by day of week and hour of day within your selected date range. Darker colors indicate higher engagement, helping you schedule campaigns when subscribers are most likely to engage. The data respects both global and chart-level date filters.
how_to_read: Each cell represents engagement at that day/hour combination
Darker blue cells indicate higher engagement activity
Rows are days of the week (Sunday through Saturday)
Columns are hours in UTC - convert to your primary timezone
Data is aggregated from your selected date range
Use the filter button to analyze specific time periods
decisions_answered: When is the best time to send emails for maximum opens?
Which days of the week have highest engagement?
Should we avoid sending at certain times?
How does engagement vary across different time periods?
klaviyo-email:engagement_heatmap.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals when your subscribers are most likely to engage with emails.

The formula: Count opens plus clicks grouped by day of week and hour, normalized to a 0-100 scale. Darker cells mean more engagement happened at that time.

Reading the heatmap: Rows are days, columns are hours in UTC. The data reflects engagement patterns from your selected date range, so you can compare different time periods.

This answers crucial timing questions: When should you schedule campaigns? Use the chart filter to analyze specific periods. Schedule your most important campaigns for peak engagement times to maximize open rates and clicks.

klaviyo-email:engagement_rate_trends.title: Engagement Rate Trends
klaviyo-email:engagement_rate_trends.formula: Daily open rate = opens ÷ sends × 100 and click-to-open rate = clicks ÷ opens × 100 over the selected range (line chart)
klaviyo-email:engagement_rate_trends.formula_explanation: Open Rate measures how many recipients opened your email as a percentage of emails sent. CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures how many openers clicked, showing content engagement quality. Together they reveal both deliverability health and content effectiveness.
how_to_read: Green line shows Open Rate - how many emails get opened
Purple line shows CTOR - how many openers take action
Stable or rising trends indicate healthy engagement
Sudden drops may signal deliverability issues or content problems
Compare both metrics together - high opens but low CTOR suggests content issues
decisions_answered: Is our email engagement improving or declining over time?
Do we have content engagement problems (low CTOR)?
Are there deliverability issues affecting open rates?
When did significant engagement changes occur?
klaviyo-email:engagement_rate_trends.narration_text:
This multi-line chart tracks your two most important engagement metrics over time.

The formulas: Open Rate is opens divided by sends, times 100. CTOR - Click-to-Open Rate - is clicks divided by opens, times 100. They measure different things: opens show deliverability and subject line effectiveness, while CTOR shows content quality.

To read this: The green line shows open rate, purple shows CTOR. You want stable or rising trends. If opens drop suddenly, check deliverability. If CTOR drops while opens stay steady, your content may need work.

This answers critical questions: Is engagement improving? Where are the problems - inbox placement or content? When did changes occur, and what might have caused them?

klaviyo-email:flow_performance.title: Flow Performance
klaviyo-email:flow_performance.formula: One row per live flow with sends, opens, clicks and revenue, plus average open rate, average click rate and average click-to-open rate; ranked by sends
klaviyo-email:flow_performance.formula_explanation: Automated flows (welcome series, abandoned cart, post-purchase) often outperform campaigns because they are triggered at optimal moments. This chart compares flow performance to identify your highest-converting automations and optimization opportunities.
how_to_read: Green bars show Open Rate for each flow
Purple bars show CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate)
Higher bars indicate better engagement
Abandoned cart flows typically have highest CTOR (motivated shoppers)
Post-purchase flows often have highest open rates (relationship building)
decisions_answered: Which automated flows are performing best?
Where should we invest optimization effort?
Are our flows meeting performance benchmarks?
Which flows have engagement problems needing attention?
klaviyo-email:flow_performance.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart compares performance across your automated email flows.

The formulas: Flow Open Rate is opens divided by sends, times 100. Flow CTOR is clicks divided by opens, times 100.

Reading this: Green bars show open rates, purple bars show CTOR. Your post-purchase flow leads with an exceptional 94% open rate - people love hearing from you after buying. Abandoned cart and browse flows have the highest CTOR, around 12-13% - these shoppers are motivated.

Key questions answered: Which automations perform best? Where should you optimize? Flows typically outperform campaigns because timing is perfect. Focus on improving your lowest performers first for the biggest impact.

klaviyo-email:predictive_analytics.title: Predictive Analytics
klaviyo-email:predictive_analytics.formula: Average of Klaviyo's per-profile predictive analytics across active subscribers: total customer lifetime value (historic CLV + predicted CLV), predicted CLV, average order value and churn probability
klaviyo-email:predictive_analytics.formula_explanation: Klaviyo's predictive analytics uses machine learning to forecast customer lifetime value and churn risk. Historic CLV shows past purchase value, Predicted CLV estimates future value, and Churn Probability indicates likelihood of customer disengagement.
how_to_read: Avg Total CLV ($152) shows the complete customer value estimate
Avg Predicted CLV ($14) indicates expected future revenue per customer
Avg Order Value ($38) benchmarks transaction size
High Churn Probability (81%) signals an at-risk subscriber base
Use these metrics for segmentation and retention targeting
decisions_answered: What is the lifetime value of our average customer?
How much future revenue can we expect per subscriber?
Is our subscriber base at risk of churning?
Where should we focus retention and win-back efforts?
klaviyo-email:predictive_analytics.narration_text:
The predictive analytics panel shows Klaviyo's machine learning forecasts for your subscriber base.

The formulas: Total CLV equals historic CLV plus predicted future CLV. Churn probability is an ML model output from 0 to 100%.

Reading these metrics: Your average total CLV is $152, meaning each customer is worth about that much over their lifetime. The $14 predicted CLV shows expected additional purchases. The 81% average churn probability is concerning - this means your typical subscriber is at high risk of disengaging.

Key decisions: What's a customer worth? Where should retention focus? With high churn probability, prioritize re-engagement campaigns and consider sunset flows for your least engaged segments before they damage deliverability.

formula_components.name: Total CLV
formula_components.formula: average historic CLV + predicted CLV per profile

formula_components.name: Churn probability
formula_components.formula: average predicted churn probability per profile

klaviyo-email:source_comparison.title: Campaign vs Flow Performance
klaviyo-email:source_comparison.formula: Campaigns versus flows compared side by side on sends, with open rate = unique opens ÷ sends × 100 and click rate = unique clicks ÷ sends × 100 for each source (bar chart)
klaviyo-email:source_comparison.formula_explanation: Compares engagement rates between campaigns (one-time sends) and flows (automated sequences). Open rate and click rate are calculated separately for each source to highlight which channel drives stronger engagement.
how_to_read: Each bar group compares campaigns vs flows side by side
Open Rate shows how many recipients opened the email
Click Rate shows how many recipients clicked a link
Flows typically outperform campaigns due to triggered, timely delivery
Large gaps between the two suggest optimization opportunities
decisions_answered: Are campaigns or flows driving better engagement?
Should we invest more in automated flows vs manual campaigns?
Where is the biggest engagement gap between sources?
Which source type needs the most improvement?
klaviyo-email:source_comparison.narration_text:
This bar chart compares engagement rates between your campaigns and automated flows.

The formulas: Open Rate is opens divided by sends, times 100. Click Rate is clicks divided by sends, times 100. Each is calculated separately for campaigns and flows.

Reading this chart: Side-by-side bars let you compare campaigns versus flows directly. Flows usually outperform campaigns because they are triggered at the right moment in the customer journey.

Key questions answered: Which source drives better engagement? Should you shift more effort toward automations? Where are the biggest gaps? Use this to decide how to allocate your email marketing resources between one-time campaigns and automated sequences.

klaviyo-email:top_campaigns.title: Top Performing Campaigns
klaviyo-email:top_campaigns.formula: Top 10 campaigns ranked by click-to-open rate (clicks ÷ opens × 100) among campaigns with significant volume, showing sends and open rate for context
klaviyo-email:top_campaigns.formula_explanation: Shows the top 10 campaigns ranked by Click-to-Open Rate among campaigns with significant volume (100K+ sends). CTOR is the best measure of content effectiveness because it isolates how compelling your email content is after someone decides to open.
how_to_read: Campaigns ranked by highest CTOR (content engagement)
Shows send volume to ensure statistical significance
Open rate shown alongside CTOR for context
Study subject lines and content from top performers
Look for patterns in timing, themes, or offers
decisions_answered: What campaigns should we try to replicate?
What content themes resonate most with subscribers?
What subject line styles drive both opens and clicks?
Which campaigns represent our best-in-class performance?
klaviyo-email:top_campaigns.narration_text:
This list showcases your top 10 campaigns by Click-to-Open Rate.

The ranking formula: Sort all campaigns with over 100K sends by CTOR descending. The minimum volume requirement ensures these results are statistically meaningful, not flukes.

How to use this: Study these campaigns closely. The Tulip Fest SOLD OUT campaign hit 10.3% CTOR - exceptional. Look at what they have in common. Is it urgency? Exclusivity? Seasonal relevance?

This answers: What should we replicate? What content themes work? What subject line approaches succeed? These aren't just your best campaigns - they're templates for future success. Analyze the patterns and apply them to upcoming campaigns.

klaviyo-email:worst_campaigns.title: Underperforming Campaigns
klaviyo-email:worst_campaigns.formula: Bottom 10 campaigns ranked by lowest open rate (opens ÷ sends × 100) among campaigns with at least 100K sends in the last 12 months, with click-to-open rate shown for context
klaviyo-email:worst_campaigns.formula_explanation: Shows bottom 10 campaigns ranked by lowest Open Rate among campaigns with significant volume. Low open rates indicate subject line, timing, or deliverability issues. This list helps identify patterns in campaign approaches that should be avoided or fixed.
how_to_read: Campaigns ranked by lowest open rate (worst inbox performance)
Red highlighting indicates critically low metrics
Compare to top performers to identify differences
Check send timing and subject lines for patterns
Investigate if specific segments are underperforming
decisions_answered: What campaign approaches should we avoid?
Are there specific content types that consistently underperform?
Do certain send times correlate with poor performance?
Are there deliverability issues with specific campaigns?
klaviyo-email:worst_campaigns.narration_text:
This list shows your 10 worst-performing campaigns by open rate.

The ranking: Sort campaigns with over 100K sends by open rate ascending. Low opens can mean poor subject lines, bad timing, or deliverability problems.

Reading this list: Red highlighting warns you these need attention. Compare them to your top performers. What's different? Did they go to less engaged segments? Were subject lines unclear? Was timing off?

Questions answered: What approaches should you avoid? Are there patterns in what fails? This isn't about blame - it's about learning. Every campaign here teaches something. Find the patterns and eliminate them from future sends.

klaviyo-flows:engagement_by_type.title: Engagement by Flow Type
klaviyo-flows:engagement_by_type.formula: Average open rate and average click rate for each flow trigger type across live flows (grouped bar)
klaviyo-flows:engagement_by_type.formula_explanation: Calculates volume-weighted average engagement rates for each trigger type. This accounts for flows of different sizes, giving larger flows proportionally more influence on the average.
how_to_read: Blue bars show Open Rate, green bars show Click Rate for each trigger type
Taller bars indicate better engagement for that metric
Compare the gap between Open and Click rates within each trigger type
Trigger types with high opens but low clicks may have content issues
Use this to identify which trigger types engage your audience best
decisions_answered: Which trigger types produce the highest engagement rates?
Are certain trigger types better for opens vs clicks?
Where is there opportunity to improve content (high opens, low clicks)?
Which automation strategies engage our audience most effectively?
klaviyo-flows:engagement_by_type.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares engagement rates across different trigger types.

The formula calculates volume-weighted averages: total unique opens divided by total recipients for Open Rate, same approach for Click Rate. This means larger flows have more influence on the average, which is appropriate.

Blue bars show Open Rate, green bars show Click Rate. Compare them side by side within each trigger type.

The insight is in the patterns. Which trigger types get the best engagement? Is there a trigger type with great opens but poor clicks - that signals content needs work even though the timing and targeting are right.

This chart answers: Which triggers produce the best engagement? Are certain triggers better for opens versus clicks? And which automation strategies truly connect with your audience?

klaviyo-flows:flow_performance.title: Flow Performance Table
klaviyo-flows:flow_performance.formula: One row per live flow with recipients, opens, clicks, bounces, unsubscribes, conversions and revenue, plus average open rate, click rate, bounce rate and unsubscribe rate; ranked by recipients
klaviyo-flows:flow_performance.formula_explanation: This table consolidates all key metrics for each flow. Green values indicate performance meeting or exceeding targets (Open Rate >= 25%, Click Rate >= 3%). Yellow indicates warning range. Red indicates underperformance requiring attention.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank flows on that metric
Green text indicates strong performance meeting targets
Yellow text indicates acceptable but improvable performance
Red text highlights metrics requiring immediate attention
Click any row to see detailed flow step analysis
decisions_answered: Which flows are underperforming and need optimization?
How do different flows compare across all key metrics?
Which flows have deliverability issues (high bounce rates)?
Where should we focus optimization efforts for maximum impact?
klaviyo-flows:flow_performance.narration_text:
This table shows detailed performance metrics for every active flow in your Klaviyo account.

The key formulas: Open Rate is unique opens divided by recipients. Click Rate is unique clicks divided by recipients. Bounce Rate is bounces divided by emails sent.

Color coding helps you spot issues quickly. Green means the metric meets targets - Open Rate at or above 25%, Click Rate at or above 3%. Red means underperformance that needs attention.

You can sort by any column to find your best and worst performers. Click any row to drill into that specific flow.

Use this table to answer: Which flows need optimization? How do flows compare across metrics? Which have deliverability problems? And where should you focus your improvement efforts for the biggest impact?

klaviyo-flows:flow_trend.title: Performance Trend
klaviyo-flows:flow_trend.formula: Daily open rate = opens ÷ recipients × 100 and click rate = clicks ÷ recipients × 100 across live flows over the selected range (line chart)
klaviyo-flows:flow_trend.formula_explanation: Both rates measure unique engagement - each recipient is counted only once regardless of how many times they open or click. This gives a truer picture of audience engagement than raw event counts.
how_to_read: Green line tracks weekly Open Rate - the percentage of recipients who opened emails
Blue line tracks weekly Click Rate - the percentage who clicked links in emails
Upward trends indicate improving engagement, downward trends signal potential issues
Look for the gap between Open and Click rates - a wide gap may indicate subject line success but content issues
Sudden drops often correlate with deliverability issues or list quality problems
decisions_answered: Are our flow engagement rates improving or declining over time?
Which weeks showed exceptional or poor performance that warrant investigation?
Is there a seasonal pattern in our flow engagement?
Are recent flow optimizations showing measurable impact?
klaviyo-flows:flow_trend.narration_text:
This chart tracks your Klaviyo flow engagement over the past 90 days, broken down by week.

The formula is straightforward: Open Rate equals unique opens divided by recipients, times 100. Click Rate uses the same formula with unique clicks.

The green line shows Open Rate - what percentage of recipients opened your flow emails. The blue line shows Click Rate - what percentage clicked through.

Watch for trends. Rising lines mean better engagement. Falling lines signal problems to investigate. Pay attention to the gap between open and click rates - if opens are high but clicks are low, your subject lines are working but your content may need improvement.

Use this chart to answer: Are engagement rates improving? Which weeks performed best or worst? And are your recent optimizations actually working?

klaviyo-flows:flow_type_comparison.title: Performance by Trigger Type
klaviyo-flows:flow_type_comparison.formula: Total recipients summed across live flows, grouped by trigger type (bar chart)
klaviyo-flows:flow_type_comparison.formula_explanation: Aggregates all recipients across flows that share the same trigger type. This shows which automation triggers reach the most people in your audience.
how_to_read: Taller bars indicate trigger types reaching more recipients
Common trigger types include: Added to List, Metric, Date, Segment, Price Drop
Hover over each bar to see exact recipient counts
Compare relative bar heights to understand your automation portfolio balance
Missing or small trigger types may represent growth opportunities
decisions_answered: Which trigger types drive the most recipient volume?
Is our automation portfolio balanced across different trigger types?
Are there underutilized trigger types we should expand?
Which trigger mechanisms are most valuable for our business?
klaviyo-flows:flow_type_comparison.narration_text:
This bar chart breaks down your flow recipients by trigger type.

The formula simply sums all unique recipients across flows that share the same trigger mechanism - whether that's being added to a list, a metric being triggered, a date condition, or joining a segment.

Taller bars mean more recipients reached through that trigger type. Compare bar heights to understand your automation portfolio.

Are you over-reliant on one trigger type? Are there trigger mechanisms you're underutilizing? For example, if Price Drop triggers are small but your business has frequent price changes, that could be an opportunity.

This chart answers: Which triggers reach the most people? Is your portfolio balanced? And where should you consider building new automations?

klaviyo-flows:top_flows.title: Top Flows by Recipients
klaviyo-flows:top_flows.formula: Recipient share of the top 5 live flows, each slice = flow recipients ÷ total recipients across the top 5 × 100; center shows the combined total (donut)
klaviyo-flows:top_flows.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of recipients among your top 5 highest-volume flows. The center displays the combined total of all top flows, while each slice represents one flow's share.
how_to_read: Each slice represents one flow - larger slices indicate higher recipient volume
Center value shows total recipients across all displayed flows
Hover over any slice to see exact recipient count and percentage
A dominant single slice may indicate over-reliance on one automation
Well-distributed slices suggest a healthy automation portfolio
decisions_answered: Which individual flows reach the most people?
Is recipient volume concentrated in a few flows or well-distributed?
Which flows should be prioritized for optimization given their reach?
Are there high-volume flows that need performance attention?
klaviyo-flows:top_flows.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how recipients are distributed across your top 5 highest-volume flows.

Each slice represents one flow. The size of the slice shows that flow's share of total recipients. The center number is the combined total across all five flows.

What to look for: If one slice dominates - say 70% or more - you're heavily reliant on that single automation. That's risky. If something breaks or performance drops, it impacts most of your flow program.

Ideally, you want reasonable distribution across multiple flows. This chart helps you identify which flows deserve the most optimization attention based purely on reach.

It answers: Which flows reach the most people? Is your volume concentrated or distributed? And which automations should get priority for improvements?

klaviyo-sms:campaigns.title: SMS Campaign Performance
klaviyo-sms:campaigns.formula: One row per SMS campaign with sends, clicks, unsubscribes, conversions and revenue, plus click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100, conversion rate = orders ÷ sends × 100 and revenue per SMS = revenue ÷ sends; ranked by sends
klaviyo-sms:campaigns.formula_explanation: This table shows performance metrics for all SMS campaigns sent in the selected period. Each row represents one campaign with its sending stats, engagement metrics, and attributed revenue.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank campaigns - try sorting by Revenue or Click Rate to find top performers
Click Rate above 5% indicates strong engagement; below 2% needs content or targeting review
Unsubscribe column shows list health impact - high unsubs relative to sends is a warning sign
Revenue attribution shows direct business impact from each campaign
Click any row to drill into detailed campaign analytics
decisions_answered: Which SMS campaigns generate the most revenue?
Which campaigns have the best engagement rates?
Are any campaigns causing excessive unsubscribes?
What campaign types should we send more often?
klaviyo-sms:campaigns.narration_text:
This table breaks down performance for every SMS campaign you have sent.

The key metrics are click rate, conversions, and revenue. Click rate tells you if your message resonated - aim for 5% or higher. Conversions and revenue show actual business impact.

Sort the table to find insights. Sort by revenue to see your money-makers. Sort by click rate to find your most engaging content. Sort by unsubscribes to catch campaigns that hurt list health.

Watch the unsubscribe column carefully. A campaign that generates revenue but causes mass opt-outs is not sustainable.

Click any campaign row to see detailed analytics - open times, device breakdown, and more.

Use this to answer: What campaigns work? What should we repeat? And what is hurting our list?

klaviyo-sms:channel_comparison.title: Channel Comparison
klaviyo-sms:channel_comparison.formula: Click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100 per message source (campaign versus flow), compared side by side (bar chart)
klaviyo-sms:channel_comparison.formula_explanation: Compares SMS engagement metrics across different channels or message sources (campaigns vs flows). This helps identify which channel drives stronger engagement and where to focus optimization efforts.
how_to_read: Each bar group compares performance across channels side by side
Higher bars indicate better engagement for that channel
Flows typically outperform campaigns due to triggered, timely delivery
Large gaps between channels suggest optimization opportunities
Use this to decide resource allocation between campaigns and flows
decisions_answered: Which SMS channel drives the best engagement?
Should we invest more in automated flows vs manual campaigns?
Where is the biggest performance gap between channels?
Which channel type needs the most improvement?
klaviyo-sms:channel_comparison.narration_text:
This chart compares SMS engagement across different channels - typically campaigns versus automated flows.

Each bar group shows metrics side by side so you can directly compare performance. Flows usually outperform campaigns because they trigger at the right moment in the customer journey.

Look for large gaps between channels. If flows dramatically outperform campaigns, consider shifting more messaging to automated sequences.

This answers: Which channel works best? Where should you focus? And how big is the performance gap between your SMS channels?

klaviyo-sms:click_rate_trend.title: Click Rate Trend
klaviyo-sms:click_rate_trend.formula: Weekly SMS click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100 over the selected range (line chart); SMS has no open tracking so click rate is the primary engagement metric
klaviyo-sms:click_rate_trend.formula_explanation: Click rate measures the percentage of SMS recipients who clicked a link in the message. This is the primary engagement metric for SMS marketing, as SMS does not have open tracking like email.
how_to_read: The purple line shows weekly click rate percentage over 12 weeks
Industry benchmark for SMS click rate is 5% or higher - rates below 2% need attention
Spikes often correlate with compelling offers or urgent messaging
Declining trends may indicate list fatigue, poor timing, or irrelevant content
Compare to your historical average to understand if current performance is normal
decisions_answered: Is our SMS click rate improving or declining over time?
Are we meeting the 5% click rate benchmark?
Which weeks had exceptional or poor engagement?
Do we have list fatigue affecting our click rates?
klaviyo-sms:click_rate_trend.narration_text:
Click rate is your most important SMS engagement metric. Unlike email, SMS has no reliable open tracking, so clicks tell you if your messages are resonating.

The formula is simple: clicks divided by sends, times 100. A 5% click rate is the target - anything below 2% needs immediate attention.

Watch the trend direction. Rising rates mean your content, timing, or targeting is improving. Falling rates often signal list fatigue - you may be messaging too frequently or with content that is not relevant.

Spikes usually correlate with compelling offers, urgency, or well-timed messages. Study your high-performing weeks to replicate success.

This chart helps answer: Are we hitting benchmarks? Is engagement trending the right direction? And where should we investigate?

klaviyo-sms:flows.title: SMS Flow Performance
klaviyo-sms:flows.formula: One row per SMS flow message with sends, clicks and conversions, plus click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100 and conversion rate = orders ÷ sends × 100; ranked by sends
klaviyo-sms:flows.formula_explanation: This table shows performance metrics for SMS messages within automated flows. Unlike campaigns, flows send continuously to qualifying contacts, making them key revenue drivers for abandoned cart, welcome series, and post-purchase sequences.
how_to_read: Flow Name shows the automation (e.g., Abandoned Cart, Welcome Series)
Message Name identifies the specific SMS within that flow sequence
Higher conversion rates in flows vs campaigns is normal - flow timing is trigger-based
Compare similar messages across flows to identify best-performing content
Click any row to navigate to the full flow analytics page
decisions_answered: Which SMS flows drive the most conversions?
Are our automated SMS messages performing well?
Which specific flow messages need optimization?
How does flow SMS performance compare to campaigns?
klaviyo-sms:flows.narration_text:
This table shows performance for SMS messages within your automated flows.

Flows are different from campaigns. They trigger automatically based on customer behavior - abandoned carts, welcome sequences, post-purchase follow-ups. They run continuously, making them reliable revenue drivers.

Each row shows a specific message within a flow. The same flow might have multiple SMS touchpoints at different stages.

Flow SMS typically outperforms campaigns on conversion rate because the timing is precise - the message arrives when the customer is most likely to act.

Compare messages within the same flow. If message 2 of your abandoned cart sequence dramatically outperforms message 1, study what makes it work.

Click any row to see the full flow and all its messages.

This answers: Which flows drive conversions? Which messages need work? And are our automations performing?

klaviyo-sms:list_growth.title: SMS List Growth
klaviyo-sms:list_growth.formula: Cumulative SMS list net growth over the selected range, where daily net growth = new subscribers (sends) − unsubscribes (opt-outs), summed into a running total (area chart)
klaviyo-sms:list_growth.formula_explanation: Tracks the growth of your SMS subscriber list over time. Shows new subscriptions, unsubscribes, and net growth to help you understand whether your list is expanding or shrinking.
how_to_read: Upward trend indicates healthy list growth
Compare new subscribers vs unsubscribes to understand churn
Flat or declining trends may signal the need for new opt-in strategies
Spikes often correlate with promotions or sign-up incentives
Watch for sudden drops that could indicate compliance or technical issues
decisions_answered: Is our SMS list growing or shrinking?
Are we acquiring subscribers faster than we lose them?
What periods show the best growth and why?
Do we need new strategies to drive SMS opt-ins?
klaviyo-sms:list_growth.narration_text:
This chart tracks your SMS subscriber list growth over time.

A healthy SMS program shows steady upward growth - new subscribers consistently outnumbering unsubscribes.

Watch for the balance between acquisition and churn. If unsubscribes are climbing toward your new subscriber rate, you need to address retention before growth stalls.

Spikes in growth often correlate with promotions, pop-up campaigns, or checkout opt-in incentives. Study what drove your best growth periods and replicate those strategies.

This answers: Is our list growing? Are we retaining subscribers? And what drives our best acquisition periods?

formula_components.name: Cumulative growth
formula_components.formula: running sum of daily net growth

klaviyo-sms:list_health.title: SMS List Health
klaviyo-sms:list_health.formula: Composition of the SMS list — subscribed, unsubscribed and contacts with a phone but no SMS consent — with subscription rate = subscribed ÷ total with phone × 100; center shows active subscribers (donut)
klaviyo-sms:list_health.formula_explanation: This donut chart shows the current composition of your SMS subscriber list. Active subscribers are those who have opted in and can receive marketing SMS. The breakdown helps identify list health issues and growth opportunities.
how_to_read: Green segment shows active subscribed contacts - these are your reachable SMS audience
Red segment shows unsubscribed contacts - a growing red segment indicates list health issues
Gray segment shows contacts with phone numbers but no SMS consent
Center value displays total active subscribers - this is your addressable SMS audience
The subscription rate percentage shows what portion of your database has SMS consent
decisions_answered: How large is our active SMS subscriber list?
What percentage of our database has SMS consent?
Is our unsubscribe segment growing concerningly?
How much opportunity exists to grow SMS opt-ins?
klaviyo-sms:list_health.narration_text:
This donut chart gives you a snapshot of your SMS list composition and health.

The green segment shows your active subscribers - people who have opted in and can receive your SMS marketing. This is your addressable audience.

The red segment shows unsubscribed contacts. Some churn is normal, but if this segment grows faster than your subscriber base, you have a retention problem.

The gray segment represents contacts with phone numbers but no SMS consent. This is your growth opportunity - these people could be converted through opt-in campaigns.

The center number is your total active subscribers. The subscription rate below tells you what percentage of your phone-number database has consented to SMS.

Use this to answer: How large is our SMS audience? Are we retaining subscribers? And where is our growth potential?

klaviyo-sms:send_time_heatmap.title: Best Send Times
klaviyo-sms:send_time_heatmap.formula: Click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100 by day-of-week and hour of send, attributing clicks to the send time (7×24 heatmap); darker cells are better send windows
klaviyo-sms:send_time_heatmap.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows click rate performance by hour of day, helping you identify optimal send times. Darker colors indicate higher click rates. Analysis is based on the last 30 days of SMS activity.
how_to_read: Darker blue cells indicate hours with higher click rates - these are your optimal send windows
Lighter cells show hours with lower engagement - avoid scheduling campaigns here
Hover over any cell to see the exact click rate percentage for that hour
Look for patterns - morning, lunch, evening - to align with customer behavior
Note that early morning and late night typically show lower engagement
decisions_answered: What time of day gets the best SMS response?
When should we avoid sending SMS messages?
Are there multiple high-engagement windows we can use?
Does our audience engage more in morning or evening?
klaviyo-sms:send_time_heatmap.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals your optimal SMS send times based on actual click performance.

Each cell represents an hour of the day. Darker blue means higher click rates at that hour. Lighter colors mean lower engagement.

Your goal is to find patterns. Most brands see peaks during morning commute, lunch breaks, and evening leisure time. But your audience may be different.

Avoid the light-colored hours - sending then wastes messages on low-engagement periods. Focus your campaigns and flows during the dark blue windows.

Hover over cells to see exact click rates. Look for 2-3 hour windows where engagement is consistently strong.

This answers critical questions: When should we send? When should we avoid? And does our audience prefer morning or evening engagement?

klaviyo-sms:sms_trend.title: SMS Performance Trend
klaviyo-sms:sms_trend.formula: SMS messages sent and clicks per period over the selected range, with click rate available per point (area chart)
klaviyo-sms:sms_trend.formula_explanation: This chart aggregates SMS sending volume and click engagement on a weekly basis over 12 weeks. Each data point represents the total count of unique SMS events for that week, allowing you to identify trends and patterns in your SMS program.
how_to_read: Blue area shows total SMS messages sent per week - higher peaks indicate more active sending periods
Green area shows total clicks received - should correlate with sending volume
Look for the gap between sent and clicks to gauge overall engagement health
Sudden drops in either metric may indicate delivery issues or list health problems
Compare week-over-week to identify seasonal patterns or campaign impact
decisions_answered: Is our SMS sending volume increasing or decreasing over time?
Are click volumes keeping pace with our sending growth?
What weeks had the best SMS engagement?
Are there seasonal patterns we should plan around?
klaviyo-sms:sms_trend.narration_text:
This chart shows your SMS performance over the past 12 weeks, tracking both sending volume and engagement.

The blue area represents total SMS messages sent each week. Higher peaks mean more active sending periods, which could be due to campaigns, promotions, or automated flows.

The green area shows clicks - the number of times recipients tapped links in your messages. Healthy SMS programs show clicks moving in proportion to sends.

Watch for the gap between these two lines. If sends are rising but clicks stay flat, engagement is dropping. If both grow together, your SMS strategy is scaling well.

Use this to answer: Is our program growing? Are we maintaining engagement as we scale? And when do we see our best performance weeks?

klaviyo-sms:top_messages.title: Top Performing Messages
klaviyo-sms:top_messages.formula: Top SMS messages with sends, clicks, conversions and revenue, plus click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100; ranked by sends (table)
klaviyo-sms:top_messages.formula_explanation: Shows the top performing individual SMS messages ranked by click rate. This helps identify which message content, offers, and copy styles resonate most with your audience.
how_to_read: Messages ranked by highest click rate
Shows send volume alongside click rate for context
Study the content of top performers for patterns
Look for common themes in offers, urgency, or personalization
Use insights to template future high-performing messages
decisions_answered: What SMS messages get the best engagement?
What content styles resonate with our audience?
Which messages should we replicate?
What patterns exist in our top performing SMS content?
klaviyo-sms:top_messages.narration_text:
This table showcases your top performing SMS messages ranked by click rate.

Study these messages carefully. What do they have in common? Is it urgency, a compelling offer, personalization, or timing?

The send volume column provides context - a high click rate on a large send is more meaningful than on a small one.

Use this to build templates for future success. The patterns in your top messages are your playbook for what works with your audience.

This answers: What content works? What should we replicate? And what makes our best messages successful?

magento-customer-analytics:aov_trend.title: Average Order Value Trend
magento-customer-analytics:aov_trend.formula: Average order value per day over the selected period (line chart). Daily AOV = that day's gross revenue ÷ that day's orders.
magento-customer-analytics:aov_trend.formula_explanation: Calculates the average order value by dividing total revenue by order count for each day. AOV is a key profitability metric - higher AOV typically means better margins.
how_to_read: The line shows daily average order value fluctuations
The period average is displayed for context
Rising AOV with stable orders indicates upselling success
Falling AOV may indicate discounting or product mix shifts
Spikes may indicate high-value orders or bulk purchases
decisions_answered: Is our average order value improving over time?
Are upselling and cross-selling strategies working?
How do promotions impact average order value?
What customer segments drive higher AOV?
magento-customer-analytics:aov_trend.narration_text:
This chart tracks your average order value over time.

The formula is straightforward: total revenue divided by total orders for each day. The period average gives you a benchmark to compare daily fluctuations.

Rising AOV is generally positive - customers are spending more per transaction. This could indicate successful upselling, cross-selling, or premium product adoption.

Falling AOV needs investigation. Heavy discounting, shifts in product mix, or changes in customer segments can all reduce AOV. A lower AOV isn't always bad if order volume compensates.

Watch for spikes - they often indicate bulk purchases or high-value customers. These might be B2B buyers or VIPs worth special attention.

This answers: How much do customers spend on average? Are our strategies to increase basket size working? And what factors influence order value?

magento-customer-analytics:churn_risk_fu.title: Churn Risk Funnel
magento-customer-analytics:churn_risk_fu.formula: Customer count by churn risk level (Critical → Minimal), shown as a funnel; wider sections hold more customers at that risk level.
magento-customer-analytics:churn_risk_fu.formula_explanation: Segments customers into churn risk tiers based on recency of last purchase, historical purchase frequency, and monetary value. Higher risk tiers indicate customers who are increasingly likely to churn.
how_to_read: The funnel shows customers flowing from low risk to high risk tiers
Wider sections indicate more customers at that risk level
Active customers at the top are your healthy base
At-risk and churned segments at the bottom need intervention
Compare tier sizes over time to track churn prevention effectiveness
decisions_answered: How many customers are at risk of churning?
Is our churn risk distribution improving or worsening?
Which risk tier should we prioritize for win-back campaigns?
How effective are our retention efforts?
magento-customer-analytics:churn_risk_fu.narration_text:
This funnel chart visualizes how your customers are distributed across churn risk tiers.

The formula analyzes each customer's recency, frequency, and monetary value to assign a risk level. Customers who haven't purchased recently and show declining patterns are flagged as higher risk.

Reading from top to bottom: active customers at the top are healthy. As you move down, risk increases. The bottom tiers represent customers who are likely churning or already lost.

Watch the proportions. If the at-risk tier is growing relative to active, your retention is weakening. A shrinking high-risk tier means win-back campaigns are working.

This answers: How many customers are we losing? Where should we focus retention efforts? And are our churn prevention strategies working?

magento-customer-analytics:cohort_retent.title: Cohort Retention
magento-customer-analytics:cohort_retent.formula: Retention rate by cohort (first-purchase month) and months elapsed, shown as a heatmap. Retention rate = customers from a cohort still ordering in month N ÷ original cohort size × 100.
magento-customer-analytics:cohort_retent.formula_explanation: Groups customers by their first purchase month (cohort) and tracks what percentage return to purchase in subsequent months. This reveals true customer retention patterns over time.
how_to_read: Each row represents a cohort of customers who first purchased in that month
Columns show retention rates for subsequent months
Darker colors indicate higher retention rates
The diagonal pattern shows how retention evolves over time
Compare cohorts to identify improvements or declines in retention
decisions_answered: What is our true customer retention rate?
Are newer cohorts retaining better than older ones?
At what point do most customers drop off?
How do retention patterns differ across acquisition periods?
magento-customer-analytics:cohort_retent.narration_text:
This heatmap shows cohort retention - how well you keep customers over time.

The formula groups customers by their first purchase month, then tracks what percentage return each subsequent month.

Each row is a cohort. The first column shows Month 1 retention - what percentage came back for a second purchase. This is typically the biggest drop. Subsequent columns show ongoing retention.

Darker cells mean better retention. Compare rows to see if newer cohorts perform better - that means your retention strategies are improving.

The critical metric is Month 1 to Month 2 retention. If you can keep customers past their second purchase, long-term loyalty increases dramatically.

This answers: How well do we retain customers? Are we improving over time? And where is the biggest retention drop-off?

magento-customer-analytics:cross_sell_op.title: Cross-Sell Opportunities
magento-customer-analytics:cross_sell_op.formula: One row per product pair frequently bought together, with co-purchase count, lift, confidence and an affinity strength rating, ranked by lift. Lift = how much more often the pair is bought together than expected if independent; confidence = probability of buying product B given product A.
magento-customer-analytics:cross_sell_op.formula_explanation: Uses market basket analysis to identify product pairs frequently purchased together. Lift measures how much more likely products are bought together versus independently. Confidence measures the probability of buying product B given product A was purchased.
how_to_read: Each row shows a product pair that customers frequently buy together
Higher lift values indicate stronger product association
Confidence shows the probability of the cross-sell succeeding
Co-purchase count shows how many times the pair was bought together
Strength rating summarizes the overall cross-sell potential
decisions_answered: Which products should we recommend together?
What are our strongest product associations?
Which cross-sell opportunities have the highest confidence?
How can we increase basket size through product bundling?
magento-customer-analytics:cross_sell_op.narration_text:
This table shows product pairs that customers frequently purchase together, revealing cross-sell opportunities.

The formula uses market basket analysis. Lift measures how much more likely two products are bought together versus independently. A lift of 2.0 means customers are twice as likely to buy both products together. Confidence measures the probability that buying product A leads to buying product B.

Higher lift and confidence values indicate stronger opportunities. Focus on pairs with both high lift and sufficient co-purchase volume for reliable recommendations.

Use these insights to power product recommendations, create bundles, and design targeted cross-sell campaigns.

This answers: What products should we recommend together? Which pairings have the strongest affinity? And how can we increase average basket size?

formula_components.name: Lift
formula_components.formula: probability of A and B bought together ÷ (probability of A × probability of B)

formula_components.name: Confidence (A→B)
formula_components.formula: co-purchases of A and B ÷ total purchases of A

magento-customer-analytics:ltv_distribut.title: LTV Distribution
magento-customer-analytics:ltv_distribut.formula: Customer count grouped into lifetime-value segments (Low → VIP), shown as bars; taller bars mean more customers in that value band.
magento-customer-analytics:ltv_distribut.formula_explanation: Calculates total lifetime value for each customer and groups them into value ranges. This shows the distribution of customer value across your base.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a lifetime value range
Taller bars indicate more customers in that range
A healthy distribution shows customers across multiple tiers
Heavy concentration in low tiers may indicate retention issues
High-value tiers represent your VIP customers
decisions_answered: How is customer value distributed across our base?
What percentage of customers are high-value?
Is there opportunity to move customers to higher tiers?
Where should we focus retention efforts?
magento-customer-analytics:ltv_distribut.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base.

The formula sums all purchases for each customer, then groups them into value ranges or tiers.

Each bar represents one value range. Taller bars mean more customers at that level. A healthy business shows customers distributed across tiers, with strategies to move customers upward.

If most customers cluster in the lowest tier, focus on second purchase campaigns. A strong mid-tier indicates good repeat customers. A meaningful high-value tier represents your VIP segment deserving special treatment.

The 80/20 rule often applies - a small percentage of customers drive a large share of revenue. Identify and nurture these high-value relationships.

This answers: How is value distributed across our customer base? What percentage are truly valuable? And where should we focus customer development efforts?

magento-customer-analytics:marketing_act.title: Marketing Actions
magento-customer-analytics:marketing_act.formula: One row per recommended marketing action and customer segment, with addressable customer count, revenue potential and an average outreach priority score, ranked by priority then customer count.
magento-customer-analytics:marketing_act.formula_explanation: Recommends targeted marketing actions based on customer segments, their size, and revenue potential. Actions are prioritized by urgency and expected impact to help allocate marketing resources effectively.
how_to_read: Each row represents a recommended marketing action
Customer count shows the addressable audience for each action
Revenue potential estimates the value of successfully engaging the segment
Priority score indicates urgency - higher scores need immediate attention
Sort by priority to focus on the most impactful actions first
decisions_answered: What marketing campaigns should we prioritize?
Which customer segments need immediate attention?
What is the potential revenue impact of each action?
How should we allocate marketing resources across segments?
magento-customer-analytics:marketing_act.narration_text:
This table provides actionable marketing recommendations based on your customer data.

The formula evaluates each customer segment's size, revenue potential, and risk level to prioritize marketing actions. Higher priority actions have the greatest urgency or potential impact.

Each action targets a specific customer behavior. Reactivation campaigns target lapsed customers. Retention actions protect at-risk relationships. Upsell and cross-sell target growth opportunities.

Focus on the highest-priority actions first. The revenue potential column helps justify budget allocation. The customer count shows the scale of each opportunity.

This answers: What should we do next? Where will marketing spend have the most impact? And which customer segments need immediate attention?

magento-customer-analytics:orders_trend.title: Orders Trend
magento-customer-analytics:orders_trend.formula: Total orders per day over the selected period (area/line chart), with completed orders and revenue carried as context.
magento-customer-analytics:orders_trend.formula_explanation: Counts total orders placed each day, providing a view of customer purchasing activity over time. This is a key indicator of business health and demand.
how_to_read: The area chart shows daily order volume
Upward trends indicate growing demand
Dips may indicate seasonal slowdowns or issues
The total displayed shows cumulative orders for the period
Compare to revenue trends to understand average order value changes
decisions_answered: Is order volume growing or declining?
What days or periods see peak ordering activity?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer ordering behavior?
How do orders correlate with marketing activities?
magento-customer-analytics:orders_trend.narration_text:
This area chart visualizes daily order volume from your customer base.

The formula counts all orders placed each day. The total displayed shows cumulative orders for your selected time period.

Watch the trend direction. Rising orders indicate growing demand and effective customer engagement. Flat trends might mean you've reached current market potential. Declining trends need investigation.

Look for patterns. Weekday versus weekend differences, monthly cycles, seasonal peaks. Understanding when customers order helps with inventory, staffing, and marketing timing.

Compare to revenue trends. If orders grow but revenue stays flat, average order value is dropping. If revenue grows faster than orders, customers are spending more per transaction.

This answers: How is our order volume trending? When do customers order most? And what factors influence ordering behavior?

magento-customer-analytics:registration_.title: Customer Registration Trend
magento-customer-analytics:registration_.formula: New customers acquired per day over the selected period (line chart), with first-order revenue carried as context.
magento-customer-analytics:registration_.formula_explanation: Counts new customer account registrations per day. This measures your ability to convert visitors into registered customers, which enables better marketing and customer relationship building.
how_to_read: The area chart shows daily new customer registrations
Rising trends indicate effective customer acquisition
Spikes may correlate with marketing campaigns or promotions
Compare to order volume to see conversion potential
Seasonal patterns may reflect industry or promotional cycles
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring new registered customers consistently?
What campaigns drive the most registrations?
Is our registration process effective?
How does registration growth align with overall business growth?
magento-customer-analytics:registration_.narration_text:
This area chart tracks daily new customer registrations over time.

The formula counts new customer accounts created each day. Registered customers are valuable - they enable personalized marketing, loyalty programs, and better lifetime value tracking.

Rising trends indicate healthy customer acquisition. Sudden spikes often correlate with campaigns, promotions, or viral moments. Declining trends suggest acquisition challenges or increased competition.

Compare registration trends to order trends. If orders grow but registrations don't, you may have more guest checkout. If registrations outpace orders, registered customers aren't converting.

This answers: Are we building our customer base? What drives registration spikes? And how effective is our registration process?

magento-customer-analytics:rfm_revenue.title: Revenue by RFM Segment
magento-customer-analytics:rfm_revenue.formula: Gross revenue contribution by RFM segment (Champions, Loyal, At Risk, etc.), shown as bars; taller bars mean more revenue from that segment.
magento-customer-analytics:rfm_revenue.formula_explanation: Sums total revenue contribution from each RFM segment. This shows which customer segments drive the most business value.
how_to_read: Bars show total revenue from each segment
Champions typically contribute disproportionate revenue
Compare segment size to revenue contribution for efficiency
High revenue from At-Risk segment indicates opportunity and risk
Low revenue from large segments indicates development potential
decisions_answered: Which customer segments drive the most revenue?
Is revenue concentrated in healthy segments?
What segments have the highest revenue potential?
How much revenue is at risk from churning customers?
magento-customer-analytics:rfm_revenue.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how much revenue each RFM segment contributes to your business.

The formula sums all order revenue from customers in each segment.

Champions often contribute disproportionately - they might be 10% of customers but 40% of revenue. This concentration is both a strength and a risk.

Compare segment sizes to revenue contribution. A small segment with high revenue means valuable customers to protect. A large segment with low revenue has development potential.

Pay attention to revenue from At-Risk and Hibernating segments. If significant revenue comes from customers who haven't purchased recently, you're vulnerable. Prioritize reactivation campaigns for these segments.

This answers: Where does our revenue actually come from? How vulnerable is our revenue to churn? And which segments offer the best growth opportunity?

magento-customer-analytics:rfm_segments.title: RFM Segments
magento-customer-analytics:rfm_segments.formula: Customer count by RFM segment (derived from each customer's Recency, Frequency and Monetary scores), shown as a donut; each slice is one segment's share of customers.
magento-customer-analytics:rfm_segments.formula_explanation: Segments customers based on Recency (how recently they purchased), Frequency (how often), and Monetary value (how much). Each customer receives a segment label based on their RFM profile.
how_to_read: Each donut slice represents one RFM segment
Champions are your best customers - recent, frequent, high-value
At-Risk and Hibernating segments need reactivation attention
New customers show acquisition health
Segment sizes indicate overall customer base health
decisions_answered: What is the health of our overall customer base?
How many customers are at risk of churning?
What segments should we prioritize for marketing?
Are we creating Champions from new customers?
magento-customer-analytics:rfm_segments.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customers segmented by RFM analysis - Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value.

The formula assigns each customer to a segment based on three factors: how recently they bought, how often they buy, and how much they spend.

Champions are your stars - recent purchasers who buy frequently and spend well. Loyal customers are consistent buyers. New customers are recently acquired. At-Risk and Hibernating segments haven't purchased recently and need reactivation.

Segment sizes matter. A healthy business shows strong Champion and Loyal segments, steady New customer acquisition, and controlled At-Risk segments.

Use this for targeted marketing. Champions get VIP treatment. At-Risk customers get win-back campaigns. New customers get nurture sequences to build loyalty.

This answers: How healthy is our customer base? Which segments need attention? And where should we focus our marketing efforts?

magento-customer-analytics:top_customers.title: Top Customers by LTV
magento-customer-analytics:top_customers.formula: One row per customer with lifetime value (sum of all their orders), order count, average order value and RFM segment, ranked by lifetime value (top 50).
magento-customer-analytics:top_customers.formula_explanation: Ranks customers by their total lifetime value - the sum of all their orders. This identifies your most valuable customers who deserve special attention.
how_to_read: Customers ranked from highest to lowest lifetime value
LTV shows total revenue from each customer
Orders column shows purchase frequency
Avg Order shows typical transaction size
RFM Segment indicates current customer health
decisions_answered: Who are our most valuable customers?
What characteristics do top customers share?
Are our VIP customers still active?
How can we replicate our best customer relationships?
magento-customer-analytics:top_customers.narration_text:
This table lists your most valuable customers ranked by total lifetime value.

The formula sums all purchases for each customer and sorts from highest to lowest.

Each row shows customer details, total LTV, order count, average order value, RFM segment, and last order date. High LTV with many orders indicates a consistent loyal customer. High LTV with few orders suggests high-ticket purchases.

Check the RFM segment column. Your top customers should be Champions or Loyal. If you see At-Risk status, act immediately - losing a top customer is costly.

Study what top customers have in common. Acquisition channel, product preferences, timing patterns. These insights help you find and develop more high-value customers.

This answers: Who are our VIPs? Are they still active and engaged? And what can we learn from our best customer relationships?

magento-orders-fulfillment:abandoned_car.title: Abandoned Cart Analysis
magento-orders-fulfillment:abandoned_car.formula: Daily cart abandonment rate over the selected period (line chart). Abandonment rate = abandoned carts ÷ total carts × 100; summary also shows total abandoned carts and the revenue value left behind.
magento-orders-fulfillment:abandoned_car.formula_explanation: Tracks daily cart abandonment trends including rate, count, and total value left behind. This reveals how much potential revenue is being lost when customers add items to cart but do not complete checkout.
how_to_read: The line chart shows daily abandonment rate over the selected period
Higher values indicate more carts being abandoned that day
Look for patterns on specific days of the week or around promotions
Summary metrics above the chart show totals for the period
Sudden spikes may indicate checkout issues or site problems
decisions_answered: What is our cart abandonment rate and is it improving?
How much revenue are we losing to abandoned carts?
Are there specific days or periods with higher abandonment?
Is our checkout optimization having a measurable impact?
magento-orders-fulfillment:abandoned_car.narration_text:
This chart tracks your daily cart abandonment trends over time.

The abandonment rate formula divides abandoned carts by total carts created, times 100. The abandoned value sums up the dollar amount left behind in incomplete checkouts.

Watch the trend line. A rising rate means more customers are leaving without buying. Industry average for e-commerce is typically 60 to 80 percent, so context matters.

Look for patterns. Higher abandonment on certain days might indicate technical issues. Spikes around promotions could mean pricing friction. A steady decline shows your checkout optimizations are working.

This answers: What is our abandonment rate? How much revenue are we losing? Are there problematic days? And are our improvements making a difference?

magento-orders-fulfillment:fulfillment_f.title: Fulfillment Funnel
magento-orders-fulfillment:fulfillment_f.formula: Order count at each fulfillment stage — Orders placed → Invoiced → Shipped → Completed — shown as sequential bars to reveal drop-off between stages.
