magento-orders-fulfillment:fulfillment_f.formula_explanation: Tracks orders through the fulfillment pipeline from placement to completion. Each stage shows how many orders have reached that milestone, revealing where orders get stuck.
how_to_read: Bars represent sequential fulfillment stages
Healthy funnels show minimal drop-off between stages
Large gaps indicate bottlenecks at that transition
The final bar shows successfully completed orders
Compare stage volumes to identify process improvements
decisions_answered: Where are orders getting stuck in fulfillment?
How quickly do orders move through each stage?
What percentage of orders reach completion?
Which fulfillment stage needs the most improvement?
magento-orders-fulfillment:fulfillment_f.narration_text:
This funnel chart tracks orders through your fulfillment pipeline.

The formula counts orders at each stage: total orders placed, orders invoiced, orders shipped, and orders completed.

A healthy funnel shows minimal drop-off between stages. If 1000 orders come in, ideally 950+ complete successfully. Large gaps between stages reveal problems.

A big drop from orders to invoiced might indicate payment failures or fraud holds. From invoiced to shipped could be inventory or picking issues. Shipped to completed might indicate delivery problems or customer disputes.

Use this to prioritize process improvements. Fix the biggest gap first for maximum impact.

This answers: How efficient is our fulfillment process? Where are orders getting stuck? And what operational changes would have the biggest impact?

magento-orders-fulfillment:monthly_trend.title: Monthly Order Trends
magento-orders-fulfillment:monthly_trend.formula: Gross revenue summed per day over the selected period (area chart), with order count carried as context per day.
magento-orders-fulfillment:monthly_trend.formula_explanation: Sums order revenue over time periods (daily, weekly, or monthly depending on granularity setting). This shows revenue trajectory and helps identify trends.
how_to_read: The area chart shows revenue accumulation over time
Rising trends indicate revenue growth
Plateaus may indicate market saturation or seasonal slowdowns
Sharp drops warrant immediate investigation
Compare different periods to spot year-over-year changes
decisions_answered: Is our revenue growing, flat, or declining?
What periods show the strongest performance?
Are there seasonal patterns in our revenue?
How does current performance compare to historical trends?
magento-orders-fulfillment:monthly_trend.narration_text:
This area chart visualizes your revenue trajectory over time.

The formula sums order totals for each time period - daily, weekly, or monthly depending on your view setting.

The filled area helps you see the cumulative pattern. Rising trends are healthy. Flat lines might be okay or might indicate stagnation depending on your goals. Declining trends need attention.

Look for patterns. Many businesses see seasonal cycles - strong Q4 for retail, summer dips, back-to-school spikes. Understanding your seasonality helps with forecasting and planning.

Compare current trends to the same period last year. Are you outperforming? Underperforming? Context matters.

This chart answers: Where is our revenue heading? Are we meeting growth targets? And what seasonal or cyclical patterns should we plan around?

magento-orders-fulfillment:order_status_.title: Order Status Distribution
magento-orders-fulfillment:order_status_.formula: Order count by order status, shown as a donut; each slice is one status's share of total orders.
magento-orders-fulfillment:order_status_.formula_explanation: Counts orders in each status category to show the current state of your order pipeline. Healthy operations show most orders in completed states with minimal stuck or canceled orders.
how_to_read: Each donut slice represents one order status
Larger slices indicate more orders in that status
Complete/Shipped should typically be the largest segments
Processing segment size indicates fulfillment backlog
Canceled/Refunded segments show lost revenue
decisions_answered: What percentage of orders complete successfully?
Is there a fulfillment backlog building up?
How many orders are getting canceled?
Are there status bottlenecks affecting customer satisfaction?
magento-orders-fulfillment:order_status_.narration_text:
This donut chart breaks down your orders by their current status.

The formula counts orders in each status category - processing, complete, shipped, canceled, pending, and others.

A healthy distribution shows most orders completed or shipped successfully. Small processing segments mean fast fulfillment. Minimal cancellations indicate satisfied customers.

Warning signs to watch: A large or growing processing segment suggests fulfillment bottlenecks. High cancellation rates need investigation - are customers changing their minds, or are there product or shipping issues? Stuck pending orders frustrate customers and tie up inventory.

The legend shows exact counts. Track these over time to spot trends.

This answers: How healthy is our order pipeline? Are we fulfilling orders efficiently? And where are orders getting stuck or lost?

magento-orders-fulfillment:orders_by_hou.title: Orders by Hour
magento-orders-fulfillment:orders_by_hou.formula: Order count by hour of day (0-23), aggregated across all days in the selected period, shown as bars.
magento-orders-fulfillment:orders_by_hou.formula_explanation: Counts total orders placed during each hour of the day (0-23), aggregated across all days in the selected period. This reveals the daily rhythm of customer purchasing behavior.
how_to_read: Each bar represents one hour of the day (midnight to 11 PM)
Taller bars indicate more orders placed during that hour
Look for peak hours that consistently drive the most orders
Early morning and late night typically show lower activity
Multiple peaks may indicate different customer segments
decisions_answered: What time of day do customers place the most orders?
When should we ensure maximum site performance?
What hours need the most customer service coverage?
When should we schedule flash sales or promotions?
magento-orders-fulfillment:orders_by_hou.narration_text:
This bar chart shows order distribution across the 24 hours of the day.

The formula counts all orders and groups them by the hour they were placed. Each bar represents one hour, from midnight through 11 PM.

Taller bars mean more orders during that hour. Most e-commerce sites see patterns - often a morning rise, lunch peak, afternoon activity, and evening browsing turning into purchases.

Understanding hourly patterns helps with operations. Schedule promotions when customers are active. Ensure your site handles peak load. Staff customer support accordingly. Plan technical maintenance during quiet hours.

If you see unusual patterns - like strong late-night activity - investigate. International customers? Night shift workers? This insight shapes your strategy.

This answers: When do customers buy? How should we allocate resources throughout the day? And when is the best time to launch time-sensitive promotions?

magento-orders-fulfillment:payment_metho.title: Payment Methods
magento-orders-fulfillment:payment_metho.formula: Order count by payment method used at checkout, shown as horizontal bars; longer bars mean more orders on that method.
magento-orders-fulfillment:payment_metho.formula_explanation: Counts orders by the payment method used at checkout. This reveals customer payment preferences and helps optimize the checkout experience.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars compare order counts by payment method
Longer bars indicate more popular payment options
Compare to see which methods customers prefer
Low usage of certain methods may indicate friction or lack of awareness
Consider removing rarely-used options to simplify checkout
decisions_answered: Which payment methods do customers prefer?
Should we add or remove payment options?
Are there regional differences in payment preferences?
Which payment methods have the highest completion rates?
magento-orders-fulfillment:payment_metho.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows which payment methods your customers prefer.

The formula counts orders completed with each payment method - credit cards, PayPal, buy-now-pay-later options, and others.

Longer bars mean more orders used that method. Most stores see credit cards dominate, but trends are shifting toward digital wallets and flexible payment options.

Payment insights matter for optimization. If a method has very low usage, consider whether it adds value or just clutters checkout. If a popular regional payment method is missing, you might be losing sales.

Also consider conversion rates by payment method - some options may attract more browsers who don't convert. Others might have higher average order values.

This answers: How do customers prefer to pay? Should we adjust our payment options? And are there opportunities to increase conversions through better payment choices?

magento-orders-fulfillment:refund_analys.title: Refund Analysis
magento-orders-fulfillment:refund_analys.formula: A small panel of refund and cart-abandonment totals for the period: refunded orders, total refund amount, average refund rate, abandoned carts, and abandoned cart value.
magento-orders-fulfillment:refund_analys.formula_explanation: Tracks refund metrics including total refund amount, refund count, and refund rate as a percentage of revenue. Also includes related metrics like abandoned cart value.
how_to_read: Refund Rate shows what percentage of revenue is refunded
Refund Amount shows total dollars returned
Refund Count shows number of refund transactions
Compare to industry benchmarks (typically 5-10% for e-commerce)
Track trends over time to spot emerging issues
decisions_answered: What is our refund rate and is it acceptable?
How much revenue are we losing to refunds?
Are refund rates trending up or down?
Which products or categories have the highest refund rates?
magento-orders-fulfillment:refund_analys.narration_text:
This metrics panel shows key refund and return statistics.

The core formula is Refund Rate: refund amount divided by total revenue, times 100. This tells you what percentage of your sales come back as returns.

E-commerce typically sees 5-10% refund rates, though this varies by category. Apparel runs higher due to fit issues. Electronics may be lower but with higher dollar values.

Track these metrics over time. Rising refund rates might indicate quality issues, misleading product descriptions, or shipping damage. A sudden spike warrants immediate investigation.

The panel also shows related metrics like abandoned cart value - revenue left behind when customers don't complete checkout.

This answers: How healthy is our return rate? Are we losing excessive revenue to refunds? And where should we focus to reduce returns?

magento-orders-fulfillment:shipping_meth.title: Shipping Methods
magento-orders-fulfillment:shipping_meth.formula: Order count by shipping method selected at checkout, shown as horizontal bars; longer bars mean more orders on that method.
magento-orders-fulfillment:shipping_meth.formula_explanation: Counts orders by the shipping method selected. This shows customer shipping preferences and helps optimize shipping options and pricing.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars compare order counts by shipping method
Longer bars indicate more popular shipping options
Free shipping often dominates when offered
Express/expedited usage indicates urgency tolerance
Multiple methods with similar usage may indicate customer confusion
decisions_answered: Which shipping methods do customers choose most?
Is free shipping effective at driving conversions?
What percentage of customers pay for expedited shipping?
Should we adjust our shipping options or pricing?
magento-orders-fulfillment:shipping_meth.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down shipping method preferences.

The formula counts orders by which shipping option customers selected at checkout.

When free shipping is available, it typically dominates. But the ratio of customers choosing paid expedited options tells you about urgency tolerance. If many customers pay extra for speed, you might increase express pricing or offer it more prominently.

Look at the distribution. If several methods have similar low usage, customers might be confused by too many options. Simplification often improves conversion.

Consider seasonal patterns too. Holiday shoppers may choose express more often. Understanding this helps with carrier negotiations and inventory positioning.

This answers: How do customers prefer their orders shipped? Is our free shipping threshold effective? And should we adjust our shipping options to better match customer needs?

magento-orders-fulfillment:top_products.title: Top Products by Revenue
magento-orders-fulfillment:top_products.formula: One row per product with its total revenue, units sold and number of orders containing it, ranked by revenue (top 10).
magento-orders-fulfillment:top_products.formula_explanation: Ranks products by their total revenue contribution, summing all line item sales. This identifies your best-selling products by dollar value.
how_to_read: Products ranked from highest to lowest revenue
Revenue shows total sales value for each product
Units shows quantity sold
Orders shows how many transactions included this product
Click rows to see detailed product analytics
decisions_answered: Which products generate the most revenue?
How concentrated is revenue across our product catalog?
Which products should we prioritize for inventory?
What products are driving our business success?
magento-orders-fulfillment:top_products.narration_text:
This table ranks your products by total revenue generated.

The formula sums all line item revenue for each product, then sorts from highest to lowest.

Each row shows product name, SKU, total revenue, units sold, and order count. High revenue with high units means consistent bestsellers. High revenue with few units indicates premium or high-ticket items.

Revenue concentration matters. If your top 5 products drive most of your revenue, that's efficient but also risky. Diversification provides stability.

Use this for inventory prioritization - never let top sellers go out of stock. Focus marketing on proven winners. Consider bundling top products with slower movers.

This answers: What products matter most? How diversified is our revenue? And where should we focus our operational and marketing efforts?

magento-store-overview:category_performa.title: Category Performance
magento-store-overview:category_performa.formula: One row per product category with its share of total revenue (%) and order count, ranked by revenue. Revenue share = a category's revenue ÷ total revenue across categories.
magento-store-overview:category_performa.formula_explanation: Aggregates revenue and order counts by product category to show which categories drive the most business. Helps identify top-performing categories and underperformers that may need attention.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product category with its total revenue and order count
Sort by revenue to find your highest-earning categories
Sort by orders to see which categories have the most purchase activity
Compare revenue per order across categories to understand average ticket size
Low revenue with high orders indicates lower-priced category items
decisions_answered: Which product categories generate the most revenue?
Are there underperforming categories that need attention?
How is revenue distributed across our product catalog?
Which categories should we prioritize for marketing and inventory?
magento-store-overview:category_performa.narration_text:
This chart breaks down performance by product category.

The formula aggregates revenue and order counts for each category, giving you a clear view of which parts of your catalog drive the most business.

Reading this: Look for your top categories by revenue. Are they also your most-ordered categories? If a category has high revenue but few orders, it contains premium products. If it has many orders but low revenue, those are your accessible everyday items.

Compare categories to understand balance. Heavy reliance on one category is risky. A diversified catalog with multiple strong categories is more resilient.

This answers: Which categories matter most? Where should we invest in inventory and marketing? And is our catalog balanced or too concentrated?

magento-store-overview:customer_mix.title: Customer Mix
magento-store-overview:customer_mix.formula: Order count split by customer type (new vs returning), shown as horizontal bars over the selected period.
magento-store-overview:customer_mix.formula_explanation: Counts unique customers and categorizes them by type (new vs returning, guest vs registered, etc.). This shows the composition of your customer base during the selected period.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars compare customer counts across different types
Longer bars indicate more customers of that type
New vs Returning split shows acquisition vs retention balance
Guest vs Registered shows account creation adoption
Compare proportions to understand your customer portfolio health
decisions_answered: What percentage of our customers are new vs returning?
Are we retaining customers effectively?
How many customers create accounts vs checkout as guests?
Is our customer mix healthy for sustainable growth?
magento-store-overview:customer_mix.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down your customer base by type.

The formula counts unique customers and groups them by category - new versus returning, guest versus registered, and other relevant segments.

How to read this: Each bar represents one customer type. Longer bars mean more customers in that category. A healthy business typically shows strong returning customer numbers alongside steady new customer acquisition.

If new customers dominate, you may have retention issues. If returning customers dominate with few new ones, growth may stall. The ideal mix depends on your business stage and goals.

Guest versus registered splits help understand account adoption. More registered customers enable better marketing and personalization.

This answers: What does our customer portfolio look like? Are we balancing acquisition with retention? And how can we optimize our customer mix?

magento-store-overview:orders_heatmap.title: Orders by Day & Hour
magento-store-overview:orders_heatmap.formula: Order count by day of week and hour of day, shown as a heatmap; darker cells mean more orders placed at that day/hour.
magento-store-overview:orders_heatmap.formula_explanation: Aggregates order counts by the day of week and hour of day they were placed. This reveals when customers are most likely to complete purchases.
how_to_read: Rows represent days of the week (Sunday through Saturday)
Columns represent hours of the day (0-23)
Darker cells indicate higher order volume at that time
Lighter cells show lower activity periods
Look for patterns to identify peak shopping times
decisions_answered: When do customers place the most orders?
Are there specific day/hour combinations that peak?
When should we schedule marketing communications?
What hours need the most customer support coverage?
magento-store-overview:orders_heatmap.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals when your customers prefer to place orders.

The formula counts orders and groups them by day of week and hour of day. Each cell represents a specific time slot - for example, Tuesday at 2 PM.

Darker blue cells indicate high order volume. Lighter cells show quieter periods. Most e-commerce sites see patterns - often higher activity during lunch hours, evening browsing, and weekend shopping sprees.

Understanding these patterns is valuable. Schedule promotions and email sends to coincide with peak shopping times. Ensure customer support coverage matches demand. Plan maintenance or updates during quiet periods.

This chart answers: When do customers shop most? What days and hours drive the most orders? And how should we allocate resources throughout the week?

magento-store-overview:orders_trend.title: Orders Trend
magento-store-overview:orders_trend.formula: Total orders per day over the selected period (line chart).
magento-store-overview:orders_trend.formula_explanation: Counts total orders placed each day over the selected time period. This shows the volume of order activity and helps identify trends, seasonality, and anomalies.
how_to_read: The line shows daily order volume over time
Upward trends indicate growing demand or successful promotions
Spikes often correlate with marketing campaigns, sales events, or seasonal peaks
Dips may indicate inventory issues, website problems, or natural demand fluctuations
Compare weekday vs weekend patterns to understand customer shopping behavior
decisions_answered: Is order volume growing or declining over time?
What days or periods see the highest order activity?
Are there seasonal patterns in our order volume?
Did a recent promotion or event impact order counts?
magento-store-overview:orders_trend.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your daily order volume over time.

The formula is simple: count all orders placed each day. Each point represents one day's total order count.

To read this chart: Rising trends mean more orders are coming in. Falling trends need investigation. Look for patterns - do weekends differ from weekdays? Do certain months consistently outperform others?

Spikes usually indicate successful campaigns or seasonal demand. Sudden drops might signal technical issues, inventory problems, or market changes.

This answers key questions: Is our business growing? When do customers prefer to shop? And how did our recent initiatives impact order volume?

magento-store-overview:revenue_trend.title: Revenue Trend
magento-store-overview:revenue_trend.formula: Gross revenue summed per day over the selected period (area chart).
magento-store-overview:revenue_trend.formula_explanation: Sums the total revenue from all orders placed each day. Revenue is calculated from order totals including products, shipping, and taxes minus any discounts applied.
how_to_read: The area chart shows cumulative daily revenue over time
Higher peaks indicate strong revenue days
The filled area helps visualize total revenue accumulation
Compare revenue trends to order trends - divergence may indicate AOV changes
Sudden spikes often correlate with high-value orders or promotions
decisions_answered: Is revenue growing in line with our business goals?
What days generate the most revenue?
Are there revenue patterns that differ from order volume patterns?
How does revenue fluctuate throughout the selected period?
magento-store-overview:revenue_trend.narration_text:
This area chart visualizes your daily revenue over the selected time period.

The formula sums all order totals for each day. This includes product prices, shipping, and taxes, minus discounts.

Reading this chart: Higher areas mean more revenue that day. The filled area helps you see cumulative performance at a glance. Compare this to your orders trend - if revenue grows faster than orders, your average order value is increasing.

Watch for patterns. Weekends might differ from weekdays. Certain months may consistently outperform. Large spikes often indicate high-value orders or successful promotions.

This chart answers: Is revenue meeting targets? What days perform best? And how does revenue relate to order volume?

magento-store-overview:seasonal_alerts.title: Seasonal Alerts
magento-store-overview:seasonal_alerts.formula: One row per product flagged at or near its peak selling week, with its seasonal index and a recommended inventory action, ranked by seasonal index. Seasonal index = a product's current-week sales ÷ its average weekly sales (above 1.0 means above-average demand).
magento-store-overview:seasonal_alerts.formula_explanation: Identifies products entering their peak selling periods by comparing current week sales to historical averages. A seasonal index above 1.0 means above-average demand. Products with high indices and peak week flags need immediate inventory attention.
how_to_read: Products are listed with their current seasonal index
Index above 1.2 indicates significantly above-average demand
Index below 0.8 indicates below-average demand
Peak week indicator shows products at their historical high-demand period
Recommended actions suggest inventory and marketing responses
decisions_answered: Which products are entering peak demand periods?
Do we have adequate inventory for seasonal surges?
Which products need immediate restocking attention?
What seasonal patterns should we prepare for?
magento-store-overview:seasonal_alerts.narration_text:
The seasonal alerts table flags products entering peak selling periods.

The formula calculates a seasonal index by comparing current week sales to historical weekly averages. An index of 1.0 means normal demand. Above 1.2 signals a seasonal surge. Below 0.8 indicates a slow period.

The peak week flag marks products at their historically highest-demand week of the year. These need immediate attention to ensure stock availability.

The recommended action column suggests what to do - restock, increase marketing spend, or prepare for the rush.

This answers critical operational questions: Which products are about to surge? Do we have enough inventory? And what actions should we take right now to capitalize on seasonal demand?

magento-store-overview:status_distributi.title: Orders by Status
magento-store-overview:status_distributi.formula: Order count by order status (complete, processing, canceled, etc.), shown as a donut; each slice is one status's share of total orders.
magento-store-overview:status_distributi.formula_explanation: Counts orders in each status category (processing, complete, canceled, etc.). This provides a snapshot of your current order pipeline health.
how_to_read: Each slice represents one order status category
Larger slices indicate more orders in that status
Green typically indicates completed/healthy statuses
Red or orange may indicate issues requiring attention
The legend shows exact counts for each status
decisions_answered: How many orders are currently in each stage?
Is our fulfillment pipeline healthy?
Are there bottlenecks causing orders to pile up?
What percentage of orders get canceled or refunded?
magento-store-overview:status_distributi.narration_text:
This donut chart shows the distribution of orders across different status categories.

The formula simply counts orders in each status - processing, complete, shipped, canceled, and so on.

Each colored slice represents one status. Larger slices mean more orders in that category. A healthy distribution typically shows most orders in completed or shipped status, with minimal cancellations or stuck orders.

Watch for warning signs. A large processing segment might indicate fulfillment bottlenecks. Growing cancellation rates need investigation. Pending orders that stay pending too long affect customer satisfaction.

The legend provides exact counts so you can track specific numbers, not just proportions.

This answers: Where are our orders in the pipeline? Are there operational issues causing delays? And what percentage of orders complete successfully versus getting canceled?

magento-store-overview:top_products.title: Top 20 Products by Revenue
magento-store-overview:top_products.formula: One row per product with its total revenue, units sold and number of orders containing it, ranked by revenue (top 20). Avg price = revenue ÷ units sold.
magento-store-overview:top_products.formula_explanation: Calculates total revenue contribution for each product by summing all line item revenues, then ranks products by this total. Shows the top 20 revenue generators.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by total revenue generated
Revenue column shows the total sales value for each product
Qty column shows units sold
Orders column shows how many unique orders included this product
Click any row to drill into product-specific analytics
decisions_answered: Which products drive the most revenue?
Are our top sellers generating healthy margins?
How concentrated is revenue across products?
Which products should we prioritize for inventory and marketing?
magento-store-overview:top_products.narration_text:
This table ranks your top 20 products by total revenue generated.

The formula sums revenue from every line item for each product, then sorts from highest to lowest.

Each row shows one product with its SKU, total revenue, quantity sold, and number of orders it appeared in. Revenue concentration matters - if your top 5 products drive 80% of revenue, that's both an opportunity and a risk.

High revenue with high quantity means consistent sellers. High revenue with low quantity indicates premium products. Compare orders to quantity - if one order buys many units, you may have wholesale or bulk customers.

Use this to prioritize. Ensure top sellers never go out of stock. Consider bundling top products with slower movers. Focus marketing on proven winners.

This answers: What products matter most to our revenue? How diversified is our product portfolio? And where should we focus inventory and promotional efforts?

marketing-mix:blended_roas_trending.title: Blended ROAS Trending
marketing-mix:blended_roas_trending.formula: Daily ROAS trended as three lines: Google platform ROAS = Google platform revenue ÷ Google spend, Meta platform ROAS = Meta platform revenue ÷ Meta spend, and GA4 ROAS = GA4-attributed paid revenue (Google + Meta sources) ÷ total ad spend (line chart).
marketing-mix:blended_roas_trending.formula_explanation: Platform ROAS uses revenue reported by Google/Meta (often inflated due to self-attribution). GA4 ROAS uses Google Analytics 4 attribution, which is typically more conservative and closer to actual performance.
how_to_read: Solid line (green) shows Platform-reported ROAS - what Google/Meta claim
Dashed line (blue) shows GA4-attributed ROAS - independent measurement
Gap between lines indicates attribution discrepancy (platform over-reporting)
Values above 3.0x target line indicate healthy returns
Look for convergence or divergence trends over time
decisions_answered: Is our overall ROAS improving or declining over time?
How much are platforms over-reporting compared to GA4?
When did significant performance changes occur?
Are we meeting our ROAS targets consistently?
marketing-mix:blended_roas_trending.narration_text:
This chart tracks your Return on Ad Spend over 90 days with two different measurements.

The solid green line shows Platform ROAS - what Google and Meta report. They tend to over-claim credit. The dashed blue line shows GA4 ROAS - Google Analytics attribution, which is more conservative and usually more accurate.

The gap between these lines reveals how much platforms over-report. When lines are far apart, be cautious about trusting platform numbers.

Look for values above 3x - that's healthy. This chart helps you understand: Is ROAS improving? How much do platforms exaggerate? And when did major performance shifts happen?

formula_components.name: Platform ROAS
formula_components.formula: Platform-reported conversion value ÷ ad spend, per platform per day

formula_components.name: GA4 ROAS
formula_components.formula: GA4 paid revenue from Google + Meta sources ÷ (Google spend + Meta spend) per day

marketing-mix:channel_comparison.title: Channel Comparison
marketing-mix:channel_comparison.formula: One row per channel (Google Ads, Meta Ads) with spend, conversions, conversion value, impressions, clicks, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPC = spend ÷ clicks, platform ROAS = conversion value ÷ spend, GA4 ROAS = GA4 paid revenue ÷ spend, and CPA = spend ÷ conversions; ranked by spend.
marketing-mix:channel_comparison.formula_explanation: This table consolidates all key metrics by channel for side-by-side comparison. Green highlighting indicates strong performance, red indicates areas needing attention. Platform vs GA4 ROAS comparison reveals attribution gaps.
how_to_read: Green values indicate strong performance (high ROAS, low CPA, high CTR)
Red values highlight areas needing attention or optimization
Sort by any column to rank channels on that metric
Compare Platform ROAS vs GA4 ROAS to see attribution gap
Click any row to drill into that channel's detailed page
decisions_answered: Which channel is most efficient overall?
Where should we consider reallocating budget?
Which specific metrics need improvement by channel?
How big is the platform attribution gap for each channel?
marketing-mix:channel_comparison.narration_text:
This comparison table shows all key metrics side by side for each channel.

The formulas are standard: ROAS is revenue divided by spend. CPA is spend divided by conversions. CTR is clicks divided by impressions times 100.

Color coding helps interpretation: Green means strong performance - high ROAS, low CPA, high CTR. Red flags areas needing attention.

Compare the Platform ROAS column to GA4 ROAS - the difference shows how much each platform over-claims credit.

Use this table to answer: Which channel performs best? Where should budget shift? And which metrics need the most improvement?

marketing-mix:channel_mix_evolution.title: Channel Mix Evolution
marketing-mix:channel_mix_evolution.formula: Stacked-area share of ad spend by platform over time: for each period, platform spend ÷ total period spend × 100; granularity auto-set to daily, weekly or monthly by date range.
marketing-mix:channel_mix_evolution.formula_explanation: Shows how budget allocation between channels has shifted over 12 months. In absolute view, see actual dollar amounts. In percentage view, see relative share changes regardless of total spend fluctuations.
how_to_read: Stacked areas show relative spend proportion over 12 months
Toggle between Absolute (dollars) and Percentage (share) views
Growing area indicates increasing investment in that channel
Shrinking area indicates decreasing allocation
Watch for seasonal patterns or strategic shifts
decisions_answered: How has our channel investment strategy shifted over time?
Are we increasing or decreasing any specific channel?
What was our historical budget allocation strategy?
Did recent changes align with performance improvements?
marketing-mix:channel_mix_evolution.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows how your budget allocation has evolved over 12 months.

You can toggle between two views: Absolute shows actual dollar amounts spent. Percentage shows relative shares, which is useful when total budgets fluctuate.

A growing area means you're investing more in that channel. Shrinking means you're pulling back. Look for patterns - are shifts deliberate strategy changes or drift?

Use this to answer: How has investment strategy evolved? Are we scaling the right channels? And did past allocation changes correlate with better results?

marketing-mix:cpa_by_channel.title: CPA by Channel
marketing-mix:cpa_by_channel.formula: Daily Cost Per Acquisition per platform = platform spend ÷ platform conversions, plotted as one line per channel over time.
marketing-mix:cpa_by_channel.formula_explanation: Cost Per Acquisition shows how much you pay to acquire each customer through that channel. Lower CPA means more efficient customer acquisition. Spikes indicate problems; sustained increases warrant investigation.
how_to_read: Lower line position indicates cheaper customer acquisition
Compare channel lines to see relative efficiency
Spikes indicate temporary efficiency problems (investigate cause)
Sustained increases suggest structural issues needing attention
Target benchmark is typically $25 per acquisition
decisions_answered: Which channel acquires customers most cheaply?
Is CPA trending up or down over time?
When did acquisition cost spikes occur?
Are we meeting our CPA targets?
marketing-mix:cpa_by_channel.narration_text:
CPA trending shows how much you pay to acquire each customer, tracked over 30 days.

The formula: total spend divided by total conversions. Simple but crucial.

Lower is better - it means cheaper customer acquisition. Compare the channel lines to see which acquires customers more efficiently.

Watch for spikes - they indicate temporary problems worth investigating. If you see sustained increases over time, that's a structural issue needing attention. The typical target is 25 dollars per acquisition.

This answers: Which channel is cheapest for acquisition? Is CPA trending the right direction? And are you hitting your targets?

marketing-mix:efficiency_frontier.title: Efficiency Frontier
marketing-mix:efficiency_frontier.formula: Scatter of daily spend versus daily revenue, one point per day: X = total daily ad spend, Y = total daily platform conversion value; point ROAS = daily revenue ÷ daily spend, used for color coding.
marketing-mix:efficiency_frontier.formula_explanation: The efficiency frontier shows the relationship between spend and revenue, revealing diminishing returns. As you spend more, each additional dollar generates less incremental revenue - this is the point where the curve flattens.
how_to_read: X-axis shows daily spend amount, Y-axis shows daily revenue
Each point represents one day's performance
The curve shows the typical spend-to-revenue relationship
Steep curve = efficient spending; Flat curve = diminishing returns
Points above the curve indicate exceptionally efficient days
decisions_answered: At what spend level do returns start diminishing significantly?
Are we over-spending relative to achievable returns?
What is the optimal daily spend level for efficiency?
Should we scale up or pull back based on marginal returns?
marketing-mix:efficiency_frontier.narration_text:
The efficiency frontier reveals diminishing returns in your advertising spend.

Each point is one day. The X axis is how much you spent, Y axis is revenue generated. The curve shows the typical relationship.

Here's what to look for: A steep curve means spending efficiently - each dollar works hard. When the curve flattens, you've hit diminishing returns - extra spend generates less extra revenue.

This answers critical questions: At what spend level do returns diminish? Are you over-spending? What's the optimal daily budget? Use this to decide whether to scale up or pull back.

marketing-mix:mer_trending.title: MER Trending
marketing-mix:mer_trending.formula: Daily Marketing Efficiency Ratio = total Magento revenue ÷ total ad spend (Google + Meta), plotted as an area over time.
marketing-mix:mer_trending.formula_explanation: Marketing Efficiency Ratio measures total business revenue against ad spend, including organic and direct traffic. Unlike ROAS which only counts paid-attributed revenue, MER shows the holistic impact of marketing on the entire business.
how_to_read: Single line shows overall business marketing efficiency
Includes ALL revenue sources, not just paid-attributed
Green zone: 4.0x and above - excellent efficiency
Yellow zone: 3.0x to 4.0x - acceptable, monitor closely
Red zone: below 3.0x - action required
decisions_answered: How efficient is marketing for the whole business?
Is our overall marketing ROI at a healthy level?
Are organic channels contributing more to offset ad costs?
What is the true holistic return on marketing investment?
marketing-mix:mer_trending.narration_text:
MER - Marketing Efficiency Ratio - is the most holistic view of marketing effectiveness.

The formula: total revenue from ALL sources divided by total ad spend. Unlike ROAS, this includes organic traffic, direct visitors, email - everything.

The zones matter: Above 4x is excellent - your marketing drives strong overall business results. Between 3 and 4x is acceptable but watch closely. Below 3x requires action.

MER answers the big questions: How efficient is marketing for the whole business? Is overall ROI healthy? And are organic channels pulling their weight alongside paid?

marketing-mix:spend_by_channel.title: Spend by Channel
marketing-mix:spend_by_channel.formula: Donut of ad spend by platform: each slice = platform spend; slice share = platform spend ÷ total spend × 100; center shows total spend across all channels.
marketing-mix:spend_by_channel.formula_explanation: Each slice represents the percentage of total marketing budget allocated to that channel. The center value shows the combined spend across all advertising platforms.
how_to_read: Larger slice indicates higher budget allocation to that channel
Center value shows total spend across all channels for the period
Hover over any slice to see exact dollar amount and percentage
Click a slice to drill down into that channel's detailed performance
decisions_answered: How is our marketing budget distributed across channels?
Which channel receives the most investment?
Is our channel mix aligned with performance goals?
Should we rebalance spend between Google and Meta?
marketing-mix:spend_by_channel.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your marketing budget is distributed across channels over the last 30 days.

The formula is simple: each channel's spend divided by total spend, times 100, gives you the percentage share.

To read this chart: larger slices mean more budget allocation. The center shows your total spend. Hover over any slice for exact amounts.

This chart helps answer: How is budget distributed? Which channel gets the most? And should you rebalance between Google and Meta based on performance?

marketing-mix:spend_vs_revenue.title: Spend vs Revenue
marketing-mix:spend_vs_revenue.formula: Scatter of daily spend versus daily platform conversion value, one point per day per platform: X = platform daily spend, Y = platform daily platform conversion value; slope approximates ROAS.
marketing-mix:spend_vs_revenue.formula_explanation: Each point shows one day's spend and revenue for a specific channel. The slope and clustering of points indicate consistency and efficiency - steeper is better, tighter clustering means more predictable returns.
how_to_read: Each point represents one day's performance for a channel
Blue points = Google Ads, Purple points = Meta Ads
Steeper slope indicates better efficiency (more revenue per dollar)
Tight clustering shows consistent, predictable performance
Scattered points indicate variable or unpredictable returns
decisions_answered: Which channel delivers more consistent returns?
Are there outlier days that need investigation?
How does daily spend correlate with revenue by channel?
Which channel is more predictable for planning?
marketing-mix:spend_vs_revenue.narration_text:
This scatter plot compares daily spend versus revenue for each channel.

Blue dots are Google Ads, purple dots are Meta Ads. Each dot is one day.

Look for slope and clustering. A steeper slope means better efficiency - more revenue per dollar spent. Tight clusters mean predictable, consistent returns. Scattered points mean high variability.

This helps you understand: Which channel is more consistent? Are there outlier days to investigate? And which channel is more reliable for budget planning?

meta-creative:copy_length.title: Copy Length Analysis
meta-creative:copy_length.formula: Average CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100 by ad-copy length category (Short, Medium, Long, Very Long), ordered shortest to longest (bar chart).
meta-creative:copy_length.formula_explanation: Categorizes ad copy by character length and measures average CTR for each category. This reveals whether your audience responds better to concise messaging or detailed descriptions.
how_to_read: Vertical bars represent average CTR for each copy length category
Short copy is typically under 50 characters, Long over 150 characters
Higher bars indicate better engagement for that copy length
Compare adjacent categories to find the optimal copy length range
Consider product complexity when interpreting - some products need more explanation
decisions_answered: What ad copy length performs best for our audience?
Should we write shorter or longer ad descriptions?
Is there an optimal copy length sweet spot?
Do different products need different copy length strategies?
meta-creative:copy_length.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how ad copy length affects click-through rates.

Copy is categorized by character count: short under 50 characters, long over 150. The formula calculates average CTR for each length category.

Taller bars mean better engagement at that length. Compare adjacent categories to find your optimal range. Remember: complex products may legitimately need longer copy.

Use this to answer: What copy length works best? Should you write shorter or longer? And is there a sweet spot for your specific products?

meta-creative:cta_performance.title: CTA Button Performance
meta-creative:cta_performance.formula: Clicks and conversions by call-to-action button type, ranked by spend (grouped bar chart; conversions are purchase counts).
meta-creative:cta_performance.formula_explanation: Compares how different call-to-action button types (Shop Now, Learn More, Sign Up, etc.) perform in terms of driving clicks and conversions. This reveals which CTAs resonate most with your audience.
how_to_read: Grouped horizontal bars show clicks (blue) and conversions (green) per CTA type
Compare the ratio between clicks and conversions for each CTA
CTAs with high clicks but low conversions may attract wrong audience
CTAs with proportionally high conversions indicate strong intent signals
The top 8 CTA types are shown ranked by performance
decisions_answered: Which call-to-action buttons drive the most conversions?
Are certain CTAs attracting clicks without converting?
What CTA types should we prioritize in new creatives?
Is there a mismatch between click volume and conversion quality?
meta-creative:cta_performance.narration_text:
This chart compares call-to-action button performance across your Meta ads.

Blue bars show clicks, green bars show conversions for each CTA type like Shop Now, Learn More, or Sign Up.

Watch the ratio between clicks and conversions. High clicks with low conversions suggests the CTA attracts the wrong audience. Proportionally high conversions indicate strong purchase intent.

This answers key questions: Which CTAs drive most conversions? Are some CTAs wasting clicks? And what CTAs should new creatives use?

meta-creative:daily_trend.title: Daily Creative Performance
meta-creative:daily_trend.formula: Daily creative spend over the selected period alongside CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100 (area chart).
meta-creative:daily_trend.formula_explanation: This chart tracks daily advertising spend and click-through rate for all creative assets over the past 30 days. The spend line shows investment patterns while CTR indicates engagement quality.
how_to_read: The area chart shows daily spend amounts with gradient fill for easy trend visualization
Upward trends indicate increasing investment or engagement with creatives
Look for correlations between spend spikes and performance patterns
Hover over any point to see exact spend and CTR values for that day
Sharp drops may indicate paused campaigns or budget exhaustion
decisions_answered: How has our creative spend varied over the past month?
Are there specific days or patterns when performance peaks?
When did significant spend or CTR changes occur?
Is our daily creative investment consistent or volatile?
meta-creative:daily_trend.narration_text:
This chart displays your daily creative performance over the past 30 days.

The area chart shows your daily advertising spend with a gradient fill, making trends easy to spot at a glance.

To read this chart: look for upward trends indicating increased investment. Correlate spend spikes with performance outcomes. Hover over any point for exact values.

Sharp drops might signal paused campaigns or budget limits. This helps you understand: How has spend varied? When do performance peaks occur? And is your investment pattern consistent or volatile?

meta-creative:fatigue_alerts.title: Creative Fatigue Alerts
meta-creative:fatigue_alerts.formula: Creatives flagged for fatigue: fatigue score = (initial CTR − current CTR) ÷ initial CTR, where initial CTR is the average CTR in the first 7 days since launch and current CTR is the average over the last 7 days; status Fatigued (≥30% decline), Warning (20–30%), else Healthy. Ranked by fatigue score (table).
meta-creative:fatigue_alerts.formula_explanation: Detects creative fatigue by comparing initial CTR (first 7 days) to current CTR. Creatives with significant CTR decline are flagged for refresh or replacement. Early detection prevents wasted spend.
how_to_read: Status column shows Healthy (green), Warning (yellow), or Fatigued (red)
Decline percentage shows how much CTR has dropped from initial performance
Days Active indicates how long the creative has been running
Recent Spend shows investment at risk from fatigued creatives
Sort by Decline to prioritize creatives needing immediate attention
decisions_answered: Which creatives are experiencing performance fatigue?
When should we refresh or replace a creative?
How much budget is at risk from fatigued creatives?
What is the typical lifespan before fatigue sets in?
meta-creative:fatigue_alerts.narration_text:
This table detects creative fatigue before it drains your budget.

The formula compares initial CTR from the first week to current CTR. A decline over 30% triggers a Fatigued status, 20 to 30% shows Warning, under 20% is Healthy.

Status is color-coded for quick scanning. The Decline column shows the exact CTR drop. Days Active reveals creative lifespan. Recent Spend shows money at risk.

Sort by decline to prioritize action. This answers: Which creatives are fatiguing? When to refresh? How much budget is at risk? And what's typical creative lifespan?

meta-creative:format_performance.title: Performance by Creative Format
meta-creative:format_performance.formula: Spend by creative format type (video, image, share, link), with CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPC, CPM and creative count per format, ranked by spend (bar chart).
meta-creative:format_performance.formula_explanation: Aggregates performance metrics by creative format type (video, image, carousel, link ads) to reveal which format delivers the best engagement and efficiency for your audience.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent total spend by creative format type
Longer bars indicate higher investment in that format
Compare bar lengths to see relative budget allocation across formats
Color coding helps distinguish between format types quickly
The format with highest spend may not always have best CTR - check both metrics
decisions_answered: Which creative format receives the most investment?
How does spend distribution align with format performance?
Should we shift budget from underperforming formats to winning ones?
Are we testing enough variety across creative formats?
meta-creative:format_performance.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down performance by creative format - video, image, carousel, and link ads.

The formula aggregates spend and CTR for each format type, revealing which format works best for your audience.

Longer bars mean more investment in that format. Compare bar lengths to see budget allocation. Remember: highest spend doesn't always mean best CTR.

Use this to answer: Which format gets most investment? Does spend match performance? Should you shift budget? And are you testing enough format variety?

meta-creative:top_creatives.title: Top Creatives by Spend
meta-creative:top_creatives.formula: Top creative assets ranked by spend, showing spend, impressions, clicks, reach, purchases and purchase value, plus ROAS = purchase value ÷ spend, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPC = spend ÷ clicks, and CPM (table).
meta-creative:top_creatives.formula_explanation: Ranks your creative assets by spend, displaying key performance metrics including ROAS, CTR, CPC, and conversion counts. Color-coded ROAS helps quickly identify winners and underperformers.
how_to_read: Table rows are sorted by total spend over the last 30 days
ROAS is color-coded: green (3x+), yellow (2-3x), red (under 2x)
Thumbnail and format columns help identify creative quickly
Click any row to drill into detailed creative-level analytics
Use search and sort to find specific creatives or rank by different metrics
decisions_answered: Which creatives are getting the most budget allocation?
Are our highest-spend creatives also highest ROAS?
Which creatives should we scale up or pause?
What creative patterns appear in top performers?
meta-creative:top_creatives.narration_text:
This table ranks your creatives by spend, showing which assets receive the most investment and how they perform.

Key formulas: ROAS is revenue divided by spend. CTR is clicks over impressions times 100. CPC is spend divided by clicks.

ROAS is color-coded for quick scanning: green means 3x or better, yellow is 2 to 3x, red is under 2x.

Click any row for detailed analytics. Use search and sort to explore. This answers: Which creatives get most budget? Are high-spend creatives also high ROAS? And what should you scale or pause?

meta-overview:action_breakdown.title: Action Breakdown (Attribution)
meta-overview:action_breakdown.formula: Conversions by action type, split into 7-day-click attribution and 1-day-view attribution; total actions = 7-day click + 1-day view (stacked bar chart, one bar per action type).
meta-overview:action_breakdown.formula_explanation: Meta uses a default attribution window of 7-day click and 1-day view. This chart shows how conversions are attributed across these windows for each action type in the conversion funnel.
how_to_read: Blue segments show 7-day click attribution - conversions after ad click
Purple segments show 1-day view attribution - conversions after viewing without clicking
Longer bars indicate more total conversions for that action type
Purchase and Add to Cart are the most valuable funnel actions
Heavy view-through attribution suggests strong brand awareness impact
decisions_answered: How do conversions distribute between click and view attribution?
Is view-through attribution a significant conversion driver?
Which funnel stages show the most activity?
Should we adjust our attribution model expectations?
meta-overview:action_breakdown.narration_text:
This chart breaks down how Meta attributes conversions using their default 7-day click and 1-day view window.

The formula: 7-day click captures conversions within 7 days after someone clicks your ad. 1-day view captures conversions within 1 day of viewing your ad without clicking.

Reading this chart: Blue segments are click-through conversions - someone clicked and later converted. Purple segments are view-through - they saw the ad, didn't click, but converted anyway.

This matters because view-through can represent significant value, especially for brand awareness campaigns. Heavy view attribution suggests your ads influence purchase decisions even without direct clicks.

Key insights: How do click versus view conversions compare? Is view-through driving meaningful value? Which funnel stages show the most activity? This helps calibrate your attribution expectations.

meta-overview:adset_table.title: Ad Set Performance Matrix
meta-overview:adset_table.formula: One row per ad set ranked by spend, showing spend, impressions, reach, clicks plus CPM = spend ÷ impressions × 1000, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPC = spend ÷ clicks, and Meta learning-stage status.
meta-overview:adset_table.formula_explanation: This table shows ad set level performance, including learning status which indicates whether Meta has optimized delivery for the ad set. Ad sets in learning may show variable performance.
how_to_read: Ad sets are sorted by spend - highest investment at top
Learning Status shows optimization progress: SUCCESS (green), LEARNING (yellow), LIMITED (orange)
SUCCESS means Meta has optimized delivery for your target audience
LIMITED indicates insufficient data or budget for proper optimization
Click any ad set to drill into individual ad performance
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are successfully optimized vs still learning?
Are there ad sets stuck in learning limited status?
Which ad set optimization goals are performing best?
Should we consolidate ad sets to accelerate learning?
meta-overview:adset_table.narration_text:
This table displays ad set performance with a critical addition: learning status.

The metrics formulas are standard: CPM, CTR, and CPC. But the learning status column is key to understanding Meta's algorithm optimization.

Reading learning status: Green SUCCESS means Meta has gathered enough data to optimize delivery effectively. Yellow LEARNING means optimization is still in progress - expect variable performance. Orange LIMITED means the ad set lacks sufficient data or budget to exit learning phase.

Ad sets stuck in LIMITED for more than 5-7 days need intervention. Consider consolidating similar ad sets or increasing budget to accelerate learning.

Key questions: Which ad sets are optimized? Which are stuck? Should you consolidate for faster learning? This insight helps maximize algorithm efficiency.

meta-overview:age_demographics.title: Age Demographics
meta-overview:age_demographics.formula: Spend by age group, with CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100 and CPM per age group, ranked by spend (bar chart).
meta-overview:age_demographics.formula_explanation: Shows spend distribution and engagement metrics across age groups. Different age groups respond differently to advertising, with some showing higher engagement rates despite lower spend allocation.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show spend amount per age group
Longer bars indicate higher budget allocation to that demographic
35-54 age range typically shows highest seed/garden purchase intent
Compare CTR across groups - higher CTR may justify increased allocation
Click any bar to filter the page by that age group
decisions_answered: Which age groups receive the most ad spend?
Are we reaching our core customer demographic effectively?
Should we adjust targeting to focus on high-performing age groups?
Are younger demographics worth additional investment?
meta-overview:age_demographics.narration_text:
This chart shows how your Meta ad spend distributes across age demographics.

The formula: Each age group's spend divided by total spend shows allocation percentage. CTR by age group reveals which demographics engage most.

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more budget going to that age group. For gardening and seed products, the 35 to 65-plus range typically shows strongest purchase intent and engagement.

Key insight: High spend doesn't always equal high performance. Some age groups may have lower spend but higher CTR and conversion rates. These represent expansion opportunities.

Questions answered: Which ages get the most spend? Are you reaching your core demographic? Should you shift budget to high-engaging age groups? Click any bar to filter and investigate that demographic further.

meta-overview:campaign_performance.title: Campaign Performance
meta-overview:campaign_performance.formula: Top campaigns ranked by spend, showing spend, impressions, reach, clicks, objective and status, plus CPM = spend ÷ impressions × 1000, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPC = spend ÷ clicks, and ROAS = conversion value ÷ spend.
meta-overview:campaign_performance.formula_explanation: This table shows the top 20 campaigns by spend with key efficiency metrics. CPM measures cost per thousand impressions, CTR measures click-through rate, and CPC measures cost per click.
how_to_read: Campaigns are sorted by spend - highest investment at top
OUTCOME_SALES objective campaigns should show strong ROAS
ACTIVE status (green) means campaign is currently running
High CPM with low CTR indicates creative or targeting issues
Click any campaign name to drill into detailed campaign analysis
decisions_answered: Which campaigns receive the most budget allocation?
Are high-spend campaigns delivering efficient results?
Which campaigns should be paused or scaled?
Do campaign objectives align with performance outcomes?
meta-overview:campaign_performance.narration_text:
This table displays your top 20 Meta campaigns ranked by spend, with key performance metrics.

The formulas are standard advertising metrics: CPM is spend divided by impressions times 1000. CTR is clicks divided by impressions times 100. CPC is simply spend divided by clicks.

Reading this table: Campaigns at the top receive the most investment. Look for the Objective column - OUTCOME_SALES campaigns should show strong returns. Active campaigns have green status indicators.

Watch for warning signs: high CPM combined with low CTR suggests creative fatigue or poor audience targeting. These campaigns need attention.

This table answers crucial questions: Where's the budget going? Are top spenders performing well? Which campaigns need scaling or pausing? Click any campaign name to investigate further.

meta-overview:creative_asset_summary.title: Creative Asset Library
meta-overview:creative_asset_summary.formula: Count of unique creative assets in the Meta library, split into images and videos (count of distinct image hashes and distinct video IDs).
meta-overview:creative_asset_summary.formula_explanation: Shows the total number of unique creative assets (images and videos) in your Meta advertising library, along with how many are currently active in running ads.
how_to_read: Images count shows total unique image creatives uploaded
Videos count shows total unique video creatives uploaded
Compare active vs total to understand utilization rate
Regular creative refresh prevents ad fatigue
Click to navigate to the full Creative Library for asset management
decisions_answered: How large is our creative asset library?
What is the ratio of images to videos in our strategy?
Are we regularly refreshing creative or reusing old assets?
Do we have enough creative variety to prevent fatigue?
meta-overview:creative_asset_summary.narration_text:
This summary shows your Meta creative asset library at a glance.

The formula counts distinct image and video IDs in your account. We track both total assets and those actively running in ads.

Reading these cards: The images count shows your static creative inventory. Videos count shows motion creative assets. A healthy library has thousands of assets with regular additions.

Creative variety matters for Meta's algorithm. Ad fatigue sets in when the same users see the same creatives repeatedly. Fresh assets maintain engagement and performance.

Key questions answered: How large is the creative library? Are you image-heavy or video-focused? When were new assets last added? Click through to the full Creative Library for detailed asset management.

meta-overview:daily_trend.title: Daily Performance Trend
meta-overview:daily_trend.formula: Daily spend over the selected period, with daily CPM = spend ÷ impressions × 1000 and CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100 (area chart).
meta-overview:daily_trend.formula_explanation: This chart tracks daily spend, CPM, and CTR over a 30-day period. Spend is the raw dollar amount, CPM measures cost efficiency per thousand impressions, and CTR measures engagement quality.
how_to_read: The solid blue line shows daily advertising spend in dollars
Watch for spending spikes or dips that may indicate campaign changes or budget adjustments
Consistent upward trends in spend should correlate with proportional revenue increases
Look for day-of-week patterns - weekends often show different performance characteristics
Hover over any point to see exact values for that date
decisions_answered: Is our daily spend consistent or highly variable?
Are there specific days where performance significantly differs?
When did notable changes in spending patterns occur?
Should we adjust budget pacing based on day-of-week performance?
meta-overview:daily_trend.narration_text:
This chart tracks your Meta advertising performance over the last 30 days.

The formula is straightforward: we sum total spend per day, calculate CPM as spend divided by impressions times 1000, and CTR as clicks divided by impressions times 100.

To read this chart, follow the solid blue line showing daily spend. Look for patterns - consistent spending is healthy, while large spikes or dips may indicate campaign issues or intentional adjustments.

Pay attention to day-of-week patterns. Weekends often perform differently than weekdays for e-commerce. Hover over any point to see exact values.

This chart answers key questions: Is spend consistent? Which days perform best? When did notable changes occur? Use these insights to optimize your budget pacing strategy.

meta-overview:device_performance.title: Device Performance
meta-overview:device_performance.formula: Spend by device platform (mobile app, mobile web, desktop), with CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100 and CPM per device, ranked by spend (bar chart).
meta-overview:device_performance.formula_explanation: Shows advertising performance across device types - mobile app, mobile web, and desktop. Mobile typically dominates Meta advertising due to platform usage patterns.
how_to_read: Vertical bars show spend by device type
Mobile App typically shows highest volume on Meta platforms
Desktop often shows higher conversion rates despite lower volume
Mobile Web represents browser-based mobile users
Compare device performance to optimize cross-device strategy
decisions_answered: How does our spend distribute across devices?
Which device type shows the best engagement?
Should we adjust bids by device based on performance?
Is desktop worth higher investment despite lower volume?
meta-overview:device_performance.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how performance varies across device types.

The formula: Device spend divided by total spend shows allocation. Device CTR measures engagement quality per device.

Reading this chart: Mobile App dominates Meta platforms - that's where most users are. Mobile Web represents browser users. Desktop typically has lower volume but often higher conversion rates and order values.

Here's the insight: Mobile may drive volume, but desktop users often convert better. Consider your attribution carefully - users may discover on mobile and purchase on desktop.

Key questions: How does spend distribute? Which device shows best engagement? Should you bid differently by device? This helps optimize your cross-device strategy.

meta-overview:gender_demographics.title: Gender Distribution
meta-overview:gender_demographics.formula: Share of spend by gender = gender spend ÷ total spend × 100 (donut chart).
meta-overview:gender_demographics.formula_explanation: Shows how advertising spend is distributed between male and female audiences. The center percentage highlights the dominant gender in your audience mix.
how_to_read: Pie slices show spend proportion by gender
Pink represents female audience, blue represents male
Center percentage shows the dominant gender share
Gardening products often skew female (60-70%) in purchasing
Compare spend share to conversion share for efficiency insights
decisions_answered: What is our audience gender split?
Does our spend allocation match our customer base?
Should we adjust targeting toward a specific gender?
Is one gender significantly more efficient than the other?
meta-overview:gender_demographics.narration_text:
This pie chart shows gender distribution in your Meta advertising.

The formula is straightforward: gender spend divided by total spend gives you the percentage split.

Reading this chart: Pink represents female audience, blue represents male. The center percentage highlights your dominant audience gender.

For gardening and seed products, female purchasers often represent 60 to 70 percent of customers. If your ad spend doesn't roughly match this ratio, you may be over or under-investing in certain segments.

Key questions: What's the gender split? Does it match your customer base? Is one gender showing better efficiency? Use this to align spend allocation with actual purchase behavior.

meta-overview:platform_distribution.title: Platform Distribution
meta-overview:platform_distribution.formula: Share of spend by Meta platform = platform spend ÷ total spend × 100 (Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network, Messenger), ranked by spend (donut chart).
meta-overview:platform_distribution.formula_explanation: Each slice represents the percentage of Meta ad spend allocated to that platform - Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network, Messenger, or Threads. The center value shows combined spend across all Meta platforms.
how_to_read: Larger slices indicate higher budget allocation to that Meta platform
Facebook (blue) typically dominates spend for most advertisers
Instagram (pink) often shows higher engagement rates despite smaller share
Audience Network and Messenger represent extended reach opportunities
Click any slice to filter the entire page by that platform
decisions_answered: How is our Meta budget distributed across platforms?
Is Facebook receiving the majority of spend as expected?
Should we increase Instagram allocation based on performance?
Are we leveraging Audience Network and Messenger effectively?
meta-overview:platform_distribution.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your Meta advertising budget is distributed across platforms.

The formula is simple: each platform's spend divided by total spend, times 100, gives you the percentage share.

Facebook typically dominates - that's normal for most advertisers. Instagram often shows higher engagement rates, making it valuable despite potentially smaller spend share.

Audience Network extends your reach beyond Facebook and Instagram apps. Messenger and Threads are newer placements worth monitoring.

Key questions this answers: How is budget distributed? Is Facebook dominant as expected? Should you shift more to Instagram? Are you maximizing extended reach through Audience Network?

Click any slice to filter the entire page and dive deeper into that platform's performance.

meta-overview:region_performance.title: Top Regions Performance
meta-overview:region_performance.formula: Spend by US state ranked by spend, with CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, CPM, and CPC = spend ÷ clicks per region (table).
meta-overview:region_performance.formula_explanation: Shows advertising performance by US state, enabling geographic analysis of spend distribution and efficiency. High-spend regions should correlate with strong customer concentrations.
how_to_read: Table shows top 20 states ranked by advertising spend
California, Texas, and Florida typically lead in spend volume
Compare CPM across regions - some states cost more to reach
CTR variations may indicate regional message resonance
Click any region to filter and analyze geographic performance
decisions_answered: Which states receive the most advertising investment?
Are there regional CPM or CTR variations to exploit?
Should we expand or reduce investment in specific states?
Do regional patterns align with customer distribution?
meta-overview:region_performance.narration_text:
This table shows advertising performance by US state, ranked by spend.

The formulas: Region spend sums all advertising in that state. Region CTR and CPC measure efficiency metrics per geography.

Reading this table: Top states by population like California, Texas, and Florida typically lead. But look beyond volume - compare CPM across regions. Some states cost more to reach than others.

CTR variations reveal regional message resonance. If certain states show higher engagement, your creative or offer may particularly resonate there. These are expansion opportunities.

Key questions: Which states get the most investment? Are there CPM or CTR variations to exploit? Should you adjust regional targeting? Does your spend match your customer geographic distribution?

meta-prospecting:adset_performance.title: Ad Set Performance
meta-prospecting:adset_performance.formula: One row per prospecting ad set ranked by spend, showing spend, reach, impressions, clicks, average CPM, average CTR, average frequency, and cost per 1k reach = spend ÷ reach × 1000 (table).
meta-prospecting:adset_performance.formula_explanation: Provides granular visibility into ad set level performance within your prospecting campaigns. Ad sets control targeting, so this view helps identify which audience segments are most efficient to reach.
how_to_read: Sorted by spend to show your highest-investment ad sets first
Compare cost per 1k reach across different targeting approaches
Green values indicate efficient ad sets worth scaling
Red values suggest targeting that is too narrow or saturated
Click any row to see detailed ad set configuration
decisions_answered: Which ad sets within campaigns are performing best?
Are certain targeting approaches more efficient than others?
Should we consolidate underperforming ad sets?
Which ad sets have room for budget scaling?
meta-prospecting:adset_performance.narration_text:
This table dives into ad set level performance.

While campaigns set objectives, ad sets control targeting. This is where you define who sees your prospecting ads. Each row is an ad set with its own audience definition.

Compare the cost per reach across ad sets. Big differences reveal which targeting works. Lookalike audiences might outperform interest targeting. Broad audiences might beat narrow ones.

The top 25 by spend are shown. Focus on the ones spending real money. If a high-spend ad set has poor efficiency, that's budget going to waste.

Use this to find your best audiences and double down on them.

meta-prospecting:age_gender.title: Demographic Summary
meta-prospecting:age_gender.formula: Spend and efficiency by age and gender segment, showing gender spend share = gender spend ÷ total spend × 100 and cost per 1k reach = spend ÷ reach × 1000 per segment (table).
meta-prospecting:age_gender.formula_explanation: Summarizes prospecting performance by demographic segments including age and gender. Identifies which demographic combinations receive the most spend and which deliver the most efficient reach.
how_to_read: Percentage circles show male vs female spend distribution
Top Spending Segment reveals where most budget flows
Most Efficient Segment shows the best cost-per-reach demographic
Compare top spending vs most efficient to find optimization opportunities
Consider if current allocation matches your target customer profile
decisions_answered: Is our prospecting reaching the right demographic mix?
Which age/gender segment is most efficient to reach?
Are we over-investing in any demographic segment?
Should we adjust targeting to focus on efficient segments?
meta-prospecting:age_gender.narration_text:
This summary card gives you a quick demographic overview.

The percentage circles show your gender split. Most businesses skew one way or another based on their products and Meta's audience availability.

Two key callouts matter here. Top Spending Segment tells you where most of your budget actually goes - maybe 35-44 females. Most Efficient Segment tells you where you get the best cost per reach - maybe 25-34 males.

If these don't match, you have an optimization opportunity. You might be spending heavily on a segment that's expensive to reach while underinvesting in a cheap, efficient one.

This helps align your demographic targeting with efficiency.

meta-prospecting:campaign_efficiency.title: Campaign Efficiency
meta-prospecting:campaign_efficiency.formula: Prospecting campaigns ranked by efficiency (lowest first), showing cost per 1k reach = spend ÷ reach × 1000, CTR, and frequency = impressions ÷ reach; only campaigns with at least $100 spend (table).
meta-prospecting:campaign_efficiency.formula_explanation: Ranks all prospecting campaigns by their reach efficiency. The primary sort is by cost per 1,000 reach - the most efficient campaigns appear first. Color coding highlights performance tiers to quickly spot winners and underperformers.
how_to_read: Green cost values indicate efficient campaigns (under $5 per 1k reach)
Yellow indicates moderate efficiency ($5-$8 per 1k reach)
Red flags expensive reach (over $8 per 1k reach)
Sort by any column to change the ranking perspective
Click any row to drill into that specific campaign
decisions_answered: Which campaigns deliver the most efficient new audience reach?
Are there campaigns we should pause due to poor efficiency?
Which campaigns should receive additional budget?
How does frequency relate to campaign efficiency?
meta-prospecting:campaign_efficiency.narration_text:
This table ranks your prospecting campaigns by reach efficiency.

The key metric is cost per 1,000 reach. Green means efficient - under $5 to reach a thousand new people. Yellow is moderate. Red means expensive reach that needs attention.

CTR and frequency columns add context. High frequency means the same people see your ads repeatedly - that's fine for retargeting but problematic for prospecting. You want fresh reach.

Look at the top performers. What makes them work? Creative, audience, or objective? Look at the bottom performers. Should they be paused or restructured?

This table helps you allocate budget to what's working and cut what's not.

meta-prospecting:day_of_week.title: Spend by Day of Week
meta-prospecting:day_of_week.formula: Total prospecting spend grouped by day of week over the selected period (bar chart).
meta-prospecting:day_of_week.formula_explanation: Aggregates prospecting spend by day of week to reveal patterns in budget delivery. This helps identify if Meta is spending more on certain days and whether those days correlate with better or worse performance.
how_to_read: Each bar shows total spend for that day of week across the period
Taller bars mean more budget was spent on that day
Weekend patterns may differ from weekdays
Look for consistent patterns vs random variation
Consider correlating with conversion data for optimization
decisions_answered: Which days of the week receive the most prospecting spend?
Is weekend performance different from weekday performance?
Should we implement day-parting strategies?
Are there days we should increase or decrease budget?
meta-prospecting:day_of_week.narration_text:
This bar chart reveals your prospecting spend patterns across the week.

Meta's delivery algorithm decides when to show your ads. But understanding the pattern helps you optimize. Maybe Mondays see heavy spend but poor conversion. Maybe weekends are efficient.

Each bar is a day's aggregated spend. Look for the tall bars - that's where most budget flows. Look for short bars - those might be opportunities or dead zones.

Day-parting strategies can help. If you know Tuesdays convert well, you might want to ensure budget is available. If Saturdays underperform, consider manual budget adjustments.

This answers: When are we spending, and should we change the pattern?

meta-prospecting:lookalike_audiences_tab.title: Lookalike Audiences
meta-prospecting:lookalike_audiences_tab.formula: Lookalike audiences available for prospecting, listing each audience's lookalike ratio and approximate size range (lower and upper bounds), ranked by ratio (table).
meta-prospecting:lookalike_audiences_tab.formula_explanation: Lists all lookalike audiences available for prospecting campaigns. Lookalike audiences find people similar to your existing customers.
