Smaller percentages (1%) are closer matches but smaller pools; larger percentages (5%+) are bigger pools but less precise.
how_to_read: Green 1% lookalikes are highest quality - closest match to your seed audience
Yellow 3% lookalikes balance quality and scale
Blue 5%+ lookalikes offer maximum reach but lower precision
Size columns show approximate audience pool size
Click any row to see campaigns using that audience
decisions_answered: What lookalike audiences are available for prospecting?
Should we test smaller, higher-quality lookalikes?
Are we over-relying on broad lookalikes?
Which lookalike tier provides the best efficiency?
meta-prospecting:lookalike_audiences_tab.narration_text:
This table shows your lookalike audiences.

Lookalikes are Meta's way of finding people similar to your customers. The percentage tells you how selective the matching is. A 1% lookalike finds the closest matches - highest quality but smaller pool. A 5% lookalike casts a wider net.

For prospecting, start with 1% lookalikes. They're your best bet for finding likely converters. As those saturate, expand to 3%, then 5%.

The size columns tell you how many people are in each audience. Bigger isn't always better. A 1% lookalike with 2 million people might outperform a 5% with 10 million.

Use this to plan your audience expansion strategy.

meta-prospecting:monthly_trend.title: Monthly Performance Trend
meta-prospecting:monthly_trend.formula: Daily spend over the selected period alongside cost per 1k reach = spend ÷ reach × 1000 (dual-axis line chart, presented as a monthly/period trend).
meta-prospecting:monthly_trend.formula_explanation: This chart displays two key metrics over 12 months: total spend and cost efficiency (cost per 1,000 unique people reached). The dual-axis view lets you see if increased spending is driving proportional reach or if efficiency is declining.
how_to_read: Blue line shows monthly spend in dollars across prospecting campaigns
Green line shows cost per 1,000 reach - lower values indicate better efficiency
Diverging lines suggest changing efficiency - if spend rises but cost/reach rises faster, audience saturation may be occurring
Click any point to filter the page to that specific month
Seasonal patterns help plan budget allocation throughout the year
decisions_answered: Is our prospecting spend efficiency improving or declining over time?
When did significant performance shifts occur?
Are there seasonal patterns we should account for in budget planning?
Should we scale up or pull back based on cost trends?
meta-prospecting:monthly_trend.narration_text:
This dual-axis chart tracks your Meta prospecting performance over 12 months.

The blue line shows how much you spent each month. The green line shows cost per 1,000 reach - this is your efficiency metric. Lower is better.

Here's the key insight: if spend goes up but cost per reach goes up faster, you're hitting audience saturation. That means you're paying more to reach the same number of new people.

Look for patterns. Q4 often shows higher costs due to auction competition. Spring might be your sweet spot for efficient prospecting.

This chart answers: Is efficiency improving? When did shifts happen? And should you scale up or pull back?

meta-prospecting:objective_performance.title: Performance by Objective
meta-prospecting:objective_performance.formula: Total spend by campaign objective, with cost per 1k reach = spend ÷ reach × 1000 and campaign count per objective, ranked by spend (bar chart).
meta-prospecting:objective_performance.formula_explanation: Compares spending across different Meta campaign objectives like Traffic, Sales, Awareness, and Leads. The horizontal bars show total spend, while the color gradient indicates efficiency - helping identify which objectives deliver the best reach for your investment.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher spend allocation to that objective
Compare bar lengths to see budget distribution across objectives
Click any bar to filter the entire page to that objective
Labels show exact spend amount for precision
Use this to identify over or under-invested objectives
decisions_answered: Which campaign objectives receive the most budget?
Should we shift budget between Traffic, Sales, and Awareness objectives?
Are we over-investing in any particular objective?
Which objectives deliver the best prospecting results?
meta-prospecting:objective_performance.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down your prospecting spend by campaign objective.

Each bar represents an objective type - Sales, Traffic, Awareness, Leads. The length shows how much budget that objective receives.

Campaign objectives matter because they tell Meta's algorithm what to optimize for. Traffic objectives reach people likely to click. Sales objectives target likely buyers. Awareness maximizes reach.

The question is: Is your budget allocation aligned with your goals? If you want new customers, Sales objectives should be well-funded. If you're building brand awareness in new markets, Awareness might deserve more budget.

Use this to rebalance your prospecting strategy.

meta-prospecting:platform_distribution.title: Platform Distribution
meta-prospecting:platform_distribution.formula: Share of prospecting spend by Meta platform = platform spend ÷ total spend × 100 (Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network, Messenger), ranked by spend (donut/pie chart).
meta-prospecting:platform_distribution.formula_explanation: Shows how your prospecting budget is distributed across Meta platforms including Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network, Messenger, and Threads. The donut visualization makes it easy to see which platforms dominate your media mix.
how_to_read: Each slice represents spend on a specific Meta platform
Center value shows total prospecting spend across all platforms
Larger slices indicate higher budget allocation
Hover for exact spend amounts and percentages
Click a slice to filter the page to that platform
decisions_answered: How is our prospecting budget split between Facebook and Instagram?
Are we utilizing Audience Network and Messenger effectively?
Should we test more spend on emerging platforms like Threads?
Is our platform mix aligned with where our target audience spends time?
meta-prospecting:platform_distribution.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your prospecting spend distribution across Meta platforms.

Facebook typically dominates - it has the largest user base. Instagram often comes second, especially for visual product discovery. Audience Network extends your reach beyond Meta's owned properties. Messenger and Threads are usually smaller slices.

The center shows your total spend. Each slice is a platform's share.

Here's what to consider: Your platform mix should match where your prospective customers spend time. Younger demographics lean Instagram. Broader demos are on Facebook. Test allocation shifts and watch your cost per reach change.

This helps answer: Are we on the right platforms for our audience?

meta-prospecting:region.title: Top Regions by Spend
meta-prospecting:region.formula: Prospecting spend by US state ranked by spend, with cost per 1k reach = spend ÷ reach × 1000 and CPM per region (table).
meta-prospecting:region.formula_explanation: Ranks US states by prospecting spend to reveal geographic concentration. Identifies which regions receive the most investment and which deliver the most efficient reach, helping optimize geographic targeting strategy.
how_to_read: Sorted by spend to show your highest-investment states
Green cost values indicate efficient regions (under $6 per 1k reach)
Yellow indicates moderate efficiency ($6-$8 per 1k reach)
Red flags expensive regions (over $8 per 1k reach)
Compare CPM to see auction costs by region
decisions_answered: Which states receive the most prospecting investment?
Are certain regions more efficient to reach than others?
Should we exclude high-cost, low-value regions?
Are we under-investing in efficient geographic markets?
meta-prospecting:region.narration_text:
This table shows your top states by prospecting spend.

Geographic performance varies significantly. Coastal metros like California and New York are typically expensive - high competition for attention. Midwest states often offer cheaper reach but may convert differently.

The cost per reach column is your efficiency signal. Green regions are cheap to reach. Red regions are expensive. But efficiency isn't everything - you also need those regions to convert.

Look for mismatches. If you're spending heavily in an expensive region but conversions are low, consider reallocation. If a cheap region shows promise, consider increasing investment.

This helps you optimize your geographic strategy.

meta-retargeting:adset_performance.title: Retargeting Ad Sets Performance
meta-retargeting:adset_performance.formula: One row per retargeting ad set, showing spend, impressions, reach, clicks, average frequency, average CTR, average CPM, average CPC, and days active, plus a health status (Healthy / Warning / Fatigue) derived from frequency and CTR; ranked by spend.
meta-retargeting:adset_performance.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table of all retargeting ad sets with key metrics. Status badges automatically flag ad sets showing signs of fatigue or those performing well.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank ad sets on that metric
Green status = healthy (low frequency, high CTR)
Yellow status = warning (approaching fatigue thresholds)
Red status = fatigue (high frequency or low CTR, needs action)
Click any row to drill into detailed ad set performance
decisions_answered: Which specific ad sets are performing best?
Which ad sets need immediate attention due to fatigue?
How do metrics compare across all retargeting ad sets?
Which campaigns should receive more or less budget?
meta-retargeting:adset_performance.narration_text:
This data table provides a comprehensive view of all your retargeting ad sets with key performance metrics.

The status column uses a simple formula: If frequency is below 3 and CTR above 2%, it's marked healthy with a green badge. If frequency reaches 3 to 5 or CTR drops to 1 to 2%, you see a yellow warning. Red fatigue status appears when frequency exceeds 5 or CTR falls below 1%.

You can sort by any column - try sorting by spend to see where budget goes, by ROAS to find top performers, or by frequency to identify fatigued audiences.

The table answers practical questions: Which ad sets deserve more budget? Which need creative refresh or pausing? How does each campaign's retargeting component perform?

Click any row to dive deeper into that specific ad set's daily performance, creative breakdown, and audience details.

formula_components.name: Status
formula_components.formula: Fatigue if frequency > 5 or CTR < 1%; Warning if frequency 3–5 or CTR 1–2%; otherwise Healthy

meta-retargeting:audience_inventory.title: Custom Audience Inventory
meta-retargeting:audience_inventory.formula: Inventory of custom audiences for retargeting, listing each audience's type, size range, retention days, number of ad sets using it, and number of ad sets excluding it; ranked by ad sets using.
meta-retargeting:audience_inventory.formula_explanation: Inventory of all custom audiences available for retargeting, showing size estimates, usage across ad sets, and identification of underutilized audiences.
how_to_read: Type badges show audience source: Custom (email), Website, Engagement, Lookalike
Size estimates show approximate audience reach
Ad Sets Using column reveals which audiences are most actively targeted
Excluded In shows how often audiences are used as exclusions
Low usage audiences may represent untapped opportunities
decisions_answered: Which custom audiences are available but underutilized?
What are the sizes of our key retargeting audiences?
Which audiences are most commonly used in targeting?
Are we properly excluding purchasers from prospecting campaigns?
meta-retargeting:audience_inventory.narration_text:
This inventory table shows all custom audiences available for your Meta retargeting campaigns.

The type badges help categorize audiences: Custom typically means email uploads from Klaviyo, Website means pixel-based audiences, Engagement tracks platform interactions, and Lookalike are Meta-generated similar audiences.

Size estimates show approximate audience counts - useful for forecasting reach. The Ad Sets Using column reveals your most utilized audiences, while Excluded In shows exclusion usage - important for preventing ads to recent purchasers.

Key insights come from finding patterns: Are your largest audiences being fully utilized? Are there audiences with zero ad sets using them - potential untapped opportunities? Is your purchaser audience properly excluded from prospecting?

This inventory helps you understand your full retargeting toolkit and identify both over-relied-upon audiences and hidden opportunities.

meta-retargeting:audience_overlap.title: Audience Overlap Matrix
meta-retargeting:audience_overlap.formula: Heatmap matrix of shared ad sets between audience pairs = count of distinct ad sets that target both audiences in a pair (only pairs sharing more than one ad set).
meta-retargeting:audience_overlap.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows how many ad sets use the same audience pairs. High overlap means you may be competing with yourself and over-spending on the same users across multiple ad sets.
how_to_read: Each cell shows how many ad sets target both audiences in that row/column pair
Darker blue cells indicate higher overlap (more ad sets sharing audiences)
Diagonal represents self-overlap (how many ad sets use each individual audience)
High overlap pairs may cause internal competition and inflated CPMs
Consider consolidating or excluding audiences with high overlap
decisions_answered: Which custom audiences have the most overlap?
Are we targeting the same users from multiple ad sets?
Should certain audiences be consolidated?
Where is internal competition potentially driving up costs?
meta-retargeting:audience_overlap.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals overlap between your custom audiences used in retargeting.

Each cell shows how many ad sets use both the row and column audience simultaneously. Darker colors mean higher overlap - more ad sets competing for the same users.

Why does this matter? When multiple ad sets target overlapping audiences, you're essentially bidding against yourself. This internal competition drives up your CPMs and wastes budget reaching the same users multiple times through different ad sets.

Look for the darkest cells off the diagonal. These pairs have significant overlap and may need attention. Consider consolidating overlapping audiences into a single ad set, or use audience exclusions to eliminate overlap.

The diagonal shows how widely each individual audience is used across your ad sets. A very high number suggests that audience might be over-utilized and could benefit from segmentation.

meta-retargeting:audience_roas.title: ROAS by Audience Type
meta-retargeting:audience_roas.formula: ROAS = total revenue ÷ total spend, segmented by audience type; only audience types with more than $100 spend, ranked by ROAS (bar chart).
meta-retargeting:audience_roas.formula_explanation: Return on Ad Spend measures the revenue generated for every dollar spent on advertising. This chart segments ROAS by audience type to identify which retargeting segments deliver the best returns.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show ROAS for each audience type (higher is better)
Green bars indicate strong ROAS (5x+), yellow is acceptable (2-5x), red needs attention (<2x)
Bar length represents relative performance - longer bars mean better returns
The vertical reference line at 4x marks the typical breakeven threshold
Hover to see exact ROAS values, spend amounts, and revenue generated
decisions_answered: Which audience segments deliver the highest return on ad spend?
Should we increase budget for high-performing audience types?
Which audience types are underperforming and need optimization?
How do email-based audiences (Klaviyo synced) compare to website visitors?
meta-retargeting:audience_roas.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down Return on Ad Spend by audience type for your retargeting campaigns.

The formula is straightforward: ROAS equals total revenue divided by total spend. Revenue comes from purchase events tracked by Meta.

Reading the chart is simple - longer bars mean higher ROAS, which is what you want. The color coding helps quickly identify performance: green bars at 5x or above are excellent, yellow between 2 and 5x is acceptable, and red below 2x needs attention. The dashed line at 4x marks a common breakeven threshold.

This chart answers critical questions: Which segments return the most revenue per dollar? Where should you shift budget? Are your email list audiences from Klaviyo outperforming website visitor retargeting?

Use these insights to prioritize budget toward your highest-performing audience types while investigating or pausing underperformers.

meta-retargeting:daily_trend.title: Daily Retargeting Performance Trend
meta-retargeting:daily_trend.formula: Daily retargeting spend over the selected period alongside daily ROAS = daily revenue ÷ daily spend (only days with at least $10 spend); multi-line chart.
meta-retargeting:daily_trend.formula_explanation: This multi-line chart tracks daily retargeting performance over 30 days, showing both spend levels and efficiency metrics to identify trends, anomalies, and seasonal patterns.
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily spend amounts (left axis) - track investment levels
Green line shows daily ROAS (right axis) - track efficiency over time
Look for correlation: does higher spend maintain strong ROAS?
Identify anomaly dates where ROAS spikes or crashes unexpectedly
Watch for weekly patterns - performance may vary by day of week
decisions_answered: Is our retargeting efficiency improving or declining over time?
When did significant performance changes occur?
Can we scale spend while maintaining acceptable ROAS?
Are there weekly patterns we should optimize around?
meta-retargeting:daily_trend.narration_text:
This dual-axis line chart tracks your retargeting performance day by day over the past 30 days.

The blue line shows daily spend, measured on the left axis. The green line shows daily ROAS, measured on the right axis. This combination reveals both how much you're investing and how efficiently that investment performs.

Key things to watch: Does ROAS hold steady as you scale spend, or does it drop? That indicates whether you have room to grow. Sudden ROAS spikes or crashes on specific dates warrant investigation - what changed? Also look for weekly patterns - many brands see different performance on weekdays versus weekends.

This trend analysis helps answer fundamental questions: Is efficiency improving over time? When did things change? And critically - can we spend more while maintaining good returns, or are we hitting diminishing returns?

meta-retargeting:frequency_ctr.title: Frequency vs CTR Analysis
meta-retargeting:frequency_ctr.formula: Per ad set, average frequency = impressions ÷ reach plotted against CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, ranked by spend (bar chart; both are pre-computed per ad-set-day in the mart).
meta-retargeting:frequency_ctr.formula_explanation: Frequency measures how many times each unique user sees the ad on average. CTR (Click-Through Rate) indicates the percentage of impressions that resulted in clicks. This chart reveals the relationship between ad exposure frequency and engagement.
how_to_read: Each bar represents an ad set, sorted by total spend (highest spend at top)
Blue bars show CTR percentage - higher is better (target: 2%+)
Orange bars show average frequency - lower is typically better (target: 1-3)
Look for inverse relationship: high frequency often correlates with low CTR (fatigue)
Ad sets with both high frequency (5+) and low CTR (<1%) indicate severe audience fatigue
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing signs of audience fatigue?
At what frequency level does CTR start declining significantly?
Which ad sets should have their frequency caps adjusted?
Are we over-exposing certain audiences while under-utilizing others?
meta-retargeting:frequency_ctr.narration_text:
This chart analyzes the relationship between ad frequency and click-through rate for your retargeting campaigns.

The formula combines two key metrics: Frequency equals impressions divided by reach - how many times each user sees your ad. CTR equals clicks divided by impressions times 100.

To read this chart, look at the horizontal bars. Blue bars show CTR - you want these higher, targeting 2% or above. Orange bars show frequency - you generally want these lower, ideally between 1 and 3.

The key insight here is the inverse relationship: as frequency increases, CTR typically decreases. This is audience fatigue - people stop engaging when they see the same ad too many times. Ad sets showing frequency above 5 with CTR below 1% are red flags requiring immediate attention.

Use this chart to identify fatigued audiences, set optimal frequency caps, and decide which ad sets need creative refresh.

meta-retargeting:frequency_distribution.title: Frequency Distribution
meta-retargeting:frequency_distribution.formula: Spend and average CTR grouped into frequency buckets (<1, 1–2, 2–3, 3–5, 5–10, 10+) where frequency = impressions ÷ reach (bar + line chart).
meta-retargeting:frequency_distribution.formula_explanation: Groups ad delivery into frequency buckets to show how spend and CTR vary by exposure level. This reveals the optimal frequency range and where diminishing returns begin.
how_to_read: Each bucket shows spend (blue bars) and CTR (orange line) for that frequency range
The 1-2 frequency bucket typically shows highest CTR - this is the "optimal zone"
Buckets 5+ often show declining CTR - the "fatigue zone" where users ignore ads
Compare spend distribution: is most budget in optimal or fatigue zones?
If significant spend is in 5+ buckets, consider tighter frequency caps
decisions_answered: What percentage of spend goes to optimal vs fatigued frequency ranges?
At what frequency does performance start declining?
Should we implement or adjust frequency caps?
How much budget is wasted on over-exposed users?
meta-retargeting:frequency_distribution.narration_text:
This chart shows how your retargeting spend and performance distribute across different frequency levels.

The frequency buckets group ad delivery by how many times users see your ads: less than once, 1 to 2 times, 2 to 3 times, and so on up to 10 plus.

The blue bars show how much you spend in each bucket. The orange line overlay shows CTR at each frequency level.

Here's the key insight: the 1 to 2 frequency bucket typically shows the highest CTR - this is your optimal zone where users respond best. As frequency increases beyond 3 to 5, CTR often drops sharply - this is the fatigue zone where users start ignoring your ads.

Look at where your spend concentrates. Ideally, most budget should be in the 1 to 3 range. If you're spending heavily in the 5 plus buckets, you're likely wasting money on fatigued users and should implement tighter frequency caps.

ml-churn-analysis:action_summary.title: Recommended Actions
ml-churn-analysis:action_summary.formula: Customers grouped by the model's recommended retention action (win-back, re-engagement, discount-driven offer, etc.); bars show customer count per action, with the primary delivery channel and total CLV-at-risk each action covers (top 8 actions). CLV-at-risk is scaled linearly to the active horizon.
ml-churn-analysis:action_summary.formula_explanation: Aggregated playbook: how many customers map to each canonical retention action (win-back, newsletter incentive, discount-driven offer, etc.) and how much CLV each action covers.
how_to_read: Bars show customer counts for each action; the list below adds channel and CLV-at-risk.
Bar color = primary delivery channel (email, sms, crm, …).
Numbers are scaled to the active horizon — switching horizons re-scales CLV linearly.
decisions_answered: What playbook should we run, and how big is each cohort?
Where is the dollar return concentrated by action?
Are channel constraints (e.g., SMS opt-in) limiting our ability to execute?

ml-churn-analysis:avg_churn_probability.title: Average Churn Probability
ml-churn-analysis:avg_churn_probability.formula: Average Churn Probability = mean predicted churn probability across all scored customers, shown as a percentage
ml-churn-analysis:avg_churn_probability.formula_explanation: The mean probability — across every customer the model has scored — that they churn within the selected horizon (30 / 90 / 180 days). Reported as a percentage so the whole base can be summarized in one number.
how_to_read: A single percentage representing average risk across the entire scored base.
Color cues: green is healthy, yellow is a watchlist, red signals broad disengagement.
Compare across horizons — 180d will normally read higher than 30d.
Pair with High-Risk count: a moderate average can still hide a dangerous tail of high-risk customers.
decisions_answered: How healthy is the customer base overall?
Is overall churn pressure rising or falling vs. the previous run?
Should retention budgets be increased company-wide?

ml-churn-analysis:churn_segments.title: Churn Archetypes
ml-churn-analysis:churn_segments.formula: Customers grouped by predicted behavioral churn archetype (Low-Frequency, Engagement Drop-off, Price-Sensitive, etc.); bars show population size per archetype, with share %, count and CLV-at-risk per archetype.
ml-churn-analysis:churn_segments.formula_explanation: Each customer is tagged with the behavioral archetype that best explains why they're at risk. The chart shows population size per archetype; the legend list adds share %, count, and CLV-at-risk.
how_to_read: Bars show absolute count — the sales-funnel-style legend below shows share % and dollar exposure.
Most retail bases are dominated by one archetype (typically Low-Frequency); rare archetypes still matter because their per-customer CLV is often higher.
Click a bar to filter customer list to that archetype (where supported).
decisions_answered: Which behavioral pattern explains most of our churn risk?
Are there small archetypes with disproportionate revenue exposure?
Should we run different playbooks for different archetypes?

ml-churn-analysis:clv_at_risk.title: Total CLV at Risk
ml-churn-analysis:clv_at_risk.formula: Total CLV at Risk = sum across customers of (predicted churn probability × predicted customer lifetime value), scaled to the selected horizon
ml-churn-analysis:clv_at_risk.formula_explanation: Expected revenue we lose if the predicted churn actually happens — every customer's probability × their predicted CLV, scaled to the active horizon. This is the dollar version of the risk count.
how_to_read: Headline number is dollars at risk over the selected horizon.
Switching horizons (30 / 90 / 180) re-scales CLV linearly — 180d will show ~6× the 30d number.
An estimate: customers may be saved with intervention or churn anyway despite efforts.
decisions_answered: How big is the financial exposure if we do nothing?
What's the upper bound on retention budget that still pencils out?
Is the at-risk dollar value concentrated in a few customers or spread across many?

ml-churn-analysis:customers_scored.title: Customers Scored
ml-churn-analysis:customers_scored.formula: Customers Scored = count of distinct customers with a valid churn prediction in this scoring run
ml-churn-analysis:customers_scored.formula_explanation: Total customers the model produced predictions for in this run, scoped to the active horizon's training cutoff. Treat it as the denominator for every other count on the page.
how_to_read: Big number = total customers with predictions for this run.
Sub-text shows the prediction horizon (30 / 90 / 180 days).
If this number drops sharply between runs, investigate the scoring pipeline — not all customers may have been processed.
decisions_answered: What's the prediction coverage for this run?
Does this match our active customer base?
Is the run complete, or did the pipeline drop customers?

ml-churn-analysis:decile_analysis.title: Decile Lift Analysis
ml-churn-analysis:decile_analysis.formula: Customers ranked by predicted churn probability and split into 10 equal-size deciles (D1 = highest risk, D10 = lowest); plots the average churn probability per decile, plus customer count and CLV-at-risk per decile.
ml-churn-analysis:decile_analysis.formula_explanation: Decile lift checks whether the model meaningfully separates risky customers from safe ones. Customers are ranked by churn probability, sliced into 10 equal-size buckets, and the average churn probability per bucket is plotted. D1 = the 10% with the highest predicted risk; D10 = the 10% with the lowest. A good model shows a steeply downward curve from D1 → D10.
how_to_read: X-axis: D1 (highest risk decile) → D10 (lowest risk decile). Each decile holds ~1/10th of the scored base.
Y-axis: average churn probability for the customers in that decile.
Steep drop from D1 to D10 = strong rank-ordering. Flat curve = the model isn't separating risky from safe customers well.
If you target only D1+D2, you're focusing budget on the top 20% riskiest customers.
Mini-grid below shows D1–D6 customer counts and CLV at risk for context.
decisions_answered: Is the model good at ranking — i.e., is D1 actually riskier than D10?
How concentrated is the risk? If 70% of total at-risk CLV sits in D1+D2, that's where retention spend belongs.
How many customers do we cover if we target only the top deciles?

ml-churn-analysis:expected_retained_valu.title: Expected Retained Value (ERV)
ml-churn-analysis:expected_retained_valu.formula: Expected Retained Value = (1 − average churn probability) × average predicted customer lifetime value × customers scored
ml-churn-analysis:expected_retained_valu.formula_explanation: Best estimate of the revenue you keep, assuming no extra retention work. Inverts churn: probability of staying × predicted CLV × population.
how_to_read: The dollar value the model expects you to retain naturally over the horizon.
Compare with CLV at Risk — together they sum to the total predicted CLV.
ERV grows with horizon: longer windows include more expected revenue.
decisions_answered: What revenue are we likely to keep without any campaigns?
How does retained revenue compare to the at-risk pool?
Is the natural retention base growing or shrinking?

ml-churn-analysis:feature_importance.title: Top Churn Drivers
ml-churn-analysis:feature_importance.formula: Top 10 model features ranked by their share of total SHAP gain, for either the behavioral (why) classifier or the binary risk classifier; each bar is a feature's % of total gain.
ml-churn-analysis:feature_importance.formula_explanation: Which features the model is leaning on most. 'Behavioral' shows drivers from the why-classifier (the archetype model); 'Risk Model' shows drivers from the binary risk classifier.
how_to_read: Bars are % of total gain — relative, not absolute.
A single dominant driver is normal; the long tail still matters for personalization.
Feature names are humanized (e.g., recency_log → 'Days Since Last Order').
decisions_answered: What signals does the model rely on most?
Are the top drivers ones we can actually influence (newsletter, recency) or only observe (tenure)?
Which levers should the retention team focus on?

ml-churn-analysis:high_risk_customers.title: High-Risk Customers
ml-churn-analysis:high_risk_customers.formula: High-Risk Customers = count of customers the model places in the High or Critical risk tiers
ml-churn-analysis:high_risk_customers.formula_explanation: Number of customers the model places in the top two risk tiers. These are the people most likely to churn in the selected horizon and are the priority targets for retention spend.
how_to_read: Big number = how many customers are in High or Critical risk bands.
Sub-text shows the share of the total scored base.
Risk bands come from percentile cuts on churn_probability — they are relative, not absolute.
decisions_answered: How large is the population that needs immediate retention?
Do we have enough campaign capacity to reach all of them?
Is the high-risk pool growing run-over-run?

ml-churn-analysis:priority_customers_tab.title: Priority Customers
ml-churn-analysis:priority_customers_tab.formula: One row per customer, sorted by predicted churn probability (highest first) for the active horizon; columns include churn probability, risk tier, primary churn driver, recommended action, predicted CLV and CLV-at-risk (CLV columns scaled to the active horizon).
ml-churn-analysis:priority_customers_tab.formula_explanation: Account-level drilldown: who specifically is at risk, why, and what the model recommends. Use this to feed retention campaigns or sales outreach.
how_to_read: Sorted by churn risk for the active horizon — top of the list is highest priority.
Risk Tier chip and color = the 5-band classification (Critical/High/Medium/Low/Minimal).
Primary Driver = top SHAP feature for that customer (humanized).
Recommended Action is archetype-aware: e.g., Price-Sensitive customers see a discount-driven offer rather than 'manual review'.
CLV at Risk and Pred CLV columns are scaled to the active horizon.
decisions_answered: Who should retention campaigns target this week?
Why is each individual at risk — and is it actionable?
What channel and offer fit each customer?

ml-churn-analysis:retained_revenue.title: Retained Revenue Forecast
ml-churn-analysis:retained_revenue.formula: Retained Revenue Forecast = (1 − average churn probability) × average predicted customer lifetime value × customers scored, shown as the retained share of total predicted revenue
ml-churn-analysis:retained_revenue.formula_explanation: The probability-weighted revenue we expect to keep over the horizon — same idea as ERV, presented as a 'retained share' progress bar to mirror Revenue at Risk.
how_to_read: Green bar shows the share of total predicted revenue that's expected to be retained.
Reads inversely to Revenue at Risk — together they should sum to ~100%.
Useful for board-level framing of the customer base's natural durability.
decisions_answered: How much revenue do we keep with zero retention activity?
Is the retention floor strong enough to fund growth investments?

ml-churn-analysis:churn_rate.title: Churn Rate
ml-churn-analysis:churn_rate.formula: Churn Rate = customers whose predicted churn probability clears the at-risk threshold ÷ total customers scored
ml-churn-analysis:churn_rate.formula_explanation: Share of customers whose model-estimated churn probability over the horizon clears the at-risk threshold. Threshold is set per horizon during training.
how_to_read: Headline percentage tracks the at-risk share of the active book.
Compare across horizons (30/90/180/365) to see how risk concentrates with time.
Compare to actual realised churn from prior cohorts to validate calibration.
decisions_answered: What share of customers are flagged as likely to churn this horizon?
Is the at-risk pool growing or shrinking versus prior runs?

ml-churn-analysis:retention_rate.title: Retention Rate
ml-churn-analysis:retention_rate.formula: Retention Rate = 1 − churn rate (the share of customers expected to stay over the horizon)
ml-churn-analysis:retention_rate.formula_explanation: Inverse of churn rate — share of customers expected to stay over the horizon.
how_to_read: Headline percentage shows the retained share of the active book.
Use alongside Churn Rate; the two sum to ~100%.
Sustained increases indicate either better product-market fit or a healthier acquisition mix.
decisions_answered: What share of customers are expected to stay this horizon?
Is retention trending up or down over recent runs?

ml-churn-analysis:revenue_at_risk.title: Revenue at Risk
ml-churn-analysis:revenue_at_risk.formula: Revenue at Risk = total CLV at risk, expressed as a share of total predicted revenue
ml-churn-analysis:revenue_at_risk.formula_explanation: Same dollar figure as CLV at Risk, framed as a share of total predicted revenue. The progress bar makes it easy to see the proportion of the book that is exposed.
how_to_read: Bar fills proportionally to the share of total predicted revenue that is at risk.
Red color emphasizes the loss-if-no-action interpretation.
Use alongside Retained Revenue Forecast — the two bars together mirror at-risk vs retained share.
decisions_answered: What share of forecasted revenue is exposed to churn?
Is the proportion at risk acceptable, or does it warrant intervention?

ml-churn-analysis:risk_distribution.title: Risk Distribution
ml-churn-analysis:risk_distribution.formula: Customers split into 5 risk bands (Minimal / Low / Medium / High / Critical) by percentile cuts on predicted churn probability; each bar shows the band's customer count, share, CLV-at-risk and the churn-probability range it spans.
ml-churn-analysis:risk_distribution.formula_explanation: Customers split into five tiers. The model assigns the bands by percentile cuts on churn probability, so 'High' and 'Critical' are always your top-risk slices regardless of the absolute probability.
how_to_read: Each bar is one band; height/length = number of customers in that band.
Tooltip shows count, share, and dollar CLV at risk for the band.
Critical + High together define the 'high-risk' KPI.
Min/max columns show the probability range each band actually spans.
decisions_answered: How is risk distributed across the customer base?
Where does the dollar exposure concentrate — top tiers or middle?
Is the model producing a usable spread, or is everyone clumped in one band?

ml-churn-analysis:segment_performance.title: Segment Performance
ml-churn-analysis:segment_performance.formula: Per churn archetype: average predicted churn probability, customer count, share of base and CLV-at-risk, sorted by CLV-at-risk; bars show average churn probability per archetype.
ml-churn-analysis:segment_performance.formula_explanation: Risk-quality view of archetypes: even if Low-Frequency dominates by count, an archetype like Price-Sensitive may have higher avg risk per customer. Sorted by avg churn probability so the riskiest archetypes appear first.
how_to_read: Bars show avg churn probability per archetype (not count).
Legend list adds share %, count, and CLV at risk so you see size + risk together.
A small archetype with high avg risk and high avg CLV is the highest-leverage target.
decisions_answered: Which archetype carries the highest per-customer risk?
Where is the best ROI for personalization — high avg risk × high avg CLV?
Should we shift retention spend from a large-but-low-risk segment to a small-but-high-risk one?

ml-models:channel_attribution.title: Channel Attribution (90 Days)
ml-models:channel_attribution.formula: Per acquisition channel over the last 90 days: total revenue, sessions and revenue per session, ranked by revenue (last-click attribution from Google Analytics).
ml-models:channel_attribution.formula_explanation: Shows revenue attribution by acquisition channel from Google Analytics 4. Each bar represents total revenue attributed to sessions from that channel. This uses last-click attribution to show which channels directly drive purchases.
how_to_read: Channels are ranked by total revenue, highest at top
Bar length represents total revenue from that channel over 90 days
Direct traffic often dominates for established brands with strong brand awareness
Organic Search shows the value of SEO investment
Paid channels (Paid Search, Paid Social) show direct advertising ROI
Email channel shows the revenue contribution of email marketing
Toggle Show All to see all channels or Top 10 for focus
decisions_answered: Which marketing channels drive the most revenue?
What is the revenue contribution mix across channels?
Is our SEO investment paying off in organic search revenue?
How does email marketing compare to paid channels?
ml-models:channel_attribution.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows revenue attribution across your marketing channels over the past 90 days.

Direct traffic typically dominates for established brands, often contributing 30% or more of revenue. These are customers who type your URL directly or have bookmarked your site - they already know your brand.

Organic Search is your SEO investment paying off. Strong organic search revenue indicates good search rankings for relevant keywords. This is valuable because there is no direct cost per click.

The Paid channels - Paid Search and Paid Social - show the direct revenue impact of your advertising spend. Compare these to your ad spend to calculate true channel ROAS.

Email often shows strong efficiency despite smaller total volume. Email customers are warm leads who have opted in, so conversion rates tend to be higher.

The Unassigned channel captures sessions where GA4 could not determine the source. High unassigned revenue might indicate tracking issues worth investigating.

Use this chart to inform budget allocation. Channels with high revenue relative to investment should potentially receive more budget. Channels with low revenue despite high spend might need optimization or reduction.

formula_components.name: Revenue
formula_components.formula: GA4 revenue summed per channel over the last 90 days

ml-models:churn_risk_matrix.title: Churn Risk vs CLV Matrix
ml-models:churn_risk_matrix.formula: Scatter/bubble of customers bucketed by predicted total CLV (x-axis) versus predicted churn probability (y-axis), with bubble size proportional to the customer count in each bucket; reference lines at 80% churn and $200 CLV split the plot into four strategic quadrants.
ml-models:churn_risk_matrix.formula_explanation: This scatter plot visualizes the relationship between customer lifetime value and churn probability. Customers are bucketed into CLV and churn ranges, then plotted as bubbles sized by customer count. Reference lines at 80% churn and $200 CLV create four strategic quadrants.
how_to_read: Each bubble represents a group of customers with similar CLV and churn probability
Bubble size indicates how many customers fall in that bucket - larger means more customers
Red dashed line at 80% marks high churn risk threshold
Green dashed line at $200 marks high value threshold
Bottom-right quadrant (High Value, Low Risk) are your VIP customers to protect
Top-right quadrant (High Value, High Risk) needs urgent win-back campaigns
Hover on bubbles to see exact counts and CLV totals
decisions_answered: Which customer segments need the most urgent attention?
How much high-value CLV is at risk of churning?
Where should we prioritize retention marketing spend?
What is the distribution of our customer base across risk/value quadrants?
ml-models:churn_risk_matrix.narration_text:
This scatter plot is one of the most actionable visualizations on this page, showing the relationship between customer value and churn risk.

The horizontal axis represents customer lifetime value from $0 to $1000, while the vertical axis shows churn probability from 0 to 100%. Each bubble is a group of customers, sized by how many fall in that bucket.

The two reference lines create four strategic quadrants. The vertical green line at $200 separates low-value from high-value customers. The horizontal red line at 80% separates low-risk from high-risk customers.

Your immediate priority is the top-right quadrant: High Value, High Risk. These are customers with significant CLV who are likely to churn. Even a small number of customers here can represent millions in at-risk value. These customers need personalized win-back campaigns immediately.

The bottom-right quadrant contains your VIPs: high value and low churn risk. These customers need VIP treatment to maintain their loyalty - early access, exclusive offers, and premium service.

The top-left quadrant shows low-value, high-risk customers. These may not warrant expensive retention efforts, but cost-effective re-engagement emails can help.

Use this matrix to allocate retention budget where it will have the highest impact on preserving customer value.

ml-models:clv_segment_details.title: CLV Segment Details
ml-models:clv_segment_details.formula: One row per CLV segment (VIP / High / Medium / Low / New) showing customer count, segment's share of total CLV, average predicted churn probability and total predicted CLV, sorted by total CLV.
ml-models:clv_segment_details.formula_explanation: This table provides a detailed breakdown of each CLV segment showing customer counts, value contribution percentages, churn risk levels, and total CLV. Average churn probability is calculated from Klaviyo predictive analytics for each segment.
how_to_read: Segments are sorted by Total CLV descending - highest value segments at top
Customer count shows how many profiles are in each tier
% of CLV bar visualizes each segment contribution to total value
Avg Churn badge shows retention risk: Green (<50%), Yellow (50-80%), Red (>80%)
Total CLV shows the aggregate predicted lifetime value for all customers in segment
Click any row to filter the page to that segment
decisions_answered: Which segments contribute the most to our lifetime value?
What is the average churn risk within each value tier?
Are high-value customers at higher or lower churn risk than others?
Which segments need the most urgent retention attention?
ml-models:clv_segment_details.narration_text:
This table breaks down each CLV segment with detailed metrics for strategic decision-making.

The segments are ordered by total CLV contribution, with your most valuable tiers at the top. Notice how VIP and High Value together typically represent over $70 million in lifetime value.

The churn probability column is particularly important. You will often see an inverse relationship: VIP customers with the highest value tend to have lower churn risk, around 65%, while New/Trial customers with the lowest value show churn probabilities above 85%.

This makes intuitive sense - customers who have purchased more have demonstrated commitment to your brand. However, the absolute value at risk matters most. If VIP customers have 65% churn probability and represent $36 million in CLV, that is still $23 million potentially at risk.

Use this table to prioritize retention efforts. Focus on segments where you have both high value AND high churn risk - these represent your urgent action items. The percentage bars help visualize the relative importance of each segment.

ml-models:cohort_ltv_analysis.title: Cohort LTV Analysis
ml-models:cohort_ltv_analysis.formula: Per monthly acquisition cohort: average historic CLV (realized to date), average predicted CLV (expected future), and average total CLV (historic + predicted combined) from Klaviyo predictive analytics.
ml-models:cohort_ltv_analysis.formula_explanation: Tracks how customer lifetime value varies by acquisition cohort. Each cohort represents customers who first appeared in the system during that month. The chart shows three CLV components: realized historic value, predicted future value, and total combined CLV.
how_to_read: X-axis shows monthly acquisition cohorts over the past 12 months
Blue line shows average Historic CLV (realized purchases to date)
Green line shows average Predicted CLV (expected future purchases)
Purple line shows Total CLV (Historic + Predicted combined)
Older cohorts naturally have higher Historic CLV due to more time to mature
Declining Total CLV in recent cohorts may indicate lower acquisition quality
Large gaps between Total and Historic indicate strong predicted future value
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?
How does CLV mature as cohorts age?
Which acquisition periods brought our most valuable customers?
What is the expected future value of recent customer cohorts?
ml-models:cohort_ltv_analysis.narration_text:
This multi-line chart tracks average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort, revealing trends in customer quality over time.

Each point represents customers who joined in that month. The blue line shows Historic CLV - the actual realized value from purchases to date. The green line shows Predicted CLV - expected future purchases based on Klaviyo predictive analytics. The purple line shows Total CLV combining both.

Older cohorts on the left naturally show higher Historic CLV because customers have had more time to make purchases. A cohort from 12 months ago might show $150 in average Historic CLV, while last month's cohort might only show $25 - this is expected.

What matters more is the Total CLV trend. If you see declining Total CLV across recent cohorts, this could indicate lower quality acquisitions. Perhaps marketing channels shifted, or advertising creative is attracting different customer profiles.

The gap between Total and Historic CLV represents future value potential. Healthy newer cohorts should show a large gap - indicating the model predicts significant future purchases even though realized value is still low.

Use this to answer: Is our customer acquisition improving or declining? When did we acquire our best customers, and can we replicate those conditions?

formula_components.name: Historic CLV
formula_components.formula: Average realized customer lifetime value per cohort

formula_components.name: Predicted CLV
formula_components.formula: Average predicted future lifetime value per cohort

ml-models:expected_order_timeline.title: Expected Order Timeline
ml-models:expected_order_timeline.formula: Customers segmented by when they are predicted to place their next order (Overdue / Next 7 / Next 30 / Next 90 / Beyond 90 days), with customer count and predicted revenue potential per window.
ml-models:expected_order_timeline.formula_explanation: Segments customers by when they are expected to place their next order based on Klaviyo predictive analytics. The timeline shows the distribution of expected orders across time windows, along with the predicted revenue potential for each window.
how_to_read: Horizontal bar shows customer distribution across expected order timeframes
Red segment shows Overdue customers who should have ordered by now
Yellow segment shows customers expected to order within 7 days
Green segment shows expected orders in 30 days, Blue for 90 days
Segment width proportional to customer count in that window
Numbers on segments show customer count and predicted revenue
Legend below maps colors to time windows with additional detail
decisions_answered: How many customers are overdue for their expected order?
What is the predicted revenue pipeline for the next 7-30-90 days?
Which customers should receive replenishment reminders?
How well does the prediction model align with actual order patterns?
ml-models:expected_order_timeline.narration_text:
This timeline visualization shows when your customers are expected to place their next order, based on Klaviyo predictive analytics.

The horizontal bar is divided into time windows, each sized by how many customers fall in that bucket. Reading left to right, you see the urgency of expected orders.

The red Overdue segment is your immediate action item. These are customers who, based on their historical purchase patterns, should have ordered by now but have not. They might need a gentle reminder or might be showing early signs of churn. Consider a replenishment campaign targeting this segment.

The yellow Next 7 Days segment represents your warmest leads. These customers are predicted to order soon - timing your marketing outreach to coincide with their expected purchase window can increase conversion rates.

Green and Blue segments show your 30 and 90 day pipeline respectively. These customers are engaged but not immediately ready to purchase. They benefit from nurturing content and seasonal recommendations rather than hard sales pushes.

The gray Beyond 90 Days segment shows customers with longer expected purchase cycles. For gardening supplies, this often includes seasonal buyers who only purchase during planting season.

Use this timeline to plan your marketing calendar. Time campaigns to match when customers are predicted to be in-market for purchases.

ml-models:ltv_distribution.title: LTV Distribution
ml-models:ltv_distribution.formula: Customers segmented into five value tiers by total predicted CLV (VIP >= $500, High $200-499, Medium $100-199, Low $50-99, New/Trial < $50); donut sized by each tier's total CLV contribution.
ml-models:ltv_distribution.formula_explanation: Customers are segmented into five value tiers based on their total Customer Lifetime Value from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Total CLV combines both historic (realized) purchases and predicted future purchases based on purchase behavior patterns.
how_to_read: Each donut segment represents a CLV tier, sized by total CLV contribution
Center value shows total customers with CLV predictions
Segment colors indicate tier: Purple for VIP, Blue for High Value, Green for Medium, Amber for Low, Gray for New/Trial
VIP segment typically represents 5-6% of customers but 30%+ of total CLV
Hover over any segment to see detailed breakdown including customer count and total CLV
Click any segment to filter the entire page to that CLV tier
decisions_answered: How is customer value distributed across our customer base?
What percentage of total CLV comes from our top-tier customers?
Is our CLV distribution healthy or over-reliant on a few high-value customers?
Which tier represents the largest opportunity for value growth?
ml-models:ltv_distribution.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base using Klaviyo predictive analytics.

Each segment represents a CLV tier, from New/Trial customers under $50 to VIP customers at $500 or more. The center displays your total customers with CLV predictions - typically around 700,000 profiles.

The key insight here is the concentration of value. VIP customers, shown in purple, often represent only 5-6% of your customer base but contribute over 34% of total lifetime value. This is the power law of customer value in action.

High Value customers in blue add another 30% of CLV with about 15% of customers. Together, your top two tiers represent roughly 65% of all customer value from just 20% of customers.

The New/Trial segment in gray is typically the largest by count at nearly 40% of customers, but contributes the smallest share of CLV. These represent your opportunity - converting just a fraction of these customers to higher tiers can significantly impact total value.

Use this chart to understand: Should you focus on retaining top-tier customers or moving more customers up tiers? Both strategies have merit, but this visualization shows where the value concentration lies.

ml-models:marketing_mix_model.title: Marketing Mix Model (90 Days)
ml-models:marketing_mix_model.formula: Daily Google Ads spend and Meta Ads spend (stacked areas) against total site revenue (line) over the last 90 days, with overall ROAS = total revenue ÷ (Google spend + Meta spend).
ml-models:marketing_mix_model.formula_explanation: This chart combines daily marketing spend from Google Ads and Meta Ads with total site revenue from Google Analytics. The Marketing Mix Model (MMM) approach helps understand how paid advertising contributes to overall revenue, accounting for both direct conversions and brand lift effects.
how_to_read: Blue area shows daily Google Ads spend over the past 90 days
Light blue area shows daily Meta Ads spend stacked on Google
Green line tracks total site revenue to compare against spend
The gap between revenue line and spend areas represents organic and other channels
Spikes in spend should correlate with revenue increases if campaigns are effective
Use the date selector to zoom into specific periods for detailed analysis
decisions_answered: How effective is our paid advertising in driving revenue?
What is the relationship between ad spend and total revenue?
Are there diminishing returns as we increase spend?
How should we allocate budget between Google and Meta?
ml-models:marketing_mix_model.narration_text:
This Marketing Mix Model chart shows the relationship between your paid advertising spend and total site revenue over the past 90 days.

The stacked areas at the bottom represent your daily ad spend: Google Ads in blue and Meta Ads in lighter blue. The green line tracking above shows your total site revenue.

The key insight is understanding the gap between spend and revenue. A healthy business shows revenue significantly above total spend - this gap represents your organic channels, direct traffic, email marketing, and the halo effect of brand awareness from paid advertising.

Look for correlation patterns. When spend increases, does revenue follow? If you see spending spikes that do not lift revenue, those campaigns may need optimization. Conversely, revenue increases without corresponding spend changes might indicate seasonal demand or successful organic initiatives.

Your overall ROAS for this period is typically displayed in the summary metrics above. A healthy e-commerce ROAS is generally 3-4x, meaning $3-4 in revenue for every $1 spent on advertising.

Use this chart to identify: Is paid advertising pulling its weight? Are there diminishing returns as we scale spend? This informs budget allocation decisions across channels.

formula_components.name: Google spend
formula_components.formula: Google Ads cost summed per day

formula_components.name: Meta spend
formula_components.formula: Meta Ads spend summed per day

formula_components.name: Total revenue
formula_components.formula: GA4 total site revenue summed per day

ml-models:next_best_action.title: Next-Best-Action Recommendations
ml-models:next_best_action.formula: Customers auto-segmented into action buckets (Win-Back, Re-Engage, Upsell, Convert One-Time, Remind Overdue, Nurture, Standard Care) from predicted churn probability, predicted CLV, order history and expected next-order date; cards show customer count and total CLV at stake per segment, sorted by CLV.
ml-models:next_best_action.formula_explanation: Customers are automatically segmented into action buckets based on their predicted churn probability, lifetime value, order history, and expected next order timing. Each segment has a recommended marketing action designed to maximize customer value retention or growth.
how_to_read: Cards are sorted by CLV at stake - highest value segments first
Red-bordered cards indicate high-priority win-back segments needing immediate action
Yellow-bordered cards show conversion opportunities
Green-bordered cards highlight upsell opportunities with loyal customers
Customer count shows segment size, CLV at Stake shows total value in segment
Recommended Action provides specific campaign guidance for each segment
Click Create Campaign to export segment to Klaviyo for campaign execution
decisions_answered: Which customer segments should receive which type of outreach?
What is the CLV at stake for each action segment?
Which retention campaigns should we prioritize?
How should we personalize messaging for different customer situations?
ml-models:next_best_action.narration_text:
The Next-Best-Action cards transform predictive analytics into actionable marketing recommendations.

Each card represents a customer segment defined by a combination of churn risk, lifetime value, and purchase timing. The segments are prioritized by CLV at stake, so your most valuable opportunities appear first.

The Win-Back High-Value segment, marked with a red Priority badge, typically shows your most urgent action item. These are customers with significant lifetime value who have high probability of churning. The recommended action is personalized win-back campaigns with exclusive discounts.

The Convert One-Time Buyers segment often represents the largest opportunity by customer count. These are customers who made one purchase and have not returned. Research shows that customers who make a second purchase are significantly more likely to become long-term customers. The recommended action is second-purchase incentives with related product recommendations.

The Remind Overdue segment catches customers who were predicted to order by now but have not. These are warm leads - they have demonstrated purchase intent through their historical patterns. A simple replenishment reminder can be highly effective.

Use the Create Campaign button to export any segment directly to Klaviyo for campaign execution. Each segment is pre-defined with the right filters to target exactly the customers who need that specific outreach.

new-products:launches_by_month.title: New Product Launches by Month
new-products:launches_by_month.formula: Count of new products per launch month, where launch month = the month a product first appears in the data; excludes unknown item ids
new-products:launches_by_month.formula_explanation: Identifies new products by finding the first date each ITEM_ID appears in the ecommerce data. Products are grouped by their launch month to show the cadence of new product introductions over time.
how_to_read: Each bar represents the count of new products launched in that month
Taller bars indicate periods of high product introduction activity
Look for seasonal patterns - seed catalogs often launch new varieties in certain periods
Compare month-over-month to understand launch cadence trends
Hover over bars to see exact counts
decisions_answered: How many new products are we launching each month?
Are there seasonal patterns in our new product introductions?
Is our product introduction cadence accelerating or slowing?
Which months show the highest new product activity?
new-products:launches_by_month.narration_text:
This bar chart shows your new product launch cadence by month - essential for understanding your catalog expansion rate.

The formula identifies new products by finding the first date each product ID appears in your ecommerce data. Products are then grouped by their launch month.

Reading this chart: Each bar shows the count of new products launched that month. Taller bars indicate periods of high introduction activity. For seed catalogs, expect peaks when new variety catalogs are released.

Look for patterns. Consistent introduction rates suggest a healthy product development pipeline. Sudden spikes may indicate catalog launches or acquisitions. Declining trends might signal inventory or sourcing challenges.

Key questions answered: How many new products per month? What's the seasonal launch pattern? Is introduction rate healthy? Use this to plan inventory and marketing for new product launches.

new-products:needs_optimization.title: Needs Optimization
new-products:needs_optimization.formula: New products with ≥5 views but conversion < 5%, where conversion = units purchased ÷ views × 100; ranked by views, top 25
new-products:needs_optimization.formula_explanation: Identifies new products with significant view volume but poor conversion rates. These products have proven demand (people are looking) but something in the buying process is broken - requiring product page, pricing, or content optimization.
how_to_read: Products listed here receive significant traffic but few purchases
High views prove customer interest exists
Low conversion indicates friction in the purchase process
Common issues: pricing, product images, descriptions, or availability
Prioritize by view count - fixing high-traffic products has most impact
decisions_answered: Which new products have traffic but poor conversion?
Where should we focus product page optimization efforts?
Are there pricing issues causing conversion problems?
What is the revenue opportunity from fixing these products?
new-products:needs_optimization.narration_text:
This table identifies your optimization opportunities - new products with traffic but poor conversion.

The formula filters for products with 1,000 or more views but less than 5% conversion. High views prove customer interest exists. Low conversion means something in the selling process is broken.

Reading this table: Each row represents lost revenue. These products attract customers but fail to close the sale. Issues might include poor product images, unclear descriptions, pricing concerns, or availability messaging.

Prioritize by view count. Fixing a 10,000 view product from 2% to 5% conversion has 10 times the impact of fixing a 1,000 view product.

Key questions answered: Where are we losing conversions? What needs page improvement? Are pricing issues causing friction? This is your optimization priority list for new products.

new-products:needs_promotion.title: Needs Promotion
new-products:needs_promotion.formula: New products with ≥5 views, conversion ≥ 2% and fewer than 5000 views, where conversion = units purchased ÷ views × 100; ranked by conversion, top 25
new-products:needs_promotion.formula_explanation: Identifies new products with excellent conversion rates but low visibility. These hidden gems sell well when found but need more traffic through promotion, featured placement, or advertising to maximize revenue potential.
how_to_read: Products listed here convert visitors to buyers effectively
Low view counts indicate these products are not being discovered
High conversion proves the product and page work - they need visibility
Prioritize by conversion rate - highest converters have most potential
Consider featured placement, email features, or paid promotion
decisions_answered: Which new products deserve more promotional support?
What hidden gems are converting well but not getting traffic?
Where should we allocate promotional budget for new products?
Which products should be featured in email campaigns?
new-products:needs_promotion.narration_text:
This table identifies your promotion opportunities - new products that convert well but need more visibility.

The formula filters for products with conversion rates of 15% or higher but fewer than 5,000 views. High conversion proves the product page works. Low views means customers simply are not finding them.

Reading this table: Each product listed is a hidden gem. When customers find these products, they buy. The opportunity is getting more eyes on them through featured placement, email campaigns, search optimization, or paid advertising.

Revenue potential is simple math. A 20% converting product with 500 views will double revenue if you double the views.

Key questions answered: Which new products need promotion? Where should we focus visibility efforts? What are the hidden gems? Use this to allocate promotional budget to maximize new product revenue.

new-products:performance_tier_distributi.title: Performance Tier Distribution
new-products:performance_tier_distributi.formula: Donut of new products by performance tier, bucketing each product on its since-launch revenue against fixed percentile thresholds: Top (≥4060), Strong (≥2001), Average (≥959), Below Average (≥409), Underperformer (>0), No Revenue (0); each tier shows product count, average revenue and average views
new-products:performance_tier_distributi.formula_explanation: Categorizes products into performance tiers based on their first 90-day revenue compared to historical percentile benchmarks. This distribution shows how new products perform relative to established success thresholds.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of products in each performance tier
Green (Top Performer) and blue (Strong) are your success stories
Gray (Average) products meet baseline expectations
Amber (Below Average) and red (Underperformer) need attention
Light gray (No Revenue) indicates products not yet generating sales
Center number shows total products being evaluated
decisions_answered: What percentage of new products meet performance benchmarks?
How many products are top performers vs underperformers?
Is our new product success rate healthy?
How many products have not generated any revenue?
new-products:performance_tier_distributi.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your new products distribute across performance tiers - a health check for your launch success.

The formula compares each product's first 90-day revenue against historical benchmarks. Top performers exceed the 90th percentile at $4,061. Strong is 75th to 90th. Average is 50th to 75th, and so on.

Reading this chart: The donut segments show what percentage of products fall into each tier. Green and blue segments represent success. Gray is acceptable. Amber and red need attention. Light gray shows products with zero revenue.

A healthy distribution has meaningful green and blue segments. If most products cluster in underperformer or no revenue segments, your product development or launch process needs review.

Key questions answered: What's our success rate? How many top performers? How many failures? Use this to evaluate and improve your new product launch effectiveness.

new-products:success_rate_trend.title: Launch Success Rate Trend
new-products:success_rate_trend.formula: Per launch month: success rate % = share of products launched that month that generated any revenue (products with revenue > 0 ÷ total launched × 100), plus counts above median (≥959) and above P75 (≥2001)
new-products:success_rate_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks the monthly success rate of new product launches, defined as the percentage of products that generate any revenue within their first 90 days. This metric evaluates launch effectiveness over time.
how_to_read: Each point shows the success rate for products launched in that month
Higher values indicate better launch performance
The target line (if shown) indicates the goal success rate
Look for trends - improving rates suggest better launch processes
Declining rates may indicate market or execution issues
decisions_answered: Is our launch success rate improving or declining?
Are we meeting our target success rate benchmarks?
Which months produced the most successful launches?
How does current success rate compare to historical average?
new-products:success_rate_trend.narration_text:
This trend line shows your monthly launch success rate - tracking whether new products are gaining traction.

The formula calculates the percentage of products launched each month that generate revenue within 90 days. Only months with 90-plus days of data are included for complete measurement.

Reading this chart: Each point represents a month's launch cohort success rate. Upward trends indicate improving launch effectiveness. Declining trends signal potential issues with product selection, positioning, or market conditions.

Context matters. A 25% success rate is a reasonable benchmark for new product introductions. Higher is better, but even successful catalogs see many new products fail to gain traction.

Key questions answered: Is success rate improving? Are we meeting targets? Which periods performed best? Use this to evaluate your product development and launch processes over time.

new-products:top_products_table.title: Top New Products by Revenue
new-products:top_products_table.formula: Top 100 new products by since-launch revenue: revenue, units, views, cart adds, days since launch, daily avg revenue = revenue ÷ days since launch, and conversion = units ÷ views × 100
new-products:top_products_table.formula_explanation: Ranks new products by total revenue since launch, with supporting metrics including units sold, daily average revenue, and view-to-purchase conversion rate. Performance status compares against historical percentile benchmarks.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by total revenue since launch - top performers first
Launch Date and Days Live show how long each product has been available
Daily Avg Revenue normalizes for different launch dates
CVR (Conversion Rate) shows product page effectiveness
Status badges indicate performance tier vs historical benchmarks
decisions_answered: Which new products are generating the most revenue?
How do new products compare in conversion effectiveness?
Which recent launches show the strongest early traction?
Are there high-revenue products with low conversion (price premium)?
new-products:top_products_table.narration_text:
This table ranks your new products by total revenue - your new product performance leaderboard.

The formula calculates multiple metrics per product: total revenue since launch, units sold, daily average revenue, and conversion rate. Status badges compare against historical performance benchmarks.

Reading this table: Top performers appear first. Compare daily average revenue across products with different launch dates for fair comparison. Conversion rate reveals product page effectiveness.

Watch for patterns. High revenue with low conversion suggests price premium products. High conversion with lower revenue indicates promotion opportunity. Use status badges to quickly identify winners and underperformers.

Key questions answered: Who are the new product winners? Which need more promotion? Which have conversion issues? Use this to prioritize marketing and optimization efforts for new products.

new-products:velocity_curve.title: Launch Velocity Curve
new-products:velocity_curve.formula: Average per-product trajectory across the first 12 weeks since launch (week = floor of days-since-launch ÷ 7): average revenue, average views and average purchases per product, plus product count, per week bucket
new-products:velocity_curve.formula_explanation: Tracks the average revenue trajectory of new products from launch through their first 12 weeks. This creates a benchmark curve showing typical performance patterns that new products follow as they mature.
how_to_read: The line shows average revenue per product at each week since launch
Week 0 represents launch week performance - often shows initial spike
Upward slope indicates products gaining momentum over time
Flat or declining curves may signal product or market issues
The benchmark line (if shown) indicates median historical performance
decisions_answered: What is the typical revenue trajectory for new products?
How long does it take new products to reach steady-state revenue?
Is there an initial launch spike followed by stabilization?
At what week should we evaluate new product success?
new-products:velocity_curve.narration_text:
This velocity curve shows the average revenue trajectory of new products over their first 12 weeks - your benchmark for launch performance.

The formula calculates weeks since launch for each product, then averages revenue across all products at each week milestone. This creates a typical performance curve.

Reading this chart: Week 0 is launch week. The line shows how average revenue evolves as products mature. An initial spike followed by dip then growth is common - launch excitement, then organic discovery.

The shape matters. Consistent upward trajectory indicates products gain momentum. Flat lines suggest products stabilize quickly. Declining curves after launch may indicate marketing or product issues.

Key questions answered: What's the typical launch trajectory? How long to steady state? When should we evaluate success? Use this to set realistic expectations for new product launches.

product-affinity:bundle_performance.title: Bundle Performance
product-affinity:bundle_performance.formula: Products named like a bundle (bundle/kit/pack/collection/combo/bulbs) with ≥3 co-purchases: orders, units = co-purchase count; revenue and avg price are placeholders (units × 50) since the mart carries no unit price; ranked by revenue, top 20
product-affinity:bundle_performance.formula_explanation: Identifies products explicitly marketed as bundles (containing keywords like bundle, kit, pack, set, or collection) and tracks their sales performance including orders, units, revenue, and average pricing.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product marketed as a bundle
Sort by Revenue to see top-performing bundles
Compare Units to Orders to see quantity per order patterns
Avg Price helps evaluate bundle pricing strategy
Empty table means no products match bundle naming conventions
Click any bundle to navigate to its product detail page
decisions_answered: Which bundles generate the most revenue?
Are our bundles priced competitively?
How many bundle units sell per order?
Which bundle types (kits, packs, collections) perform best?
product-affinity:bundle_performance.narration_text:
This table tracks performance of products explicitly marketed as bundles - products named with bundle, kit, pack, set, or collection keywords.

The formula filters products by naming convention, then aggregates order count, units sold, total revenue, and average price for each bundle product.

Reading this table: Sort by Revenue to identify your best-performing bundles. Compare the Units column to Orders - higher ratios mean customers buy multiple of the same bundle. Average Price helps evaluate your bundle pricing strategy.

Note that this table may be empty if no products use bundle-related naming. Consider standardizing bundle naming conventions to enable this tracking.

Key questions: What bundles drive the most revenue? Is bundle pricing effective? Which bundle types perform best? Click any row to see that product in detail.

product-affinity:category_heatmap.title: Category Affinity Matrix
product-affinity:category_heatmap.formula: Matrix of how often products from two different categories appear in the same order; cell = sum of co-purchase counts per category pair (categories inferred from product-name keywords), kept where the pair total ≥ 10, top 20 pairs
product-affinity:category_heatmap.formula_explanation: Creates a matrix showing how often products from different categories appear in the same order. Higher values indicate categories frequently purchased together, revealing cross-category shopping patterns.
how_to_read: Each cell shows co-purchase frequency between two categories
Darker colors indicate more frequent co-purchases
Diagonal cells show same-category purchases
Look for bright off-diagonal cells - these reveal cross-category affinities
Categories are limited to top 10 by order volume
Click any cell to explore products in that category combination
decisions_answered: Which product categories are purchased together?
What cross-category merchandising opportunities exist?
Should we create cross-category bundles?
How do customers navigate between product categories?
product-affinity:category_heatmap.narration_text:
This heatmap visualizes category-level purchasing patterns - showing which product categories are bought together.

The formula counts orders containing products from both categories in each cell. The matrix is symmetric - the cell for Seeds plus Tools equals Tools plus Seeds.

Reading this heatmap: Color intensity shows co-purchase frequency. Darker cells indicate categories frequently bought together. The diagonal shows same-category purchases.

The key insight comes from off-diagonal cells. Bright cells reveal cross-category shopping patterns. These inform store layout, email campaigns, and cross-category recommendations.

Key questions: What categories pair together? Where are cross-category opportunities? Should we create cross-category bundles? Click any cell to explore specific products in that category combination.

product-affinity:cross_sell_recommendati.title: Cross-Sell Recommendations
