product-affinity:cross_sell_recommendati.formula: Cross-sell pairs (≥5 co-purchases, initial product with ≥20 orders): cross-sell rate = share of buyers of the initial product who also bought the follow-on, plus customer count = co-purchase count; ranked by cross-sell rate, top 50
product-affinity:cross_sell_recommendati.formula_explanation: Analyzes sequential purchase patterns to identify products frequently bought together by the same customer across multiple orders. Higher cross-sell rates indicate strong follow-on purchase potential.
how_to_read: Initial Product is the first purchase; Recommended is the follow-on
Customer Count shows how many customers bought both products
Cross-Sell Rate shows what percentage of initial buyers also bought the recommendation
High rates (>10%) indicate strong follow-on potential
Moderate rates (5-10%) still represent viable recommendations
Click any row to explore the customer segment
decisions_answered: What product should we recommend after a specific purchase?
Which products drive repeat purchases from the same customers?
What cross-sell recommendations have the highest success rate?
How should we sequence post-purchase email recommendations?
product-affinity:cross_sell_recommendati.narration_text:
This table reveals sequential purchase patterns - what customers buy after their initial purchase. This powers your cross-sell strategy.

The formula tracks customers who bought an initial product and later returned to buy a different product. The cross-sell rate shows what percentage of initial buyers became followup buyers.

Reading this table: The Initial Product column shows the first purchase. Recommended Cross-Sell shows what they bought next. Customer Count gives sample size. The Cross-Sell Rate is the key metric.

Rates above 10 percent indicate strong follow-on potential - these should definitely appear in post-purchase emails and recommendation widgets. Rates between 5 and 10 percent are still actionable.

Key questions: What should we recommend after each purchase? What drives repeat buying? How should we sequence email recommendations? Click any row to explore that customer segment.

product-affinity:multi_item_distribution.title: Order Distribution by Item Count
product-affinity:multi_item_distribution.formula: Orders bucketed by item count (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6+) derived from average items per order; each bar = order count in the bucket, with average order value per bucket. Approximated from daily aggregates, not true per-order grain
product-affinity:multi_item_distribution.formula_explanation: Groups orders by the number of unique products purchased, then counts orders and calculates average order value for each bucket. Shows the distribution of single-item versus multi-item orders.
how_to_read: Each bar represents orders with a specific number of items (1-item, 2-item, etc.)
Bar height shows the count of orders in each bucket
Single-item orders typically dominate, representing cross-sell opportunities
Multi-item orders (3+) indicate successful product discovery and bundling
The 6+ bucket aggregates high-quantity orders to prevent chart distortion
Click any bar to drill down into orders with that item count
decisions_answered: What percentage of orders are single-item vs multi-item?
Are customers discovering and purchasing complementary products?
Is our cross-sell strategy effectively increasing items per order?
Which item count bucket contributes most to total orders?
product-affinity:multi_item_distribution.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how orders are distributed by the number of items purchased - a key indicator of cross-sell success.

The formula counts unique products per order, then groups orders into buckets: 1-item, 2-item, 3-item, and so on. The 6-plus bucket aggregates larger orders.

Reading this chart: Each bar represents orders with that specific item count. Single-item orders typically form the tallest bar - this is normal but represents your biggest cross-sell opportunity.

Multi-item orders at 3-plus indicate customers who discovered complementary products. Higher bars in these buckets mean your product recommendations and bundling strategy are working.

Key questions answered: What percentage are single vs multi-item orders? Are customers finding complementary products? Is cross-selling effective? Click any bar to explore orders in that bucket.

product-affinity:top_product_pairs.title: Top Product Pairs (Frequently Bought Together)
product-affinity:top_product_pairs.formula: Product pairs most often bought together (≥5 co-purchases): bar = co-purchase count, with support % = how frequently the pair co-occurs; top 30 by co-purchase count (top 10 rendered)
product-affinity:top_product_pairs.formula_explanation: Identifies product pairs that appear together in the same order most frequently. Uses self-join on order items to find co-occurrences, then ranks by frequency.
how_to_read: Each bar shows a product pair that frequently appears in the same order
Longer bars indicate pairs purchased together more often
Product names are truncated - hover for full names
Top pairs are natural candidates for bundle creation
Consider these pairs for product page recommendations
Click any bar to see detailed pair analysis
decisions_answered: Which products are most commonly purchased together?
What product pairs should we promote as bundles?
Which products should appear in "Frequently Bought Together" sections?
Are there unexpected product affinities we should leverage?
product-affinity:top_product_pairs.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows your most frequently co-purchased product pairs - the foundation of your bundling strategy.

The formula identifies every pair of products appearing in the same order, counts distinct orders containing each pair, and ranks by frequency.

Reading this chart: Longer bars indicate pairs purchased together more often. These are natural bundle candidates. Product names may be truncated - hover to see full names.

The top pairs here should directly inform your merchandising decisions. Add these to Frequently Bought Together recommendations on product pages. Consider creating pre-made bundles with discounts.

Key questions answered: What products are bought together most? What should we bundle? What goes in product recommendations? Click any bar for deeper pair analysis.

product-category:avg_item_price.title: Average Item Price
product-category:avg_item_price.formula: Average Item Price = category revenue ÷ items sold (level-3 categories, over the period)
product-category:avg_item_price.formula_explanation: Calculates the average selling price per item by dividing total revenue by total units sold. This metric helps track pricing trends, mix shifts, and promotional impact on average order composition.
how_to_read: The main value shows average price per item sold
Rising average price may indicate mix shift to premium items
Declining price could signal promotional activity or category mix changes
The sparkline shows 14-day price trend stability
Click to view price distribution by category
decisions_answered: What is the typical price point customers are paying?
Is our product mix shifting toward higher or lower prices?
Are promotions impacting average selling price?
How does our average price compare to category benchmarks?
product-category:avg_item_price.narration_text:
This KPI shows your average item price - a crucial metric for understanding pricing and product mix.

The formula divides total revenue by total items sold. This weighted average reflects the actual prices customers pay across your catalog.

Reading this card: The main value shows your average selling price. Changes here don't always mean you changed prices - they often reflect shifts in product mix. More premium item sales raise the average; more value items lower it.

Price trends are diagnostic. A rising average price with flat volume may indicate successful premium positioning. A declining price with rising volume could signal effective promotional activity or mix shift to essentials.

Key questions: What's the typical item price? Is the mix shifting? How are promotions impacting pricing? Click to explore price distribution across categories.

product-category:items_sold.title: Items Sold
product-category:items_sold.formula: Items Sold = sum of items purchased across level-3 categories over the period
product-category:items_sold.formula_explanation: Counts total units purchased across all categories. This volume metric helps understand demand patterns independent of price changes and is critical for inventory planning and fulfillment capacity.
how_to_read: The main value shows total units sold across all categories
Compare to revenue growth to identify pricing changes
Faster item growth than revenue may indicate discounting
The sparkline shows 14-day volume trends
Click to view item breakdown by category
decisions_answered: How many items are customers purchasing?
Is unit volume growing faster or slower than revenue?
Are we seeing healthy demand across the catalog?
Do we need to adjust inventory levels?
product-category:items_sold.narration_text:
This KPI tracks total items sold across all product categories - a key volume metric for understanding customer demand.

The formula sums all items purchased within the selected date range. This is a pure volume measure independent of pricing.

Reading this card: The main number shows total units sold. The percentage change compares to the prior period. Compare this growth rate to revenue growth - if items grow faster than revenue, you may be discounting.

Volume trends matter for inventory planning. Strong item growth requires adequate stock levels. Declining volume may indicate demand issues even if revenue holds steady through price increases.

Key questions: How many items are selling? Is volume trending up? Are items and revenue growing at similar rates? Click to explore volume by category.

product-category:purchase_rate.title: Purchase Rate
product-category:purchase_rate.formula: Purchase Rate = (items purchased ÷ items viewed) × 100 (level-3 categories)
product-category:purchase_rate.formula_explanation: Measures conversion efficiency from product view to purchase. For a seed company with high-intent traffic, rates of 15-25% are typical. Low rates may indicate pricing issues, poor imagery, or catalog navigation problems.
how_to_read: The main value shows percentage of views that convert to purchases
Green color (20%+) indicates strong conversion efficiency
Yellow (15-20%) suggests room for optimization
Red (below 15%) may indicate conversion blockers
Click to analyze conversion by category
decisions_answered: How efficiently are product views converting to purchases?
Is conversion rate healthy compared to benchmarks?
Which categories may have conversion optimization opportunities?
Are there product pages that need improvement?
product-category:purchase_rate.narration_text:
This KPI measures your purchase rate - the percentage of product views that result in a purchase. This is a key conversion efficiency metric.

The formula divides items purchased by items viewed, expressed as a percentage. For specialty retailers with high-intent traffic, 15 to 25 percent is healthy.

Reading this card: The value shows your overall purchase rate. Color coding helps interpretation - green above 20 percent is strong, yellow between 15 and 20 percent is acceptable, red below 15 percent needs attention.

A declining purchase rate with stable traffic suggests conversion problems - perhaps pricing, product information, or user experience issues. Rising rates indicate optimization success.

Key questions: How efficiently do views convert? Is the rate healthy? Which categories underperform? Click to analyze conversion patterns across your catalog.

product-overview:conversion_analysis.title: Conversion Rate Analysis
product-overview:conversion_analysis.formula: Per top product (500+ views): cart-to-view % = cart adds ÷ views × 100 and purchase-to-view % = units purchased ÷ views × 100; ranked by purchase-to-view %, top 20
product-overview:conversion_analysis.formula_explanation: Compares cart-to-view and purchase-to-view conversion rates across top products. Filters to products with sufficient view volume (500+) to ensure statistically meaningful comparisons.
how_to_read: Blue bars show Cart Rate - percentage of views that add to cart
Green bars show Purchase Rate - percentage of views that purchase
Higher bars indicate better converting products
Gap between blue and green shows cart abandonment
Products are ordered by purchase conversion rate
decisions_answered: Which products convert views to purchases most effectively?
Where is cart abandonment highest (large gap between cart and purchase)?
Are there products with high cart adds but low purchases (pricing issue)?
Which product pages should we use as templates for optimization?
product-overview:conversion_analysis.narration_text:
This chart compares conversion rates across products - identifying your most effective sellers.

The formula calculates two rates: cart-to-view shows how many views add to cart, purchase-to-view shows complete conversions. We filter to products with 500 plus views for statistical validity.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show cart interest. Green bars show purchases. A large gap between them indicates cart abandonment. Products with high green bars are your conversion champions - study their pages for best practices.

Low green bars with high blue might indicate pricing friction or checkout issues specific to those products.

Key questions answered: Which products convert best? Where is cart abandonment an issue? What can we learn from top converters? Use this to prioritize conversion optimization efforts.

product-overview:new_products.title: New Product Tracking
product-overview:new_products.formula: Products first seen in the last 60 days (looking back 90 days): each row shows revenue, units, views, days since launch and conversion % = units ÷ views × 100; ranked by revenue, top 25
product-overview:new_products.formula_explanation: Identifies products first appearing in the data within the last 60 days and tracks their early performance. Monitors revenue, units, and conversion rates to assess new product introduction success.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by revenue since launch
Launch Date shows when the product first appeared
Days Live indicates how long the product has been available
Conversion Rate helps assess product page effectiveness
Compare early performers to set expectations for future launches
decisions_answered: Which new products are gaining traction quickly?
Are new product introductions meeting performance expectations?
Should we increase promotion for promising new products?
Which new products may need page optimization or repositioning?
product-overview:new_products.narration_text:
This table tracks your newest products - measuring launch success.

The formula identifies products first seen in the last 60 days. We track revenue, units, and conversion to gauge early traction.

Reading this table: High revenue in few days suggests a winner - consider promotion. Low conversion despite views suggests the product page needs work. Compare against historical new product benchmarks.

Early performance often predicts long-term success. Products that gain quick traction tend to maintain momentum.

Key questions answered: Are new introductions succeeding? Which deserve more promotion? Which need optimization? Use this to refine your new product launch process.

product-overview:revenue_trend.title: Product Revenue Trend
product-overview:revenue_trend.formula: Total product revenue summed per period (daily, weekly or monthly per the granularity toggle) over the selected range (area chart)
product-overview:revenue_trend.formula_explanation: Aggregates product revenue from GA4 ecommerce data at the selected granularity (daily, weekly, or monthly). Each data point represents total revenue from all products sold during that time period.
how_to_read: The area chart displays total product revenue over the selected date range
Toggle between Daily, Weekly, and Monthly views using the dropdown
Peaks indicate high-revenue periods - correlate with campaigns or seasonality
Look for consistent upward trends indicating healthy business growth
Hover over any point to see exact revenue for that period
decisions_answered: Is product revenue trending up or down over time?
Which days or weeks generated the most product revenue?
Are there seasonal patterns in product sales?
How does current performance compare to previous periods?
product-overview:revenue_trend.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your product revenue over time - the fundamental measure of your e-commerce health.

The formula sums all item revenue from GA4 data, grouped by your selected time period. You can view daily, weekly, or monthly trends depending on your analysis needs.

Reading this chart: The shaded area represents total product revenue. Rising slopes indicate growth periods. Look for peaks that may correlate with marketing campaigns, seasonal events like spring planting season, or catalog launches.

For Rare Seeds, expect significant peaks in Q1 when gardeners prepare for spring planting. December often shows catalog pre-order activity.

Key questions answered: Is revenue growing? What periods perform best? Are there patterns to plan around? Use this to align inventory and marketing with demand cycles.

product-seasonality:product_seasonality_.title: Product Seasonality Details
product-seasonality:product_seasonality_.formula: One row per product above the minimum-revenue threshold: total revenue, Q1–Q4 share = quarter revenue ÷ total revenue × 100, peak quarter, a seasonality type (Spring/Fall/Holiday/Year-Round/Mixed), seasonality index = peak-quarter share ÷ 25%, and YoY growth % vs last year
product-seasonality:product_seasonality_.formula_explanation: Comprehensive product-level table showing revenue, quarterly distribution, seasonality classification, and year-over-year growth. Enables detailed analysis and identification of individual products with unusual or changing seasonal patterns.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product meeting the minimum revenue threshold
Q1-Q4 columns show percentage of annual revenue in each quarter with visual bars
Type column shows seasonality classification with color-coded badges
Index column shows seasonality intensity (higher = more concentrated demand)
YoY column shows year-over-year revenue growth with color coding
Click column headers to sort by any metric
Use search to filter to specific products
Export button downloads data for external analysis
decisions_answered: What are the detailed seasonal patterns for individual products?
Which products have the most concentrated demand periods?
Are specific products showing unusual seasonal behavior?
Which products are growing or declining year-over-year?
How do top products compare in their seasonality profiles?
product-seasonality:product_seasonality_.narration_text:
This data table provides product-level seasonality details - the comprehensive view for individual product analysis and inventory planning.

The table shows revenue, quarterly distribution percentages, seasonality type, index, and year-over-year growth for each product meeting minimum revenue thresholds.

Reading this table: Sort by revenue to see top performers. The Q1-Q4 columns show visual bars proportional to quarterly concentration. High seasonality index values indicate products with very concentrated demand.

Look for anomalies. Products switching seasonality type year-over-year may indicate market shifts. High-revenue products with negative YoY growth need attention.

Key questions: Which products are most seasonal? Who are the top performers? Are patterns changing? Use search to find specific products, sort to rank by any metric, export for deeper analysis.

product-seasonality:seasonality_distribu.title: Seasonality Type Distribution
product-seasonality:seasonality_distribu.formula: Donut of product counts by seasonality type, classifying each product (revenue > 1000) on its quarterly revenue mix: Spring Peak (Q1 > 60%), Fall Peak (Q3 > 50%), Holiday Peak (Q4 > 50%), Year-Round (max quarter < 40%), else Mixed
product-seasonality:seasonality_distribu.formula_explanation: Classifies each product into a seasonality type based on quarterly revenue distribution. Products are filtered by minimum revenue threshold to ensure meaningful patterns. This enables portfolio-level understanding of demand timing.
how_to_read: The donut chart shows distribution of products by seasonality type
Center number displays total products analyzed
Each segment is color-coded by type (green=Spring, orange=Fall, red=Holiday, blue=Year-Round, purple=Mixed)
Segment size indicates count of products in each category
Hover over segments for exact product counts and revenue totals
Click a segment to filter the table below to that seasonality type
decisions_answered: What portion of the catalog is spring-peak versus year-round?
How many products are holiday-dependent?
Is the product mix well-diversified across seasons?
Which seasonality type drives the most revenue?
Are there concentration risks in seasonal timing?
product-seasonality:seasonality_distribu.narration_text:
This donut chart reveals your product seasonality distribution - how your catalog breaks down by demand timing patterns.

The formula classifies each product based on quarterly revenue concentration. Spring Peak products have over 60 percent of revenue in Q1. Year-Round products have no quarter exceeding 40 percent.

Reading this chart: The center number shows total products analyzed. Segment sizes indicate how many products fall into each type. For a seed company, expect a large Spring Peak segment.

Diversification matters. Heavy concentration in one seasonality type creates cash flow challenges and inventory risks. A healthy portfolio balances seasonal peaks with year-round sellers.

Key questions: How seasonal is the catalog? Is there diversity across seasons? Which type dominates? Click segments to explore products in each category.

sms-performance:sms_channel_comparison.title: SMS vs Email Comparison
sms-performance:sms_channel_comparison.formula: SMS versus Email compared on click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100 and unsubscribe rate = unsubscribes ÷ sends × 100 per channel (bar chart)
sms-performance:sms_channel_comparison.formula_explanation: This chart compares key engagement metrics between SMS and Email channels. It shows click rate and unsubscribe rate side by side, helping you understand relative channel performance and allocate marketing resources effectively.
how_to_read: Green bars show click rate for each channel - higher is better
Red bars show unsubscribe rate for each channel - lower is better
SMS typically has higher click rates than email but higher costs
Email reaches more people but with lower engagement rates
Compare the ratio of clicks to unsubscribes for channel health
decisions_answered: How does SMS engagement compare to email?
Which channel has better click-through performance?
Are opt-out rates concerning for either channel?
Should we shift budget between SMS and email?
sms-performance:sms_channel_comparison.narration_text:
This comparison chart puts SMS and Email performance side by side to help you understand relative channel strength.

Green bars show click rates. SMS typically outperforms email here because messages are more immediate and personal. However, SMS reaches a smaller audience and costs more per message.

Red bars show unsubscribe rates. Watch this closely for SMS - high opt-out rates can quickly erode your list. SMS unsubscribes should stay below 1% to maintain healthy growth.

Use this comparison to make budget decisions. If SMS has dramatically higher engagement and lower opt-outs, it may deserve more investment. If opt-out rates are high, focus on improving SMS content and timing first.

This answers: How do channels compare? Where should we invest? And are we maintaining healthy engagement on both?

sms-performance:sms_list_growth.title: SMS List Growth
sms-performance:sms_list_growth.formula: Cumulative net SMS list growth over 90 days, where daily net change = new subscribers − unsubscribes, accumulated into a running total (area chart)
sms-performance:sms_list_growth.formula_explanation: This area chart tracks the cumulative net growth of your SMS subscriber list over 90 days. It shows the running total of new subscriptions minus unsubscriptions, helping you understand if your list is growing or shrinking.
how_to_read: Rising line indicates net positive list growth - more subscribers joining than leaving
Flat line means new subscriptions equal unsubscriptions - no net growth
Declining line indicates list shrinkage - more people leaving than joining
Steep positive slopes often follow successful acquisition campaigns
Sudden drops may indicate high-unsubscribe campaigns or delivery issues
decisions_answered: Is our SMS list growing or shrinking over time?
What is our net subscriber growth rate?
When did major list growth or decline events occur?
Are acquisition efforts outpacing list churn?
sms-performance:sms_list_growth.narration_text:
This area chart tracks your SMS list growth over the past 90 days as a cumulative net number.

Each point shows the running total of new subscribers minus unsubscribers. A rising line means your list is growing - more people opt in than opt out. A flat line means you are treading water. A declining line means your list is shrinking.

Watch for patterns. Steep rises often follow acquisition campaigns - sign-up promotions, contests, or checkout opt-ins. Sudden drops may indicate a problematic campaign that drove mass unsubscribes.

Healthy SMS programs show steady upward momentum. If you see prolonged flatness or decline, investigate your acquisition channels and your messaging quality.

This answers: Is our list healthy? Are we growing? And when did significant changes occur that need investigation?

formula_components.name: Cumulative growth
formula_components.formula: running sum of daily net change

sms-performance:sms_list_health.title: SMS List Health
sms-performance:sms_list_health.formula: Composition of the SMS list — subscribed, unsubscribed and contacts with a phone but no SMS consent — with consent rate = subscribed ÷ total with phone × 100; center shows active subscribers (donut)
sms-performance:sms_list_health.formula_explanation: This donut chart shows the current composition of your SMS subscriber list. Active subscribers are those who have opted in and can receive marketing SMS. The breakdown helps identify list health issues and growth opportunities.
how_to_read: Green segment shows active subscribed contacts - these are your reachable SMS audience
Red segment shows unsubscribed contacts - a growing red segment indicates list health issues
Gray segment shows contacts with phone numbers but no SMS consent
Center value displays total active subscribers - this is your addressable SMS audience
The consent rate percentage shows what portion of your database has SMS consent
decisions_answered: How large is our active SMS subscriber list?
What percentage of our database has SMS consent?
Is our unsubscribe segment growing concerningly?
How much opportunity exists to grow SMS opt-ins?
sms-performance:sms_list_health.narration_text:
This donut chart gives you a snapshot of your SMS list composition and health.

The green segment shows your active subscribers - people who have opted in and can receive your SMS marketing. This is your addressable audience.

The red segment shows unsubscribed contacts. Some churn is normal, but if this segment grows faster than your subscriber base, you have a retention problem.

The gray segment represents contacts with phone numbers but no SMS consent. This is your growth opportunity - these people could be converted through opt-in campaigns.

The center number is your total active subscribers. The consent rate below tells you what percentage of your phone-number database has consented to SMS.

Use this to answer: How large is our SMS audience? Are we retaining subscribers? And where is our growth potential?

sms-performance:sms_performance_trend.title: SMS Performance Trend
sms-performance:sms_performance_trend.formula: SMS messages sent and clicks per week over 12 weeks (area chart)
sms-performance:sms_performance_trend.formula_explanation: This chart aggregates SMS sending volume and click engagement on a weekly basis over 12 weeks. Each data point represents the total count of unique SMS events for that week, allowing you to identify trends and patterns in your SMS program.
how_to_read: Blue area shows total SMS messages sent per week - higher peaks indicate more active sending periods
Green area shows total clicks received - should correlate with sending volume
Look for the gap between sent and clicks to gauge overall engagement health
Sudden drops in either metric may indicate delivery issues or list health problems
Compare week-over-week to identify seasonal patterns or campaign impact
decisions_answered: Is our SMS sending volume increasing or decreasing over time?
Are click volumes keeping pace with our sending growth?
What weeks had the best SMS engagement?
Are there seasonal patterns we should plan around?
sms-performance:sms_performance_trend.narration_text:
This chart shows your SMS performance over the past 12 weeks, tracking both sending volume and engagement.

The blue area represents total SMS messages sent each week. Higher peaks mean more active sending periods, which could be due to campaigns, promotions, or automated flows.

The green area shows clicks - the number of times recipients tapped links in your messages. Healthy SMS programs show clicks moving in proportion to sends.

Watch for the gap between these two lines. If sends are rising but clicks stay flat, engagement is dropping. If both grow together, your SMS strategy is scaling well.

Use this to answer: Is our program growing? Are we maintaining engagement as we scale? And when do we see our best performance weeks?

sms-performance:sms_send_time_heatmap.title: Best Send Times
sms-performance:sms_send_time_heatmap.formula: Click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100 by hour of day and day of week from the last 30 days (heatmap); darker cells are better send windows
sms-performance:sms_send_time_heatmap.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows click rate performance by hour of day and day of week, helping you identify optimal send times. Darker colors indicate higher click rates. Analysis is based on the last 30 days of SMS activity.
how_to_read: Each cell represents a specific hour (column) and day of week (row)
Darker blue cells indicate hours with higher click rates - these are your optimal send windows
Lighter cells show hours with lower engagement - avoid scheduling campaigns here
Hover over any cell to see the exact click rate percentage
Look for patterns across days - some hours may perform well consistently
decisions_answered: What time of day gets the best SMS response?
Which day of the week has the highest SMS engagement?
When should we avoid sending SMS messages?
Are there multiple high-engagement windows we can use?
sms-performance:sms_send_time_heatmap.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals your optimal SMS send times based on actual click performance across day and hour combinations.

Each cell represents a specific hour on a specific day of the week. Darker blue means higher click rates at that time. Lighter colors mean lower engagement.

Look for patterns. You might see consistently strong performance during lunch hours, evening commute times, or weekend mornings. These are your prime sending windows.

Avoid the light-colored cells. Sending during low-engagement periods wastes messages and can increase opt-outs from poor timing.

Also respect quiet hours - typically before 8am and after 9pm local time. This is both a best practice and often a compliance requirement.

Use this to answer: When should we send? When should we avoid? And what are our peak engagement windows?

sms-performance:sms_source_comparison.title: Campaign vs Flow Performance
sms-performance:sms_source_comparison.formula: Total SMS sent and clicks by source type (campaign versus flow), compared side by side (bar chart)
sms-performance:sms_source_comparison.formula_explanation: This bar chart compares SMS performance between manually-sent campaigns and automated flows. Campaigns are one-time sends to segments, while flows are triggered automatically by customer behavior or events.
how_to_read: Blue bars show total SMS messages sent by source type (Campaign vs Flow)
Green bars show total clicks received by source type
Compare bar heights to see which source drives more volume and engagement
Flows typically have higher engagement rates due to behavioral triggers
A balanced mix of campaigns and flows indicates a mature SMS program
decisions_answered: Are we relying too heavily on campaigns versus automated flows?
Which source type drives more SMS volume?
Should we invest more in building SMS flows?
Are our campaign and flow SMS performing comparably?
sms-performance:sms_source_comparison.narration_text:
This bar chart compares your SMS performance between campaigns and automated flows.

Blue bars show total SMS sent from each source. Green bars show the clicks generated. Compare the heights to understand where your volume and engagement come from.

Campaigns are one-time sends you manually trigger - promotions, announcements, flash sales. Flows are automated sequences triggered by customer behavior - abandoned carts, welcome series, post-purchase.

A mature SMS program has a healthy balance of both. Flows tend to have higher engagement because they are timely and relevant. If your campaigns vastly outweigh flows, consider building more automation.

Use this to answer: Where does our SMS volume come from? Are we leveraging automation? Which source performs better?

sms-performance:sms_top_messages.title: Top SMS Messages by Revenue
sms-performance:sms_top_messages.formula: One row per SMS message ranked by attributed revenue, with revenue per SMS = attributed revenue ÷ total SMS sent and click rate = clicks ÷ sends × 100; type column distinguishes campaign versus flow
sms-performance:sms_top_messages.formula_explanation: This table ranks all SMS messages by attributed revenue, showing the business impact of each campaign and flow message. It includes engagement metrics to help identify what makes top performers successful.
how_to_read: Messages are ranked by total revenue attributed - top performers are your money makers
Click Rate column shows engagement - aim for 5%+ on successful messages
Type column distinguishes between Campaign and Flow messages
Revenue per SMS shows efficiency - higher means better ROI per message sent
Click any row to drill into detailed message analytics
decisions_answered: Which SMS messages generate the most revenue?
What do our best-performing SMS have in common?
Are flows or campaigns driving more revenue?
Which messages should we replicate or optimize?
sms-performance:sms_top_messages.narration_text:
This table shows your SMS messages ranked by the revenue they generate.

The top rows are your money makers - the messages driving real business results. Study what makes them successful. Is it the offer? The timing? The audience? Understanding this helps you create more winners.

Look at both revenue and click rate together. High revenue with high click rate means the message resonated and converted. High revenue with low click rate might mean a smaller but very qualified audience.

The Type column tells you if it is a campaign or flow. Flows often dominate revenue because they fire at exactly the right moment - abandoned cart, browse abandonment, post-purchase.

Revenue per SMS is your efficiency metric. A message that generates $10,000 from 100,000 sends is less efficient than one generating $5,000 from 10,000 sends.

Use this to answer: What works? What should we repeat? And where should we focus optimization?

website-performance:audiences.title: Audience Segments
website-performance:audiences.formula: One row per GA4 audience segment with its sessions, active users, new users, average session duration and revenue, plus revenue ÷ active users; ranked by sessions
website-performance:audiences.formula_explanation: Shows performance of GA4 audience segments like All Users, Purchasers, Cart Abandoners, and custom segments. Helps identify high-value audience characteristics.
how_to_read: Audience Name identifies the segment definition
Sessions show engagement level of each segment
Active Users indicates segment size
New Users shows growth of each segment
Revenue Per User identifies highest-value segments
Purchasers segment shows confirmed buyer behavior
Cart Abandoners segment reveals retargeting opportunity size
decisions_answered: Which audience segments are most valuable?
How large is our cart abandoner segment?
What is the revenue per user for purchasers vs general users?
Which segments should we prioritize for remarketing?
How do custom audience segments perform?
website-performance:audiences.narration_text:
This table shows performance by GA4 audience segment - revealing which user groups drive the most value.

The formula aggregates metrics by audience name, including both standard segments like Purchasers and custom-defined audiences.

Reading this table: Audience Name shows the segment. Active Users shows size. Revenue Per User identifies value. Compare segments to understand what makes users valuable.

Key segments to watch: Purchasers are confirmed buyers. Cart Abandoners represent recovery opportunity. High-value users warrant VIP treatment. New Users show acquisition effectiveness.

Key questions: Which segments are valuable? How large is cart abandonment? What defines high-value users? Where should remarketing focus? Use for audience-based marketing.

formula_components.name: Audience Sessions
formula_components.formula: Sessions for the segment over the selected range

formula_components.name: Revenue per User
formula_components.formula: Segment revenue ÷ active users in the segment

website-performance:browser_os_performan.title: Browser & OS Performance
website-performance:browser_os_performan.formula: Users by web browser (top bars) and by operating system (bottom bars), each ranked by user count
website-performance:browser_os_performan.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution by web browser (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, etc.) and operating system (iOS, Android, Windows, macOS). Important for QA prioritization and identifying compatibility issues.
how_to_read: Top bars show browser distribution - Chrome typically leads
Bottom bars show operating system distribution
iOS and Safari dominance indicates strong Apple user base
Chrome + Android combination shows Google ecosystem users
Lower bars may reveal niche browsers needing attention
Compare engagement rates to spot browser-specific issues
decisions_answered: Which browsers do our users prefer?
What operating systems should we prioritize for testing?
Are there browser-specific performance or conversion issues?
Should we support legacy browsers like Internet Explorer?
Is our mobile app consideration warranted based on OS distribution?
website-performance:browser_os_performan.narration_text:
This dual bar chart shows user distribution by browser and operating system - essential for QA prioritization and compatibility planning.

The formula counts users by browser and operating system separately, showing which technologies your visitors use most.

Reading this chart: Top bars show browser distribution. Chrome typically leads for general audiences, while Safari indicates Apple users. Bottom bars show operating systems - iOS and Android for mobile, Windows and macOS for desktop.

Use this for testing prioritization. Focus QA on your top browsers. If a browser has high users but low engagement, investigate compatibility issues. Consider dropping support for browsers below 1 percent share.

Key questions: Which browsers need testing? What OS mix do we have? Are there browser-specific issues? Should we support legacy browsers? This guides technical decisions.

website-performance:campaigns.title: Campaign Performance
website-performance:campaigns.formula: One row per named GA4 campaign (generic buckets like direct/organic excluded) with its sessions, users, engagement rate, revenue and revenue ÷ sessions; ranked by sessions
website-performance:campaigns.formula_explanation: Shows performance of named marketing campaigns from Google Ads, Meta, email, and other sources. Excludes generic traffic categories like (direct) and (organic).
how_to_read: Each row represents a named campaign driving traffic
Sessions show how much traffic each campaign generates
Engagement Rate indicates traffic quality from each campaign
Revenue shows direct attribution to campaigns
Revenue per Session measures campaign efficiency
Sort by any metric to identify top or underperforming campaigns
Campaign names often indicate platform, audience, and strategy
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most traffic?
What is the revenue return from each campaign?
Which campaigns have the best engagement rates?
Are there campaigns with high traffic but low conversion?
How does campaign performance compare to spend?
website-performance:campaigns.narration_text:
This table shows performance of named marketing campaigns - essential for evaluating campaign effectiveness.

The formula aggregates sessions, users, engagement, and revenue by campaign name, excluding generic categories.

Reading this table: Each row is a named campaign. Sessions show traffic volume. Engagement rate indicates quality. Revenue shows direct attribution. Revenue per session measures efficiency.

Compare campaigns to find winners and losers. High sessions with low engagement may need audience refinement. High engagement with low revenue may need landing page optimization. Campaign names often reveal strategy - PMAX, Brand, Retargeting.

Key questions: Which campaigns perform best? What's the revenue return? Are there efficiency issues? Should budget shift? Cross-reference with ad platform data for full picture.

website-performance:channel_breakdown.title: Traffic by Channel
website-performance:channel_breakdown.formula: Sessions grouped by GA4 default channel grouping (Direct, Organic Search, Paid Search, Email, Social, Referral), ranked by session volume (horizontal bars)
website-performance:channel_breakdown.formula_explanation: Groups all website sessions by their default channel grouping from GA4 - including Direct, Organic Search, Paid Search, Email, Social, and Referral. This shows where your traffic originates.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent each traffic channel, sorted by session volume
Longer bars indicate channels driving more traffic
Direct traffic comes from users typing your URL or using bookmarks
Organic Search represents unpaid Google/Bing search traffic
Paid Search and Paid Social show advertising-driven traffic
Email shows traffic from email marketing campaigns
Click any channel bar to filter the entire page to that channel
decisions_answered: Which channels drive the most traffic to our website?
What is our paid vs organic traffic mix?
Is our email marketing driving significant traffic?
Should we invest more in specific channels?
How dependent are we on any single traffic source?
website-performance:channel_breakdown.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows traffic distribution across marketing channels - essential for understanding your traffic mix and acquisition strategy.

The formula groups sessions by GA4's default channel grouping and calculates each channel's share of total traffic. Channels include Direct, Organic Search, Paid Search, Email, Social, and Referral.

Reading this chart: Bars are sorted by session volume. Longer bars mean more traffic from that channel. Direct typically leads for established brands. Organic Search shows SEO effectiveness. Paid channels show advertising ROI.

A healthy mix avoids over-dependence on any single channel. If one channel dominates excessively, diversification may reduce risk. Compare engagement rates across channels for quality insights.

Key questions: Which channels drive traffic? What's the paid vs organic mix? Is email effective? Should you shift budget? Click any bar to analyze that channel in depth.

website-performance:demographics.title: Demographics
website-performance:demographics.formula: Users by age bracket (bar chart) and by gender (donut), each from GA4 demographic data
website-performance:demographics.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution by age bracket and gender from GA4 demographic data. Age distribution is displayed as a bar chart and gender as a donut chart. These inform messaging, channel selection, creative direction, and product positioning decisions.
how_to_read: Left side shows age distribution - bars for each bracket from 18-24 to 65+
Right side shows gender split as a donut chart
The dominant age bracket indicates your core audience
Gender composition affects creative direction and targeting
Compare engagement rates across age groups for content insights
Note: GA4 demographic data requires user consent and may be sampled
decisions_answered: What is the age and gender distribution of our visitors?
Which age groups are most engaged with our content?
Should our marketing messaging target specific demographics?
Does our demographic mix match our target audience?
How do demographic patterns align with our product positioning?
website-performance:demographics.narration_text:
This combined chart shows user demographics - age distribution as a bar chart and gender split as a donut chart.

The formula counts users by GA4 age brackets and gender, excluding unknown values. Together they paint a picture of who your visitors are.

Reading this chart: On the left, bars show user count per age group, ordered 18-24 through 65+. Your dominant bracket reveals the core audience. On the right, the donut shows gender split.

Consider the implications. If 35-44 dominates, messaging should resonate with established adults. If 25-34 leads, social media may be more effective. A female-dominant audience suggests imagery and copy resonating with women.

Key questions: What's the age and gender mix? Which demographics engage most? Should messaging target specific groups? Use this for persona development and creative direction.

website-performance:device_breakdown.title: Device Breakdown
website-performance:device_breakdown.formula: Users split across mobile, desktop and tablet (donut), with each device's share of total users
website-performance:device_breakdown.formula_explanation: Segments users by their device type. Mobile, desktop, and tablet users often have different behaviors, conversion rates, and session characteristics that inform UX and marketing decisions.
how_to_read: The donut chart shows user distribution across device types
Center displays total users across all devices
Mobile typically dominates for most e-commerce sites (60%+)
Desktop users often have higher conversion rates and order values
Tablet represents a smaller but distinct user segment
The trend chart shows how device mix changes over time
Click any segment to filter the page to that device type
decisions_answered: What percentage of users access our site via mobile?
Is our mobile vs desktop split healthy for our industry?
Are device proportions changing over time?
Should we prioritize mobile-first design improvements?
Does tablet traffic warrant dedicated optimization?
website-performance:device_breakdown.narration_text:
This donut chart and trend visualization shows how users access your website across device types - critical for UX prioritization.

The formula counts unique users by device category, filtered to mobile, desktop, and tablet. The share percentage shows each device's proportion of total users.

Reading this chart: The donut shows current device distribution. Mobile typically dominates at 60 percent or more. The center displays total users. The trend chart shows how the mix evolves over time.

Device insights matter for design decisions. Mobile users may have lower conversion rates but higher volume. Desktop users often spend more per order. Watch for mobile growth trends.

Key questions: What's the mobile percentage? Is the mix changing? Should you prioritize mobile UX? How does each device convert? Click a segment for device-specific analysis.

website-performance:geographic.title: Geographic Performance
website-performance:geographic.formula: Users by US state/region with each region's engagement rate and revenue ÷ users, ranked by user count (top 25)
website-performance:geographic.formula_explanation: Maps user distribution across US states/regions, revealing geographic patterns in traffic, engagement, and customer value. Useful for regional marketing and logistics decisions.
how_to_read: Bars show user count by state, ranked highest to lowest
Large population states (CA, TX, FL) typically lead in volume
Look for states with high users relative to population (brand strength)
Engagement rate varies by region - some areas convert better
Revenue per user identifies high-value geographic segments
Climate patterns may affect gardening product interest
decisions_answered: Where are our customers concentrated geographically?
Which states have the highest customer density?
Are there regional engagement or conversion differences?
Should we run geo-targeted marketing campaigns?
Which regions represent expansion opportunities?
website-performance:geographic.narration_text:
This bar chart shows user distribution across US states - revealing geographic patterns in your customer base.

The formula counts users by region, calculates engagement rates, and computes revenue per user for each state.

Reading this chart: Bars ranked by user count. Large states like California and Texas naturally lead. More interesting is density - states with high users relative to population indicate brand strength.

Look beyond volume. Some states may have fewer users but higher engagement or revenue per user. These represent quality expansion opportunities. Consider climate zones for seasonal products.

Key questions: Where are customers concentrated? Which states over-index? Are there regional conversion differences? Should you geo-target? This informs regional strategy.

website-performance:source_medium.title: Source / Medium
website-performance:source_medium.formula: One row per source/medium pair with its sessions, users, engagement rate and revenue, ranked by sessions (top 25)
website-performance:source_medium.formula_explanation: Breaks down traffic by specific source (like google, facebook, newsletter) and medium (like organic, cpc, email). This provides granular insight into exactly where traffic originates.
how_to_read: Source identifies the specific origin (google, facebook, direct, etc.)
Medium identifies the marketing mechanism (organic, cpc, email, referral)
Sessions column shows traffic volume from each source/medium pair
Engagement Rate indicates traffic quality - higher is better
Revenue shows the direct value generated by each traffic source
Sort by any column to identify top performers or problem areas
Use the search box to find specific sources or mediums
decisions_answered: Which specific sources drive the most traffic?
What mediums are most effective for each source?
Which source/medium combinations have the best engagement?
Where is our revenue coming from at a granular level?
Are there underperforming sources that need optimization?
website-performance:source_medium.narration_text:
This table shows traffic broken down by source and medium - the most granular view of where your visitors originate.

The formula groups sessions by both source (the specific origin like google or facebook) and medium (the mechanism like organic or cpc). Engagement rate measures traffic quality.

Reading this table: Each row represents a unique source/medium combination. Google organic is typically the largest. Compare engagement rates to identify quality sources. Revenue shows direct attribution.

Sort by different columns to find insights. High sessions with low engagement may indicate targeting issues. High engagement with low sessions suggests promotion opportunities.

Key questions: Which sources drive traffic? What mediums work best? Where does revenue come from? Which sources need optimization? Use search to find specific sources.

website-performance:top_events.title: Top Events
website-performance:top_events.formula: GA4 event types ranked by total event count, with average events per user (top 12)
website-performance:top_events.formula_explanation: Ranks user events by frequency, showing which actions visitors take most often. Events include page views, product interactions, cart actions, and conversions.
how_to_read: Bars show event frequency, ranked by total count
view_item_list indicates product browsing behavior
add_to_cart shows shopping intent
begin_checkout and purchase show conversion funnel progress
page_view is a baseline engagement metric
Unusual event spikes may indicate bugs or bot activity
Events per user shows engagement depth
decisions_answered: What actions do users take most frequently?
How many users progress through the purchase funnel?
Is add_to_cart volume healthy relative to purchases?
Are there unexpected events indicating issues?
How engaged are users based on events per session?
website-performance:top_events.narration_text:
This bar chart shows top user events by frequency - revealing how visitors interact with your website.

The formula counts event occurrences and calculates events per user for engagement depth measurement.

Reading this chart: Bars show event count, ranked highest to lowest. View_item_list shows browsing. Add_to_cart shows intent. Purchase is your conversion event. Page_view is baseline engagement.

Ratios matter. Compare add_to_cart to purchase - a large gap indicates cart abandonment. Compare view_item_list to add_to_cart for product page effectiveness. Unusual event spikes warrant investigation.

Key questions: What actions are common? How's the funnel ratio? Is cart abandonment high? Are there unusual patterns? Events reveal user journey details.

website-performance:top_pages.title: Top Landing Pages
website-performance:top_pages.formula: One row per page ranked by users, showing page views, users, average engagement time per user, and revenue (top 50)
website-performance:top_pages.formula_explanation: Ranks pages by user visits, showing which content drives the most traffic. Includes engagement duration to indicate content quality and revenue attribution for commerce pages.
how_to_read: Pages are ranked by unique user visits, not just page views
Homepage (/) typically leads but product pages show intent
Page Views vs Users ratio indicates repeat viewing behavior
Avg Time on Page shows content engagement - longer is usually better
Revenue column shows direct attribution for product/category pages
Search result pages indicate high search usage - consider improving navigation
Use search to find specific pages or URL patterns
decisions_answered: Which pages attract the most visitors?
What product pages are most popular?
Are users spending enough time on key pages?
Which pages generate the most revenue?
Are there high-traffic pages with low engagement needing optimization?
website-performance:top_pages.narration_text:
This table shows your top landing pages by traffic - revealing which content drives visitors and engagement.

The formula counts page views and users, calculates average engagement time, and attributes revenue to each page path.

Reading this table: Pages ranked by user count, not views. Homepage typically leads. Product pages show purchase intent. Check the views-to-users ratio - high ratios mean repeat viewing. Average time indicates engagement quality.

Look for opportunities. High-traffic pages with low engagement need optimization. Product pages with high time-on-page may have friction. Search results pages suggest navigation improvements needed.

Key questions: Which pages attract visitors? What products are popular? Are users engaging? Which pages need optimization? Search for specific URL patterns.

website-performance:traffic_trend.title: Traffic Trend
website-performance:traffic_trend.formula: Daily sessions and users (from GA4) plus daily orders and revenue (from Magento) over the selected range (multi-line)
website-performance:traffic_trend.formula_explanation: Aggregates session counts, unique user counts, and revenue by day from GA4 traffic acquisition data. Sessions represent individual visits, users represent unique visitors, and revenue shows e-commerce transactions attributed to each day.
how_to_read: The blue line shows daily session counts - total visits to your website
The green line shows daily unique users who initiated sessions
The purple dashed line shows daily revenue (right axis scale)
Peaks indicate high-traffic days, often correlating with marketing campaigns or seasonal events
Look for patterns: weekday vs weekend traffic, monthly trends, holiday impacts
Hover over any point to see exact values for all metrics on that date
decisions_answered: How is our website traffic trending over time?
Which days had the highest traffic and revenue?
Are there seasonal patterns in our traffic?
Is revenue correlated with traffic volume?
Did recent campaigns impact traffic levels?
website-performance:traffic_trend.narration_text:
This multi-line chart shows your website traffic trend over time - the foundation for understanding visitor patterns and marketing impact.

The formula aggregates three key metrics by day: sessions (total visits), users (unique visitors), and revenue. Each metric comes from GA4 traffic acquisition reports.

Reading this chart: The blue area shows session volume. The green line tracks unique users. Purple shows revenue on a secondary axis. Peaks indicate high-traffic periods.

Look for patterns. Does traffic spike on certain weekdays? Are there seasonal trends? Do revenue peaks align with traffic peaks? Gaps between sessions and users indicate returning visitors.

Key questions answered: How is traffic trending? Which days perform best? Are campaigns driving traffic? Is revenue correlated with visits? Click any point for daily details.

formula_components.name: Daily Orders & Revenue
formula_components.formula: Magento orders and net revenue per day

weekly-exec:budget_pacing.title: Budget Pacing by Channel
weekly-exec:budget_pacing.formula: For each ad channel: month-to-date spend versus the projected monthly budget; target spend = monthly budget × (day of month ÷ days in month), and pacing % flags the channel as over-pacing (above 110%), under-pacing (below 90%), or on track.
weekly-exec:budget_pacing.formula_explanation: Compares actual month-to-date spending against expected spending based on daily budgets. Helps ensure you are not over-spending or under-utilizing available budget.
how_to_read: Progress bars show spend pacing relative to monthly budget
Green (ON_TRACK): Spending is 90-110% of expected pace
Blue (UNDER_PACING): Below 90% - budget room available
Orange (OVER_PACING): Above 110% - may exceed budget
Percentages show how much of the budget has been consumed
decisions_answered: Are we on track with monthly advertising budgets?
Which channels have room for additional spend?
Do we need to pull back on any over-pacing channels?
Will we hit our monthly spending targets?
weekly-exec:budget_pacing.narration_text:
This component tracks budget pacing - whether you're spending at the right rate to hit monthly targets without going over.

The formula divides actual month-to-date spend by expected spend based on daily budgets and days elapsed. Status thresholds flag under-pacing below 90% and over-pacing above 110%.

Reading this panel: Green bars indicate healthy pacing. Blue under-pacing means budget room available - consider scaling performing channels. Orange over-pacing warns of potential budget exhaustion before month end.

Budget pacing matters for planning: Under-pacing misses growth opportunities. Over-pacing may require mid-month budget adjustments or pausing campaigns.

Key questions: Are budgets on track? Where can we spend more? Do we need to pull back anywhere? Will we hit monthly targets?

formula_components.name: Target Spend
formula_components.formula: Monthly budget × day of month ÷ days in month

formula_components.name: Pacing Status
formula_components.formula: Over-pacing when pacing % > 110, under-pacing when < 90, else on track

weekly-exec:channel_matrix.title: Channel Performance Matrix
weekly-exec:channel_matrix.formula: Each campaign (with over 50 spend, top 25) plotted by spend on one axis and ROAS (revenue ÷ spend) on the other; the campaign is placed in a Star, Opportunity, Question, or Monitor quadrant by comparing its spend and ROAS to the median across campaigns.
weekly-exec:channel_matrix.formula_explanation: A 2x2 quadrant analysis plotting each channel by spend (X-axis) and ROAS (Y-axis). Channels above median ROAS with high spend are Stars to scale. High ROAS with low spend are Opportunities. High spend with low ROAS are Questions requiring optimization.
how_to_read: Each bubble represents a marketing channel (Google Search, Shopping, Meta, etc.)
Bubble position shows spend (X) vs ROAS (Y) performance
STAR quadrant (upper-right): Scale these channels - high spend AND high ROAS
OPPORTUNITY quadrant (upper-left): Increase budget - high ROAS but low spend
QUESTION quadrant (lower-right): Optimize or cut - high spend but low ROAS
MONITOR quadrant (lower-left): Test or pause - low spend AND low ROAS
decisions_answered: Which channels should we scale (increase budget)?
Which channels need optimization or budget cuts?
Where are untapped growth opportunities?
How does performance compare across all channels?
weekly-exec:channel_matrix.narration_text:
This scatter plot creates a strategic view of all marketing channels based on their spend and return on ad spend.

The formula assigns quadrants based on median values. Channels above median on both axes are Stars. Above median ROAS but below median spend are Opportunities. High spend with below-median ROAS are Question Marks requiring attention.

Reading this chart: Look for clustering patterns. Multiple channels in the Star quadrant indicates healthy diversification. Concentration in Question or Monitor quadrants signals optimization urgency.

Strategic implications: Scale Stars confidently - they've proven efficiency at volume. Increase Opportunity budgets carefully - test if efficiency holds at higher spend. Fix or cut Question marks - they're consuming budget without adequate return.

Key questions: What channels should we scale? Where should we cut? Where are growth opportunities? This is your channel strategy decision tool.

formula_components.name: Quadrant
formula_components.formula: Star = spend and ROAS both above median; Opportunity = ROAS above, spend below; Question = spend above, ROAS below; Monitor = both below

weekly-exec:concerns.title: Top 5 Concerns This Week
weekly-exec:concerns.formula: The five campaigns most needing attention: each campaign's ROAS (revenue ÷ spend) is flagged by severity — zero revenue or ROAS below 1, ROAS below 2, or ROAS dropped sharply versus the prior period — and shown with its spend and ROAS, ranked by severity then spend.
weekly-exec:concerns.formula_explanation: Identifies campaigns and ads requiring immediate attention. Severity levels prioritize which issues need the most urgent intervention to stop wasting budget.
how_to_read: Each row shows a campaign or ad requiring attention
Red badges indicate high severity (1-2), orange for medium (3)
ROAS and spend columns show the efficiency problem
Concern reason explains why this item flagged
Address severity 1-2 items immediately to stop losses
decisions_answered: What is underperforming and needs immediate attention?
Which campaigns should we pause or reduce budget?
How much money are we losing on poor performers?
What is the urgency level for each concern?
weekly-exec:concerns.narration_text:
This table flags your top five concerns - campaigns and ads that are underperforming and may need intervention.

The formula assigns severity levels: 1 for losing money or zero conversions, 2 for below-breakeven ROAS, 3 for significant efficiency drops, 4 for declining but still profitable items.

Reading this table: Focus on severity 1 and 2 first - these are actively losing money. The ROAS and spend columns quantify the problem magnitude. Concern reasons explain the specific issue.

Action bias matters: Every day a severity 1 concern runs, you're losing money. Quick decisions to pause, reduce budget, or fix these items protect profitability.

Key questions: What needs immediate attention? Should we pause anything? How much are we losing? What's the priority order for fixes?

formula_components.name: Severity
formula_components.formula: 1 if zero revenue or ROAS < 1; 2 if ROAS < 2; 3 if ROAS fell below half the prior period; else 4

weekly-exec:forecast.title: Forecast vs Actual
weekly-exec:forecast.formula: Actual revenue so far this week is extrapolated to a full week (actual ÷ days elapsed × 7) and compared to a forecast equal to the average weekly revenue over the trailing four weeks; the status reads above, below, or on forecast based on one standard deviation, with year-over-year context.
weekly-exec:forecast.formula_explanation: Projects full-week revenue based on days elapsed, then compares to a 4-week rolling average forecast. Helps identify if current week is tracking above or below expectations.
how_to_read: Actual MTD shows revenue collected so far this week
Projected Week extrapolates the full week based on current pace
Forecast shows the expected value based on 4-week average
Variance percentage and status badge show performance vs expectation
YoY comparison provides year-over-year context
decisions_answered: Are we on track to hit this week's revenue target?
How does current performance compare to recent weeks?
Should we take any action to close a forecast gap?
How does this week compare to the same week last year?
weekly-exec:forecast.narration_text:
This panel compares projected weekly revenue against forecast - helping you know if action is needed before the week ends.

The formula projects full-week revenue by extrapolating current daily pace. The 4-week rolling average sets the forecast baseline. Variance shows how far above or below expectations you're tracking.

Reading this panel: Green ABOVE FORECAST status means you're beating expectations. Red BELOW FORECAST requires investigation or action. The YoY comparison adds historical context.

Early week readings are noisy: Monday projections based on one day are less reliable than Thursday projections based on four days. Statistical confidence improves through the week.

Key questions: Are we on track for the week? Do we need corrective action? How does this compare to recent and historical performance?

formula_components.name: Forecast Revenue
formula_components.formula: Average weekly revenue over the trailing 4 weeks

formula_components.name: Variance %
formula_components.formula: (Projected week revenue − forecast revenue) ÷ forecast revenue × 100

weekly-exec:orders.title: Weekly Orders
weekly-exec:orders.formula: Weekly Orders (estimated) = weekly revenue ÷ $50 assumed average order value; WoW Change % = revenue WoW
weekly-exec:orders.formula_explanation: Estimated order count based on revenue divided by a typical average order value. This provides a volume metric to complement revenue totals.
how_to_read: The main value shows estimated order count for the week
Higher order counts with stable AOV indicate healthy demand
Compare order trends alongside revenue trends
Significant order declines may indicate conversion issues
Week-over-week percentage shows volume trajectory
decisions_answered: How many orders did we process this week?
Is order volume growing alongside revenue?
Are we seeing conversion rate issues?
Is demand consistent with expectations?
weekly-exec:orders.narration_text:
This KPI estimates weekly order volume - a key demand indicator separate from revenue.

The formula estimates orders by dividing revenue by typical average order value. For precise counts, Google Ads conversion tracking provides actual purchase events.

Reading this card: Order count shows demand volume independent of price changes. Stable orders with declining revenue may indicate AOV pressure. Growing orders with flat revenue suggests product mix shifts.

Week-over-week changes reveal demand trends. Significant order drops warrant investigation into site issues, inventory problems, or conversion funnel problems.

Key questions: How many orders this week? Is volume growing? Are there conversion issues? Is demand meeting expectations?

formula_components.name: WoW Change %
formula_components.formula: revenue week-over-week % (current revenue − prior week) ÷ prior week × 100

weekly-exec:revenue.title: Weekly Revenue
weekly-exec:revenue.formula: Weekly Revenue = GA4 channel-attributed revenue across all channels for the week; WoW Change %
weekly-exec:revenue.formula_explanation: Total revenue from all channels this week based on GA4 attribution. Revenue is attributed at the session level using the default channel grouping from Google Analytics.
how_to_read: The main value shows total revenue for the current week
The percentage change indicates week-over-week performance
Green indicates positive growth, red indicates decline
The sparkline shows the 8-week revenue trend
Target weekly revenue is typically $500K or higher
decisions_answered: How did we perform this week compared to last week?
Are we on track to hit our monthly/quarterly revenue targets?
Is revenue trending upward or downward over recent weeks?
Does the current week require any immediate intervention?
weekly-exec:revenue.narration_text:
This KPI card displays your weekly revenue - the primary measure of business performance for the Monday leadership meeting.

The formula sums all revenue from GA4's traffic acquisition report for the current week. The week-over-week percentage compares to the previous Monday-Sunday period.

Reading this card: The main number shows total weekly revenue. Green percentages indicate growth over last week, red indicates decline. The sparkline provides 8-week context.

For e-commerce, weekly revenue over $500K is typically healthy. Significant drops of 10% or more warrant investigation into specific channels or campaigns.

Key questions answered: How did we perform this week? Are we on pace for monthly targets? Is revenue trending in the right direction?

formula_components.name: Revenue
formula_components.formula: sum of channel revenue over the week

formula_components.name: WoW Change %
formula_components.formula: (current revenue − last week revenue) ÷ last week revenue × 100

weekly-exec:roas.title: Blended ROAS
weekly-exec:roas.formula: Blended ROAS = weekly ad revenue ÷ weekly ad spend (Google + Meta); WoW Change %
weekly-exec:roas.formula_explanation: Return on Ad Spend measures advertising efficiency. A 4x ROAS means $4 revenue for every $1 spent. This blends Google and Meta performance into a single efficiency metric.
how_to_read: ROAS above 4.0x indicates healthy paid media efficiency
ROAS between 2.5x-4.0x suggests optimization opportunities
ROAS below 2.5x typically indicates unprofitable acquisition
The target line at 4.0x shows your efficiency threshold
Week-over-week changes signal efficiency trends
decisions_answered: Are our ads profitable overall?
Is advertising efficiency improving or declining?
Should we increase or decrease ad spend?
Do we need to optimize underperforming campaigns?
weekly-exec:roas.narration_text:
Blended ROAS is the single most important efficiency metric for paid media - showing how much revenue you generate per dollar spent.

The formula divides total attributed revenue by total ad spend across platforms. A 4x ROAS target is standard for e-commerce, meaning $4 revenue for every $1 invested.

Reading this card: 4x or above is healthy and indicates room to scale. Between 2.5x and 4x suggests optimization opportunities but still profitable. Below 2.5x typically means acquisition is unprofitable after accounting for product costs and overhead.

Week-over-week changes matter more than absolute numbers. Declining ROAS with increasing spend signals efficiency problems requiring immediate attention.

Key questions: Are ads profitable? Is efficiency improving? Should we scale or optimize?

formula_components.name: Blended ROAS
formula_components.formula: total ad-platform revenue ÷ total ad spend for the latest week

weekly-exec:sessions.title: Weekly Sessions
weekly-exec:sessions.formula: Weekly Sessions = total website sessions from all traffic sources for the week; WoW Change %
weekly-exec:sessions.formula_explanation: Total website sessions from all traffic sources including organic, paid, direct, email, and referral. Sessions measure top-of-funnel traffic volume.
how_to_read: The main value shows total website visits for the week
Session growth indicates healthy traffic acquisition
Compare session changes with revenue changes
High sessions with low revenue signals conversion issues
Week-over-week percentage reveals traffic trends
decisions_answered: How much traffic are we generating?
Is traffic growing week over week?
Are we driving enough top-of-funnel volume?
Do traffic patterns align with marketing investments?
weekly-exec:sessions.narration_text:
This KPI tracks total website sessions - the top-of-funnel traffic metric that feeds all downstream conversion.

The formula sums sessions from GA4's traffic acquisition report across all channel groupings. This includes organic, paid, direct, email, social, and referral traffic.

Reading this card: Sessions measure traffic volume regardless of quality. Growing sessions indicate healthy reach and awareness. Compare session growth to revenue growth to assess traffic quality.

High sessions with low revenue signals conversion rate problems. Low sessions with stable revenue may indicate improving traffic quality or higher intent visitors.

Key questions: How much traffic this week? Is traffic growing? Does traffic volume support revenue goals? Are marketing investments driving expected traffic?

formula_components.name: Sessions
formula_components.formula: sum of sessions over the week

formula_components.name: WoW Change %
formula_components.formula: (current sessions − last week sessions) ÷ last week sessions × 100

weekly-exec:spend.title: Total Ad Spend
weekly-exec:spend.formula: Total Ad Spend = combined Google Ads + Meta Ads spend for the latest week; WoW Change %
weekly-exec:spend.formula_explanation: Combined advertising spend across Google Ads and Meta Ads platforms. Google costs are stored in micros (millionths of currency) and converted. This represents your total paid media investment.
how_to_read: The main value shows combined Google + Meta ad spend for the week
Week-over-week change indicates spending trajectory
Higher spend is not inherently good or bad - context matters
Compare spend changes alongside ROAS to assess efficiency
The sparkline shows 8-week spending trend
decisions_answered: How much did we invest in paid advertising this week?
Is our spending pacing ahead of or behind budget?
Are we scaling spend appropriately with performance?
Do we need to adjust budget allocations?
weekly-exec:spend.narration_text:
This KPI tracks your total advertising investment across Google and Meta platforms combined.

The formula adds Google Ads cost (converted from micros) and Meta Ads spend. This represents your total paid media investment for the week.

Reading this card: The main number shows weekly ad spend. Week-over-week changes should be interpreted alongside ROAS and revenue changes. Higher spend at good ROAS is positive; higher spend at declining ROAS needs attention.

Budget pacing matters: If spending significantly below budget, you may be missing growth opportunities. Overspending may indicate runaway campaigns or bid issues.

Key questions: How much did we invest? Is spending on pace? Are we scaling appropriately with performance?

formula_components.name: Total Spend
formula_components.formula: sum of Google + Meta spend over the latest week

weekly-exec:weekly_trend.title: 8-Week Revenue Trend
weekly-exec:weekly_trend.formula: Weekly ad-attributed revenue over the last eight weeks, stacked by platform (Google Ads and Meta Ads), with each platform's spend, revenue and ROAS available per week.
weekly-exec:weekly_trend.formula_explanation: Shows weekly revenue contribution by platform over the past 8 weeks. The stacked bars reveal revenue mix while enabling trend analysis across time.
how_to_read: Stacked bars show total revenue split by Google (blue) and Meta (purple)
Bar height represents total weekly ad-attributed revenue
Compare bar heights across weeks to see revenue trends
Look for seasonal patterns or campaign impact timing
Use the legend to toggle individual platform views
decisions_answered: How is revenue trending over the past 8 weeks?
What is the platform mix contribution to revenue?
Are there seasonal patterns affecting performance?
Which platform is growing or declining in contribution?
weekly-exec:weekly_trend.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows your 8-week revenue trend - providing context for whether current performance is normal or anomalous.

The formula sums ad-attributed revenue weekly, separated by Google and Meta contributions. This reveals both total trends and platform mix changes.

Reading this chart: Compare bar heights across weeks. Consistent or growing bars indicate healthy trajectory. Declining bars signal problems. The platform split shows if one channel is driving or dragging performance.

Trend context matters: A 20% revenue drop is less concerning if it follows a promotional spike versus a gradual decline. Eight weeks provides enough context to identify patterns.

Key questions: How is revenue trending? Is one platform growing or declining? Are there seasonal patterns? Is current week normal or anomalous?

weekly-exec:wins.title: Top 5 Wins This Week
weekly-exec:wins.formula: The five best-performing items this week: campaigns whose ROAS improved versus the prior period and products whose revenue rose versus the prior period (with floors on prior and current revenue), each shown with its week-over-week improvement percentage, ranked by improvement.
weekly-exec:wins.formula_explanation: Identifies the top performing campaigns and products that exceeded expectations this week. Uses week-over-week improvement percentages to highlight what is working well.
how_to_read: Each row shows a campaign or product with exceptional performance
Green badges indicate the source (Google, Meta, Product)
The improvement percentage shows week-over-week gain
Win reason explains what made this item stand out
Use wins to identify successful strategies to replicate
decisions_answered: What worked exceptionally well this week?
Which campaigns should we consider scaling?
What products are driving unexpected growth?
Are there successful patterns we can replicate elsewhere?
weekly-exec:wins.narration_text:
