This table highlights your top five wins for the week - the campaigns and products that outperformed expectations.

The formula identifies wins based on improvement thresholds: campaigns with 20% or greater ROAS improvement above 3x baseline, and products with 30% or greater revenue growth above $500.

Reading this table: Each row shows a standout performer. The improvement percentage quantifies the win magnitude. The reason field explains what drove the performance - use this to identify replicable patterns.

Strategic value: Wins aren't just celebration fodder. They reveal what's working. Scaling successful campaigns and understanding product momentum compounds business growth.

Key questions: What worked well this week? Should we scale winning campaigns? What products are gaining traction? Can we replicate success patterns?

ml-intervention-propensity:actionable_cu.title: Actionable Customers
ml-intervention-propensity:actionable_cu.formula: Actionable Customers = count of customers whose predicted intervention propensity is at or above the optimal threshold (≈11.4%)
ml-intervention-propensity:actionable_cu.formula_explanation: Customers whose predicted propensity meets the optimal threshold, making them eligible for a retention campaign.
how_to_read: Count of customers worth targeting with a retention message.
Subtitle shows percentage of total scored base that is actionable.
Use this to size your campaign: budget, CRM list, send volume.
decisions_answered: How large should our next intervention campaign be?
Are we under- or over-targeting compared to capacity?
ml-intervention-propensity:actionable_cu.narration_text: Actionable Customers shows how many people the model believes will respond to a retention intervention within 90 days.

ml-intervention-propensity:high_response.title: High-Response Pool
ml-intervention-propensity:high_response.formula: High-Response Pool = count of customers whose predicted intervention propensity is at or above 50%
ml-intervention-propensity:high_response.formula_explanation: Customers with propensity above 50% -- prime candidates for premium-tier intervention spend.
how_to_read: A smaller, higher-quality audience within the actionable pool.
These customers should receive your highest-effort interventions: personal calls, exclusive offers.
Subtitle shows their share of the total scored base.
decisions_answered: Who should receive our most personalised interventions?
How much budget should we reserve for VIP-tier outreach?
ml-intervention-propensity:high_response.narration_text: The High-Response Pool highlights customers most likely to respond -- prime candidates for premium campaigns.

ml-intervention-propensity:mean_propensi.title: Mean Propensity Score
ml-intervention-propensity:mean_propensi.formula: Mean Propensity Score = average predicted intervention propensity across all scored customers, shown as a percentage
ml-intervention-propensity:mean_propensi.formula_explanation: Average predicted probability across all scored customers that they will order within 90 days if sent an intervention.
how_to_read: Overall responsiveness health of the customer base.
Rising mean signals the base is becoming more intervention-ready.
Falling mean may indicate customer fatigue or data staleness.
decisions_answered: Is the customer base becoming more or less responsive to interventions?
Should we scale up or pull back campaign frequency?
ml-intervention-propensity:mean_propensi.narration_text: Mean Propensity Score is the average response probability -- your overall campaign health indicator.

ml-intervention-propensity:top_intervent.title: Top Intervention Type
ml-intervention-propensity:top_intervent.formula: Top Intervention Type = the intervention trigger category (e.g. Abandoned Browse / Checkout / Cart) with the highest customer count across scored customers
ml-intervention-propensity:top_intervent.formula_explanation: The intervention trigger category with the highest customer count: Abandoned Browse, Checkout, or Cart.
how_to_read: Tells you where the volume of your intervention opportunity sits.
Abandoned Checkout is the highest-intent signal.
Use this to prioritise which automation flow to optimise first.
decisions_answered: Which intervention trigger should we prioritise in our next CRM sprint?
Is the mix shifting toward higher-intent signals?
ml-intervention-propensity:top_intervent.narration_text: Top Intervention Type shows which customer behaviour triggers the most intervention opportunities.

ml-intervention-propensity:model_auc.title: Model AUC
ml-intervention-propensity:model_auc.formula: Model AUC = area under the ROC curve on the held-out test set, shown as a percentage (how reliably the model ranks responders above non-responders)
ml-intervention-propensity:model_auc.formula_explanation: Measures how well the model separates responders from non-responders. 71.8% means the model ranks a random responder above a random non-responder 71.8% of the time.
how_to_read: 50% = random guessing. 71.8% is meaningfully better.
Values above 65% are considered usable for campaign targeting.
Validates that propensity scores are trustworthy.
decisions_answered: Can we trust the propensity scores to guide targeting decisions?
Should we re-train with more data or new features?
ml-intervention-propensity:model_auc.narration_text: Model AUC validates the quality of intervention propensity scores -- higher values mean more reliable targeting.

ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_di.title: Propensity Score Distribution
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_di.formula: Histogram of intervention propensity across 10 equal bins from 0-10% to 90-100%; bars show how many customers fall into each propensity bucket.
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_di.formula_explanation: Shows how many customers fall into each propensity bucket, revealing the shape of the response opportunity.
how_to_read: A spike at 10-20% means most customers are just above threshold -- they need a gentle nudge.
A fat right tail (50%+) means you have a large premium-tier audience.
Grey = low propensity; amber = borderline; green = high-value targets.
decisions_answered: How should we tier campaign spend across the audience?
What proportion of customers are high-priority vs low-priority?
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_di.narration_text: The Propensity Score Distribution shows where your customers cluster across the response likelihood spectrum.

ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_ti.title: Response Propensity Tiers
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_ti.formula: Customers divided into four equal-size quartile tiers by intervention propensity (Low / Medium / High / Top); each bar shows the tier's customer count, share and propensity range.
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_ti.formula_explanation: Divides the customer base into four equal-size tiers by propensity score, each representing a distinct campaign treatment level.
how_to_read: Top tier = highest propensity quartile -- allocate premium budget here.
High tier = strong candidates for standard personalised emails.
Medium tier = worth a low-cost automated touchpoint.
Low tier = unlikely to be cost-effective; monitor passively.
decisions_answered: How should we allocate campaign budget across the 4 tiers?
How many customers warrant personalised vs automated outreach?
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_ti.narration_text: Response Propensity Tiers divides customers into four quartiles to match campaign investment to response likelihood.

ml-intervention-propensity:intervention_.title: Intervention Type Breakdown
ml-intervention-propensity:intervention_.formula: Customers split across intervention trigger types (Abandoned Browse / Checkout / Cart) with the count, average intervention propensity and historical response rate per type.
ml-intervention-propensity:intervention_.formula_explanation: Customer split across Abandoned Browse, Checkout, and Cart trigger types with average propensity and historical response rate.
how_to_read: Abandoned Checkout = highest purchase intent.
Abandoned Cart = high intent -- product added but not checked out.
Abandoned Browse = lowest intent.
Response rate shows historical conversion for each trigger type.
decisions_answered: Which automation flow should we invest in improving first?
What is the expected response rate for each campaign type?
ml-intervention-propensity:intervention_.narration_text: Intervention Type Breakdown reveals how customers are distributed across trigger types and which yields the strongest response.

formula_components.name: Avg propensity
formula_components.formula: Average intervention propensity within the trigger type × 100

formula_components.name: Response rate
formula_components.formula: Customers who responded ÷ customers in the trigger type × 100

ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_by.title: Propensity Tiers by Intervention Type
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_by.formula: Stacked bar showing, within each intervention trigger type, the share of customers in each propensity quartile tier (Low / Medium / High / Top), normalised to % of that type.
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_by.formula_explanation: Stacked bar showing the tier composition (Low/Medium/High/Top) within each intervention trigger type.
how_to_read: A larger Top segment for Checkout abandoners confirms they are your highest-quality audience.
A type dominated by Low bars means most of that audience is unlikely to respond.
Compare tier mix across types to decide where to shift campaign volume.
decisions_answered: Which intervention type has the richest high-propensity audience?
Should we rebalance campaign volume toward higher-propensity trigger types?
ml-intervention-propensity:propensity_by.narration_text: Propensity Tiers by Intervention Type shows the quality composition of each trigger category.

ml-intervention-propensity:response_driv.title: Top Response Drivers
ml-intervention-propensity:response_driv.formula: Top 10 model features ranked by their share of total gain (LightGBM information gain); each bar is a feature's % of total gain.
ml-intervention-propensity:response_driv.formula_explanation: Features that most influence whether a customer will respond to an intervention, ranked by information gain.
how_to_read: Purchase Frequency is the #1 driver -- loyal repeat buyers respond best.
Total Spend confirms higher-value customers are more responsive.
Email Open Rate in the top 10 means email-engaged customers are better targets.
Recency matters: target customers who shopped recently.
decisions_answered: What customer attributes should we use to build targeting segments?
Should we prioritise loyal buyers or lapsed one-time buyers?
ml-intervention-propensity:response_driv.narration_text: Top Response Drivers reveals which behaviours predict intervention success -- your guide to smarter segmentation.

ml-intervention-propensity:frequency_seg.title: Propensity by Purchase Frequency
ml-intervention-propensity:frequency_seg.formula: Average intervention propensity for customers grouped by total order-count bucket (1x / 2-3x / 4-7x / 8-15x / 16x+); bars show average propensity per purchase-frequency tier.
ml-intervention-propensity:frequency_seg.formula_explanation: Average intervention propensity for customers grouped by total order count, showing how loyalty tier affects response likelihood.
how_to_read: Higher-frequency buyers show the strongest intervention response — prioritise 4x+ buyers for high-effort campaigns.
A rising trend from 1x to 16x+ confirms loyal buyers are better intervention candidates.
Personalise: frequent buyers get loyalty rewards; new buyers get discovery offers.
Bar labels show the average propensity (%) for each segment.
decisions_answered: Should we prioritise win-back campaigns for frequent buyers or nudges for 1x buyers?
How should we personalise intervention messaging by loyalty tier?
ml-intervention-propensity:frequency_seg.narration_text: Propensity by Purchase Frequency shows how loyalty tier drives intervention responsiveness.

ml-intervention-propensity:cohort_trend.title: Quarterly Cohort Trend
ml-intervention-propensity:cohort_trend.formula: Per quarterly cohort (grouped by scoring cutoff date): average intervention propensity and the actual historical response rate; line chart tracking how propensity and response evolve across cohorts.
ml-intervention-propensity:cohort_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks how average propensity and actual response rates have evolved across quarterly cohorts from Q1 2025 to Q2 2026.
how_to_read: Rising propensity = the base is becoming more intervention-ready.
Falling trend may indicate customer fatigue -- consider frequency capping.
Compare propensity vs response rate to assess model calibration.
Seasonal spikes inform which quarters to run highest-volume campaigns.
decisions_answered: Is intervention responsiveness improving or declining across quarters?
Which quarter should we plan our largest campaign push?
ml-intervention-propensity:cohort_trend.narration_text: The Quarterly Cohort Trend shows how intervention responsiveness has evolved over six quarters.

formula_components.name: Avg propensity
formula_components.formula: Average intervention propensity of customers in the cohort × 100

formula_components.name: Response rate
formula_components.formula: Customers who responded to an intervention ÷ cohort size × 100

ml-intervention-propensity:email_engagem.title: Email Engagement vs Propensity
ml-intervention-propensity:email_engagem.formula: Average intervention propensity for customers bucketed by 30-day email open rate (0%, 1-10%, 11-25%, 26-50%, 50%+); bars show average propensity per email-engagement bucket.
ml-intervention-propensity:email_engagem.formula_explanation: Average intervention propensity for customers binned by 30-day email open rate, revealing how email engagement correlates with response likelihood.
how_to_read: Higher email engagement directly predicts intervention response — prioritise the email channel for 26%+ openers.
A clear upward trend confirms email engagement is a strong proxy for intervention readiness.
26-50% and 50%+ openers are your email-channel priority.
0% openers should be routed to SMS, push, or retargeting ads instead.
decisions_answered: Which customers should receive email interventions vs alternative channels?
What email open rate threshold should we use to qualify customers for email campaigns?
ml-intervention-propensity:email_engagem.narration_text: Email Engagement vs Propensity helps you route customers to the right intervention channel based on email responsiveness.

ml-marketing-mix:channel_roas.title: ROAS by Channel
ml-marketing-mix:channel_roas.formula: Per channel, the average return on ad spend = attributed revenue ÷ spend, taken as the mean of the model's posterior distribution. Higher bars mean a more efficient channel.
ml-marketing-mix:channel_roas.formula_explanation: Mean of the posterior distribution for return on ad spend, per channel.
how_to_read: Bars show average revenue produced per dollar spent.
Higher bars = more efficient channel.
Compare with ROAS CI in the table for uncertainty width.
decisions_answered: Which channel returns the most revenue per dollar?
Where should we scale spend up vs throttle?

ml-marketing-mix:attribution.title: Revenue Attribution
ml-marketing-mix:attribution.formula: Donut splitting total modelled revenue into the organic baseline and each paid channel's attributed revenue. Each slice share = that channel's modelled revenue ÷ total revenue. Organic = total revenue − paid revenue, where total revenue = paid revenue ÷ (1 − organic revenue %).
ml-marketing-mix:attribution.formula_explanation: How total revenue splits between organic baseline and each paid channel.
how_to_read: Donut slices are proportional to revenue contribution.
Organic baseline reflects revenue not driven by paid media.
Center value is the total modelled revenue.
decisions_answered: How dependent are we on paid media vs organic demand?
Which channel would hurt the most if it went dark?

ml-marketing-mix:saturation.title: Channel Saturation
ml-marketing-mix:saturation.formula: Per channel, where current spend sits relative to the model's efficient (optimal) spend = current spend ÷ optimal spend × 100%. Under 70% has headroom; 95-100% is at the frontier; over 110% is over-saturated.
ml-marketing-mix:saturation.formula_explanation: Where current spend sits relative to the model's efficient frontier per channel.
how_to_read: Under 70% = headroom: extra spend still produces strong returns.
95–100% = at the efficient frontier.
Over 110% = over-saturated: extra spend wasted.
decisions_answered: Which channels can absorb additional budget?
Which channels should be throttled before reallocation?

ml-marketing-mix:budget_optimization.title: Recommended Budget Allocation
ml-marketing-mix:budget_optimization.formula: Per channel, grey bar = current daily spend, blue bar = the model's recommended spend that maximises total attributed revenue while holding the total budget fixed. Only the mix shifts; the total stays the same.
ml-marketing-mix:budget_optimization.formula_explanation: Optimal per-channel spend the model would prefer at the same total budget.
how_to_read: Grey bar = current spend; blue bar = model recommendation.
Same total budget — only the mix changes.
Larger gaps = higher reallocation upside.
decisions_answered: How should we redistribute existing budget for more revenue?
Which channels deserve more vs less spend?

formula_components.name: Recommended spend
formula_components.formula: model-optimal spend per channel that maximises total attributed revenue at the same total budget

ml-marketing-mix:response_curves.title: Response Curves (Diminishing Returns)
ml-marketing-mix:response_curves.formula: Per channel, modelled revenue at each spend level following a Hill saturation shape: response = ceiling × spend^shape ÷ (half-saturation^shape + spend^shape). Steep curves still have scaling headroom; flat curves are saturated.
ml-marketing-mix:response_curves.formula_explanation: Modeled revenue at each spend level per channel — Hill saturation transform.
how_to_read: Each mini-chart is one channel; scales differ — don't compare heights across panels.
Steep curves = early-stage scaling potential.
Flat curves = channel saturated; extra spend yields little.
decisions_answered: How much more revenue would another $X of spend produce?
Where is the inflection point past which spend stops paying off?

ml-marketing-mix:marginal_roas.title: Marginal ROAS by Spend
ml-marketing-mix:marginal_roas.formula: Per channel, the revenue produced by the very next dollar of spend at each spend level (the slope of the response curve). At 1.0 the next dollar breaks even; below 1.0 extra spend loses money; above 1.0 it is still profitable.
ml-marketing-mix:marginal_roas.formula_explanation: Revenue produced by the very next dollar of spend at each spend level.
how_to_read: When marginal ROAS = 1, the next dollar exactly breaks even.
Below 1 = additional spend loses money — pull back.
Above 1 = additional spend still profitable — scale up.
decisions_answered: Should we add or remove budget on this channel right now?
What is the breakeven spend ceiling per channel?

ml-marketing-mix:adstock_decay.title: Adstock Decay
ml-marketing-mix:adstock_decay.formula: Per channel, the fraction of an ad's effect that carries into each following day: effect on day t = decay rate to the power of t (geometric decay). Half-life is the day where effect falls to 50%.
ml-marketing-mix:adstock_decay.formula_explanation: Geometric decay — α is the fraction of yesterday's effect that carries into today.
how_to_read: Steeper decay = effect ends quickly (single-day impact).
Shallower decay = lingering brand-style influence over many days.
Half-life is the day where effect drops to 50%.
decisions_answered: Should this channel run as bursts or steady flighting?
How quickly does each channel's halo fade after spend stops?

ml-marketing-mix:model_fit.title: Model Fit — Actual vs. Predicted
ml-marketing-mix:model_fit.formula: Daily modelled revenue (baseline + each channel's response to its adstocked spend) overlaid on actual observed revenue, downsampled bi-weekly. Tighter overlap means more trustworthy channel attribution.
ml-marketing-mix:model_fit.formula_explanation: Daily modelled revenue overlaid on actual observed revenue.
how_to_read: Tighter overlap = more trustworthy channel-level attribution downstream.
Persistent one-sided gaps signal model bias.
Bi-weekly downsample is used for legibility.
decisions_answered: How much should we trust this model's channel-level outputs?
Are there time periods where the model breaks down?

formula_components.name: Predicted revenue
formula_components.formula: baseline + sum across channels of response(adstocked spend)

ml-category-affinity:category_distributi.title: Category Prediction Distribution
ml-category-affinity:category_distributi.formula: Share of scored customers whose top-1 predicted category is each category (bars sum to 100% of scored customers)
ml-category-affinity:category_distributi.formula_explanation: The model assigns each customer one top-1 predicted category. This chart shows how those predictions are distributed across the 10 product categories.
how_to_read: Longer bar = more customers are predicted to buy next from that category.
Bars sum to 100% of scored customers.
Tomato Seeds typically dominates because it has the most historical purchasers.
decisions_answered: Which categories should anchor email campaign targeting?
Which categories are under-represented and might need cold-start fallbacks?
How much personalisation reach does each category have?

ml-category-affinity:confidence_tier.title: Confidence Tier Breakdown
ml-category-affinity:confidence_tier.formula: Count and share of scored customers in each confidence tier (High / Medium / Low), based on prediction uncertainty
ml-category-affinity:confidence_tier.formula_explanation: High confidence = uncertainty below 40% (model is decisive). Medium = 40–60%. Low = uncertainty above 60% (model is unsure, so a 3-category shortlist is served instead of a single pick).
how_to_read: High share = most customers get a single, precise recommendation.
Low share = model uncertainty is high — these customers get a 3-category shortlist.
The 60% uncertainty threshold was calibrated to balance precision vs. coverage.
decisions_answered: How much of the customer base can be targeted with high-precision single-category emails?
How many customers need a broader content block instead of a focused recommendation?
Is the model confident enough to drive personalisation for this segment?

ml-category-affinity:entropy_distributio.title: Prediction Uncertainty Distribution
ml-category-affinity:entropy_distributio.formula: Histogram of each customer's normalized prediction uncertainty, where 0% = all probability on one category (confident) and 100% = probability spread evenly across all categories
ml-category-affinity:entropy_distributio.formula_explanation: Measures how spread the model's probability is across all 10 categories for each customer. 0% = all weight on one category (very confident). 100% = weight spread equally across all categories (random guess).
how_to_read: Tall bars near 0% = most customers have a clear category preference — the model is confident.
Flat or right-shifted distribution = widespread uncertainty across the customer base.
Color gradient: blue (confident) to red (uncertain).
decisions_answered: Is the model overall confidence level acceptable for production serving?
Should confidence thresholds be recalibrated?
Where is the bulk of uncertainty concentrated?

ml-category-affinity:feature_importance.title: Key Prediction Signals (Top 10)
ml-category-affinity:feature_importance.formula: Top 10 model features ranked by total information gain across all trees; longer bar = stronger influence on the category prediction
ml-category-affinity:feature_importance.formula_explanation: Measures how much each data signal contributes to reducing prediction error. Previous-category purchase signals dominate because what a customer bought last is the strongest indicator of what they'll buy next.
how_to_read: Longer bar = stronger influence on the recommendation.
Top signals are all previous-purchase category indicators.
Customers with no purchase history will have weaker signals — consider a fallback strategy for them.
decisions_answered: What signals drive category recommendations?
Are cold-start customers underserved by this feature set?
Which data sources are most critical to maintain?

ml-category-affinity:per_class_metrics.title: Category Prediction Quality
ml-category-affinity:per_class_metrics.formula: F1 score per category on held-out test data, where F1 = 2 x (Precision x Recall) ÷ (Precision + Recall)
ml-category-affinity:per_class_metrics.formula_explanation: Prediction quality score (F1) for each category on the held-out test data. Balances how often the model correctly identifies customers for a category against how many it misses.
how_to_read: Higher score = model predicts that category more reliably.
Low-scoring categories are conservative — the model misses some customers in that group.
Tomato Seeds leads partly because of its large training sample.
decisions_answered: Which categories are safe to drive with fully-automated personalisation?
Which categories need a manual review layer or fallback?
Should we add more training data for low-F1 categories?

ml-category-affinity:recommendation_stra.title: Recommendation Strategy Split
ml-category-affinity:recommendation_stra.formula: Share of customers served a single category (low uncertainty) versus a 3-category shortlist (high uncertainty), based on the uncertainty confidence gate
ml-category-affinity:recommendation_stra.formula_explanation: The serving policy uses the uncertainty score as a confidence gate. Low-uncertainty customers receive a single focused category. High-uncertainty customers receive three options to cover the spread.
how_to_read: Top-1 cohort (blue) = confident customers, ideal for single-category email banners.
Top-3 cohort (purple) = uncertain customers, better for multi-category carousels.
The split is tuned to balance email precision vs. coverage breadth.
decisions_answered: How should email creative be split between focused vs. multi-category layouts?
Is the current entropy threshold the right cut-off for production serving?
How many customers can receive the high-precision single-category experience?

ml-category-affinity:top_category_score.title: Avg Confidence Score by Category
ml-category-affinity:top_category_score.formula: Average top-1 model confidence score across customers predicted to belong to each category; higher = more decisive predictions for that category
ml-category-affinity:top_category_score.formula_explanation: For each customer predicted to belong to a category, the average confidence score the model assigned. Higher = more decisive predictions for that category.
how_to_read: Higher bar = model is more confident when predicting that category.
Low avg score (below 40%) means predictions for that category are marginal — validate before campaign activation.
Cross-check with Category Prediction Quality — high confidence and high quality both indicate reliable predictions.
decisions_answered: Which category predictions are confident enough for automated personalisation?
Which categories should be flagged for manual review?
Is average confidence consistent with the model Hit Rate@3?

ml-category-affinity:campaign_audience.title: Campaign Audience by Confidence
ml-category-affinity:campaign_audience.formula: For each top-1 predicted category, customers are split into High / Medium / Low confidence tiers, shown as a stacked bar of audience size per category
ml-category-affinity:campaign_audience.formula_explanation: For each category, the total targetable audience is broken down into High (send now), Medium (review first), and Low (hold) confidence groups. Color intensity shows how much of each category's audience is campaign-ready.
how_to_read: Green = high-confidence customers — safe to include in automated campaigns.
Amber = medium confidence — worth including but consider a manual check.
Grey = low confidence — the model is uncertain, skip or use a generic message.
Longer total bar = larger campaign audience for that category.
decisions_answered: Which category campaigns have the most high-quality audience to target?
How many customers are safe to include in an automated send?
Which campaigns need a review layer before activation?

ml-category-affinity:recommendation_dept.title: Campaign Reach — Primary vs Extended
ml-category-affinity:recommendation_dept.formula: Per category, primary reach = customers whose top-1 prediction is this category, plus extended reach = customers who have this category in their top-2 or top-3 shortlist but not as top-1
ml-category-affinity:recommendation_dept.formula_explanation: Shows the full potential reach of a category campaign. Primary audience = highest-priority targets. Extended audience = additional customers for whom this category appeared as a secondary option.
how_to_read: Blue = primary audience — the model's first pick for that category.
Purple = extended audience — secondary reach if you broaden the campaign.
Wider total bar = more overall reach for that category.
Use primary-only for tight, high-precision campaigns; add extended for broader coverage.
decisions_answered: What is the full potential audience if we broaden a category campaign?
How much incremental reach does including shortlist customers add?
Which categories have the deepest combined audience?

formula_components.name: Primary reach
formula_components.formula: Customers whose top-1 predicted category is this category

formula_components.name: Extended reach
formula_components.formula: Customers with this category in their top-2 or top-3 shortlist but not as top-1

ml-category-affinity:confidence_heatmap.title: Category × Confidence Heat Map
ml-category-affinity:confidence_heatmap.formula: Grid of customer counts for each category by confidence tier; darker cells = more customers in that category-tier combination
ml-category-affinity:confidence_heatmap.formula_explanation: A grid showing how many customers fall into each category-confidence combination. Darker cells = more customers. Reveals which categories have concentrated high-confidence audiences and which are spread across tiers.
how_to_read: Darker green in the High column = large, confident audience — ideal for automated campaigns.
Rows dominated by amber/grey = category predictions are uncertain overall.
Compare rows to identify which categories are most campaign-ready.
The percentage in each cell shows that tier's share of that category's total audience.
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most campaign-ready (high-confidence) audience?
Which categories are dominated by uncertain predictions and need a fallback approach?
How should we prioritise category campaigns this month?

ml-category-affinity:ready_to_activate.title: Campaign Readiness by Category
ml-category-affinity:ready_to_activate.formula: One row per category with its campaign readiness status (Ready if over 50% of its top-1 audience is high-confidence, Review if 25-50%, Hold if under 25%), plus extended shortlist reach
ml-category-affinity:ready_to_activate.formula_explanation: Summarises each category's campaign readiness in one table. Status is determined by the proportion of high-confidence predictions: Ready means the majority of the audience has a decisive model prediction.
how_to_read: Ready (green) = over half the audience has a high-confidence prediction — safe to activate.
Review (amber) = substantial audience but confidence is mixed — spot-check before sending.
Hold (grey) = most predictions are uncertain — wait for more data or use a generic fallback.
Extended reach = additional customers who have this category in their shortlist.
decisions_answered: Which category campaigns can we activate this week without manual review?
What is the combined primary + shortlist audience for each category?
Which categories should be held until the next model run?

ml-high-value-propensity:score_distribut.title: Propensity Score Distribution
ml-high-value-propensity:score_distribut.formula: Histogram of high-value propensity scores (0-1) across all scored customers, in 20 bins; bars show how many customers fall into each score range.
ml-high-value-propensity:score_distribut.formula_explanation: Histogram of propensity scores (0-1) across all scored customers.
how_to_read: Each bar is a score bin
Scores above 0.7 = high-propensity
Look at proportion above 0.7 for opportunity pool size
decisions_answered: How many customers have high propensity?
Where to set the activation threshold?
How confident is the model overall?

ml-high-value-propensity:tier_breakdown.title: Propensity Tier Breakdown
ml-high-value-propensity:tier_breakdown.formula: Customers split into high-value propensity tiers by score band (Very High > 0.85, High 0.70-0.85, Medium 0.50-0.70, Low < 0.50); donut sized by customer count per tier.
ml-high-value-propensity:tier_breakdown.formula_explanation: Donut: Very High (>0.85), High (0.70-0.85), Medium (0.50-0.70), Low (<0.50) tiers.
how_to_read: Each slice is one propensity tier
Larger Very High + High = larger immediate opportunity
Use to size campaigns per tier
decisions_answered: How large is the high-value prospect pool?
Which tier warrants the most campaign investment?

ml-high-value-propensity:revenue_concent.title: Revenue Concentration by Tier
ml-high-value-propensity:revenue_concent.formula: Customer count per high-value propensity tier (Very High / High / Medium / Low), ordered by tier; horizontal bars show how many customers sit in each propensity tier.
ml-high-value-propensity:revenue_concent.formula_explanation: Bar chart comparing current revenue contribution per tier vs predicted if high-propensity customers convert.
how_to_read: Each bar group is one tier
Height shows revenue
Compare current vs predicted to see opportunity gap
decisions_answered: Which tier has the most revenue upside?
How much incremental revenue is at stake?

ml-high-value-propensity:feature_importa.title: Top Predictive Features
ml-high-value-propensity:feature_importa.formula: Top 10 model features ranked by gain (LightGBM high-value model); horizontal bars show each feature's predictive contribution.
ml-high-value-propensity:feature_importa.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar of top 10 features ranked by gain.
how_to_read: Longer bars = more predictive power
Top features guide targeting criteria
decisions_answered: What behaviors predict high-value status?
Which data sources are most valuable?

ml-category-purchase-propensity:category.title: Top Categories by Audience Size
ml-category-purchase-propensity:category.formula: Customer count per top predicted next-purchase category (how many customers have each category as their highest-propensity category)
ml-category-purchase-propensity:category.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: how many customers have each category as their highest-propensity next purchase.
how_to_read: Longer bars = more customers ready to buy in that category
Top bars for highest-reach campaigns
decisions_answered: Which category has the largest ready-to-activate audience?
Where should category budgets go first?

ml-category-purchase-propensity:purchase.title: Purchase Likelihood Distribution
ml-category-purchase-propensity:purchase.formula: 20-bin histogram of each customer's highest category purchase probability (0 to 1)
ml-category-purchase-propensity:purchase.formula_explanation: Distribution of each customer's highest category purchase probability.
how_to_read: Right-skewed = most customers have low propensity
Left-skewed = most customers are ready to buy
decisions_answered: How confident is the model overall?
What threshold should trigger a campaign?

ml-category-purchase-propensity:feature_.title: Top Predictive Features
ml-category-purchase-propensity:feature_.formula: Top 10 model features predicting the next category purchase, ranked by gain
ml-category-purchase-propensity:feature_.formula_explanation: Top 10 features predicting next category purchase.
how_to_read: Longer bars = stronger predictors
Past category purchases dominate
decisions_answered: What drives next-category purchase behavior?

ml-category-purchase-propensity:category.title: Category Propensity Heatmap
ml-category-purchase-propensity:category.formula: Grid derived from the category distribution showing average purchase propensity across categories and confidence tiers; darker cells = higher average propensity
ml-category-purchase-propensity:category.formula_explanation: Heatmap: average purchase propensity across categories and confidence tiers.
how_to_read: Darker cells = higher average propensity
Each row is a confidence tier, column is category
decisions_answered: Which categories perform best in the High confidence tier?

ml-discount-response-propensity:response.title: Response Probability Distribution
ml-discount-response-propensity:response.formula: Histogram of the predicted discount-response probability across all scored customers, bucketed into 20 bins from 0 to 1.
ml-discount-response-propensity:response.formula_explanation: Distribution of discount response probability per customer.
how_to_read: Bimodal = clear responders vs non-responders
Zone above 0.6 = respond-only group
decisions_answered: What portion of customers need a discount to convert?
Where is the optimal discount response threshold?

ml-discount-response-propensity:revenue_.title: Revenue Risk from Over-Discounting
ml-discount-response-propensity:revenue_.formula: Customers split into Protected (would buy without a discount, margin-safe) vs At-Risk (unnecessarily discounted) (donut).
ml-discount-response-propensity:revenue_.formula_explanation: Donut: Protected (buy without discount) vs At-Risk (unnecessarily discounted) customers.
how_to_read: Protected slice = margin-safe customers
At-Risk = unnecessary discount recipients
decisions_answered: How much margin is lost to unnecessary discounts?
What % of customers are discount-insensitive?

ml-discount-response-propensity:tier_seg.title: Discount Tier Segmentation
ml-discount-response-propensity:tier_seg.formula: Customer count per discount-responder tier — High, Medium, Low.
ml-discount-response-propensity:tier_seg.formula_explanation: Bar chart of discount responder tiers.
how_to_read: High Responders need discount to convert
Low Responders buy without incentives
decisions_answered: How should we split campaign audience for discount offers?

ml-discount-response-propensity:feature_.title: Top Predictive Features
ml-discount-response-propensity:feature_.formula: Top features predicting discount response, ranked by model gain.
how_to_read: Price sensitivity signals dominate
RFM features show context
decisions_answered: What signals indicate a discount-sensitive customer?

ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_distribution.title: Customer Stage Distribution
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_distribution.formula: Customer count per predicted lifecycle stage (Active / Recent / At-Risk / Lapsed / Churned); donut sized by customers in each stage.
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_distribution.formula_explanation: Donut: New, Growing, Mature, At-Risk, Lapsed, Reactivated stages.
how_to_read: Each slice is a lifecycle stage
At-Risk + Lapsed = total churn risk
decisions_answered: How is our customer base distributed across stages?
How large is our at-risk population?

ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_revenue.title: Revenue by Lifecycle Stage
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_revenue.formula: Total revenue attributed to each predicted lifecycle stage; horizontal bars show revenue per stage.
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_revenue.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: total revenue per lifecycle stage.
how_to_read: Mature stage typically dominates
At-Risk revenue signals near-term churn risk
decisions_answered: Which stages drive the most revenue?
How much revenue is at risk?

ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_migration_flow.title: Stage Migration Flow
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_migration_flow.formula: Net customer transitions between lifecycle stages over the last 30 days (current stage to predicted stage); bars show how many customers entered or left each stage.
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_migration_flow.formula_explanation: Bar: net stage transitions in the past month.
how_to_read: Positive bars = customers entering stage
Large negative At-Risk = successful interventions
decisions_answered: Are customers moving toward or away from At-Risk?

ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_cohort_trend.title: Stage Cohort Trend
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_cohort_trend.formula: Stacked area showing the mix of customers across predicted lifecycle stages over the trailing months (per-cutoff stage distribution); each band is one stage's share over time.
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_cohort_trend.formula_explanation: Stacked area: stage mix evolution over time.
how_to_read: Expanding At-Risk + Lapsed = early churn warning
Growing Mature = healthy base
decisions_answered: Is our customer base maturing or deteriorating?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:trajectory_d.title: Trajectory Distribution
ml-segment-value-trajectory:trajectory_d.formula: Share of customers in each predicted value trajectory class — Ascending, Stable or Declining (donut), collapsed from the model's 5-class trajectory output.
ml-segment-value-trajectory:trajectory_d.formula_explanation: Donut: customer split by 180d value trajectory.
how_to_read: Ascending = growing value, nurture
Declining = revenue at risk, intervene
decisions_answered: What % are growing vs declining?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:value_change.title: Predicted Value Change by Segment
ml-segment-value-trajectory:value_change.formula: Average model confidence (predicted-class probability) per trajectory bucket (Ascending, Stable, Declining). Despite the title, this is prediction certainty, not a dollar value change — the model emits no value-change column.
ml-segment-value-trajectory:value_change.formula_explanation: Bar: average predicted $ change per trajectory segment.
how_to_read: Ascending bar = avg growth per customer
Declining bar = avg loss if no action
decisions_answered: What revenue is at stake from declining customers?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:category_bre.title: Ascending vs Declining by Category
ml-segment-value-trajectory:category_bre.formula: For each current value segment (Whale, VIP, High, Medium, Low): the number of customers predicted Ascending, Stable and Declining (grouped bar). The 'category' axis holds the value segment, not a product category.
ml-segment-value-trajectory:category_bre.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: ascending vs declining customers per category.
how_to_read: Categories with many Declining = category-level problem
Many Ascending = growth opportunity
decisions_answered: Which categories drive customer value decline?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:feature_impo.title: Top Predictive Features
ml-segment-value-trajectory:feature_impo.formula: Top 10 features that most influence the predicted value trajectory, ranked by model gain.
how_to_read: Order-level trends dominate
Category mix shifts signal trajectory change
decisions_answered: What early signals indicate a customer is declining?

ml-product-affinity:product_distribution.title: Top Products by Audience Size
ml-product-affinity:product_distribution.formula: Top 15 products by the number of customers for whom each is the top recommended product
ml-product-affinity:product_distribution.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar of top 15 products by affinity audience size.
how_to_read: Longer bars = more customers ready to buy that product
Top products are highest-reach cross-sell targets
decisions_answered: Which products have the largest recommendation audience?
Where should product-specific campaigns focus?

ml-product-affinity:confidence_tier.title: Confidence Tier Breakdown
ml-product-affinity:confidence_tier.formula: Count and share of customers in each recommendation confidence tier (High / Medium / Low)
how_to_read: High confidence = model is certain
Focus campaigns on High confidence for highest lift
decisions_answered: How reliable are our recommendations overall?
What % can we target with high-confidence recs?

ml-product-affinity:category_product_hea.title: Category x Product Heatmap
ml-product-affinity:category_product_hea.formula: Grid of model recommendation confidence for each category-product combination; darker cells = stronger cross-sell recommendation
ml-product-affinity:category_product_hea.formula_explanation: Heatmap of model confidence for category-product combinations.
how_to_read: Dark cells = strong recommendation confidence
Use to find cross-category opportunities
decisions_answered: Which category-product combos produce strongest recommendations?

ml-product-affinity:feature_importance.title: Top Predictive Features
ml-product-affinity:feature_importance.formula: Top 10 model features driving product recommendations, ranked by gain; longer bar = stronger predictor
how_to_read: Past purchase history dominates
Category browsing signals add lift
decisions_answered: What customer signals best predict next product purchase?

ml-price-affinity:tier_distribution.title: Price Tier Distribution
ml-price-affinity:tier_distribution.formula: Count and share of customers in each price affinity tier (Premium / Value / Budget)
ml-price-affinity:tier_distribution.formula_explanation: Donut: customer distribution across price affinity tiers.
how_to_read: Premium = full-price buyers
Budget = needs discount to convert
decisions_answered: How price-sensitive is our customer base?
Should pricing strategy shift based on tier mix?

ml-price-affinity:wtp_distribution.title: WTP Score Distribution
ml-price-affinity:wtp_distribution.formula: 20-bin histogram of customer willingness-to-pay scores (0 to 1)
ml-price-affinity:wtp_distribution.formula_explanation: Distribution of WTP scores across all customers.
how_to_read: Right-skewed = most customers are price-sensitive
Bimodal = clear Premium vs Budget segmentation
decisions_answered: What is the price sensitivity profile?
Where should we set pricing tiers?

ml-price-affinity:aov_by_tier.title: Average Order Value by Tier
ml-price-affinity:aov_by_tier.formula: Average order value of customers in each price affinity tier (Premium / Value / Budget)
ml-price-affinity:aov_by_tier.formula_explanation: Bar: average order value per price affinity tier.
how_to_read: Premium tier should have the highest AOV
Gaps show real revenue difference between tiers
decisions_answered: Do our price tiers reflect purchase behavior?
Can we nudge Value-tier customers toward Premium AOV?

ml-price-affinity:feature_importance.title: Top Predictive Features
ml-price-affinity:feature_importance.formula: Top model features predicting price tier classification, ranked by gain
ml-price-affinity:feature_importance.formula_explanation: Top features predicting price tier classification.
how_to_read: Historical discount usage is top predictor
Full-price purchase rate confirms tier
decisions_answered: What signals reliably predict price sensitivity?

ml-brand-affinity:brand_distribution.title: Brand Preference Distribution
ml-brand-affinity:brand_distribution.formula: Top 20 brands by the number of customers whose top brand by affinity is each brand
ml-brand-affinity:brand_distribution.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: customers per brand by affinity.
how_to_read: Longer bars = more loyal customers to that brand
Identify partnership and campaign anchor brands
decisions_answered: Which brands have the most loyal cohorts?
Are there niche brands with surprisingly loyal followings?

ml-brand-affinity:loyalty_index.title: Brand Loyalty Index
ml-brand-affinity:loyalty_index.formula: Count and share of customers in each brand-loyalty type (Loyalist / Explorer / Switcher)
ml-brand-affinity:loyalty_index.formula_explanation: Donut of brand loyalty type distribution.
how_to_read: Loyalists = strong single-brand preference
Switchers = at risk of competitor acquisition
decisions_answered: How loyal is our customer base?
What % are at risk of switching?

ml-brand-affinity:cross_brand_matrix.title: Cross-Brand Affinity Matrix
ml-brand-affinity:cross_brand_matrix.formula: Grid of co-affinity scores for brand pairs; darker cells = customers who favor brand A also tend to favor brand B
ml-brand-affinity:cross_brand_matrix.formula_explanation: Heatmap of how often customers who like Brand A also like Brand B.
how_to_read: Dark cells = strong cross-brand affinity
High off-diagonal = bundling opportunities
decisions_answered: Which brands naturally co-occur in preferences?
Are there brand pairings we haven't exploited?

ml-brand-affinity:brand_by_tier.title: Brand Affinity by Value Tier
ml-brand-affinity:brand_by_tier.formula: Stacked bar of customer counts per top brand broken down by data-quality tier
ml-brand-affinity:brand_by_tier.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: brand preference by customer value tier.
how_to_read: High-value customers' preferences guide premium positioning
Tier-specific brand strategies outperform one-size-fits-all
decisions_answered: Which brands resonate most with high-value customers?
Do brand preferences differ across value tiers?

ml-channel-affinity:channel_distribution.title: Channel Preference Distribution
ml-channel-affinity:channel_distribution.formula: Count and share of customers per preferred marketing channel (Email / SMS / Social / Search / Direct)
ml-channel-affinity:channel_distribution.formula_explanation: Bar: customer distribution across preferred channels.
how_to_read: Taller bars = more customers prefer that channel
Single dominant channel = concentration risk
decisions_answered: Which channel does most of our customer base prefer?
How should we allocate campaign budget?

ml-channel-affinity:channel_by_tier.title: Channel Affinity by Value Tier
ml-channel-affinity:channel_by_tier.formula: Stacked bar of customer counts per preferred channel broken down by data-quality tier
ml-channel-affinity:channel_by_tier.formula_explanation: Stacked bar: channel preferences by customer value tier.
how_to_read: High-value tiers often prefer Email or Direct
Tier-specific channel allocation outperforms uniform spend
decisions_answered: Do high-value customers prefer different channels?

ml-channel-affinity:confidence_heatmap.title: Channel Confidence Heatmap
ml-channel-affinity:confidence_heatmap.formula: Grid of average channel-assignment confidence for each preferred channel by data-quality tier; darker cells = higher confidence
ml-channel-affinity:confidence_heatmap.formula_explanation: Heatmap of channel assignment confidence per value tier.
how_to_read: Dark cells = high confidence channel-tier assignment
Focus personalized targeting on high-confidence cells
decisions_answered: Where are channel assignments most reliable?

ml-channel-affinity:feature_importance.title: Top Predictive Features
ml-channel-affinity:feature_importance.formula: Top model features predicting preferred marketing channel, ranked by gain
ml-channel-affinity:feature_importance.formula_explanation: Top features predicting preferred marketing channel.
how_to_read: Email engagement history dominates
SMS click rates distinguish SMS-preferrers
decisions_answered: What signals best predict channel preference?

ml-message-affinity:message_distribution.title: Message Type Distribution
ml-message-affinity:message_distribution.formula: Count and share of customers per preferred message type (Promotional / Educational / Seasonal / Triggered)
how_to_read: Each slice is a message content preference
Balance content calendar across message types
decisions_answered: Which message type resonates with the most customers?
Is our content mix aligned with audience preferences?

ml-message-affinity:message_by_tier.title: Message Preference by Value Tier
ml-message-affinity:message_by_tier.formula: Stacked bar of customer counts per preferred message type broken down by data-quality tier
ml-message-affinity:message_by_tier.formula_explanation: Stacked bar: message type preference by customer value tier.
how_to_read: High-value customers often prefer Loyalty-Reward or Educational
Tier-specific content personalization lifts engagement
decisions_answered: Do high-value customers want different message styles?

ml-message-affinity:message_channel_heat.title: Message x Channel Effectiveness Heatmap
ml-message-affinity:message_channel_heat.formula: Grid of customer counts per preferred message archetype by data-quality tier (channel dimension is unpopulated, so tier is used)
ml-message-affinity:message_channel_heat.formula_explanation: Heatmap of which message types work best in which channels.
how_to_read: Dark cells = strong message type + channel pairing
Weak pairings explain poor campaign performance
decisions_answered: Which message type performs best in Email vs SMS?

ml-price-elasticity:elasticity_distribut.title: Elasticity Distribution
ml-price-elasticity:elasticity_distribut.formula: 15-bin histogram of product price elasticity coefficients from -3 to 0, where below -1 is elastic and -1 to 0 is inelastic
ml-price-elasticity:elasticity_distribut.formula_explanation: Distribution of elasticity values: < -1 = elastic, -1 to 0 = inelastic.
how_to_read: Bars left of -1 = elastic products
Bars near 0 = nearly inelastic
decisions_answered: What % of products are highly price-sensitive?
Are most products elastic or inelastic?

ml-price-elasticity:category_breakdown.title: Elasticity by Category
ml-price-elasticity:category_breakdown.formula: Average price elasticity coefficient per product category, sorted from most elastic to least
ml-price-elasticity:category_breakdown.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar of average price elasticity per product category.
how_to_read: Furthest left = most elastic
Nearest 0 = pricing power here
decisions_answered: Which categories are most price-sensitive?
Where do we have pricing power?

ml-price-elasticity:demand_curves.title: Price-Demand Curves
ml-price-elasticity:demand_curves.formula: Modelled demand response per category showing the predicted demand change for a given price change
ml-price-elasticity:demand_curves.formula_explanation: Bar: average demand elasticity response per category.
how_to_read: Each bar shows demand change per 1% price change
Larger negative = more elastic
decisions_answered: How does demand respond to price changes?
What price change maximizes revenue?

ml-price-elasticity:sensitivity_heatmap.title: Revenue Sensitivity Heatmap
ml-price-elasticity:sensitivity_heatmap.formula: Grid of predicted revenue impact for each product category across a range of price-change scenarios
ml-price-elasticity:sensitivity_heatmap.formula_explanation: Heatmap of revenue impact for different price scenarios.
how_to_read: Dark red = large revenue decline from price increase
Dark green = large gain from price decrease
decisions_answered: Which category-price combinations have the best revenue impact?

ml-discount-response:response_curves.title: Discount Response Curves
ml-discount-response:response_curves.formula: Line/bar chart of predicted response (conversion) rate at each discount depth, per segment. Steeply rising curves indicate high discount sensitivity; early flattening signals diminishing returns.
ml-discount-response:response_curves.formula_explanation: Line/bar showing conversion rate change as discount increases.
how_to_read: Curves rising steeply = high discount sensitivity
Flattening early = diminishing returns
decisions_answered: At what discount depth do response rates peak?
Is there a point of diminishing returns?

ml-discount-response:segment_breakdown.title: Response by Segment at Optimal Depth
ml-discount-response:segment_breakdown.formula: Bar chart of the predicted response rate per segment at each segment's optimal discount depth. Higher bars are segments that respond more strongly; a low optimal response means a segment is not worth discounting.
ml-discount-response:segment_breakdown.formula_explanation: Bar: response rate per segment at its optimal discount depth.
how_to_read: Higher bars = segments that respond more strongly
Low optimal response = not worth discounting
decisions_answered: Which segments respond most efficiently?
Are some segments not worth discounting?

ml-discount-response:revenue_impact.title: Revenue Impact by Discount Level
ml-discount-response:revenue_impact.formula: Grouped bars at each discount level showing revenue uplift (gross benefit) versus margin sacrifice (gross cost), with net = uplift revenue − discount cost. Maximise the net.
ml-discount-response:revenue_impact.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: revenue uplift vs margin sacrifice at each discount level.
how_to_read: Revenue uplift = gross benefit
Margin sacrifice = gross cost
Net = maximize this
decisions_answered: At what discount level is net revenue maximized?
Where do costs exceed benefits?

ml-discount-response:recommendations.title: Recommended Discount by Segment
ml-discount-response:recommendations.formula: Bar chart of the model-recommended discount depth (optimal discount %) per customer segment, balancing response rate against margin impact.
ml-discount-response:recommendations.formula_explanation: Bar: model-recommended discount depth per segment.
how_to_read: Each bar = model's optimal discount recommendation
Balance response rate vs margin impact
decisions_answered: What discount should we offer each segment?
Are current depths aligned with model recommendations?

ml-channel-response-curve:response_curve.title: Response Curves by Channel
ml-channel-response-curve:response_curve.formula: Per channel, grouped bars of modelled revenue at each spend level. Channels where revenue growth slows are approaching saturation; steeper curves carry higher marginal ROI.
ml-channel-response-curve:response_curve.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: revenue generated at different spend levels per channel.
how_to_read: Channels where revenue growth slows = approaching saturation
Steeper curves = higher marginal ROI
decisions_answered: Where is spend generating the highest return?
Which channels are approaching saturation?

ml-channel-response-curve:marginal_roi.title: Marginal ROI by Channel
ml-channel-response-curve:marginal_roi.formula: Per channel, grouped bars compare current vs optimal marginal ROI (additional revenue per additional dollar of spend). Bars above 1× still generate positive returns; the gap between current and optimal is the reallocation opportunity.
ml-channel-response-curve:marginal_roi.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: current vs optimal marginal ROI per channel.
how_to_read: Bars above 1x = still generating positive returns
Gap between current and optimal = reallocation opportunity
decisions_answered: Which channels have marginal ROI below 1x?
How much can ROI improve with optimal allocation?

ml-channel-response-curve:saturation_sum.title: Saturation Point Summary
ml-channel-response-curve:saturation_sum.formula: Per channel, horizontal bar of the percent of model-optimal budget currently allocated = current spend ÷ optimal spend × 100%. Bars over 100% are over-invested (saturated); bars under 80% are under-invested.
ml-channel-response-curve:saturation_sum.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: % of model-optimal budget each channel receives.
how_to_read: Bars >100% = over-invested (saturated)
Bars <80% = under-invested
decisions_answered: Which channels are over or under-invested?

ml-channel-response-curve:budget_allocat.title: Budget Allocation Recommendation
ml-channel-response-curve:budget_allocat.formula: Donut showing the model-recommended budget split across channels. Each slice = a channel's recommended budget ÷ total recommended budget. Compare to current spend to find the changes needed.
ml-channel-response-curve:budget_allocat.formula_explanation: Donut: model-recommended budget split across channels.
how_to_read: Each slice = channel's recommended budget share
Compare to current to find required changes
decisions_answered: What should our channel budget split look like?

ml-offer-type-response:conversion_by_typ.title: Conversion Rate by Offer Type
ml-offer-type-response:conversion_by_typ.formula: Bar chart of the predicted conversion rate (conversions ÷ sends) for each offer mechanic. Taller bars are higher-converting offer types.
ml-offer-type-response:conversion_by_typ.formula_explanation: Bar: conversion rate for each offer mechanic.
how_to_read: Taller bars = higher converting offer types
Rotate offer types to prevent fatigue
decisions_answered: Which offer mechanic converts best?
Should we retire any low-converting types?

ml-offer-type-response:segment_heatmap.title: Offer Preference by Segment
ml-offer-type-response:segment_heatmap.formula: Heatmap of predicted conversion rate for each customer segment × offer type pairing. Dark cells are strong segment-offer matches; use it to personalise offer type by segment.
ml-offer-type-response:segment_heatmap.formula_explanation: Heatmap: which offer types convert best for each segment.
how_to_read: Dark cells = strong segment-offer pairing
Use to personalize offer type by segment
decisions_answered: Which offer type should each segment receive?

ml-offer-type-response:offer_distributio.title: Offer Type Distribution in Campaigns
ml-offer-type-response:offer_distributio.formula: Donut of the current send-volume distribution across offer types = count of offers sent per type ÷ total offers sent. Compare to conversion-by-type to spot over-represented low-converting offers wasting volume.
ml-offer-type-response:offer_distributio.formula_explanation: Donut: current send volume distribution across offer types.
how_to_read: Compare to conversion_by_type
Over-represented low-converting = wasted volume
decisions_answered: Are high-converting offers sent at high enough volume?

ml-offer-type-response:revenue_by_type.title: Revenue per Offer Type
ml-offer-type-response:revenue_by_type.formula: Bar chart of total revenue attributable to each offer type. Taller bars mean more revenue from that offer type; compare against cost to assess profitability.
ml-offer-type-response:revenue_by_type.formula_explanation: Bar: total revenue attributable to each offer type.
how_to_read: Taller = more revenue from that offer type
Compare against cost to compute profitability
decisions_answered: Which offer type generates the most revenue?

ml-timing-response:hourly_response.title: Response Rate by Hour of Day
ml-timing-response:hourly_response.formula: Area/line chart of the predicted response rate across all 24 hours of the day = customers whose best send hour is that hour ÷ total scored customers. Peaks are the most receptive hours; troughs are hours to avoid.
ml-timing-response:hourly_response.formula_explanation: Area/line: customer response rate across 24 hours.
how_to_read: Peak hours = most receptive times
Troughs = hours to avoid
decisions_answered: When are customers most likely to engage?
Should we shift send times to align with peaks?

ml-timing-response:daily_response.title: Response Rate by Day of Week
ml-timing-response:daily_response.formula: Bar chart of the predicted response rate for each day of the week = customers whose best send day is that day ÷ total scored customers. Taller bars are higher-response days.
ml-timing-response:daily_response.formula_explanation: Bar: response rates for each day of the week.
how_to_read: Taller bars = higher response on that day
Weekday vs weekend patterns vary by segment
decisions_answered: Which days generate highest response?
Are we sending on optimal days?

ml-timing-response:seasonal_response.title: Seasonal Response Pattern
ml-timing-response:seasonal_response.formula: Area chart of the predicted response rate by month over 12 months = customers whose best send month is that month ÷ total scored customers. Peaks are high-engagement seasons; troughs are months to reduce frequency.
how_to_read: Peaks = high-engagement seasons
Troughs = months to reduce frequency
decisions_answered: When do customers engage most throughout the year?
Which months should we front-load budget?

ml-timing-response:timing_heatmap.title: Timing Heatmap (Hour x Day)
ml-timing-response:timing_heatmap.formula: Heatmap of the predicted response rate for each hour × day-of-week combination = customers whose best send slot is that hour and day ÷ total scored customers. Darkest cells are the optimal send slots.
ml-timing-response:timing_heatmap.formula_explanation: Heatmap of response rate for each hour-day combination.
how_to_read: Dark cells = optimal send time slots
Cluster targeting rules around darkest cells
decisions_answered: What are the 3-5 best send windows?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:product_fo.title: Forecasted Demand by Top Products
ml-product-demand-forecasting:product_fo.formula: Forecasted unit demand for the top 15 products at the selected horizon, shown as a horizontal bar chart; longer bars mean more units expected.
ml-product-demand-forecasting:product_fo.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: forecasted unit demand for top 15 products at selected horizon.
how_to_read: Longer bars = more units expected
Combine with inventory to identify reorder needs
decisions_answered: Which products need the most inventory?
Are any high-volume products at risk of stocking out?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:forecast_v.title: Forecast vs Actual
ml-product-demand-forecasting:forecast_v.formula: Model forecast versus actual unit sales over the last 90-day validation window, shown as grouped bars; bars close together mean an accurate model.
ml-product-demand-forecasting:forecast_v.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: model forecast vs actual sales in the last 90-day validation window.
how_to_read: Bars close together = accurate model
Systematic bias shows directional error
decisions_answered: How accurate was the model in the last 90 days?
Is it reliable enough for purchase orders?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:growth_dis.title: Growth Rate Distribution
ml-product-demand-forecasting:growth_dis.formula: A 20-bin histogram of predicted product growth rates across all forecasted products; right-skew means most products are flat with a few high-growth ones.
ml-product-demand-forecasting:growth_dis.formula_explanation: Distribution of growth rate predictions across all forecasted products.
how_to_read: Right-skewed = most products flat, few high-growth
Focus on right tail for proactive inventory action
decisions_answered: How many products are forecasted to grow vs decline?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:category_f.title: Category-Level Demand Forecast
ml-product-demand-forecasting:category_f.formula: Total forecasted units per category at the 30-day, 60-day and 90-day horizons, shown as grouped bars; comparing 30d to 90d reveals accelerating demand.
ml-product-demand-forecasting:category_f.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: total forecasted demand per category at three horizons.
how_to_read: Higher bars = more volume in that category
Compare 30d vs 90d to see accelerating demand
decisions_answered: Which categories will see the most demand?
Are there categories with accelerating trends?

ml-category-seasonality:seasonality_heat.title: Seasonality Index Heatmap
ml-category-seasonality:seasonality_heat.formula: Seasonality index per category and month, where index = that month's revenue ÷ the category's average monthly revenue; cells above 1 are above-average months.
ml-category-seasonality:seasonality_heat.formula_explanation: Heatmap: seasonality index per category x month (green=peak, red=trough).
how_to_read: Index > 1 = above-average month
Dark green cells = pre-season campaign windows
decisions_answered: Which category-month combos are strongest opportunities?
When should each category's marketing peak?

ml-category-seasonality:monthly_revenue.title: Revenue by Month
ml-category-seasonality:monthly_revenue.formula: Total revenue per month across all categories, plotted as an area chart over the last 12 months.
ml-category-seasonality:monthly_revenue.formula_explanation: Area: total monthly revenue for the last 12 months.
how_to_read: Peaks = high-revenue months
Troughs = budget defensively
decisions_answered: When does total revenue peak?
How should we structure the annual budget calendar?

ml-category-seasonality:yoy_comparison.title: YoY Category Revenue Comparison
ml-category-seasonality:yoy_comparison.formula: This year versus last year revenue per category, shown as grouped bars; the difference is each category's year-over-year change.
ml-category-seasonality:yoy_comparison.formula_explanation: Grouped bar: this year vs last year revenue per category.
how_to_read: Taller current_year = growth
Taller prior_year = decline
decisions_answered: Which categories are growing vs shrinking?
Should budget shift toward growing categories?

ml-category-seasonality:peak_trough.title: Peak vs Trough by Category
ml-category-seasonality:peak_trough.formula: Peak and trough seasonality index for each category, shown as paired bars; a wide gap means the category is highly seasonal.
ml-category-seasonality:peak_trough.formula_explanation: Bar: peak and trough index per category.
how_to_read: Wide gap = highly seasonal
Narrow gap = consistently stable demand
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most extreme seasonality?
How much safety stock is needed for seasonal peaks?

ml-stock-out-risk:risk_distribution.title: Risk Level Distribution
ml-stock-out-risk:risk_distribution.formula: Count of products in each risk level (Critical / High / Medium / Low), shown as a donut with each slice's share of all products.
how_to_read: Critical + High = products needing immediate action
Low = safe for now
decisions_answered: How many products are at critical/high risk?
What % of inventory needs immediate reordering?

ml-stock-out-risk:revenue_at_risk.title: Revenue at Risk by Category
ml-stock-out-risk:revenue_at_risk.formula: Total revenue at stake from stock-outs, summed per category for high-risk products and shown as horizontal bars; longer bars carry more revenue at risk.
ml-stock-out-risk:revenue_at_risk.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: total revenue at stake from stock-outs per category.
how_to_read: Longer bars = more revenue at risk
Prioritize reorders in highest-revenue-at-risk categories
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most revenue at stake?
Where should reorder budget go first?

ml-stock-out-risk:days_until_risk.title: Days Until Risk by Top Products
ml-stock-out-risk:days_until_risk.formula: Days until each product hits projected stock-out, for the top 15 highest-risk products sorted soonest-first; bars near zero need an immediate reorder.
ml-stock-out-risk:days_until_risk.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: top 15 high-risk products sorted by days until risk (ascending).
how_to_read: Bars near zero = critical, reorder immediately
Color shows urgency level
decisions_answered: Which products are closest to stocking out?
Which need reorders placed today vs this week?

ml-stock-out-risk:risk_heatmap.title: Risk Heatmap (Category x Risk Level)
ml-stock-out-risk:risk_heatmap.formula: Number of products in each combination of category and risk level, shown as a heatmap; dark cells flag many at-risk products in a category.
ml-stock-out-risk:risk_heatmap.formula_explanation: Heatmap: risk distribution across categories and risk levels.
how_to_read: Dark cells = many products at that risk level in category
All-dark high-risk row = category-wide problem
decisions_answered: Which categories have systemic stock-out risk?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:urgency_distr.title: Markdown Urgency Distribution
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:urgency_distr.formula: Count of products in each markdown-urgency bucket (Now / Soon / Hold), shown as a donut with each bucket's share of all products.
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:urgency_distr.formula_explanation: Donut: product split by markdown urgency.
how_to_read: Now = markdown immediately
Hold = full price sustainable for now
decisions_answered: How many products need immediate markdown?
Are we sitting on inventory that should have been marked down?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:revenue_recov.title: Projected Revenue Recovery by Category
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:revenue_recov.formula: Projected margin recovery available through optimal markdowns, summed per category for products flagged to mark down now and shown as horizontal bars.
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:revenue_recov.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar: margin recovery available through optimal markdowns per category.
how_to_read: Longer bars = more recovery available
Compare against markdown cost to assess ROI
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most margin recovery available?
Where should merchandising focus first?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:markdown_dist.title: Optimal Markdown % Distribution
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:markdown_dist.formula: Distribution of the model's recommended markdown depth across products, bucketed into bins from 5% to 50%; a right tail at 30%+ signals severely slow-moving inventory.
how_to_read: Most products at 5-15% = conservative markdowns
Right tail at 30%+ = severely slow-moving inventory
decisions_answered: What is the typical recommended markdown depth?
How much total discount budget is needed?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:timing_scatte.title: Timing vs Margin Trade-off
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:timing_scatte.formula: Each product plotted by its markdown urgency score against its recommended markdown depth; upper-right means urgent and needing a deep discount.
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:timing_scatte.formula_explanation: Chart: urgency score vs markdown depth for all products.
how_to_read: Upper-right = urgent AND deep discount needed
Lower-left = low urgency AND shallow discount
decisions_answered: Are there products needing urgent AND deep discounts?
Which products offer best margin recovery for least depth?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:total_spend.title: Total Media Spend
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:total_spend.formula: Total Media Spend = sum of paid media spend across all channels (Google Ads + Meta Ads + Email) in the attribution period
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:total_spend.formula_explanation: Total paid media spend summed across all channels (Google Ads, Meta Ads, Email) in the attribution period; read directly from the MMM summary artifact.
how_to_read: Single value = all paid spend in the analyzed window.
It is the denominator of Blended ROAS below.
Covers paid channels only; organic spend is excluded.
decisions_answered: How much are we spending across all paid channels?
What is the spend base the model is attributing revenue against?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:total_incremen.title: Total Incremental Revenue
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:total_incremen.formula: Total Incremental Revenue = sum of incremental revenue the Bayesian MMM credits to paid channels (in-sample attribution, baseline/organic excluded)
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:total_incremen.formula_explanation: Total incremental revenue attributed to paid channels by the Bayesian MMM (in-sample attribution, not a forecast); summed from the channel-level decomposition.
how_to_read: Single value = paid-driven revenue the model credits to media.
It is the numerator of Blended ROAS below.
Incremental, not gross: baseline/organic revenue is excluded.
decisions_answered: How much revenue did paid media actually drive?
What is the model crediting to advertising vs baseline?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:blended_roas.title: Blended ROAS
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:blended_roas.formula: Blended ROAS = total incremental revenue ÷ total media spend across all channels (0 when spend = 0)
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:blended_roas.formula_explanation: Blended return on ad spend: total incremental revenue divided by total paid spend across all channels. Returns 0 when spend is 0.
how_to_read: Shown as a multiple, e.g. 2.40x revenue per dollar spent.
Above 1.0x means paid media is revenue-positive on an incremental basis.
Blended across channels; per-channel ROAS varies widely around this.
decisions_answered: Is our overall paid media profitable on an incremental basis?
How efficient is the total media program right now?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:reallocation_u.title: Reallocation Upside
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:reallocation_u.formula: Reallocation Upside = (optimizer's expected daily response ÷ current daily response − 1) × 100 — extra revenue from shifting the same total budget toward higher-marginal-return channels
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:reallocation_u.formula_explanation: Extra revenue available from shifting the same total budget toward higher-marginal-return channels, with no added spend; the optimizer's expected daily response over the current daily response, expressed as a percent gain.
how_to_read: Shown as a positive percentage, e.g. +12% more revenue.
Same total budget assumed; only the channel mix changes.
Higher = the current allocation is further from optimal.
Drawn from the budget-optimization artifact, not realized results.
decisions_answered: How much more revenue can we get by re-mixing the existing budget?
Is it worth re-allocating spend before asking for more budget?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:saturation_cur.title: Saturation / Response Curves
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:saturation_cur.formula: Per channel, response curves mapping spend to modelled incremental response, with each channel's current saturation percent marking how far up the diminishing-returns curve today's spend sits. Flattening shows diminishing returns.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:saturation_cur.formula_explanation: Per-channel response curves mapping spend to modeled incremental response (response_mean) from the saturation analysis artifact, with each channel's current saturation_pct marking how far up the diminishing-returns curve today's spend sits.
how_to_read: One curve per channel; x = spend, y = modeled response.
Flattening of the curve shows diminishing returns.
A high saturation percent means added spend buys little extra response.
Compare channels to see where headroom remains.
decisions_answered: Which channels still have room to scale profitably?
Where is incremental spend hitting diminishing returns?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:campaign_perfo.title: Campaign Performance Map
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:campaign_perfo.formula: Bubble map of the top 40 campaigns by spend: horizontal = spend, vertical = ROAS (attributed revenue ÷ spend), bubble area = incremental revenue. A breakeven line sits at ROAS = 1.0; bubbles below it lose money incrementally.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:campaign_perfo.formula_explanation: Bubble map of the top 40 campaigns by spend from the channel/campaign drill-down: horizontal position is spend, vertical position is ROAS, and bubble area scales with incremental revenue. The y=1.0 gridline marks breakeven.
how_to_read: Each bubble is a campaign; x = spend, y = ROAS, size = incremental revenue.
Bubbles below the ROAS=1.0 line are losing money incrementally.
Big bubbles high and to the right are the efficient revenue drivers.
Only the top 40 campaigns by spend are plotted.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are efficient revenue drivers vs money-losers?
Where is large spend producing weak ROAS?

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:quarter_total_.title: Forecast Quarter Total
