ml-marketing-mix-forecast:quarter_total_.formula: Forecast Quarter Total = sum of point-forecast weekly revenue across the next 13 weeks
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:quarter_total_.formula_explanation: Point-forecast sum of weekly revenue across the next 13 weeks, read from the forecast summary artifact (sum of forecast_revenue over the horizon).
how_to_read: Single value = expected total revenue for the coming quarter.
This is the central (point) estimate, not the range.
See Quarter Range below for the 80% prediction interval.
decisions_answered: How much revenue should we expect next quarter?
What total should we plan and resource against?

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:quarter_range.title: Quarter Range (80% PI)
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:quarter_range.formula: Quarter Range (80% PI) = [sum of weekly lower bounds, sum of weekly upper bounds] of the 80% prediction interval across the 13 horizon weeks
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:quarter_range.formula_explanation: 80% prediction interval for total quarterly revenue: the summed lower and upper weekly bounds (pi80_low / pi80_high) from the forecast. Actual revenue should fall inside this range about 8 times in 10.
how_to_read: Shown as a low-to-high dollar range.
The point forecast sits inside this band.
A wider band means more uncertainty about the quarter.
decisions_answered: What is the plausible range of next-quarter revenue?
How conservative or aggressive should the plan be?

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:growth_vs_prio.title: Growth vs Prior Year
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:growth_vs_prio.formula: Growth vs Prior Year = (forecasted quarter total ÷ actual revenue of the matching weeks 52 weeks earlier − 1) × 100 (blank when fewer than 52 weeks of history exist)
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:growth_vs_prio.formula_explanation: Forecasted quarter total compared against the actual revenue of the matching weeks one year earlier (the history series shifted back 52 weeks), expressed as a percent change. Requires at least 52 weeks of history.
how_to_read: Positive = forecast exceeds the same period last year.
Aligns forecast weeks to the same calendar weeks 52 weeks back.
Shows blank when fewer than 52 weeks of history exist.
decisions_answered: Are we forecast to grow or shrink versus last year?
Is the projected trajectory ahead of or behind prior-year pace?

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:peak_week.title: Peak Week
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:peak_week.formula: Peak Week = the largest single-week forecast revenue across the 13-week horizon, with the week it falls in
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:peak_week.formula_explanation: Highest single-week point forecast across the 13-week horizon, taken from the weekly forecast series along with the week it falls in.
how_to_read: Value = the largest expected weekly revenue in the horizon.
The label names the week of the peak.
Useful for spotting seasonality spikes within the quarter.
decisions_answered: Which week should we expect peak demand?
When should inventory and staffing be highest?

ml-order-value:total_customers_scored.title: Customers Profiled
ml-order-value:total_customers_scored.formula: Customers Profiled = count of customers with a predicted next-order value in this scoring run
ml-order-value:total_customers_scored.formula_explanation: How many customers the order-value model scored this run. Read straight from the model's dashboard summary artifact; falls back to the row count of the scored-customers table if the summary omits it.
how_to_read: A single count of every customer the model could score.
Bigger pools give more reliable segment-level rollups (premium, whale, high-confidence).
If this drops sharply between runs, the upstream serving table likely shrank — investigate before trusting the other KPIs.
decisions_answered: How large is the addressable base the model is reasoning over?
Did this scoring run cover the customer population I expect?

ml-order-value:total_expected_revenue.title: Forecast Revenue
ml-order-value:total_expected_revenue.formula: Forecast Revenue = sum of predicted next-cycle order value across all scored customers
ml-order-value:total_expected_revenue.formula_explanation: Total dollars the model expects from every scored customer's next order combined. Read from the dashboard summary's total_expected_next_cycle_revenue field.
how_to_read: The aggregate predicted order-value opportunity for the next purchase cycle.
Use it as a top-of-funnel revenue target, not a guaranteed booking.
Pair with the AOV and confidence KPIs — a big number built on low-confidence predictions is softer than it looks.
decisions_answered: What is the total predicted next-cycle order-value opportunity?
How big a revenue target can I set against this scored base?

ml-order-value:avg_predicted_aov.title: Expected Avg Order
ml-order-value:avg_predicted_aov.formula: Expected Avg Order = mean predicted next order value across all scored customers
ml-order-value:avg_predicted_aov.formula_explanation: The average order value the model predicts across all scored customers, taken from the summary's avg_predicted_aov field.
how_to_read: The typical predicted order size, in dollars.
Compare against historical AOV to see if the model expects spending to rise or fall.
It is an average — the AOV distribution chart shows how skewed the underlying spread is.
decisions_answered: What order size should I plan offers and merchandising around?
Is the model predicting AOV growth or contraction overall?

ml-order-value:aov_uplift.title: Predicted Growth vs Past
ml-order-value:aov_uplift.formula: Predicted Growth vs Past = (predicted average order value − historical average order value) ÷ historical average order value × 100; blank when no historical baseline exists
ml-order-value:aov_uplift.formula_explanation: How much higher (or lower) the model's predicted order value is versus each customer's own historical average, expressed as a percent. Shown only when historical data is available; null otherwise.
how_to_read: Positive (+) means the model expects orders to grow above the historical baseline; negative means contraction.
It is a relative percent, not a dollar figure — read it alongside Expected Avg Order.
Displays a dash when there is no historical baseline to compare against.
decisions_answered: Is order value trending up or down versus history?
Should I lean into upsell, or defend against a predicted decline?

ml-order-value:premium_count.title: High-Value Targets
ml-order-value:premium_count.formula: High-Value Targets = count of customers the model places in the Premium value tier
ml-order-value:premium_count.formula_explanation: Number of customers the model places in the Premium value tier, read from the summary's tier_breakdown map.
how_to_read: The size of the premium-spending pool worth prioritising for richer offers.
These customers anchor the upper end of the value-tier distribution chart.
A growing count signals more headroom for premium merchandising and VIP treatment.
decisions_answered: How many customers qualify for premium-tier targeting?
Is the premium pool large enough to justify a dedicated campaign?

ml-order-value:whale_target_count.title: Top Spenders
ml-order-value:whale_target_count.formula: Top Spenders = count of customers flagged as whale targets — predicted high-value orders among the very top spenders
ml-order-value:whale_target_count.formula_explanation: Number of customers flagged as whale targets — those whose predicted order value places them among the very top spenders. Read from the summary's high_value_predicted_orders_count field.
how_to_read: The narrow pool of highest-predicted-value customers (whales).
Concentrate VIP outreach and premium offers here — these few customers drive a disproportionate share of revenue.
Use with the model's whale-AUC metric to judge how reliably the model identifies them.
decisions_answered: Who are the few customers most worth VIP-level investment?
How big is the whale segment for a high-touch program?

ml-order-value:high_confidence_count.title: Reliable Predictions
ml-order-value:high_confidence_count.formula: Reliable Predictions = count of customers whose prediction confidence-interval width falls in the narrowest (≤25th percentile) quartile
ml-order-value:high_confidence_count.formula_explanation: How many predictions are high-confidence. Read from the summary's high_confidence_customer_count; if absent, computed on the fly as the count of customers whose prediction confidence-interval width is at or below the 25th percentile (the tightest, most certain quartile).
how_to_read: More high-confidence predictions = more of the base you can act on without hedging.
Confidence is defined by how tight each prediction's interval is — narrower means more certain.
Low-confidence predictions are not wrong, just wider — treat their dollar figures as ranges.
decisions_answered: How much of the scored base can I act on with confidence?
Which predictions are tight enough to drive automated decisions?

ml-order-value:median_uncertainty.title: Typical Prediction Range
ml-order-value:median_uncertainty.formula: Typical Prediction Range = median width of the prediction confidence interval across scored customers (in dollars)
ml-order-value:median_uncertainty.formula_explanation: The median width of the prediction confidence interval, in dollars — the typical +/- band around a predicted order value. Read from the summary; falls back to the median of confidence_interval_width over scored customers.
how_to_read: A dollar figure: half of predictions have an interval narrower than this, half wider.
Smaller is better — it means the model's order-value estimates are typically tighter.
Use it to set realistic expectations: individual predictions can swing by roughly this much.
decisions_answered: How precise are the order-value predictions on a typical customer?
What error band should I assume when budgeting off these forecasts?

ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_rate.title: Growth Rate
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_rate.formula: Growth Rate = flagged growth customers ÷ total scored customers × 100
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_rate.formula_explanation: The share of scored customers the model flags as likely to grow their wallet share / spend. Computed as flagged growth customers divided by the total scored, expressed as a percent.
how_to_read: A percent: what fraction of the base is a genuine expansion opportunity.
Growth events are rare, so this is typically a minority of the base.
A rising rate means more customers worth an expansion play; falling means tighten targeting.
decisions_answered: What share of my customers are realistic expansion candidates?
How big is the overall growth opportunity right now?

ml-wallet-share-growth:avg_grow_probabil.title: Avg Growth Probability
ml-wallet-share-growth:avg_grow_probabil.formula: Avg Growth Probability = average model-predicted probability of growing wallet share across all scored customers, shown as a percent
ml-wallet-share-growth:avg_grow_probabil.formula_explanation: The average model-predicted probability of growing wallet share across all scored customers, read from the summary's mean_prob_grow field.
how_to_read: The baseline grow-probability of a typical customer, shown as a percent.
Compare individual customers and segments against this average to spot over-performers.
The grow-probability distribution chart shows how the population spreads around this mean.
decisions_answered: What is the typical likelihood a customer will grow their spend?
Which segments sit above the average and deserve expansion focus?

ml-wallet-share-growth:untapped_low_spen.title: Untapped Low-Spenders
ml-wallet-share-growth:untapped_low_spen.formula: Untapped Low-Spenders = count of growth-flagged customers in the lowest historical AOV (spend) tier
ml-wallet-share-growth:untapped_low_spen.formula_explanation: Count of customers in the lowest historical spend tier who are flagged as likely to grow — the under-monetised pool with the most upside. Read from the flagged_count of the smallest AOV-tier bucket in the model summary.
how_to_read: The number of low-spending customers the model thinks can grow.
This is your highest-leverage expansion pool: low current value, high growth signal.
Cross-reference with the Growth-by-Spend-Tier chart to see where these sit.
decisions_answered: Which low-spenders are worth investing in to grow?
How large is the untapped expansion opportunity at the bottom of the value pyramid?

ml-wallet-share-growth:top_growth_driver.title: Top Growth Driver
ml-wallet-share-growth:top_growth_driver.formula: Top Growth Driver = the model feature with the highest gain in the grow-probability model (top-ranked feature-importance entry, humanized for display)
ml-wallet-share-growth:top_growth_driver.formula_explanation: The single feature that most influences the grow-probability model, taken as the top-ranked entry by gain in the feature-importance artifact and formatted for display.
how_to_read: Names the strongest signal the model uses to predict growth.
Treat it as influence, not causation — it is the most predictive feature, not a guaranteed lever.
The What-Drives-Growth chart shows the full ranking behind this single driver.
decisions_answered: What customer behaviour most signals expansion potential?
Where should I look first when designing a growth play?

ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_segments.title: Growth Segments
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_segments.formula: Customers bucketed by growth segment (High Growth / Likely Growth / Stable / Declining) with each bucket's customer count, share of total, and average grow probability.
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_segments.formula_explanation: Customers bucketed by growth_segment (High_Growth, Likely_Growth, Stable, Declining) with their counts, share of total, and average grow probability per bucket.
how_to_read: Each bar is a growth segment; the size shows how many customers fall in it.
High_Growth and Likely_Growth are your action segments; Stable/Declining are hold or defend.
Average grow probability per segment confirms the buckets are ordered as expected.
decisions_answered: How is my base distributed across growth potential?
How many customers sit in the actionable high-growth segments?

formula_components.name: Average grow probability
formula_components.formula: Average grow probability of customers within the segment

ml-wallet-share-growth:grow_distribution.title: Grow-Probability Distribution
ml-wallet-share-growth:grow_distribution.formula: A 20-bin histogram of every scored customer's grow probability (0-100%); bar height is how many customers fall in each band, and the thin right tail is the concentrated high-growth pool.
ml-wallet-share-growth:grow_distribution.formula_explanation: A 20-bin histogram of every scored customer's grow probability, built directly over the pred_prob_grow column.
how_to_read: X-axis is grow probability (0–100%); bar height is how many customers fall in each band.
A right-skewed shape (mass near zero) is normal — growth is a rare event.
Watch the right tail: that thin band is your concentrated high-growth pool.
decisions_answered: How is grow probability spread across the customer base?
Where should I set a probability cutoff for targeting?

ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_by_spend.title: Growth Propensity by Spend Tier
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_by_spend.formula: Average grow probability broken out by historical spend (AOV) tier, with each tier's customer count and the count and share flagged as growth candidates; high growth probability in a low-spend tier is the strongest under-monetisation signal.
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_by_spend.formula_explanation: Average grow probability broken out by historical spend (AOV) tier, with each tier's customer count and the count/share flagged as growth candidates.
how_to_read: Each row/bar is a past-spend tier (low → high AOV).
Compare growth propensity across tiers to see where expansion potential concentrates.
High growth propensity in a low-spend tier is the strongest under-monetisation signal.
decisions_answered: Do low- or high-spenders have more room to grow?
Which spend tier should anchor my expansion strategy?

formula_components.name: Mean growth propensity per tier
formula_components.formula: Average grow probability of customers in each past-AOV tier

formula_components.name: Flagged share
formula_components.formula: Flagged growth candidates in tier ÷ customers in tier × 100

ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_drivers.title: What Drives Growth
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_drivers.formula: The top 15 features driving the grow-probability model, ranked by their gain (relative contribution to the model's predictions); gain is influence, not causation.
ml-wallet-share-growth:growth_drivers.formula_explanation: The top 15 features driving the grow-probability model, ranked by LightGBM gain (relative contribution to the model's predictions).
how_to_read: Each bar is a feature; length is its share of the model's predictive power (gain).
Higher bars are stronger signals the model relies on to flag growth.
Gain is influence, not causation — use it to understand the model, not as a direct lever.
decisions_answered: Which behaviours most predict a customer will grow?
What signals should inform how I design expansion campaigns?

ml-activation:total_prospects.title: Total Prospects
ml-activation:total_prospects.formula: Total Prospects = count of prospects (not-yet-first-purchase customers) scored by the activation model this run
ml-activation:total_prospects.formula_explanation: How many prospects (customers who have not yet made a first purchase) the activation model scored this run. Read from the summary's total_prospects field, falling back to the scored-customers row count.
how_to_read: A single count of the addressable prospect pool.
Larger pools make the segment and decile rollups more stable.
A sudden drop suggests the upstream prospect feed changed — verify before acting on downstream KPIs.
decisions_answered: How large is the prospect base the model is working with?
Did this run cover the full prospect population I expect?

ml-activation:avg_activation_propensity.title: Avg Activation Propensity
ml-activation:avg_activation_propensity.formula: Avg Activation Propensity = average predicted first-purchase (activation) propensity across all scored prospects
ml-activation:avg_activation_propensity.formula_explanation: The average predicted first-purchase (activation) propensity across all scored prospects, read from the summary's score_stats.mean, with a fallback to the mean of the activation_propensity column.
how_to_read: The baseline likelihood a typical prospect converts to a first purchase, as a percent.
Use it as the reference line to judge which prospects and segments over-index.
The propensity distribution chart shows how the pool spreads around this mean.
decisions_answered: What is the typical conversion likelihood across prospects?
Which segments sit above the average and warrant activation spend?

ml-activation:priority_count.title: High-Propensity Pool
ml-activation:priority_count.formula: High-Propensity Pool = count of prospects the model places in the top 'priority' activation tier (highest first-purchase propensity)
ml-activation:priority_count.formula_explanation: Number of prospects the model places in the top 'priority' activation tier — the most likely first-buyers. Read from the summary's by_tier breakdown, with a fallback count over the activation_segment column.
how_to_read: The size of the highest-propensity prospect pool worth activating first.
These prospects anchor the top of the segment-breakdown and decile charts.
Focus limited activation budget here for the best conversion-per-dollar.
decisions_answered: How many prospects are prime targets for an activation push?
Is the priority pool large enough to justify a dedicated campaign?

ml-activation:watch_count.title: Watch Pool
ml-activation:watch_count.formula: Watch Pool = count of prospects in the middle 'watch' activation tier (moderate first-purchase likelihood)
ml-activation:watch_count.formula_explanation: Number of prospects in the middle 'watch' activation tier — moderate first-purchase likelihood worth nurturing rather than pushing hard. Read from the summary's by_tier breakdown.
how_to_read: The mid-propensity pool to nurture with lighter-touch activation.
Sits between the priority pool and the long tail of low-propensity prospects.
Promote these into priority over time with nurture sequences before heavy spend.
decisions_answered: How many prospects belong in a nurture (not push) track?
Where is the mid-tier opportunity that could be moved up with light touch?

ml-activation:segments_breakdown.title: Segment Breakdown
ml-activation:segments_breakdown.formula: Prospects grouped by activation segment; each segment shows its prospect count, share of the total, and average activation propensity.
ml-activation:segments_breakdown.formula_explanation: Prospects grouped by activation segment with each segment's count, share of total, and average activation propensity.
how_to_read: Each bar is an activation segment (priority, watch, …); size = number of prospects.
Average propensity per segment confirms the tiers are ordered correctly.
Use the split to size each activation track before allocating budget.
decisions_answered: How is the prospect base distributed across activation tiers?
How should I split effort between priority, watch, and the long tail?

ml-activation:expected_first_buyers.title: Expected First-Buyers by Decile
ml-activation:expected_first_buyers.formula: Per propensity decile: expected first-time buyers = prospect count in the decile × that decile's average activation propensity (rounded).
ml-activation:expected_first_buyers.formula_explanation: Translates each propensity decile into a volume estimate by multiplying the decile's prospect count by its average activation propensity — i.e. how many first-time buyers that decile is expected to produce.
how_to_read: X-axis is propensity decile (D1 lowest → D10 highest); bar height is expected conversions.
The top decile has the highest per-prospect odds, but a larger lower decile can still contribute more total buyers.
Use it as a volume signal to balance precision (top deciles) against reach (broader deciles).
decisions_answered: Where will the actual first-purchase volume come from?
Should I target the highest-odds decile or go broader for total conversions?

ml-activation:propensity_distribution.title: Propensity Distribution by Decile
ml-activation:propensity_distribution.formula: Prospects ranked by activation propensity and split into ten equal deciles; each decile shows its prospect count and the average, minimum and maximum activation propensity.
ml-activation:propensity_distribution.formula_explanation: Prospects ranked by activation propensity and cut into ten equal-size deciles, each reporting its count and the average, minimum, and maximum propensity in that band.
how_to_read: D10 holds the highest-propensity prospects; D1 the lowest.
A steep gradient from D1 to D10 means the model separates prospects well — strong targeting signal.
Flat deciles mean weak separation; targeting by decile will add little lift.
decisions_answered: How sharply does the model rank prospects by conversion likelihood?
Which deciles are worth targeting and where do diminishing returns set in?

ml-activation:feature_importance.title: Feature Importance (Top 20)
ml-activation:feature_importance.formula: Top 20 model features ranked by LightGBM gain, each shown as a percent of the model's total gain.
ml-activation:feature_importance.formula_explanation: Top model features by LightGBM gain, normalised so each feature's contribution is shown as a percent of the model's total gain.
how_to_read: Each bar is a feature; length is its share of the model's total predictive power.
Higher-ranked features are the strongest signals the model uses to predict activation.
Gain reflects influence inside the model, not a guaranteed causal lever.
decisions_answered: What signals most predict a prospect's first purchase?
Which features should inform how I design activation campaigns?

ml-cart-recovery:abandoned_carts.title: Abandoned Carts
ml-cart-recovery:abandoned_carts.formula: Abandoned Carts = count of abandoned carts scored by the recovery model this run
ml-cart-recovery:abandoned_carts.formula_explanation: How many abandoned carts (quotes) the recovery model scored this run, read from the summary's n_scored field with a fallback to the scored-rows count.
how_to_read: A single count of the abandoned carts in scope for recovery.
This is the universe the recovery score, distribution, and pool KPIs are built on.
A small or volatile count limits how much you can read into the segment-level rollups.
decisions_answered: How many recoverable abandoned carts are there right now?
Is the cart volume large enough to run a recovery campaign against?

ml-cart-recovery:avg_recovery_score.title: Avg Recovery Score
ml-cart-recovery:avg_recovery_score.formula: Avg Recovery Score = average predicted recovery propensity across all scored abandoned carts
ml-cart-recovery:avg_recovery_score.formula_explanation: The average predicted recovery propensity across all scored abandoned carts, read from the summary's score_mean field with a fallback to the mean of the cart_recovery_propensity column.
how_to_read: The baseline likelihood a typical abandoned cart can be recovered.
Compare individual carts and tiers against this average to prioritise outreach.
The recovery-score distribution chart shows how carts spread around this mean.
decisions_answered: What is the typical recovery likelihood across abandoned carts?
Which carts beat the average and deserve recovery effort?

ml-cart-recovery:high_medium_pool.title: High+Medium Tier Pool
ml-cart-recovery:high_medium_pool.formula: High+Medium Tier Pool = count of abandoned carts in the high and medium recovery-score tiers combined (the pool most worth a recovery touch)
ml-cart-recovery:high_medium_pool.formula_explanation: Number of abandoned carts in the high and medium recovery-score tiers combined — the pool most worth a recovery touch. Derived from the summary's tier_counts map.
how_to_read: The count of carts with the strongest recovery odds (high + medium tiers).
Focus recovery messaging here for the best return on effort.
Low-tier carts are excluded — they are unlikely to recover regardless of outreach.
decisions_answered: How many carts are worth actively trying to recover?
How large is the addressable recovery pool for a campaign?

ml-cart-recovery:score_distribution.title: Recovery Score Distribution
ml-cart-recovery:score_distribution.formula: Abandoned carts ranked by recovery propensity and split into ten equal deciles; each decile shows its cart count and average recovery score (rendered as a score histogram).
ml-cart-recovery:score_distribution.formula_explanation: Abandoned carts cut into ten equal-size deciles by recovery propensity, each reporting its count and average recovery score; the frontend renders this as a score histogram.
how_to_read: Higher deciles hold carts with the strongest recovery odds.
A steep gradient across deciles means the model separates carts well.
Use the bands to set a recovery-score cutoff for who gets contacted.
decisions_answered: How are recovery scores spread across abandoned carts?
Where should I draw the line on which carts to pursue?

ml-cart-recovery:expected_recovered_cart.title: Expected Recovered Carts by Decile
ml-cart-recovery:expected_recovered_cart.formula: Per recovery-score decile: expected recovered carts = cart count in the decile × that decile's average recovery propensity (rounded).
ml-cart-recovery:expected_recovered_cart.formula_explanation: Each recovery-score decile's cart count multiplied by its average recovery propensity — an estimate of how many carts that decile is expected to recover.
how_to_read: X-axis is decile (D1 lowest score → D10 highest); bar height is expected recoveries.
Top deciles have the best per-cart odds, but a larger lower decile can still yield more total recoveries.
Use it as a volume signal to balance recovery precision against reach.
decisions_answered: Where will the actual recovered-cart volume come from?
Should I target only the top deciles or pursue volume further down?

ml-cart-recovery:horizon_comparison.title: Recovery Score by Window (Avg x1000)
ml-cart-recovery:horizon_comparison.formula: For each recovery window (3-day, 7-day, 14-day): average recovery propensity scaled ×1000 for readability, alongside the number of carts scored.
ml-cart-recovery:horizon_comparison.formula_explanation: Compares the average recovery propensity (scaled ×1000 for readability) and scored-cart count across the model's recovery windows by calling the summary endpoint once per horizon.
how_to_read: Each group is a recovery window (3-day, 7-day, 14-day).
Average score is scaled ×1000 so small propensity differences are visible.
Compare windows to choose the recovery horizon that best balances odds and reachable volume.
decisions_answered: Which recovery window gives the best score-to-volume tradeoff?
How quickly does recovery likelihood decay as carts age?

formula_components.name: Average recovery score
formula_components.formula: Average recovery propensity across scored carts × 1000

formula_components.name: Carts scored
formula_components.formula: Total abandoned carts scored in the window

ml-cart-recovery:feature_importance.title: Feature Importance (Top 15)
ml-cart-recovery:feature_importance.formula: Top 15 model features ranked by LightGBM gain, each shown as a percent of the model's total gain.
ml-cart-recovery:feature_importance.formula_explanation: Top model features by LightGBM gain, normalised so each feature's contribution shows as a percent of total model gain.
how_to_read: Each bar is a feature; length is its share of the model's predictive power.
Higher-ranked features are the strongest signals for predicting cart recovery.
Gain reflects influence inside the model, not a guaranteed causal lever.
decisions_answered: What signals most predict whether a cart can be recovered?
Which factors should shape recovery messaging and offers?

ml-first-repeat:avg_repeat_propensity.title: Avg Repeat Propensity
ml-first-repeat:avg_repeat_propensity.formula: Avg Repeat Propensity = average predicted probability that a first-time buyer makes a repeat purchase, across all scored first-time buyers
ml-first-repeat:avg_repeat_propensity.formula_explanation: The average predicted probability that a first-time buyer makes a repeat purchase, read from the summary's score_mean with a fallback to the mean of the repeat_propensity column.
how_to_read: The baseline likelihood a typical first-time buyer comes back, as a percent.
Use it as the reference line to judge which buyers and segments over-index.
The propensity-by-decile chart shows how buyers spread around this mean.
decisions_answered: What is the typical repeat-purchase likelihood after a first order?
Which segments beat the average and warrant a repeat-purchase nudge?

ml-first-repeat:median_repeat_propensity.title: Median Repeat Propensity
ml-first-repeat:median_repeat_propensity.formula: Median Repeat Propensity = midpoint of the repeat-purchase propensity distribution across scored first-time buyers (half score higher, half lower)
ml-first-repeat:median_repeat_propensity.formula_explanation: The median repeat-purchase propensity across scored first-time buyers — half score higher, half lower — read from the summary's score_median field.
how_to_read: A percent: the midpoint of the repeat-propensity distribution.
Comparing median to mean reveals skew — a median below the mean means a high-propensity tail pulls the average up.
Less sensitive to outliers than the average, so a steadier read of the typical buyer.
decisions_answered: What does the typical (middle) first-time buyer's repeat likelihood look like?
Is the repeat-propensity distribution skewed by a small high-propensity group?

ml-first-repeat:one_time_buyers.title: One-Time Buyers Scored
ml-first-repeat:one_time_buyers.formula: One-Time Buyers Scored = count of registered first-time buyers scored by the repeat model this run
ml-first-repeat:one_time_buyers.formula_explanation: How many one-time (first-purchase) buyers the repeat model scored this run, read from the summary's n_scored field with a fallback to the scored-rows count.
how_to_read: A single count of the first-time-buyer pool eligible for a repeat-purchase play.
This is the universe the propensity, segment, and decile KPIs are built on.
A shrinking count suggests fewer recent first purchases — worth checking acquisition.
decisions_answered: How many first-time buyers could be converted to repeat customers?
Is the first-buyer pool large enough for a dedicated retention campaign?

ml-first-repeat:top_decile_pool.title: Top-Decile Pool (Loyalist Likely)
ml-first-repeat:top_decile_pool.formula: Top-Decile Pool = count of first-time buyers in the highest repeat-propensity segment ('loyalist_likely')
ml-first-repeat:top_decile_pool.formula_explanation: Number of first-time buyers in the highest repeat-propensity segment ('loyalist_likely') — those most likely to become repeat customers. Counted over the repeat_segment column.
how_to_read: The size of the most-likely-to-repeat pool worth prioritising.
These buyers anchor the top of the segment-breakdown and decile charts.
Focus retention spend here for the highest conversion-per-dollar.
decisions_answered: How many first-time buyers are prime repeat-purchase targets?
Is the high-propensity pool big enough to justify a dedicated nurture?

ml-first-repeat:segments_breakdown.title: Repeat Segments Breakdown
ml-first-repeat:segments_breakdown.formula: First-time buyers grouped by repeat-propensity segment; each segment shows its buyer count, share of the total, and average repeat propensity.
ml-first-repeat:segments_breakdown.formula_explanation: First-time buyers grouped by repeat-propensity segment with each segment's count, share of total, and average repeat propensity.
how_to_read: Each bar is a repeat segment (loyalist_likely down to least likely); size = buyer count.
Average propensity per segment confirms the tiers are ordered correctly.
Use the split to size each retention track before allocating budget.
decisions_answered: How is the first-buyer base distributed across repeat likelihood?
How should I split retention effort across the segments?

ml-first-repeat:propensity_by_decile.title: Repeat-Purchase Propensity by Decile
ml-first-repeat:propensity_by_decile.formula: First-time buyers ranked by repeat propensity and split into ten equal deciles; each decile shows its buyer count and the average, minimum and maximum repeat propensity.
ml-first-repeat:propensity_by_decile.formula_explanation: First-time buyers cut into ten equal-size deciles by repeat propensity, each reporting its count and the average, minimum, and maximum propensity in that band.
how_to_read: D10 holds the highest-propensity buyers; D1 the lowest.
A steep gradient from D1 to D10 means the model separates buyers well — strong targeting signal.
Flat deciles mean weak separation; decile targeting will add little lift.
decisions_answered: How sharply does the model rank first-buyers by repeat likelihood?
Which deciles are worth targeting and where do returns flatten?

ml-first-repeat:expected_repeat_buyers.title: Expected Repeat-Buyers by Decile
ml-first-repeat:expected_repeat_buyers.formula: Per repeat-propensity decile: expected repeat buyers = first-time-buyer count in the decile × that decile's average repeat propensity (rounded).
ml-first-repeat:expected_repeat_buyers.formula_explanation: Each repeat-propensity decile's buyer count multiplied by its average propensity — an estimate of how many repeat purchases that decile is expected to produce.
how_to_read: X-axis is decile (D1 lowest → D10 highest); bar height is expected repeat conversions.
D10 has the highest per-buyer odds, but a larger lower decile can still contribute more total volume.
Use it as a volume signal to balance precision against reach.
decisions_answered: Where will the actual repeat-purchase volume come from?
Should I target the highest-odds decile or go broader for total repeats?

ml-first-repeat:feature_importance.title: Top Features (LightGBM Gain)
ml-first-repeat:feature_importance.formula: Top model features ranked by LightGBM gain, each shown as a percent of the model's total gain.
ml-first-repeat:feature_importance.formula_explanation: Top model features by LightGBM gain, normalised so each feature's contribution shows as a percent of the model's total gain.
how_to_read: Each bar is a feature; length is its share of the model's predictive power.
Higher-ranked features are the strongest signals for predicting repeat purchase.
Gain reflects influence inside the model, not a guaranteed causal lever.
decisions_answered: What signals most predict a first-time buyer will return?
Which factors should shape repeat-purchase campaigns?

ml-acquisition-forecast:total_forecast.title: Forecast New Customers (next 4w)
ml-acquisition-forecast:total_forecast.formula: Forecast New Customers (next 4w) = sum of the predicted new customers across every entry category over the next four weeks
ml-acquisition-forecast:total_forecast.formula_explanation: Total predicted new customers across every entry category for the next four weeks (t+1..t+4). Each per-category forecast comes from the acquisition LightGBM ensemble (Tweedie base + log-smear correction + calibration + weekly reconciliation) trained on weekly new-customer counts by entry category.
how_to_read: A single number: the predicted new-customer total for the coming 4 weeks.
It sums forecasts across all entry categories, so it is a portfolio-level signal.
Treat it as a target with uncertainty — judge it against the model's accuracy metrics (wMAPE, skill vs naive).
decisions_answered: How many new customers should I plan to acquire over the next month?
Is acquisition trending up or down versus recent weeks?

ml-acquisition-forecast:categories_forec.title: Entry Categories Forecast
ml-acquisition-forecast:categories_forec.formula: Entry Categories Forecast = count of distinct entry categories that carry a next-4-week new-customer forecast
ml-acquisition-forecast:categories_forec.formula_explanation: How many entry categories (the product category through which a new customer first enters) carry a next-4-week new-customer forecast. Computed as the distinct entity count over the predictions table.
how_to_read: A single count of forecastable entry categories.
An entry category is the product category of a new customer's first order.
More categories with forecasts means broader acquisition coverage across the catalog.
decisions_answered: Across how many entry points is the model forecasting acquisition?
How diversified are my new-customer entry categories?

ml-acquisition-forecast:avg_per_category.title: Avg / Entry Category
ml-acquisition-forecast:avg_per_category.formula: Avg per Entry Category = total forecast new customers ÷ number of forecast entry categories
ml-acquisition-forecast:avg_per_category.formula_explanation: Average predicted new customers per entry category — the total 4-week forecast divided by the number of forecastable entry categories.
how_to_read: The typical per-category new-customer forecast.
Compare individual categories against this average to spot over- and under-performers.
A low average with a high total signals acquisition is concentrated in a few categories (see Pareto / treemap).
decisions_answered: What is the typical acquisition contribution of an entry category?
Which categories are pulling above or below their weight?

ml-acquisition-forecast:top_category.title: Top Entry Category
ml-acquisition-forecast:top_category.formula: Top Entry Category = the entry category with the highest forecast new customers, with its share = that category's forecast ÷ total forecast new customers
ml-acquisition-forecast:top_category.formula_explanation: The single highest-forecast entry category, with its predicted new customers and its share of the total — derived in the frontend from the per-category forecast points returned by the forecast endpoint.
how_to_read: Names the entry category expected to bring in the most new customers next month.
The share shows how dominant that one category is in total acquisition.
A very high share is a concentration risk — diversify entry points if one category dominates.
decisions_answered: Which entry category will drive the most new-customer acquisition?
How dependent is acquisition on a single entry category?

formula_components.name: Top Entry Category
formula_components.formula: entry category with the maximum forecast new customers

formula_components.name: Share
formula_components.formula: top category's forecast ÷ total forecast new customers

ml-acquisition-forecast:top10_concentrat.title: Top-10 Concentration
ml-acquisition-forecast:top10_concentrat.formula: Top-10 Concentration = forecast new customers of the 10 highest entry categories ÷ total forecast new customers × 100
ml-acquisition-forecast:top10_concentrat.formula_explanation: Share of total forecast new customers concentrated in the ten highest-forecast entry categories — a frontend rollup over the per-category forecast points.
how_to_read: A percent: how much of next month's acquisition comes from just the top 10 entry categories.
High concentration means acquisition rests on a few entry points; low means it is broad-based.
Pair with the Pareto chart to see the full concentration curve.
decisions_answered: How concentrated is new-customer acquisition across entry categories?
Is there entry-category concentration risk I should diversify away from?

ml-acquisition-forecast:top_categories.title: Top 15 Entry Categories by Forecast New Customers
ml-acquisition-forecast:top_categories.formula: The 15 entry categories with the highest next-4-week forecast new customers, shown as a horizontal bar chart; an entry category is the product category of a new customer's first order.
ml-acquisition-forecast:top_categories.formula_explanation: The fifteen entry categories with the highest predicted next-4-week new customers, sorted by the model's per-category forecast.
how_to_read: Each bar is an entry category; length is its predicted new customers over the next 4 weeks.
An entry category is the product category of a new customer's first order.
The longest bars are where acquisition campaigns and inventory should focus.
decisions_answered: Which entry categories will drive the most new-customer acquisition?
Where should I concentrate acquisition spend and stock?

ml-acquisition-forecast:pareto.title: New Customers Concentration (Pareto)
ml-acquisition-forecast:pareto.formula: Entry categories sorted from most to fewest forecast new customers, plotting the running cumulative share of the total with an 80% marker; a curve that hits 80% with few categories means acquisition is highly concentrated.
ml-acquisition-forecast:pareto.formula_explanation: A Pareto curve of the cumulative share of forecast new customers as entry categories are added from largest to smallest, with an 80% reference marker.
how_to_read: X-axis ranks entry categories from largest to smallest; the line is the running cumulative share.
A curve that hits 80% with few categories means acquisition is highly concentrated.
Read where the line crosses the 80% marker to see how many categories carry most acquisition.
decisions_answered: How few entry categories make up most of new-customer acquisition?
How concentrated (or diversified) is my acquisition base?

ml-acquisition-forecast:distribution.title: Forecast Distribution by New Customers Band
ml-acquisition-forecast:distribution.formula: Count of entry categories in each forecast-new-customers band (<1, 1-5, 5-10, 10-25, 25-50, 50-100, 100+); mass in the low bands means most categories are small acquisition contributors.
ml-acquisition-forecast:distribution.formula_explanation: How many entry categories fall into each band of predicted new customers, bucketing the per-category forecasts into fixed ranges.
how_to_read: X-axis is a new-customers band; bar height is how many entry categories land in it.
Mass in the low bands means most categories are small acquisition contributors.
A few categories in the high bands (50–100, 100+) are the acquisition workhorses.
decisions_answered: Are most entry categories small or large acquisition drivers?
How many categories are meaningful enough to invest in?

ml-acquisition-forecast:share_treemap.title: Forecast New Customers Share
ml-acquisition-forecast:share_treemap.formula: A treemap where each tile's area is an entry category's forecast new customers; the 20 largest categories are shown individually and the rest pooled into an "Other" tile.
ml-acquisition-forecast:share_treemap.formula_explanation: A treemap where each tile is an entry category sized by its predicted new customers; the top 20 categories are shown individually and the rest are rolled into an 'Other' tile.
how_to_read: Each rectangle is an entry category; its area is its predicted new customers.
Larger tiles dominate acquisition; the 'Other' tile aggregates the long tail.
Use the visual weight to grasp relative contribution at a glance.
decisions_answered: What share of new customers does each entry category contribute?
Which categories dominate acquisition and which sit in the long tail?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:total.title: Forecast Buyers (next 4w)
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:total.formula: Forecast Buyers (next 4w) = sum of the LightGBM-predicted distinct-buyer (reach) score across every category over the next four weeks (approximate reach, not additive across weeks)
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:total.formula_explanation: Adds up the LightGBM-predicted distinct-buyer (reach) score across every category for the next four weeks (t+1..t+4). The total comes from the precomputed summary.json artifact (total_forecast_buyers); the fallback sums the model score column directly.
how_to_read: A single headline of total distinct buyers expected across the whole catalog over the next 4 weeks.
Distinct-buyer counts are not additive across weeks, so treat this as an approximate reach figure, not a hard headcount.
Rising vs a prior run signals broadening demand; falling signals contraction.
Pair with Categories Forecast to see whether the total is spread across many categories or driven by a few.
decisions_answered: How many buyers do we expect to engage across all categories in the next 4 weeks?
Is overall buyer reach trending up or down versus the last forecast?
What is the demand baseline I should plan inventory and staffing against?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:categ.title: Categories Forecast
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:categ.formula: Categories Forecast = count of distinct categories that have a next-4-week buyer forecast
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:categ.formula_explanation: Counts the number of distinct product categories that have a next-4-week buyer forecast. Read from summary.json (n_categories); the fallback computes df['entity'].nunique().
how_to_read: The count of categories the model produced a buyer forecast for.
A higher count means broader coverage of the catalog.
If this drops between runs, some categories may have lost enough signal to be forecast.
Use alongside the total to compute average demand per category.
decisions_answered: How many categories are actively being forecast?
Has forecast coverage of the catalog changed since the last run?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:avg_p.title: Avg / Category
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:avg_p.formula: Avg per Category = total forecast buyers ÷ number of forecast categories
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:avg_p.formula_explanation: Total forecast buyers divided by the number of forecast categories, giving the mean predicted buyers per category. Read from summary.json (mean_forecast_per_category).
how_to_read: The typical number of forecast buyers a category attracts over the next 4 weeks.
Compare a category's own forecast against this average to spot over- and under-performers.
A high average with few categories points to concentrated demand.
This is a mean, so a handful of very large categories can pull it above the typical category.
decisions_answered: What is the average buyer demand per category?
Which categories are above or below the typical category?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top_c.title: Top Category
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top_c.formula: Top Category = the category with the highest forecast buyers, with its share = that category's forecast buyers ÷ total forecast buyers
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top_c.formula_explanation: Identifies the single category with the highest forecast buyers and shows its share of the total. Computed client-side by forecastStats() over the forecast points (top_value, top_share).
how_to_read: Names the category expected to draw the most buyers and its value.
The share in parentheses is that category's slice of all forecast buyers.
A large share signals heavy dependence on one category.
Watch this category closely — a miss here moves the whole forecast.
decisions_answered: Which category will draw the most buyers next month?
How dependent is total demand on a single category?

formula_components.name: Top Category
formula_components.formula: category with the maximum forecast buyers

formula_components.name: Share
formula_components.formula: top category's forecast buyers ÷ total forecast buyers

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top10.title: Top-10 Concentration
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top10.formula: Top-10 Concentration = forecast buyers of the 10 highest categories ÷ total forecast buyers
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top10.formula_explanation: Share of total forecast buyers held by the 10 highest-forecast categories. Computed client-side by forecastStats() (top10_share) after sorting categories by forecast buyers descending.
how_to_read: The percentage of all forecast buyers concentrated in the top 10 categories.
A high value (e.g. 80%+) means demand is concentrated and risk is concentrated too.
A low value means demand is spread broadly across the catalog.
Track over time to see whether the catalog is becoming more or less top-heavy.
decisions_answered: How concentrated is buyer demand among a few top categories?
Is the catalog becoming more or less reliant on its top categories?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top_c.title: Top 15 Categories by Forecast Buyers
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top_c.formula: The 15 categories with the highest next-4-week forecast buyers (the per-category LightGBM buyer-breadth score), shown as a horizontal bar chart.
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:top_c.formula_explanation: Sorts categories by their next-4-week forecast buyers (the per-category model score) and shows the 15 largest as a horizontal bar chart. The underlying value is forecast_buyers from the forecast series.
how_to_read: Each bar is one category; longer bars mean more forecast buyers.
Bars are drawn smallest-to-largest, so the biggest category sits at the top.
Use it to see which categories drive the next-4-week buyer base.
Categories beyond the top 15 are excluded — see the table for the full list.
decisions_answered: Which categories will attract the most buyers next month?
Where should merchandising and stock focus to capture the most demand?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:paret.title: Buyer Concentration (Pareto)
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:paret.formula: Categories sorted from most to fewest forecast buyers, plotting the running cumulative share of total buyers with an 80% marker; where the curve crosses 80% shows how few categories make up most demand.
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:paret.formula_explanation: Sorts categories from most to fewest forecast buyers, then plots the running cumulative share of total buyers. The 80% marker highlights how few categories make up the bulk of demand.
how_to_read: The x-axis ranks categories from largest to smallest by buyers.
The curve shows what cumulative percentage of all buyers the top-N categories account for.
Where the curve crosses the 80% line tells you how many categories make up most demand.
A steep early curve means demand is concentrated in a small head of categories.
decisions_answered: How many categories account for 80% of forecast buyers?
Is buyer demand concentrated in a few categories or spread out?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:distr.title: Forecast Distribution by Buyer Band
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:distr.formula: Count of categories in each forecast-buyers band (<10, 10-50, 50-100, 100-500, 500-1k, 1k-5k, 5k+); shows the shape of buyer demand across the catalog.
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:distr.formula_explanation: Buckets each category by its forecast buyers into fixed bands and counts how many categories fall in each band. Shows the shape of demand across the catalog.
how_to_read: Each bar is a buyer band; its height is how many categories land in that band.
A long left tail means most categories are small by buyer count.
A heavier right side means several high-demand categories.
Compare bands over time to see demand shifting up or down the catalog.
decisions_answered: Are most categories small or large by forecast buyers?
How many categories fall into each demand tier?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:share.title: Forecast Buyer Share
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:share.formula: A treemap where each tile's area is a category's forecast buyers; the 20 largest categories are shown individually and the rest pooled into an "Other" tile.
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:share.formula_explanation: Each tile's area is proportional to that category's forecast buyers. The 20 largest categories are shown individually and the rest are pooled into a single "Other" tile.
how_to_read: Bigger tiles mean more forecast buyers for that category.
Relative tile sizes show each category's slice of total demand at a glance.
The "Other" tile sums all categories beyond the top 20.
Use it to spot dominant categories versus the long tail visually.
decisions_answered: Which categories make up the largest share of forecast buyers?
How much demand sits in the long tail beyond the top 20 categories?

ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:forec.title: Category Forecast (next 4 weeks)
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:forec.formula: One row per category with its next-4-week forecast distinct buyers (the LightGBM buyer-breadth score), ranked highest-first, plus each category's share = forecast buyers ÷ total forecast buyers.
ml-category-buyer-breadth-forecast:forec.formula_explanation: Lists every category sorted by next-4-week forecast buyers, with each category's share of the total. Values are the per-category LightGBM buyer-breadth scores from the forecast series.
how_to_read: Rows are ranked from most to fewest forecast buyers.
The Share column shows each category's percentage of total forecast buyers.
The Type column flags whether a category is real or uncategorized.
Page through to inspect the full catalog, not just the top performers.
decisions_answered: What is the exact forecast for any given category?
Which categories sit just below the top tier and may be worth pushing?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:total_forec.title: Forecast Revenue (next 4w)
ml-category-revenue-forecast:total_forec.formula: Forecast Revenue (next 4w) = sum of the LightGBM-predicted revenue across every category over the next four weeks
ml-category-revenue-forecast:total_forec.formula_explanation: Adds up the LightGBM-predicted revenue across every category for the next four weeks (t+1..t+4). Read from the precomputed summary.json (total_forecast_revenue); the fallback sums the model score column directly.
how_to_read: A single headline of expected revenue across the whole catalog over the next 4 weeks.
Use it as the top-line revenue target to plan against.
Rising vs a prior run signals growing demand; falling signals softening.
Pair with Categories Forecast to see how the total is spread.
decisions_answered: How much revenue do we expect across all categories in the next 4 weeks?
Is total forecast revenue trending up or down versus the last run?
What revenue baseline should I plan inventory and budget against?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:categories_.title: Categories Forecast
ml-category-revenue-forecast:categories_.formula: Categories Forecast = count of distinct categories that have a next-4-week revenue forecast
ml-category-revenue-forecast:categories_.formula_explanation: Counts the number of distinct product categories that have a next-4-week revenue forecast. Read from summary.json (n_categories); the fallback computes df['entity'].nunique().
how_to_read: The count of categories with a revenue forecast.
A higher count means broader coverage of the catalog.
If this drops between runs, some categories may have lost enough signal to be forecast.
Use alongside the total to compute average revenue per category.
decisions_answered: How many categories are actively being forecast for revenue?
Has forecast coverage of the catalog changed since the last run?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:avg_per_cat.title: Avg / Category
ml-category-revenue-forecast:avg_per_cat.formula: Avg per Category = total forecast revenue ÷ number of forecast categories
ml-category-revenue-forecast:avg_per_cat.formula_explanation: Total forecast revenue divided by the number of forecast categories, giving the mean predicted revenue per category. Read from summary.json (mean_forecast_per_category).
how_to_read: The typical revenue a category is forecast to generate over the next 4 weeks.
Compare a category's own forecast against this average to spot over- and under-performers.
A high average with few categories points to concentrated revenue.
This is a mean, so a few very large categories can pull it above the typical category.
decisions_answered: What is the average forecast revenue per category?
Which categories are above or below the typical category?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:top_categor.title: Top Category
ml-category-revenue-forecast:top_categor.formula: Top Category = the category with the highest forecast revenue, with its share = that category's forecast revenue ÷ total forecast revenue
ml-category-revenue-forecast:top_categor.formula_explanation: Identifies the single category with the highest forecast revenue and shows its share of the total. Computed client-side by forecastStats() over the forecast points (top_value, top_share).
how_to_read: Names the category expected to generate the most revenue and its value.
The share in parentheses is that category's slice of total forecast revenue.
A large share signals heavy dependence on one category.
Watch this category closely — a miss here moves the whole forecast.
decisions_answered: Which category will generate the most revenue next month?
How dependent is total revenue on a single category?

formula_components.name: Top Category
formula_components.formula: category with the maximum forecast revenue

formula_components.name: Share
formula_components.formula: top category's forecast revenue ÷ total forecast revenue

ml-category-revenue-forecast:top10_conce.title: Top-10 Concentration
ml-category-revenue-forecast:top10_conce.formula: Top-10 Concentration = forecast revenue of the 10 highest categories ÷ total forecast revenue
ml-category-revenue-forecast:top10_conce.formula_explanation: Share of total forecast revenue held by the 10 highest-forecast categories. Computed client-side by forecastStats() (top10_share) after sorting categories by forecast revenue descending.
how_to_read: The percentage of all forecast revenue concentrated in the top 10 categories.
A high value (e.g. 80%+) means revenue is concentrated and risk is concentrated too.
A low value means revenue is spread broadly across the catalog.
Track over time to see whether the catalog is becoming more or less top-heavy.
decisions_answered: How concentrated is revenue among a few top categories?
Is the catalog becoming more or less reliant on its top categories?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:top_categor.title: Top 15 Categories by Forecast Revenue
ml-category-revenue-forecast:top_categor.formula: The 15 categories with the highest next-4-week forecast revenue (the per-category LightGBM revenue score), shown as a horizontal bar chart.
ml-category-revenue-forecast:top_categor.formula_explanation: Sorts categories by their next-4-week forecast revenue (the per-category model score) and shows the 15 largest as a horizontal bar chart. The underlying value is forecast_revenue from the forecast series.
how_to_read: Each bar is one category; longer bars mean more forecast revenue.
Bars are drawn smallest-to-largest, so the biggest category sits at the top.
Use it to see which categories drive next-4-week revenue.
Categories beyond the top 15 are excluded — see the table for the full list.
decisions_answered: Which categories will generate the most revenue next month?
Where should merchandising and stock focus to capture the most revenue?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:pareto.title: Revenue Concentration (Pareto)
ml-category-revenue-forecast:pareto.formula: Categories sorted from most to least forecast revenue, plotting the running cumulative share of total revenue with an 80% marker; where the curve crosses 80% shows how few categories make up most revenue.
ml-category-revenue-forecast:pareto.formula_explanation: Sorts categories from most to least forecast revenue, then plots the running cumulative share of total revenue. The 80% marker highlights how few categories make up the bulk of revenue.
how_to_read: The x-axis ranks categories from largest to smallest by revenue.
The curve shows what cumulative percentage of all revenue the top-N categories account for.
Where the curve crosses the 80% line tells you how many categories make up most revenue.
A steep early curve means revenue is concentrated in a small head of categories.
decisions_answered: How many categories account for 80% of forecast revenue?
Is revenue concentrated in a few categories or spread out?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:distributio.title: Forecast Distribution by Revenue Band
ml-category-revenue-forecast:distributio.formula: Count of categories in each forecast-revenue band (<$100, $100-500, $500-1k, $1k-5k, $5k-10k, $10k-50k, $50k+); shows the shape of revenue across the catalog.
ml-category-revenue-forecast:distributio.formula_explanation: Buckets each category by its forecast revenue into fixed dollar bands and counts how many categories fall in each band. Shows the shape of revenue across the catalog.
how_to_read: Each bar is a revenue band; its height is how many categories land in that band.
A long left tail means most categories are small by revenue.
A heavier right side means several high-revenue categories.
Compare bands over time to see revenue shifting up or down the catalog.
decisions_answered: Are most categories small or large by forecast revenue?
How many categories fall into each revenue tier?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:share_treem.title: Forecast Revenue Share
ml-category-revenue-forecast:share_treem.formula: A treemap where each tile's area is a category's forecast revenue; the 20 largest categories are shown individually and the rest pooled into an "Other" tile.
ml-category-revenue-forecast:share_treem.formula_explanation: Each tile's area is proportional to that category's forecast revenue. The 20 largest categories are shown individually and the rest are pooled into a single "Other" tile.
how_to_read: Bigger tiles mean more forecast revenue for that category.
Relative tile sizes show each category's slice of total revenue at a glance.
The "Other" tile sums all categories beyond the top 20.
Use it to spot dominant categories versus the long tail visually.
decisions_answered: Which categories make up the largest share of forecast revenue?
How much revenue sits in the long tail beyond the top 20 categories?

ml-category-revenue-forecast:forecast_ta.title: Category Forecast (next 4 weeks)
ml-category-revenue-forecast:forecast_ta.formula: One row per category with its next-4-week forecast revenue (the LightGBM revenue score), ranked highest-first, plus each category's share = forecast revenue ÷ total forecast revenue.
ml-category-revenue-forecast:forecast_ta.formula_explanation: Lists every category sorted by next-4-week forecast revenue, with each category's share of the total. Values are the per-category LightGBM revenue scores from the forecast series.
how_to_read: Rows are ranked from most to least forecast revenue.
The Share column shows each category's percentage of total forecast revenue.
The Type column flags whether a category is real or uncategorized.
Page through to inspect the full catalog, not just the top performers.

Which categories sit just below the top tier and may be worth pushing?

ml-product-demand-forecast:total_forecas.title: Forecast Units (next 4w)
ml-product-demand-forecast:total_forecas.formula: Forecast Units (next 4w) = sum of the LightGBM-predicted unit demand across every product over the next four weeks
ml-product-demand-forecast:total_forecas.formula_explanation: Adds up the LightGBM-predicted unit demand across every product for the next four weeks (t+1..t+4). Read from the precomputed summary.json (total_forecast_units); the fallback sums the model score column directly.
how_to_read: A single headline of total units expected to sell across the catalog over the next 4 weeks.
Use it as the demand baseline for inventory and replenishment planning.

What demand baseline should I plan inventory and fulfillment against?

ml-product-demand-forecast:products_fore.title: Products Forecast

ml-product-demand-forecast:products_fore.formula_explanation: Counts the number of distinct products that have a next-4-week units forecast. Read from summary.json (n_products); the fallback computes df['entity'].nunique().

ml-product-demand-forecast:avg_per_produ.title: Avg / Product
ml-product-demand-forecast:avg_per_produ.formula: Avg per Product = total forecast units ÷ number of forecast products
ml-product-demand-forecast:avg_per_produ.formula_explanation: Total forecast units divided by the number of forecast products, giving the mean predicted units per product. Read from summary.json (mean_forecast_per_product).

annual-planning:total_budget.title: Total Budget
annual-planning:total_budget.formula: Total Budget = total planned ad spend for the year (sum of channel annual spend; falls back to configured $2.24M default when no actuals); YoY Change % vs prior-year spend
annual-planning:total_budget.formula_explanation: Total planned marketing budget for the planning year. The backend sums annual ad spend from the planning summary mart; when no spend has been recorded yet it falls back to the configured $2.24M default. The change is measured against the prior year's spend.
how_to_read: The headline value is the total ad budget allocated for the planning year.
The percentage compares this budget to last year's total spend.
An up trend means you are investing more than the prior year; down means a leaner plan.
The sparkline shows the recent 7-day spend trajectory from the daily pulse mart.
decisions_answered: How much am I planning to invest in marketing this year?
Is my budget growing or shrinking versus last year?
Do I have a configured plan, or am I defaulting to the baseline budget?

formula_components.name: Total Budget
formula_components.formula: sum of annual ad spend for the year (default $2.24M when no spend recorded)

annual-planning:projected_revenue.title: Projected Revenue
annual-planning:projected_revenue.formula: Projected Revenue = (Google budget × Google ROAS) + (Meta budget × Meta ROAS) + (Email budget × Email ROAS), where budget split is 52% Google / 46% Meta / 2% Email and channel ROAS are prior-year actuals (Email fixed at 25x)
annual-planning:projected_revenue.formula_explanation: Expected annual revenue derived from the budget split across channels multiplied by each channel's historical ROAS. The default channel mix is 52% Google / 46% Meta / 2% Email, and prior-year channel ROAS values drive the projection (Email ROAS fixed at 25x). Change is measured against prior-year revenue.
how_to_read: The value is the revenue the plan is expected to generate at the chosen budget and channel mix.
It is built bottom-up: each channel's budget times that channel's historical efficiency.
Compare it to prior-year revenue (shown as the change) to gauge growth ambition.
Email contributes little in absolute dollars because it gets only ~2% of budget despite a high ROAS.
decisions_answered: What revenue should I expect from this budget plan?
How does projected revenue compare to last year?
Which channels are driving the bulk of expected revenue?

formula_components.name: Channel budgets
formula_components.formula: Google = budget × 0.52; Meta = budget × 0.46; Email = budget × 0.02

annual-planning:blended_roas.title: Blended ROAS
annual-planning:blended_roas.formula: Blended ROAS = prior-year total ad-platform conversion value ÷ prior-year total spend across all channels
annual-planning:blended_roas.formula_explanation: Expected blended return on ad spend across all channels, computed as prior-year total platform conversion value divided by total spend from the marketing-mix mart. It expresses, in aggregate, how many dollars of revenue each dollar of ad spend returns.
how_to_read: A value of 7.5x means every $1 of ad spend is expected to return $7.50 in revenue.
It blends all channels together, so high-efficiency channels (Email) and lower-efficiency paid channels are averaged by spend weight.
Use it as the single headline efficiency number for the whole plan.
It is derived from prior-year actuals, so it reflects realized performance, not a target.
decisions_answered: How efficient is my marketing spend overall?
Is the plan's blended efficiency healthy relative to my benchmarks?
If I shift budget between channels, is blended ROAS likely to improve or erode?

annual-planning:yoy_change.title: YoY Change
annual-planning:yoy_change.formula: YoY Change % = (most recent completed year revenue − prior year revenue) ÷ prior year revenue × 100
annual-planning:yoy_change.formula_explanation: Year-over-year revenue change, comparing the most recent completed year's annual revenue to the year before it from the planning summary mart. Positive values indicate growth; negative values indicate decline.
how_to_read: A positive percentage means revenue grew versus the prior year.
The comparison is between two completed years of annual revenue, not budget.
An up trend arrow confirms growth; down confirms contraction.
Use it as the historical momentum signal feeding into this year's plan.
decisions_answered: Is the business growing year over year?
What growth rate should I assume as a baseline for next year's plan?
Has revenue momentum been accelerating or slowing?

annual-planning:historical_annual.title: Historical Annual Spend by Channel
annual-planning:historical_annual.formula: Annual ad spend by channel stacked over a three-year window, with each channel's yearly revenue carried alongside.
annual-planning:historical_annual.formula_explanation: Stacked bar chart of annual ad spend by channel over a 3-year window, pulled from the annual planning summary mart. Each segment is one channel's yearly spend, and revenue is attached as metadata for context.
how_to_read: Each bar is one year; segments within it are channels stacked to show total spend.
Compare bar heights across years to see whether overall investment is rising or falling.
Compare segment sizes to see how the channel mix has shifted over time.
Hover metadata reveals the revenue each channel generated that year.
decisions_answered: How has my total marketing spend changed over the last 3 years?
Which channels have grown or shrunk as a share of spend?
Is my historical investment pattern consistent with my plan?

annual-planning:monthly_trend.title: Monthly Spend & Revenue Trend
annual-planning:monthly_trend.formula: Monthly ad spend per channel over the trailing 36 months (line chart), with monthly revenue (platform conversion value) carried alongside.
annual-planning:monthly_trend.formula_explanation: Line chart of monthly ad spend (and revenue as metadata) per channel across the last 36 months from the marketing-mix mart. It reveals month-to-month spend cadence and longer-term trends by channel.
how_to_read: Each line is a channel; the x-axis is calendar month over 3 years.
Rising lines indicate increasing monthly spend in that channel.
Look for recurring seasonal humps that repeat year to year.
Revenue is carried in metadata to pair spend with its return.
decisions_answered: How does my monthly spend fluctuate through the year?
Which channels show seasonal spend patterns?
Are there months where spend has consistently grown or shrunk?

annual-planning:quarterly_roas.title: Quarterly ROAS by Channel
annual-planning:quarterly_roas.formula: Return on ad spend by quarter and channel (line chart) read from the pre-computed quarterly ROAS columns over a three-year window.
annual-planning:quarterly_roas.formula_explanation: Line chart of return on ad spend by quarter and channel, read directly from the pre-computed quarterly ROAS columns in the annual planning summary mart over a 3-year window. It smooths out daily noise to show efficiency trends per channel.
how_to_read: Each line is a channel; the x-axis is year-quarter (e.g. 2025-Q3).
Higher points mean that channel returned more revenue per dollar that quarter.
Compare channels at the same quarter to see relative efficiency.
Watch for declining slopes that signal efficiency erosion over time.
decisions_answered: Which channel delivers the best return each quarter?
Is any channel's efficiency trending down over recent quarters?
Are there seasonal quarters where ROAS reliably peaks or dips?

annual-planning:campaign_type.title: Google Ads Performance by Campaign Type
annual-planning:campaign_type.formula: Google Ads spend by campaign type (Search, Shopping, Performance Max, etc.) stacked per year over the last three years, with each segment's revenue, conversions and ROAS (conversions value ÷ spend) alongside.
annual-planning:campaign_type.formula_explanation: Stacked bar chart breaking down Google Ads spend by campaign type (Search, Shopping, Performance Max, etc.) per year, with revenue, conversions, and ROAS attached as metadata. Sourced from the Google campaign performance mart.
how_to_read: Each bar is a year; segments are Google campaign types stacked by spend.
Larger segments indicate where Google budget is concentrated.
Hover metadata exposes ROAS so you can see which campaign types are efficient, not just large.
Compare across years to see how the campaign-type mix is evolving.
decisions_answered: Where is my Google Ads budget going by campaign type?
Which Google campaign types deliver the best ROAS?
Has my Google campaign-type mix shifted over the last few years?

annual-planning:seasonality.title: Seasonality Coefficients by Month
annual-planning:seasonality.formula: Monthly revenue seasonality index where 1.0 is an average month; above 1.0 are peak months and below 1.0 are slow months, with the spend index and recommended budget percentage carried per month.
annual-planning:seasonality.formula_explanation: Chart of the monthly revenue seasonality index from the seasonality coefficients mart, where 1.0 represents an average month. Values above 1.0 are peak months and below 1.0 are slow months; spend index and recommended budget allocation accompany each month as metadata.
how_to_read: Each point/bar is a month; the value is its revenue index relative to the yearly average.
Above 1.0 means that month historically over-performs; below 1.0 means it under-performs.
Use peaks to time budget concentration and promotions.
Metadata includes a recommended budget percentage per month for planning.
decisions_answered: Which months are my strongest and weakest historically?
When should I concentrate budget to match demand?
How should I distribute the annual budget across months?

annual-planning:spend_buckets.title: ROAS by Spend Level (Diminishing Returns)
annual-planning:spend_buckets.formula: Historical days grouped into daily-spend bands ($0-1K, $1K-2K, ... $5K+); each bar is the average ROAS (daily revenue ÷ daily spend) of days in that band over the last three years, surfacing diminishing returns as spend rises.
annual-planning:spend_buckets.formula_explanation: Bar chart that groups historical days by their daily spend level and shows the average ROAS in each band. It surfaces diminishing returns: if ROAS falls as the daily-spend band rises, higher spend days were less efficient.
how_to_read: The x-axis is daily spend bands from lowest to highest.
The bar height is the average ROAS observed on days in that band.
A downward staircase means efficiency drops as you spend more per day (diminishing returns).
A flat or rising pattern means you have room to scale spend without losing efficiency.
decisions_answered: At what daily spend level does my efficiency start to drop?
Can I scale spend further without sacrificing ROAS?
What is the optimal daily spend ceiling for the plan?

formula_components.name: Average ROAS per Band
formula_components.formula: Average of (daily revenue ÷ daily spend) across days in the spend band

annual-planning:yoy_growth.title: YoY Growth Rates
annual-planning:yoy_growth.formula: Year-over-year revenue change percentage per year over the last three years (bar chart), with annual spend, revenue and the spend year-over-year change carried alongside.
annual-planning:yoy_growth.formula_explanation: Bar chart of the year-over-year revenue change percentage from the pre-computed YoY columns in the annual planning summary mart. Each bar is a year's revenue growth rate; spend growth and absolute spend/revenue are available as metadata.
how_to_read: Each bar is a year; the value is that year's revenue growth versus the prior year.
Positive bars indicate growth; negative bars indicate contraction.
Compare consecutive bars to see whether growth is accelerating or decelerating.
Metadata pairs revenue growth with spend growth so you can see if growth came from more spend.
decisions_answered: What growth rate has the business sustained year over year?
Is revenue growth accelerating or slowing?
Did revenue grow faster or slower than spend?

annual-planning:channel_revenue.title: Channel Revenue Attribution (GA4)
annual-planning:channel_revenue.formula: GA4-attributed revenue by channel grouping stacked per year over the last three years, with sessions and revenue-per-session (revenue ÷ sessions) alongside each segment; channels with revenue are kept.
annual-planning:channel_revenue.formula_explanation: Stacked bar chart of GA4-attributed revenue by channel grouping per year, with sessions and revenue-per-session as metadata. It reflects how GA4 attributes revenue across acquisition channels rather than ad-platform-reported conversions.
how_to_read: Each bar is a year; segments are GA4 channel groupings stacked by attributed revenue.
This is GA4's attribution view, which can differ from ad platforms' self-reported numbers.
Hover metadata shows sessions and revenue-per-session to judge channel quality.
Compare years to see which channels are gaining or losing attributed revenue share.
decisions_answered: Which channels does GA4 credit with driving revenue?
