How does GA4 attribution compare to platform-reported performance?
Which channels convert sessions into revenue most effectively?

annual-planning:channel_efficiency.title: Channel Efficiency (Sharpe Ratio)
annual-planning:channel_efficiency.formula: Per channel (Google Ads, Meta Ads) a Sharpe-style efficiency ratio = average daily ROAS ÷ the standard deviation of daily ROAS over the trailing two years, where daily ROAS = revenue ÷ spend; higher means strong, steady returns.
annual-planning:channel_efficiency.formula_explanation: Bar chart ranking channels by a Sharpe-style efficiency ratio: average daily ROAS divided by the standard deviation of daily ROAS. A higher value means a channel delivers strong returns consistently, which is the basis for the AI budget recommendation.
how_to_read: Each bar is a channel; taller bars mean better risk-adjusted efficiency.
It rewards channels that are both high-ROAS and low-volatility.
A high-ROAS but erratic channel can score lower than a steadier one.
Metadata exposes the underlying average ROAS and volatility separately.
decisions_answered: Which channel offers the best return per unit of volatility?
Should I weight budget toward consistent performers over erratic high-ROAS ones?
What drives the AI-recommended allocation between Google and Meta?

formula_components.name: Sharpe Ratio
formula_components.formula: Average daily ROAS ÷ standard deviation of daily ROAS

budget-pacing:historical_spend_trend.title: Historical Spend Trend
budget-pacing:historical_spend_trend.formula: Stacked bar of monthly total spend split by platform (Google Ads + Meta Ads) with month-over-month variance. Monthly spend = daily spend summed per month; MoM variance % = (current month − previous month) ÷ previous month × 100.
budget-pacing:historical_spend_trend.formula_explanation: Shows historical monthly spend totals from MART_BUDGET_PACING, split by Google Ads and Meta Ads. Month-over-month variance reveals spending patterns, seasonality, and growth trends across the selected period.
how_to_read: Each bar is one calendar month's total ad spend, stacked by platform (Google Ads + Meta Ads).
Taller bars mean higher spend; compare bar heights to spot upward or downward trajectories.
The MoM Variance % shows the percentage change from the prior month — positive means spend grew, negative means it shrank.
Use it to identify seasonal peaks and to sanity-check whether current pacing is in line with history.
decisions_answered: Is our monthly spend trending up, down, or holding steady?
Which months historically see the biggest spend swings?
How does this month's spend compare to recent history?

formula_components.name: Monthly spend
formula_components.formula: Daily spend summed within each calendar month

formula_components.name: Month-over-month variance
formula_components.formula: (Current month spend − previous month spend) ÷ previous month spend × 100

budget-pacing:month_end_revenue.title: Month End Revenue
budget-pacing:month_end_revenue.formula: Projected month-end revenue = month-to-date revenue + (average daily revenue × days left), using paid-media conversion value. Current ROAS = period revenue ÷ period spend; projected ROAS = projected revenue ÷ projected spend.
budget-pacing:month_end_revenue.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month revenue from MART_BUDGET_PACING by adding the period's average daily revenue (CONVERSION_VALUE) for each remaining day to month-to-date revenue. Current and projected ROAS show whether efficiency is holding as spend scales toward month end.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total revenue for the current month at the present run rate.
Current ROAS is revenue divided by spend for the selected period — your efficiency right now.
Projected ROAS applies the same trajectory to month end; compare it to Current ROAS to see if returns are improving or eroding.
A projected ROAS below target signals the extra spend may not be paying off.
decisions_answered: How much revenue will this month likely close at?
Is our return on spend (ROAS) holding as we pace toward month end?
Are we forecast to hit our revenue goal at the current run rate?

formula_components.name: Projected revenue
formula_components.formula: Month-to-date revenue + (average daily revenue × days left)

budget-pacing:month_end_projection.title: Month End Projection
budget-pacing:month_end_projection.formula: Projected month-end spend. Simple projection = month-to-date spend + (average daily spend × days left). Weighted projection = MTD spend + (weekday average × weekdays left) + (weekend average × weekends left). Confidence range = MTD spend + ((average daily ± standard deviation) × days left).
budget-pacing:month_end_projection.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month spend from MART_BUDGET_PACING using multiple methods. The simple projection uses the period's overall daily average; the weighted projection accounts for distinct weekday-versus-weekend spending; the confidence range is built from the spend standard deviation to bound expected variance.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total spend for the current month at the present run rate.
The weighted projection is usually more accurate when weekday and weekend spend differ noticeably.
The confidence range (low to high) shows how much the final number could swing given recent volatility.
Compare the projection against the monthly budget to see whether you are tracking to over- or under-spend.
decisions_answered: How much will we spend by month end if nothing changes?
Are we projected to overspend or underspend the monthly budget?
How wide is the uncertainty around the month-end spend forecast?

formula_components.name: Simple projection
formula_components.formula: Month-to-date spend + (average daily spend × days left)

formula_components.name: Weighted projection
formula_components.formula: MTD spend + (weekday-average spend × weekdays left) + (weekend-average spend × weekends left)

formula_components.name: Confidence range
formula_components.formula: MTD spend + ((average daily spend ± standard deviation) × days left)

competitive:budget_opportunity.title: Budget Opportunity Analysis
competitive:budget_opportunity.formula: Table of high-ROAS Google Ads campaigns held back by weak placement. Potential top impressions = impressions × (0.9 − top-position rate ÷ 100). Estimated additional cost = cost-per-click × 1.2 × potential top impressions × CTR. Shown only for campaigns with top-position rate below 70% and ROAS above 2× (and 500+ impressions).
competitive:budget_opportunity.formula_explanation: Identifies high-ROAS campaigns held back by weak ad position (top position below 70%). It estimates the extra top-of-page impressions available if the campaign reached a 90% top-position rate, then prices the rough additional cost to capture them at a 20% higher CPC. Only campaigns with ROAS above 2x are shown so recommendations stay profitable.
how_to_read: Each row is a profitable campaign that is currently winning fewer top placements than it could.
Potential Top Impressions is the additional above-organic impressions available by lifting position to ~90%.
Est. Additional Cost is the rough spend needed to capture them — weigh it against the campaign's ROAS.
Higher current ROAS plus lower top-position rate means a stronger case to invest more.
decisions_answered: Which profitable campaigns are constrained by weak ad position?
How much extra would it cost to capture the missed top-of-page impressions?
Where should I invest more to grab profitable share?

competitive:budget_vs_rank.title: Ad Position by Campaign
competitive:budget_vs_rank.formula: Stacked bar of each campaign's below-top-position share = 100 − impression-weighted top-position rate (top-position rate weighted by impressions). Top 15 campaigns by below-top share with more than 100 impressions; metadata carries the top-position and absolute-top (#1) rates.
competitive:budget_vs_rank.formula_explanation: For each campaign this stacked bar shows the share of impressions that landed below top position (below organic results), computed as 100 minus the impression-weighted top-position rate. Hover metadata also carries the top-position and absolute-top (#1) rates. Campaigns are ranked by the largest below-top share to surface the weakest placements first.
how_to_read: Taller bars mean a larger share of impressions fell below top position — worse competitive placement.
The metadata splits each campaign into top-position and absolute-top (#1) rates.
Only campaigns with more than 100 impressions are shown; the top 15 by below-top share are ranked first.
Focus on the tallest bars first — those campaigns are losing the most visibility to placement.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are losing the most impressions to weak ad position?
How does placement strength vary across my campaigns?
Where should I improve bids or quality to climb above organic results?

formula_components.name: Impression-weighted top-position rate
formula_components.formula: Sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions

competitive:campaign_share.title: Campaign Ad Position Performance
competitive:campaign_share.formula: Table of the top 20 campaigns by impressions. Per campaign: impression-weighted top-position rate, absolute-top (#1) rate, below-top share = 100 − top-position rate, CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100, spend, and ROAS = conversion value ÷ spend.
competitive:campaign_share.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table of competitive and performance metrics per campaign from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE: impression-weighted top-position and absolute-top rates, below-top share, CTR, spend and ROAS. The top 20 campaigns by impressions are shown so you can sort to find opportunities.
how_to_read: Each row is one campaign with its position rates (top, absolute-top, below-top) alongside CTR, spend, and ROAS.
Higher top-position and lower below-top rates indicate stronger placement.
Sort by ROAS to find efficient campaigns, or by below-top to find placement problems.
Color coding flags healthy versus caution versus action-needed metrics.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns combine strong position with strong ROAS?
Where is weak placement dragging down otherwise profitable campaigns?
Which campaigns deserve more or less investment?

formula_components.name: Top-position rate
formula_components.formula: Sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions

competitive:channel_share.title: Ad Position by Channel Type
competitive:channel_share.formula: Bar chart of impression-weighted top-position rate per Google Ads channel type (Search, Shopping, Performance Max, Display). Each bar = sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions for that channel; channels ordered by total impressions.
competitive:channel_share.formula_explanation: Shows the impression-weighted average top-position rate for each Google Ads campaign type (Search, Shopping, Performance Max, Display) from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Channels are ordered by total impressions to highlight where most of your volume sits and how strong placement is there.
how_to_read: Each bar is a channel type; the value is the share of impressions in top position for that channel.
Taller bars mean that channel wins above-organic placement more often.
Compare channels to spot which formats hold position well versus which lag.
Channels are ordered by total impressions, so the leftmost typically carry the most volume.
decisions_answered: Which Google Ads channel types win top placement most often?
Where is placement weakest across my channel mix?
Should I shift effort toward channels with stronger position?

competitive:performance_correlation.title: Position vs Performance
competitive:performance_correlation.formula: Scatter plot of campaigns: x = impression-weighted top-position rate, y = ROAS = conversion value ÷ spend. Quadrant lines are the averages of top-position rate and ROAS across the shown campaigns (impressions > 1000, spend > 0).
competitive:performance_correlation.formula_explanation: Scatter plot mapping each campaign by competitive position (impression-weighted top-position rate on the X-axis) against efficiency (ROAS on the Y-axis). Quadrant boundaries are the averages of both axes across the displayed campaigns (impressions > 1000, spend > 0), so points sort into stars, opportunities, and laggards.
how_to_read: X-axis is top-position rate (placement strength); Y-axis is ROAS (efficiency).
Top-right campaigns are stars — strong position and strong returns.
Bottom-left campaigns lag on both and need attention.
High-ROAS but low-position points (top-left) are prime candidates to push for more placement.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are my stars (strong position and strong ROAS)?
Which efficient campaigns could win more by improving position?
Which campaigns underperform on both fronts and need rework?

formula_components.name: Top-position rate (x)
formula_components.formula: Sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions

competitive:wow_change.title: Week-over-Week Ad Position Change
competitive:wow_change.formula: Bar chart of weekly impression-weighted top-position rate with week-over-week change. Per week: top-position rate = sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions; WoW change = this week's rate − last week's rate. Up to the last 8 weeks, most recent first.
competitive:wow_change.formula_explanation: Aggregates impressions to weekly buckets and tracks the impression-weighted top-position rate week over week from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. The WoW change is the point difference versus the prior week, showing whether competitive placement is improving or slipping over the last several weeks.
how_to_read: Each bar is one week's average top-position rate.
WoW Change shows the point movement versus the previous week — positive means placement improved.
A run of declining bars signals competitors are gaining ground on placement.
Up to the last 8 weeks are shown, most recent first.
decisions_answered: Is our ad placement improving or slipping week over week?
Did a recent bid or budget change move our position?
Are competitors gaining ground on top-of-page placement?

formula_components.name: Weekly top-position rate
formula_components.formula: Sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions per week

formula_components.name: Week-over-week change
formula_components.formula: Current week top-position rate − previous week top-position rate

competitive:impressions.title: Impressions
competitive:impressions.formula: Impressions = total ad impressions across all campaigns over the period (Google Search)
competitive:impressions.formula_explanation: Total number of times your ads were shown across all campaigns in the selected period, summed from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Higher impressions indicate greater visibility and reach in the market.
how_to_read: The headline value is total ad impressions for the selected date range.
The change versus the comparison period shows whether reach is growing or shrinking.
Read it alongside top-position rate — high impressions at weak position still means lost visibility.
Falling impressions can signal lost eligibility, lower budgets, or tighter targeting.
decisions_answered: How much overall ad visibility did we have this period?
Is our reach growing or shrinking versus the prior period?
Do volume changes line up with budget or targeting changes?

competitive:top_position.title: Top Position Rate
competitive:top_position.formula: Top-position rate card: impression-weighted percentage of impressions appearing above organic results = sum of (top-position rate × impressions) ÷ total impressions across the period, with a weekly trend sparkline. Metadata also carries the absolute-top (#1) rate.
competitive:top_position.formula_explanation: Impression-weighted percentage of impressions where your ads appeared in top position (above the organic search results), computed from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Higher rates indicate stronger competitive positioning. The card also surfaces the absolute-top (#1) rate in metadata.
how_to_read: The value is the share of impressions that landed above organic results.
Higher is better — it means your ads are winning prominent placement.
The sparkline shows the weekly trend of top-position rate over the period.
Compare against absolute-top rate to see how often top placement is also the #1 spot.
decisions_answered: How often do our ads appear above organic results?
Is our competitive placement strengthening or weakening over time?
How much of our top placement is actually the #1 position?

competitive:budget_lost.title: Below Top Position
competitive:budget_lost.formula: Below Top Position % = 100 − top-position rate, where top-position rate = impression-weighted average of the share of impressions landing above organic results
competitive:budget_lost.formula_explanation: Percentage of impressions that did NOT land in top position (i.e. below organic results), computed as 100 minus the impression-weighted top-position rate from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Lower is better — it indicates stronger ad placement. Note: the backend metric id is below_top; the absence of true impression-share data means this is a top-position proxy, not a budget-lost-impression-share figure.
how_to_read: The value is the share of impressions that fell below top position.
Lower is better — a high value means most impressions are losing prominent placement.
Track the change versus the comparison period; a rising value means placement is eroding.
Pair it with top-position rate; together they account for all impressions.
decisions_answered: What share of our impressions are stuck below top position?
Is our weak-placement share growing or shrinking?
How much visibility are we losing to poor ad placement?

competitive:rank_lost.title: Position Gap
competitive:rank_lost.formula: Position Gap % = top-position rate − absolute-top (#1) rate (both impression-weighted)
competitive:rank_lost.formula_explanation: The gap between the top-position rate and the absolute-top (#1) rate, computed from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. It represents impressions that appeared above organic results but not in the #1 spot — the opportunity to climb from 'above organic' to the top slot. Note: the backend metric id is position_gap; this is a top-position proxy, not a rank-lost-impression-share figure.
how_to_read: The value is the percentage of impressions in top position but not at #1.
A larger gap means more room to push from above-organic into the absolute top spot.
It is a neutral metric — neither inherently good nor bad — read it as headroom.
Compare with absolute-top rate to judge how aggressively to bid for #1.
decisions_answered: How much headroom is there to move from above-organic to the #1 position?
Are we capturing top placement but missing the #1 spot?
Is it worth bidding up to close the gap to absolute top?

creative-performance:total_spend.title: Total Creative Spend
creative-performance:total_spend.formula: Total Creative Spend = Google creative spend + Meta creative spend over the period
creative-performance:total_spend.formula_explanation: Total advertising spend across Google Ads and Meta Ads creatives for the selected period, with a Google/Meta split. Platform filtering scopes the sum to a single channel.
how_to_read: The headline value is combined Google + Meta creative spend for the date range.
Compare against the previous period to see whether creative investment is rising or falling.
Use the platform filter to isolate Google-only or Meta-only spend.
Read alongside Avg ROAS to judge whether higher spend is producing proportionate returns.
decisions_answered: How much am I investing in creative across paid channels?
Is my creative spend trending up or down vs the prior period?
Which platform is absorbing the larger share of creative budget?

formula_components.name: Google Spend
formula_components.formula: sum of Google Ads creative spend

formula_components.name: Meta Spend
formula_components.formula: sum of Meta Ads creative spend

creative-performance:active_ads.title: Active Ads
creative-performance:active_ads.formula: Active Ads = count of distinct ad IDs that served ≥1 impression in the period (Google + Meta combined)
creative-performance:active_ads.formula_explanation: Number of unique ads that served at least one impression during the period across Google and Meta. An ad with zero impressions is not counted as active.
how_to_read: The value is the count of distinct ad IDs that received impressions in the date range.
A growing count means more creatives are live; a shrinking count may mean ads are being paused or consolidated.
Filter by platform to see how many active creatives exist on Google vs Meta.
Pair with Total Spend to see average spend per active ad.
decisions_answered: How many creatives are actively running right now?
Has the size of my active creative library changed vs the prior period?
Do I have enough creative variety in market across platforms?

creative-performance:blended_ctr.title: Blended CTR
creative-performance:blended_ctr.formula: Blended CTR = total clicks ÷ total impressions × 100 (impression-weighted across Google + Meta)
creative-performance:blended_ctr.formula_explanation: Impression-weighted average click-through rate across Google and Meta creatives. Because it weights by impressions, high-volume ads dominate the figure rather than a simple average of per-ad CTRs.
how_to_read: The value is total clicks divided by total impressions, expressed as a percentage.
It is impression-weighted, so it reflects how the bulk of your delivered impressions actually performed.
Compare to the prior period to detect overall creative engagement gains or declines.
Filter by platform to compare Google CTR against Meta CTR on equal footing.
decisions_answered: How engaging is my creative overall across paid channels?
Is blended engagement improving or eroding over time?
Which platform's creatives earn the higher click-through rate?

creative-performance:avg_roas.title: Average ROAS
creative-performance:avg_roas.formula: Average ROAS = total conversion value ÷ total spend (Google + Meta, spend-weighted)
creative-performance:avg_roas.formula_explanation: Return on ad spend for creatives, computed as total conversion value divided by total spend across Google and Meta. Note: this uses platform-reported revenue, which is typically 20-50% higher than actual Magento revenue.
how_to_read: A value of 3.0 means every $1 of creative spend returned $3 of platform-reported conversion value.
It is a spend-weighted ratio, not an average of per-ad ROAS values.
Treat the figure as directional: platform-attributed revenue overstates true store revenue.
Compare to the prior period to see whether creative efficiency is improving.
decisions_answered: Are my creatives generating a positive return on spend?
Is creative efficiency trending in the right direction?
Should I shift budget toward higher-ROAS creative formats or platforms?

audience-performance:audience_spend.title: Audience Spend
audience-performance:audience_spend.formula: Audience Spend = total spend on audience-targeted campaigns over the period (Google + Meta)
audience-performance:audience_spend.formula_explanation: Total advertising spend on audience-targeted campaigns across Google Ads and Meta Ads for the selected period. A daily-spend sparkline accompanies the headline value.
how_to_read: The headline is total spend across all targeted audiences for the date range.
The sparkline shows the daily spend trajectory within the period.
Compare against the prior period to see whether audience-targeting investment is rising or falling.
Read with Average ROAS to judge whether the spend is producing efficient returns.
decisions_answered: How much am I spending on audience-targeted campaigns?
Is audience spend trending up or down vs the prior period?
Are there daily spikes or dips in audience spend I should investigate?

audience-performance:active_audiences.title: Active Audiences
audience-performance:active_audiences.formula: Active Audiences = count of distinct audience IDs with activity in the period (Google ad groups + Meta ad sets)
audience-performance:active_audiences.formula_explanation: Count of distinct audiences that recorded activity during the period across Google and Meta. Each unique audience ID with data in the date range is counted once.
how_to_read: The value is the number of distinct audience IDs active in the date range.
A larger count means broader audience coverage; a smaller count means a more concentrated targeting strategy.
Use alongside Audience Spend to gauge average investment per active audience.
Track over time to see whether your audience portfolio is expanding or consolidating.
decisions_answered: How many distinct audiences am I actively targeting?
Is my audience portfolio broadening or narrowing?
Am I spreading budget across too many or too few audiences?

audience-performance:audience_conversion.title: Audience Conversions
audience-performance:audience_conversion.formula: Audience Conversions = sum of conversions credited to audience-targeted campaigns over the period (Google + Meta)
audience-performance:audience_conversion.formula_explanation: Total conversions attributed to audience-targeted campaigns across Google and Meta for the period. Total attributed revenue (SUM of CONVERSION_VALUE) is carried alongside as supporting context.
how_to_read: The value is the sum of conversions credited to targeted audiences in the date range.
Compare to the prior period to see whether audience targeting is driving more outcomes.
Read with Audience Spend to understand cost per audience conversion.
Supporting revenue context shows the conversion value those conversions generated.
decisions_answered: How many conversions are my audience-targeted campaigns producing?
Are audience conversions growing vs the prior period?
Which targeting effort is converting most efficiently relative to spend?

audience-performance:average_frequency.title: Average Frequency
audience-performance:average_frequency.formula: Average Frequency = Meta impressions ÷ Meta reach (Meta only)
audience-performance:average_frequency.formula_explanation: Average number of times each reached user saw an ad, computed from Meta impressions divided by Meta reach. The optimal range is roughly 1.5-3.0x; above 5.0x is flagged critical and below 1.0x or above 3.0x is a warning.
how_to_read: A value of 2.0 means the average reached user saw your ads twice in the period (Meta only).
Values in the 1.5-3.0x band are healthy; very high frequency signals audience fatigue.
Above 5.0x is critical and below 1.0x or above 3.0x carries a warning status.
Read with the saturation curves to see how rising frequency erodes CTR.
decisions_answered: Are users seeing my Meta ads too often (creative fatigue risk)?
Is my frequency within the healthy targeting range?
Do I need to expand reach or refresh creative to lower frequency?

audience-performance:total_reach.title: Total Reach
audience-performance:total_reach.formula: Total Reach = sum of daily Meta reach over the period (Meta only; approximate, daily reach can overlap across days)
audience-performance:total_reach.formula_explanation: Estimated unique users reached via Meta Ads in the period. This is approximate because daily reach values can overlap across days, so summing them may double-count some users.
how_to_read: The value is the summed Meta reach for the date range (Meta only).
Treat it as an approximate ceiling on unique users, since daily reach can overlap.
A daily-average reach figure is provided as supporting context.
Read with Average Frequency to balance breadth of reach against repetition.
decisions_answered: How many unique users am I reaching on Meta?
Is my reach growing or shrinking vs the prior period?
Do I have enough reach to support my frequency goals?

audience-performance:average_roas.title: Average ROAS
audience-performance:average_roas.formula: Average ROAS = total conversion value ÷ total spend across audience-targeted campaigns (Google + Meta, spend-weighted)
audience-performance:average_roas.formula_explanation: Return on ad spend for audience-targeted campaigns, computed as total conversion value divided by total spend across Google and Meta. Note: this uses platform-reported revenue, typically 20-50% higher than actual Magento revenue. Benchmark is 3.0x.
how_to_read: A value of 3.0 means each $1 spent on audiences returned $3 of platform-reported conversion value.
It is a spend-weighted ratio across all targeted audiences, not an average of per-audience ROAS.
The 3.0x benchmark is a reference point for healthy efficiency.
Treat the figure as directional, since platform-attributed revenue overstates true store revenue.
decisions_answered: Are my audience-targeted campaigns returning a positive ROAS?
Is audience efficiency above or below the 3.0x benchmark?
Should I reallocate budget toward higher-ROAS audiences?

funnel:total_revenue.title: Total Revenue
funnel:total_revenue.formula: Total Revenue = sum of Magento net revenue over the period
funnel:total_revenue.formula_explanation: Total revenue generated from purchases over the selected period, summed from the daily funnel mart. The trend compares against the prior equivalent period.
how_to_read: The headline figure is the sum of revenue across all days in the selected range.
The change indicator compares this period's revenue against the previous period of the same length.
The sparkline shows daily revenue, revealing spikes (promotions, launches) and dips.
Revenue here is sourced from the funnel mart (MART_FUNNEL_DAILY), aligning with the rest of the funnel view.
decisions_answered: How much revenue did we generate this period versus last?
Are there specific days driving most of the revenue?
Is revenue trending up or down over time?

funnel:aov.title: Average Order Value
funnel:aov.formula: Average Order Value = total Magento revenue ÷ number of Magento orders
funnel:aov.formula_explanation: Average Order Value divides total revenue by the count of Magento orders for the period. Magento orders (rather than GA4 purchases) are used for financial accuracy.
how_to_read: The value is total revenue divided by the number of completed Magento orders.
A rising AOV means customers are spending more per transaction (bundling, upsells, premium mix).
The sparkline tracks daily AOV, which can be volatile on low-order days.
Compare against the previous period via the change indicator to spot pricing or mix shifts.
decisions_answered: How much does a typical order bring in?
Is AOV improving, suggesting effective upsell or pricing changes?
Should we focus on raising order value or order volume?

funnel:conversion_rate.title: Conversion Rate
funnel:conversion_rate.formula: Conversion Rate = GA4 purchases ÷ GA4 sessions × 100
funnel:conversion_rate.formula_explanation: Overall site conversion rate: the percentage of sessions that ended in a purchase. Purchases are GA4 unique purchasers for funnel consistency.
how_to_read: The value is total purchases divided by total sessions, expressed as a percentage.
Higher is better: more visitors are completing a purchase.
The sparkline shows the daily conversion rate, useful for spotting outage days or campaign effects.
The change indicator compares against the previous period of the same length.
decisions_answered: What share of visitors actually purchase?
Is our conversion efficiency improving or declining?
Did a site change or campaign move the conversion rate?

funnel:purchase_reconciliation.title: Purchase Reconciliation
funnel:purchase_reconciliation.formula: Purchase Reconciliation Delta = GA4 purchases − Magento orders; Gap % = delta ÷ Magento orders × 100
funnel:purchase_reconciliation.formula_explanation: Measures the gap between GA4-tracked purchases and Magento orders for the period. A non-zero delta flags tracking discrepancies between analytics and the order system.
how_to_read: The value is GA4 purchases minus Magento orders; positive means GA4 over-counts, negative means it under-counts.
The percentage gap contextualizes the delta relative to actual Magento orders.
A delta near zero indicates analytics and order data agree (healthy tracking).
The sparkline plots the daily GA4-minus-Magento difference to localize when drift occurs.
decisions_answered: Do GA4 purchases and Magento orders agree, or is tracking off?
How large is the analytics-to-orders gap this period?
Which days show the biggest tracking discrepancies?

campaign-matrix:quadrant_summary.title: Quadrant Summary
campaign-matrix:quadrant_summary.formula: Four summary cards (Scale, Scale Opportunity, Optimize/Cut, Test/Kill) rolling up the campaign matrix: campaign count, total spend, and average ROAS per quadrant. Quadrants assigned by median spend and median ROAS of campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
campaign-matrix:quadrant_summary.formula_explanation: Four summary cards roll up the campaign matrix by quadrant, showing how many campaigns, how much spend, and the average ROAS sit in each. Quadrant boundaries use the median spend and median ROAS of campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
how_to_read: Each card represents one quadrant: Scale, Scale Opportunity, Optimize/Cut, Test/Kill.
Campaign count shows how concentrated your portfolio is in each performance zone.
Total spend exposes how much budget is committed to each quadrant - watch Optimize/Cut.
Average ROAS summarizes the efficiency of campaigns within that quadrant.
decisions_answered: How is spend distributed across performance quadrants?
How many campaigns are winners versus underperformers?
Which quadrant holds the most budget and at what efficiency?

formula_components.name: Campaign count
formula_components.formula: number of campaigns in the quadrant

formula_components.name: Total spend
formula_components.formula: sum of spend of campaigns in the quadrant

formula_components.name: Average ROAS
formula_components.formula: average of campaign ROAS in the quadrant

campaign-matrix:winners.title: Top Performing Campaigns
campaign-matrix:winners.formula: Bars of current ROAS for up to five top-performing campaigns — 'Top Winner' (ROAS at least 5 and spend at least $10,000) or 'Winner' (ROAS at least 3 and spend at least $5,000) — ranked by spend, with ROAS change versus the prior period.
campaign-matrix:winners.formula_explanation: Surfaces the highest-performing campaigns - those combining strong ROAS with meaningful spend. Campaigns are labeled Top Winner or Winner by ROAS and spend thresholds, then the top five by spend are shown.
how_to_read: Each bar is a campaign's current ROAS; only Top Winners and Winners qualify.
Qualification requires both high ROAS and substantial spend, so these are proven at scale.
ROAS change versus the prior period indicates whether a winner is still accelerating.
Up to five campaigns are shown, ordered by spend (largest committed budget first).
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are our proven, high-spend winners?
Are our top performers still improving period over period?
Where is high-efficiency spend already concentrated?

campaign-matrix:hidden_gems.title: Hidden Gems
campaign-matrix:hidden_gems.formula: Bars of current ROAS for up to five 'Hidden Gem' campaigns — those with ROAS at least 5 and spend under $5,000 (high efficiency, low spend) — ranked by spend, with ROAS change versus the prior period.
campaign-matrix:hidden_gems.formula_explanation: Identifies high-efficiency campaigns that are under-invested: strong ROAS but low spend. These are the clearest candidates for additional budget.
how_to_read: Each bar shows a campaign's current ROAS; all qualify as Hidden Gems (ROAS >= 5x, spend < $5,000).
Low spend with high ROAS means there is likely room to scale before efficiency drops.
ROAS change versus the prior period helps confirm the efficiency is stable, not a fluke.
Up to five gems are shown - prioritize testing budget increases here.
decisions_answered: Which efficient campaigns are we under-funding?
Where can incremental budget likely earn the highest return?
Are these gems consistently efficient or just lucky this period?

campaign-matrix:needs_attention.title: Needs Attention
campaign-matrix:needs_attention.formula: Bars of current ROAS for up to five low-efficiency, high-spend campaigns — 'Top Loser' (ROAS under 1 and spend at least $10,000) or 'Loser' (ROAS under 2 and spend at least $5,000) — ranked by spend, with ROAS change versus the prior period.
campaign-matrix:needs_attention.formula_explanation: Flags campaigns burning budget inefficiently: low ROAS combined with high spend. These require immediate optimization or budget reduction.
how_to_read: Each bar shows a campaign's current ROAS; only Losers and Top Losers (low ROAS, high spend) appear.
These campaigns are actively wasting budget - the larger the spend, the higher the urgency.
ROAS change versus the prior period shows whether the campaign is deteriorating further.
Up to five are shown, ordered by spend so the biggest budget leaks surface first.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are wasting the most budget?
Where should we cut or optimize spend immediately?
Are underperformers getting worse over time?

campaign-matrix:active_campaigns.title: Active Campaigns
campaign-matrix:active_campaigns.formula: Active Campaigns = count of distinct campaigns with any activity (spend, revenue, impressions, or clicks) in the period, across Google + Meta
campaign-matrix:active_campaigns.formula_explanation: Total count of distinct campaigns with activity in the period across Google Ads, Meta Ads, and Klaviyo. Only campaigns with spend, revenue, impressions, or clicks are counted.
how_to_read: The value is the number of distinct campaigns active in the selected period.
The description breaks the count down by channel (Google, Meta, Klaviyo).
The change indicator compares the campaign count against the previous period.
A growing count can mean expanded testing; a shrinking count can mean consolidation or pausing.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns are we running this period?
How is activity split across Google, Meta, and Klaviyo?
Is our campaign footprint growing or contracting?

campaign-matrix:total_spend.title: Total Spend
campaign-matrix:total_spend.formula: Total Spend = Google Ads spend + Meta Ads spend over the period
campaign-matrix:total_spend.formula_explanation: Combined advertising spend across paid channels (Google Ads and Meta Ads) for the period. The description splits the total into Google and Meta components.
how_to_read: The headline is total paid media spend for the selected period.
The description shows the Google versus Meta split of that spend.
The sparkline tracks daily spend, exposing pacing and burst patterns.
Compare against the previous period via the change indicator to monitor budget growth.
decisions_answered: How much did we spend on paid media this period?
How is spend split between Google and Meta?
Is spend pacing up or down versus the prior period?

campaign-matrix:average_roas.title: Average ROAS
campaign-matrix:average_roas.formula: Average ROAS = total platform-reported revenue ÷ total ad spend (Google + Meta); also surfaces an actual ROAS using Magento revenue
campaign-matrix:average_roas.formula_explanation: Weighted (blended) return on ad spend: platform-reported revenue divided by total spend. The metric also surfaces an 'actual' ROAS using Magento revenue, and the gap between platform and Magento revenue.
how_to_read: The headline is platform-reported revenue divided by total ad spend (a multiplier).
A value of 3.0x means three dollars of attributed revenue per dollar spent.
The description compares platform revenue to Magento revenue, flagging attribution inflation.
The sparkline tracks daily blended ROAS for pacing and volatility.
decisions_answered: What return are we getting on every ad dollar?
How does platform-attributed ROAS compare to actual (Magento) ROAS?
Is overall efficiency improving or declining?

formula_components.name: Blended ROAS
formula_components.formula: total platform-reported revenue ÷ total ad spend

formula_components.name: Actual ROAS
formula_components.formula: total Magento store revenue ÷ total ad spend

campaign-matrix:budget_at_risk.title: Budget at Risk
campaign-matrix:budget_at_risk.formula: Budget at Risk = total spend on campaigns in the Optimize/Cut quadrant (high spend, low ROAS); % of Total = budget at risk ÷ total spend × 100
campaign-matrix:budget_at_risk.formula_explanation: The dollar amount of spend sitting in the Optimize/Cut quadrant (high spend, low ROAS). This is budget being spent inefficiently that could be reallocated.
how_to_read: The value is total spend on campaigns classified as Optimize/Cut (above-median spend, below-median ROAS).
The description shows how many campaigns are at risk and what share of total spend they represent.
Higher values mean more budget is being deployed inefficiently.
Treat this as the reallocation opportunity: money that could move to Scale or Hidden Gem campaigns.
decisions_answered: How much budget is being spent inefficiently right now?
What share of total spend is at risk in the Optimize/Cut quadrant?
How much could be freed up for reallocation to winners?

formula_components.name: Budget at Risk
formula_components.formula: sum of spend for campaigns classified Optimize/Cut (spend ≥ median spend and ROAS < median ROAS)

campaign-matrix:scale.title: Scale Campaigns
campaign-matrix:scale.formula: Scale Campaigns = count of campaigns with spend ≥ median spend and ROAS ≥ median ROAS (medians taken over campaigns with positive spend and ROAS)
campaign-matrix:scale.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Scale quadrant (High spend, high ROAS). Campaigns that are both high-spending and high-performing - proven winners that are prime candidates for additional budget. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Scale.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Scale quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Scale group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

campaign-matrix:scale_opportunity.title: Scale Opportunity Campaigns
campaign-matrix:scale_opportunity.formula: Scale Opportunity Campaigns = count of campaigns with spend < median spend and ROAS ≥ median ROAS (also includes campaigns with zero spend but positive revenue); medians over campaigns with positive spend and ROAS
campaign-matrix:scale_opportunity.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Scale Opportunity quadrant (Low spend, high ROAS). Efficient but under-invested campaigns - high ROAS with low spend, representing untapped growth potential. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Scale Opportunity.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Scale Opportunity quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Scale Opportunity group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

campaign-matrix:optimize_cut.title: Optimize/Cut Campaigns
campaign-matrix:optimize_cut.formula: Optimize/Cut Campaigns = count of campaigns with spend ≥ median spend and ROAS < median ROAS (medians taken over campaigns with positive spend and ROAS)
campaign-matrix:optimize_cut.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Optimize/Cut quadrant (High spend, low ROAS). Campaigns spending heavily with poor returns - they require immediate optimization or budget reduction. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Optimize/Cut.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Optimize/Cut quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Optimize/Cut group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

campaign-matrix:test_kill.title: Test/Kill Campaigns
campaign-matrix:test_kill.formula: Test/Kill Campaigns = count of campaigns with spend < median spend and ROAS < median ROAS (medians taken over campaigns with positive spend and ROAS)
campaign-matrix:test_kill.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Test/Kill quadrant (Low spend, low ROAS). Campaigns with both low spend and low ROAS - either early tests not yet optimized or underperformers to pause. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Test/Kill.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Test/Kill quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Test/Kill group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

marketing-mix:total_spend.title: Total Marketing Spend
marketing-mix:total_spend.formula: Total Marketing Spend = Google Ads spend + Meta Ads spend (excludes Klaviyo)
marketing-mix:total_spend.formula_explanation: Sum of advertising spend across Google Ads and Meta Ads only (Klaviyo/email is excluded). This is the paid-media budget the rest of the dashboard's efficiency metrics are measured against.
how_to_read: The headline value is the combined Google + Meta spend for the selected date range.
The sparkline shows daily spend (Google + Meta) so you can spot ramps, pauses, or anomalies.
Use the comparison period to see whether spend is trending up or down versus the prior window.
This figure deliberately excludes non-ad channels, so it will not match a full P&L marketing line.
decisions_answered: How much am I currently investing in paid media across Google and Meta?
Is my paid-media budget growing, shrinking, or holding steady?
Are there daily spikes or drops in spend that need investigation?

marketing-mix:blended_roas.title: Blended ROAS
marketing-mix:blended_roas.formula: Blended ROAS = GA4-attributed paid revenue (Google + Meta sources) ÷ (Google spend + Meta spend)
marketing-mix:blended_roas.formula_explanation: Return on ad spend measured against GA4-attributed revenue from Google and Meta paid sources rather than the platforms' self-reported revenue. Because GA4 last-click is more conservative, this typically reads lower than platform-reported ROAS but is closer to actual performance.
how_to_read: A value of 3.0 means every $1 of Google + Meta spend returned $3 of GA4-attributed paid revenue.
Revenue comes from GA4 (medium cpc/paid for google + facebook/ig sources), not from Google/Meta's own reporting.
Higher is better; values below 1.0 mean paid media is losing money on a GA4-attributed basis.
The sparkline shows daily ROAS so you can see volatility within the period.
decisions_answered: Is my paid media profitable on a conservative (GA4) attribution basis?
How efficient is my blended Google + Meta spend right now?
Is paid-media efficiency improving or deteriorating over the period?

marketing-mix:mer.title: Marketing Efficiency Ratio
marketing-mix:mer.formula: Marketing Efficiency Ratio = total Magento store revenue ÷ (Google spend + Meta spend)
marketing-mix:mer.formula_explanation: Marketing Efficiency Ratio measures total store revenue (Magento, the source of truth) against total ad spend. Unlike ROAS, which only counts paid-attributed revenue, MER captures the holistic impact of marketing on the whole business including organic and direct halo effects.
how_to_read: A MER of 4.0 means the business generated $4 of Magento revenue for every $1 of Google + Meta spend.
Numerator is total Magento store revenue, not just paid-attributed revenue, so MER is usually higher than ROAS.
Rising MER alongside flat spend signals marketing is lifting the whole business efficiently.
The sparkline shows daily MER computed from daily Magento revenue divided by daily spend.
decisions_answered: How efficiently is marketing driving total business revenue, not just attributed revenue?
Is overall marketing efficiency improving as I scale spend?
How does whole-business efficiency (MER) compare to channel-attributed efficiency (ROAS)?

marketing-mix:total_revenue.title: Total Paid Revenue
marketing-mix:total_revenue.formula: Total Paid Revenue = sum of GA4 revenue for all sessions whose medium is cpc or paid
marketing-mix:total_revenue.formula_explanation: Total GA4-attributed revenue from all paid traffic sources (any source where the session medium is cpc or paid). This is the revenue side of paid-media performance and feeds the blended ROAS calculation.
how_to_read: This sums GA4 revenue for every session whose medium is cpc or paid, across all paid sources.
It is broader than blended ROAS revenue, which is restricted to Google + Meta paid sources only.
The sparkline shows daily GA4 paid revenue so you can spot which days drove the total.
Compare against Total Marketing Spend to gauge the gross return from paid traffic.
decisions_answered: How much revenue is GA4 attributing to paid traffic in this period?
Which days contributed the most paid-attributed revenue?
Is paid-traffic revenue growing relative to the comparison period?

marketing-mix:total_conversions.title: Total Conversions
marketing-mix:total_conversions.formula: Total Conversions = sum of platform-reported conversions across Google + Meta
marketing-mix:total_conversions.formula_explanation: The total number of conversions reported by the ad platforms (Google + Meta) for the selected period. These are platform self-reported and feed the Average CPA calculation.
how_to_read: This counts platform-reported conversions, summed across Google and Meta.
Platform-reported conversions can include view-through and modeled conversions, so they may exceed GA4 last-click counts.
The sparkline shows daily conversion volume to reveal pacing within the period.
Pair with Total Marketing Spend to derive cost per acquisition.
decisions_answered: How many conversions did paid media drive this period?
Is conversion volume trending up or down versus the prior period?
Which days produced the most conversions?

marketing-mix:average_cpa.title: Average CPA
marketing-mix:average_cpa.formula: Average CPA = (Google spend + Meta spend) ÷ total platform-reported conversions
marketing-mix:average_cpa.formula_explanation: Average Cost Per Acquisition is total paid spend divided by total platform-reported conversions. Lower is better. Note the trend is inverted on the card: a rising CPA is flagged as a negative trend.
how_to_read: A CPA of $40 means it costs $40 of Google + Meta spend to generate one platform-reported conversion.
Lower CPA means more efficient acquisition; sustained increases warrant investigation.
The trend arrow is inverted (down/green when CPA falls, up/red when CPA rises).
The sparkline shows daily CPA computed as daily spend divided by daily conversions.
decisions_answered: How much am I paying to acquire each conversion right now?
Is my cost of acquisition getting cheaper or more expensive?
Are there days where acquisition cost spiked abnormally?

marketing-mix:revenue_reconciliation.title: Revenue Reconciliation
marketing-mix:revenue_reconciliation.formula: Revenue Reconciliation headline = total Magento store revenue (source of truth), with gap percentages versus platform-reported and GA4-attributed revenue
marketing-mix:revenue_reconciliation.formula_explanation: Reconciles three revenue views: platform self-reported revenue, GA4-attributed paid revenue, and actual Magento store revenue. Magento is treated as the source of truth, and the gap percentages quantify how much platform and GA4 figures over- or under-state real revenue.
how_to_read: The headline value is actual Magento store revenue for the period.
Platform vs Magento gap shows how much Google/Meta self-reported revenue exceeds real revenue (usually inflated).
GA4 vs Magento gap shows how GA4 paid revenue compares to real revenue.
Large positive gaps mean platform/GA4 numbers should be trusted less for budgeting decisions.
decisions_answered: How much are the ad platforms over-reporting revenue versus my actual store sales?
Which revenue source should I trust for budgeting and forecasting?
How big is the attribution gap between reported and actual revenue?

formula_components.name: Headline (Magento)
formula_components.formula: total Magento store revenue over the period

formula_components.name: GA4 vs Magento Gap %
formula_components.formula: (GA4 paid revenue − Magento revenue) ÷ Magento revenue × 100

attribution:total_revenue.title: Total GA4 Revenue
attribution:total_revenue.formula: Total GA4 Revenue = sum of GA4 revenue across all traffic sources and channels
attribution:total_revenue.formula_explanation: Sum of all GA4-tracked revenue across every traffic source and channel (paid, organic, direct, referral, email, etc.) for the selected period. This is the baseline against which channel-level attribution gaps are measured.
how_to_read: This totals GA4 revenue across all channels, not just paid traffic.
It is the denominator context for share metrics like Direct Traffic Share.
The sparkline shows daily GA4 total revenue across the period.
Use it as the all-channel revenue baseline when comparing platform-reported figures.
decisions_answered: What is my total GA4-tracked revenue across all channels this period?
Is overall tracked revenue growing or shrinking?
What baseline should I measure channel attribution gaps against?

attribution:google_gap.title: Google Attribution Gap
attribution:google_gap.formula: Google Attribution Gap = Google platform-reported revenue − GA4 Google revenue; Gap % = gap ÷ GA4 Google revenue × 100
attribution:google_gap.formula_explanation: The dollar difference between what Google Ads self-reports as revenue and what GA4 attributes to Google. The gap percentage is expressed relative to GA4 Google revenue. A positive gap means Google over-reports versus GA4 last-click, driven by view-through, cross-device, and wider attribution windows.
how_to_read: The headline value is the absolute dollar gap (Google platform revenue minus GA4 Google revenue).
The percentage is the gap divided by GA4 Google revenue, not by platform revenue.
A larger positive gap means Google's self-reported numbers are more inflated relative to GA4.
The card trends down/negative when the gap is positive, since over-reporting is undesirable.
decisions_answered: How much is Google Ads over-reporting revenue versus GA4?
Can I trust Google's self-reported ROAS for budget decisions?
Is the Google attribution gap widening over time?

formula_components.name: Google Gap
formula_components.formula: Google platform-reported revenue − GA4 Google revenue

attribution:meta_gap.title: Meta Attribution Gap
attribution:meta_gap.formula: Meta Attribution Gap = Meta platform-reported revenue − GA4 Meta revenue; Gap % = gap ÷ GA4 Meta revenue × 100
attribution:meta_gap.formula_explanation: The dollar difference between Meta (Facebook/Instagram) self-reported revenue and GA4-attributed Meta revenue, with the gap percentage expressed relative to GA4 Meta revenue. Meta typically shows larger gaps than Google because of its 7-day-click / 1-day-view attribution windows that GA4 last-click does not capture.
how_to_read: The headline value is the absolute dollar gap (Meta platform revenue minus GA4 Meta revenue).
The percentage is the gap divided by GA4 Meta revenue, not by platform revenue.
Meta gaps are usually wider than Google gaps due to view-through and longer attribution windows.
The card trends down/negative when the gap is positive, since over-reporting is undesirable.
decisions_answered: How much is Meta over-reporting revenue versus GA4?
Should I discount Meta's self-reported ROAS when allocating budget?
Is the Meta attribution gap larger than Google's?

formula_components.name: Meta Gap
formula_components.formula: Meta platform-reported revenue − GA4 Meta revenue

attribution:klaviyo_gap.title: Klaviyo Attribution Gap
attribution:klaviyo_gap.formula: Klaviyo Attribution Gap = Klaviyo self-reported revenue − GA4 email revenue; Gap % = gap ÷ GA4 email revenue × 100
attribution:klaviyo_gap.formula_explanation: The difference between revenue Klaviyo claims for email/SMS and the email revenue GA4 attributes. The daily gap is pre-computed in the mart and summed; the percentage is relative to GA4 email revenue. A positive gap means Klaviyo over-reports email's contribution versus GA4.
how_to_read: The headline value is the summed daily Klaviyo gap (Klaviyo self-reported minus GA4 email revenue).
The percentage is the gap divided by GA4 email revenue.
A positive gap means email's self-reported revenue is inflated relative to GA4 attribution.
The card trends down/negative when the gap is positive.
decisions_answered: How much is Klaviyo over-crediting email/SMS revenue versus GA4?
Can I trust Klaviyo's revenue numbers for the email channel?
Is the email attribution gap growing?

formula_components.name: Klaviyo Gap
formula_components.formula: Klaviyo self-reported revenue − GA4 email revenue

attribution:ga4_magento_gap.title: GA4 vs Magento Gap
attribution:ga4_magento_gap.formula: GA4 vs Magento Gap = total GA4 revenue − Magento store revenue; Gap % = gap ÷ Magento revenue × 100
attribution:ga4_magento_gap.formula_explanation: The difference between GA4-reported total revenue and actual Magento store revenue, with the percentage taken relative to Magento revenue. A positive gap means GA4 over-reports versus the store's actual sales of record.
how_to_read: The headline value is GA4 total revenue minus actual Magento revenue.
The percentage is the gap divided by Magento revenue (the source of truth).
A positive gap means GA4 is over-counting revenue relative to real store sales.
Warning is flagged above 10% and critical above 25% over-reporting.
decisions_answered: How closely does GA4 revenue match my actual store revenue?
Is GA4 over- or under-counting versus Magento?
Has GA4 tracking accuracy degraded relative to real sales?

formula_components.name: GA4 vs Magento Gap
formula_components.formula: total GA4 revenue − Magento store revenue

attribution:revenue_reconciliation.title: Revenue Reconciliation
attribution:revenue_reconciliation.formula: Revenue Reconciliation headline = total Magento store revenue (source of truth), reconciled against combined platform-reported revenue and GA4 total revenue
attribution:revenue_reconciliation.formula_explanation: Reconciles three revenue views for the period: combined platform self-reported revenue (Google + Meta), GA4 total revenue, and actual Magento revenue. Magento is the source of truth; the two gaps quantify how platform and GA4 figures over- or under-state real revenue.
how_to_read: The headline value is actual Magento store revenue.
Platform vs GA4 gap compares combined Google + Meta self-reported revenue to GA4 total.
GA4 vs Magento gap compares GA4 total to actual store revenue.
Use Magento as the trusted figure and treat large gaps as a confidence signal on the other sources.
decisions_answered: How do platform, GA4, and actual store revenue compare for this period?
Which revenue source is most trustworthy for planning?
How large are the gaps between reported and real revenue?

formula_components.name: Headline (Magento)
formula_components.formula: total Magento store revenue over the period

formula_components.name: Platform Total
formula_components.formula: Google platform-reported revenue + Meta platform-reported revenue

formula_components.name: GA4 vs Magento Gap
formula_components.formula: total GA4 revenue − Magento store revenue

attribution:direct_share.title: Direct Traffic Share
attribution:direct_share.formula: Direct Traffic Share = GA4 Direct revenue ÷ total GA4 revenue × 100
attribution:direct_share.formula_explanation: Percentage of total GA4 revenue attributed to Direct traffic (visitors with no tracked referral source). High Direct share usually signals tracking gaps, untagged campaigns, or dark-social traffic that could not be attributed.
how_to_read: The value is Direct revenue divided by total GA4 revenue, expressed as a percentage.
A high or rising share often means attribution is leaking, not that Direct is genuinely growing.
The sparkline shows daily Direct share to reveal sudden tracking changes.
Pair with the Direct Traffic Black Hole chart to estimate what is hidden inside Direct.
decisions_answered: How much of my revenue is going untracked into the Direct bucket?
Are tracking or tagging issues inflating Direct traffic?
Is Direct share trending up in a way that signals attribution loss?

google-overview:daily_trend.title: Daily Performance Trend
google-overview:daily_trend.formula: Daily spend and ROAS across all campaign types over the selected period (line chart)
google-overview:daily_trend.formula_explanation: Aggregates each day's Google Ads spend and ROAS over the selected period. Spend is read directly from the campaign-performance mart (already in dollars), and ROAS is conversion value divided by spend for that day.
how_to_read: The line tracks total daily spend across all campaign types for the period
Higher peaks indicate days with more ad spend deployed
Hover any point to see clicks, conversions, and that day's ROAS
Look for consistent spend versus erratic spikes and dips
Sudden drops can signal budget exhaustion or paused campaigns worth investigating
decisions_answered: How consistent is our daily Google Ads spend?
Are there weekday versus weekend spending patterns?
When did significant spend or efficiency changes occur?
Is ROAS holding steady as spend moves day to day?

google-overview:yearly_trend.title: Yearly Performance Trend
google-overview:yearly_trend.formula: Total spend and ROAS rolled up to each calendar year
google-overview:yearly_trend.formula_explanation: Rolls all campaign-performance rows up to the calendar year, summing spend and computing ROAS per year. This reveals long-term spend growth and year-over-year efficiency trends rather than short-term noise.
how_to_read: Each bar represents one calendar year's total spend
Hover a bar to see that year's clicks, conversions, and ROAS
Compare bar heights to gauge year-over-year spend growth
Watch whether ROAS holds as yearly spend increases
Use this for strategic budget planning, not day-to-day tuning
decisions_answered: How has our Google Ads investment grown year over year?
Is efficiency (ROAS) improving or eroding as we scale?
Which years delivered the strongest returns?
Should we plan a larger or smaller annual budget?

google-overview:campaign_type_spend.title: Spend by Campaign Type
google-overview:campaign_type_spend.formula: Spend split across campaign types (Performance Max, Search, Shopping, Video, etc.) over the selected period, with the number of campaigns behind each slice
google-overview:campaign_type_spend.formula_explanation: Each slice represents total spend for one campaign type (Performance Max, Search, Shopping, Video, etc.) over the period, summed from the campaign-performance mart. The campaign count behind each type is also surfaced on hover.
how_to_read: Larger slices indicate higher budget allocation to that campaign type
Hover over any slice to see exact spend and how many campaigns it covers
Purple is typically Performance Max, blue is Search, green is Shopping
Compare slices to understand where the bulk of budget sits
Click a slice to drill into that campaign type's detailed page
decisions_answered: How is our Google Ads budget distributed across campaign types?
Which campaign type receives the most investment?
Should we shift budget between Search and Performance Max?
Is our campaign type mix aligned with performance goals?

formula_components.name: Campaign Count
formula_components.formula: Count of distinct campaigns of that type

google-overview:quality_score.title: Quality Score Distribution
google-overview:quality_score.formula: Count of distinct keywords at each Quality Score from 1 to 10 (keywords with no Quality Score excluded)
google-overview:quality_score.formula_explanation: Shows how scored keywords are distributed across Quality Scores from 1 to 10, counting distinct keywords at each score from the keyword-performance mart. Higher Quality Scores mean lower CPCs and better ad positions.
how_to_read: Each bar shows the count of keywords with that Quality Score
Scores 7-10 are healthy, 4-6 are average, 1-3 are poor
Taller bars at higher scores indicate a strong keyword portfolio
Many keywords at low scores signal cost-saving optimization potential
Only keywords that have a Quality Score are counted here
decisions_answered: What is the overall quality health of our keyword portfolio?
How many keywords need Quality Score improvement?
Are we paying premium CPCs due to low Quality Scores?
Which score buckets should we prioritize for optimization?

google-overview:rsa_strength.title: RSA Ad Strength
google-overview:rsa_strength.formula: Count and percentage share of Responsive Search Ads at each Ad Strength level (Excellent, Good, Average, Poor); ads with no strength are excluded
google-overview:rsa_strength.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of Responsive Search Ads across Google's Ad Strength ratings (Excellent, Good, Average, Poor), counting distinct ads at each level from the ad-performance mart and computing each level's share of the total.
how_to_read: The donut shows the proportion of RSAs at each strength level
Excellent and Good are the target band - aim for ~80% combined
Average leaves room for improvement; Poor needs immediate attention
Ads with no strength (NULL or UNSPECIFIED) are excluded from the chart
Hover a slice to see the ad count and its percentage share
decisions_answered: What percentage of our RSAs have Good or Excellent strength?
How many ads need creative improvement?
Are we meeting the 80% Good-plus target for RSA quality?
Where should we add headlines and descriptions to lift strength?

formula_components.name: Strength Count
formula_components.formula: Count of distinct ads at each strength level

formula_components.name: Percentage
formula_components.formula: Ad count at that level ÷ total ads × 100

google-overview:search_terms.title: Top Search Terms
google-overview:search_terms.formula: Top 100 search terms by spend over the selected period, each with spend, clicks, impressions, conversions, CTR and CPA
google-overview:search_terms.formula_explanation: Aggregates the actual search queries that triggered your ads, ranking the top 100 by spend over the date range. CTR and CPA are computed per term from the search-terms mart to reveal relevance and efficiency.
how_to_read: Terms are ranked by spend to show where budget is going
High spend with no conversions flags negative-keyword candidates
Strong conversions with low CPA are candidates to add as exact-match keywords
CTR indicates how relevant your ads are to that query
Search to focus on specific products or brand terms
decisions_answered: Which search queries drive the most conversions?
Are any terms wasting budget with no conversions?
What negative keywords should we add?
Which high-performing terms should become exact-match keywords?

google-overview:campaign_performance.title: Campaign Performance
google-overview:campaign_performance.formula: One row per campaign (top 50 by spend) with spend, clicks, impressions, conversions, revenue, ROAS, CPA, CTR and conversion rate over the selected period
google-overview:campaign_performance.formula_explanation: Consolidates every campaign's key performance metrics over the period, grouped by campaign from the campaign-performance mart (top 50 by spend). Sort by any column to surface top performers or budget drains.
how_to_read: Sort by Spend to see where budget goes, by ROAS to find efficient campaigns
Status reflects campaign state: enabled, paused, or removed
Campaign type badges distinguish PMax, Search, and Shopping
ROAS and CPA are color-coded so winners and laggards stand out
Only the top 50 campaigns by spend are shown
decisions_answered: Which campaigns generate the best return on ad spend?
Which campaigns have high spend but low performance?
Are there campaigns that should be paused or scaled up?
How does performance vary across campaign types?

google-overview:audience_summary.title: Audience Infrastructure
google-overview:audience_summary.formula: One row per audience type (Standard, Custom, Combined) showing the total number of audiences and how many are enabled, rolled up from the Google Ads audience snapshot
google-overview:audience_summary.formula_explanation: Rolls up the account's audience assets (Standard, Custom, and Combined audiences) from the raw Google Ads audience tables, showing the total and enabled count for each type. These assets power audience targeting and bid adjustments.
how_to_read: Total count shows all audiences of that type in the account
Enabled count shows how many are actively usable for targeting
Standard audiences derive from the SCOPE field; Custom and Combined are separate types
Custom Audiences support intent and interest-based targeting
This is snapshot data - date and campaign filters do not apply
decisions_answered: How many targeting audiences do we have available?
Are our audiences properly set up and enabled?
Do we have enough custom audiences for prospecting?
Is our audience infrastructure sufficient for targeting needs?

formula_components.name: Total Count
formula_components.formula: Count of all audiences of that type in the account

formula_components.name: Enabled Count
formula_components.formula: Count of those audiences whose status is Enabled

google-overview:account_structure.title: Account Structure
google-overview:account_structure.formula: One row per entity type (Campaigns, Ad Groups, Ads, Keywords) showing the total count plus a breakdown into Enabled, Paused and Removed, from the current Google Ads snapshot
google-overview:account_structure.formula_explanation: Counts the live entities in the account - Campaigns, Ad Groups, Ads, and Keywords - from the current Fivetran snapshot of the raw Google Ads history tables, broken down by status. This is the structural inventory of the account.
how_to_read: Each row is one entity type with its total count
Enabled, Paused, and Removed columns split the total by status
Only currently active (_FIVETRAN_ACTIVE) records are counted
Keywords are filtered to criteria of TYPE = KEYWORD
This is snapshot data - date and campaign filters do not apply
decisions_answered: How large is our account across campaigns, ad groups, ads, and keywords?
What share of each entity type is actually enabled?
Do we have a backlog of paused or removed entities to clean up?
Is the account structure proportionate to our spend?

formula_components.name: Total
formula_components.formula: Count of currently-active entities of that type

formula_components.name: Enabled / Paused / Removed
formula_components.formula: Count of those entities whose status is Enabled, Paused or Removed

google-overview:spend.title: Spend
google-overview:spend.formula: Spend = total Google Ads spend across all campaigns over the selected period
google-overview:spend.formula_explanation: Total Google Ads spend across all campaigns in the selected date range, summed directly from the campaign-performance mart (values already in dollars).
how_to_read: Shows total spend across all Google Ads campaigns
Compared against the previous period of equal length
Percentage change indicates the spend trajectory
Sparkline traces daily spend within the period
decisions_answered: How much are we spending on Google Ads?
Is spend increasing or decreasing versus the prior period?
Are we on track with our overall budget?

google-overview:roas.title: ROAS
google-overview:roas.formula: ROAS = total conversion value ÷ total Google Ads spend
google-overview:roas.formula_explanation: Return on ad spend across all Google Ads campaigns - total conversion value divided by total spend for the period. A ROAS of 5.0 means $5 of revenue for every $1 spent.
how_to_read: Shows the revenue return for every dollar of Google Ads spend
Higher values indicate more efficient spend
Compared against the previous period of equal length
Sparkline traces daily ROAS within the period
decisions_answered: Are our Google Ads campaigns profitable?
Is overall efficiency improving or declining?
Should we adjust budget based on returns?

google-overview:cpa.title: CPA
google-overview:cpa.formula: CPA = total Google Ads spend ÷ total conversions
google-overview:cpa.formula_explanation: Cost per acquisition - total spend divided by total conversions for the period. Shows the average cost to generate one conversion across all Google Ads campaigns.
how_to_read: Shows the average cost of each conversion
Lower values indicate cheaper, more efficient acquisition
Compared against the previous period of equal length
Sparkline traces daily CPA within the period
decisions_answered: How much does it cost us to acquire a conversion?
Is acquisition getting cheaper or more expensive?
Are CPA trends consistent with our target economics?

google-overview:conversions.title: Conversions
google-overview:conversions.formula: Conversions = total conversions attributed to Google Ads campaigns over the selected period
google-overview:conversions.formula_explanation: Total conversions attributed to Google Ads campaigns in the selected date range, summed from the campaign-performance mart. Includes all conversion actions configured in the account.
how_to_read: Shows total conversions across all Google Ads campaigns
Compared against the previous period of equal length
Percentage change indicates the conversion trajectory
Sparkline traces daily conversions within the period
decisions_answered: How many conversions are Google Ads generating?
Is conversion volume trending up or down?
Are campaigns meeting conversion targets?

google-overview:clicks.title: Clicks
google-overview:clicks.formula: Clicks = total Google Ads clicks over the selected period
google-overview:clicks.formula_explanation: Total clicks on Google Ads in the selected date range, summed from the campaign-performance mart. Each click represents a user engaging with an ad.
how_to_read: Shows total click volume across all Google Ads campaigns
Compared against the previous period of equal length
Percentage change indicates the engagement trajectory
Sparkline traces daily clicks within the period
decisions_answered: How much traffic are Google Ads driving?
Is click volume growing or declining?
Are our ads generating enough engagement?

google-overview:impressions.title: Impressions
google-overview:impressions.formula: Impressions = total Google Ads ad impressions over the selected period
google-overview:impressions.formula_explanation: Total ad impressions for Google Ads campaigns in the selected date range, summed from the campaign-performance mart. Each impression is one display of an ad.
how_to_read: Shows total impression volume across all Google Ads campaigns
Compared against the previous period of equal length
Percentage change indicates reach trajectory
Sparkline traces daily impressions within the period
decisions_answered: How much reach are Google Ads delivering?
Is impression volume growing or declining?
Is our reach proportional to our spend?

google-search:search_trend.title: Search Performance Trend
google-search:search_trend.formula: Daily spend and ROAS for Search campaigns over the selected period (area chart)
google-search:search_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks daily spend and ROAS for Search campaigns over the selected period. All rows are filtered to CAMPAIGN_TYPE = 'SEARCH' and aggregated by day from the campaign-performance mart.
how_to_read: The area shows daily Search spend over the time period
Higher peaks indicate days with more Search budget deployed
Hover any point to see revenue, clicks, conversions, and ROAS
Look for consistent spend versus erratic spikes and dips
Sudden drops can indicate paused campaigns or budget caps
decisions_answered: How consistent is our daily Search spend?
Are there weekday versus weekend patterns?
When did significant spend or efficiency changes occur?
Is Search ROAS holding steady over time?

formula_components.name: Daily Spend
formula_components.formula: Spend summed per day for Search campaigns

google-search:match_type.title: Spend by Match Type
google-search:match_type.formula: Search keyword spend split by match type (Broad, Phrase, Exact), with CTR and CPC available per match type
google-search:match_type.formula_explanation: Groups Search keyword spend by match type (Broad, Phrase, Exact) from the keyword-performance mart. Each slice shows the budget share for that targeting strategy, with CTR and CPC available per match type.
how_to_read: Each donut slice represents spend proportion for a match type
Broad has widest reach but lowest precision; Exact is the reverse
Phrase match sits between the two on reach and control
Hover a slice to see exact spend, keyword count, CTR, and CPC
Compare slices to assess whether your mix favors reach or precision
decisions_answered: How is Search budget distributed across match types?
Are we over-investing in broad match with lower ROAS?
Should we shift budget to exact match for higher precision?
What is the optimal match type mix for our campaigns?

formula_components.name: Match Type Spend
formula_components.formula: Spend summed per keyword match type

google-search:search_intent.title: Search Intent Distribution
google-search:search_intent.formula: Search terms classified by user intent (Brand, Transactional, Informational, Product, Other) using keyword patterns, then spend summed per intent category
google-search:search_intent.formula_explanation: Classifies search terms by user intent using keyword pattern matching on the search-terms mart - Brand (e.g. baker creek, rareseeds), Transactional (buy, shop, order), Informational (how to, guide, tips), Product (seed), and Other - then sums spend, clicks, and conversions per intent.
how_to_read: Each slice shows the spend share for one intent category
Brand terms typically carry the highest ROAS
Transactional terms signal strong purchase intent
Informational terms usually convert at lower rates
Hover a slice for unique terms, clicks, and conversions
decisions_answered: What intent types drive the most conversions?
How much are we spending on brand versus non-brand intent?
Are transactional keywords generating expected returns?
Should we invest more in product-specific keywords?

formula_components.name: Intent
formula_components.formula: Search term mapped to Brand / Transactional / Informational / Product / Other by keyword patterns

google-search:ad_position.title: Ad Position Trend
