Sudden drops can signal site or checkout issues
Click to analyze conversion by device, channel, or region
decisions_answered: What share of visitors are buying?
Is conversion healthy versus recent performance?
Are there site issues hurting conversion?

cro:cart_abandonment.title: Cart Abandonment Rate
cro:cart_abandonment.formula_explanation: Share of add-to-cart sessions that did not result in a purchase, from the funnel mart. It quantifies how much demand is lost between cart and checkout.
how_to_read: The main value shows the cart abandonment rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track checkout friction
The sparkline shows the daily abandonment trend
High abandonment points to shipping, payment, or account friction
Click to view the funnel and checkout drop-off
decisions_answered: How many shoppers abandon their carts?
Is checkout friction worsening?
Where in checkout are we losing buyers?

cro:checkout_completion.title: Checkout Completion Rate
cro:checkout_completion.formula: Checkout Completion Rate = purchases ÷ begun checkouts × 100
cro:checkout_completion.formula_explanation: Share of begun checkouts that completed in a purchase, from the funnel mart. It isolates friction inside the checkout flow itself.
how_to_read: The main value shows the checkout completion rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track checkout health
The sparkline shows the daily completion trend
Low completion points to payment or form friction
Click to view the checkout drop-off breakdown
decisions_answered: Once started, how often is checkout completed?
Is checkout friction increasing?
Which checkout step is leaking buyers?

cro:revenue.title: Revenue
cro:revenue.formula: Revenue = sum of net revenue from Magento orders over the period
cro:revenue.formula_explanation: Total revenue over the period from the funnel mart. It ties conversion performance to financial outcome.
how_to_read: The main value shows total revenue for the period
Compare to the prior period to track sales
The sparkline shows the daily revenue trend
Pair with conversion rate and AOV to interpret changes
Click to connect funnel stages to revenue
decisions_answered: How much revenue did conversions generate?
Is revenue growing with conversion?
What is driving revenue - rate or order value?

cro:aov.title: Average Order Value
cro:aov.formula: Average Order Value = revenue ÷ purchases over the period
cro:aov.formula_explanation: Average revenue per purchase over the period, dividing revenue by purchases from the funnel mart.
how_to_read: The main value shows average revenue per purchase
Compare to the prior period to track basket size
The sparkline shows the daily AOV trend
Rising AOV grows revenue even at a flat conversion rate
Click to see what drives basket size
decisions_answered: How large is the typical purchase?
Is basket size growing?
Should we push upsells to lift AOV?

cro:rpu.title: Revenue per Session
cro:rpu.formula: Revenue per Session = revenue ÷ sessions over the period
cro:rpu.formula_explanation: Average revenue generated per session, dividing revenue by sessions. Despite the 'per user' shorthand, the backend computes this per session - it blends conversion rate and AOV into one efficiency number.
how_to_read: The main value shows revenue earned per session
Compare to the prior period to track monetization
The sparkline shows the daily revenue-per-session trend
It combines conversion rate and AOV into one figure
Click to compare monetization by channel and device
decisions_answered: How well does each visit monetize?
Is our traffic getting more or less valuable?
Which channels monetize best per session?

customer-acquisition:blended_cac.title: Blended CAC
customer-acquisition:blended_cac.formula: Blended CAC = total ad spend (Google + Meta) ÷ new customers acquired
customer-acquisition:blended_cac.formula_explanation: Blended Customer Acquisition Cost divides all paid marketing spend across every channel by the total number of new customers acquired in the period. It shows the true all-in average cost to win one customer, regardless of which channel they came from.
how_to_read: The main value is the average dollars spent to acquire one new customer
It blends spend across all paid channels, not a single platform
Lower is better - it means acquisition is getting more efficient
Compare against first-purchase AOV and LTV to judge if acquisition is profitable
Rising CAC with flat new-customer counts signals diminishing ad returns
decisions_answered: How much are we really paying to acquire each new customer?
Is our acquisition cost rising or falling over time?
Is our spend producing enough new customers to justify the budget?
Can we afford to scale spend without breaking acquisition economics?

formula_components.name: Total Ad Spend
formula_components.formula: sum of paid spend across all channels (Google + Meta)

formula_components.name: New Customers
formula_components.formula: sum of newly acquired customers over the period

customer-acquisition:cac_efficiency.title: CAC Efficiency
customer-acquisition:cac_efficiency.formula: CAC Efficiency = first-purchase AOV ÷ blended CAC (ratio above 1 means the first order alone recoups acquisition cost)
customer-acquisition:cac_efficiency.formula_explanation: CAC Efficiency divides the average first-order value by the blended cost to acquire a customer. A ratio above 1 means a customer's first purchase already covers more than what it cost to acquire them; below 1 means the first order does not yet pay back acquisition spend.
how_to_read: The value is a ratio of first-purchase value to acquisition cost
Above 1.0 means the first order alone recoups acquisition spend
Below 1.0 means you rely on repeat purchases to become profitable
Higher is better - it reflects healthier upfront acquisition economics
Track alongside LTV:CAC for the full payback picture
decisions_answered: Does a customer's first purchase pay back what we spent to acquire them?
How quickly do we recoup acquisition cost?
Is acquisition efficiency improving or eroding?
Are we over-spending relative to the value new customers bring in?

customer-acquisition:first_purchase_aov.title: First Purchase AOV
customer-acquisition:first_purchase_aov.formula: First Purchase AOV = total first-order revenue ÷ new customers acquired
customer-acquisition:first_purchase_aov.formula_explanation: First Purchase Average Order Value divides total first-order revenue by the number of newly acquired customers in the period. It captures how much a customer typically spends on their very first purchase.
how_to_read: The main value is the average value of a customer's first order
Higher first-purchase AOV improves acquisition payback
Compare against blended CAC to gauge upfront profitability
A rising trend suggests better targeting or higher-intent traffic
Channel-level differences reveal which sources bring bigger first baskets
decisions_answered: How much do new customers spend on their first order?
Is our first-purchase value high enough to offset acquisition cost?
Are recent acquisition efforts attracting higher-value buyers?
Which channels deliver the most valuable first purchases?

customer-acquisition:new_customers.title: New Customers
customer-acquisition:new_customers.formula: New Customers = count of first-time buyers whose first order date falls within the selected period
customer-acquisition:new_customers.formula_explanation: Counts customers whose first-ever order falls within the selected date range. This is the canonical count of genuinely new buyers acquired in the period, matching the Customer Overview definition.
how_to_read: The main value is the number of first-time buyers acquired in the period
It counts customers by their first order date, not signup or email-only contacts
The change percent compares against the prior equal-length period
A declining trend signals weakening top-of-funnel acquisition
Pair with blended CAC to see what those new customers cost
decisions_answered: How many genuinely new buyers did we acquire this period?
Is new-customer acquisition growing or slowing versus the prior period?
Are marketing efforts converting traffic into first-time buyers?
Do we need to adjust acquisition spend or strategy?

customer-retention:avg_clv.title: Average CLV
customer-retention:avg_clv.formula: Average CLV = average net lifetime value across customers with positive lifetime value
customer-retention:avg_clv.formula_explanation: Average Customer Lifetime Value is the mean net lifetime value across all customers who have generated positive value. It reflects the typical total worth of a retained customer to the business.
how_to_read: The main value is the average net lifetime value per customer
Only customers with positive net LTV are included
Higher CLV indicates stronger retention and repeat spending
Compare against acquisition cost to assess long-term profitability
A rising trend means customers are becoming more valuable over time
decisions_answered: What is a retained customer typically worth to us?
Is customer lifetime value improving as retention efforts mature?
How much can we justify spending to acquire and keep customers?
Are we growing the long-term value of our base?

customer-retention:avg_orders.title: Avg Orders per Customer
customer-retention:avg_orders.formula: Avg Orders per Customer = average number of orders across customers with ≥1 order
customer-retention:avg_orders.formula_explanation: The average number of orders placed per purchasing customer. It measures purchase repetition - how many times a typical customer buys before they stop.
how_to_read: The main value is the mean number of orders per customer with at least one order
Values above 1 indicate customers are returning to buy again
Higher numbers reflect stronger loyalty and repeat-purchase behavior
Compare with repeat rate to understand depth versus breadth of loyalty
Track over time to see if retention initiatives drive more orders
decisions_answered: How many times does a typical customer buy from us?
Are customers building a repeat-purchase habit?
Is our average order count improving with retention efforts?
How much room is there to grow purchase frequency?

customer-retention:days_between_orders.title: Avg Days Between Orders
customer-retention:days_between_orders.formula: Avg Days Between Orders = average customer lifespan in days (first-to-last order gap), averaged across customers with ≥1 order
customer-retention:days_between_orders.formula_explanation: This card reports average customer lifespan in days - the span between a customer's first and last order, averaged across the base. Despite the label, the backend computes lifespan (first-to-last order gap), not the gap between consecutive orders. Lower values indicate customers churn sooner; longer spans indicate sustained relationships.
how_to_read: The main value is the average number of days between a customer's first and last order
It represents active customer lifespan, not the interval between individual purchases
Longer spans indicate customers stay engaged over a longer period
A short span warns that customers lapse quickly after acquisition
Use it to time win-back and re-engagement campaigns
decisions_answered: How long do customers typically stay active with us?
Are customers lapsing soon after their first purchase?
When should re-engagement campaigns fire to extend the relationship?
Is our retention window expanding or shrinking?

customer-retention:repeat_rate.title: Repeat Rate
customer-retention:repeat_rate.formula: Repeat Rate = customers with more than one order ÷ customers with ≥1 order × 100
customer-retention:repeat_rate.formula_explanation: The percentage of purchasing customers who have placed more than one order. It is a direct measure of how well the business converts first-time buyers into repeat customers.
how_to_read: The main value is the share of buyers who ordered more than once
Only customers with at least one order are in the denominator
Higher repeat rates signal strong loyalty and product-market fit
A low repeat rate points to a one-and-done purchasing problem
Track the trend to gauge whether retention efforts are working
decisions_answered: What share of our customers come back for a second purchase?
Are we successfully converting first-time buyers into repeat customers?
Is our repeat rate healthy relative to benchmarks?
Should we invest more in second-purchase and retention campaigns?

customer-journey:aov.title: Average Order Value
customer-journey:aov.formula: Average Order Value = total converting-touchpoint revenue ÷ count of converting touchpoints
customer-journey:aov.formula_explanation: Average Order Value is total converting-touchpoint revenue divided by the count of converting touchpoints in the period. It reflects the typical revenue captured per conversion across the customer journey.
how_to_read: The main value is average revenue per conversion in the journey data
Only converting touchpoints (IS_CONVERSION = TRUE) contribute to the value
The change percent compares the current and prior periods
Rising AOV means each conversion is worth more
Pair with conversion rate to see whether more or bigger orders drive revenue
decisions_answered: How much revenue does a typical conversion generate?
Is order value trending up or down?
Are upsell and bundling efforts lifting basket size?
Should we focus on more conversions or larger ones?

customer-journey:conversion_rate.title: Conversion Rate
customer-journey:conversion_rate.formula: Conversion Rate = converting touchpoints ÷ all journey touchpoints × 100
customer-journey:conversion_rate.formula_explanation: Conversion Rate is the share of all journey touchpoints that are conversions, expressed as a percentage. It measures how effectively touchpoints across the journey turn into completed purchases.
how_to_read: The main value is the percentage of touchpoints that converted
It is computed across all journey touchpoints, not just sessions
The change percent compares against the prior period
Higher rates indicate a more efficient path to purchase
A falling rate signals friction or lower-intent traffic in the journey
decisions_answered: What share of journey touchpoints lead to a purchase?
Is our conversion efficiency improving over time?
Are recent campaigns bringing in higher- or lower-intent traffic?
Where should we focus to lift overall conversion?

customer-journey:engagement_rate.title: Engagement Rate
customer-journey:engagement_rate.formula: Engagement Rate = touchpoints with a non-zero value ÷ all journey touchpoints × 100 (proxy for engagement, not a native field)
customer-journey:engagement_rate.formula_explanation: Engagement Rate approximates the percentage of journey touchpoints that carried a non-zero value. Because MART_CUSTOMER_JOURNEY has no direct engagement field, the backend proxies engagement as the share of touchpoints with value greater than zero.
how_to_read: The main value is the share of touchpoints with a non-zero value
It is a proxy for engagement, not a native GA4 engagement-rate field
Higher values suggest a larger portion of touchpoints carry economic weight
The change percent compares against the prior period
Read it alongside conversion rate for a fuller engagement picture
decisions_answered: What portion of journey touchpoints show meaningful activity?
Is journey engagement rising or falling?
Are our touchpoints attracting active, valuable interactions?
Where is engagement weakest across the journey?

customer-journey:revenue.title: Total Revenue
customer-journey:revenue.formula: Total Revenue = sum of converting-touchpoint values (order revenue) over the period
customer-journey:revenue.formula_explanation: Total Revenue sums the value of all converting touchpoints in the period. It represents the revenue attributed to conversions captured in the customer journey data.
how_to_read: The main value is total revenue from converting touchpoints
Only touchpoints flagged as conversions contribute revenue
The change percent compares against the prior period
Use the sparkline to spot revenue spikes tied to campaigns or seasonality
Pair with conversion rate and AOV to explain revenue movement
decisions_answered: How much revenue did the customer journey drive this period?
Is journey-attributed revenue growing or declining?
Which periods produced revenue peaks worth investigating?
Are conversion and order-value trends explaining revenue changes?

customer-journey:sessions.title: Total Sessions
customer-journey:sessions.formula: Total Sessions = count of journey touchpoint rows in the period (each order touchpoint counts as one session)
customer-journey:sessions.formula_explanation: Total Sessions counts every touchpoint row in MART_CUSTOMER_JOURNEY within the period. In this dashboard each touchpoint stands in for a session-level interaction in the journey.
how_to_read: The main value is the total count of journey touchpoints in the period
Each row in the journey mart counts as one session/touchpoint
The change percent compares against the prior period
The sparkline shows the day-by-day (or chosen granularity) trend
Rising volume indicates more journey activity, not necessarily more revenue
decisions_answered: How much journey activity are we seeing this period?
Is touchpoint/session volume growing or shrinking?
Are there volume spikes tied to campaigns or seasonality?
Is traffic volume keeping pace with revenue and conversions?

customer-journey:users.title: Total Users
customer-journey:users.formula: Total Users = count of distinct customers appearing in the journey data during the period
customer-journey:users.formula_explanation: Total Users counts the distinct customers appearing in the journey data during the period. It deduplicates touchpoints down to unique people who interacted with the brand.
how_to_read: The main value is the number of unique customers in the journey data
It counts distinct CUSTOMER_ID, so repeat touchpoints don't inflate it
The change percent compares against the prior period
Compare against sessions to gauge touchpoints per person
Growth here reflects reach; pair with conversion rate for quality
decisions_answered: How many unique customers engaged in the journey this period?
Is our reach growing or contracting?
How many touchpoints does each customer generate on average?
Is rising activity coming from more people or more touches per person?

customer-ltv:total_clv.title: Total CLV
customer-ltv:total_clv.formula: Total CLV = sum of realized (actual) lifetime value across all acquisition-cohort customers in the selected period
customer-ltv:total_clv.formula_explanation: Sums the realized (actual) lifetime value across every customer acquired in the selected date range, drawn from MART_CUSTOMER_LTV. The date filter is applied on each customer's first_order_date, so the value reflects the total CLV booked by that acquisition cohort.
how_to_read: The main value is the combined lifetime value of all qualifying customers
The date range filters customers by when they placed their first order, not by recent activity
Compare against Average CLV and Customers with CLV to understand whether the total is driven by volume or by value per customer
Only customers with a non-null ACTUAL_LTV are included
A rising total indicates either more acquired customers or higher per-customer value
decisions_answered: What is the total realized lifetime value of our customer base?
How much value did the customers acquired in this period generate?
Is total customer value growing or shrinking across periods?

customer-ltv:avg_clv.title: Average CLV
customer-ltv:avg_clv.formula: Average CLV = average realized lifetime value across customers in the acquisition cohort (non-null lifetime value)
customer-ltv:avg_clv.formula_explanation: The mean realized lifetime value per customer for the acquisition cohort in the selected date range, from MART_CUSTOMER_LTV. It tells you how much an average qualifying customer is worth across their full purchase history.
how_to_read: The main value is realized lifetime value divided by the number of customers with CLV
Average can be pulled up by a small number of very high-value customers - check Median CLV for the typical customer
Higher average CLV with stable customer counts indicates improving per-customer value
The date range filters on first_order_date (acquisition), not recent activity
Compare to Total CLV to separate value-per-customer effects from volume effects
decisions_answered: What is a typical customer worth over their lifetime?
Is per-customer value increasing or decreasing across periods?
Are we acquiring higher- or lower-value customers over time?

customer-ltv:median_clv.title: Median CLV
customer-ltv:median_clv.formula: Median CLV = midpoint (50th-percentile) realized lifetime value across acquisition-cohort customers
customer-ltv:median_clv.formula_explanation: The midpoint of realized lifetime value across qualifying customers - half are worth more, half are worth less. Because it is unaffected by extreme high-value outliers, the median represents the truly typical customer better than the average.
how_to_read: The main value is the 50th-percentile customer lifetime value
A median much lower than Average CLV signals a value distribution skewed by a few high-value customers
A median close to the average indicates a more even spread of customer value
The date range filters customers by first_order_date (acquisition cohort)
Use median for setting realistic per-customer value expectations
decisions_answered: What is the value of a genuinely typical customer?
How skewed is our customer value distribution?
Are most customers low-value with a few outliers driving the average?

customer-ltv:customers_with_clv.title: Customers with CLV
customer-ltv:customers_with_clv.formula: Customers with CLV = count of acquisition-cohort customers that have a non-null lifetime value
customer-ltv:customers_with_clv.formula_explanation: Counts the customers acquired in the selected period who have a computed lifetime value in MART_CUSTOMER_LTV. This is the denominator behind the average and median CLV metrics, indicating how many customers the value figures are based on.
how_to_read: The main value is the number of customers with a non-null lifetime value
This is the population the Average and Median CLV are calculated over
A larger count alongside a stable average indicates healthy acquisition without value dilution
The date range filters customers by first_order_date (acquisition)
Compare to Total CLV to gauge value contributed per qualifying customer
decisions_answered: How many customers do our CLV figures actually represent?
Is the acquisition cohort large enough to trust the average and median?
How many customers did we acquire in this period?

customer-ltv:predicted_clv.title: Predicted CLV
customer-ltv:predicted_clv.formula: Predicted CLV = sum of realized (actual) lifetime value across the acquisition cohort — placeholder; no forward-looking prediction exists in the mart, so it equals Total CLV
customer-ltv:predicted_clv.formula_explanation: Intended to show total predicted future lifetime value, but the mart does not yet carry a forward-looking prediction, so the backend returns the sum of ACTUAL_LTV (the realized total) as a placeholder. The value therefore matches Total CLV until a prediction model is wired in.
how_to_read: The main value currently mirrors Total CLV because no predicted figure exists in the mart
Treat this as realized value, not a true forward forecast, for now
The date range filters customers by first_order_date (acquisition)
When a prediction model is added, this will diverge from Total CLV to reflect expected future value
Until then, do not interpret a gap between this and Total CLV - there is none
decisions_answered: What is the projected total value of our customer base? (currently realized-only)
How much future revenue can we expect from existing customers? (placeholder)
Is our customer base set up to generate value going forward?

customer-ltv:at_risk_value.title: At-Risk Value
customer-ltv:at_risk_value.formula: At-Risk Value = sum of net lifetime value of customers flagged High or Critical churn risk, scoped to the acquisition cohort in the selected period
customer-ltv:at_risk_value.formula_explanation: Sums the net lifetime value of customers flagged High or Critical churn risk in MART_CUSTOMER_HEALTH, scoped to the acquisition cohort in the selected date range. It quantifies the lifetime value that could be lost if those high-risk customers churn.
how_to_read: The main value is total NET_LTV held by High- and Critical-risk customers
Risk levels are pre-computed in MART_CUSTOMER_HEALTH, not derived from a probability cutoff here
A large figure means significant revenue is concentrated in churn-prone customers
The date range filters by first_order_date (acquisition cohort)
Use this to size the financial stakes of your retention program
decisions_answered: How much customer value is at risk of churning?
Is the potential loss large enough to justify a retention investment?
Which acquisition cohorts carry the most at-risk value?

customer-ltv:top_customers.title: Top Customers by CLV
customer-ltv:top_customers.formula: Top 100 customers ranked by realized lifetime value; each row shows CLV, total orders, average order value, churn probability (churn risk score ÷ 100) and a churn-risk band (Critical ≥80, High ≥60, Medium ≥40, Low ≥20, Minimal below 20), plus tenure from first order date.
customer-ltv:top_customers.formula_explanation: Lists the highest-value customers (up to 100) ranked by realized lifetime value (ACTUAL_LTV) from MART_CUSTOMER_LTV, joined to MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY for order history and churn score. Each row shows CLV, orders, AOV, and a churn-risk band derived from the customer's CHURN_RISK_SCORE, enabling VIP management and proactive retention.
how_to_read: Customers are ranked by total CLV, highest value first (top 100)
Orders and AOV reveal the purchase pattern behind each customer's value
Churn Risk Level is banded from CHURN_RISK_SCORE (Critical >=80 down to Minimal <20)
Emails are masked for privacy when a full name is unavailable
Customer Since shows tenure from first_order_date - the date range filters this acquisition window
decisions_answered: Who are our most valuable customers?
Are any high-value customers at risk of churning?
What purchase characteristics define our best customers?
Which VIP customers warrant personal outreach?

formula_components.name: Churn risk band
formula_components.formula: Critical if score ≥ 80, High ≥ 60, Medium ≥ 40, Low ≥ 20, else Minimal

customer-rfm:total_customers.title: Total Customers
customer-rfm:total_customers.formula: Total Customers = count of customers with at least one order whose active span (first order to last order) overlaps the selected period
customer-rfm:total_customers.formula_explanation: Counts customers with purchase history (total_orders > 0) who were active within the selected window, joining MART_CUSTOMER_RFM to MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY. The window overlap condition (first_order before the end, last_order after the start) includes any customer whose active span touches the period.
how_to_read: The main value is the count of purchasing customers overlapping the selected period
Only customers with at least one order are counted
The window uses span overlap, not first_order_date alone, so long-tenured active customers are included
Compare to Active Customers to see what share of the base purchased in the last 30 days
This is the denominator behind Repeat Rate and the average metrics
decisions_answered: How many purchasing customers do we have in this period?
What is the size of the base our RFM segments are built on?
Is our purchasing customer count growing across periods?

customer-rfm:active_customers.title: Active Customers
customer-rfm:active_customers.formula: Active Customers = count of purchasing customers whose most recent order is within the trailing 30 days
customer-rfm:active_customers.formula_explanation: Counts purchasing customers whose most recent order was within the last 30 days (days_since_last_order <= 30), from MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY. This is a purchase-recency measure of how many customers are currently active buyers, not an engagement/event measure.
how_to_read: The main value is customers who ordered within the last 30 days
Activity here means a recent purchase, not email opens or site visits
Compare to Total Customers to gauge the active share of your purchasing base
A declining count signals customers lapsing toward churn
Only customers with prior order history (total_orders > 0) are eligible
decisions_answered: How many customers are currently active buyers (last 30 days)?
Is our active purchasing base growing or shrinking?
What share of customers have lapsed and may need win-back?

customer-rfm:avg_clv.title: Average CLV
customer-rfm:avg_clv.formula: Average CLV = average net lifetime value per purchasing customer active in the period
customer-rfm:avg_clv.formula_explanation: The mean net lifetime value per purchasing customer in the selected window, taken from MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY.NET_LTV. It indicates the average revenue value of a customer in the RFM base.
how_to_read: The main value is average NET_LTV across qualifying customers
Net LTV is net of returns/adjustments, so it can be lower than gross revenue figures
A high average can be skewed by Champions - cross-check with the segment table
Only purchasing customers (total_orders > 0) within the active window are included
Compare to Total CLV to separate per-customer value from base size
decisions_answered: What is the average value of a purchasing customer?
Is per-customer value rising or falling across periods?
How does average value differ from the high-value segments?

customer-rfm:repeat_rate.title: Repeat Rate
customer-rfm:repeat_rate.formula: Repeat Rate = customers with 2 or more orders ÷ all purchasing customers × 100%
customer-rfm:repeat_rate.formula_explanation: The percentage of purchasing customers who have placed two or more orders. It measures how well the business converts first-time buyers into repeat customers, a core driver of lifetime value.
how_to_read: The main value is the share of customers with 2+ orders, expressed as a percentage
Higher is better - it indicates stronger retention and habit formation
Only customers with at least one order are in the denominator
Compare across periods to track whether repeat behavior is improving
Pair with Average CLV - repeat customers typically drive disproportionate value
decisions_answered: What share of our customers come back for a second purchase?
Is our repeat-purchase behavior improving over time?
Should we invest more in first-to-second-order conversion?

customer-rfm:avg_churn_risk.title: Average Churn Risk
customer-rfm:avg_churn_risk.formula: Average Churn Risk = average of the per-customer churn-risk score (0-100) across purchasing customers active in the period
customer-rfm:avg_churn_risk.formula_explanation: The mean churn-risk score across all purchasing customers, read directly from MART_CUSTOMER_RFM.CHURN_RISK_SCORE (a 0-100 score). A higher average means the base as a whole is more at risk of lapsing.
how_to_read: The main value is the average churn-risk score on a 0-100 scale, shown as a percentage
Higher values indicate a more at-risk customer base overall
The score is pre-computed in the RFM mart, not derived on the fly
Track across periods - a rising average is an early warning of base erosion
Pair with the At-Risk Customers table to act on the highest-value risks
decisions_answered: How at-risk is our customer base on average?
Is overall churn risk trending up or down?
Do we need a broader retention push or just targeted outreach?

customer-rfm:total_clv.title: Total CLV
customer-rfm:total_clv.formula: Total CLV = sum of net lifetime value across all purchasing customers active in the period
customer-rfm:total_clv.formula_explanation: Sums net lifetime value (NET_LTV) across all purchasing customers active in the selected window, from MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY. It is the total realized portfolio value behind the RFM segmentation.
how_to_read: The main value is the combined NET_LTV of all qualifying customers
Net LTV is net of returns, so it reflects realized value rather than gross sales
Compare to Average CLV and Total Customers to see whether the total is driven by volume or value
Only purchasing customers within the active window contribute
A rising total reflects more customers, higher value per customer, or both
decisions_answered: What is the total realized value of our purchasing customer base?
Is total customer value growing across periods?
How much of the total is concentrated in top segments?

customer-feedback:nps-score.title: NPS Score
customer-feedback:nps-score.formula: NPS Score = average product-review star rating (1-5) over the period, used as a satisfaction proxy (not a true -100..+100 NPS); change = current average − prior average
customer-feedback:nps-score.formula_explanation: Magento reviews carry no true NPS survey, so this metric repurposes the average star rating (1-5 scale) from MART_CUSTOMER_FEEDBACK_DETAIL as an NPS proxy. Despite the -100 to +100 label, the value is the mean star rating for the period, and the change is the simple difference versus the prior period.
how_to_read: The main value is the average star rating used as a satisfaction proxy, not a classic -100 to +100 NPS
Zero-rating reviews are excluded so they do not drag the average down
The change is the absolute point difference versus the comparison period
Higher is better - it reflects more positive product reviews
Read alongside Promoters % and Rating Distribution for the full sentiment picture
decisions_answered: Is overall review sentiment improving or declining?
How did satisfaction shift versus the prior period?
Are customers rating our products positively on average?

customer-feedback:total-responses.title: Survey Responses
customer-feedback:total-responses.formula: Survey Responses = count of rated reviews (rating above zero) submitted in the period; change % = (current − previous) ÷ previous × 100
customer-feedback:total-responses.formula_explanation: Counts the number of customer reviews (with a valid rating) submitted in the selected period, from MART_CUSTOMER_FEEDBACK_DETAIL. It is the volume of feedback the period's sentiment metrics are based on.
how_to_read: The main value is the count of rated reviews in the period
Only reviews with a rating above zero are counted
The percentage change compares volume to the prior period
Higher volume gives more reliable NPS, Promoter %, and rating figures
A drop in responses can make sentiment metrics noisier
decisions_answered: How much customer feedback are we collecting?
Is review volume growing or shrinking versus the prior period?
Do we have enough responses to trust the sentiment metrics?

customer-feedback:avg-rating.title: Average Rating
customer-feedback:avg-rating.formula: Average Rating = average of each day's average star rating (1-5) over the trailing 7 days; change = current 7-day average − prior 7-day average
customer-feedback:avg-rating.formula_explanation: Computed from the 7-day trend ending at date_to: the backend takes each day's average star rating and then averages those daily values (an unweighted mean of daily means). The comparison value is the same calculation over the previous period's 7-day window.
how_to_read: The main value is the average star rating (1-5) over the trailing 7 days
It is an average of daily averages, so each day counts equally regardless of volume
Days with no rated reviews simply do not contribute
The change is the point difference versus the prior 7-day window
Cross-check with Rating Distribution to see whether the average hides polarized ratings
decisions_answered: What rating are customers giving our products lately?
Has average satisfaction moved versus the prior period?
Is recent product sentiment healthy?

formula_components.name: Average Rating
formula_components.formula: unweighted mean of daily average star ratings over the trailing 7 days (days with no rated reviews excluded)

customer-feedback:promoter-percent.title: Promoters
customer-feedback:promoter-percent.formula: Promoters % = reviews rated 4 or 5 stars ÷ all rated reviews × 100%; change = current % − prior %
customer-feedback:promoter-percent.formula_explanation: The share of rated reviews that are promoters, where promoters are defined as 4-5 star reviews (proxy for NPS promoters). Computed in MART_CUSTOMER_FEEDBACK_DETAIL over the selected period, with the change as the simple percentage-point difference versus the prior period.
how_to_read: The main value is the percentage of reviews rated 4 or 5 stars
Promoters are 4-5 stars; passives are 3 stars; detractors are 1-2 stars
Only reviews with a rating above zero are included
The change is the absolute percentage-point shift versus the comparison period
Read with NPS Distribution to see the full promoter/passive/detractor split
decisions_answered: What share of customers are enthusiastic promoters (4-5 stars)?
Is our promoter base growing or shrinking?
Are we generating enough advocacy to offset detractors?

customer-health:customers_with_scores.title: Customers with Scores
customer-health:customers_with_scores.formula: Customers with Scores = count of customers that have a computed health score
customer-health:customers_with_scores.formula_explanation: Counts how many customers have a computed health score (i.e. a usable churn-risk prediction). This is the scored population that every other health metric on this dashboard is calculated against.
how_to_read: The main value is the number of customers who currently have a health score
A larger scored population means the dashboard's risk and CLV metrics are more representative
Compare against your total customer base to gauge prediction coverage
A sudden drop can signal a data-pipeline or scoring-model gap
Use the sparkline to see how scored coverage has trended month over month
decisions_answered: How many customers do we have a churn-risk score for?
Is our predictive coverage broad enough to trust the other health metrics?
Has scoring coverage changed recently, hinting at a data issue?

cohort-analysis:avg_churn.title: Average Churn Risk
cohort-analysis:avg_churn.formula: Average Churn Risk = average of the per-customer churn-risk score (0-100) across customers acquired in the selected period; change = current − comparison
cohort-analysis:avg_churn.formula_explanation: Averages the per-customer churn-risk score (0-100 scale) across all customers acquired in the selected period. It summarizes how at-risk the cohort population is overall.
how_to_read: The value is the average churn-risk percentage across the cohort
Higher values mean more of the cohort is likely to lapse
The change versus the comparison period shows whether risk is rising or easing
It pairs with the critical-risk count to gauge how concentrated the risk is
Use it as a portfolio-level health gauge, not a per-customer prediction
decisions_answered: How at-risk is this cohort of customers on average?
Is overall churn risk trending up or down versus the prior period?
Do we need a broad retention push for recently acquired customers?

cohort-analysis:avg_clv.title: Average CLV
cohort-analysis:avg_clv.formula: Average CLV = average net lifetime value across customers acquired in the period with at least one order
cohort-analysis:avg_clv.formula_explanation: Averages net lifetime value across all customers who have placed at least one order in the selected period. It shows the typical value of a customer in the cohort.
how_to_read: The value is the average lifetime value per paying customer
Higher CLV indicates more valuable customers being acquired or retained
Compare to the comparison period to see if customer quality is improving
Read it alongside Average Orders and Repeat Rate to understand what drives value
Multiply by customer count to approximate total cohort value
decisions_answered: What is a typical customer worth in this cohort?
Is the value of acquired customers rising or falling?
Are we attracting higher-value customers over time?

cohort-analysis:avg_orders.title: Avg Orders Per Customer
cohort-analysis:avg_orders.formula: Avg Orders Per Customer = average total orders per customer across customers acquired in the period with at least one order
cohort-analysis:avg_orders.formula_explanation: Averages the total number of orders per customer across everyone who has ordered at least once in the period. It measures how frequently customers come back to purchase.
how_to_read: The value is the average number of orders each customer has placed
Values above 1 indicate meaningful repeat purchasing
Rising averages signal improving customer loyalty and frequency
Read it with Repeat Rate to separate frequency from one-time buyers
Low averages suggest most customers are not coming back
decisions_answered: How often does a typical customer purchase?
Is purchase frequency improving over time?
Should we invest more in repeat-purchase and loyalty programs?

cohort-analysis:repeat_rate.title: Repeat Purchase Rate
cohort-analysis:repeat_rate.formula: Repeat Purchase Rate = customers with 2 or more orders ÷ customers with at least one order × 100%
cohort-analysis:repeat_rate.formula_explanation: The share of customers who have placed two or more orders out of all customers who have placed at least one. It measures how well first-time buyers are converted into repeat buyers.
how_to_read: The value is the percentage of buyers who came back for a second order
Higher rates mean stronger retention of first-time purchasers
The change versus the prior period shows whether loyalty is improving
A low repeat rate points to a one-and-done acquisition problem
Pair it with Avg Orders to see depth of repeat behavior
decisions_answered: What share of our customers buy more than once?
Is our repeat-purchase conversion improving or declining?
Do we need stronger post-purchase or win-back flows?

cohort-analysis:total_ltv.title: Total Customer LTV
cohort-analysis:total_ltv.formula: Total Customer LTV = sum of net lifetime value across customers acquired in the period with at least one order
cohort-analysis:total_ltv.formula_explanation: Sums net lifetime value across every customer who has placed at least one order in the selected period. It represents the total realized value of the cohort.
how_to_read: The value is the combined lifetime value of all customers in the cohort
It scales with both customer count and per-customer value
Compare to the prior period to track overall portfolio growth
A rising total with flat customer count means CLV per customer is improving
Use it as the top-line value of the customers acquired in this window
decisions_answered: What is the total value of customers acquired in this period?
Is total customer value growing versus the comparison period?
Which cohorts contribute the most to overall LTV?

churn-analysis:avg_churn_probability.title: Average Churn Probability
churn-analysis:avg_churn_probability.formula: Average Churn Probability = average of the per-customer churn-risk score (0-100) across all customers that existed by the end of the period
churn-analysis:avg_churn_probability.formula_explanation: Averages the per-customer churn-risk score (0-100 scale) across all customers who existed by the end of the period. It is the headline gauge of how likely the customer base is to churn.
how_to_read: The value is the average churn probability across all customers
Higher percentages mean a greater portion of customers are at risk
Track the trend to see whether retention efforts are working
Read it alongside High-Risk Customers to see how the average is distributed
Spikes warrant investigating recent acquisition or engagement changes
decisions_answered: How likely is our overall customer base to churn?
Is average churn risk improving or worsening over time?
Are our retention initiatives moving the needle?

churn-analysis:clv_at_risk.title: CLV at Risk
churn-analysis:clv_at_risk.formula: CLV at Risk = sum of net lifetime value of customers at High or Critical churn-risk level (churn-risk score ≥ 60); percentage = at-risk CLV ÷ total portfolio CLV × 100
churn-analysis:clv_at_risk.formula_explanation: Sums the lifetime value of every customer flagged High or Critical churn risk. It quantifies the revenue exposure tied up in customers most likely to leave.
how_to_read: The main value is the total CLV held by high- and critical-risk customers
The percentage shows what share of total CLV is at risk
Large values mean retention has a high financial payoff
Compare against total portfolio CLV to size the exposure
Use it to justify and prioritize retention spend
decisions_answered: How much customer value is exposed to churn?
What share of total CLV sits with high-risk customers?
How much could we save by retaining at-risk customers?

churn-analysis:high_risk_customers.title: High-Risk Customers
churn-analysis:high_risk_customers.formula: High-Risk Customers = count of customers at High or Critical churn-risk level (churn-risk score ≥ 60); percentage = high-risk count ÷ customers with a prediction × 100
churn-analysis:high_risk_customers.formula_explanation: Counts customers whose mart-assigned churn-risk level is High or Critical (churn score of 60 or above). These are the customers most likely to lapse without intervention.
how_to_read: The main value is the number of High and Critical risk customers
The percentage shows their share of customers with a prediction
Higher counts mean a larger retention workload
Compare to your outreach capacity to prioritize action
Pair with CLV at Risk to focus on the most valuable at-risk customers
decisions_answered: How many customers are at high or critical churn risk?
What share of scored customers need intervention?
Do we have enough capacity to reach all at-risk customers?

churn-analysis:unsubscribe_rate.title: Unsubscribe Rate
churn-analysis:unsubscribe_rate.formula: Unsubscribe Rate = email unsubscribes ÷ total customers × 100%
churn-analysis:unsubscribe_rate.formula_explanation: Divides the count of email unsubscribes (from Klaviyo list health) by the total customer count to express unsubscribes as a percentage. It is an email-channel engagement-erosion signal shown alongside the churn metrics.
how_to_read: The value is the percentage of customers who have unsubscribed from email
Higher rates signal weakening email engagement and reachability
Rising unsubscribe rates often precede higher churn
Read it with churn probability to connect email fatigue to attrition
Persistent increases point to messaging frequency or relevance problems
decisions_answered: What portion of customers are opting out of email?
Is email engagement eroding over time?
Should we adjust email cadence or content to retain subscribers?

ceo-pulse:revenue.title: Today's Revenue
ceo-pulse:revenue.formula: Today's Revenue = net revenue over the selected period (Magento source of truth); Change % vs comparison period
ceo-pulse:revenue.formula_explanation: Sum of NET_REVENUE from the pre-computed mart_ceo_daily_pulse table (Magento source of truth) aggregated across all days in the selected range. The change percentage compares the current period total against the comparison-period total when a comparison range is supplied; the sparkline plots daily NET_REVENUE.
how_to_read: The main value shows total revenue for the selected period
Green percentage indicates improvement vs the comparison period
Red percentage indicates decline vs the comparison period
The sparkline shows the daily revenue trend for context
Click to drill down into revenue by hour, product, or channel
decisions_answered: How much revenue have we made this period?
Are we ahead or behind the comparison period?
Is there a concerning revenue decline requiring investigation?
How does the daily trajectory look within the period?

formula_components.name: Revenue
formula_components.formula: sum of net revenue over the period

ceo-pulse:orders.title: Today's Orders
ceo-pulse:orders.formula: Today's Orders = total orders over the selected period; Change % vs comparison period
ceo-pulse:orders.formula_explanation: Count of purchase orders from the pre-computed mart_ceo_daily_pulse table, summed across all days in the selected range. Order count is a leading indicator: changes in volume affect revenue and fulfillment planning. The sparkline shows daily TOTAL_ORDERS.
how_to_read: The main value shows total orders for the selected period
The sparkline shows the daily order trend for pattern recognition
Compare the change percentage to gauge momentum vs the comparison period
Watch for unusual drops that might indicate site issues
Click to drill down into orders by hour, product, or customer type
decisions_answered: How many orders have come in this period?
Is order volume trending up or down?
Are we on pace to hit our order targets?
Should fulfillment prepare for higher or lower volume?

formula_components.name: Orders
formula_components.formula: sum of orders over the period

ceo-pulse:aov.title: Average Order Value
ceo-pulse:aov.formula: Average Order Value = net revenue ÷ orders over the selected period; Change % vs comparison period
ceo-pulse:aov.formula_explanation: Average revenue per order, computed as period NET_REVENUE divided by period TOTAL_ORDERS from mart_ceo_daily_pulse. AOV indicates basket size and is influenced by product mix, promotions, and cross-selling effectiveness. The sparkline shows per-day AOV.
how_to_read: The main value shows the period's average order value in dollars
Compare to the comparison period via the change percentage
Higher AOV often correlates with catalog orders or bulk purchases
Lower AOV may indicate successful acquisition of new small-order customers
Click to analyze AOV by product category, customer type, or promo code
decisions_answered: What is our average basket size this period?
Is AOV higher or lower than the comparison period?
Are promotions affecting basket size positively or negatively?
Should we focus on upselling and cross-selling strategies?

formula_components.name: AOV
formula_components.formula: net revenue ÷ total orders over the period

ceo-pulse:roas.title: Blended ROAS
ceo-pulse:roas.formula: Blended ROAS = net revenue ÷ total ad spend (Google + Meta); with platform=google uses Google conversion value ÷ Google spend
ceo-pulse:roas.formula_explanation: Return on Ad Spend computed as period NET_REVENUE divided by period TOTAL_AD_SPEND (Google + Meta) from mart_ceo_daily_pulse. With platform=google the metric instead uses GOOGLE_CONVERSION_VALUE / GOOGLE_SPEND; platform=meta falls back to blended because Meta conversion value is not in the mart. Values above 4.0x indicate excellent efficiency.
how_to_read: The main value shows blended ROAS as a multiplier (e.g., 5.96x)
Green color (4.0x+) indicates excellent efficiency
Amber color (3.0-4.0x) indicates acceptable but monitor closely
Red color (below 2.5x) indicates concerning efficiency - investigate
The sparkline shows the daily BLENDED_ROAS trend for momentum
decisions_answered: What is our overall marketing efficiency?
Are we meeting our ROAS targets?
Is ROAS trending up or down over the period?
Should we scale up or pull back marketing spend?

formula_components.name: Blended ROAS
formula_components.formula: net revenue ÷ total ad spend (Google + Meta)

ceo-pulse:user_segments.title: New vs Returning
ceo-pulse:user_segments.formula: Split of site visitors into new versus returning, where new users = GA4 new users and returning users = total users − new users; the donut center shows total users and each slice its share of total users.
ceo-pulse:user_segments.formula_explanation: Distribution of period site visitors between new and returning users from mart_ceo_daily_pulse GA4 user counts: new_customers = NEW_USERS, returning_customers = TOTAL_USERS - NEW_USERS. Donut center shows TOTAL_USERS. A healthy mix shows both strong acquisition and retention.
how_to_read: The donut shows the proportion of new (blue) vs returning (purple) users
Center value shows total unique users for the period
Healthy range: 25-40% new users, 60-75% returning
High new % may indicate strong acquisition or loss of returning customers
High returning % may indicate retention strength or acquisition challenges
decisions_answered: What is our mix of new vs returning visitors this period?
Are we acquiring enough new customers?
Is our returning customer base engaging as expected?
Do we have a healthy balance for sustainable growth?

weekly-exec:conversion_rate.title: Conv. Rate
weekly-exec:conversion_rate.formula: Conversion Rate (estimated) = engaged sessions ÷ sessions × 100 × 0.1 (engaged-session proxy); WoW Change %
weekly-exec:conversion_rate.formula_explanation: Conversion rate is an APPROXIMATION computed in build_kpi_summary as engaged_sessions / sessions * 100 * 0.1 (using engaged sessions as a proxy for conversions, with a 0.1 scaling factor). It is NOT a true transactions/sessions rate. Data source: MART_CHANNEL_REVENUE_DAILY engaged_sessions and sessions.
how_to_read: The main value is an ESTIMATED conversion rate (engaged-session proxy)
Use the trend rather than the absolute percentage
WoW change compares the estimated rate week over week
For an exact CVR, use real transaction counts
Treat this as directional only
decisions_answered: Is our estimated conversion rate improving or declining?
Is engagement quality trending up week over week?
Does the proxy CVR align with revenue trends?

weekly-exec:channel_performance.title: Channel Performance
weekly-exec:channel_performance.formula: One row per channel for the current period: revenue, sessions, engagement rate and revenue-per-session, plus week-over-week revenue and session change percentages versus the prior period, ranked by current revenue.
weekly-exec:channel_performance.formula_explanation: Per-channel breakdown from MART_CHANNEL_REVENUE_DAILY for the current period vs the comparison period: revenue, sessions, engagement rate, revenue-per-session, plus WoW revenue and session change percentages. Ordered by current revenue descending.
how_to_read: Each row is a marketing channel sorted by revenue
Revenue and sessions columns show absolute scale
Change percentages compare against the prior period
Engagement rate and revenue/session show traffic quality
Click a channel to drill into its detailed dashboard
decisions_answered: Which channels drive the most revenue and traffic?
Which channels are growing or declining week over week?
Which channels convert traffic most efficiently?
Where should we focus channel investment?

monthly-review:channel_profitability.title: Channel Profitability
monthly-review:channel_profitability.formula: One row per channel for the current month: profit = attributed revenue − ad spend, with ROAS (revenue ÷ spend) and revenue-per-session. Google spend is split across paid search/shopping/cross-network by revenue share, Meta spend maps to paid social, and owned/earned channels carry zero spend. Ranked by revenue.
monthly-review:channel_profitability.formula_explanation: Table showing profitability metrics for each marketing channel for the current month (month-start through date_to). Revenue and sessions come from MART_CHANNEL_REVENUE_DAILY; ad spend is pulled from MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE (GOOGLE_SPEND/META_SPEND) and mapped onto paid channels. Owned and earned channels (Direct, Organic, Email, Referral, SMS) carry zero ad spend and are inherently profitable.
how_to_read: Table sorted by attributed revenue descending - highest-revenue channels at top
Revenue is attributed channel revenue from GA4 channel marts for the month
Spend is Google/Meta ad cost mapped onto paid channels (owned/earned channels show 0)
Profit column = Revenue - Spend; green for positive, red for negative
ROAS = Revenue / Spend; shows N/A for owned/earned channels with no spend
decisions_answered: Which channels are most profitable after accounting for ad spend?
What is the ROAS for each paid channel?
Which channels should we scale vs. reduce spending on?
How much are owned channels (Email, Organic) contributing?

formula_components.name: ROAS
formula_components.formula: Attributed revenue ÷ allocated ad spend (blank when spend is 0)

monthly-review:cohort_retention.title: Cohort Retention
monthly-review:cohort_retention.formula: Heatmap of customer retention rate by monthly acquisition cohort; each row is a cohort (first-order month) and each column is months since first order (M0 through M6), the cell being active customers ÷ cohort size × 100 over the trailing six cohorts.
monthly-review:cohort_retention.formula_explanation: Heatmap of customer retention rates by monthly acquisition cohort, read directly from the pre-computed MART_CUSTOMER_COHORT mart. Each row is a cohort (COHORT_MONTH); columns are months since first order (M0 through M6). The cell value is the mart's RETENTION_RATE (active customers / cohort size x 100). M0 is the acquisition month.
how_to_read: Each row represents a monthly acquisition cohort (COHORT_MONTH)
Columns show months since first order (M0 through M6)
Cell color intensity indicates retention rate - darker = higher retention
M0 is the acquisition month; M1 is the critical first-repurchase window
Compare rows to see whether newer cohorts retain better than older cohorts
decisions_answered: How well are we retaining customers after acquisition?
What is our typical M1 (first month) retention rate?
Are recent cohorts retaining better or worse than historical cohorts?
When do most customers make their second purchase?

monthly-review:gross_margin_trend.title: Gross Margin Trend
monthly-review:gross_margin_trend.formula: Monthly estimated gross margin percentage over the trailing 12 months = (net revenue × 50% assumed COGS − total ad spend) ÷ net revenue × 100.
monthly-review:gross_margin_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks estimated gross margin percentage by month over the trailing 12 months. Since true COGS requires ERP integration, the mart query assumes a 50% COGS benchmark on NET_REVENUE, then subtracts TOTAL_AD_SPEND, divided by revenue. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months over the trailing 12-month period
Y-axis shows estimated gross margin percentage after ad spend
The line trends margin over time - look for upward or downward patterns
Each point is the monthly margin: (Revenue x 0.50 - Ad Spend) / Revenue x 100
Sharp drops may indicate increased ad spend or promotional periods
decisions_answered: Is our gross margin trending up or down over time?
Are we hitting our margin target after ad costs?
How is marketing spend impacting our profitability?
Are there seasonal patterns in our margin performance?

monthly-review:ltv_cac_trend.title: LTV:CAC Trend
monthly-review:ltv_cac_trend.formula: Monthly ratio of estimated lifetime value to acquisition cost over the trailing 12 months: LTV = (net revenue ÷ orders) × 1.5, CAC = total ad spend ÷ new users, ratio = LTV ÷ CAC.
monthly-review:ltv_cac_trend.formula_explanation: Tracks the ratio of estimated Customer Lifetime Value to Customer Acquisition Cost by month. LTV is estimated as monthly AOV (revenue / orders) times a 1.5 repeat-purchase multiplier. CAC is total ad spend divided by NEW_USERS for the month. Computed in SQL/Python against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months over the trailing 12-month period
Y-axis shows the LTV:CAC ratio for each month
A ratio of 3x or more indicates healthy unit economics
Per-point metadata carries the underlying LTV, CAC, AOV and new-user counts
A falling ratio means acquisition cost is rising faster than customer value
decisions_answered: Are our unit economics healthy (LTV > 3x CAC)?
Is customer acquisition cost rising faster than lifetime value?
Should we invest more in acquisition or focus on retention?
How has customer value vs. acquisition cost changed over time?

monthly-review:mer_trend.title: MER Trend
monthly-review:mer_trend.formula: Monthly Marketing Efficiency Ratio over the trailing 12 months = net revenue ÷ total ad spend (0 when spend is 0).
monthly-review:mer_trend.formula_explanation: Marketing Efficiency Ratio by month: total revenue divided by total ad spend. A higher MER means more revenue generated per ad dollar. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE over a trailing 12-month window.
how_to_read: Chart shows MER trending over the trailing 12 months
Y-axis shows MER as a multiple (e.g., 4.0x means $4 revenue per $1 ad spend)
Higher values are better - more revenue per ad dollar
Each point is a month's NET_REVENUE / TOTAL_AD_SPEND
Sharp spikes may indicate reduced ad spend or seasonal revenue increases
decisions_answered: How efficient is our overall marketing spend?
Are we meeting our MER target?
Is marketing efficiency improving or declining over time?
Should we increase or decrease ad spend based on efficiency?

monthly-review:new_vs_returning.title: New vs Returning Revenue
monthly-review:new_vs_returning.formula: Monthly split of orders between new and returning customers over the trailing 12 months: new % = new customer orders ÷ total orders × 100, returning % = returning customer orders ÷ total orders × 100, shown as stacked series.
monthly-review:new_vs_returning.formula_explanation: Shows the monthly split between new and returning customers by order share. Each month emits two stacked series: new customer percentage and returning customer percentage, both computed as order counts over total orders. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE. Note: the split is order-based (NEW/RETURNING_CUSTOMER_ORDERS), not strictly revenue-based.
how_to_read: Stacked chart shows the new vs returning split per month
New series = new customer orders as a percent of total orders
Returning series = returning customer orders as a percent of total orders
X-axis shows months over the trailing 12-month period
Watch whether the returning share grows as the customer base matures
decisions_answered: What percentage of orders come from new vs returning customers?
Are we over-reliant on new customer acquisition?
Is our retention strategy driving repeat purchases?
How is the new/returning mix evolving over time?

monthly-review:revenue_vs_plan.title: Revenue vs Plan
monthly-review:revenue_vs_plan.formula: Per channel (top 15 by revenue), the variance of actual revenue against plan, where plan = prior-period channel revenue grown 5%; variance = actual − plan and variance % = (actual − plan) ÷ plan × 100.
monthly-review:revenue_vs_plan.formula_explanation: Compares actual revenue against plan for each channel, where plan is the comparison-period (or prior-month) channel revenue grown 5%. The chart value is the variance (Actual - Plan) per channel, with variance % in metadata. Computed in SQL against MART_CHANNEL_REVENUE_DAILY, top 15 channels by actual revenue.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a channel's variance vs plan, sorted by actual revenue
Positive (green) bars exceed plan; negative (red) bars fall short
Plan = prior-period channel revenue grown by 5%
Metadata carries actual revenue, plan revenue, and variance %
Focus on channels with the largest absolute variances first
decisions_answered: Which channels are driving revenue growth vs. underperforming?
Did we hit our overall revenue plan this period?
Where should we investigate performance issues?
Which channels should we consider scaling based on performance?

monthly-review:gross_margin.title: Gross Margin
monthly-review:gross_margin.formula: Gross Margin % (estimated) = (net revenue × 50% assumed COGS − total ad spend) ÷ net revenue × 100
monthly-review:gross_margin.formula_explanation: Estimated gross margin percentage for the selected period, assuming a 50% COGS benchmark on NET_REVENUE less total ad spend, over revenue. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE. Because true COGS is not available, this is an estimate.
how_to_read: The main value shows the period's estimated gross margin percentage
Assumes 50% COGS, then subtracts ad spend, divided by revenue
Compare to the prior period to see whether margin is expanding
A declining margin can signal rising ad costs or heavier discounting
This is an estimate until true COGS data is integrated
decisions_answered: What is our estimated gross margin after ad costs this period?
Is margin improving or eroding versus the prior period?
Is marketing spend compressing profitability?

monthly-review:ltv_cac_ratio.title: LTV:CAC Ratio
monthly-review:ltv_cac_ratio.formula: LTV:CAC = estimated LTV ÷ CAC, where LTV = (net revenue ÷ orders) × 1.5 and CAC = total ad spend ÷ new users
monthly-review:ltv_cac_ratio.formula_explanation: Ratio of estimated Customer Lifetime Value to Customer Acquisition Cost for the period. LTV is AOV (revenue / orders) times a 1.5 repeat-purchase multiplier; CAC is total ad spend divided by NEW_USERS. Computed in SQL/Python against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE. A ratio of 3x or more is considered healthy.
how_to_read: The main value shows the LTV:CAC ratio for the period
3x or higher indicates healthy unit economics
LTV is estimated as AOV x 1.5; CAC is ad spend per new user
Compare to prior period to spot deteriorating economics
A falling ratio means acquisition cost is outpacing customer value
decisions_answered: Are our unit economics healthy (LTV > 3x CAC)?
Is CAC rising faster than estimated LTV?
Should we invest more in acquisition or retention?

monthly-review:mer.title: MER (Marketing Efficiency)
monthly-review:mer.formula: MER (Marketing Efficiency Ratio) = net revenue ÷ total ad spend (Google + Meta), 0 when spend is 0
monthly-review:mer.formula_explanation: Marketing Efficiency Ratio for the period: total revenue divided by total ad spend. Measures how much revenue is generated per ad dollar across all platforms. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE.
how_to_read: The main value shows MER as a multiple (e.g., 4.0x = $4 revenue per $1 spend)
Higher is better - more revenue per ad dollar
Includes all revenue, not just ad-attributed revenue
Compare to the prior period to see efficiency trend
Declining MER suggests diminishing returns or saturation
decisions_answered: How efficient is our overall marketing spend?
Are we meeting our MER target?
Should we scale or pull back ad spend based on efficiency?

monthly-review:new_customer_revenue.title: New Customer Revenue %
monthly-review:new_customer_revenue.formula: New Customer Revenue % = new-customer orders ÷ total orders × 100 (order-share proxy)
monthly-review:new_customer_revenue.formula_explanation: Share of the period's orders that came from new customers, used as a proxy for new-customer revenue mix. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE as new customer orders over total orders. Note: this is order-share based, not strictly dollar-revenue based.
how_to_read: The main value shows new customers as a percent of total orders
A healthy mature mix is often 35-45% new
Too high may indicate over-reliance on acquisition
Too low may indicate growth stagnation
Compare to prior period to track the acquisition/retention balance
decisions_answered: What share of orders comes from new customers?
Are we over-investing in acquisition vs retention?
Is the new-customer mix trending the right way?

monthly-review:revenue.title: Revenue vs Plan
monthly-review:revenue.formula: Revenue vs Plan: net revenue over the period, measured against target = prior-period revenue × 1.05
monthly-review:revenue.formula_explanation: Total net revenue for the selected period, measured against a target of prior-period revenue grown 5%. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE. NET_REVENUE is the Magento-sourced revenue field.
how_to_read: The main value shows total net revenue for the period
It is compared against a target of prior period + 5%
Green change indicates beating the growth target; red indicates a miss
Revenue is summed from NET_REVENUE in the daily pulse mart
Use alongside MER and gross margin for a full profitability picture
decisions_answered: How much revenue did we generate this period?
Did we beat our 5% growth target?
Is revenue trending above or below plan?

formula_components.name: Revenue
formula_components.formula: sum of net revenue over the period

quarterly-review:channel_saturation.title: Channel Saturation Analysis
quarterly-review:channel_saturation.formula: Per Google campaign type and quarter, ROAS (conversions value ÷ spend) plotted against running cumulative spend and revenue, revealing where extra spend stops yielding proportional return; only quarters with over 1,000 spend, trailing ~3 years.
quarterly-review:channel_saturation.formula_explanation: Shows diminishing returns as cumulative ad spend grows per Google campaign type. For each quarter and CAMPAIGN_TYPE the query computes ROAS (conversions value / spend) plus running cumulative spend and revenue via window functions, revealing where additional spend stops yielding proportional return. Computed in SQL against MART_GOOGLE_CAMPAIGN_PERFORMANCE.
how_to_read: Each line/series represents a Google campaign type (channel)
X-axis tracks quarters; ROAS and cumulative spend/revenue are in metadata
Flattening ROAS as cumulative spend rises signals saturation
Only channels spending over $1,000 in a quarter are shown
Compare channels to see which still have room to scale profitably
decisions_answered: Which channels are approaching diminishing returns?
Where does additional ad spend stop driving proportional revenue?
Which channels still have room to scale efficiently?
How has ROAS evolved as we have invested more over time?

formula_components.name: Cumulative Spend / Revenue
formula_components.formula: Running total of spend and revenue across quarters within each channel

quarterly-review:channel_yoy_growth.title: YoY Revenue by Channel
quarterly-review:channel_yoy_growth.formula: For each channel (Google, Meta, Organic, Email, Direct), the year-over-year growth of the selected quarter's GA4-attributed revenue = (current-year revenue − prior-year revenue) ÷ prior-year revenue × 100.
quarterly-review:channel_yoy_growth.formula_explanation: For each channel and comparison year, compares the selected quarter's attributed revenue against the same quarter one year earlier. Channel revenue is built from MART_ATTRIBUTION_DAILY columns (GOOGLE_GA4_REVENUE, META_GA4_REVENUE, ORGANIC_REVENUE, EMAIL_REVENUE, DIRECT_REVENUE). The chart value is the YoY growth percentage.
how_to_read: Each bar/cluster represents a channel for a given year
Bar value is the YoY revenue growth percentage for that channel and quarter
Channels: Google, Meta, Organic, Email, Direct (attribution-based)
Compare years within a channel to see the growth trajectory
Sorted by current revenue, highest first
decisions_answered: Which channels are growing or declining year-over-year?
How does this quarter compare to the same quarter in prior years?
Which channels deserve more investment based on growth?
Are there channels showing concerning YoY decline?

quarterly-review:customer_acquisition.title: Customer Acquisition Trend
quarterly-review:customer_acquisition.formula: New customer orders per quarter (area chart) over roughly the trailing three years up to the selected quarter, with returning customer orders carried alongside each point (Magento order data).
quarterly-review:customer_acquisition.formula_explanation: Tracks new customer acquisition by quarter using Magento order data (NEW_CUSTOMER_ORDERS), not Klaviyo subscribers. The chart value is the new-customer count per quarter; returning customers are carried in metadata. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE, covering the trailing 3 years up to the selected quarter.
how_to_read: X-axis shows quarters over roughly the past 3 years
Y-axis / value shows new customer orders per quarter (Magento-based)
Returning-customer counts are available per point in metadata
Look for seasonal patterns and overall acquisition trajectory
Compare the current quarter against the same quarter in prior years
decisions_answered: Is customer acquisition growing or shrinking over time?
Are there seasonal patterns in new-customer acquisition?
How does current-quarter acquisition compare to history?
How does new vs returning order volume trend by quarter?

quarterly-review:efficiency_metrics.title: Quarterly Efficiency Metrics
quarterly-review:efficiency_metrics.formula: One row per platform for the selected quarter and the same quarter a year earlier: ROAS = conversion value ÷ spend, CPC = spend ÷ clicks, CPA = spend ÷ conversions, CPM = spend ÷ impressions × 1000.
quarterly-review:efficiency_metrics.formula_explanation: Consolidates ad efficiency metrics by platform (CHANNEL) for the selected quarter and the same quarter one year earlier. ROAS, CPC, CPA and CPM are each computed from summed spend, clicks, conversions, conversion value and impressions. Computed in SQL against MART_MARKETING_DAILY.
how_to_read: Each row represents one platform for one quarter (current and prior year)
ROAS = conversion value / spend; higher is better
CPC = spend / clicks; CPA = spend / conversions; lower is better
CPM = spend / impressions x 1000
Compare rows across years to see whether efficiency is improving
decisions_answered: Which platform delivers better ROAS?
Is our efficiency improving or declining year-over-year?
What is our cost per acquisition by platform?
How do CPC and CPM trends indicate competition and costs?

quarterly-review:market_opportunities.title: Market Opportunity Sizing
quarterly-review:market_opportunities.formula: Per channel (top 10 with over 10,000 quarter-to-date revenue), current-year QTD attributed revenue with a sized growth opportunity = QTD revenue × 15%, and a growth rate versus the same prior-year period.
quarterly-review:market_opportunities.formula_explanation: Sizes growth opportunity by channel using quarter-to-date attributed revenue (current year Q1 through the selected quarter) versus the same period prior year. The opportunity is a conservative 15% of current QTD revenue. Channel revenue is built from MART_ATTRIBUTION_DAILY (Google, Meta, Organic, Email, Direct). Computed in SQL.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show QTD revenue by channel, ranked highest first
The opportunity segment is an estimated 15% of current QTD revenue
Growth rate vs prior-year QTD is carried per channel in metadata
Only channels above $10K QTD revenue are shown (top 10)
Weigh opportunity relative to current revenue, not just bar length
decisions_answered: Which channels have the largest absolute revenue opportunity?
Where should incremental marketing budget be allocated?
How does current performance map to growth potential?
Which channels are already large vs. have room to grow?

formula_components.name: Growth Rate
formula_components.formula: (QTD revenue − prior-year QTD revenue) ÷ prior-year QTD revenue

quarterly-review:seasonality.title: Seasonality Pattern Analysis
quarterly-review:seasonality.formula: Heatmap of monthly seasonality per channel for the months in the selected quarter (selected year vs prior year): seasonality index = each month's average daily revenue ÷ the channel's overall average daily revenue × 100, where 100 is average; channels under 100 average daily revenue are excluded.
quarterly-review:seasonality.formula_explanation: Reveals monthly seasonality per channel for the months in the selected quarter, comparing the selected year vs prior year. The seasonality index is each month's average daily revenue divided by the channel's overall average daily revenue, times 100 (100 = average). Computed in SQL against MART_CHANNEL_REVENUE_DAILY; the chart value is average daily revenue with the index in metadata.
how_to_read: Rows represent marketing channels (CHANNEL_GROUPING)
Columns represent the months within the selected quarter
Seasonality index of 100 = average; above 100 = peak, below 100 = slow
Cell value/intensity reflects average daily revenue; index is in metadata
Channels below $100 overall avg daily revenue are excluded
decisions_answered: When are peak periods for each marketing channel?
Which months should we increase budget for specific channels?
Are there low-performing periods where budget could be reallocated?
How do seasonality patterns differ between channels?

quarterly-review:strategic_initiatives.title: Strategic Initiatives
quarterly-review:strategic_initiatives.formula: Per Google campaign type (initiative) for the selected quarter: current revenue (conversions value), with ROAS = revenue ÷ spend and year-over-year growth = (current revenue − prior-year revenue) ÷ prior-year revenue × 100; only initiatives with spend, ranked by revenue.
quarterly-review:strategic_initiatives.formula_explanation: Classifies Google campaigns by CAMPAIGN_TYPE (e.g., PMAX, Video, Shopping, Search) and reports current-quarter revenue, spend, ROAS and YoY revenue growth versus the same quarter prior year. The chart value is current revenue per initiative. Computed in SQL against MART_GOOGLE_CAMPAIGN_PERFORMANCE.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a campaign type / strategic initiative
Bar value is current-quarter revenue (CONVERSIONS_VALUE)
ROAS (revenue / spend) and YoY growth are carried in metadata
Only initiatives with current spend > 0 are shown
Sorted by current revenue, highest first
decisions_answered: Which campaign types are driving the most revenue this quarter?
Which initiatives have the best ROAS?
Which initiatives are growing or declining year-over-year?
Where should we double down or pull back on campaign types?

quarterly-review:ad_spend.title: Ad Spend
quarterly-review:ad_spend.formula: Ad Spend = Google Ads spend + Meta Ads spend for the quarter; YoY change vs same quarter prior year
quarterly-review:ad_spend.formula_explanation: Total quarterly ad spend across Google and Meta, with year-over-year comparison against the same quarter prior year. Google and Meta sub-totals are carried in context. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE.
how_to_read: The main value shows total ad spend (Google + Meta) for the quarter
Compared against the same quarter one year earlier
Google and Meta sub-totals are available in the metric context
Sparkline shows ad spend across recent quarters
Read alongside ROAS to judge whether spend is efficient
decisions_answered: How much did we spend on ads this quarter?
Is ad spend up or down year-over-year?
How is spend split between Google and Meta?

formula_components.name: Ad Spend
formula_components.formula: Google spend + Meta spend for the quarter

formula_components.name: YoY Change %
formula_components.formula: (current − same quarter prior year) ÷ prior year × 100

quarterly-review:new_customers.title: New Customers
quarterly-review:new_customers.formula: New Customers = count of new-customer orders (first-time buyers) in the quarter; YoY change vs the same quarter last year
quarterly-review:new_customers.formula_explanation: Count of new customer orders in the quarter, sourced from Magento order data (NEW_CUSTOMER_ORDERS), compared against the same quarter prior year. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE.
how_to_read: The main value shows new customer orders for the quarter (Magento-based)
Compared against the same quarter one year earlier
An upward trend indicates accelerating acquisition
Read with ad spend to gauge cost of acquisition
This is order-based, not unique-customer based
decisions_answered: How many new customers did we acquire this quarter?
Is acquisition up or down year-over-year?
Is acquisition keeping pace with ad spend?

formula_components.name: New Customers
