formula_components.formula: count of new subscriber/person records created in the quarter

formula_components.name: YoY Change %
formula_components.formula: (current − same quarter prior year) ÷ prior year × 100

quarterly-review:revenue.title: Quarterly Revenue
quarterly-review:revenue.formula: Quarterly Revenue = total net revenue (Magento) for the quarter; YoY Growth % vs the same quarter last year
quarterly-review:revenue.formula_explanation: Total quarterly net revenue from all channels, using Magento NET_REVENUE as the source of truth, compared against the same quarter prior year. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE.
how_to_read: The main value shows total net revenue for the quarter
Compared against the same quarter one year earlier (YoY)
Sparkline shows revenue across recent quarters
Revenue is Magento-sourced (NET_REVENUE), not GA4
Use with ad spend and ROAS for a full quarterly picture
decisions_answered: How much revenue did we generate this quarter?
Is revenue growing year-over-year?
How does this quarter compare to recent quarters?

formula_components.name: Revenue
formula_components.formula: sum of GA4 total revenue for the quarter

quarterly-review:roas.title: Blended ROAS
quarterly-review:roas.formula: Blended ROAS = quarterly net revenue (Magento) ÷ total ad spend (Google + Meta); YoY change vs the same quarter last year
quarterly-review:roas.formula_explanation: Blended return on ad spend for the quarter: total net revenue divided by total (Google + Meta) ad spend, compared against the same quarter prior year. Computed in SQL against MART_CEO_DAILY_PULSE.
how_to_read: The main value shows blended ROAS as a multiple (revenue per ad dollar)
Computed as quarterly NET_REVENUE / TOTAL_AD_SPEND
Compared against the same quarter one year earlier
Sparkline shows ROAS across recent quarters
Higher is better; falling ROAS warrants investigation
decisions_answered: How efficient was our ad spend this quarter?
Is ROAS improving or declining year-over-year?
Should we scale or pull back spend next quarter?

formula_components.name: Blended ROAS
formula_components.formula: quarterly revenue ÷ total ad spend (Google + Meta)

product-seasonality:category_heatmap.title: Category Seasonal Heatmap
product-seasonality:category_heatmap.formula: Heatmap of monthly revenue per top-level category (category L1, defaulting blanks to 'Other'); rows = categories, columns = months, cell = revenue summed for that category-month
product-seasonality:category_heatmap.formula_explanation: Monthly revenue per top-level category (CATEGORY_L1), arranged as a heatmap with categories as rows and calendar months as columns. Color intensity reflects revenue magnitude, revealing which categories peak in which months.
how_to_read: Each row is a top-level product category; each column is a calendar month
Brighter / more intense cells indicate higher revenue for that category-month
Scan a row left-to-right to see a category's seasonal shape across the year
Compare rows to find categories that peak in the same months
Empty or dim cells show months where a category generates little revenue
decisions_answered: Which categories peak in which months?
When should I plan inventory and promotions for each category?
Are there categories with complementary seasonal patterns?
Which category-month combinations drive the most revenue?

product-seasonality:day_of_week.title: Revenue Pattern
product-seasonality:day_of_week.formula: Average revenue by day of week = revenue on that weekday ÷ number of distinct dates of that weekday; with order volume (units purchased) and percent of week = that day's average ÷ sum of all days' averages × 100
product-seasonality:day_of_week.formula_explanation: Average per-day revenue for each day of the week, computed as total revenue on that weekday divided by the number of distinct dates of that weekday in the period. Percent-of-week shows each day's share of the summed daily averages.
how_to_read: Each bar is the average revenue for one day of the week
Higher bars indicate consistently stronger sales days
Order Count (ITEMS_PURCHASED) provides volume context behind revenue
Percent of Week shows each day's share of the weekly average total
Compare weekday vs weekend bars to see the shopping rhythm
decisions_answered: Which days of the week drive the most revenue?
When should I schedule promotions or email sends for maximum impact?
Are weekends or weekdays stronger for this catalog?
Which days are underperforming and may need a boost?

formula_components.name: Avg daily revenue
formula_components.formula: weekday revenue ÷ distinct dates of that weekday

formula_components.name: Percent of week
formula_components.formula: day average ÷ sum of daily averages × 100

product-seasonality:quarterly_by_categor.title: Revenue by Category
product-seasonality:quarterly_by_categor.formula: Revenue split into the four calendar quarters for each top-level category (category L1, blanks → 'Other'), limited to the top 10 categories by total revenue
product-seasonality:quarterly_by_categor.formula_explanation: Revenue split into the four calendar quarters for each top-level category (CATEGORY_L1), limited to the top 10 categories by total revenue. Reveals which quarter each category leans on most.
how_to_read: Each category shows its revenue broken out across Q1-Q4
Compare the four quarter segments within a category to find its peak quarter
Categories are ranked by total annual revenue (top 10 shown)
A category dominated by one quarter is highly seasonal
Even quarter splits indicate a year-round seller
decisions_answered: Which quarter drives the most revenue for each category?
Which categories are highly seasonal vs year-round?
How should quarterly inventory and budget be allocated by category?
Which categories contribute the most total revenue?

product-seasonality:weekly_trend.title: Weekly Trend vs Historical Average
product-seasonality:weekly_trend.formula: Actual weekly revenue vs a baseline that averages revenue across weeks sharing the same week-of-year within the range; variance % = (actual − baseline) ÷ baseline × 100
product-seasonality:weekly_trend.formula_explanation: Actual weekly revenue compared to a baseline that averages revenue across weeks with the same week-of-year number within the selected window. Variance shows how far each week deviates from its same-week-of-year baseline.
how_to_read: The actual line shows weekly revenue for the selected period
The baseline line is the average for that week-of-year across the range
Weeks above the baseline have positive variance; below is negative
Large variances flag unusual weeks worth investigating
Sustained deviations from baseline signal a shift in trend
decisions_answered: Are recent weeks outperforming or underperforming their historical norm?
Which weeks deviated most from the expected baseline?
Is revenue trending above or below seasonal expectations?
Where should I dig in to explain an anomalous week?

product-seasonality:yoy_comparison.title: Revenue Year-over-Year Comparison
product-seasonality:yoy_comparison.formula: Monthly revenue grouped by year and month across the current year plus the two prior years, with monthly units and the count of unique products sold for context
product-seasonality:yoy_comparison.formula_explanation: Monthly revenue (plus units and unique products) grouped by year and month, spanning the current year and the two prior years so the same calendar months can be compared across years.
how_to_read: Each series is a year; the x-axis is the month
Compare the same month across years to gauge year-over-year change
Diverging lines show months where performance shifted between years
Three years of history reveal whether a pattern is consistent
Units and unique-product counts add context behind revenue swings
decisions_answered: Is this month's revenue up or down versus the same month last year?
Which months show the strongest year-over-year growth or decline?
Are seasonal peaks consistent across years?
Is the catalog's overall trajectory improving year over year?

new-products:product_stages.title: Performance by Product Stage
new-products:product_stages.formula: Products bucketed into lifecycle stages by days since first appearance (Launch 0–30, Early 31–60, Ramping 61–90, Growing 91–180, Established 180+), then aggregated over the last 90 days: product count, revenue, views, purchases, conversion = purchases ÷ views × 100, and avg revenue per product = revenue ÷ product count
new-products:product_stages.formula_explanation: Products are bucketed into lifecycle stages by days since first appearance (launch date = MIN(DATE_DAY)), then aggregated over the last 90 days of activity to show count, revenue, views, purchases, conversion and average revenue per product per stage.
how_to_read: Each row is a lifecycle stage from Launch through Established
Product Count shows how many products sit in each stage
Revenue and Avg Revenue/Product reveal which stages monetize best
Conversion Rate (purchases / views) compares efficiency across stages
Aggregates reflect only the last 90 days of activity
decisions_answered: How do new products perform as they age through their lifecycle?
At which stage do products start meaningfully converting?
Are recently launched products underperforming established ones?
Where in the lifecycle should I focus optimization effort?

new-products:avg_revenue_per_product.title: Avg Revenue Per Product
new-products:avg_revenue_per_product.formula: Avg Revenue Per Product = total revenue from new products ÷ count of distinct new products (each new product's activity from its launch date through the end date)
new-products:avg_revenue_per_product.formula_explanation: Total revenue from newly launched products divided by the count of distinct new products, giving the average revenue each new product has generated since its launch.
how_to_read: The value is the mean revenue per newly launched product
A higher figure means new launches are monetizing more effectively on average
It is diluted by products that launched but generated little or no revenue
Read alongside Success Rate to separate breadth from depth of performance
Compare to prior period to see if launch quality is improving
decisions_answered: How much revenue does a typical new product generate?
Is the quality of new launches improving over time?
Are we launching too many low-revenue products?
How does average new-product revenue compare to the prior period?

new-products:launch_count.title: New Products Launched
new-products:launch_count.formula: New Products Launched = count of distinct products whose first-ever appearance falls within the launch window (placeholder IDs '(not set)' and '(other)' excluded; a product launches on its earliest activity date)
new-products:launch_count.formula_explanation: Count of distinct products whose first-ever appearance in the data falls within the selected launch window. Each product is counted as launched on the first date it shows any activity.
how_to_read: The value is the number of products that launched in the selected period
Products are identified by their first appearance (MIN(DATE_DAY))
Placeholder IDs '(not set)' and '(other)' are excluded
A rising count indicates an accelerating launch cadence
Pair with revenue metrics to judge whether volume is translating to results
decisions_answered: How many new products did we launch this period?
Is our launch cadence accelerating or slowing?
Are we hitting our new-product introduction targets?
Is launch volume aligned with the revenue these products produce?

new-products:success_rate.title: Success Rate
new-products:success_rate.formula: Success Rate = (count of new products that earned any revenue since launch ÷ total new products in the window) × 100
new-products:success_rate.formula_explanation: Percentage of newly launched products that generated any revenue (revenue > 0) since launch, out of all new products in the window. Measures the share of launches that achieved at least minimal commercial traction.
how_to_read: The value is the percent of new products that earned any revenue
Only revenue > 0 counts as a 'success' here (a low bar by design)
A low rate means many launches never sold at all
Read with Avg Revenue Per Product to gauge both breadth and depth
Compare to prior period to track launch-quality trend
decisions_answered: What share of our new products actually sold anything?
Are too many launches failing to gain traction?
Is launch success improving versus the prior period?
Should we tighten the criteria for what we launch?

new-products:total_revenue.title: Total Revenue
new-products:total_revenue.formula: Total Revenue = sum of revenue across all new products from each product's launch date through the end date (new product = first appearance within the launch window, placeholders excluded)
new-products:total_revenue.formula_explanation: Total revenue generated by all products that launched within the selected window, summed across their activity from launch through the end date.
how_to_read: The value is total revenue attributable to this period's new launches
It accumulates each new product's revenue from its launch date onward
A larger figure means new products are contributing more to the top line
Read with Launch Count to see revenue per unit of launch volume
Compare to prior period to track the revenue impact of new launches
decisions_answered: How much revenue are our new products contributing in total?
Are new launches a meaningful share of overall revenue?
Is new-product revenue growing versus the prior period?
Do launch volume and revenue contribution move together?

product-overview:revenue.title: Total Revenue
product-overview:revenue.formula: Total Revenue = sum of product revenue over the selected period
product-overview:revenue.formula_explanation: Sum of all product revenue from MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY over the selected period. Daily revenue is summed across every product to give the total realized product revenue, with a per-day sparkline showing the trend.
how_to_read: The main value shows total product revenue for the selected period
The percentage change compares to the comparison period
Green upward trend indicates strong product demand
The sparkline shows the daily revenue trajectory across the period
Click to drill into revenue by product or category
decisions_answered: How much product revenue did we generate this period?
Is product revenue growing or declining vs the comparison period?
Which days drove the most revenue?
Is overall product performance healthy?

product-overview:units.title: Units Sold
product-overview:units.formula: Units Sold = sum of units purchased over the selected period
product-overview:units.formula_explanation: Total number of product units purchased across all products in the period, from MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY. A pure volume measure independent of price, useful for demand and inventory planning.
how_to_read: The main value shows total units sold across all products
Compare units growth to revenue growth to detect pricing/mix shifts
Units growing faster than revenue can indicate discounting
The sparkline shows daily unit volume across the period
Click to view units by product or category
decisions_answered: How many product units did customers buy?
Is unit volume growing faster or slower than revenue?
Is demand healthy across the catalog?
Do we need to adjust inventory levels?

product-overview:avg_price.title: Avg Product Price
product-overview:avg_price.formula: Avg Product Price = product revenue ÷ units sold (over the selected period)
product-overview:avg_price.formula_explanation: Average selling price per unit, calculated as total revenue divided by total units sold over the period (MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY). Reflects product mix and promotional impact rather than list price alone.
how_to_read: The main value shows the average price per unit sold
Rising average price may indicate a shift toward premium products
Declining price can signal promotions or a mix shift to value items
The sparkline shows the daily average-price trend
Click to view price distribution by product or category
decisions_answered: What is the typical price point customers are paying?
Is our product mix shifting toward higher or lower prices?
Are promotions affecting average selling price?
How is pricing trending over the period?

product-overview:conversion.title: Conversion Rate
product-overview:conversion.formula_explanation: Product purchase-to-view conversion rate: units purchased divided by units viewed, expressed as a percentage (MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY). Measures how effectively product views turn into purchases across the catalog.
how_to_read: The main value shows the percentage of product views that convert to purchases
Higher rates indicate stronger product-page and pricing effectiveness
Compare to the comparison period to spot conversion shifts
The sparkline shows the daily conversion-rate trend
Click to analyze conversion by product or category
decisions_answered: How efficiently are product views converting to purchases?
Is conversion improving or deteriorating vs the prior period?
Are there products dragging overall conversion down?
Where should conversion-optimization efforts focus?

product-overview:categories.title: Category Performance
product-overview:categories.formula: Treemap of product-category revenue nested L1 → L2 → L3; rectangle size = revenue summed per category branch (kept only where revenue > 0)
product-overview:categories.formula_explanation: Displays product category performance as a hierarchical treemap where rectangle size represents revenue contribution. Revenue is summed per L1/L2/L3 category and nested into the category hierarchy (e.g., Plants and Seeds > Vegetable Seeds > Tomato Seeds).
how_to_read: Rectangle size indicates revenue contribution - larger = more revenue
Colors differentiate category groups for visual distinction
Click on a category to drill down into sub-categories
Use the breadcrumb trail to navigate back up the hierarchy
Labels show category name and revenue for larger segments
decisions_answered: Which product categories drive the most revenue?
How is revenue distributed across the category hierarchy?
Are there underperforming categories with growth potential?
Which sub-categories within a parent are the strongest?

product-overview:opportunities.title: Opportunity Finder
product-overview:opportunities.formula: Two-tab table. Optimize = products with ≥1000 views and conversion < 5%; Promote = products with 100–1000 views and conversion > 15%. Conversion % = units purchased ÷ views × 100
product-overview:opportunities.formula_explanation: Single chart with two tabs. The Optimize tab lists high-traffic, low-conversion products (>=1000 views, <5% conversion) that have proven demand but fail to convert. The Promote tab lists low-traffic, high-conversion products (100-1000 views, >15% conversion) that sell well but need visibility. The backend returns the two sets pre-split as optimize[] and promote[].
how_to_read: Use the Optimize / Promote tabs to switch between the two opportunity types
Optimize products get many views but convert poorly - fix product page, price, or positioning
Promote products convert well but get few views - feature, email, or advertise them
Conversion Rate and Views columns prioritize where to act first
The Action column gives the recommended next step
decisions_answered: Which high-traffic products are underconverting and need page/pricing fixes?
Which proven converters deserve more promotion and visibility?
Where is the largest revenue opportunity - optimization or promotion?
What products should be featured in campaigns?

product-affinity:avg_items_per_order.title: Avg Items per Order
product-affinity:avg_items_per_order.formula: Avg Items per Order = total items ordered ÷ total orders (over the period)
product-affinity:avg_items_per_order.formula_explanation: Average number of items per order, computed by dividing total items ordered by total orders over the period from MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY_DAILY. Higher values indicate more successful cross-selling and basket building.
how_to_read: The main value shows the average number of items per order
Higher values indicate effective cross-sell and bundling
Compare to the comparison period to gauge basket-size momentum
The sparkline shows the daily average-items trend
Click to explore the item-count distribution
decisions_answered: How many items does a typical order contain?
Is our cross-sell strategy increasing basket size?
Are customers discovering complementary products?
Is items-per-order trending up or down?

product-affinity:bundle_pct.title: Bundle Penetration
product-affinity:bundle_pct.formula: Bundle Penetration = (orders containing a bundle product ÷ total orders) × 100
product-affinity:bundle_pct.formula_explanation: Percentage of orders that contain bundle products, computed as bundle orders divided by total orders over the period from MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY_DAILY. Indicates how much of the order base is driven by bundle/kit/pack products.
how_to_read: The main value shows the percentage of orders containing bundle products
Rising bundle penetration indicates successful bundle merchandising
Compare to the comparison period to track bundle adoption
The sparkline shows the daily bundle-percentage trend
Click to explore which bundles are driving the share
decisions_answered: What share of orders include bundle products?
Is bundle adoption growing over time?
Are our bundle merchandising efforts working?
Should we create or promote more bundles?

product-affinity:repeat_purchase_rate.title: Repeat Purchase Rate
product-affinity:repeat_purchase_rate.formula: Repeat Purchase Rate = (repeat customers ÷ unique customers) × 100 (rolling 90-day basis)
product-affinity:repeat_purchase_rate.formula_explanation: Percentage of customers who made repeat purchases, computed as repeat customers divided by unique customers over the period from MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY_DAILY. A retention/loyalty signal tied to cross-sell and follow-on purchase behaviour.
how_to_read: The main value shows the percentage of customers with repeat purchases
Higher rates indicate stronger loyalty and follow-on buying
Compare to the comparison period to track retention momentum
The sparkline shows the daily repeat-rate trend
Click to explore cross-sell patterns driving repeat purchases
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers buy again?
Is repeat purchasing improving or declining?
Is our retention and cross-sell strategy effective?
Should we invest more in follow-on campaigns?

product-affinity:top_lift.title: Top Affinity Lift
product-affinity:top_lift.formula: Top Affinity Lift = highest lift score among product pairs with at least 5 co-purchases, where lift = probability of buying A and B together ÷ (probability of A × probability of B)
product-affinity:top_lift.formula_explanation: Highest affinity lift score among product pairs from the pre-computed MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY mart, restricted to pairs with at least 5 co-purchases. Lift above 1 indicates a positive association; the maximum surfaces the single strongest product relationship.
how_to_read: The main value shows the strongest lift score among all product pairs
Lift > 3.0 indicates a very strong, actionable affinity
Lift near 1.0 means products are bought independently
Only pairs with 5+ co-purchases are considered for stability
Click to view the full affinity table behind this score
decisions_answered: What is the strongest product affinity in the catalog?
Is there a pair worth bundling or cross-promoting?
How strong is our best co-purchase relationship?
Which pairing should anchor recommendation widgets?

product-affinity:affinity_table.title: Top Product Affinities
product-affinity:affinity_table.formula: Top 30 product pairs by affinity lift (lift > 1): each row shows lift, support (co-occurrence frequency as a fraction), and the two directional confidences (share of A-buyers who also bought B, and vice-versa)
product-affinity:affinity_table.formula_explanation: Uses association-rule-mining metrics from the pre-computed MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY mart. Support shows co-occurrence frequency, confidence shows conditional purchase probability, and lift shows affinity relative to random chance (values above 1 are positive associations). Pairs are filtered to lift > 1 and ranked by lift.
how_to_read: Sort by Lift to find strongest true affinities (not just popular products)
Lift > 3.0 indicates very strong affinity worth acting on
Lift > 1.5 shows moderate positive association
Lift = 1.0 means products are purchased independently
Use Support % to ensure statistical significance (higher is better)
decisions_answered: Which product pairs have the strongest true affinity?
Are high co-occurrence pairs actually related or just popular?
Which affinities are statistically significant enough to act on?
What is the probability of buying B given purchase of A?

formula_components.name: Confidence A→B
formula_components.formula: confidence A→B ÷ 100 (share of A buyers who also bought B)

formula_components.name: Confidence B→A
formula_components.formula: confidence B→A ÷ 100 (share of B buyers who also bought A)

product-overview:product_matrix.title: Product Performance Matrix
product-overview:product_matrix.formula: Scatter of products: x = current-period revenue, y = revenue growth % = (current revenue − prior revenue) ÷ prior revenue × 100, bubble size = current units; only products with prior revenue > 100, quadranted at revenue 5000 / growth 0
product-overview:product_matrix.formula_explanation: Plots products on a 2x2 matrix with revenue on the x-axis and growth rate on the y-axis. Products are classified into quadrants: Stars (high revenue, high growth), Rising Stars (low revenue, high growth), Cash Cows (high revenue, low growth), and Dogs (low revenue, low growth).
how_to_read: Each bubble represents a product - size indicates units sold
Top-right (green): Stars - high revenue AND growing fast - your winners
Top-left (blue): Rising Stars - low revenue but growing - watch and nurture
Bottom-right (yellow): Cash Cows - high revenue but slowing - milk but watch
Bottom-left (red): Dogs - low revenue and declining - consider action
Hover over bubbles to see product details
decisions_answered: Which products are our current stars driving growth?
Which emerging products should we invest in promoting?
Are any high-revenue products showing declining trends?
Which products might need to be discontinued or repositioned?

product-overview:funnel.title: Product View to Purchase Funnel
product-overview:funnel.formula: Site-wide product funnel: Views → Cart Adds → Purchases (each summed over the period), with view-to-cart % = cart adds ÷ views × 100 and cart-to-purchase % = purchases ÷ cart adds × 100
product-overview:funnel.formula_explanation: Tracks the product conversion journey from initial view through cart addition to final purchase. Each stage shows the volume of products that progressed, with conversion rates calculated between stages.
how_to_read: The funnel visualizes the customer journey: Views → Cart Adds → Purchases
Width of each stage represents relative volume - wider means more products
Drop-off between stages shows where customers abandon the path
Conversion rates below the funnel show percentage progression between stages
A healthy funnel maintains reasonable ratios at each stage
decisions_answered: At which stage are we losing the most potential customers?
What is our overall product conversion rate from view to purchase?
Is the cart-to-purchase rate healthy or is cart abandonment high?
Should we focus on improving product pages or checkout experience?

product-overview:seasonal.title: Seasonal Product Performance
product-overview:seasonal.formula: Calendar heatmap of monthly revenue with years as rows and months as columns; each cell = revenue summed for that year-month over a fixed trailing 2-year window
product-overview:seasonal.formula_explanation: Displays monthly revenue as a calendar heatmap with years as rows and months as columns. Color intensity indicates revenue magnitude, enabling quick identification of seasonal patterns and year-over-year comparisons.
how_to_read: Rows represent years, columns represent months (Jan-Dec)
Darker colors indicate higher revenue months
Compare same months across years to see YoY trends
Look for consistent patterns - spring planting season typically shows Q1 peaks
Hover over cells for exact revenue values and YoY change
decisions_answered: What are the seasonal revenue patterns for our products?
Which months consistently perform best year over year?
Is this year tracking ahead or behind prior years?
When should we ramp up inventory and marketing?

product-overview:top_products.title: Top Products by Revenue
product-overview:top_products.formula: Top 50 products by revenue; columns: revenue, units, avg price = revenue ÷ units, cart-to-view % = cart adds ÷ views × 100, purchase-to-view % = units ÷ views × 100, and revenue share % = product revenue ÷ total revenue × 100
product-overview:top_products.formula_explanation: Ranks products by total revenue with supporting metrics including units sold, average selling price, view-to-purchase conversion rate, and revenue share. Enables identification of top performers and comparison of efficiency metrics.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by revenue - highest performers at top
Compare conversion rates to identify efficiency differences
Revenue Share shows concentration - high share in few products = concentration risk
Sort by any column to reorder the analysis
Use search to find specific products
Export data for offline analysis
decisions_answered: Which individual products generate the most revenue?
How concentrated is revenue among top products?
Which products have high revenue but low conversion (price premium)?
Which products have high conversion but low revenue (underpriced or under-promoted)?

product-category:revenue.title: Category Revenue
product-category:revenue.formula: Category Revenue = sum of item revenue across level-3 categories over the period
product-category:revenue.formula_explanation: Aggregates total revenue from all product categories at Level 3 (e.g., Vegetable Seeds, Flower Seeds) within the selected date range. Excludes unclassified items to ensure accurate category attribution.
how_to_read: The main value shows total revenue across all product categories
The percentage change indicates growth or decline vs. prior period
Green upward trend suggests strong category performance
Sparkline shows the 14-day revenue trend pattern
Click to drill into detailed category breakdown
decisions_answered: What is the total revenue from categorized products?
Is overall category revenue growing or declining?
How does current period compare to the same period last year?
Which time periods show the strongest category performance?

product-category:revenue_trend.title: Category Revenue Trend
product-category:revenue_trend.formula: Daily revenue per category (multi-line) for the top level-3 categories (Vegetable Seeds, Flower Seeds, Herb Seeds, Bulbs, Plants) over the selected range
product-category:revenue_trend.formula_explanation: Time series visualization of daily revenue for selected top categories. Reveals seasonal patterns, promotional impacts, and relative performance trends across the category portfolio.
how_to_read: Each line represents a different product category
Color coding matches category legend below the chart
Peaks indicate high-revenue days (promotions, seasonality)
Hover over any point for exact values
Click legend items to show/hide specific categories
decisions_answered: How does category revenue fluctuate over time?
Are there seasonal patterns in category performance?
Which categories show the most volatility?
How do promotional events impact different categories?

product-category:treemap.title: Category Mix Treemap
product-category:treemap.formula: Treemap of revenue across the top 25 level-3 categories (flat 'All Products' → category); rectangle size = category revenue summed over the period
product-category:treemap.formula_explanation: Hierarchical visualization showing revenue distribution with nested rectangles. Rectangle size represents revenue proportion while color indicates year-over-year growth. Enables quick identification of both scale and momentum.
how_to_read: Rectangle size shows revenue proportion - larger areas mean more revenue
Nested rectangles show parent-child category relationships
Color indicates YoY growth: green for growth, red for decline
Labels show category name and abbreviated revenue
Click any rectangle to drill into its subcategories
decisions_answered: How is revenue distributed across the category hierarchy?
Which categories are large but declining (red, large boxes)?
Which small categories show strong growth potential?
What is the relationship between parent and child categories?

product-category:leaderboard.title: Category Revenue Leaderboard
product-category:leaderboard.formula: Top 15 level-3 categories ranked by revenue; each bar carries YoY growth % vs same dates last year and revenue share % = category revenue ÷ total revenue × 100
product-category:leaderboard.formula_explanation: Ranks categories by total revenue with year-over-year growth indicators. This view reveals which categories drive the business and how their performance is trending relative to historical baselines.
how_to_read: Bars show revenue for top 10 categories, ranked highest to lowest
Longer bars indicate higher revenue contribution
Growth badges at bar ends show YoY change (green positive, red negative)
Vegetable Seeds typically leads for this seed company
Click any bar to drill into that category's subcategories
decisions_answered: Which categories generate the most revenue?
How are top categories trending year-over-year?
Is there healthy diversity or concentration in top categories?
Which categories are gaining or losing market share?

product-category:category_table.title: Category Performance Table
product-category:category_table.formula: One row per level-3 category (top 50 by revenue): revenue, items sold, views, purchase rate = items ÷ views × 100, avg price = revenue ÷ items, YoY growth % vs same dates last year, and a 14-day revenue sparkline
product-category:category_table.formula_explanation: Comprehensive sortable table showing all key metrics for each category. Includes inline sparklines for trend visualization and enables multi-metric comparison and export for deeper analysis.
how_to_read: Click column headers to sort by any metric
Sparklines show 14-day trend direction
Growth badges are color-coded (green positive, red negative)
Use search to filter specific categories
Export button downloads data as CSV
decisions_answered: How do all metrics compare across categories?
Which categories excel in specific metrics but lag in others?
What are the detailed numbers behind chart visualizations?
Which categories need attention based on multiple metrics?

product-category:growth_scatter.title: YoY Growth Analysis
product-category:growth_scatter.formula: Scatter of level-4 categories: x = current-year revenue, y = YoY growth % = (current revenue − prior-year revenue) ÷ prior-year revenue × 100, bubble size = items sold; only categories with current revenue > 10000
product-category:growth_scatter.formula_explanation: Scatter plot positioning categories by both their absolute revenue (X-axis) and growth rate (Y-axis). Bubble size represents volume. This reveals the strategic position of each category in your portfolio.
how_to_read: X-axis shows current year revenue (right = more revenue)
Y-axis shows YoY growth (up = faster growth)
Bubble size indicates items sold volume
Green bubbles are growing, red are declining
Upper-right quadrant = Stars (high revenue, high growth)
decisions_answered: Which categories are stars (high revenue, high growth)?
Which are cash cows (high revenue, low/negative growth)?
Which are rising opportunities (low revenue, high growth)?
Which categories need turnaround or discontinuation?

product-category:conversion_funnel.title: Category Conversion Funnel
product-category:conversion_funnel.formula: Per level-4 category (1000+ views, top 20 by revenue): Views → Cart Adds → Purchases with view-to-cart % = cart adds ÷ views × 100 and cart-to-purchase % = purchases ÷ cart adds × 100
product-category:conversion_funnel.formula_explanation: Shows the conversion funnel stages for a selected category. Tracks customer journey from product view through cart addition to purchase, identifying where drop-offs occur and optimization opportunities exist.
how_to_read: Three horizontal stages show Views, Cart Adds, and Purchases
Stage width narrows proportionally to drop-off rate
Conversion rates between stages shown as percentages
Use dropdown to switch between categories
Wide drop-offs indicate optimization opportunities
decisions_answered: Where do customers drop off in the purchase journey?
Is the view-to-cart rate healthy for this category?
Is cart abandonment a problem for certain categories?
Which categories have the best end-to-end conversion?

product-category:seasonality.title: Category Seasonality Heatmap
product-category:seasonality.formula: Month-by-category heatmap; each cell = monthly revenue per top level-3 category over a trailing 24-month window (color normalized per category row)
product-category:seasonality.formula_explanation: Month-by-category heatmap showing seasonal revenue patterns. Color intensity is normalized within each category to reveal relative seasonality regardless of absolute revenue differences between categories.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months, Y-axis shows categories
Darker cells indicate higher revenue for that month
Color is normalized per row - compare horizontally within a category
Look for vertical patterns indicating market-wide seasonality
Click any cell to filter the page to that month and category
decisions_answered: When do different categories peak throughout the year?
Are there off-season opportunities to address?
How should marketing and inventory align to seasonality?
Which categories are most and least seasonal?

ml-life-cycle-stage:active_customers.title: Active Customers
ml-life-cycle-stage:active_customers.formula: Active Customers = count of customers whose predicted lifecycle stage is 'Active'
ml-life-cycle-stage:active_customers.formula_explanation: Number of customers the lifecycle model places in the 'Active' stage for the prediction window. Sourced from the model's stage counts.
how_to_read: Higher is healthier — a large active base means most customers are still transacting.
Compare against At-Risk and Lapsed counts to gauge overall base health.
Track over time to see whether the active pool is growing or eroding.
decisions_answered: How large is my currently active customer base?
Is my active pool growing or shrinking versus prior runs?

ml-life-cycle-stage:at_risk_customers.title: At-Risk Customers
ml-life-cycle-stage:at_risk_customers.formula: At-Risk Customers = count of customers the lifecycle model flags as at risk of churning in the prediction horizon
ml-life-cycle-stage:at_risk_customers.formula_explanation: Count of customers the model flags as at risk of churning in the prediction horizon. These are the priority targets for retention action.
how_to_read: The bigger this number, the more retention budget is at stake.
Cross-reference with Stage Migration Flow to see where these customers are drifting.
Use it to size retention campaigns before customers fully lapse.
decisions_answered: How many customers need a retention intervention now?
How big is my imminent churn exposure?

ml-life-cycle-stage:avg_stage_duration_d.title: Avg Stage Duration
ml-life-cycle-stage:avg_stage_duration_d.formula: Avg Stage Duration = average number of days customers remain in their current lifecycle stage
ml-life-cycle-stage:avg_stage_duration_d.formula_explanation: Average time, in days, customers remain in their current lifecycle stage. A direct pass-through of the model's summary metric.
how_to_read: Longer durations in early stages can mean slow activation; shorter ones mean fast progression.
Read alongside Stage Migration Flow to interpret whether movement is healthy.
Shifts over time signal a change in how quickly customers move through the lifecycle.
decisions_answered: How long do customers typically stay in a lifecycle stage?
Is the pace of lifecycle movement speeding up or slowing down?

ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_transition_rat.title: Stage Transition Rate
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_transition_rat.formula: Stage Transition Rate = (customers predicted to improve stage + customers predicted to decline stage) ÷ total customers scored
ml-life-cycle-stage:stage_transition_rat.formula_explanation: Fraction of all scored customers whose lifecycle stage is predicted to change (either improving or declining). Higher values mean a more dynamic base.
how_to_read: High transition rates mean lifecycle positions are unstable and worth watching.
Pair with the migration flow to see whether transitions skew positive or negative.
A near-zero rate means most customers are holding their current stage.
decisions_answered: How dynamic is my customer base right now?
What share of customers are about to change lifecycle stage?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:customers_sc.title: Customers Scored
ml-segment-value-trajectory:customers_sc.formula: Customers Scored = total number of customers the value-trajectory model scored this run
ml-segment-value-trajectory:customers_sc.formula_explanation: Total number of customers the value-trajectory model scored in this run. The denominator for every share metric on the page.
how_to_read: Confirms the model ran over your full expected base.
A sudden drop versus prior runs usually means a data or scoring issue.
Use it as the denominator when interpreting ascending/declining counts.
decisions_answered: How many customers did the trajectory model score this run?
Is coverage complete versus the prior run?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:model_accura.title: Model Accuracy
ml-segment-value-trajectory:model_accura.formula: Model Accuracy = top-1 classification accuracy of the trajectory model on the holdout set (how often the predicted class matched the actual outcome)
ml-segment-value-trajectory:model_accura.formula_explanation: How often the model's predicted trajectory class matched the actual outcome on held-out data. Higher means more trustworthy predictions.
how_to_read: Treat as a confidence dial for everything else on the page.
Compare to a naive baseline — meaningfully above chance means the signal is real.
If accuracy is low, weight the trajectory calls more cautiously.
decisions_answered: How much can I trust the trajectory predictions?
Is the model performing well enough to act on?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:ascending_cu.title: Ascending Customers
ml-segment-value-trajectory:ascending_cu.formula: Ascending Customers = count of customers predicted to move to a higher value trajectory (predicted class upgrade or big_upgrade)
ml-segment-value-trajectory:ascending_cu.formula_explanation: Number of customers whose spend trajectory is predicted to rise. These are growth opportunities worth nurturing.
how_to_read: A growing ascending pool signals upside in future revenue.
Target these customers with upsell/cross-sell to accelerate the rise.
Compare against Declining Customers to see net momentum.
decisions_answered: How many customers are on the way up in value?
Where should I focus growth investment?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:declining_cu.title: Declining Customers
ml-segment-value-trajectory:declining_cu.formula: Declining Customers = count of customers predicted to move to a lower value trajectory (predicted class downgrade or big_downgrade)
ml-segment-value-trajectory:declining_cu.formula_explanation: Number of customers whose spend trajectory is predicted to fall. These are the revenue-at-risk customers to defend.
how_to_read: A rising declining pool is an early warning of revenue erosion.
Prioritise retention/win-back for these customers before they slip further.
Read alongside Ascending Customers to judge overall direction.
decisions_answered: How many customers are losing value momentum?
Where is my revenue most at risk of eroding?

ml-segment-value-trajectory:stable_high_.title: Stable High-Value
ml-segment-value-trajectory:stable_high_.formula: Stable High-Value = count of top-tier customers (High, VIP or Whale segment) predicted to hold their value (trajectory class 'hold')
ml-segment-value-trajectory:stable_high_.formula_explanation: Count of top-tier customers predicted to hold their value (neither rising nor falling). This is your dependable revenue core.
how_to_read: A large stable high-value core means predictable base revenue.
Protect these customers — churn here is disproportionately costly.
Shrinkage here is a serious signal even if total counts look fine.
decisions_answered: How solid is my dependable high-value core?
Are my best customers staying put or drifting?

ml-high-value-propensity:model_auc.title: Model AUC
ml-high-value-propensity:model_auc.formula: Model AUC = area under the ROC curve for the high-value propensity classifier on the holdout set (0.5 = random, closer to 1.0 = cleaner separation)
ml-high-value-propensity:model_auc.formula_explanation: Ranking quality of the high-value propensity model. AUC of 0.5 is random; closer to 1.0 means it cleanly separates future high-value customers from the rest.
how_to_read: Above ~0.7 is usable; above ~0.8 is strong.
Higher AUC means the propensity scores rank customers more reliably.
Use it to decide how aggressively to act on the propensity tiers.
decisions_answered: Can I trust the high-value propensity scores?
How well does the model separate high-value from ordinary customers?

ml-high-value-propensity:high_propensity.title: High-Propensity Pool
ml-high-value-propensity:high_propensity.formula: High-Propensity Pool = count of customers scored into the high propensity-to-become-high-value tier
ml-high-value-propensity:high_propensity.formula_explanation: Number of customers scored into the high propensity-to-become-high-value tier. These are your prime nurture targets.
how_to_read: A larger pool means more upside to chase, but also more spend to cover it.
Pair with revenue concentration to see how much value the pool represents.
Use it to size high-value-acquisition campaigns.
decisions_answered: How many customers are likely to become high value?
How large is my prime nurture audience?

ml-high-value-propensity:predicted_new_h.title: Predicted New High-Value
ml-high-value-propensity:predicted_new_h.formula: Predicted New High-Value = count of customers the model expects to cross into high-value status
ml-high-value-propensity:predicted_new_h.formula_explanation: Count of customers the model expects to cross into high-value status. The forward-looking growth number for your top tier.
how_to_read: This is the expected expansion of your high-value segment.
Compare to current high-value count to gauge growth rate.
Use it to forecast future top-tier revenue contribution.
decisions_answered: How many new high-value customers can I expect?
How fast is my high-value tier set to grow?

ml-high-value-propensity:total_customers.title: Total Customers Scored
ml-high-value-propensity:total_customers.formula: Total Customers Scored = count of customers with a valid high-value propensity prediction in this run
ml-high-value-propensity:total_customers.formula_explanation: Total population the propensity model scored this run. The denominator behind every tier share and the pool sizes.
how_to_read: Confirms full base coverage for the run.
A drop versus prior runs usually flags a data/scoring problem.
Use it to put pool and tier counts into context.
decisions_answered: How many customers were scored this run?
Is propensity coverage complete?

ml-discount-response-propensity:model_au.title: Model AUC
ml-discount-response-propensity:model_au.formula: Model AUC = area under the ROC curve for the discount-response classifier on the holdout set (closer to 1.0 means cleaner separation of responders from non-responders)
ml-discount-response-propensity:model_au.formula_explanation: Ranking quality of the discount-response model. Closer to 1.0 means it cleanly separates customers who respond to discounts from those who don't.
how_to_read: Above ~0.7 is usable; above ~0.8 is strong.
Higher AUC means the response-probability scores are more trustworthy.
Use it to decide how much to lean on the responder tiers.
decisions_answered: Can I trust the discount-response scores?
How reliably does the model find genuine discount responders?

ml-discount-response-propensity:discount.title: Discount-Responsive Customers
ml-discount-response-propensity:discount.formula: Discount-Responsive Customers = count of customers the model predicts will respond to a discount offer
ml-discount-response-propensity:discount.formula_explanation: Number of customers the model predicts will respond to a discount offer. These are the customers where promotions are most efficient.
how_to_read: Target these customers with discounts to maximise incremental response.
A larger pool means promotions can drive more volume.
Pair with Revenue Protected to balance response against margin.
decisions_answered: Who actually responds to discounts?
How big is my efficient-discount audience?

ml-discount-response-propensity:high_res.title: High Responders
ml-discount-response-propensity:high_res.formula: High Responders = count of customers in the highest discount-response tier (strongest reactors to promotional pricing)
ml-discount-response-propensity:high_res.formula_explanation: Count of customers in the highest discount-response tier — the strongest reactors to promotional pricing.
how_to_read: These are your most discount-elastic customers.
Reserve your deepest, most targeted offers for this group.
Watch margin: high responders can erode profit if over-discounted.
decisions_answered: Which customers react most strongly to discounts?
Where will a promotion move the most volume?

ml-discount-response-propensity:revenue_.title: Revenue Protected
ml-discount-response-propensity:revenue_.formula: Revenue Protected = count of customers predicted NOT to need a discount to convert (would buy at full price, so exclude from discounting)
ml-discount-response-propensity:revenue_.formula_explanation: Number of customers predicted NOT to need a discount to convert. Discounting them just gives away margin, so the model recommends protecting that revenue.
how_to_read: Exclude these customers from blanket discount campaigns.
The larger this group, the more margin a smarter targeting saves.
Use it to quantify the cost of over-discounting today.
decisions_answered: Which customers should I NOT discount?
How much margin can I protect by targeting discounts better?

ml-message-affinity:total_segmented.title: Total Customers Segmented
ml-message-affinity:total_segmented.formula: Total Customers Segmented = number of customers the message-affinity model assigned a preferred message archetype to in the latest run
ml-message-affinity:total_segmented.formula_explanation: Total number of customers the message-affinity model assigned a preferred message archetype to. The denominator for all message shares.
how_to_read: Confirms full base coverage for the run.
A drop versus prior runs usually flags a data/scoring issue.
Use as the base when reading message-type distribution.
decisions_answered: How many customers have a message preference assigned?
Is message-affinity coverage complete?

ml-message-affinity:top_message_type.title: Top Message Type
ml-message-affinity:top_message_type.formula: Top Message Type = the most common predicted preferred message archetype across all scored customers (most frequent winning archetype)
ml-message-affinity:top_message_type.formula_explanation: The single message archetype (Promotional / Educational / Seasonal / Triggered) most customers prefer. Your default content angle.
how_to_read: Tells you which content theme resonates with the largest audience.
Use it to set the default tone for broad sends.
Read with the full distribution to avoid over-indexing on the mode.
decisions_answered: What message theme works for the most customers?
What should my default content angle be?

ml-message-affinity:high_confidence_pool.title: High-Confidence Pool
ml-message-affinity:high_confidence_pool.formula: High-Confidence Pool = count of scored customers whose winning message archetype scored above 50% predicted probability
ml-message-affinity:high_confidence_pool.formula_explanation: Number of customers where the model is confident about the preferred message type. Personalised messaging is safest for this group.
how_to_read: Personalise messaging for this pool first — the model is sure here.
Low-confidence customers are better served by your default theme.
A small pool means message preferences are diffuse across the base.
decisions_answered: For whom can I confidently personalise message content?
How large is my reliable personalisation audience?

ml-message-affinity:avg_confidence.title: Average Confidence
ml-message-affinity:avg_confidence.formula: Average Confidence = mean predicted confidence (softmax probability) of each customer's winning message archetype across all scored customers
ml-message-affinity:avg_confidence.formula_explanation: Average certainty of the message-affinity predictions. Higher means the model distinguishes message preferences more cleanly.
how_to_read: Higher average confidence means the whole page is more actionable.
For a 3-4 class model, values well above ~0.33 indicate real signal.
Track over runs — a drop can mean preferences are becoming noisier.
decisions_answered: How sure is the model about message preferences overall?
How much should I trust the message-affinity calls?

ml-message-affinity:top_affinity_signals.title: Top Affinity Signals
ml-message-affinity:top_affinity_signals.formula: Top model features (by gain) that most drive which message type a customer prefers
ml-message-affinity:top_affinity_signals.formula_explanation: The behavioural features that most influence which message type a customer prefers, ranked by model gain.
how_to_read: Top signals tell you what behaviour predicts message preference.
Use them to sanity-check that the model keys on sensible drivers.
Feed these signals back into segmentation and content strategy.
decisions_answered: What customer behaviours drive message preference?
Is the model relying on sensible signals?

ml-channel-affinity:avg_confidence.title: Average Confidence
ml-channel-affinity:avg_confidence.formula: Average Confidence = mean predicted channel confidence of the preferred channel across all scored customers
ml-channel-affinity:avg_confidence.formula_explanation: Average certainty of the channel-affinity predictions. Higher means the model distinguishes channel preferences more cleanly.
how_to_read: Higher average confidence makes channel routing decisions safer.
Compare to channel match (>0.6 threshold) to see how much of the base is confidently routed.
A drop over runs signals noisier channel preferences.
decisions_answered: How sure is the model about channel preferences overall?
How trustworthy are the channel-affinity calls?

ml-channel-affinity:channel_matched.title: Channel-Matched Customers
ml-channel-affinity:channel_matched.formula: Channel-Matched Customers = count of scored customers whose preferred-channel prediction confidence is above 0.6
ml-channel-affinity:channel_matched.formula_explanation: Number of customers the model confidently assigns to a single best channel (confidence above 0.6). These can be safely routed channel-first.
how_to_read: Route these customers channel-first — the model is confident.
Unmatched customers should stay on your default multi-channel mix.
A larger matched pool means more efficient channel spend.
decisions_answered: How many customers can I confidently route to a single channel?
How much of my base is channel-addressable?

ml-channel-affinity:multi_channel_pool.title: Multi-Channel Pool
ml-channel-affinity:multi_channel_pool.formula: Multi-Channel Pool = count of scored customers whose predicted preferred channel is Multi-channel rather than a single channel
ml-channel-affinity:multi_channel_pool.formula_explanation: Count of customers whose predicted preference is a multi-channel mix rather than a single channel. These reward cross-channel orchestration.
how_to_read: Reach this pool with coordinated cross-channel campaigns.
Single-channel sends under-serve these customers.
A large multi-channel pool argues for orchestration investment.
decisions_answered: Which customers need a cross-channel approach?
How big is my multi-channel-responsive audience?

ml-channel-affinity:top_channel.title: Top Channel
ml-channel-affinity:top_channel.formula: Top Channel = the most common predicted preferred marketing channel across all scored customers (most frequent preferred channel)
ml-channel-affinity:top_channel.formula_explanation: The single channel most customers prefer. Your default reach channel when you have to pick one.
how_to_read: Tells you where the largest addressable audience lives.
Use it to set default channel allocation.
Read with the full distribution to avoid starving smaller-but-valuable channels.
decisions_answered: Which channel reaches the most customers?
Where should default channel budget go?

ml-channel-response-curve:channels_at_sa.title: Channels at Saturation
ml-channel-response-curve:channels_at_sa.formula: Channels at Saturation = count of channels spending above 80% of their model-estimated optimal budget (current spend ÷ optimal spend > 0.8)
ml-channel-response-curve:channels_at_sa.formula_explanation: Number of channels already spending above 80% of their model-estimated optimal budget. Extra dollars here yield diminishing returns.
how_to_read: Stop pouring budget into saturated channels — marginal ROI is low.
Reallocate from saturated to under-spent channels.
Zero saturated channels means you still have room to scale spend.
decisions_answered: Which channels have I maxed out?
Where is additional spend being wasted?

ml-channel-response-curve:optimal_budget.title: Optimal Budget — Top Channel
ml-channel-response-curve:optimal_budget.formula: Optimal Budget — Top Channel = the largest recommended budget across channels in the model's optimal allocation
ml-channel-response-curve:optimal_budget.formula_explanation: The recommended budget for the single channel slated to receive the most spend under the model's optimal allocation.
how_to_read: Shows where the model wants the biggest single bet.
Compare to that channel's current spend to size the move.
Read with the full budget allocation, not in isolation.
decisions_answered: Which channel deserves the largest budget?
How much should my top channel receive?

ml-channel-response-curve:projected_incr.title: Projected Incremental Revenue
ml-channel-response-curve:projected_incr.formula: Projected Incremental Revenue = the additional revenue the model expects from reallocating spend to its recommended budget mix versus the current allocation
ml-channel-response-curve:projected_incr.formula_explanation: Additional revenue the model expects if you reallocate spend to its recommended budget mix versus the current allocation.
how_to_read: This is the prize for acting on the recommendation.
Weigh it against the effort/risk of shifting budget.
If small, the current allocation is already near-optimal.
decisions_answered: What's the upside of reallocating my channel budget?
Is rebalancing spend worth doing?

ml-channel-response-curve:total_recommen.title: Total Recommended Budget
ml-channel-response-curve:total_recommen.formula: Total Recommended Budget = sum of the recommended budgets across all channels under the model's optimal allocation
ml-channel-response-curve:total_recommen.formula_explanation: The total spend the model recommends across all channels under its optimal allocation. The envelope the per-channel recommendations sum to.
how_to_read: Compare to your current total spend to see if the plan asks for more or less.
Use it as the budget envelope when splitting across channels.
Read alongside projected incremental revenue to judge efficiency.
decisions_answered: What total budget does the optimal plan require?
Should I spend more, less, or just reallocate?

ml-timing-response:peak_response_hour.title: Peak Response Hour
ml-timing-response:peak_response_hour.formula: Peak Response Hour = the most common predicted best-response hour (0–23) across customers (mode of each customer's best hour)
ml-timing-response:peak_response_hour.formula_explanation: The single hour (0-23) when the largest share of customers are predicted to engage. Your default send time.
how_to_read: Schedule broad sends around this hour for the best aggregate response.
Read with the hourly response chart to see how sharp the peak is.
Per-customer best hours still vary — use this only as the default.
decisions_answered: When is the best time of day to send?
What default send hour maximises response?

ml-timing-response:best_day_of_week.title: Best Day of Week
ml-timing-response:best_day_of_week.formula: Best Day of Week = the most common predicted best-response weekday across customers (mode of each customer's best day)
ml-timing-response:best_day_of_week.formula_explanation: The weekday when the largest share of customers are predicted to engage. Your default send day.
how_to_read: Default broad campaigns to this day for peak aggregate response.
Compare with the daily response chart for the size of the edge.
Individual best days vary — treat this as a starting point.
decisions_answered: Which day of the week should I send on?
What default send day maximises response?

ml-timing-response:seasonal_sensitivity.title: Seasonal Sensitivity
ml-timing-response:seasonal_sensitivity.formula: Seasonal Sensitivity = average of each customer's seasonal responsiveness index — how much customer response swings with the season
ml-timing-response:seasonal_sensitivity.formula_explanation: How much customer response swings with the season, on average. Higher means timing campaigns to seasonal peaks matters more.
how_to_read: High sensitivity means seasonal timing is a real lever.
Low sensitivity means send timing matters less than content.
Read with the seasonal response chart to find the peak months.
decisions_answered: How much does seasonality affect response?
Is it worth timing campaigns to seasonal peaks?

ml-timing-response:personalization_oppor.title: Personalization Opportunity
ml-timing-response:personalization_oppor.formula: Personalization Opportunity = count of customers with a confident, distinct best send time (flagged high-confidence timing)
ml-timing-response:personalization_oppor.formula_explanation: Number of customers where the model is confident about their individual best send time, so per-customer timing personalisation will pay off.
how_to_read: Personalise send times for this pool first.
Customers outside it are fine on the default schedule.
A large pool argues for investing in send-time optimisation.
decisions_answered: For whom is send-time personalisation worth it?
How big is my timing-personalisation opportunity?

ml-discount-response:optimal_discount_ra.title: Optimal Discount Range
ml-discount-response:optimal_discount_ra.formula: Optimal Discount Range = the model's metadata range, else the revenue-maximizing discount ± 2.5 percentage points — the band expected to maximise net revenue
ml-discount-response:optimal_discount_ra.formula_explanation: The discount-depth band the model expects to maximise net revenue. Stay inside it to balance response uplift against margin given away.
how_to_read: Set promo depths within this band as the default.
Going deeper usually erodes margin faster than it lifts response.
Going shallower may leave incremental revenue on the table.
decisions_answered: What discount depth should I offer?
How deep can I discount before it stops paying off?

ml-discount-response:peak_response_rate.title: Peak Response Rate
ml-discount-response:peak_response_rate.formula: Peak Response Rate = the highest predicted response rate across all discount depths in the response curves
ml-discount-response:peak_response_rate.formula_explanation: The highest predicted response rate achievable at any discount depth. The ceiling on how much a promotion can move customers.
how_to_read: Shows the best-case response a discount can produce.
Read with the revenue curve — peak response rarely equals peak profit.
If the peak is low, discounting may not be your best lever.
decisions_answered: How much can discounts lift response at best?
Is the discount lever strong enough to matter?

ml-discount-response:rev_max_discount.title: Revenue-Maximizing Discount
ml-discount-response:rev_max_discount.formula: Revenue-Maximizing Discount = the discount depth of the row with the highest net revenue (uplift minus discount cost)
ml-discount-response:rev_max_discount.formula_explanation: The single discount depth that produces the highest net revenue (uplift minus discount cost). The profit-optimal promo depth.
how_to_read: This is the depth to default to when maximising net revenue.
Deeper than this gives away more margin than it earns.
Pair with the optimal range to set a practical promo band.
decisions_answered: What discount depth maximises net revenue?
Where is the profit-optimal promo point?

ml-discount-response:break_even_discount.title: Break-Even Discount
ml-discount-response:break_even_discount.formula: Break-Even Discount = the shallowest discount depth where net revenue first turns non-negative (uplift just covers the discount cost)
ml-discount-response:break_even_discount.formula_explanation: The shallowest discount depth at which incremental uplift just covers the cost of the discount. Below this, the promotion loses money.
how_to_read: Discounts shallower than break-even don't pay for themselves.
Use it as the floor for any promo depth.
The gap to the revenue-maximising depth shows your profitable band.
decisions_answered: What's the minimum discount that pays for itself?
Below what depth do promotions lose money?

ml-offer-type-response:top_converting_of.title: Top Converting Offer
ml-offer-type-response:top_converting_of.formula: Top Converting Offer = the offer type with the highest predicted conversion rate across all offer types
ml-offer-type-response:top_converting_of.formula_explanation: The offer type that converts at the highest rate. Your strongest default promotional mechanic.
how_to_read: Lead with this offer type for the best aggregate conversion.
Read with revenue-per-type — highest conversion isn't always highest revenue.
Segment heatmap shows where other offer types beat it.
decisions_answered: Which offer type converts best overall?
What promotional mechanic should I default to?

ml-offer-type-response:avg_conversion_ra.title: Avg Conversion Rate
ml-offer-type-response:avg_conversion_ra.formula: Avg Conversion Rate = mean of the predicted conversion rate across all offer types
ml-offer-type-response:avg_conversion_ra.formula_explanation: Average conversion rate across the offer types the model evaluated. A baseline to judge any single offer type against.
how_to_read: Use it as the bar — offer types above it are over-performers.
A low average means offers in general aren't landing well.
Track over runs to see whether offer effectiveness is trending.
decisions_answered: What's a typical conversion rate for my offers?
Which offer types beat the average?

ml-offer-type-response:offer_segment_mat.title: Offer-Segment Match Rate
ml-offer-type-response:offer_segment_mat.formula: Offer-Segment Match Rate = fraction of segment-offer cells flagged as a recommended match in the segment heatmap
ml-offer-type-response:offer_segment_mat.formula_explanation: Share of customer segments that have a clearly recommended best offer type. Higher means offers can be confidently tailored by segment.
how_to_read: High match rate means segment-level offer targeting will pay off.
Low match rate means a single default offer is safer.
Use it to decide between tailored vs. blanket offer strategy.
decisions_answered: Can I confidently tailor offers by segment?
How segment-specific should my offer strategy be?

ml-offer-type-response:revenue_per_top_o.title: Revenue per Top Offer
ml-offer-type-response:revenue_per_top_o.formula: Revenue per Top Offer = the revenue attributable to the top-converting offer type
ml-offer-type-response:revenue_per_top_o.formula_explanation: The revenue attributable to the best-converting offer type. Confirms whether high conversion also translates into dollars.
how_to_read: Check that your top-converting offer also earns real revenue.
If revenue lags conversion, the offer may discount too deeply.
Read with revenue-by-type to compare against other offers.
decisions_answered: Does my best-converting offer also drive revenue?
Is high conversion translating into dollars?

ml-offer-value-lift:customers_scored.title: Customers Scored
ml-offer-value-lift:customers_scored.formula: Customers Scored = total population the response-score ranker scored for the selected horizon
ml-offer-value-lift:customers_scored.formula_explanation: Total population the response-score ranker scored for the selected horizon. The denominator behind the priority pool and share.
how_to_read: Confirms full base coverage for the run.
A drop versus prior runs usually flags a data/scoring issue.
Use it to put the high-priority pool and share into context.
decisions_answered: How many customers were scored this run?
Is response-score coverage complete?

ml-offer-value-lift:high_priority_pool.title: High-Priority Pool
ml-offer-value-lift:high_priority_pool.formula: High-Priority Pool = count of customers ranked into the highest-priority tier for offers at the selected horizon
ml-offer-value-lift:high_priority_pool.formula_explanation: Number of customers ranked into the highest-priority tier for offers at the chosen horizon. Your prime targeting audience.
how_to_read: Target this pool first with your best offers.
Pair with high-priority share to see how concentrated value is.
Pool size shifts by horizon — check the horizon selector.
decisions_answered: How many customers belong in my top offer-priority tier?
How large is my prime targeting audience?

ml-offer-value-lift:high_priority_share.title: High-Priority Share
ml-offer-value-lift:high_priority_share.formula: High-Priority Share = high-priority pool ÷ customers scored × 100
ml-offer-value-lift:high_priority_share.formula_explanation: Percentage of all scored customers that fall in the high-priority tier. A higher share means value is spread; a lower share means it's concentrated.
how_to_read: A small share means a tight, high-value audience to focus on.
A large share may mean the priority cut is too loose.
Read with the priority breakdown chart for the full tier split.
decisions_answered: What fraction of customers are high priority?
How concentrated is my offer-targeting opportunity?

ml-offer-value-lift:top_decile_concentra.title: Top-Decile Concentration
ml-offer-value-lift:top_decile_concentra.formula: Top-Decile Concentration = top-decile mean response score ÷ overall mean response score (how many times stronger the top 10% of customers' scores are versus the average)
ml-offer-value-lift:top_decile_concentra.formula_explanation: How many times stronger the top-decile customers' response scores are versus the average. A direct read of how well the model concentrates value.
how_to_read: A higher multiple means targeting the top decile pays off more.
Near 1x means the model barely separates customers — target broadly.
Read with model ranking quality (lift/Spearman) for confidence.
decisions_answered: How much more valuable is my top 10% than average?
Is it worth concentrating offers on the top decile?

ml-offer-value-lift:score_distribution.title: Response-Score Distribution
ml-offer-value-lift:score_distribution.formula: Histogram of predicted offer-response scores across all customers for the selected horizon (bin counts from the distribution artifact).
ml-offer-value-lift:score_distribution.formula_explanation: Distribution of predicted response scores across all customers. Shows whether response potential is widespread or concentrated in a few customers.
how_to_read: A long right tail means a small set of very high-response customers.
A bunched-up distribution means response potential is even across the base.
Use the shape to decide how tightly to target offers.
decisions_answered: How is response potential spread across customers?
Are high-response customers rare or common?

ml-offer-value-lift:horizon_comparison.title: High-Priority Pool by Horizon
ml-offer-value-lift:horizon_comparison.formula: For each prediction horizon (30/60/90/180 days): high-priority pool size, top-decile lift, and holdout ranking quality (Spearman).
ml-offer-value-lift:horizon_comparison.formula_explanation: Compares the high-priority pool size and ranking quality across each prediction horizon, so you can pick the horizon that best fits your campaign window.
how_to_read: Pick the horizon whose pool and ranking quality fit your campaign timeline.
Larger pools at longer horizons may carry weaker ranking quality.
Use lift/Spearman to judge which horizon's ranking to trust.
decisions_answered: Which horizon should I target offers for?
How does pool size and ranking quality change by horizon?

formula_components.name: High-priority pool
formula_components.formula: Number of customers in the high priority tier for the horizon

formula_components.name: Top-decile lift
formula_components.formula: Holdout top-decile lift of the ranking for the horizon

formula_components.name: Ranking quality
formula_components.formula: Holdout Spearman correlation of the ranking for the horizon

ml-offer-value-lift:priority_breakdown.title: Priority Tier Breakdown
ml-offer-value-lift:priority_breakdown.formula: Count and share of customers in each offer-priority tier — High, Medium, Low — for the selected horizon.
ml-offer-value-lift:priority_breakdown.formula_explanation: Splits the scored base into high, medium, and low offer-priority tiers with counts and shares. Shows the overall shape of your targeting opportunity.
how_to_read: The high tier is your first-call audience; medium is a secondary wave.
A thin high tier means a focused, efficient campaign.
Compare tier shares across horizons to plan campaign waves.
decisions_answered: How is my base split across offer-priority tiers?
How big is each targeting wave?

ml-offer-value-lift:feature_importance.title: Top Drivers of Response
ml-offer-value-lift:feature_importance.formula: Top customer features driving the predicted offer-response score for the selected horizon, ranked by model gain.
ml-offer-value-lift:feature_importance.formula_explanation: The customer features that most influence the predicted response score, ranked by model gain. Explains why the model prioritises certain customers.
how_to_read: Top drivers tell you what behaviour predicts offer response.
Use them to sanity-check the model keys on sensible signals.
Feed drivers back into segmentation and offer design.
decisions_answered: What drives a customer's predicted response to offers?
Is the model relying on sensible signals?

ml-product-affinity:total_scored.title: Customers Scored
ml-product-affinity:total_scored.formula: Customers Scored = number of customers who received a product affinity recommendation in the latest scoring run
ml-product-affinity:total_scored.formula_explanation: Total number of customers who received a product affinity recommendation in the latest scoring run (the row count of the scored-customer artifact).
how_to_read: The value is the size of the audience the model scored this run
A larger pool means more customers are eligible for product-affinity targeting
Compare across runs to confirm scoring coverage stays stable
decisions_answered: How many customers did the model score this run?
Is scoring coverage holding steady run-over-run?

ml-product-affinity:high_affinity_pool.title: High Affinity Pool
ml-product-affinity:high_affinity_pool.formula: High Affinity Pool = count of scored customers whose top product recommendation carries a High confidence tier
ml-product-affinity:high_affinity_pool.formula_explanation: Count of customers whose top product recommendation carries a High confidence tier — the most reliable cross-sell audience.
how_to_read: Larger pool = more customers you can target with confidence
These customers have the model's strongest product signal
Use this pool first for product-specific campaigns
decisions_answered: How big is the high-confidence cross-sell audience?
Which customers should product campaigns prioritise?

ml-product-affinity:top_recommended_prod.title: Top Recommended Product
ml-product-affinity:top_recommended_prod.formula: Top Recommended Product = the single product most frequently predicted as a customer's top-1 product across all scored customers (most common top product)
ml-product-affinity:top_recommended_prod.formula_explanation: The single product recommended most frequently across all scored customers (the most common top-1 product).
how_to_read: Shows the product with the broadest recommendation reach
A concentrated top product signals a strong cross-sell hero SKU
Pair with audience size to size the campaign opportunity
decisions_answered: Which product has the widest recommendation audience?
What is the single best hero product for cross-sell?

ml-product-affinity:hit_rate_at_3.title: Hit Rate @3
ml-product-affinity:hit_rate_at_3.formula: Hit Rate @3 = share of customers whose actually-purchased product appeared in the model's top-3 recommendations (offline evaluation metric read from the model artifact)
ml-product-affinity:hit_rate_at_3.formula_explanation: Model-quality metric read from the training artifact: the fraction of customers whose actually-purchased product appeared among the model's top-3 recommendations.
how_to_read: Higher hit rate = recommendations more often match real purchases
Read as a confidence check on the model before activating
It is an offline evaluation metric, not a live campaign result
decisions_answered: How accurate are the top-3 product recommendations?
Can we trust the model's recommendations for activation?

ml-category-affinity:total_scored.title: Customers Scored
ml-category-affinity:total_scored.formula: Customers Scored = number of customers who received a category recommendation in the latest scoring run
ml-category-affinity:total_scored.formula_explanation: Total customers who received a category recommendation in the latest scoring run (row count of the scored-customer artifact).
how_to_read: The value is the audience size the category model scored this run
Larger pool = more customers eligible for category targeting
Track across runs to verify scoring coverage is stable
decisions_answered: How many customers did the category model score?
Is scoring coverage stable run-over-run?

ml-category-affinity:high_confidence.title: High Confidence
ml-category-affinity:high_confidence.formula: High Confidence = count of scored customers whose predicted top category carries a High confidence tier; share = that count ÷ total customers scored
