ml-category-affinity:high_confidence.formula_explanation: Customers where one category clearly stands out and the model is very certain. These customers are served a single top-1 recommendation.
how_to_read: Larger count = more customers with a decisive category prediction
These are the safest audience to activate without review
The percentage shows what share of the base is high-confidence
decisions_answered: How many customers have a decisive category prediction?
How large is the safe-to-activate audience?

formula_components.name: High Confidence Count
formula_components.formula: count of customers with confidence tier = High

ml-category-affinity:top1_served.title: Single Recommendation
ml-category-affinity:top1_served.formula: Single Recommendation = count of scored customers whose recommendation strategy is a single top-1 category; share = that count ÷ total customers scored
ml-category-affinity:top1_served.formula_explanation: Customers receiving exactly one category recommendation — the model is confident enough to commit to a single best match.
how_to_read: Higher count = more customers the model can pin to one category
Use these for tightly targeted single-category campaigns
Contrast with the 3-category basket to gauge model decisiveness
decisions_answered: How many customers get a single confident category match?
What share of the base supports single-category targeting?

formula_components.name: Single Recommendation Count
formula_components.formula: count of customers with recommendation strategy = top1

ml-category-affinity:top3_served.title: 3-Category Basket
ml-category-affinity:top3_served.formula: 3-Category Basket = count of scored customers whose recommendation strategy serves three categories; share = that count ÷ total customers scored
ml-category-affinity:top3_served.formula_explanation: Customers receiving three category recommendations — used when the model is less certain. Suited to broader personalisation in email and homepage carousels.
how_to_read: Higher count = more customers needing a broader shortlist
Use these for multi-category carousels and discovery placements
A rising top3 share signals more model uncertainty
decisions_answered: How many customers need a 3-category shortlist?
Where should broader multi-category personalisation run?

formula_components.name: 3-Category Basket Count
formula_components.formula: count of customers with recommendation strategy = top3

ml-category-affinity:customers_table.title: Customer Recommendations
ml-category-affinity:customers_table.formula: One row per scored customer showing top-1/2/3 predicted categories with their scores, confidence tier, recommendation strategy and shortlist depth
ml-category-affinity:customers_table.formula_explanation: Row-level table of each scored customer's category recommendations: their top-1/2/3 categories with scores, confidence tier, strategy, and shortlist depth, served paginated from the customers endpoint.
how_to_read: Each row is one customer's full category recommendation set
Top-1 score and confidence tier indicate how decisive the match is
Sort or filter to build a targeting list for a specific category
Click a row to open that customer's detailed profile
decisions_answered: Which exact customers should receive a given category campaign?
What does an individual customer's category shortlist look like?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:products_f.title: Products Forecasted
ml-product-demand-forecasting:products_f.formula: Products Forecasted = count of products the demand model produced a forecast for in this run
ml-product-demand-forecasting:products_f.formula_explanation: Number of products the demand model produced a forecast for in this run.
how_to_read: The value is how many SKUs have a demand forecast
Wider coverage means more of the catalog is planned for
Track run-over-run to confirm forecast coverage holds
decisions_answered: How many products have a demand forecast?
Is forecast coverage of the catalog stable?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:total_fore.title: Forecasted Units (90d)
ml-product-demand-forecasting:total_fore.formula: Forecasted Units (90d) = sum of each product's predicted 90-day unit demand across all forecasted products
ml-product-demand-forecasting:total_fore.formula_explanation: Total predicted unit demand across all forecasted products over the next 90 days.
how_to_read: The value is total expected demand in units for the next 90 days
Use it to anchor purchasing and inventory budgets
Compare against current stock to spot aggregate shortfalls
decisions_answered: How many units of total demand should we plan for over 90 days?
Is aggregate demand rising or falling versus prior forecasts?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:forecast_a.title: Forecast Accuracy (MAPE)
ml-product-demand-forecasting:forecast_a.formula: Forecast Accuracy (MAPE) = mean absolute percentage error of the demand forecasts on the holdout period (lower is better; read from the training artifact)
ml-product-demand-forecasting:forecast_a.formula_explanation: Model-quality metric read from the training artifact: the mean absolute percentage error of forecasts in the holdout period. Lower is better.
how_to_read: Lower MAPE = more accurate forecasts you can plan against
It is an offline evaluation metric, not a live result
Use it as a trust gauge before committing inventory spend
decisions_answered: How accurate is the demand forecast?
Can we trust these forecasts for inventory planning?

ml-product-demand-forecasting:high_growt.title: High-Growth Products (>20%)
ml-product-demand-forecasting:high_growt.formula: High-Growth Products (>20%) = count of products whose forecasted growth rate exceeds 20%
ml-product-demand-forecasting:high_growt.formula_explanation: Number of products whose forecasted growth rate exceeds 20% — the fastest-rising SKUs.
how_to_read: Higher count = more breakout products to lean into
These SKUs are candidates for extra stock and promotion
Cross-check with stock-out risk to avoid missing the upside
decisions_answered: Which products are forecast to grow fastest?
Where should we increase stock to capture demand growth?

ml-stock-out-risk:immediate_action_requi.title: Immediate Action (<7d)
ml-stock-out-risk:immediate_action_requi.formula: Immediate Action (<7d) = count of Critical/High-risk products projected to stock out within the next 7 days
ml-stock-out-risk:immediate_action_requi.formula_explanation: High-risk products projected to stock out within the next 7 days — the items needing reorder action now.
how_to_read: The value is the count of items needing reorder this week
These are the most time-critical stock-out threats
Treat this as the urgent action queue for purchasing
decisions_answered: Which products need reordering this week to avoid stock-out?
How large is the urgent replenishment queue?

ml-stock-out-risk:high_risk_products.title: High-Risk Products
ml-stock-out-risk:high_risk_products.formula: High-Risk Products = count of products the model flags at Critical or High stock-out risk
ml-stock-out-risk:high_risk_products.formula_explanation: Total count of products flagged at Critical or High stock-out risk.
how_to_read: Higher count = more of the catalog is at stock-out risk
Use it to size the overall replenishment workload
Trend it down as reorders clear the backlog
decisions_answered: How many products are at high stock-out risk?
How big is the overall replenishment problem?

ml-stock-out-risk:revenue_at_stockout_ri.title: Revenue at Stockout Risk
ml-stock-out-risk:revenue_at_stockout_ri.formula: Revenue at Stockout Risk = sum of the revenue-at-risk across all Critical/High-risk products
ml-stock-out-risk:revenue_at_stockout_ri.formula_explanation: Total revenue exposed across all Critical/High-risk products — the sales at stake if stock-outs are not prevented.
how_to_read: The value is the dollar exposure of unaddressed stock-out risk
Prioritise reorders that protect the most revenue
Compare against reorder cost to justify expedited shipping
decisions_answered: How much revenue is at risk from potential stock-outs?
Which replenishment actions protect the most revenue?

ml-stock-out-risk:avg_days_until_risk.title: Avg Days Until Risk
ml-stock-out-risk:avg_days_until_risk.formula: Avg Days Until Risk = average projected days-until-stock-out across the Critical/High-risk products
ml-stock-out-risk:avg_days_until_risk.formula_explanation: Average number of days until projected stock-out across the high-risk products — how much lead time remains on average.
how_to_read: Lower average = less runway before stock-outs hit
Use it to gauge how aggressive replenishment timing must be
Pair with the immediate-action count for the urgent slice
decisions_answered: How much lead time do we have before high-risk items stock out?
Is the replenishment window tightening overall?

ml-category-purchase-propensity:total_cu.title: Customers Scored
ml-category-purchase-propensity:total_cu.formula: Customers Scored = number of customers with a category purchase-propensity prediction in the latest run
ml-category-purchase-propensity:total_cu.formula_explanation: Total customers with a category purchase-propensity prediction in the latest run.
how_to_read: The value is the audience size the propensity model scored
Larger pool = more customers eligible for propensity targeting
Track across runs to confirm scoring coverage is stable
decisions_answered: How many customers were scored for category purchase propensity?
Is scoring coverage stable run-over-run?

ml-category-purchase-propensity:high_pur.title: High Propensity Pool
ml-category-purchase-propensity:high_pur.formula: High Propensity Pool = count of customers with high-confidence category purchase predictions; share = that count ÷ total customers scored
ml-category-purchase-propensity:high_pur.formula_explanation: Count of customers with high-confidence category purchase predictions — the audience most likely to convert.
how_to_read: Larger pool = more customers primed to purchase
These are the highest-converting targets for category offers
The percentage shows what share of the base is high-propensity
decisions_answered: How many customers are highly likely to purchase?
Which audience should category offers prioritise?

formula_components.name: High Propensity Count
formula_components.formula: high-propensity customer count from the precomputed summary

ml-category-purchase-propensity:model_au.title: Model AUC
ml-category-purchase-propensity:model_au.formula: Model AUC = area under the ROC curve for the category purchase-propensity model (offline quality metric read from the artifact; 0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect)
ml-category-purchase-propensity:model_au.formula_explanation: Model-quality metric read from the artifact: area under the ROC curve for the category purchase-propensity model. Higher is better (0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect).
how_to_read: Higher AUC = better separation of buyers from non-buyers
Above ~0.7 is generally usable for targeting
It is an offline evaluation metric, not a live result
decisions_answered: How well does the propensity model rank likely buyers?
Can we trust the propensity scores for targeting?

ml-category-purchase-propensity:top_oppo.title: Top Opportunity Category
ml-category-purchase-propensity:top_oppo.formula: Top Opportunity Category = the product category with the strongest purchase-propensity signals across all scored customers (read from the precomputed summary artifact)
ml-category-purchase-propensity:top_oppo.formula_explanation: The product category with the strongest purchase-propensity signals across all customers, surfaced from the precomputed summary artifact.
how_to_read: Names the category with the biggest conversion opportunity
Use it to choose where to focus the next category push
Pair with the high-propensity pool to size the audience
decisions_answered: Which category has the strongest purchase opportunity?
Where should the next category campaign focus?

ml-category-seasonality:peak_season_cate.title: Peak Season Category
ml-category-seasonality:peak_season_cate.formula: Peak Season Category = the category whose single highest monthly seasonality index is the largest across all categories
ml-category-seasonality:peak_season_cate.formula_explanation: The category with the single strongest seasonal peak — the one whose best month most exceeds its yearly average.
how_to_read: Names the category with the most pronounced seasonal surge
Plan inventory and promotion around this category's peak month
A high peak index means demand concentrates in a short window
decisions_answered: Which category has the sharpest seasonal peak?
Where should seasonal stocking and promotion concentrate?

ml-category-seasonality:avg_seasonality_.title: Avg Seasonality Index
ml-category-seasonality:avg_seasonality_.formula: Avg Seasonality Index = average of every category's 12 monthly seasonality index values (index 1.0 = a month at that category's yearly average)
ml-category-seasonality:avg_seasonality_.formula_explanation: Portfolio-wide average of the monthly seasonality indices (index 1.0 = a month at the yearly average). Indicates how seasonal the catalog is overall.
how_to_read: Values near 1.0 mean demand is spread evenly across the year
Wide swings around 1.0 mean strongly seasonal demand
Use it as a baseline to compare individual categories against
decisions_answered: How seasonal is the catalog overall?
Is demand evenly spread or concentrated in peak months?

ml-category-seasonality:highest_yoy_grow.title: Highest YoY Growth Category
ml-category-seasonality:highest_yoy_grow.formula: Highest YoY Growth Category = the category with the largest year-over-year revenue growth %
ml-category-seasonality:highest_yoy_grow.formula_explanation: The category with the largest year-over-year revenue growth.
how_to_read: Names the fastest-growing category versus last year
A clear growth leader is a candidate for added investment
Cross-check seasonality to time the push correctly
decisions_answered: Which category is growing fastest year-over-year?
Where is the strongest momentum to invest behind?

ml-category-seasonality:off_peak_revenue.title: Off-Peak Revenue at Risk
ml-category-seasonality:off_peak_revenue.formula: Off-Peak Revenue at Risk = sum of each category's weakest-month (trough) revenue, across all categories
ml-category-seasonality:off_peak_revenue.formula_explanation: Total revenue earned in each category's weakest (trough) month, summed across categories — the soft-season revenue most exposed without intervention.
how_to_read: The value is the revenue concentrated in low-season troughs
Use it to size the opportunity for off-peak promotions
Targeting troughs smooths revenue across the year
decisions_answered: How much revenue sits in low-season troughs?
Where could off-peak campaigns recover soft-season sales?

ml-brand-affinity:single_brand_loyalists.title: Single Brand Loyalists
ml-brand-affinity:single_brand_loyalists.formula: Single Brand Loyalists = count of scored customers the model classifies as Loyalists (loyalty type = Loyalist)
ml-brand-affinity:single_brand_loyalists.formula_explanation: Customers who exclusively prefer one brand, classified as Loyalists by the model.
how_to_read: Higher count = a larger committed single-brand base
Loyalists are efficient targets for that brand's offers
A shrinking loyalist base can signal eroding brand pull
decisions_answered: How many customers are committed to a single brand?
How large is the loyal base to protect and reward?

ml-brand-affinity:multi_brand_customers.title: Multi-Brand Customers
ml-brand-affinity:multi_brand_customers.formula: Multi-Brand Customers = count of scored customers the model classifies as Explorers or Switchers (buying across multiple brands)
ml-brand-affinity:multi_brand_customers.formula_explanation: Customers who buy across multiple brands (Explorers and Switchers) rather than staying with one.
how_to_read: Higher count = more customers open to cross-brand offers
These are prime targets for cross-brand merchandising
Contrast with loyalists to gauge portfolio cross-shopping
decisions_answered: How many customers shop across multiple brands?
How large is the cross-brand merchandising audience?

ml-brand-affinity:top_brand.title: Top Brand by Affinity
ml-brand-affinity:top_brand.formula: Top Brand by Affinity = the single brand most frequently identified as a customer's top brand across all scored customers (most common top brand)
ml-brand-affinity:top_brand.formula_explanation: The brand most frequently identified as a customer's top brand across all scored customers.
how_to_read: Names the brand with the widest customer affinity
Use it to anchor brand-led campaigns and placements
Pair with loyalty mix to judge how defensible that lead is
decisions_answered: Which brand has the broadest customer affinity?
Which brand should anchor brand-led campaigns?

ml-brand-affinity:brand_switcher_pool.title: Brand Switcher Pool
ml-brand-affinity:brand_switcher_pool.formula: Brand Switcher Pool = count of scored customers the model classifies as brand Switchers (loyalty type = Switcher)
ml-brand-affinity:brand_switcher_pool.formula_explanation: Customers who frequently switch between brands — the highest-opportunity audience for brand-retention campaigns.
how_to_read: Higher count = more customers at risk of defecting
Switchers are the best target for retention incentives
Reducing this pool over time signals improving stickiness
decisions_answered: How many customers are prone to switching brands?
Who should brand-retention campaigns target first?

ml-price-affinity:premium_customers.title: Premium Customers
ml-price-affinity:premium_customers.formula: Premium Customers = count of scored customers the model assigns to the Premium price tier
ml-price-affinity:premium_customers.formula_explanation: Customers classified into the Premium price tier — those with high willingness-to-pay.
how_to_read: Higher count = a larger high-margin, premium-priced audience
Target these with premium products and full-price offers
Avoid over-discounting to this tier
decisions_answered: How many customers will pay premium prices?
Which audience supports full-price and premium offers?

ml-price-affinity:value_seekers.title: Value Seekers
ml-price-affinity:value_seekers.formula: Value Seekers = count of scored customers the model assigns to the Value price tier
ml-price-affinity:value_seekers.formula_explanation: Customers in the Value price tier who balance quality against price.
how_to_read: Higher count = a larger value-conscious audience
Target with bundles and quality-for-price messaging
These respond to perceived value more than deep discounts
decisions_answered: How many customers are value-conscious buyers?
Where should value and bundle messaging focus?

ml-price-affinity:budget_customers.title: Budget Customers
ml-price-affinity:budget_customers.formula: Budget Customers = count of scored customers the model assigns to the Budget price tier
ml-price-affinity:budget_customers.formula_explanation: Customers in the Budget tier who are highly price-sensitive.
how_to_read: Higher count = a larger discount-sensitive audience
Reserve deeper promotions and clearance for this tier
Full-price offers are least effective here
decisions_answered: How many customers are highly price-sensitive?
Who should clearance and deep-discount offers target?

ml-price-affinity:avg_wtp_score.title: Avg WTP Score
ml-price-affinity:avg_wtp_score.formula: Avg WTP Score = mean predicted willingness-to-pay score (0–1) across all scored customers
ml-price-affinity:avg_wtp_score.formula_explanation: Average willingness-to-pay score (0-1) across all scored customers — a portfolio-wide read on pricing headroom.
how_to_read: Higher average = more pricing headroom across the base
A rising score suggests room to test higher price points
Read alongside the tier mix for the full pricing picture
decisions_answered: How much pricing headroom does the customer base have overall?
Is average willingness-to-pay trending up or down?

ml-price-affinity:customers_table.title: Customer Price Profiles
ml-price-affinity:customers_table.formula: One row per scored customer showing their assigned price tier, willingness-to-pay score and average order value, sorted by WTP score descending
ml-price-affinity:customers_table.formula_explanation: Row-level table of each scored customer's pricing profile: assigned price tier, willingness-to-pay score, and average order value, served paginated from the customers endpoint.
how_to_read: Each row is one customer's price tier, WTP score, and AOV
Sort by WTP to find the best premium-offer targets
Filter by tier to build a pricing-specific audience
Click a row to open that customer's detailed profile
decisions_answered: Which exact customers belong in each price tier?
Who are the best individual targets for premium pricing?

ml-price-elasticity:highly_elastic_produ.title: Highly Elastic Products
ml-price-elasticity:highly_elastic_produ.formula: Highly Elastic Products = count of products whose predicted elasticity coefficient is below −1.0 (a small price change moves demand sharply)
ml-price-elasticity:highly_elastic_produ.formula_explanation: Products where a small price change causes a large demand shift (elasticity below -1.0).
how_to_read: Higher count = more SKUs where price moves demand sharply
These respond strongly to promotions and discounts
Avoid raising prices on these without volume impact
decisions_answered: Which products react most strongly to price changes?
Where will promotions move the most volume?

ml-price-elasticity:inelastic_products.title: Inelastic Products
ml-price-elasticity:inelastic_products.formula: Inelastic Products = count of products whose predicted elasticity coefficient is between −1.0 and 0 (demand relatively insensitive to price)
ml-price-elasticity:inelastic_products.formula_explanation: Products where demand is relatively insensitive to price (elasticity between -1.0 and 0).
how_to_read: Higher count = more SKUs that tolerate price increases
These are candidates for margin-protecting price rises
Discounting these wastes margin with little volume gain
decisions_answered: Which products can absorb a price increase?
Where can we raise prices to protect margin?

ml-price-elasticity:avg_elasticity_coeff.title: Avg Elasticity Coefficient
ml-price-elasticity:avg_elasticity_coeff.formula: Avg Elasticity Coefficient = mean predicted price-elasticity coefficient across all scored products (values below −1 indicate broadly price-sensitive demand)
ml-price-elasticity:avg_elasticity_coeff.formula_explanation: Portfolio-wide average price elasticity. Values below -1 indicate broadly elastic (price-sensitive) demand.
how_to_read: More negative = the catalog is more price-sensitive overall
Near 0 means demand is broadly insensitive to price
Use as a baseline to compare categories and SKUs against
decisions_answered: How price-sensitive is the product portfolio overall?
Is the catalog broadly elastic or inelastic?

ml-price-elasticity:revenue_sensitivity_.title: Revenue Sensitivity Score
ml-price-elasticity:revenue_sensitivity_.formula: Revenue Sensitivity Score = mean of (absolute predicted elasticity × each product's revenue weight) across products — a revenue-weighted measure of how exposed revenue is to price changes
ml-price-elasticity:revenue_sensitivity_.formula_explanation: A revenue-weighted average of absolute elasticity — how sensitive total revenue is to price changes, emphasising high-revenue products.
how_to_read: Higher score = revenue is more exposed to price moves
Weights elasticity by each product's revenue contribution
Use it to gauge the revenue risk of pricing changes
decisions_answered: How exposed is total revenue to price changes?
Should pricing changes be made cautiously given revenue risk?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:markdown_read.title: Markdown-Ready Now
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:markdown_read.formula: Markdown-Ready Now = count of products the model flags with markdown urgency = 'Now'
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:markdown_read.formula_explanation: Products the model flags for immediate markdown — the items where acting now optimises margin recovery.
how_to_read: The value is the count of items to mark down this cycle
These are the most time-sensitive markdown decisions
Treat it as the immediate markdown action queue
decisions_answered: Which products should be marked down right now?
How large is the immediate markdown queue?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:projected_mar.title: Projected Margin Recovery
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:projected_mar.formula: Projected Margin Recovery = sum of the model's projected margin recovery across the markdown-urgency = 'Now' products
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:projected_mar.formula_explanation: Total margin the model projects recovering by acting on the markdown-ready-now products at their optimal timing.
how_to_read: The value is the margin upside of acting on Now items
Use it to prioritise which markdowns to execute first
Compare against doing nothing to justify the markdowns
decisions_answered: How much margin can we recover by marking down now?
Which markdowns deliver the biggest margin upside?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:avg_optimal_m.title: Avg Optimal Markdown Depth
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:avg_optimal_m.formula: Avg Optimal Markdown Depth = average of the model's recommended markdown % across all products
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:avg_optimal_m.formula_explanation: Average recommended markdown percentage across all products — the typical discount depth the model suggests.
how_to_read: Higher average = deeper recommended discounts overall
Use it to set guardrails for promotional depth
Outliers above the average warrant a manual review
decisions_answered: How deep are the recommended markdowns on average?
What discount depth should be the planning baseline?

ml-markdown-optimal-timing:revenue_uplif.title: Revenue Uplift vs No Markdown
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:revenue_uplif.formula: Revenue Uplift vs No Markdown = model-estimated revenue gain from following the optimal markdown plan versus no markdown action (read from the training artifact)
ml-markdown-optimal-timing:revenue_uplif.formula_explanation: Model-estimated revenue gain from following the optimal markdown plan versus taking no markdown action, read from the training artifact.
how_to_read: The value is the modelled revenue gain of acting vs not acting
It is an estimate from the model, not a realised result
Use it to justify the markdown program to stakeholders
decisions_answered: How much extra revenue does optimal markdown timing produce?
Is the markdown program worth running versus doing nothing?

ml-marketing-mix:total_revenue.title: Total Revenue
ml-marketing-mix:total_revenue.formula: Total Revenue = model-attributed paid revenue ÷ (1 − organic revenue share) — paid revenue grossed up by the model's organic share so paid + organic sum to the whole
ml-marketing-mix:total_revenue.formula_explanation: Total revenue over the modelled window: paid (attributed) revenue grossed up by the model's organic share so paid + organic sum to the whole.
how_to_read: The value is paid plus organic revenue for the modelled window
It is the denominator for revenue-mix and attribution views
Compare against spend to read overall efficiency
decisions_answered: What is total revenue across the modelled window?
How do paid and organic combine into the whole?

ml-marketing-mix:paid_revenue.title: Paid Revenue
ml-marketing-mix:paid_revenue.formula: Paid Revenue = sum of model-attributed revenue across all paid channels (Google, Meta, Email)
ml-marketing-mix:paid_revenue.formula_explanation: Revenue attributed to paid media by the marketing-mix model, summed across all channels (Google, Meta, Email, etc.).
how_to_read: The value is the revenue the model credits to paid media
Use it with spend to compute blended paid ROAS
It excludes the organic baseline
decisions_answered: How much revenue did paid media drive?
What share of total revenue is paid versus organic?

ml-marketing-mix:organic_revenue.title: Organic Revenue
ml-marketing-mix:organic_revenue.formula: Organic Revenue = max(0, total revenue − model-attributed paid revenue) — the baseline revenue not driven by paid media
ml-marketing-mix:organic_revenue.formula_explanation: Baseline revenue not driven by paid media (brand demand, repeat orders) — total revenue less the model's paid-attributed revenue.
how_to_read: The value is revenue that would occur without paid media
A large organic base means strong underlying brand demand
Use it to avoid over-crediting paid channels
decisions_answered: How much revenue comes from organic demand?
How strong is the brand's non-paid baseline?

ml-marketing-mix:blended_roas.title: Blended ROAS
ml-marketing-mix:blended_roas.formula: Blended ROAS = model-attributed paid revenue ÷ total paid media spend across all channels (0 when spend ≤ 0)
ml-marketing-mix:blended_roas.formula_explanation: Total paid-attributed revenue divided by total paid spend across all channels — the overall return on media investment.
how_to_read: Higher ROAS = more revenue returned per dollar of spend
Below 1.0 means paid media lost money on an attributed basis
Read alongside marginal ROAS to judge headroom to scale
decisions_answered: What is the overall return on paid media spend?
Is blended paid investment profitable on an attributed basis?

ml-marketing-mix:total_spend.title: Total Paid Spend
ml-marketing-mix:total_spend.formula: Total Paid Spend = sum of media spend across all paid channels (Google + Meta + Email) over the modelled window
ml-marketing-mix:total_spend.formula_explanation: Sum of media spend across all paid channels in the modelled window.
how_to_read: The value is total paid media investment for the window
It is the denominator for blended ROAS
Compare against paid revenue to read efficiency
decisions_answered: How much did we spend on paid media in total?
How does spend compare to the revenue it drove?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:share_budget_r.title: Share of Budget vs Share of Return
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:share_budget_r.formula: Per channel, grouped bars compare share of total media spend against share of total incremental revenue, both normalised to 100% across channels. Share of spend = channel spend ÷ total spend; share of return = channel incremental revenue ÷ total incremental revenue. A taller spend bar than return bar signals over-investment.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:share_budget_r.formula_explanation: Grouped bars compare each channel's percentage of total media spend against its percentage of total incremental revenue. Inputs are the channel-level rows from the MMM predictions table (spend, incremental_revenue); both shares are computed in the frontend from the channel totals.
how_to_read: Two bars per channel: share of spend vs share of return.
A taller spend bar than return bar means that channel is over-invested.
A taller return bar than spend bar means there is room to scale it.
Bars are normalized to 100% across channels, so this shows relative mix, not absolute dollars.
decisions_answered: Which channels are over- or under-invested relative to what they return?
Where should budget move to balance spend against return?

formula_components.name: Share of return
formula_components.formula: channel incremental revenue ÷ total incremental revenue × 100%

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:budget_allocat.title: Budget Allocator — Same Budget, More Revenue
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:budget_allocat.formula: Per channel, grouped bars compare today's daily spend (current allocation) with the model's recommended daily spend at the same total budget. Headline lift = optimised daily response ÷ current daily response − 1, expressed as a percent.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:budget_allocat.formula_explanation: Grouped bars show current vs model-recommended daily spend per channel from the budget_optimization artifact (current_allocation / recommended_allocation). The headline projects the revenue lift from holding total budget fixed while shifting the mix toward the highest marginal-ROAS channels: optimized_daily_response over current_daily_response minus one.
how_to_read: Two bars per channel: today's daily spend vs the model's recommended daily spend.
The headline percent is the projected revenue lift at the same total budget.
Money is shifted toward channels with the highest return on the next dollar (marginal ROAS).
Directional guidance — validate with a live spend test before reallocating fully.
decisions_answered: How should I reallocate budget to make more revenue at the same total spend?
Which channels should get more vs less daily budget?

formula_components.name: Recommended allocation
formula_components.formula: model-optimal daily spend per channel at the same total budget

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:campaign_treem.title: Where the Spend Goes — and What It Returns
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:campaign_treem.formula: Treemap where each rectangle is a campaign sized by media spend and coloured by its ROAS band (under 1× red, 1-3× amber, 3× or more green). The top 24 campaigns by spend are shown individually; the rest are pooled into one 'All other' block.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:campaign_treem.formula_explanation: Treemap of campaigns where each rectangle is sized by media spend and colored by that campaign's ROAS band. Built from the all-campaigns drill-down (campaign-level spend and roas); the top 24 campaigns by spend are shown individually and the rest are pooled into a single 'All other' block. Campaign-level figures are spend-weighted allocations of the channel-grain MMM result.
how_to_read: Box size = media spend; color = return on that spend (ROAS).
Big red boxes are large spend losing money (<1x ROAS) — the wasted budget.
Big green boxes are large spend returning strongly (>=3x ROAS).
Only the top 24 campaigns by spend are shown individually; the rest are pooled.
decisions_answered: Where is the bulk of campaign spend going, and is it returning?
Which large campaigns are wasting budget?

formula_components.name: Colour band
formula_components.formula: campaign ROAS = attributed revenue ÷ spend, bucketed into <1×, 1-3×, ≥3×

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.title: Top Campaigns by Incremental Revenue (Pareto)
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.formula: Horizontal bars rank the top 15 campaigns by incremental revenue, with a running cumulative share alongside each: cumulative % at position i = running sum of incremental revenue through i ÷ total incremental revenue of the top 15. Bars turn red when ROAS is below 1×.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.formula_explanation: Horizontal bars rank the top 15 campaigns by incremental revenue, with a running cumulative share alongside each. Bars use campaign-level incremental_revenue and roas (color: red when roas<1x, channel color otherwise); the cumulative percentage is computed against the sum of the top-15 revenue, highlighting how few campaigns drive most of the return.
how_to_read: Bars are sorted by incremental revenue, largest at the top.
Cum.% shows the running share of the top-15 revenue captured so far.
Rows where cumulative reaches 80% (highlighted) are the vital few campaigns.
Red bars are campaigns below 1.0x ROAS even if high revenue.
decisions_answered: Which few campaigns drive most of the incremental revenue?
How concentrated is return across the campaign portfolio?

formula_components.name: Cumulative %
formula_components.formula: running sum of incremental revenue through this campaign ÷ sum of incremental revenue over the top 15 × 100%

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.title: Top Campaigns by Spend
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.formula: Horizontal bar chart of the 15 highest-spend campaigns within the selected channel; bar length = campaign media spend, sorted descending.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar chart of the 15 highest-spend campaigns within the selected channel. Values are campaign-level spend from the channel drill-down (predictions table, level=campaign), sorted descending by spend.
how_to_read: Each bar is a campaign in the selected channel; length = media spend.
Shows where the channel's budget is concentrated.
Pair with Top Campaigns by Incremental Revenue to spot high-spend / low-return campaigns.
Only the top 15 campaigns by spend are shown.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns consume the most spend in this channel?
Is budget concentrated in a few campaigns or spread out?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.title: Top Campaigns by Incremental Revenue
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.formula: Horizontal bar chart of the 15 campaigns with the highest incremental revenue within the selected channel; bar length = campaign incremental revenue, sorted descending.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:top_campaigns_.formula_explanation: Horizontal bar chart of the 15 campaigns with the highest incremental revenue within the selected channel. Values are campaign-level incremental_revenue from the channel drill-down (predictions table, level=campaign), sorted descending.
how_to_read: Each bar is a campaign in the selected channel; length = incremental revenue.
Shows which campaigns actually drive return in this channel.
Pair with Top Campaigns by Spend to find efficient vs inefficient campaigns.
Only the top 15 campaigns by incremental revenue are shown.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns produce the most incremental revenue in this channel?
Where is the channel's return actually coming from?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:attribution_me.title: Attribution Method Mix
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:attribution_me.formula: Donut splitting attributed revenue by how each portion was measured (e.g. Email PersonFlow, Google native, Meta spend-share). Each slice = a method's revenue; slice share = method revenue ÷ total attributed revenue × 100%.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:attribution_me.formula_explanation: Donut chart splitting attributed revenue by how it was measured, read from the MMM summary artifact's by_attribution_method map (Email PersonFlow, Google native, Meta spend-share). Each slice is a method's revenue; the percentage is that method's share of total attributed revenue.
how_to_read: Each slice is revenue attributed by one method.
Larger direct-measurement slices (e.g. Google native, Email PersonFlow) mean more trustworthy numbers.
A large modeled / spend-share slice means more of the result is inferred.
Use it to gauge how much to trust the attributed revenue overall.
decisions_answered: How much of the attributed revenue is directly measured vs modeled?
How trustworthy are the attribution numbers on this dashboard?

ml-marketing-mix-campaign:data_quality_t.title: Data-Quality Tier Coverage
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:data_quality_t.formula: Two stacked bars (one split by spend, one by unit count) plus a table breaking spend, units and incremental revenue across data-quality tiers (high = direct measurement, medium = modelled, low = spend-share proxy). Spend share = tier spend ÷ total spend; count share = tier count ÷ total count.
ml-marketing-mix-campaign:data_quality_t.formula_explanation: Two stacked bars (by spend and by unit count) plus a table breaking spend, units, and incremental revenue across data-quality tiers, read from the segments artifact's data_quality_tier map. High = direct measurement, Medium = modeled, Low = spend-share proxy; shares are each tier's portion of total spend and total units.
how_to_read: Top bar splits spend across tiers; bottom bar splits unit count across tiers.
More high-tier (direct measurement) means more reliable attribution.
A large low-tier (spend-share proxy) share means treat those numbers as directional.
The table shows units, spend, and incremental revenue per tier.
decisions_answered: How much of the attribution is directly measured vs modeled vs proxied?
Should I trust the campaign numbers, or are they mostly inferred?

formula_components.name: Spend share
formula_components.formula: tier spend ÷ total spend across tiers

formula_components.name: Count share
formula_components.formula: tier unit count ÷ total unit count across tiers

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:forecast.title: Revenue: Recent Actuals & 13-Week Forecast
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:forecast.formula: Line chart joining recent weekly actual revenue (last 16 history weeks, solid) to the 13-week point forecast (dashed), with an 80% prediction band from the lower to upper bound per forecast week. The last actual week connects to the first forecast week with no gap.
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:forecast.formula_explanation: Line chart joining recent weekly actual revenue (solid green, last 16 history weeks) to the 13-week point forecast (dashed blue), with an 80% prediction band (pi80_low to pi80_high). Series come from the forecast artifact points (week_start, forecast_revenue, pi80_low, pi80_high) and the history series (actual_revenue); the last actual week is prepended to the forecast so the line connects at 'today'.
how_to_read: Green solid = actual revenue; blue dashed = forecast.
The shaded band is the 80% likely range and widens further out.
The forecast line connects seamlessly from the last actual week.
It projects the revenue trajectory; it does not model a spend change.
decisions_answered: What revenue trajectory should we expect over the next quarter?
How wide is the uncertainty around the forecast?

formula_components.name: Forecast
formula_components.formula: weekly forecast revenue per horizon week

formula_components.name: Prediction band
formula_components.formula: weekly 80% lower bound to upper bound

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:cumulative_pac.title: Cumulative Quarter Pacing
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:cumulative_pac.formula: Line chart tracking running cumulative forecast revenue week by week against a flat quarter target (the full-quarter forecast total), with a cumulative 80% prediction band. Cumulative forecast through week k = running sum of weekly forecast revenue; the band sums the weekly lower and upper 80% bounds the same way.
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:cumulative_pac.formula_explanation: Line chart tracking the running cumulative forecast revenue against the flat quarter target (quarter_total_forecast = SUM of all weekly forecast_revenue), with a cumulative 80% prediction band. Cumulative series are built in the frontend by summing the weekly forecast_revenue / pi80_low / pi80_high points in order.
how_to_read: Blue line = cumulative forecast revenue building week by week.
Gold dashed = the flat quarter target (full-quarter forecast total).
Grey dashed lines = cumulative 80% lower and upper bounds.
Where the cumulative line sits vs the band shows whether the quarter is pacing ahead or behind.
decisions_answered: Is the quarter pacing to hit its forecast total?
By how much could cumulative revenue run ahead of or behind plan?

formula_components.name: Cumulative forecast
formula_components.formula: running sum of weekly forecast revenue through week k

formula_components.name: Cumulative 80% band
formula_components.formula: running sums of the weekly lower and upper 80% bounds

formula_components.name: Quarter target
formula_components.formula: sum of all weekly forecast revenue (constant line)

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:peak_seasonali.title: Weekly Revenue Seasonality
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:peak_seasonali.formula: Bar chart of forecast revenue for each of the 13 horizon weeks. The highest-revenue week is coloured green (peak) and the lowest red (trough), identified as the max and min over the weekly forecast values.
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:peak_seasonali.formula_explanation: Bar chart of forecast revenue for each of the 13 horizon weeks, with the highest-revenue week colored green (peak) and the lowest red (trough). Values are the per-week forecast_revenue points from the forecast artifact; peak and trough are identified in the frontend as the max and min over those points.
how_to_read: Each bar is one forecast week; height = forecast revenue.
Green bar = the peak (highest) revenue week of the quarter.
Red bar = the trough (lowest) revenue week.
Use the shape to time staffing, inventory, and campaigns to the high weeks.
decisions_answered: Which weeks are the seasonal peaks and troughs in the coming quarter?
When should we line up inventory and campaigns?

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:downside_risk.title: Downside Risk Floor
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:downside_risk.formula: Line chart contrasting the cumulative point forecast against a conservative floor equal to the cumulative 80% lower bound. Point forecast through week k = running sum of weekly forecast revenue; floor through week k = running sum of weekly 80% lower bound. The gap between them is the quarter's downside exposure.
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:downside_risk.formula_explanation: Line chart contrasting the cumulative point forecast against a conservative floor equal to the cumulative 80% prediction lower bound. Both series are built by summing the weekly forecast_revenue and pi80_low points; the quarter floor equals quarter_total_pi80[0] = SUM(pi80_low) from the summary artifact.
how_to_read: Blue line = cumulative point forecast (the expected case).
Red dashed = conservative floor (cumulative 80% lower bound).
The gap between them is the downside exposure for the quarter.
The quarter total could be as low as the cumulative floor's end value.
decisions_answered: What is the realistic worst-case revenue floor for the quarter?
How much downside should we plan a buffer against?

formula_components.name: Point forecast
formula_components.formula: running sum of weekly forecast revenue through week k

formula_components.name: Conservative floor
formula_components.formula: running sum of weekly 80% lower bound through week k

ml-marketing-mix-forecast:forecast_vs_la.title: Forecast vs Same Weeks Last Year
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:forecast_vs_la.formula: Line chart overlaying the 13-week revenue forecast (dashed) on actual revenue from the same 13 calendar weeks one year earlier (solid, taken from history 52 weeks back). Year-over-year change = total forecast revenue ÷ total prior-year actual revenue − 1.
ml-marketing-mix-forecast:forecast_vs_la.formula_explanation: Line chart overlaying the 13-week revenue forecast (dashed) on the actual revenue from the same 13 calendar weeks one year earlier (solid). The prior-year window is taken from the history series at index len-52 through len-52+horizon (requires >=52 weeks of history); the overall year-over-year percent is total forecast revenue over total prior-year actual revenue minus one.
how_to_read: Dashed line = this year's forecast; solid line = same weeks last year (actual).
Weeks where the dashed line is above last year are expected to grow.
Weeks where it sits below are expected to soften.
The headline percent is the like-for-like, seasonality-safe year-over-year change.
decisions_answered: Is the coming quarter expected to beat or trail last year, week by week?
What is the like-for-like year-over-year growth?

formula_components.name: This year
formula_components.formula: weekly forecast revenue per horizon week

formula_components.name: Last year
formula_components.formula: weekly actual revenue for the same calendar weeks 52 weeks earlier

formula_components.name: YoY %
formula_components.formula: (total forecast revenue ÷ total prior-year actual revenue − 1) × 100%

ml-acquisition-forecast:forecast_table.title: Entry Category Forecast (next 4 weeks)
ml-acquisition-forecast:forecast_table.formula: One row per entry category (the product category of a new customer's first order) with its next-4-week forecast new customers, ranked highest-first, plus each category's share = forecast new customers ÷ total forecast new customers.
ml-acquisition-forecast:forecast_table.formula_explanation: A paginated table of every entry category's next-4-week new-customer forecast. Rows come from the acquisition forecast points (entity, entity_kind, week_start, forecast_new_customers) produced by the LightGBM ensemble, sorted highest-first; the Share column is each category's percentage of the total forecast new customers.
how_to_read: Each row is an entry category (the product category of a new customer's first order); rows are ranked by predicted new customers, highest first.
The Forecast New Customers column is the model's predicted new-customer count for that category over the next 4 weeks.
The Share column is that category's percentage of the total forecast, so the top rows show where new-customer acquisition will concentrate.
decisions_answered: Which specific entry categories will bring in the most new customers next month?
How should acquisition spend and inventory be allocated across categories?

customer-journey:source_medium_attributi.title: Source/Medium Attribution
customer-journey:source_medium_attributi.formula: One row per GA4 source/medium pair (top 15 by sessions): sessions, revenue, conversions, revenue per session (revenue ÷ sessions) and conversion rate (conversions ÷ sessions × 100).
customer-journey:source_medium_attributi.formula_explanation: Aggregates session-level traffic, revenue, and conversion metrics by GA4 source/medium pair from the source-medium daily mart. Shows which specific traffic sources (e.g. google / cpc, klaviyo / email, (direct) / (none)) drive sessions and revenue, enabling granular acquisition-channel decisions below the high-level channel grouping.
how_to_read: Each row is a source/medium pair ranked by session volume (top 15)
Sort by Sessions to see which sources drive the most traffic
Sort by Revenue to see which sources drive the most value
Revenue Per Session reveals the quality of traffic from each source
Conversion Rate shows how efficiently each source turns sessions into conversions
Compare high-traffic / low-conversion sources against low-traffic / high-conversion sources to rebalance spend
decisions_answered: Which specific source/medium pairs drive the most sessions and revenue?
Which traffic sources convert most efficiently?
Where is high-volume traffic failing to convert?
Which sources deserve more or less acquisition investment?

customer-journey:campaign_performance.title: Campaign Journey Performance
customer-journey:campaign_performance.formula: Campaigns ranked by revenue (top 20), each labelled with its platform: clicks (shown as sessions), conversion value (revenue), conversions, revenue per click, conversion rate (conversions ÷ clicks × 100) and click-through rate (engagement rate).
customer-journey:campaign_performance.formula_explanation: Surfaces campaign-level engagement and conversion metrics from the campaign performance mart, labelled with platform. Clicks stand in for sessions and CTR for engagement rate, giving a paid-campaign view of how each campaign contributes to the customer journey and revenue.
how_to_read: Each row is a campaign (with its platform) ranked by revenue (top 20)
Sessions here are campaign clicks; Engagement Rate is the campaign CTR
Sort by Revenue to find the top revenue-driving campaigns
Conversion Rate shows how well a campaign's clicks turn into conversions
Revenue Per Session highlights campaigns delivering high value per click
Compare engagement rate against conversion rate to spot campaigns that attract clicks but fail to convert
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most revenue and conversions?
Which campaigns engage users but fail to convert them?
How does campaign performance compare across platforms?
Where should campaign budget be scaled up or cut?

inventory:items_at_risk.title: Items At Risk
inventory:items_at_risk.formula: Items At Risk = count of distinct products with zero units purchased but more than 50 cart adds (cart adds capped below 10,000 to exclude anomalies) over the last 30 days
inventory:items_at_risk.formula_explanation: Counts products that customers added to their cart but nobody actually purchased in the past 30 days. The threshold of more than 50 cart adds filters out incidental activity so only items with real, repeated purchase intent are flagged. Zero purchases despite genuine cart demand is a strong signal of a stockout, broken checkout, or pricing problem on that item.
how_to_read: The number is a count of distinct products meeting the at-risk criteria over the last 30 days
Higher counts mean more products are attracting demand but failing to convert to sales
Items must clear the >50 cart-add bar, so each one represents meaningful lost demand
Pair this with Est. Lost Revenue to size the financial impact
Investigate flagged items for stockouts, pricing, or checkout issues
decisions_answered: How many products are generating cart demand but zero sales?
Where should we focus availability and merchandising fixes first?
Is the at-risk pool growing or shrinking over time?
Are conversion failures concentrated in a few SKUs or widespread?
inventory:items_at_risk.narration_text:
This metric counts products that customers tried to buy but could not, over the last thirty days.

The formula is direct: an item is at risk when it has more than fifty cart adds yet zero purchases. The fifty-add threshold ensures we are seeing real, repeated demand rather than a one-off click.

Reading this number: every counted item is unmet demand sitting in your catalog. A rising count means more of your assortment is failing to convert, often because of stockouts, pricing, or a broken checkout path.

Use it as a triage list. Cross-reference at-risk items with estimated lost revenue to prioritize the fixes that recover the most sales.

inventory:estimated_lost_revenue.title: Est. Lost Revenue
inventory:estimated_lost_revenue.formula: Estimated Lost Revenue = sum over items with >50 cart adds of (benchmark conversion − item's own conversion) × the item's cart adds × the item's price, over the last 30 days, where benchmark conversion = average of units purchased ÷ cart adds across all items with >50 adds, and price = item revenue ÷ units purchased (falling back to the catalog average price when the item has no purchases)
inventory:estimated_lost_revenue.formula_explanation: Estimates the revenue you are leaving on the table because under-converting items are not selling at the benchmark rate. For each item with real cart demand, it takes the gap between the catalog-wide average conversion rate and the item's own conversion rate, multiplies by its cart adds to get the missed purchases, then multiplies by the item's price to convert that into dollars.
how_to_read: The value is a dollar estimate of revenue missed by under-converting items over 30 days
Only items with more than 50 cart adds contribute, so it reflects real demand
The gap is measured against the catalog-wide benchmark conversion rate
Items priced higher contribute more lost revenue per missed purchase
Treat it as an upper-bound opportunity, not guaranteed recoverable revenue
decisions_answered: How much revenue are we losing to availability and conversion gaps?
Is the lost-revenue opportunity large enough to justify intervention?
Which under-converting items are worth fixing first by dollar impact?
How does the opportunity trend as we resolve issues?
inventory:estimated_lost_revenue.narration_text:
This metric estimates the dollars you are losing because some products convert below your store's benchmark.

Here is the logic: for every item with more than fifty cart adds, we compare its conversion rate to the catalog-wide average. The shortfall, multiplied by cart adds, gives the missed purchases. We then multiply by the item's price to turn that into revenue.

Reading the figure: it is the size of the prize if your weakest converters performed at the average. A large number means availability or conversion problems are materially denting sales.

Use it to justify and prioritize fixes. Combine it with the items-at-risk list to attack the highest-dollar opportunities first.

inventory:true_oos_count.title: True OOS Count
inventory:true_oos_count.formula: True OOS Count = count of distinct products with zero units purchased but more than 100 cart adds (cart adds capped below 10,000) over the last 30 days — a stricter bar than Items At Risk
inventory:true_oos_count.formula_explanation: A high-confidence count of products that are effectively out of stock based on customer behavior. It uses a stricter bar than Items At Risk (more than 100 cart adds versus more than 50) so that only items with very strong, sustained demand and zero sales are counted. These are your most certain stockouts where many customers tried to buy and none succeeded.
how_to_read: The number counts distinct products with heavy cart demand and zero purchases over 30 days
The >100 cart-add threshold makes this stricter than the Items At Risk metric
Each counted item is a near-certain stockout or fulfillment failure
Lower is better; a rising count signals worsening availability
Use the Out of Stock Alerts table to see the specific items
decisions_answered: Which products are almost certainly out of stock right now?
How severe is our availability problem?
How many strong-demand items are we completely failing to fulfill?
Is our stockout situation improving or deteriorating?
inventory:true_oos_count.narration_text:
This metric is your high-confidence stockout counter.

An item is counted when it has more than one hundred cart adds in the last thirty days and zero purchases. That hundred-add bar is deliberately strict, so these are not maybes, they are near-certain stockouts.

Reading the number: every count is a product many customers actively tried to buy and could not. A rising count means availability is slipping and revenue is walking out the door.

Treat this as your urgent restock list. Open the Out of Stock Alerts table to see exactly which items to act on.

inventory:cart_to_purchase_rate.title: Cart-to-Purchase Rate
inventory:cart_to_purchase_rate.formula: Cart-to-Purchase Rate = average across qualifying products of (each product's units purchased ÷ its cart adds) × 100, over the last 30 days, limited to items with 50–10,000 cart adds and at least one purchase
inventory:cart_to_purchase_rate.formula_explanation: The benchmark conversion rate from cart add to completed purchase across products with meaningful demand. It is computed as the average of each qualifying product's own purchased-over-added ratio, expressed as a percent, limited to items with 50 to 10000 cart adds and at least one purchase so outliers and noise do not skew the benchmark.
how_to_read: Shown as a percentage of cart adds that convert into purchases
It is a benchmark across qualifying products, not a single sitewide ratio
Only items with 50 to 10000 cart adds and at least one purchase are included
Higher is better; a falling rate signals widening availability or checkout friction
Use it as the reference line for spotting under-converting items
decisions_answered: What is our typical cart-to-purchase conversion benchmark?
Are products converting at or below the expected rate?
Is overall conversion health improving or declining?
What benchmark should under-performing items be measured against?
inventory:cart_to_purchase_rate.narration_text:
This metric is your benchmark for how well cart adds turn into purchases.

We take every product with between fifty and ten thousand cart adds and at least one sale, compute its own conversion rate, and average them. The result is a clean, outlier-resistant benchmark expressed as a percentage.

Reading it: this is the bar your catalog converts at on average. When an individual item sits well below this line, it is a candidate for the items-at-risk and lost-revenue analyses.

Watch the trend. A declining benchmark usually means availability or checkout friction is spreading across the catalog.

inventory:declining_items.title: Declining Items
inventory:declining_items.formula: Declining Items = count of distinct products whose current-week units purchased fell below half of their level four weeks earlier, among items that had more than 20 purchases four weeks ago (fixed trailing 60-day window anchored to today, independent of the dashboard date filter)
inventory:declining_items.formula_explanation: Counts products whose weekly sales have collapsed to less than half of what they were four weeks ago. Only items that had a meaningful base (more than 20 purchases four weeks ago) qualify, so the metric flags real momentum loss rather than normal small-number noise. It intentionally uses a fixed 60-day window anchored to today so the week-over-week comparison always has data, regardless of the dashboard date filter.
how_to_read: The number counts products whose current-week sales dropped below half their level four weeks ago
Only items with more than 20 purchases four weeks ago are eligible, filtering out noise
It always uses a fixed 60-day lookback, independent of the dashboard date range
A rising count means more of your assortment is losing momentum
Investigate flagged items for stockouts, pricing, seasonality, or competitive pressure
decisions_answered: Which products are rapidly losing sales momentum?
Is demand softening across a growing share of the catalog?
Where should we intervene before a slow item becomes a dead item?
Are declines driven by availability, pricing, or seasonality?
inventory:declining_items.narration_text:
This metric flags products that are losing steam fast.

An item is counted when its sales this week are less than half of what they were four weeks ago, and only if it had a real base of more than twenty purchases back then. That base requirement keeps small-number noise out.

Note the window: this metric always looks back a fixed sixty days from today, so the week-over-week comparison always has data even if your dashboard date filter is narrower.

Reading it: a rising count means momentum is draining from more of your catalog. Use it as an early-warning list to act before a fading item becomes a dead one.

inventory:funnel_drop_rate.title: Funnel Drop Rate
inventory:funnel_drop_rate.formula: Funnel Drop Rate = (1 − purchase events ÷ add-to-cart events) × 100, sitewide over the last 30 days
inventory:funnel_drop_rate.formula_explanation: The percentage of add-to-cart events that never reach a purchase event over the past 30 days. It is one minus the ratio of purchase events to add-to-cart events, expressed as a percent. Unlike the cart-to-purchase rate (which is a product-level benchmark from product daily data), this is a sitewide event-funnel measure drawn from the GA4 events mart.
how_to_read: Shown as a percentage of add-to-cart events that drop off before purchase
It is the inverse of the sitewide add-to-cart to purchase conversion
Computed from GA4 event counts, not product-level rows
Lower is better; a high drop rate signals checkout or availability friction
Spikes warrant investigating the checkout flow and stock levels
decisions_answered: What share of cart additions fail to convert into purchases?
How leaky is our overall add-to-cart to purchase funnel?
Is checkout friction getting better or worse?
Should we prioritize fixing the funnel over driving more traffic?
inventory:funnel_drop_rate.narration_text:
This metric measures how leaky your add-to-cart to purchase funnel is.

The formula is one minus the ratio of purchase events to add-to-cart events, shown as a percentage. So if eighty percent of cart adds never become purchases, the drop rate is eighty percent.

Unlike the cart-to-purchase benchmark, which looks at individual products, this is a sitewide view built from GA4 event counts over the last thirty days.

Reading it: lower is better. A high or rising drop rate points to checkout friction, stockouts, or pricing shock at the cart stage. When this climbs, fixing the funnel usually beats buying more traffic.
